D: Original: The Republic - Plato (Benjamin Jowett translation)
D: Narrative: The Republic - Plato - Narrative
D: Refashioned: The Republic - Plato - Refashioned

D: Original:
I went down yesterday to the Piraeus with Glaucon the son of Ariston, that I might offer up my prayers to the goddess; and also because I wanted to see in what manner they would celebrate the festival, which was a new thing. I was delighted with the procession of the inhabitants; but that of the Thracians was equally, if not more, beautiful. When we had finished our prayers and viewed the spectacle, we turned in the direction of the city; and at that instant Polemarchus the son of Cephalus chanced to catch sight of us from a distance as we were starting on our way home, and told his servant to run and bid us wait for him. The servant took hold of me by the cloak behind, and said: Polemarchus desires you to wait.

I turned round, and asked him where his master was. There he is, said the youth, coming after you, if you will only wait.

Certainly we will, said Glaucon; and in a few minutes Polemarchus appeared, and with him Adeimantus, Glaucon's brother, Niceratus the son of Nicias, and several others who had been at the procession.
D: Narrative:
I went down yesterday to the Piraeus with Glaucon the son of Ariston, that I might offer up my prayers to the goddess; and also because I wanted to see in what manner they would celebrate the festival, which was a new thing. I was delighted with the procession of the inhabitants; but that of the Thracians was equally, if not more, beautiful. When we had finished our prayers and viewed the spectacle, we turned in the direction of the city; and at that instant Polemarchus the son of Cephalus chanced to catch sight of us from a distance as we were starting on our way home, and told his servant to run and bid us wait for him. The servant took hold of me by the cloak behind, and said, "Polemarchus desires you to wait."

I turned round, and asked him where his master was.

"There he is," said the youth, "coming after you, if you will only wait."

"Certainly we will," said Glaucon; and in a few minutes Polemarchus appeared, and with him Adeimantus, Glaucon's brother, Niceratus the son of Nicias, and several others who had been at the procession.
D: Refashioned:
Yesterday I went down to the Piraeus with Glaucon, son of Ariston, to pray to the goddess and to see how they would celebrate the festival -- it was a new event. I enjoyed the local procession, though the Thracian one was just as impressive, if not more so. After finishing our prayers and watching the spectacle, we headed back toward the city. Just then, Polemarchus, son of Cephalus, spotted us from a distance and sent his servant running to stop us. The servant grabbed my cloak from behind and said, "Polemarchus wants you to wait."

I turned around and asked where his master was.

"He's right there," the young man said, "coming up behind you -- just wait."

"Of course we will," said Glaucon. A few minutes later Polemarchus arrived, along with Adeimantus (Glaucon's brother), Niceratus (son of Nicias), and several others from the procession.
D: Elegant: Socrates and Glaucon visit the Piraeus for a festival and are impressed by both the local and Thracian processions. As they head home, Polemarchus intercepts them, and a group gathers -- Adeimantus, Niceratus, and others from the celebration.
D: Summary: Socrates narrates his visit to the Piraeus with Glaucon for a religious festival. On their way home, Polemarchus sends a servant to detain them, and a group including Adeimantus and Niceratus joins them.
D: Distilled: Socrates and Glaucon attend a festival at the Piraeus and are intercepted by Polemarchus and friends on their way home.
Notes: Socrates narrates the scene-setting of the entire Republic. -- The Piraeus was the port district of Athens, about five miles from the city center, a cosmopolitan commercial hub. -- The festival is the Bendideia, honoring the Thracian goddess Bendis, whose worship had recently been officially recognized in Athens. -- Glaucon and Adeimantus are Plato's own brothers, making this a family affair for the author. -- Polemarchus, son of Cephalus, is a metic (resident alien) whose family was wealthy from their shield-manufacturing business. -- Niceratus is the son of the famous Athenian general Nicias, who would later die in the Sicilian Expedition. -- The dramatic date is approximately 421 BCE. -- The casual social setting contrasts with the weighty philosophical discussion that will follow -- a characteristic Platonic technique.

D: Original:
Polemarchus said to me: I perceive, Socrates, that you and our companion are already on your way to the city.

You are not far wrong, I said. But do you see, he rejoined, how many we are? Of course. And are you stronger than all these? for if not, you will have to remain where you are.

May there not be the alternative, I said, that we may persuade you to let us go?

But can you persuade us, if we refuse to listen to you? he said.

Certainly not, replied Glaucon. Then we are not going to listen; of that you may be assured.

Adeimantus added: Has no one told you of the torch-race on horseback in honour of the goddess which will take place in the evening?

With horses! I replied: That is a novelty. Will horsemen carry torches and pass them one to another during the race?

Yes, said Polemarchus, and not only so, but a festival will he celebrated at night, which you certainly ought to see. Let us rise soon after supper and see this festival; there will be a gathering of young men, and we will have a good talk. Stay then, and do not be perverse.

Glaucon said: I suppose, since you insist, that we must.

Very good, I replied.
D: Narrative:
Polemarchus said to me, "I perceive, Socrates, that you and our companion are already on your way to the city."

"You are not far wrong," I said.

"But do you see," he rejoined, "how many we are? Of course. And are you stronger than all these? For if not, you will have to remain where you are."

"May there not be the alternative," I said, "that we may persuade you to let us go?"

"But can you persuade us, if we refuse to listen to you?" he said.

"Certainly not," replied Glaucon.

"Then we are not going to listen; of that you may be assured."

Adeimantus added, "Has no one told you of the torch-race on horseback in honour of the goddess which will take place in the evening?"

"With horses!" I replied. "That is a novelty. Will horsemen carry torches and pass them one to another during the race?"

"Yes," said Polemarchus, "and not only so, but a festival will be celebrated at night, which you certainly ought to see. Let us rise soon after supper and see this festival; there will be a gathering of young men, and we will have a good talk. Stay then, and do not be perverse."

Glaucon said, "I suppose, since you insist, that we must."

"Very good," I replied.
D: Refashioned:
Polemarchus called out to me, "I see you and your friend are already heading back to the city, Socrates."

"That's right," I said.

"But look how many of us there are," he replied. "Are you stronger than all of us? Because if not, you'll have to stay."

"Isn't there another option?" I asked. "Maybe we could persuade you to let us go."

"Can you persuade people who refuse to listen?" he said.

"Obviously not," Glaucon admitted.

"Well then, we're not going to listen. Count on it."

Adeimantus chimed in, "Hasn't anyone told you about the torch-race on horseback tonight in honor of the goddess?"

"On horseback!" I said. "That's new. Will the riders carry torches and pass them to each other during the race?"

"Yes," said Polemarchus, "and there's also an all-night festival you really should see. We'll go right after dinner. There'll be lots of young people, and we can have a real conversation. So stay -- don't be difficult."

"I suppose we have no choice," said Glaucon.

"Very well," I replied.
D: Elegant: Polemarchus playfully detains Socrates by force of numbers, refusing to be "persuaded" to let him leave. The promise of an evening torch-race on horseback and a nighttime festival -- with good conversation -- convinces the group to stay.
D: Summary: Polemarchus jokingly threatens to keep Socrates by force, noting that persuasion requires willing listeners. The group agrees to stay for the evening's torch-race on horseback and the nighttime festival.
D: Distilled: Polemarchus playfully compels Socrates to stay by refusing to listen to persuasion, foreshadowing the dialogue's central concern with the power of rational argument.
Notes: Socrates, Polemarchus, Glaucon, and Adeimantus speak. -- The exchange about force vs. persuasion is deeply ironic and foreshadows the entire Republic's argument: can justice be established through reason, or only through power? -- Polemarchus's 'But can you persuade us, if we refuse to listen?' anticipates Thrasymachus's later position that might makes right. -- The torch-race on horseback (lampadedromia) was an innovation for the Bendideia festival. -- The playful coercion also prefigures the Allegory of the Cave (Book VII), where the philosopher must be compelled to return. -- This passage establishes the voluntary/involuntary tension that runs through the dialogue's treatment of justice.

D: Original: Accordingly we went with Polemarchus to his house; and there we found his brothers Lysias and Euthydemus, and with them Thrasymachus the Chalcedonian, Charmantides the Paeanian, and Cleitophon the son of Aristonymus. There too was Cephalus the father of Polemarchus, whom I had not seen for a long time, and I thought him very much aged. He was seated on a cushioned chair, and had a garland on his head, for he had been sacrificing in the court; and there were some other chairs in the room arranged in a semicircle, upon which we sat down by him. He saluted me eagerly, and then he said: --
D: Narrative: Accordingly we went with Polemarchus to his house; and there we found his brothers Lysias and Euthydemus, and with them Thrasymachus the Chalcedonian, Charmantides the Paeanian, and Cleitophon the son of Aristonymus. There too was Cephalus the father of Polemarchus, whom I had not seen for a long time, and I thought him very much aged. He was seated on a cushioned chair, and had a garland on his head, for he had been sacrificing in the court; and there were some other chairs in the room arranged in a semicircle, upon which we sat down by him. He saluted me eagerly, and then he said --
D: Refashioned: So we went with Polemarchus to his house. There we found his brothers Lysias and Euthydemus, along with Thrasymachus of Chalcedon, Charmantides of Paeania, and Cleitophon son of Aristonymus. Cephalus, Polemarchus's father, was also there -- I hadn't seen him in a long time and thought he looked much older. He was sitting in a cushioned chair wearing a garland, having just finished making sacrifices in the courtyard. Other chairs were arranged in a semicircle, and we sat down beside him. He greeted me warmly and began to speak.
D: Elegant: At Polemarchus's house, the company gathers: his brothers Lysias and Euthydemus, along with Thrasymachus, Charmantides, and Cleitophon. The aged Cephalus, freshly garlanded from sacrifice, greets Socrates warmly from his chair.
D: Summary: Socrates arrives at Polemarchus's house and finds a gathering that includes Thrasymachus and the elderly Cephalus, who has been performing sacrifices and greets Socrates eagerly.
D: Distilled: The scene is set at Cephalus's house with the key interlocutors assembled.
Notes: Socrates narrates the scene. -- Lysias, brother of Polemarchus, would become one of the most famous Attic orators; his speech 'Against Eratosthenes' describes the family's persecution under the Thirty Tyrants. -- Euthydemus shares a name with the subject of another Platonic dialogue, though they are different people. -- Thrasymachus of Chalcedon was a real historical sophist and rhetorician, known for his theory that justice serves the powerful. -- Cephalus's garland and sacrifice establish a religious context that will become significant when he later discusses fear of the afterlife. -- The semicircle seating arrangement suggests a philosophical symposium setting. -- Cleitophon appears in a short Platonic dialogue bearing his name, where he criticizes Socrates for exhorting virtue without defining it.

D: Original: You don't come to see me, Socrates, as often as you ought: If I were still able to go and see you I would not ask you to come to me. But at my age I can hardly get to the city, and therefore you should come oftener to the Piraeus. For let me tell you, that the more the pleasures of the body fade away, the greater to me is the pleasure and charm of conversation. Do not then deny my request, but make our house your resort and keep company with these young men; we are old friends, and you will be quite at home with us.
D: Narrative: Cephalus said, "You don't come to see me, Socrates, as often as you ought. If I were still able to go and see you I would not ask you to come to me. But at my age I can hardly get to the city, and therefore you should come oftener to the Piraeus. For let me tell you, that the more the pleasures of the body fade away, the greater to me is the pleasure and charm of conversation. Do not then deny my request, but make our house your resort and keep company with these young men; we are old friends, and you will be quite at home with us."
D: Refashioned: Cephalus said, "You don't visit me as often as you should, Socrates. If I could still travel to see you, I wouldn't ask you to come to me. But at my age, getting to the city is nearly impossible, so you should come to the Piraeus more often. I'll tell you something: the more the body's pleasures fade, the more I enjoy and value good conversation. So please, don't refuse me -- make our home your gathering place, spend time with these young men. We're old friends, and you'll feel right at home."
D: Elegant: Cephalus invites Socrates to visit more often, observing that as physical pleasures decline with age, the pleasure of conversation grows. He asks Socrates to make his home a place of regular gathering.
D: Summary: The elderly Cephalus warmly chides Socrates for not visiting enough, noting that as bodily pleasures fade with age, intellectual conversation becomes his greatest joy.
D: Distilled: Cephalus tells Socrates that conversation has replaced bodily pleasure as his chief delight in old age.
Notes: Cephalus speaks to Socrates. -- Cephalus's observation that conversation replaces bodily pleasure in old age introduces the dialogue's concern with the relationship between the body and the soul. -- This anticipates the Phaedo's argument that philosophy is preparation for death through the soul's liberation from bodily desires. -- The invitation to 'keep company with these young men' is ironic, given that Socrates was later charged with corrupting the youth of Athens. -- Cephalus represents the traditional, pre-philosophical view of virtue that Socrates will gently probe.

D: Original: I replied: There is nothing which for my part I like better, Cephalus, than conversing with aged men; for I regard them as travellers who have gone a journey which I too may have to go, and of whom I ought to enquire, whether the way is smooth and easy, or rugged and difficult. And this is a question which I should like to ask of you who have arrived at that time which the poets call the 'threshold of old age' --Is life harder towards the end, or what report do you give of it?
D: Narrative: I replied, "There is nothing which for my part I like better, Cephalus, than conversing with aged men; for I regard them as travellers who have gone a journey which I too may have to go, and of whom I ought to enquire, whether the way is smooth and easy, or rugged and difficult. And this is a question which I should like to ask of you who have arrived at that time which the poets call the 'threshold of old age' -- Is life harder towards the end, or what report do you give of it?"
D: Refashioned: I replied, "There's nothing I enjoy more, Cephalus, than talking with people who are older. I think of them as travelers who've already walked a road I'll have to walk myself -- and I want to ask them whether the path is smooth or rough. So let me put this question to you, since you've reached what the poets call the 'threshold of old age': Does life get harder toward the end? What's your report?"
D: Elegant: Socrates compares the elderly to travelers on a road he must also walk, and asks Cephalus directly: what is old age really like? Is the path harder toward the end?
D: Summary: Socrates tells Cephalus he values conversation with the elderly because they are like travelers on a road he must also travel, and asks whether life becomes more difficult in old age.
D: Distilled: Socrates asks Cephalus to report on old age, comparing the elderly to travelers who have gone ahead on life's journey.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Cephalus. -- The metaphor of life as a journey is ancient and recurrent in Greek literature; cf. the Odyssey's voyage as a metaphor for the soul's wandering. -- The 'threshold of old age' likely alludes to Homer or another early poet. -- Socrates's method of learning from experience anticipates his later argument that philosophers must return to the cave to share their knowledge (Republic Book VII). -- The question about old age opens the path to the dialogue's central question about justice, as Cephalus's answer will lead to reflections on death, the afterlife, and what it means to have lived well.

D: Original: I will tell you, Socrates, he said, what my own feeling is. Men of my age flock together; we are birds of a feather, as the old proverb says; and at our meetings the tale of my acquaintance commonly is --I cannot eat, I cannot drink; the pleasures of youth and love are fled away: there was a good time once, but now that is gone, and life is no longer life. Some complain of the slights which are put upon them by relations, and they will tell you sadly of how many evils their old age is the cause. But to me, Socrates, these complainers seem to blame that which is not really in fault. For if old age were the cause, I too being old, and every other old man, would have felt as they do. But this is not my own experience, nor that of others whom I have known. How well I remember the aged poet Sophocles, when in answer to the question, How does love suit with age, Sophocles, --are you still the man you were? Peace, he replied; most gladly have I escaped the thing of which you speak; I feel as if I had escaped from a mad and furious master. His words have often occurred to my mind since, and they seem as good to me now as at the time when he uttered them. For certainly old age has a great sense of calm and freedom; when the passions relax their hold, then, as Sophocles says, we are freed from the grasp not of one mad master only, but of many. The truth is, Socrates, that these regrets, and also the complaints about relations, are to be attributed to the same cause, which is not old age, but men's characters and tempers; for he who is of a calm and happy nature will hardly feel the pressure of age, but to him who is of an opposite disposition youth and age are equally a burden.
D: Narrative:
"I will tell you, Socrates," he said, "what my own feeling is. Men of my age flock together; we are birds of a feather, as the old proverb says; and at our meetings the tale of my acquaintance commonly is: 'I cannot eat, I cannot drink; the pleasures of youth and love are fled away: there was a good time once, but now that is gone, and life is no longer life.' Some complain of the slights which are put upon them by relations, and they will tell you sadly of how many evils their old age is the cause.

"But to me, Socrates, these complainers seem to blame that which is not really in fault. For if old age were the cause, I too being old, and every other old man, would have felt as they do. But this is not my own experience, nor that of others whom I have known.

"How well I remember the aged poet Sophocles, when in answer to the question, 'How does love suit with age, Sophocles -- are you still the man you were?' 'Peace,' he replied; 'most gladly have I escaped the thing of which you speak; I feel as if I had escaped from a mad and furious master.' His words have often occurred to my mind since, and they seem as good to me now as at the time when he uttered them. For certainly old age has a great sense of calm and freedom; when the passions relax their hold, then, as Sophocles says, we are freed from the grasp not of one mad master only, but of many.

"The truth is, Socrates, that these regrets, and also the complaints about relations, are to be attributed to the same cause, which is not old age, but men's characters and tempers; for he who is of a calm and happy nature will hardly feel the pressure of age, but to him who is of an opposite disposition youth and age are equally a burden."
D: Refashioned:
"I'll tell you how I see it, Socrates," he said. "Old men tend to stick together -- birds of a feather, as they say. And when we meet, the usual complaints go like this: 'I can't eat, I can't drink, the pleasures of youth and love are gone -- there was a good time once, but now it's over, and life isn't really living anymore.' Some grumble about being disrespected by their families, and they'll list every misery they blame on old age.

"But it seems to me these complainers are blaming the wrong thing. If old age itself were the cause, then I and every other old person would feel the same way. But that's not my experience, nor the experience of others I've known.

"I remember the old poet Sophocles being asked, 'How do you get along with love in old age, Sophocles? Are you still the man you were?' And he replied, 'Don't even mention it -- I'm thrilled to have escaped that thing. It's like escaping from a raving, violent master.' I've thought about his words many times since, and they ring just as true now as when he first said them. Old age really does bring a deep calm and freedom. When the passions loosen their grip, as Sophocles put it, you're freed from not just one mad master, but many.

"The truth is, Socrates, these regrets and complaints about family aren't caused by old age at all -- they come from people's characters and temperaments. A person with a calm and contented nature will barely notice the weight of aging, but someone with the opposite disposition finds both youth and old age equally burdensome."
D: Elegant: Cephalus rejects the common complaint that old age is miserable. He cites Sophocles, who celebrated escaping sexual desire as liberation from a 'mad and furious master.' The real cause of unhappiness in old age, Cephalus argues, is not age itself but a person's character -- the calm-natured barely feel it, while the discontented find every stage of life a burden.
D: Summary: Cephalus argues that old age is not inherently miserable, citing Sophocles's relief at escaping sexual desire. He contends that unhappiness in old age stems from character, not from aging itself -- a calm disposition makes all stages of life bearable.
D: Distilled: The misery of old age comes not from age itself but from one's character; the calm-natured find freedom when passion fades.
Notes: Cephalus speaks to Socrates. -- Sophocles (c. 496-406 BCE) was one of the three great Athenian tragedians; the anecdote about his relief from sexual desire was well known in antiquity. -- The image of desire as a 'mad and furious master' anticipates the Republic's tripartite soul (Book IV), where reason must govern the appetitive and spirited parts. -- Cephalus's argument that character determines experience of old age parallels the Republic's later claim that the just soul is harmonious regardless of external circumstances. -- The phrase 'birds of a feather' translates the Greek proverb about age-mates seeking each other out. -- This passage connects to the Phaedo's theme that the philosopher welcomes the body's declining hold on the soul. -- Cephalus's view is essentially a precursor to Stoic philosophy: it is not circumstances but our disposition toward them that determines happiness.

D: Original: I listened in admiration, and wanting to draw him out, that he might go on --Yes, Cephalus, I said: but I rather suspect that people in general are not convinced by you when you speak thus; they think that old age sits lightly upon you, not because of your happy disposition, but because you are rich, and wealth is well known to be a great comforter.
D: Narrative: I listened in admiration, and wanting to draw him out, that he might go on -- "Yes, Cephalus," I said, "but I rather suspect that people in general are not convinced by you when you speak thus; they think that old age sits lightly upon you, not because of your happy disposition, but because you are rich, and wealth is well known to be a great comforter."
D: Refashioned: I listened with admiration and wanted to keep him talking, so I said, "That's well put, Cephalus, but I suspect most people wouldn't buy it. They'd say old age is easy for you not because of your temperament, but because you're wealthy -- and everyone knows money is a great comfort."
D: Elegant: Socrates challenges Cephalus: most people would attribute his contentment in old age not to his character but to his wealth.
D: Summary: Socrates provocatively suggests that people would dismiss Cephalus's contentment as a product of wealth rather than character, drawing him into deeper reflection.
D: Distilled: Socrates challenges whether Cephalus's easy old age comes from good character or simply from being rich.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Cephalus. -- Socrates employs his characteristic technique of the elenchus: drawing out a position, then probing it with a counterargument. -- His mention of 'wanting to draw him out' reveals the strategic, midwife-like quality of Socratic questioning (cf. Theaetetus 150a-d on Socrates as intellectual midwife). -- The question of whether wealth or virtue produces happiness is central to the Republic; Thrasymachus will later argue that injustice (and the wealth it brings) is more profitable than justice. -- This also anticipates Book IX's argument that the philosopher's pleasure is superior to the pleasure of wealth.

D: Original:
You are right, he replied; they are not convinced: and there is something in what they say; not, however, so much as they imagine. I might answer them as Themistocles answered the Seriphian who was abusing him and saying that he was famous, not for his own merits but because he was an Athenian: 'If you had been a native of my country or I of yours, neither of us would have been famous.' And to those who are not rich and are impatient of old age, the same reply may be made; for to the good poor man old age cannot be a light burden, nor can a bad rich man ever have peace with himself.

May I ask, Cephalus, whether your fortune was for the most part inherited or acquired by you?
D: Narrative:
"You are right," he replied; "they are not convinced: and there is something in what they say; not, however, so much as they imagine. I might answer them as Themistocles answered the Seriphian who was abusing him and saying that he was famous, not for his own merits but because he was an Athenian: 'If you had been a native of my country or I of yours, neither of us would have been famous.' And to those who are not rich and are impatient of old age, the same reply may be made; for to the good poor man old age cannot be a light burden, nor can a bad rich man ever have peace with himself."

"May I ask, Cephalus," I said, "whether your fortune was for the most part inherited or acquired by you?"
D: Refashioned:
"You're right," he replied, "they aren't convinced. And there's some truth to what they say -- but not as much as they think. I'd answer them the way Themistocles answered the man from Seriphos who said he was only famous because he was Athenian, not through any merit of his own. Themistocles replied, 'If you had been born in my city, or I in yours, neither of us would be famous.' The same applies here: a good but poor man won't find old age easy, but a bad rich man will never find peace with himself either."

"May I ask, Cephalus," I said, "was your wealth mostly inherited or did you earn it yourself?"
D: Elegant: Cephalus concedes wealth helps but insists character matters more, citing Themistocles's retort that neither nature nor circumstance alone determines greatness. Both wealth and virtue are needed: a good poor man still suffers in old age, while a bad rich man knows no peace. Socrates then asks whether Cephalus's wealth was inherited or self-made.
D: Summary: Cephalus acknowledges wealth matters but argues it is not sufficient without good character, using Themistocles's famous retort to illustrate that both circumstances and nature play a role. Socrates then asks about the source of his fortune.
D: Distilled: Wealth helps in old age, but without good character even the rich find no peace.
Notes: Cephalus and Socrates speak. -- Themistocles (c. 524-459 BCE) was the Athenian statesman and naval strategist who engineered the Greek victory at Salamis. -- Seriphos was a tiny, insignificant island in the Cyclades; the anecdote illustrates that both individual merit and favorable circumstances matter. -- Cephalus's nuanced position -- that both wealth and character contribute to happiness -- is more sophisticated than Socrates initially credits him for. -- The question about inherited vs. acquired wealth steers the conversation toward the relationship between money and justice, which will become the dialogue's central concern. -- This balanced view anticipates Aristotle's position in the Nicomachean Ethics that some external goods are necessary for the good life.

D: Original: Acquired! Socrates; do you want to know how much I acquired? In the art of making money I have been midway between my father and grandfather: for my grandfather, whose name I bear, doubled and trebled the value of his patrimony, that which he inherited being much what I possess now; but my father Lysanias reduced the property below what it is at present: and I shall be satisfied if I leave to these my sons not less but a little more than I received.
D: Narrative: "Acquired!" Cephalus said. "Do you want to know how much I acquired? In the art of making money I have been midway between my father and grandfather: for my grandfather, whose name I bear, doubled and trebled the value of his patrimony, that which he inherited being much what I possess now; but my father Lysanias reduced the property below what it is at present: and I shall be satisfied if I leave to these my sons not less but a little more than I received."
D: Refashioned: "I earned it, Socrates! Want to know how much? When it comes to making money, I've fallen somewhere between my father and grandfather. My grandfather -- my namesake -- doubled or tripled what he inherited, starting from about what I have now. But my father Lysanias spent it down to less than its current value. I'll be content if I leave my sons a little more than what I started with."
D: Elegant: Cephalus describes his moderate relationship to wealth: his grandfather multiplied the family fortune, his father diminished it, and he aims simply to leave his sons slightly more than he received.
D: Summary: Cephalus explains he falls between his wealth-building grandfather and his wealth-spending father, and that he would be satisfied to pass on slightly more than he inherited.
D: Distilled: Cephalus has a moderate, unattached relationship to money -- he neither obsessively accumulates nor recklessly spends.
Notes: Cephalus speaks to Socrates. -- Cephalus's grandfather, also named Cephalus, was a successful businessman who built the family's shield-manufacturing enterprise in the Piraeus. -- The family were metics (resident aliens) from Syracuse, not Athenian citizens, which meant they could not own land and thus focused on commerce. -- Lysanias, Cephalus's father, appears to have been less business-minded. -- Cephalus's moderation about wealth -- neither grasping nor indifferent -- is relevant to the dialogue's later discussions of the just person's relationship to material goods. -- His contentment with 'a little more' contrasts sharply with Thrasymachus's later celebration of the tyrant who takes everything.

D: Original:
That was why I asked you the question, I replied, because I see that you are indifferent about money, which is a characteristic rather of those who have inherited their fortunes than of those who have acquired them; the makers of fortunes have a second love of money as a creation of their own, resembling the affection of authors for their own poems, or of parents for their children, besides that natural love of it for the sake of use and profit which is common to them and all men. And hence they are very bad company, for they can talk about nothing but the praises of wealth. That is true, he said.

Yes, that is very true, but may I ask another question? What do you consider to be the greatest blessing which you have reaped from your wealth?
D: Narrative:
"That was why I asked you the question," I replied, "because I see that you are indifferent about money, which is a characteristic rather of those who have inherited their fortunes than of those who have acquired them; the makers of fortunes have a second love of money as a creation of their own, resembling the affection of authors for their own poems, or of parents for their children, besides that natural love of it for the sake of use and profit which is common to them and all men. And hence they are very bad company, for they can talk about nothing but the praises of wealth."

"That is true," he said.

"Yes, that is very true, but may I ask another question? What do you consider to be the greatest blessing which you have reaped from your wealth?"
D: Refashioned:
"That's exactly why I asked," I replied. "You seem relatively indifferent to money, which is more typical of people who inherited their wealth than those who made it themselves. Self-made people have a double attachment to money -- they love it not just for its usefulness, but as their own creation, the way authors love their own writings or parents love their children. That makes them terrible company, because all they can talk about is how wonderful wealth is."

"That's true," he said.

"Very true indeed. But let me ask you something else: What do you consider the greatest benefit you've gained from your wealth?"
D: Elegant: Socrates observes that self-made people love money doubly -- for its utility and as their own creation, like authors with their writings. Since Cephalus lacks this possessive attachment, Socrates asks the pivotal question: what is the greatest blessing wealth has given him?
D: Summary: Socrates notes that self-made people love money as their own creation, making them obsessed with it. Since Cephalus is detached from wealth, Socrates asks what he considers its greatest blessing -- a question that will lead directly to the topic of justice.
D: Distilled: Those who make their own fortunes love money as a creation; Socrates asks the detached Cephalus what wealth's greatest benefit actually is.
Notes: Socrates and Cephalus speak. -- The analogy between wealth-creators and authors/parents is psychologically acute and anticipates modern research on the 'IKEA effect' and endowment bias. -- Socrates's observation about self-made people being 'bad company' is humorously pointed. -- The question about wealth's greatest blessing is a classic Socratic steering maneuver: it will lead Cephalus to discuss death, the afterlife, and the importance of having lived honestly -- which opens the door to the central question 'What is justice?' -- This passage connects to the Republic's later argument (Books VIII-IX) that the oligarchic personality is defined by the love of money above all else.

D: Original:
One, he said, of which I could not expect easily to convince others. For let me tell you, Socrates, that when a man thinks himself to be near death, fears and cares enter into his mind which he never had before; the tales of a world below and the punishment which is exacted there of deeds done here were once a laughing matter to him, but now he is tormented with the thought that they may be true: either from the weakness of age, or because he is now drawing nearer to that other place, he has a clearer view of these things; suspicions and alarms crowd thickly upon him, and he begins to reflect and consider what wrongs he has done to others. And when he finds that the sum of his transgressions is great he will many a time like a child start up in his sleep for fear, and he is filled with dark forebodings. But to him who is conscious of no sin, sweet hope, as Pindar charmingly says, is the kind nurse of his age:

Hope, he says, cherishes the soul of him who lives in justice and holiness and is the nurse of his age and the companion of his journey; --hope which is mightiest to sway the restless soul of man.
D: Narrative:
"One," he said, "of which I could not expect easily to convince others. For let me tell you, Socrates, that when a man thinks himself to be near death, fears and cares enter into his mind which he never had before; the tales of a world below and the punishment which is exacted there of deeds done here were once a laughing matter to him, but now he is tormented with the thought that they may be true: either from the weakness of age, or because he is now drawing nearer to that other place, he has a clearer view of these things; suspicions and alarms crowd thickly upon him, and he begins to reflect and consider what wrongs he has done to others.

"And when he finds that the sum of his transgressions is great he will many a time like a child start up in his sleep for fear, and he is filled with dark forebodings. But to him who is conscious of no sin, sweet hope, as Pindar charmingly says, is the kind nurse of his age:

"'Hope,' he says, 'cherishes the soul of him who lives in justice and holiness and is the nurse of his age and the companion of his journey; -- hope which is mightiest to sway the restless soul of man.'"
D: Refashioned:
"It's one that most people probably wouldn't accept," he said. "But let me explain, Socrates. When a man senses death approaching, fears enter his mind that were never there before. Stories about an underworld where you're punished for what you did in life -- he used to laugh at those. But now they torment him with the thought that they might be true. Whether it's the frailty of age or simply drawing closer to that other place, he sees these things more clearly. Suspicions and anxieties crowd in, and he starts reviewing the wrongs he's done to others.

"When he discovers that his offenses are serious, he'll bolt awake in the night like a frightened child, filled with dread. But for the man whose conscience is clear, there is what Pindar beautifully calls 'sweet hope -- the kind nurse of old age.'

"As Pindar says, hope sustains the soul of whoever lives justly and devoutly; it is the companion of his journey and the nurse of his old age -- hope, which has the greatest power to calm the restless human soul."
D: Elegant: Cephalus reveals that the approach of death transforms a man's relationship to morality. Stories of punishment in the afterlife, once dismissed, become terrifying possibilities. The guilty bolt awake in dread; the innocent are sustained by what Pindar calls 'sweet hope' -- the nurse of old age and companion of the soul's journey.
D: Summary: Cephalus says the greatest benefit of wealth is peace of mind near death. Those who have wronged others are tormented by fear of the afterlife, while those with a clear conscience are comforted by hope, which Pindar calls the nurse of old age.
D: Distilled: Proximity to death transforms afterlife stories from jokes into terrors; only a clear conscience brings peace.
Notes: Cephalus speaks to Socrates. -- Pindar (c. 518-438 BCE) was the greatest Greek lyric poet, famous for his victory odes; the fragment quoted here (fr. 214 Snell) is preserved only in this passage of Plato. -- The image of bolting awake 'like a child' is psychologically vivid and anticipates the Republic's later analysis of dreams as revelations of the soul's hidden desires (Book IX, 571c-572b). -- Cephalus's fear of the afterlife connects to the Myth of Er at the end of the Republic (Book X), where souls are judged and punished or rewarded. -- The shift from dismissing afterlife tales to taking them seriously parallels Pascal's later 'wager' argument. -- This passage is crucial because it links wealth to justice: Cephalus will argue that money's chief value is enabling one to live honestly, which provides comfort at death. -- The Phaedo similarly explores the philosopher's relationship to death, arguing that the examined life removes the fear of dying.

D: Original: How admirable are his words! And the great blessing of riches, I do not say to every man, but to a good man, is, that he has had no occasion to deceive or to defraud others, either intentionally or unintentionally; and when he departs to the world below he is not in any apprehension about offerings due to the gods or debts which he owes to men. Now to this peace of mind the possession of wealth greatly contributes; and therefore I say, that, setting one thing against another, of the many advantages which wealth has to give, to a man of sense this is in my opinion the greatest.
D: Narrative: "How admirable are his words! And the great blessing of riches, I do not say to every man, but to a good man, is, that he has had no occasion to deceive or to defraud others, either intentionally or unintentionally; and when he departs to the world below he is not in any apprehension about offerings due to the gods or debts which he owes to men. Now to this peace of mind the possession of wealth greatly contributes; and therefore I say, that, setting one thing against another, of the many advantages which wealth has to give, to a man of sense this is in my opinion the greatest."
D: Refashioned: "What wonderful words those are! And the real blessing of wealth -- not for everyone, but for a good person -- is that it removes the need to cheat or defraud anyone, whether on purpose or by accident. When such a man faces death, he has no anxiety about sacrifices owed to the gods or debts owed to other people. Wealth contributes enormously to this peace of mind. So weighing everything up, of all the advantages money can give, I believe this is the greatest for any sensible person."
D: Elegant: Wealth's greatest blessing, for a good person, is that it eliminates the need to deceive or defraud. This means dying without anxiety about unpaid debts to gods or men -- and that peace of mind, Cephalus argues, is money's highest gift.
D: Summary: Cephalus concludes that wealth's greatest benefit is enabling a good person to live honestly, so that he can face death without fear of owing debts to gods or men.
D: Distilled: Wealth's supreme value is the peace of mind that comes from never having needed to cheat anyone.
Notes: Cephalus speaks to Socrates. -- This passage completes Cephalus's answer to Socrates's question about wealth's greatest blessing (1.10) and implicitly offers his definition of justice: speaking the truth and paying one's debts. -- The concern with 'offerings due to the gods' reflects traditional Greek piety, which was transactional -- one owed the gods proper sacrifice. -- Cephalus's view is essentially conventional or 'pre-philosophical' morality: justice as honesty and debt-repayment. -- Socrates will immediately probe this definition with a counterexample (the madman's weapons), beginning the elenctic process. -- The distinction between intentional and unintentional wrongdoing anticipates Aristotle's treatment of voluntary and involuntary action in the Nicomachean Ethics (Book III). -- Cephalus's linkage of wealth, honesty, and peace of mind represents the respectable upper-class Athenian view that Socrates will show to be philosophically inadequate.

D: Original:
Well said, Cephalus, I replied; but as concerning justice, what is it? --to speak the truth and to pay your debts --no more than this? And even to this are there not exceptions? Suppose that a friend when in his right mind has deposited arms with me and he asks for them when he is not in his right mind, ought I to give them back to him? No one would say that I ought or that I should be right in doing so, any more than they would say that I ought always to speak the truth to one who is in his condition.

You are quite right, he replied. But then, I said, speaking the truth and paying your debts is not a correct definition of justice.
D: Narrative:
"Well said, Cephalus," I replied; "but as concerning justice, what is it? -- to speak the truth and to pay your debts -- no more than this? And even to this are there not exceptions? Suppose that a friend when in his right mind has deposited arms with me and he asks for them when he is not in his right mind, ought I to give them back to him? No one would say that I ought or that I should be right in doing so, any more than they would say that I ought always to speak the truth to one who is in his condition."

"You are quite right," he replied.

"But then," I said, "speaking the truth and paying your debts is not a correct definition of justice."
D: Refashioned:
"Well said, Cephalus," I replied. "But about justice itself -- what exactly is it? Just speaking the truth and repaying your debts? Nothing more? And aren't there exceptions even to that? Suppose a friend leaves weapons with me while he's sane, then comes to collect them when he's lost his mind. Should I give them back? No one would say I should, just as no one would say I should always tell the truth to someone in that state."

"You're quite right," he said.

"Then speaking the truth and paying your debts isn't a correct definition of justice."
D: Elegant: Socrates extracts Cephalus's implicit definition of justice -- truth-telling and debt-repayment -- then demolishes it with a single counterexample: should you return weapons to a friend who has gone mad? The definition fails because justice requires judgment, not mere rule-following.
D: Summary: Socrates draws out Cephalus's implicit definition of justice as truth-telling and debt-paying, then refutes it with the counterexample of returning weapons to a madman, showing the definition cannot be correct.
D: Distilled: Justice cannot simply mean speaking the truth and repaying debts, since returning a madman's weapons would be wrong.
Notes: Socrates and Cephalus speak. -- This is the first explicit formulation and refutation of a definition of justice in the Republic, setting the pattern for the entire dialogue. -- The counterexample of the madman's deposited weapons is one of the most famous in philosophical history and is still discussed in ethics courses today. -- It demonstrates that moral rules require contextual judgment -- a principle later developed by Aristotle as phronesis (practical wisdom) in the Nicomachean Ethics. -- The argument form -- accepting a general principle then finding a counterexample -- is the classic Socratic elenchus. -- This passage also raises the question of whether justice is a matter of following rules or exercising judgment, a debate that continues in deontological vs. virtue ethics. -- Cephalus's graceful acceptance of the refutation contrasts with Thrasymachus's later combativeness.

D: Original:
Quite correct, Socrates, if Simonides is to be believed, said Polemarchus interposing.

I fear, said Cephalus, that I must go now, for I have to look after the sacrifices, and I hand over the argument to Polemarchus and the company.

Is not Polemarchus your heir? I said. To be sure, he answered, and went away laughing to the sacrifices.
D: Narrative:
"Quite correct, Socrates, if Simonides is to be believed," said Polemarchus, interposing.

"I fear," said Cephalus, "that I must go now, for I have to look after the sacrifices, and I hand over the argument to Polemarchus and the company."

"Is not Polemarchus your heir?" I said.

"To be sure," he answered, and went away laughing to the sacrifices.
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly right, Socrates -- if we can trust Simonides," Polemarchus interjected.

"I'm afraid I have to leave now," said Cephalus. "I need to attend to the sacrifices. I'll hand the argument over to Polemarchus and the rest of you."

"Isn't Polemarchus your heir?" I asked.

"He certainly is," Cephalus replied, and left laughing to tend to the sacrifices.
D: Elegant: Polemarchus jumps in citing Simonides, and Cephalus cheerfully excuses himself for the sacrifices, bequeathing the argument to his son. Socrates's wry question -- 'Isn't Polemarchus your heir?' -- plays on the double inheritance of wealth and argument.
D: Summary: Polemarchus enters the discussion by invoking Simonides, while Cephalus excuses himself to attend to his sacrifices. Socrates wittily asks if Polemarchus is his 'heir' -- to both the fortune and the argument.
D: Distilled: Cephalus exits laughing, bequeathing the argument about justice to his son Polemarchus.
Notes: Polemarchus, Cephalus, and Socrates speak. -- Simonides of Ceos (c. 556-468 BCE) was a celebrated lyric poet known for his moral maxims; his definition of justice as 'giving each person what is owed' will drive the next section of dialogue. -- Cephalus's departure is thematically significant: he represents traditional, unreflective morality that cannot withstand philosophical scrutiny, and he literally walks away from the argument rather than engaging further. -- The pun on 'heir' (klēronomos) works in both Greek and English -- Polemarchus inherits both the family estate and the philosophical discussion. -- Cephalus's laughter and return to sacrifice symbolize the comfortable, pious life that never fully examines its own premises. -- This is one of the Republic's gentlest transitions; later transitions (especially Thrasymachus's eruption) will be far more dramatic.

D: Original:
Tell me then, O thou heir of the argument, what did Simonides say, and according to you truly say, about justice?

He said that the repayment of a debt is just, and in saying so he appears to me to be right.
D: Narrative:
"Tell me then, O thou heir of the argument, what did Simonides say, and according to you truly say, about justice?"

"He said that the repayment of a debt is just, and in saying so he appears to me to be right."
D: Refashioned:
"Then tell me, heir of the argument -- what did Simonides say about justice, and why do you think he was right?"

"He said that repaying what you owe is just, and I think he's correct."
D: Elegant: Socrates playfully addresses Polemarchus as 'heir of the argument' and asks him to defend Simonides's claim that justice is repaying what is owed.
D: Summary: Socrates asks Polemarchus to explain and defend Simonides's definition of justice as the repayment of debts.
D: Distilled: Polemarchus inherits the argument and defends Simonides's view that justice means repaying what is owed.
Notes: Socrates and Polemarchus speak. -- 'O thou heir of the argument' continues the inheritance joke from 1.14, but also signals that Polemarchus is now the primary interlocutor. -- Simonides's formulation -- that justice is giving to each what is owed -- is closely related to the later Latin legal maxim suum cuique ('to each his own'). -- This definition will prove more resilient than Cephalus's simpler version, but Socrates will still find it inadequate. -- Polemarchus's appeal to poetic authority reflects the pre-philosophical Greek tradition of looking to poets as moral teachers -- a practice the Republic will later challenge in Books II-III and X. -- The definition anticipates Aristotle's concept of distributive justice in the Nicomachean Ethics (Book V).

D: Original: I should be sorry to doubt the word of such a wise and inspired man, but his meaning, though probably clear to you, is the reverse of clear to me. For he certainly does not mean, as we were now saying that I ought to return a return a deposit of arms or of anything else to one who asks for it when he is not in his right senses; and yet a deposit cannot be denied to be a debt.
D: Narrative: "I should be sorry to doubt the word of such a wise and inspired man, but his meaning, though probably clear to you, is the reverse of clear to me. For he certainly does not mean, as we were now saying, that I ought to return a deposit of arms or of anything else to one who asks for it when he is not in his right senses; and yet a deposit cannot be denied to be a debt."
D: Refashioned: "I'd hate to question someone so wise and inspired, but his meaning, while probably clear to you, is the opposite of clear to me. He certainly can't mean what we were just discussing -- that I should return deposited weapons, or anything else, to someone who's lost his mind. And yet a deposit is undeniably a debt."
D: Elegant: Socrates feigns reverence for Simonides while noting the obvious problem: a deposit is a debt, but we already agreed that returning weapons to a madman is wrong. So 'repaying debts' cannot be the whole story.
D: Summary: Socrates professes respect for Simonides but presses the point that a deposit is certainly a debt, yet returning weapons to someone who has gone mad cannot be just -- so what does Simonides really mean?
D: Distilled: If a deposit is a debt but returning a madman's weapons is wrong, then justice must mean something more than debt-repayment.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Polemarchus. -- Socrates's profession of respect for Simonides is characteristically ironic: he is about to dismantle the poet's authority on the subject. -- The technique of claiming ignorance while actually leading the argument is classic Socratic irony (eirōneia). -- By recycling the madman counterexample from the conversation with Cephalus, Socrates shows continuity between the two discussions. -- The passage raises a question that will recur throughout the Republic: can poets be trusted as moral authorities? Books II-III will argue that poets must be censored, and Book X will banish them from the ideal city entirely. -- The distinction between what Simonides said and what he meant introduces the hermeneutic problem: texts require interpretation, and surface meaning may not capture deeper truth.

D: Original:
True. Then when the person who asks me is not in his right mind I am by no means to make the return?

Certainly not. When Simonides said that the repayment of a debt was justice, he did not mean to include that case?

Certainly not; for he thinks that a friend ought always to do good to a friend and never evil.

You mean that the return of a deposit of gold which is to the injury of the receiver, if the two parties are friends, is not the repayment of a debt, --that is what you would imagine him to say?

Yes. And are enemies also to receive what we owe to them? To be sure, he said, they are to receive what we owe them, and an enemy, as I take it, owes to an enemy that which is due or proper to him --that is to say, evil.

Simonides, then, after the manner of poets, would seem to have spoken darkly of the nature of justice; for he really meant to say that justice is the giving to each man what is proper to him, and this he termed a debt.

That must have been his meaning, he said. By heaven! I replied; and if we asked him what due or proper thing is given by medicine, and to whom, what answer do you think that he would make to us?

He would surely reply that medicine gives drugs and meat and drink to human bodies.

And what due or proper thing is given by cookery, and to what?

Seasoning to food. And what is that which justice gives, and to whom? If, Socrates, we are to be guided at all by the analogy of the preceding instances, then justice is the art which gives good to friends and evil to enemies.

That is his meaning then? I think so. And who is best able to do good to his friends and evil to his enemies in time of sickness?

The physician. Or when they are on a voyage, amid the perils of the sea?

The pilot. And in what sort of actions or with a view to what result is the just man most able to do harm to his enemy and good to his friends?
D: Narrative:
"True. Then when the person who asks me is not in his right mind I am by no means to make the return?"

"Certainly not."

"When Simonides said that the repayment of a debt was justice, he did not mean to include that case?"

"Certainly not; for he thinks that a friend ought always to do good to a friend and never evil."

"You mean that the return of a deposit of gold which is to the injury of the receiver, if the two parties are friends, is not the repayment of a debt -- that is what you would imagine him to say?"

"Yes."

"And are enemies also to receive what we owe to them?"

"To be sure," he said, "they are to receive what we owe them, and an enemy, as I take it, owes to an enemy that which is due or proper to him -- that is to say, evil."

"Simonides, then, after the manner of poets, would seem to have spoken darkly of the nature of justice; for he really meant to say that justice is the giving to each man what is proper to him, and this he termed a debt."

"That must have been his meaning," he said.

"By heaven!" I replied. "And if we asked him what due or proper thing is given by medicine, and to whom, what answer do you think that he would make to us?"

"He would surely reply that medicine gives drugs and meat and drink to human bodies."

"And what due or proper thing is given by cookery, and to what?"

"Seasoning to food."

"And what is that which justice gives, and to whom?"

"If, Socrates, we are to be guided at all by the analogy of the preceding instances, then justice is the art which gives good to friends and evil to enemies."

"That is his meaning then?"

"I think so."

"And who is best able to do good to his friends and evil to his enemies in time of sickness?"

"The physician."

"Or when they are on a voyage, amid the perils of the sea?"

"The pilot."

"And in what sort of actions or with a view to what result is the just man most able to do harm to his enemy and good to his friends?"
D: Refashioned:
"True. So when the person asking isn't in his right mind, I definitely shouldn't return the deposit?"

"Definitely not."

"Then Simonides didn't mean to include that case when he said repaying debts is just?"

"Of course not. He believed a friend should always do good to a friend, never harm."

"So you're saying that returning a deposit of gold to a friend, when it would hurt him, isn't really repaying a debt -- that's how you'd interpret Simonides?"

"Yes."

"And what about enemies? Do they also receive what we owe them?"

"Absolutely," he said. "We owe enemies what's appropriate to them -- which is to say, harm."

"So Simonides, speaking in the cryptic way poets do, actually meant that justice is giving each person what's appropriate to them, and he called this a 'debt.'"

"That must be what he meant."

"Interesting!" I said. "If we asked him, 'What appropriate thing does medicine give, and to whom?' -- what would he say?"

"That medicine gives drugs, food, and drink to human bodies."

"And what appropriate thing does cooking give, and to what?"

"Seasoning to food."

"Then what does justice give, and to whom?"

"If we follow the pattern of those examples, Socrates, justice is the skill of giving good to friends and harm to enemies."

"That's what he means?"

"I believe so."

"And who is best at helping friends and harming enemies when someone is sick?"

"The doctor."

"Or during a dangerous sea voyage?"

"The navigator."

"Then in what circumstances is the just person best at helping friends and harming enemies?"
D: Elegant: Through careful questioning, Socrates and Polemarchus refine Simonides's definition: justice means giving each person what is 'proper' or 'appropriate' to them -- good to friends, evil to enemies. But Socrates then applies the craft analogy: medicine gives health, cooking gives seasoning. What exactly does justice give, and when? The doctor helps in sickness, the navigator at sea -- so when is justice specifically useful?
D: Summary: Socrates draws out Polemarchus's interpretation of Simonides: justice means giving what is appropriate -- good to friends, evil to enemies. Using analogies to medicine and cookery, Socrates then asks in what specific domain the just person excels at helping friends and harming enemies.
D: Distilled: If justice means giving each person what is appropriate, Socrates asks: in what specific domain does the just person operate?
Notes: Socrates and Polemarchus speak. -- The reinterpretation of Simonides's 'debt' as 'what is proper or appropriate' (to prosēkon) shifts the definition from a rigid rule to a contextual judgment. -- The craft analogy (technē analogy) is one of Socrates's favorite argumentative tools, appearing across many Platonic dialogues (Gorgias, Ion, Euthydemus). -- The logic is: every craft has a specific domain and product; if justice is a craft, what is its specific product? -- The friend/enemy distinction reflects the traditional Greek ethic of helping friends and harming enemies, which Socrates will systematically dismantle. -- This ethic was widespread in Greek culture; cf. Archilochus: 'One great thing I know: how to repay with evil whoever does evil to me.' -- The passage sets up a reductio: if justice is only useful in specific practical domains, then specialized crafts always outperform it, making justice seemingly useless -- a paradox Socrates will exploit.

D: Original:
In going to war against the one and in making alliances with the other.

But when a man is well, my dear Polemarchus, there is no need of a physician?

No. And he who is not on a voyage has no need of a pilot? No. Then in time of peace justice will be of no use? I am very far from thinking so. You think that justice may be of use in peace as well as in war?

Yes. Like husbandry for the acquisition of corn? Yes. Or like shoemaking for the acquisition of shoes, --that is what you mean?

Yes. And what similar use or power of acquisition has justice in time of peace?

In contracts, Socrates, justice is of use. And by contracts you mean partnerships? Exactly. But is the just man or the skilful player a more useful and better partner at a game of draughts?

The skilful player. And in the laying of bricks and stones is the just man a more useful or better partner than the builder?

Quite the reverse. Then in what sort of partnership is the just man a better partner than the harp-player, as in playing the harp the harp-player is certainly a better partner than the just man?

In a money partnership. Yes, Polemarchus, but surely not in the use of money; for you do not want a just man to be your counsellor the purchase or sale of a horse; a man who is knowing about horses would be better for that, would he not?

Certainly. And when you want to buy a ship, the shipwright or the pilot would be better?

True. Then what is that joint use of silver or gold in which the just man is to be preferred?

When you want a deposit to be kept safely. You mean when money is not wanted, but allowed to lie? Precisely. That is to say, justice is useful when money is useless?

That is the inference. And when you want to keep a pruning-hook safe, then justice is useful to the individual and to the state; but when you want to use it, then the art of the vine-dresser?
D: Narrative:
"In going to war against the one and in making alliances with the other."

"But when a man is well, my dear Polemarchus, there is no need of a physician?"

"No."

"And he who is not on a voyage has no need of a pilot?"

"No."

"Then in time of peace justice will be of no use?"

"I am very far from thinking so."

"You think that justice may be of use in peace as well as in war?"

"Yes."

"Like husbandry for the acquisition of corn?"

"Yes."

"Or like shoemaking for the acquisition of shoes -- that is what you mean?"

"Yes."

"And what similar use or power of acquisition has justice in time of peace?"

"In contracts, Socrates, justice is of use."

"And by contracts you mean partnerships?"

"Exactly."

"But is the just man or the skilful player a more useful and better partner at a game of draughts?"

"The skilful player."

"And in the laying of bricks and stones is the just man a more useful or better partner than the builder?"

"Quite the reverse."

"Then in what sort of partnership is the just man a better partner than the harp-player, as in playing the harp the harp-player is certainly a better partner than the just man?"

"In a money partnership."

"Yes, Polemarchus, but surely not in the use of money; for you do not want a just man to be your counsellor in the purchase or sale of a horse; a man who is knowing about horses would be better for that, would he not?"

"Certainly."

"And when you want to buy a ship, the shipwright or the pilot would be better?"

"True."

"Then what is that joint use of silver or gold in which the just man is to be preferred?"

"When you want a deposit to be kept safely."

"You mean when money is not wanted, but allowed to lie?"

"Precisely."

"That is to say, justice is useful when money is useless?"

"That is the inference."

"And when you want to keep a pruning-hook safe, then justice is useful to the individual and to the state; but when you want to use it, then the art of the vine-dresser?"
D: Refashioned:
"In war -- fighting enemies and forming alliances with friends."

"But when someone is healthy, Polemarchus, there's no need for a doctor?"

"No."

"And someone who isn't at sea doesn't need a navigator?"

"No."

"Then in peacetime, justice is useless?"

"I wouldn't say that at all."

"So justice is useful in peace as well as war?"

"Yes."

"Like farming is useful for growing grain?"

"Yes."

"Or shoemaking for making shoes?"

"Yes."

"Then what equivalent use does justice have in peacetime?"

"In contracts and partnerships, Socrates."

"But in a board game, would you rather have the just man or the skilled player as your partner?"

"The skilled player."

"And for building a wall, is the just man more useful than the actual builder?"

"Of course not."

"Then in what kind of partnership is the just man better than the specialist? A musician is obviously better for music."

"Financial partnerships."

"But not for spending money, surely. If you're buying a horse, you want a horse expert, not just an honest man?"

"Certainly."

"And for buying a ship, the shipwright or captain would be better?"

"True."

"So in what specific use of money is the just man preferred?"

"When you need money kept safe."

"Meaning when money is sitting idle, not being used?"

"Precisely."

"So justice is useful when money is useless?"

"That seems to follow."

"And when you want a pruning hook kept safe, justice is useful -- but when you want to actually use it, you need a vine-dresser?"
D: Elegant: Polemarchus claims justice is useful in war, but Socrates presses: what about peacetime? Through a series of analogies -- board games, building, music, horses, ships -- every practical domain has its own expert who outperforms the merely just man. Justice, it turns out, is useful only for safekeeping money and tools that aren't being used. The paradoxical conclusion: justice is useful when things are useless.
D: Summary: Socrates systematically shows that in every practical partnership -- games, building, music, commerce -- the specialist is always more useful than the just man. Justice's domain shrinks to mere safekeeping of idle goods, yielding the paradox that justice is useful only when things are useless.
D: Distilled: In every practical domain a specialist outperforms the just man, reducing justice to the guardianship of things not being used.
Notes: Socrates and Polemarchus speak. -- The craft analogy here reaches its most devastating application: if every domain has its own expert, what domain belongs uniquely to justice? -- The paradox that 'justice is useful when money is useless' is a reductio ad absurdum -- it cannot be what justice really is. -- The progression from war to peace to contracts to specific partnerships methodically eliminates every candidate domain. -- The 'pruning-hook' example is characteristically Socratic in its mundane specificity. -- This argument anticipates the modern philosophical problem of 'the unity of virtue' -- whether justice is one thing or many. -- In Book IV, Plato will offer his own answer: justice is the proper ordering of the soul's parts, not a specialized skill at all. -- The passage also anticipates the distinction between justice as a disposition (hexis) and justice as a specific activity (energeia) that Aristotle will develop.

D: Original:
Clearly. And when you want to keep a shield or a lyre, and not to use them, you would say that justice is useful; but when you want to use them, then the art of the soldier or of the musician?

Certainly. And so of all the other things; --justice is useful when they are useless, and useless when they are useful?

That is the inference. Then justice is not good for much. But let us consider this further point: Is not he who can best strike a blow in a boxing match or in any kind of fighting best able to ward off a blow?

Certainly. And he who is most skilful in preventing or escaping from a disease is best able to create one?

True. And he is the best guard of a camp who is best able to steal a march upon the enemy?

Certainly. Then he who is a good keeper of anything is also a good thief?

That, I suppose, is to be inferred. Then if the just man is good at keeping money, he is good at stealing it.

That is implied in the argument. Then after all the just man has turned out to be a thief. And this is a lesson which I suspect you must have learnt out of Homer; for he, speaking of Autolycus, the maternal grandfather of Odysseus, who is a favourite of his, affirms that

He was excellent above all men in theft and perjury. And so, you and Homer and Simonides are agreed that justice is an art of theft; to be practised however 'for the good of friends and for the harm of enemies,' --that was what you were saying?

No, certainly not that, though I do not now know what I did say; but I still stand by the latter words.

Well, there is another question: By friends and enemies do we mean those who are so really, or only in seeming?

Surely, he said, a man may be expected to love those whom he thinks good, and to hate those whom he thinks evil.
D: Narrative:
"Clearly. And when you want to keep a shield or a lyre, and not to use them, you would say that justice is useful; but when you want to use them, then the art of the soldier or of the musician?"

"Certainly."

"And so of all the other things -- justice is useful when they are useless, and useless when they are useful?"

"That is the inference."

"Then justice is not good for much. But let us consider this further point: Is not he who can best strike a blow in a boxing match or in any kind of fighting best able to ward off a blow?"

"Certainly."

"And he who is most skilful in preventing or escaping from a disease is best able to create one?"

"True."

"And he is the best guard of a camp who is best able to steal a march upon the enemy?"

"Certainly."

"Then he who is a good keeper of anything is also a good thief?"

"That, I suppose, is to be inferred."

"Then if the just man is good at keeping money, he is good at stealing it."

"That is implied in the argument."

"Then after all the just man has turned out to be a thief. And this is a lesson which I suspect you must have learnt out of Homer; for he, speaking of Autolycus, the maternal grandfather of Odysseus, who is a favourite of his, affirms that 'He was excellent above all men in theft and perjury.'

"And so, you and Homer and Simonides are agreed that justice is an art of theft; to be practised however 'for the good of friends and for the harm of enemies' -- that was what you were saying?"

"No, certainly not that, though I do not now know what I did say; but I still stand by the latter words."

"Well, there is another question: By friends and enemies do we mean those who are so really, or only in seeming?"

"Surely," he said, "a man may be expected to love those whom he thinks good, and to hate those whom he thinks evil."
D: Refashioned:
"Right. And if you want a shield or a lyre kept safe but not used, justice is helpful. But to actually use them, you need a soldier or a musician?"

"Yes."

"So across the board: justice is useful when things are useless, and useless when they're useful?"

"That seems to follow."

"Then justice isn't worth much, is it? But let's push further. Isn't the best boxer -- the one who can land a blow -- also the best at blocking one?"

"Certainly."

"And the person most skilled at preventing disease is also best at causing it?"

"True."

"And the best camp guard is the one most capable of sneaking past the enemy?"

"Of course."

"Then a good protector of anything is also a good thief?"

"I suppose that follows."

"So if the just man is good at guarding money, he's also good at stealing it."

"That's what the argument implies."

"So the just man turns out to be a thief! You must have picked this up from Homer, who praises Autolycus, Odysseus's grandfather, as 'excellent above all men in theft and perjury.'

"So you, Homer, and Simonides all agree: justice is the art of theft -- practiced 'for the benefit of friends and the harm of enemies.' Wasn't that your position?"

"No, definitely not -- though I'm not sure anymore what I was saying. But I still stand by the principle about friends and enemies."

"Fair enough. But here's another question: when we say 'friends' and 'enemies,' do we mean people who truly are, or only those who appear to be?"

"Surely," he said, "people love those they believe to be good and hate those they believe to be evil."
D: Elegant: Socrates draws out an absurd consequence: since the best defender is also the best attacker, the just man -- as guardian of property -- is also the best thief. Homer even celebrates Odysseus's grandfather Autolycus for his excellence in theft. Polemarchus retreats in confusion but holds to his core principle: help friends, harm enemies. Socrates then probes a new weakness: do we mean real friends and enemies, or merely apparent ones?
D: Summary: Socrates argues that skill in guarding implies skill in stealing, making the just man paradoxically a thief. Polemarchus, confused, abandons the specifics but clings to the friend/enemy principle. Socrates then asks whether 'friends' and 'enemies' refers to reality or appearance.
D: Distilled: The good guardian is also the good thief; confused by the paradox, Polemarchus retreats to the friend/enemy distinction, which Socrates now targets.
Notes: Socrates and Polemarchus speak. -- The argument that defensive skill implies offensive skill is a recurring Socratic move; cf. Hippias Minor, where Socrates argues that the person who lies voluntarily is 'better' (more skilled) than the involuntary liar. -- Autolycus, Odysseus's maternal grandfather, was a legendary trickster figure associated with Hermes, the god of thieves (Odyssey 19.394-398). -- Homer's celebration of Autolycus's 'theft and perjury' was controversial even in antiquity and contributes to Plato's case against the moral authority of poetry. -- The shift from 'what is justice?' to 'who are friends and enemies?' marks a new phase of the argument, moving from the content of justice to the epistemological problem of moral knowledge. -- The question of appearance vs. reality is fundamental to Plato's philosophy, culminating in the Forms and the Allegory of the Cave.

D: Original:
Yes, but do not persons often err about good and evil: many who are not good seem to be so, and conversely?

That is true. Then to them the good will be enemies and the evil will be their friends? True.

And in that case they will be right in doing good to the evil and evil to the good?

Clearly. But the good are just and would not do an injustice? True. Then according to your argument it is just to injure those who do no wrong?

Nay, Socrates; the doctrine is immoral. Then I suppose that we ought to do good to the just and harm to the unjust?

I like that better. But see the consequence: --Many a man who is ignorant of human nature has friends who are bad friends, and in that case he ought to do harm to them; and he has good enemies whom he ought to benefit; but, if so, we shall be saying the very opposite of that which we affirmed to be the meaning of Simonides.

Very true, he said: and I think that we had better correct an error into which we seem to have fallen in the use of the words 'friend' and 'enemy.'

What was the error, Polemarchus? I asked. We assumed that he is a friend who seems to be or who is thought good.

And how is the error to be corrected? We should rather say that he is a friend who is, as well as seems, good; and that he who seems only, and is not good, only seems to be and is not a friend; and of an enemy the same may be said.

You would argue that the good are our friends and the bad our enemies?

Yes. And instead of saying simply as we did at first, that it is just to do good to our friends and harm to our enemies, we should further say: It is just to do good to our friends when they are good and harm to our enemies when they are evil?

Yes, that appears to me to be the truth. But ought the just to injure any one at all? Undoubtedly he ought to injure those who are both wicked and his enemies.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, but do not persons often err about good and evil: many who are not good seem to be so, and conversely?"

"That is true."

"Then to them the good will be enemies and the evil will be their friends?"

"True."

"And in that case they will be right in doing good to the evil and evil to the good?"

"Clearly."

"But the good are just and would not do an injustice?"

"True."

"Then according to your argument it is just to injure those who do no wrong?"

"Nay, Socrates; the doctrine is immoral."

"Then I suppose that we ought to do good to the just and harm to the unjust?"

"I like that better."

"But see the consequence: Many a man who is ignorant of human nature has friends who are bad friends, and in that case he ought to do harm to them; and he has good enemies whom he ought to benefit; but, if so, we shall be saying the very opposite of that which we affirmed to be the meaning of Simonides."

"Very true," he said; "and I think that we had better correct an error into which we seem to have fallen in the use of the words 'friend' and 'enemy.'"

"What was the error, Polemarchus?" I asked.

"We assumed that he is a friend who seems to be or who is thought good."

"And how is the error to be corrected?"

"We should rather say that he is a friend who is, as well as seems, good; and that he who seems only, and is not good, only seems to be and is not a friend; and of an enemy the same may be said."

"You would argue that the good are our friends and the bad our enemies?"

"Yes."

"And instead of saying simply as we did at first, that it is just to do good to our friends and harm to our enemies, we should further say: It is just to do good to our friends when they are good and harm to our enemies when they are evil?"

"Yes, that appears to me to be the truth."

"But ought the just to injure any one at all?"

"Undoubtedly he ought to injure those who are both wicked and his enemies."
D: Refashioned:
