"But don't people frequently misjudge good and evil? Many who aren't good appear to be, and vice versa?"

"That's true."

"So for these people, the genuinely good become their enemies and the genuinely bad become their friends?"

"Yes."

"And under your definition, they'd be right to help the bad and harm the good?"

"Apparently so."

"But the good are just people who wouldn't commit injustice?"

"True."

"So your argument makes it just to harm people who've done nothing wrong?"

"No, Socrates -- that's clearly immoral."

"Then perhaps we should say: do good to the just and harm to the unjust?"

"That's better."

"But consider the consequence. Many people misjudge character -- they have bad friends and good enemies. In that case, they should harm their friends and help their enemies, which is the exact opposite of what we said Simonides meant."

"You're right," he said. "I think we need to correct our use of 'friend' and 'enemy.'"

"What was the error?"

"We assumed that a friend is someone who merely seems or is thought to be good."

"How do we fix it?"

"A true friend is someone who is genuinely good, not just apparently good. Someone who only seems good but isn't is only an apparent friend, not a real one. The same applies to enemies."

"So the good are our true friends and the bad our true enemies?"

"Yes."

"Then instead of simply saying 'do good to friends and harm to enemies,' we should say: do good to friends who are genuinely good, and harm enemies who are genuinely evil?"

"Yes, I think that's right."

"But should the just person harm anyone at all?"

"Surely he should harm those who are both wicked and his enemies."
D: Elegant: Socrates reveals the fatal flaw in the friend/enemy principle: people misjudge character, so they end up helping the wicked and harming the good. Polemarchus tries to fix the definition by distinguishing real from apparent friends -- a true friend must actually be good, not just seem so. But Socrates presses further: should a just person harm anyone at all?
D: Summary: Socrates shows that since people misjudge character, the friend/enemy definition leads to harming the good and helping the wicked. Polemarchus refines the definition to require genuine goodness, but Socrates raises the deeper question: should justice ever involve harming anyone?
D: Distilled: Because people misjudge character, helping friends and harming enemies can mean helping the wicked and harming the good -- so the definition must be refined or abandoned.
Notes: Socrates and Polemarchus speak. -- The appearance/reality distinction is central to Platonic epistemology: people mistake apparent goods for real goods, apparent friends for real friends. -- Polemarchus's correction -- that a true friend must be good, not just seem good -- moves toward the Platonic position that knowledge of the Good is necessary for moral action. -- The question 'But ought the just to injure any one at all?' introduces a revolutionary moral principle that will challenge the traditional Greek ethic of retaliatory justice. -- This foreshadows Socrates's argument in the Crito (49b-c) that it is never right to return evil for evil. -- The traditional Greek ethic of helping friends and harming enemies was deeply embedded in the culture; cf. Meno 71e, where Meno defines virtue partly as 'the ability to harm enemies.' -- Socrates is laying the groundwork for the Republic's radical claim that justice is good in itself, not merely as a tool for reward and punishment.

D: Original:
When horses are injured, are they improved or deteriorated?

The latter. Deteriorated, that is to say, in the good qualities of horses, not of dogs?

Yes, of horses. And dogs are deteriorated in the good qualities of dogs, and not of horses?

Of course. And will not men who are injured be deteriorated in that which is the proper virtue of man?

Certainly. And that human virtue is justice? To be sure. Then men who are injured are of necessity made unjust? That is the result. But can the musician by his art make men unmusical? Certainly not. Or the horseman by his art make them bad horsemen? Impossible. And can the just by justice make men unjust, or speaking general can the good by virtue make them bad?

Assuredly not. Any more than heat can produce cold? It cannot. Or drought moisture? Clearly not. Nor can the good harm any one? Impossible. And the just is the good? Certainly. Then to injure a friend or any one else is not the act of a just man, but of the opposite, who is the unjust?

I think that what you say is quite true, Socrates. Then if a man says that justice consists in the repayment of debts, and that good is the debt which a man owes to his friends, and evil the debt which he owes to his enemies, --to say this is not wise; for it is not true, if, as has been clearly shown, the injuring of another can be in no case just.

I agree with you, said Polemarchus. Then you and I are prepared to take up arms against any one who attributes such a saying to Simonides or Bias or Pittacus, or any other wise man or seer?

I am quite ready to do battle at your side, he said. Shall I tell you whose I believe the saying to be? Whose? I believe that Periander or Perdiccas or Xerxes or Ismenias the Theban, or some other rich and mighty man, who had a great opinion of his own power, was the first to say that justice is 'doing good to your friends and harm to your enemies.'

Most true, he said. Yes, I said; but if this definition of justice also breaks down, what other can be offered?
D: Narrative:
"When horses are injured, are they improved or deteriorated?"

"The latter."

"Deteriorated, that is to say, in the good qualities of horses, not of dogs?"

"Yes, of horses."

"And dogs are deteriorated in the good qualities of dogs, and not of horses?"

"Of course."

"And will not men who are injured be deteriorated in that which is the proper virtue of man?"

"Certainly."

"And that human virtue is justice?"

"To be sure."

"Then men who are injured are of necessity made unjust?"

"That is the result."

"But can the musician by his art make men unmusical?"

"Certainly not."

"Or the horseman by his art make them bad horsemen?"

"Impossible."

"And can the just by justice make men unjust, or speaking generally can the good by virtue make them bad?"

"Assuredly not."

"Any more than heat can produce cold?"

"It cannot."

"Or drought moisture?"

"Clearly not."

"Nor can the good harm any one?"

"Impossible."

"And the just is the good?"

"Certainly."

"Then to injure a friend or any one else is not the act of a just man, but of the opposite, who is the unjust?"

"I think that what you say is quite true, Socrates."

"Then if a man says that justice consists in the repayment of debts, and that good is the debt which a man owes to his friends, and evil the debt which he owes to his enemies -- to say this is not wise; for it is not true, if, as has been clearly shown, the injuring of another can be in no case just."

"I agree with you," said Polemarchus.

"Then you and I are prepared to take up arms against any one who attributes such a saying to Simonides or Bias or Pittacus, or any other wise man or seer?"

"I am quite ready to do battle at your side," he said.

"Shall I tell you whose I believe the saying to be?"

"Whose?"

"I believe that Periander or Perdiccas or Xerxes or Ismenias the Theban, or some other rich and mighty man, who had a great opinion of his own power, was the first to say that justice is 'doing good to your friends and harm to your enemies.'"

"Most true," he said.

"Yes," I said; "but if this definition of justice also breaks down, what other can be offered?"
D: Refashioned:
"When you injure a horse, does it get better or worse?"

"Worse."

"Worse as a horse specifically -- not worse as a dog?"

"Right, worse as a horse."

"And injured dogs get worse as dogs, not as horses?"

"Obviously."

"Then when people are injured, they deteriorate in the quality that's distinctly human?"

"Yes."

"And that quality is justice?"

"Exactly."

"So injuring people necessarily makes them more unjust?"

"That follows."

"But can a musician, through the practice of music, make people unmusical?"

"No."

"Can a horseman, through horsemanship, make people bad riders?"

"Impossible."

"Then can a just person, through the exercise of justice, make others unjust? Can goodness produce badness?"

"Absolutely not."

"Any more than heat can produce cold?"

"It can't."

"Or drought produce moisture?"

"No."

"Then the good cannot harm anyone?"

"Impossible."

"And the just person is good?"

"Yes."

"So harming a friend -- or anyone else -- is not the act of a just person, but of an unjust one?"

"I think you're absolutely right, Socrates."

"Then saying that justice means repaying debts -- good to friends, evil to enemies -- is simply wrong. As we've shown, harming others can never be just."

"I agree," said Polemarchus.

"So we're ready to fight anyone who attributes this saying to Simonides or Bias or Pittacus or any other sage?"

"I'm ready to fight alongside you."

"Want to know who I think actually invented it?"

"Who?"

"Some rich and powerful man -- Periander, Perdiccas, Xerxes, Ismenias of Thebes -- someone with an inflated sense of his own power. That's where 'do good to friends and harm to enemies' really comes from."

"Absolutely right."

"Well then -- if this definition of justice also collapses, what can replace it?"
D: Elegant: Socrates's decisive argument: injuring a horse makes it a worse horse; injuring a person makes them more unjust. Since no quality can produce its own opposite -- heat cannot create cold, music cannot create anti-music -- justice cannot produce injustice through harming others. Therefore, the just person never harms anyone. The 'help friends, harm enemies' doctrine belongs not to wise men like Simonides but to tyrants like Xerxes who confuse power with justice. With both definitions refuted, the question remains: what is justice?
D: Summary: Socrates proves that since injury makes people worse in their defining virtue (justice), and since no quality can produce its opposite, the just person can never harm anyone. The friend/enemy doctrine is attributed not to sages but to powerful tyrants, and the search for justice's true definition must continue.
D: Distilled: Justice cannot involve harming anyone, since injury degrades the human virtue of justice itself -- and no excellence can produce its own opposite.
Notes: Socrates and Polemarchus speak. -- This argument is one of Socrates's most important ethical conclusions and represents a radical break from traditional Greek morality. -- The principle that the good cannot harm is restated in the Crito (49b-d) and Gorgias (469b-c). -- The chain of analogies (horses, dogs, musicians, horsemen) builds to the general principle through induction. -- Bias and Pittacus were two of the legendary Seven Sages of Greece. -- Periander was tyrant of Corinth; Perdiccas was king of Macedon; Xerxes was the Persian king who invaded Greece; Ismenias was a wealthy Theban politician. -- The attribution of the friend/enemy doctrine to tyrants rather than sages is politically charged: it associates conventional morality with the exercise of raw power, anticipating Thrasymachus's explicit argument that justice serves the strong. -- The concluding question -- 'what other definition can be offered?' -- creates the dramatic opening for Thrasymachus's eruption.

D: Original: Several times in the course of the discussion Thrasymachus had made an attempt to get the argument into his own hands, and had been put down by the rest of the company, who wanted to hear the end. But when Polemarchus and I had done speaking and there was a pause, he could no longer hold his peace; and, gathering himself up, he came at us like a wild beast, seeking to devour us. We were quite panic-stricken at the sight of him.
D: Narrative: Several times in the course of the discussion Thrasymachus had made an attempt to get the argument into his own hands, and had been put down by the rest of the company, who wanted to hear the end. But when Polemarchus and I had done speaking and there was a pause, he could no longer hold his peace; and, gathering himself up, he came at us like a wild beast, seeking to devour us. We were quite panic-stricken at the sight of him.
D: Refashioned: Throughout our conversation, Thrasymachus had tried several times to seize control of the discussion, but the others kept holding him back because they wanted to hear how it ended. When Polemarchus and I finally stopped and there was a pause, he couldn't restrain himself any longer. He coiled up and lunged at us like a wild animal going in for the kill. We were genuinely terrified.
D: Elegant: Thrasymachus, held back throughout the discussion, finally erupts when there is a pause -- charging at Socrates and Polemarchus like a wild beast. The company is terrified.
D: Summary: Thrasymachus, who has been impatiently trying to interrupt, erupts into the discussion like a wild animal the moment Socrates and Polemarchus pause, terrifying the assembled company.
D: Distilled: Thrasymachus, unable to restrain himself any longer, erupts into the conversation with predatory fury.
Notes: Socrates narrates. -- The animal imagery ('like a wild beast, seeking to devour us') characterizes Thrasymachus's approach to argument as aggressive and predatory, contrasting with Socrates's cooperative dialectical method. -- This vivid narrative interlude is unusual in the Republic and heightens the dramatic tension. -- Thrasymachus's violent entry into the dialogue mirrors his philosophical position: just as he forces his way into the conversation, he will argue that justice is merely the imposition of the stronger's will. -- The other guests restraining him suggests that the group valued the Socratic method of patient inquiry, while Thrasymachus represents the sophistic preference for rhetorical dominance. -- The description anticipates the Republic's later analysis of the tyrannical soul (Books VIII-IX), which is governed by violent appetites that overwhelm reason. -- Thrasymachus of Chalcedon was historically known for his emotional rhetorical style; Aristotle references his ability to arouse pity and indignation (Rhetoric III.1).

D: Original:
He roared out to the whole company: What folly. Socrates, has taken possession of you all? And why, sillybillies, do you knock under to one another? I say that if you want really to know what justice is, you should not only ask but answer, and you should not seek honour to yourself from the refutation of an opponent, but have your own answer; for there is many a one who can ask and cannot answer. And now I will not have you say that justice is duty or advantage or profit or gain or interest, for this sort of nonsense will not do for me; I must have clearness and accuracy.

I was panic-stricken at his words, and could not look at him without trembling. Indeed I believe that if I had not fixed my eye upon him, I should have been struck dumb: but when I saw his fury rising, I looked at him first, and was therefore able to reply to him.
D: Narrative:
He roared out to the whole company, "What folly, Socrates, has taken possession of you all? And why, sillybillies, do you knock under to one another? I say that if you want really to know what justice is, you should not only ask but answer, and you should not seek honour to yourself from the refutation of an opponent, but have your own answer; for there is many a one who can ask and cannot answer. And now I will not have you say that justice is duty or advantage or profit or gain or interest, for this sort of nonsense will not do for me; I must have clearness and accuracy."

I was panic-stricken at his words, and could not look at him without trembling. Indeed I believe that if I had not fixed my eye upon him, I should have been struck dumb: but when I saw his fury rising, I looked at him first, and was therefore able to reply to him.
D: Refashioned:
He bellowed at the entire room, "What is this nonsense, Socrates? What's gotten into all of you? Why are you fools deferring to each other? If you actually want to know what justice is, stop just asking questions and give some answers. Stop scoring points by tearing down other people's positions and put forward your own. Plenty of people can ask questions but can't answer them. And I'm warning you now -- don't tell me justice is 'duty' or 'advantage' or 'profit' or 'gain' or 'interest.' That vague garbage won't fly with me. I demand clarity and precision."

I was shaken by his outburst and could barely look at him without trembling. I believe that if I hadn't forced myself to meet his gaze, I would have been struck speechless. But seeing his rage build, I looked at him directly, and that gave me the courage to respond.
D: Elegant: Thrasymachus erupts, denouncing the entire discussion as foolish mutual deference. He demands that Socrates stop merely asking and start answering, and pre-emptively bans a list of common definitions. Socrates, trembling, forces himself to meet Thrasymachus's gaze and finds the nerve to reply.
D: Summary: Thrasymachus explodes into the conversation, attacking Socrates for only asking questions, never answering. He bans several common definitions of justice in advance, demanding precision. Socrates is visibly shaken but steadies himself by looking directly at his challenger.
D: Distilled: Thrasymachus demands that Socrates stop hiding behind questions and give his own definition of justice.
Notes: Thrasymachus and Socrates speak; Socrates narrates. -- Thrasymachus's complaint that Socrates 'only asks and never answers' is a recurring criticism of the Socratic method, also voiced in the Meno (80a) and Gorgias (461c-d). -- The pre-emptive ban on certain answers is a rhetorical power play, designed to control the terms of debate. -- Socrates's description of his own fear is likely ironic or at least dramatized -- Socrates was famously courageous (cf. his military service at Potidaea and Delium described in the Symposium and Laches). -- The detail about meeting Thrasymachus's gaze 'first' suggests that facing aggression directly is better than avoiding it -- a principle Socrates will apply philosophically. -- 'Sillybillies' translates a dismissive Greek term (euētheis) that implies naivety or simple-mindedness. -- Thrasymachus's demand for 'clearness and accuracy' is ironic, given that his own definition will prove to be neither clear nor accurate.

D: Original:
Thrasymachus, I said, with a quiver, don't be hard upon us. Polemarchus and I may have been guilty of a little mistake in the argument, but I can assure you that the error was not intentional. If we were seeking for a piece of gold, you would not imagine that we were 'knocking under to one another,' and so losing our chance of finding it. And why, when we are seeking for justice, a thing more precious than many pieces of gold, do you say that we are weakly yielding to one another and not doing our utmost to get at the truth? Nay, my good friend, we are most willing and anxious to do so, but the fact is that we cannot. And if so, you people who know all things should pity us and not be angry with us.

How characteristic of Socrates! he replied, with a bitter laugh; --that's your ironical style! Did I not foresee --have I not already told you, that whatever he was asked he would refuse to answer, and try irony or any other shuffle, in order that he might avoid answering?
D: Narrative:
"Thrasymachus," I said, with a quiver, "don't be hard upon us. Polemarchus and I may have been guilty of a little mistake in the argument, but I can assure you that the error was not intentional. If we were seeking for a piece of gold, you would not imagine that we were 'knocking under to one another,' and so losing our chance of finding it. And why, when we are seeking for justice, a thing more precious than many pieces of gold, do you say that we are weakly yielding to one another and not doing our utmost to get at the truth? Nay, my good friend, we are most willing and anxious to do so, but the fact is that we cannot. And if so, you people who know all things should pity us and not be angry with us."

"How characteristic of Socrates!" he replied, with a bitter laugh; "that's your ironical style! Did I not foresee -- have I not already told you, that whatever he was asked he would refuse to answer, and try irony or any other shuffle, in order that he might avoid answering?"
D: Refashioned:
"Thrasymachus," I said, my voice shaking a little, "don't be so hard on us. If Polemarchus and I made a mistake in our reasoning, I promise it wasn't on purpose. If we were searching for gold, you wouldn't accuse us of 'deferring to each other' and missing our chance to find it. So why, when we're searching for justice -- something far more valuable than gold -- do you accuse us of going easy on each other instead of pursuing the truth? Believe me, we desperately want to find the answer. We just can't. And if that's the case, people like you who have all the answers should feel sorry for us, not get angry."

"How typical of Socrates!" he shot back with a bitter laugh. "There's that famous irony! Didn't I predict this? Didn't I tell everyone that he'd refuse to answer and resort to irony or some other dodge to avoid committing himself?"
D: Elegant: Socrates humbly defends the inquiry: searching for justice, more precious than gold, deserves compassion for those who struggle, not anger from those who claim to know. Thrasymachus laughs bitterly and accuses Socrates of his trademark irony -- using false modesty to avoid giving real answers.
D: Summary: Socrates appeals to Thrasymachus for patience, comparing their search for justice to a search for gold and asking for pity rather than anger. Thrasymachus dismisses this as classic Socratic irony, accusing him of deliberately avoiding direct answers.
D: Distilled: Socrates asks for compassion in the search for justice; Thrasymachus accuses him of hiding behind irony.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- The comparison of justice to gold inverts the typical Greek valuation and anticipates the Republic's argument that justice is the soul's highest good. -- Socrates's claim of inability (we 'cannot' find the answer) is the posture of Socratic ignorance -- not false modesty but a genuine philosophical position that recognizing one's ignorance is the beginning of wisdom (cf. Apology 21d). -- Thrasymachus's accusation of 'irony' (eirōneia) reveals his awareness of Socrates's characteristic method, but he interprets it as dishonesty rather than philosophical technique. -- In Greek, eirōneia originally meant 'dissembling' or 'pretending ignorance,' with more negative connotations than the modern English 'irony.' -- The tension between Socrates's cooperative inquiry and Thrasymachus's competitive rhetoric embodies the Republic's later distinction between dialectic and eristic (Book VII).

D: Original: You are a philosopher, Thrasymachus, I replied, and well know that if you ask a person what numbers make up twelve, taking care to prohibit him whom you ask from answering twice six, or three times four, or six times two, or four times three, 'for this sort of nonsense will not do for me,' --then obviously, that is your way of putting the question, no one can answer you. But suppose that he were to retort, 'Thrasymachus, what do you mean? If one of these numbers which you interdict be the true answer to the question, am I falsely to say some other number which is not the right one? --is that your meaning?' -How would you answer him?
D: Narrative: "You are a philosopher, Thrasymachus," I replied, "and well know that if you ask a person what numbers make up twelve, taking care to prohibit him whom you ask from answering twice six, or three times four, or six times two, or four times three, 'for this sort of nonsense will not do for me' -- then obviously, if that is your way of putting the question, no one can answer you. But suppose that he were to retort, 'Thrasymachus, what do you mean? If one of these numbers which you interdict be the true answer to the question, am I falsely to say some other number which is not the right one? -- is that your meaning?' -- How would you answer him?"
D: Refashioned: "You're a man of learning, Thrasymachus," I replied. "You know perfectly well that if you ask someone what numbers make twelve, but then forbid them from saying twice six, three times four, six times two, or four times three -- 'none of that nonsense' -- then obviously no one can answer you. But what if they pushed back and said, 'What are you asking? If one of the answers you've banned is actually correct, am I supposed to give a wrong answer just to satisfy you?' What would you say to that?"
D: Elegant: Socrates turns Thrasymachus's pre-emptive bans against him: banning all the correct answers to a math problem doesn't make the question harder -- it makes it unanswerable. If the forbidden definitions happen to be right, should one lie to comply?
D: Summary: Socrates counters Thrasymachus's tactic of pre-emptively banning definitions with a mathematical analogy: if you forbid all the correct answers to 'what makes twelve,' no one can answer. Banning answers doesn't invalidate them.
D: Distilled: Banning correct answers to a question does not make them wrong -- it just makes the question impossible to answer honestly.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Thrasymachus. -- The mathematical analogy is effective because mathematics admits of definite, demonstrable answers -- Socrates implies that justice may similarly have a definite nature that certain formulations correctly capture. -- The reference to 'twice six, three times four' etc. shows that the same truth can be expressed in multiple equivalent ways -- a principle relevant to the question of whether different definitions of justice might capture the same underlying reality. -- Socrates's address 'You are a philosopher' is likely ironic, since Thrasymachus is a rhetorician, not a philosopher in Socrates's sense. -- The passage demonstrates Socrates's skill at turning an opponent's rhetorical tactics back on themselves -- a form of argumentative judo.

D: Original:
Just as if the two cases were at all alike! he said. Why should they not be? I replied; and even if they are not, but only appear to be so to the person who is asked, ought he not to say what he thinks, whether you and I forbid him or not?

I presume then that you are going to make one of the interdicted answers?

I dare say that I may, notwithstanding the danger, if upon reflection I approve of any of them.

But what if I give you an answer about justice other and better, he said, than any of these? What do you deserve to have done to you?

Done to me! --as becomes the ignorant, I must learn from the wise --that is what I deserve to have done to me.

What, and no payment! a pleasant notion! I will pay when I have the money, I replied.
D: Narrative:
"Just as if the two cases were at all alike!" he said.

"Why should they not be?" I replied. "And even if they are not, but only appear to be so to the person who is asked, ought he not to say what he thinks, whether you and I forbid him or not?"

"I presume then that you are going to make one of the interdicted answers?"

"I dare say that I may, notwithstanding the danger, if upon reflection I approve of any of them."

"But what if I give you an answer about justice other and better," he said, "than any of these? What do you deserve to have done to you?"

"Done to me! -- as becomes the ignorant, I must learn from the wise -- that is what I deserve to have done to me."

"What, and no payment! A pleasant notion!"

"I will pay when I have the money," I replied.
D: Refashioned:
"As if the two cases are even comparable!" he said.

"Why wouldn't they be?" I replied. "And even if they're not, but seem comparable to the person being asked -- shouldn't he say what he honestly thinks, regardless of what you or I prohibit?"

"So you're planning to give one of the forbidden answers?"

"I might, despite the risk, if on reflection I think any of them is correct."

"But what if I give you a definition of justice that's better than all of those?" he said. "What do you deserve then?"

"What I deserve? The same thing any ignorant person deserves -- to learn from someone wiser. That's my punishment."

"What -- and pay nothing? How convenient!"

"I'll pay when I have the money," I said.
D: Elegant: Thrasymachus scoffs at the analogy, but Socrates insists a person should speak honestly regardless of arbitrary prohibitions. Thrasymachus demands to know Socrates's penalty if a superior answer is produced. Socrates's answer is characteristically self-deprecating: the ignorant man's due is simply to learn from the wise.
D: Summary: Thrasymachus rejects Socrates's analogy and demands to set the stakes: what does Socrates deserve if Thrasymachus provides a better answer? Socrates cheerfully proposes that learning from the wise is the proper 'punishment' for ignorance, and promises to pay -- when he has money.
D: Distilled: Socrates proposes that the proper reward for ignorance is education, and cheerfully acknowledges his own poverty.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- The exchange about payment reflects the real economic context: sophists like Thrasymachus charged fees for their teaching, while Socrates famously did not (cf. Apology 19d-20c). -- Socrates's 'I will pay when I have the money' is both humorous and pointed -- his poverty is genuine and stands as an implicit reproach to paid teachers of virtue. -- The question of what 'punishment' the ignorant deserve introduces the theme of punishment and education that will be developed throughout the Republic, especially in the Allegory of the Cave where the philosopher 'punishes' himself by returning to teach. -- Thrasymachus's competitive framing (better/worse answers, stakes, punishment) contrasts with Socrates's cooperative framing (learning, teaching, mutual inquiry). -- The passage also prefigures the discussion of how rulers must be compensated (1.53), where Socrates argues that the best rulers serve reluctantly and require inducement.

D: Original:
But you have, Socrates, said Glaucon: and you, Thrasymachus, need be under no anxiety about money, for we will all make a contribution for Socrates.

Yes, he replied, and then Socrates will do as he always does --refuse to answer himself, but take and pull to pieces the answer of some one else.
D: Narrative:
"But you have, Socrates," said Glaucon; "and you, Thrasymachus, need be under no anxiety about money, for we will all make a contribution for Socrates."

"Yes," he replied, "and then Socrates will do as he always does -- refuse to answer himself, but take and pull to pieces the answer of some one else."
D: Refashioned:
"But you do have money, Socrates," Glaucon interjected. "And Thrasymachus, don't worry about payment -- we'll all chip in on Socrates's behalf."

"Right," Thrasymachus replied, "and then Socrates will do what he always does -- refuse to give his own answer and just tear apart someone else's."
D: Elegant: Glaucon offers to fund Socrates's 'tuition,' but Thrasymachus predicts that Socrates will dodge giving his own answer and simply demolish whatever is offered.
D: Summary: Glaucon volunteers the group to pay Thrasymachus on Socrates's behalf. Thrasymachus predicts Socrates will avoid committing to his own position and instead dismantle others' answers.
D: Distilled: Glaucon offers to cover Socrates's debt, but Thrasymachus predicts Socrates will only critique, never commit.
Notes: Glaucon and Thrasymachus speak. -- Glaucon's offer to pay is generous and suggests the solidarity of the group against Thrasymachus's aggression. -- Thrasymachus's complaint accurately describes the elenctic method: Socrates characteristically examines others' positions rather than advancing his own. -- This criticism recurs in the Cleitophon dialogue, where Cleitophon explicitly accuses Socrates of being a great exhorter but a poor teacher who never reveals his own positive doctrines. -- The tension between Socratic questioning (which reveals what justice is not) and positive doctrine (which reveals what justice is) drives the structure of the entire Republic: Book I is elenctic, while Books II-X attempt the constructive project.

D: Original: Why, my good friend, I said, how can any one answer who knows, and says that he knows, just nothing; and who, even if he has some faint notions of his own, is told by a man of authority not to utter them? The natural thing is, that the speaker should be some one like yourself who professes to know and can tell what he knows. Will you then kindly answer, for the edification of the company and of myself ?
D: Narrative: "Why, my good friend," I said, "how can any one answer who knows, and says that he knows, just nothing; and who, even if he has some faint notions of his own, is told by a man of authority not to utter them? The natural thing is, that the speaker should be some one like yourself who professes to know and can tell what he knows. Will you then kindly answer, for the edification of the company and of myself?"
D: Refashioned: "My good friend," I said, "how can someone give an answer when he admits he knows nothing -- and when the little he might have to say has been pre-emptively forbidden by someone in authority? The person who should be speaking is someone like you, who claims to know the answer and can share it. So please, enlighten us all -- myself included."
D: Elegant: Socrates deflects: the person who professes knowledge should be the one to speak. Since Thrasymachus claims expertise and Socrates claims ignorance, the burden of answering falls naturally on Thrasymachus.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that since he professes ignorance and Thrasymachus claims knowledge, Thrasymachus should be the one to provide the definition of justice for the benefit of all.
D: Distilled: The burden of answering belongs to whoever claims knowledge -- and that is Thrasymachus, not Socrates.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Thrasymachus. -- Socrates's profession of ignorance is the hallmark of the Socratic method, rooted in the Delphic oracle's pronouncement that no one is wiser than Socrates (Apology 21a). -- The phrase 'a man of authority' ironically refers to Thrasymachus himself, who has attempted to dictate the terms of the discussion. -- Socrates's request 'for the edification of the company and of myself' maintains the fiction of student-teacher relationship while actually setting Thrasymachus up for examination. -- The passage exemplifies the inversion of power that characterizes Socratic dialogue: the one who claims ignorance controls the conversation, while the one who claims knowledge becomes vulnerable to refutation.

D: Original: Glaucon and the rest of the company joined in my request and Thrasymachus, as any one might see, was in reality eager to speak; for he thought that he had an excellent answer, and would distinguish himself. But at first he to insist on my answering; at length he consented to begin. Behold, he said, the wisdom of Socrates; he refuses to teach himself, and goes about learning of others, to whom he never even says thank you.
D: Narrative:
Glaucon and the rest of the company joined in my request, and Thrasymachus, as any one might see, was in reality eager to speak; for he thought that he had an excellent answer, and would distinguish himself. But at first he affected to insist on my answering; at length he consented to begin.

"Behold," he said, "the wisdom of Socrates; he refuses to teach himself, and goes about learning of others, to whom he never even says thank you."
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon and the others backed me up. It was obvious that Thrasymachus was actually eager to speak -- he believed he had a brilliant answer that would make him look impressive. But he kept up the pretense of insisting I go first. Finally, he gave in.

"Behold the wisdom of Socrates!" he announced. "He refuses to teach anyone anything, goes around learning from everyone else, and never even says thank you."
D: Elegant: The group presses Thrasymachus to speak, and despite his pretense of reluctance, he is visibly eager to showcase his answer. He opens with a sarcastic indictment: Socrates takes from everyone and gives nothing back.
D: Summary: Under pressure from the group, Thrasymachus agrees to speak, though he was clearly eager all along. He opens with a cutting remark about Socrates's habit of learning from others without teaching or thanking them.
D: Distilled: Thrasymachus, eager beneath his show of reluctance, accuses Socrates of intellectual parasitism -- always learning, never teaching.
Notes: Socrates narrates; Thrasymachus speaks. -- Socrates's observation that Thrasymachus 'was in reality eager to speak' but 'affected to insist' on Socrates answering reveals the sophist's competitive motivation: he wants to display his cleverness, not genuinely inquire. -- The accusation that Socrates 'never says thank you' echoes the historical criticism of Socrates as ungrateful to his teachers; cf. Xenophon's Memorabilia. -- Thrasymachus's sarcastic 'Behold the wisdom of Socrates' inverts the genuine admiration others express for Socratic wisdom. -- The tension between appearance (reluctance) and reality (eagerness) in Thrasymachus's behavior ironically mirrors the appearance/reality distinction that Socrates has been exploring in the definitions of justice.

D: Original: That I learn of others, I replied, is quite true; but that I am ungrateful I wholly deny. Money I have none, and therefore I pay in praise, which is all I have: and how ready I am to praise any one who appears to me to speak well you will very soon find out when you answer; for I expect that you will answer well.
D: Narrative: "That I learn of others," I replied, "is quite true; but that I am ungrateful I wholly deny. Money I have none, and therefore I pay in praise, which is all I have: and how ready I am to praise any one who appears to me to speak well you will very soon find out when you answer; for I expect that you will answer well."
D: Refashioned: "It's true that I learn from others," I replied. "But ungrateful? I completely deny that. I have no money, so I pay the only way I can -- with praise. And you'll see soon enough how generous I am with praise when someone speaks well. I fully expect you will speak well."
D: Elegant: Socrates accepts the charge of always learning but denies ingratitude: he pays in the only currency he has -- praise. And he flatters Thrasymachus with the expectation of an excellent answer.
D: Summary: Socrates admits he learns from others but denies being ungrateful, saying he pays in praise since he has no money. He flatters Thrasymachus by expressing confidence that his answer will be good.
D: Distilled: Socrates pays for his education with praise, since he has no money -- and expects Thrasymachus will earn it.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Thrasymachus. -- Socrates's claim to pay in praise rather than money draws a sharp contrast between philosophical inquiry (which circulates ideas freely) and sophistic teaching (which charges fees). -- The promise to praise a good answer is both genuine and strategic: it creates a positive incentive for Thrasymachus to commit to a position that can then be examined. -- Socrates's poverty was legendary and is referenced in multiple dialogues (Apology, Symposium) as evidence of his philosophical sincerity. -- The passage also contains subtle irony: Socrates 'expects' a good answer, but the reader knows he will systematically dismantle whatever Thrasymachus says. -- The contrast between payment in money and payment in praise anticipates the Republic's discussion of honor vs. material wealth in the analysis of the timocratic and oligarchic personalities (Book VIII).

D: Original:
Listen, then, he said; I proclaim that justice is nothing else than the interest of the stronger. And now why do you not me? But of course you won't.

Let me first understand you, I replied. justice, as you say, is the interest of the stronger. What, Thrasymachus, is the meaning of this? You cannot mean to say that because Polydamas, the pancratiast, is stronger than we are, and finds the eating of beef conducive to his bodily strength, that to eat beef is therefore equally for our good who are weaker than he is, and right and just for us?

That's abominable of you, Socrates; you take the words in the sense which is most damaging to the argument.

Not at all, my good sir, I said; I am trying to understand them; and I wish that you would be a little clearer.

Well, he said, have you never heard that forms of government differ; there are tyrannies, and there are democracies, and there are aristocracies?
D: Narrative:
"Listen, then," he said; "I proclaim that justice is nothing else than the interest of the stronger. And now why do you not praise me? But of course you won't."

"Let me first understand you," I replied. "Justice, as you say, is the interest of the stronger. What, Thrasymachus, is the meaning of this? You cannot mean to say that because Polydamas, the pancratiast, is stronger than we are, and finds the eating of beef conducive to his bodily strength, that to eat beef is therefore equally for our good who are weaker than he is, and right and just for us?"

"That's abominable of you, Socrates; you take the words in the sense which is most damaging to the argument."

"Not at all, my good sir," I said; "I am trying to understand them; and I wish that you would be a little clearer."

"Well," he said, "have you never heard that forms of government differ; there are tyrannies, and there are democracies, and there are aristocracies?"
D: Refashioned:
"Listen up," he said. "I declare that justice is nothing other than the interest of the stronger. Now -- aren't you going to applaud? Of course not."

"Let me make sure I understand you," I said. "Justice is the interest of the stronger -- what exactly does that mean? Surely you don't mean that because Polydamas the wrestler is stronger than us and benefits from eating beef, eating beef is therefore just and beneficial for the rest of us too?"

"That's a deliberately terrible interpretation, Socrates. You're twisting my words into their worst possible meaning."

"Not at all," I said. "I'm genuinely trying to understand. Perhaps you could be clearer."

"Have you never heard," he said, "that there are different forms of government -- tyrannies, democracies, aristocracies?"
D: Elegant: Thrasymachus declares his thesis: justice is nothing but the interest of the stronger. Socrates feigns confusion with a deliberately absurd interpretation involving a wrestler's beef diet. Thrasymachus, irritated, clarifies that he means political strength: different forms of government each define justice according to their own interests.
D: Summary: Thrasymachus boldly proclaims that justice is the interest of the stronger. Socrates tests the claim with a comically literal reading -- does a wrestler's diet become just for everyone? -- forcing Thrasymachus to clarify that he means political power, not physical strength.
D: Distilled: Thrasymachus's thesis: justice is nothing but the interest of the stronger, meaning the ruling power in any state.
Notes: Thrasymachus and Socrates speak. -- Thrasymachus's definition is one of the most famous in the history of philosophy and has been interpreted as a precursor to legal positivism, Marxist ideology critique, and Nietzschean power ethics. -- Polydamas was a famous pancratiast (all-round fighter) of the era, an Olympic victor known for his extraordinary physical strength. -- Socrates's deliberate misinterpretation is a classic elenctic technique: by taking a claim too literally, he forces the speaker to clarify and thereby commit to a more precise (and more vulnerable) position. -- The clarification through forms of government (tyranny, democracy, aristocracy) shows that Thrasymachus means political, not physical, strength. -- This thesis anticipates Thomas Hobbes's argument in Leviathan that justice is whatever the sovereign commands, and Karl Marx's claim that the ruling ideas of any era are the ideas of the ruling class.

D: Original: Yes, I know. And the government is the ruling power in each state? Certainly. And the different forms of government make laws democratical, aristocratical, tyrannical, with a view to their several interests; and these laws, which are made by them for their own interests, are the justice which they deliver to their subjects, and him who transgresses them they punish as a breaker of the law, and unjust. And that is what I mean when I say that in all states there is the same principle of justice, which is the interest of the government; and as the government must be supposed to have power, the only reasonable conclusion is, that everywhere there is one principle of justice, which is the interest of the stronger.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, I know."

"And the government is the ruling power in each state?"

"Certainly."

"And the different forms of government make laws democratical, aristocratical, tyrannical, with a view to their several interests; and these laws, which are made by them for their own interests, are the justice which they deliver to their subjects, and him who transgresses them they punish as a breaker of the law, and unjust. And that is what I mean when I say that in all states there is the same principle of justice, which is the interest of the government; and as the government must be supposed to have power, the only reasonable conclusion is, that everywhere there is one principle of justice, which is the interest of the stronger."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, I know."

"And in each state, the government holds power?"

"Of course."

"Each form of government makes laws to serve its own interests -- democratic laws for the democracy's interest, aristocratic laws for the aristocracy's, tyrannical laws for the tyrant's. These laws, designed for the rulers' benefit, are what they impose on their subjects as 'justice,' and anyone who breaks them is punished as a lawbreaker -- as 'unjust.' That's my point. In every state, the same principle holds: justice is what serves the government's interest. And since the government is by definition the power in control, the only logical conclusion is that justice everywhere is the interest of the stronger."
D: Elegant: Thrasymachus completes his argument: every government -- democratic, aristocratic, tyrannical -- makes laws to serve its own interests and calls obedience to those laws 'justice.' Since government is the ruling power, justice universally reduces to the interest of whoever holds power.
D: Summary: Thrasymachus argues that all governments legislate in their own interest, labeling obedience to those laws as 'justice' and disobedience as 'injustice.' Since the government holds power, justice everywhere is simply the interest of the stronger.
D: Distilled: Every government defines justice as obedience to its own self-serving laws, making justice universally the interest of the ruling power.
Notes: Thrasymachus speaks to Socrates. -- This is the fullest statement of Thrasymachus's position, which combines descriptive political sociology (how governments actually behave) with a normative claim (this is what justice really is). -- The argument has three premises: (1) governments make laws to serve their own interests; (2) they call these laws 'justice'; (3) governments are the strongest party. The conclusion: justice = the interest of the stronger. -- This position has been compared to the Melian Dialogue in Thucydides (History 5.84-116), where Athens tells Melos that 'the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must.' -- The argument conflates legality with justice, a conflation that Socrates will exploit. -- In modern terms, Thrasymachus's position resembles legal positivism (the view that law is whatever the sovereign commands) rather than natural law theory (the view that law must conform to some independent standard of justice). -- The Republic's remaining nine books can be read as an extended response to this challenge.

D: Original:
Now I understand you, I said; and whether you are right or not I will try to discover. But let me remark, that in defining justice you have yourself used the word 'interest' which you forbade me to use. It is true, however, that in your definition the words 'of the stronger' are added.

A small addition, you must allow, he said. Great or small, never mind about that: we must first enquire whether what you are saying is the truth. Now we are both agreed that justice is interest of some sort, but you go on to say 'of the stronger'; about this addition I am not so sure, and must therefore consider further.

Proceed. I will; and first tell me, Do you admit that it is just or subjects to obey their rulers?

I do. But are the rulers of states absolutely infallible, or are they sometimes liable to err?

To be sure, he replied, they are liable to err. Then in making their laws they may sometimes make them rightly, and sometimes not?

True. When they make them rightly, they make them agreeably to their interest; when they are mistaken, contrary to their interest; you admit that?

Yes. And the laws which they make must be obeyed by their subjects, --and that is what you call justice?

Doubtless. Then justice, according to your argument, is not only obedience to the interest of the stronger but the reverse?

What is that you are saying? he asked. I am only repeating what you are saying, I believe. But let us consider: Have we not admitted that the rulers may be mistaken about their own interest in what they command, and also that to obey them is justice? Has not that been admitted?
D: Narrative:
"Now I understand you," I said; "and whether you are right or not I will try to discover. But let me remark, that in defining justice you have yourself used the word 'interest' which you forbade me to use. It is true, however, that in your definition the words 'of the stronger' are added."

"A small addition, you must allow," he said.

"Great or small, never mind about that: we must first enquire whether what you are saying is the truth. Now we are both agreed that justice is interest of some sort, but you go on to say 'of the stronger'; about this addition I am not so sure, and must therefore consider further."

"Proceed."

"I will; and first tell me, Do you admit that it is just for subjects to obey their rulers?"

"I do."

"But are the rulers of states absolutely infallible, or are they sometimes liable to err?"

"To be sure," he replied, "they are liable to err."

"Then in making their laws they may sometimes make them rightly, and sometimes not?"

"True."

"When they make them rightly, they make them agreeably to their interest; when they are mistaken, contrary to their interest; you admit that?"

"Yes."

"And the laws which they make must be obeyed by their subjects -- and that is what you call justice?"

"Doubtless."

"Then justice, according to your argument, is not only obedience to the interest of the stronger but the reverse?"

"What is that you are saying?" he asked.

"I am only repeating what you are saying, I believe. But let us consider: Have we not admitted that the rulers may be mistaken about their own interest in what they command, and also that to obey them is justice? Has not that been admitted?"
D: Refashioned:
"Now I understand you," I said. "Whether you're right, I'll try to determine. But notice: in defining justice, you yourself used the word 'interest,' which you banned me from using. You've just added 'of the stronger.'"

"A minor addition," he said.

"Major or minor, let's set that aside and examine whether what you're saying is true. We agree justice involves interest of some kind, but you add 'of the stronger' -- and that's the part I need to think about."

"Go ahead."

"First: do you agree it's just for subjects to obey their rulers?"

"Yes."

"Are rulers infallible, or do they sometimes make mistakes?"

"They make mistakes," he admitted.

"So sometimes they enact laws that serve their interests, and sometimes laws that don't?"

"True."

"And either way, subjects must obey -- and that obedience is what you call justice?"

"Yes."

"Then justice, on your account, is sometimes obedience to the stronger's interest and sometimes obedience to what hurts the stronger?"

"What are you saying?" he demanded.

"Just following your own logic. We've agreed that rulers sometimes enact laws against their own interest, and that obeying those laws is just. Right?"
D: Elegant: Socrates first catches Thrasymachus using the very word 'interest' he banned from the discussion. Then he springs the trap: if rulers sometimes err and enact bad laws, and subjects must obey all laws, then justice sometimes requires acting against the ruler's interest -- the opposite of what Thrasymachus claimed.
D: Summary: Socrates notes that Thrasymachus used the forbidden word 'interest' in his own definition. He then extracts a critical admission: rulers can err. Since subjects must obey even mistaken laws, justice would sometimes mean acting against the stronger's interest -- contradicting the thesis.
D: Distilled: If rulers are fallible but obedience is still just, then justice sometimes works against the interest of the stronger.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- Socrates's observation about the word 'interest' is both humorous and logically pointed: Thrasymachus has violated his own rule. -- The argument that follows is a classic Socratic trap set through a series of small, seemingly innocuous admissions that lead to a devastating conclusion. -- The logical structure: (1) Rulers sometimes err; (2) Erring rulers command things contrary to their interest; (3) Subjects must obey; (4) Therefore justice sometimes = acting against the stronger's interest. -- This contradiction forces Thrasymachus to either abandon his thesis or retreat to a more extreme position (which he will do in 1.37 by introducing the concept of the 'ruler in the strict sense'). -- The fallibility argument anticipates modern criticisms of legal positivism: if the sovereign can command unjust laws, then identifying justice with sovereign command is problematic.

D: Original:
Yes. Then you must also have acknowledged justice not to be for the interest of the stronger, when the rulers unintentionally command things to be done which are to their own injury. For if, as you say, justice is the obedience which the subject renders to their commands, in that case, O wisest of men, is there any escape from the conclusion that the weaker are commanded to do, not what is for the interest, but what is for the injury of the stronger?

Nothing can be clearer, Socrates, said Polemarchus.
D: Narrative:
"Yes."

"Then you must also have acknowledged justice not to be for the interest of the stronger, when the rulers unintentionally command things to be done which are to their own injury. For if, as you say, justice is the obedience which the subject renders to their commands, in that case, O wisest of men, is there any escape from the conclusion that the weaker are commanded to do, not what is for the interest, but what is for the injury of the stronger?"

"Nothing can be clearer, Socrates," said Polemarchus.
D: Refashioned:
"Yes."

"Then you've effectively conceded that justice is not always for the interest of the stronger. When rulers mistakenly command things that harm themselves, and subjects obey -- as you say they justly must -- then justice requires the weaker to do what injures the stronger. O wisest of men, can you escape that conclusion?"

"Nothing could be clearer, Socrates," said Polemarchus.
D: Elegant: Socrates drives the point home: when fallible rulers command self-harming laws, obedient subjects justly act against the ruler's interest. Polemarchus confirms the conclusion is inescapable.
D: Summary: Socrates completes the refutation: if fallible rulers sometimes command what harms themselves, and obedience to those commands is just, then justice sometimes works against the stronger's interest. Polemarchus emphatically agrees.
D: Distilled: Obedience to a mistaken ruler's commands means justice can require acting against the stronger's interest -- a direct contradiction.
Notes: Socrates and Polemarchus speak. -- The address 'O wisest of men' is pointed Socratic irony, turning Thrasymachus's pretensions to wisdom against him. -- Polemarchus's interjection shows he has fully come over to Socrates's side after their earlier disagreement. -- This is the first time Thrasymachus's position has been formally contradicted through his own admissions. -- The argument demonstrates the power of the elenctic method: Thrasymachus has been refuted using only premises he himself accepted. -- Thrasymachus will attempt to escape this trap (in 1.37) by distinguishing between the ruler in the 'common' sense (who can err) and the ruler in the 'strict' sense (who, by definition, never errs). -- The logical structure parallels the problem of divine command theory in theology: if God commands something harmful, is it still good?

D: Original:
Yes, said Cleitophon, interposing, if you are allowed to be his witness.

But there is no need of any witness, said Polemarchus, for Thrasymachus himself acknowledges that rulers may sometimes command what is not for their own interest, and that for subjects to obey them is justice.

Yes, Polemarchus, --Thrasymachus said that for subjects to do what was commanded by their rulers is just.

Yes, Cleitophon, but he also said that justice is the interest of the stronger, and, while admitting both these propositions, he further acknowledged that the stronger may command the weaker who are his subjects to do what is not for his own interest; whence follows that justice is the injury quite as much as the interest of the stronger.

But, said Cleitophon, he meant by the interest of the stronger what the stronger thought to be his interest, --this was what the weaker had to do; and this was affirmed by him to be justice.

Those were not his words, rejoined Polemarchus.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," said Cleitophon, interposing, "if you are allowed to be his witness."

"But there is no need of any witness," said Polemarchus, "for Thrasymachus himself acknowledges that rulers may sometimes command what is not for their own interest, and that for subjects to obey them is justice."

"Yes, Polemarchus -- Thrasymachus said that for subjects to do what was commanded by their rulers is just."

"Yes, Cleitophon, but he also said that justice is the interest of the stronger, and, while admitting both these propositions, he further acknowledged that the stronger may command the weaker who are his subjects to do what is not for his own interest; whence follows that justice is the injury quite as much as the interest of the stronger."

"But," said Cleitophon, "he meant by the interest of the stronger what the stronger thought to be his interest -- this was what the weaker had to do; and this was affirmed by him to be justice."

"Those were not his words," rejoined Polemarchus.
D: Refashioned:
"Sure," Cleitophon interjected, "if you're allowed to speak as his witness."

"We don't need witnesses," Polemarchus replied. "Thrasymachus himself admitted that rulers sometimes command things that aren't in their own interest, and that obeying them is still just."

"True, Polemarchus. He did say that obeying rulers is just."

"Yes, Cleitophon, but he also said justice is the interest of the stronger. And after admitting both points, he conceded that the stronger can command the weaker to do things against his own interest. So it follows that justice is just as much the injury of the stronger as it is the interest of the stronger."

"But what he meant," Cleitophon said, "was that justice is whatever the stronger believes to be his interest -- that's what the weaker must do."

"That's not what he said," Polemarchus replied.
D: Elegant: A brief dispute erupts among the bystanders. Cleitophon tries to rescue Thrasymachus by reinterpreting his position: justice is what the ruler believes to be his interest, not what actually is. Polemarchus insists this revision was not what Thrasymachus actually said. The distinction between subjective and objective interest becomes the crux.
D: Summary: Cleitophon attempts to save Thrasymachus's position by suggesting he meant justice is what the ruler believes to be his interest, not what objectively is. Polemarchus rejects this interpretation as a distortion of Thrasymachus's actual words.
D: Distilled: Cleitophon tries to rescue Thrasymachus by shifting from actual interest to perceived interest, but Polemarchus insists those were not his words.
Notes: Cleitophon, Polemarchus, and Socrates speak. -- Cleitophon's intervention introduces the crucial philosophical distinction between subjective and objective interest -- what the ruler thinks is best vs. what actually is best. -- This is the only significant speaking role Cleitophon has in the Republic, though an entire (short) Platonic dialogue bears his name. -- Polemarchus's insistence on Thrasymachus's actual words shows careful attention to the logical commitments made during dialectic. -- The debate between Cleitophon and Polemarchus mirrors a perennial problem in political theory: does a government's authority derive from its actual wisdom or merely from its claim to authority? -- Thrasymachus will resolve this dispute himself in 1.36-37 by introducing the concept of the 'true ruler' who, by definition, never errs -- a more radical position than Cleitophon's attempt at a moderate rescue.

D: Original:
Never mind, I replied, if he now says that they are, let us accept his statement. Tell me, Thrasymachus, I said, did you mean by justice what the stronger thought to be his interest, whether really so or not?

Certainly not, he said. Do you suppose that I call him who is mistaken the stronger at the time when he is mistaken?

Yes, I said, my impression was that you did so, when you admitted that the ruler was not infallible but might be sometimes mistaken.
D: Narrative:
"Never mind," I replied, "if he now says that they are, let us accept his statement. Tell me, Thrasymachus," I said, "did you mean by justice what the stronger thought to be his interest, whether really so or not?"

"Certainly not," he said. "Do you suppose that I call him who is mistaken the stronger at the time when he is mistaken?"

"Yes," I said, "my impression was that you did so, when you admitted that the ruler was not infallible but might be sometimes mistaken."
D: Refashioned:
"Never mind," I said. "If he wants to change his position now, let's go with that. Thrasymachus, did you mean that justice is whatever the stronger believes to be his interest, whether it actually is or not?"

"Absolutely not," he said. "Do you think I'd call someone 'the stronger' at the very moment he's making a mistake?"

"Yes, actually," I said. "That was my impression when you admitted that rulers aren't infallible and can sometimes be wrong."
D: Elegant: Socrates puts the question directly: did Thrasymachus mean actual or perceived interest? Thrasymachus rejects Cleitophon's rescue attempt and takes a harder line: a ruler who makes mistakes isn't truly 'the stronger' in that moment of error. Socrates notes the tension with his earlier admission of fallibility.
D: Summary: Socrates asks Thrasymachus directly whether he meant subjective or objective interest. Thrasymachus rejects the subjective reading, insisting that a ruler who errs is not truly 'the stronger' while erring -- contradicting his earlier admission that rulers can be fallible.
D: Distilled: Thrasymachus denies that a mistaken ruler counts as 'the stronger,' setting up his retreat to the idealized 'ruler in the strict sense.'
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- Thrasymachus's response rejects Cleitophon's moderate interpretation and moves toward a more extreme position: the 'true' ruler, by definition, does not err. -- This is a significant philosophical move, introducing the distinction between empirical rulers (who can be mistaken) and the ideal ruler (who is infallible qua ruler). -- Socrates will turn this distinction against Thrasymachus by applying the same 'strict sense' logic to other crafts. -- The move parallels Plato's own method of idealization: just as Thrasymachus defines the 'ruler in the strict sense,' Plato will define the 'philosopher-king' -- but with very different conclusions about whose interests such a ruler serves. -- Socrates's pointed reminder ('my impression was that you did so') highlights the contradiction between Thrasymachus's earlier concession and his current retreat.

D: Original: You argue like an informer, Socrates. Do you mean, for example, that he who is mistaken about the sick is a physician in that he is mistaken? or that he who errs in arithmetic or grammar is an arithmetician or grammarian at the me when he is making the mistake, in respect of the mistake? True, we say that the physician or arithmetician or grammarian has made a mistake, but this is only a way of speaking; for the fact is that neither the grammarian nor any other person of skill ever makes a mistake in so far as he is what his name implies; they none of them err unless their skill fails them, and then they cease to be skilled artists. No artist or sage or ruler errs at the time when he is what his name implies; though he is commonly said to err, and I adopted the common mode of speaking. But to be perfectly accurate, since you are such a lover of accuracy, we should say that the ruler, in so far as he is the ruler, is unerring, and, being unerring, always commands that which is for his own interest; and the subject is required to execute his commands; and therefore, as I said at first and now repeat, justice is the interest of the stronger.
D: Narrative: "You argue like an informer, Socrates. Do you mean, for example, that he who is mistaken about the sick is a physician in that he is mistaken? Or that he who errs in arithmetic or grammar is an arithmetician or grammarian at the time when he is making the mistake, in respect of the mistake? True, we say that the physician or arithmetician or grammarian has made a mistake, but this is only a way of speaking; for the fact is that neither the grammarian nor any other person of skill ever makes a mistake in so far as he is what his name implies; they none of them err unless their skill fails them, and then they cease to be skilled artists. No artist or sage or ruler errs at the time when he is what his name implies; though he is commonly said to err, and I adopted the common mode of speaking. But to be perfectly accurate, since you are such a lover of accuracy, we should say that the ruler, in so far as he is the ruler, is unerring, and, being unerring, always commands that which is for his own interest; and the subject is required to execute his commands; and therefore, as I said at first and now repeat, justice is the interest of the stronger."
D: Refashioned:
"You argue like a dishonest prosecutor, Socrates. Let me ask you this: when a doctor makes a wrong diagnosis, is he acting as a doctor in that moment of error? When someone makes a math mistake, are they a mathematician while making it? We loosely say 'the doctor made a mistake' or 'the mathematician got it wrong,' but that's just common speech. In reality, no skilled person ever makes a mistake insofar as they are skilled. When their skill fails, they temporarily cease to be what their title implies. No craftsman, sage, or ruler errs while truly exercising their role -- though we commonly say they do, and I was using that common language.

"But since you love precision so much, let me be perfectly precise: the ruler, insofar as he is truly a ruler, is infallible. Being infallible, he always commands what serves his own interest. And subjects must obey. Therefore, as I said from the beginning: justice is the interest of the stronger."
D: Elegant: Thrasymachus makes a sophisticated move: a doctor who misdiagnoses is not acting as a doctor in that moment. No craftsman errs insofar as he truly exercises his craft. Applying this logic, the ruler 'in the strict sense' never errs, always commands his own interest, and subjects must obey. The thesis is rescued -- but at the cost of defining the ruler as an impossible ideal.
D: Summary: Thrasymachus introduces the crucial distinction between the ruler in common speech and the ruler 'in the strict sense.' Just as a doctor who misdiagnoses is not truly practicing medicine, a ruler who errs is not truly ruling. The true ruler, by definition infallible, always commands his own interest -- so justice remains the interest of the stronger.
D: Distilled: The ruler in the strict sense never errs, so justice -- as obedience to the ruler's infallible commands -- is always the interest of the stronger.
Notes: Thrasymachus speaks to Socrates. -- The distinction between the craftsman 'in the strict sense' and the ordinary craftsman is one of Thrasymachus's most philosophically significant contributions to the dialogue. -- Ironically, this distinction -- between the ideal and the empirical -- is deeply Platonic; Thrasymachus is using proto-Platonic reasoning to defend an anti-Platonic conclusion. -- Socrates will adopt this same 'strict sense' framework and turn it against Thrasymachus: if the doctor qua doctor aims at health (not money), then the ruler qua ruler aims at the good of his subjects (not his own interest). -- The accusation of 'arguing like an informer' (sykophantēs) was a serious charge in Athens; sykophants were malicious prosecutors who brought false accusations for personal gain. -- The passage raises the philosophical problem of idealization: does defining something by its ideal form illuminate reality or merely create an unfalsifiable abstraction?

D: Original:
Indeed, Thrasymachus, and do I really appear to you to argue like an informer?

Certainly, he replied. And you suppose that I ask these questions with any design of injuring you in the argument?

Nay, he replied, 'suppose' is not the word --I know it; but you will be found out, and by sheer force of argument you will never prevail.

I shall not make the attempt, my dear man; but to avoid any misunderstanding occurring between us in future, let me ask, in what sense do you speak of a ruler or stronger whose interest, as you were saying, he being the superior, it is just that the inferior should execute --is he a ruler in the popular or in the strict sense of the term?

In the strictest of all senses, he said. And now cheat and play the informer if you can; I ask no quarter at your hands. But you never will be able, never.

And do you imagine, I said, that I am such a madman as to try and cheat, Thrasymachus? I might as well shave a lion.

Why, he said, you made the attempt a minute ago, and you failed.

Enough, I said, of these civilities. It will be better that I should ask you a question: Is the physician, taken in that strict sense of which you are speaking, a healer of the sick or a maker of money? And remember that I am now speaking of the true physician.

A healer of the sick, he replied. And the pilot --that is to say, the true pilot --is he a captain of sailors or a mere sailor?

A captain of sailors. The circumstance that he sails in the ship is not to be taken into account; neither is he to be called a sailor; the name pilot by which he is distinguished has nothing to do with sailing, but is significant of his skill and of his authority over the sailors.

Very true, he said. Now, I said, every art has an interest? Certainly. For which the art has to consider and provide? Yes, that is the aim of art. And the interest of any art is the perfection of it --this and nothing else?
D: Narrative:
"Indeed, Thrasymachus, and do I really appear to you to argue like an informer?"

"Certainly," he replied.

"And you suppose that I ask these questions with any design of injuring you in the argument?"

"Nay," he replied, "'suppose' is not the word -- I know it; but you will be found out, and by sheer force of argument you will never prevail."

"I shall not make the attempt, my dear man; but to avoid any misunderstanding occurring between us in future, let me ask, in what sense do you speak of a ruler or stronger whose interest, as you were saying, he being the superior, it is just that the inferior should execute -- is he a ruler in the popular or in the strict sense of the term?"

"In the strictest of all senses," he said. "And now cheat and play the informer if you can; I ask no quarter at your hands. But you never will be able, never."

"And do you imagine," I said, "that I am such a madman as to try and cheat Thrasymachus? I might as well shave a lion."

"Why," he said, "you made the attempt a minute ago, and you failed."

"Enough," I said, "of these civilities. It will be better that I should ask you a question: Is the physician, taken in that strict sense of which you are speaking, a healer of the sick or a maker of money? And remember that I am now speaking of the true physician."

"A healer of the sick," he replied.

"And the pilot -- that is to say, the true pilot -- is he a captain of sailors or a mere sailor?"

"A captain of sailors."

"The circumstance that he sails in the ship is not to be taken into account; neither is he to be called a sailor; the name pilot by which he is distinguished has nothing to do with sailing, but is significant of his skill and of his authority over the sailors."

"Very true," he said.

"Now," I said, "every art has an interest?"

"Certainly."

"For which the art has to consider and provide?"

"Yes, that is the aim of art."

"And the interest of any art is the perfection of it -- this and nothing else?"
D: Refashioned:
"Do I really seem to you like a dishonest prosecutor, Thrasymachus?"

"Absolutely."

"And you think I'm asking these questions just to sabotage your argument?"

"'Think' isn't the word -- I know it. But you'll be exposed, and you'll never beat me by force of argument alone."

"I wouldn't dream of trying. But to prevent future misunderstandings, let me clarify: this 'ruler' or 'stronger' whose interest defines justice -- are you using the word in its everyday sense or its strict, precise sense?"

"The strictest possible sense," he said. "Now try to trip me up if you can. I won't back down. But you'll never manage it."

"Do you think I'm crazy enough to try to outwit Thrasymachus?" I said. "I might as well try to shave a lion."

"You tried it a moment ago," he said, "and you failed."

"Enough of these pleasantries. Let me ask you something. The physician -- in the strict sense you're insisting on -- is he a healer of the sick or a money-maker? Remember, I mean the true physician."

"A healer of the sick."

"And the pilot -- the true pilot -- is he a commander of sailors or just another sailor?"

"A commander of sailors."

"The fact that he's on the ship doesn't make him a sailor. The title 'pilot' reflects his skill and his authority over the crew, not just the fact that he's aboard."

"True."

"Now: every art has a subject it serves?"

"Yes."

"And the art exists to consider and provide for that subject's interest?"

"That's its purpose."

"And the interest of any art is the perfection of its subject -- nothing else?"
D: Elegant: After an exchange of barbs -- Socrates compares outwitting Thrasymachus to shaving a lion -- Socrates accepts Thrasymachus's 'strict sense' framework and turns it against him. The true physician heals, not profits. The true pilot commands sailors through skill, not mere presence on the ship. Every art serves the interest of its subject, not of the practitioner. This will prove fatal to Thrasymachus's thesis.
D: Summary: Socrates accepts Thrasymachus's demand for strict definitions, then applies the same logic to medicine and navigation. The true physician is a healer, not a money-maker; the true pilot commands through skill, not proximity. Socrates establishes that every art serves the interest of its subject, setting up the decisive counterargument.
D: Distilled: Accepting the 'strict sense' framework, Socrates shows that the true doctor heals and the true pilot commands -- each art serves its subject, not itself.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- The proverb about 'shaving a lion' means attempting something foolish and dangerous; it suggests both Thrasymachus's ferocity and Socrates's mock-humility. -- Socrates's acceptance of the 'strict sense' framework is a masterful rhetorical judo: by adopting Thrasymachus's own tool, he will use it to reach the opposite conclusion. -- The distinction between the pilot qua pilot (commander) and the pilot qua sailor (just another person on the ship) parallels the later distinction between the philosopher-king as ruler and the philosopher-king as private person. -- The principle that 'every art has an interest' and aims at 'the perfection of its subject' is foundational to Plato's ethical theory: each art (technē) has a proper function (ergon) directed at a specific good. -- This framework will be crucial in Book IV's argument about the soul's parts, each with its proper function. -- The exchange about payment anticipates 1.48-53, where Socrates argues that the art of ruling and the art of earning are distinct.

D: Original: What do you mean? I mean what I may illustrate negatively by the example of the body. Suppose you were to ask me whether the body is self-sufficing or has wants, I should reply: Certainly the body has wants; for the body may be ill and require to be cured, and has therefore interests to which the art of medicine ministers; and this is the origin and intention of medicine, as you will acknowledge. Am I not right?
D: Narrative:
"What do you mean?"

"I mean what I may illustrate negatively by the example of the body. Suppose you were to ask me whether the body is self-sufficing or has wants, I should reply: Certainly the body has wants; for the body may be ill and require to be cured, and has therefore interests to which the art of medicine ministers; and this is the origin and intention of medicine, as you will acknowledge. Am I not right?"
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean?"

"Let me explain with an example. If you asked me whether the body is self-sufficient or has needs, I'd say: obviously it has needs. The body can get sick and need healing, so it has interests that medicine exists to serve. That's the whole origin and purpose of medicine -- you'd agree with that?"
D: Elegant: Socrates illustrates his principle: the body has wants (it can be sick), and medicine exists to serve those wants. Arts arise from the needs of their subjects, not from the needs of the practitioners.
D: Summary: Socrates explains his point through the example of the body: the body has needs (it gets sick), and medicine was invented to serve those needs. This establishes that arts exist to serve their subjects.
D: Distilled: Medicine exists because the body has needs it cannot fulfill on its own -- arts serve their subjects.
