Notes: Socrates speaks to Thrasymachus. -- The example of the body's needs is strategically chosen because Thrasymachus has already conceded that the true physician is a healer of the sick (1.38). -- The argument moves from the particular (medicine) toward the general principle that all arts serve the needs of their subjects, not of the practitioners. -- This principle will be devastating to Thrasymachus's claim that ruling serves the ruler's interest: if ruling is an art like medicine, then it must serve the ruled. -- The passage introduces the notion of 'self-sufficiency' (autarkeia), which becomes important in Aristotle's ethics and politics: what is self-sufficient needs no external art to serve it. -- The body/soul parallel is implicit: just as the body needs medicine, the soul needs justice -- and the art of ruling is to the city what medicine is to the body.

D: Original: Quite right, he replied. But is the art of medicine or any other art faulty or deficient in any quality in the same way that the eye may be deficient in sight or the ear fail of hearing, and therefore requires another art to provide for the interests of seeing and hearing --has art in itself, I say, any similar liability to fault or defect, and does every art require another supplementary art to provide for its interests, and that another and another without end? Or have the arts to look only after their own interests? Or have they no need either of themselves or of another? --having no faults or defects, they have no need to correct them, either by the exercise of their own art or of any other; they have only to consider the interest of their subject-matter. For every art remains pure and faultless while remaining true --that is to say, while perfect and unimpaired. Take the words in your precise sense, and tell me whether I am not right."
D: Narrative:
"Quite right," he replied.

"But is the art of medicine or any other art faulty or deficient in any quality in the same way that the eye may be deficient in sight or the ear fail of hearing, and therefore requires another art to provide for the interests of seeing and hearing -- has art in itself, I say, any similar liability to fault or defect, and does every art require another supplementary art to provide for its interests, and that another and another without end? Or have the arts to look only after their own interests? Or have they no need either of themselves or of another? -- having no faults or defects, they have no need to correct them, either by the exercise of their own art or of any other; they have only to consider the interest of their subject-matter. For every art remains pure and faultless while remaining true -- that is to say, while perfect and unimpaired. Take the words in your precise sense, and tell me whether I am not right."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly," he said.

"But does the art of medicine itself have deficiencies the way an eye can lack sight or an ear can fail to hear? Does medicine need some additional art to look after its interests, and then that art would need yet another, and so on forever? Or do arts only need to look after their subjects? I'd say arts have no deficiencies of their own -- they have no need to be corrected by themselves or by some other art. They exist solely to serve the interest of their subject matter. Any art, insofar as it remains true to itself -- pure, perfect, unimpaired -- considers only the good of what it governs. Using your own demand for strict precision, tell me: am I wrong?"
D: Elegant: Socrates argues that an art, insofar as it is truly an art, has no deficiencies of its own and therefore needs no supplementary art to serve its interests. Arts exist solely to serve their subjects. This principle -- using Thrasymachus's own demand for 'strict precision' -- leads to the conclusion that ruling, as an art, serves the ruled.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes that a true art has no defects requiring correction and therefore needs no supplementary art to serve its own interests. Arts exist purely to serve their subject matter. Using Thrasymachus's own insistence on strict definitions, Socrates builds toward the conclusion that ruling must serve the ruled.
D: Distilled: A true art has no deficiencies and serves only the interest of its subject -- not its own interests or those of the practitioner.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Thrasymachus. -- The argument about arts requiring supplementary arts anticipates the later separation of the art of ruling from the art of wage-earning (1.48-53). -- The claim that a true art is 'pure and faultless while remaining true' applies Thrasymachus's own 'strict sense' methodology to devastating effect. -- The potential infinite regress (art needing art needing art) is an early instance of what becomes a common philosophical technique; cf. Aristotle's argument against infinite causal chains in the Metaphysics. -- By establishing that arts serve only their subjects, Socrates has laid the logical groundwork for his decisive argument: if the ruler in the strict sense is an artist of governance, then he must serve the governed, not himself. -- The phrase 'take the words in your precise sense' throws Thrasymachus's demand for precision back at him -- a rhetorical masterstroke.

D: Original:
Yes, clearly. Then medicine does not consider the interest of medicine, but the interest of the body?

True, he said. Nor does the art of horsemanship consider the interests of the art of horsemanship, but the interests of the horse; neither do any other arts care for themselves, for they have no needs; they care only for that which is the subject of their art?

True, he said. But surely, Thrasymachus, the arts are the superiors and rulers of their own subjects?

To this he assented with a good deal of reluctance. Then, I said, no science or art considers or enjoins the interest of the stronger or superior, but only the interest of the subject and weaker?

He made an attempt to contest this proposition also, but finally acquiesced.

Then, I continued, no physician, in so far as he is a physician, considers his own good in what he prescribes, but the good of his patient; for the true physician is also a ruler having the human body as a subject, and is not a mere money-maker; that has been admitted?

Yes. And the pilot likewise, in the strict sense of the term, is a ruler of sailors and not a mere sailor?

That has been admitted. And such a pilot and ruler will provide and prescribe for the interest of the sailor who is under him, and not for his own or the ruler's interest?

He gave a reluctant 'Yes.' Then, I said, Thrasymachus, there is no one in any rule who, in so far as he is a ruler, considers or enjoins what is for his own interest, but always what is for the interest of his subject or suitable to his art; to that he looks, and that alone he considers in everything which he says and does.

When we had got to this point in the argument, and every one saw that the definition of justice had been completely upset, Thrasymachus, instead of replying to me, said: Tell me, Socrates, have you got a nurse?

Why do you ask such a question, I said, when you ought rather to be answering?

Because she leaves you to snivel, and never wipes your nose: she has not even taught you to know the shepherd from the sheep.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, clearly."

"Then medicine does not consider the interest of medicine, but the interest of the body?"

"True," he said.

"Nor does the art of horsemanship consider the interests of the art of horsemanship, but the interests of the horse; neither do any other arts care for themselves, for they have no needs; they care only for that which is the subject of their art?"

"True," he said.

"But surely, Thrasymachus, the arts are the superiors and rulers of their own subjects?"

To this he assented with a good deal of reluctance.

"Then," I said, "no science or art considers or enjoins the interest of the stronger or superior, but only the interest of the subject and weaker?"

He made an attempt to contest this proposition also, but finally acquiesced.

"Then," I continued, "no physician, in so far as he is a physician, considers his own good in what he prescribes, but the good of his patient; for the true physician is also a ruler having the human body as a subject, and is not a mere money-maker; that has been admitted?"

"Yes."

"And the pilot likewise, in the strict sense of the term, is a ruler of sailors and not a mere sailor?"

"That has been admitted."

"And such a pilot and ruler will provide and prescribe for the interest of the sailor who is under him, and not for his own or the ruler's interest?"

He gave a reluctant "Yes."

"Then," I said, "Thrasymachus, there is no one in any rule who, in so far as he is a ruler, considers or enjoins what is for his own interest, but always what is for the interest of his subject or suitable to his art; to that he looks, and that alone he considers in everything which he says and does."

When we had got to this point in the argument, and every one saw that the definition of justice had been completely upset, Thrasymachus, instead of replying to me, said, "Tell me, Socrates, have you got a nurse?"

"Why do you ask such a question," I said, "when you ought rather to be answering?"

"Because she leaves you to snivel, and never wipes your nose: she has not even taught you to know the shepherd from the sheep."
D: Refashioned:
"Clearly."

"So medicine doesn't serve the interests of medicine -- it serves the body?"

"True."

"And horsemanship doesn't serve horsemanship -- it serves the horse? No art serves itself, because no art has needs of its own. Each serves only its subject?"

"True," he said.

"But arts are the rulers and superiors of their subjects, aren't they?"

He agreed reluctantly.

"Then no art or science serves the interest of the superior -- only the interest of the subject and the weaker?"

He tried to fight this but eventually gave in.

"Then no true physician prescribes for his own benefit, only for the patient's. The real doctor rules over the body, not over a bank account. We've agreed on that?"

"Yes."

"And the true pilot governs sailors, not just sits in the boat?"

"Agreed."

"And this pilot prescribes for the sailors' benefit, not his own?"

Another reluctant "Yes."

"Then no ruler, insofar as he is truly ruling, ever considers his own interest. He considers only the interest of his subject. That's all he thinks about, in everything he says and does."

At this point everyone in the room could see that Thrasymachus's definition had been demolished. Instead of responding, he said, "Tell me, Socrates -- do you have a nurse?"

"Why are you asking that when you should be answering my question?"

"Because she lets you walk around sniveling with a runny nose. She hasn't even taught you to tell the shepherd from the sheep."
D: Elegant: Socrates completes the devastating argument: medicine serves the body, horsemanship serves the horse, and no art serves itself or its practitioner -- only its subject. Since rulers are practitioners of an art, the true ruler must serve the ruled. Thrasymachus, cornered and visibly defeated, abandons argument for personal insult, asking whether Socrates has a nurse who lets him snivel -- because he can't tell the shepherd from the sheep.
D: Summary: Socrates systematically establishes that every art serves its subject, not the practitioner. Therefore the true ruler serves the ruled. With his definition demolished, Thrasymachus abandons logical argument for a personal attack, mocking Socrates for not understanding that shepherds exploit their sheep.
D: Distilled: No art serves the stronger; every art serves the weaker -- so ruling must serve the ruled, not the ruler.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- Thrasymachus's reluctant assent throughout this passage shows the power of the elenctic method: he cannot find logical grounds to resist but is emotionally unwilling to accept the conclusion. -- The nurse insult is both crude and revealing: Thrasymachus cannot answer the argument and resorts to ad hominem attack. -- The shepherd metaphor is crucial: Thrasymachus will argue (1.42) that shepherds fatten sheep for their own profit, not the sheep's benefit -- challenging Socrates's idealized view of crafts. -- This is a genuinely strong objection and represents Thrasymachus's most philosophically interesting move. -- The image of the shepherd-ruler recurs throughout Greek political thought; cf. Homer's formula 'shepherd of the people' (poimēn laōn) for kings. -- Plato returns to the shepherd analogy in the Statesman (Politikos), where the question of whether the ruler is a shepherd of his human flock is examined at length.

D: Original: What makes you say that? I replied. Because you fancy that the shepherd or neatherd fattens of tends the sheep or oxen with a view to their own good and not to the good of himself or his master; and you further imagine that the rulers of states, if they are true rulers, never think of their subjects as sheep, and that they are not studying their own advantage day and night. Oh, no; and so entirely astray are you in your ideas about the just and unjust as not even to know that justice and the just are in reality another's good; that is to say, the interest of the ruler and stronger, and the loss of the subject and servant; and injustice the opposite; for the unjust is lord over the truly simple and just: he is the stronger, and his subjects do what is for his interest, and minister to his happiness, which is very far from being their own. Consider further, most foolish Socrates, that the just is always a loser in comparison with the unjust. First of all, in private contracts: wherever the unjust is the partner of the just you will find that, when the partnership is dissolved, the unjust man has always more and the just less. Secondly, in their dealings with the State: when there is an income tax, the just man will pay more and the unjust less on the same amount of income; and when there is anything to be received the one gains nothing and the other much. Observe also what happens when they take an office; there is the just man neglecting his affairs and perhaps suffering other losses, and getting nothing out of the public, because he is just; moreover he is hated by his friends and acquaintance for refusing to serve them in unlawful ways.
D: Narrative:
"What makes you say that?" I replied.

"Because you fancy that the shepherd or neatherd fattens or tends the sheep or oxen with a view to their own good and not to the good of himself or his master; and you further imagine that the rulers of states, if they are true rulers, never think of their subjects as sheep, and that they are not studying their own advantage day and night. Oh, no; and so entirely astray are you in your ideas about the just and unjust as not even to know that justice and the just are in reality another's good; that is to say, the interest of the ruler and stronger, and the loss of the subject and servant; and injustice the opposite; for the unjust is lord over the truly simple and just: he is the stronger, and his subjects do what is for his interest, and minister to his happiness, which is very far from being their own.

"Consider further, most foolish Socrates, that the just is always a loser in comparison with the unjust. First of all, in private contracts: wherever the unjust is the partner of the just you will find that, when the partnership is dissolved, the unjust man has always more and the just less. Secondly, in their dealings with the State: when there is an income tax, the just man will pay more and the unjust less on the same amount of income; and when there is anything to be received the one gains nothing and the other much. Observe also what happens when they take an office; there is the just man neglecting his affairs and perhaps suffering other losses, and getting nothing out of the public, because he is just; moreover he is hated by his friends and acquaintance for refusing to serve them in unlawful ways."
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean?" I asked.

"You naively believe that a shepherd fattens his sheep for the sheep's benefit, not for his own profit or his master's. And you imagine that political rulers, if they're 'true' rulers, never treat their subjects as livestock or study their own advantage day and night. You're completely wrong. Justice -- real justice -- is actually someone else's good: the ruler's interest and the subject's loss. Injustice is the opposite. The unjust man dominates the simple and just; he's the stronger one, and his subjects serve his happiness at the expense of their own.

"Think about it, you fool. The just person always loses compared to the unjust. In business partnerships, when the books are settled, the unjust partner always walks away with more and the just one with less. In dealings with the state, the just person pays more in taxes on the same income and receives fewer benefits. When they hold public office, the just person neglects his own affairs, suffers personal losses, gains nothing from the public treasury because he won't cheat -- and on top of all that, his friends and family hate him for refusing to bend the rules for them."
D: Elegant: Thrasymachus unleashes his counter-thesis with force: the shepherd fattens sheep for slaughter, not for the sheep's sake. Real rulers treat subjects the same way. Justice is always another's good -- the ruler's gain, the subject's loss. In every domain -- business, taxation, public office -- the just person loses. The just man pays more, receives less, neglects his affairs, and is despised for his integrity.
D: Summary: Thrasymachus argues that shepherds exploit sheep for profit, and rulers exploit subjects the same way. Justice is always the loss of the subject and gain of the ruler. In business, taxes, and politics, the just person consistently loses while the unjust person profits.
D: Distilled: Justice is another person's good and the just man's loss -- in every arena of life, the honest person finishes last.
Notes: Thrasymachus speaks to Socrates. -- The shepherd counter-argument is Thrasymachus's strongest philosophical contribution: it challenges Socrates's idealized craft analogy by noting that real-world practitioners often exploit their subjects. -- The claim that 'justice is another's good' (allotrion agathon) has been called the strongest formulation of the immoralist position in ancient philosophy. -- The empirical examples (business, taxes, public office) give the argument rhetorical force by grounding it in everyday experience. -- The description of the just officeholder who suffers personal loss and social hatred for refusing corruption is remarkably modern and resonates with contemporary debates about public integrity. -- Thrasymachus's position anticipates Glaucon's more refined version of the same challenge in Book II (the Ring of Gyges). -- The address 'most foolish Socrates' marks the emotional peak of Thrasymachus's aggression. -- The passage sets up the central project of the Republic: proving that justice benefits the just person, not just others.

D: Original: But all this is reversed in the case of the unjust man. I am speaking, as before, of injustice on a large scale in which the advantage of the unjust is more apparent; and my meaning will be most clearly seen if we turn to that highest form of injustice in which the criminal is the happiest of men, and the sufferers or those who refuse to do injustice are the most miserable --that is to say tyranny, which by fraud and force takes away the property of others, not little by little but wholesale; comprehending in one, things sacred as well as profane, private and public; for which acts of wrong, if he were detected perpetrating any one of them singly, he would be punished and incur great disgrace --they who do such wrong in particular cases are called robbers of temples, and man-stealers and burglars and swindlers and thieves.
D: Narrative: "But all this is reversed in the case of the unjust man. I am speaking, as before, of injustice on a large scale in which the advantage of the unjust is more apparent; and my meaning will be most clearly seen if we turn to that highest form of injustice in which the criminal is the happiest of men, and the sufferers or those who refuse to do injustice are the most miserable -- that is to say tyranny, which by fraud and force takes away the property of others, not little by little but wholesale; comprehending in one, things sacred as well as profane, private and public; for which acts of wrong, if he were detected perpetrating any one of them singly, he would be punished and incur great disgrace -- they who do such wrong in particular cases are called robbers of temples, and man-stealers and burglars and swindlers and thieves."
D: Refashioned: "But for the unjust man, everything is reversed. I'm talking about injustice on a grand scale, where the advantage is most visible. The clearest case is tyranny -- the supreme form of injustice, where the criminal is the happiest person alive and his victims the most miserable. The tyrant seizes others' property wholesale through fraud and force -- sacred and secular, private and public, all at once. If someone committed any one of these crimes individually -- temple robbery, kidnapping, burglary, fraud, theft -- he'd be punished and disgraced."
D: Elegant: Thrasymachus points to tyranny as the supreme demonstration: the tyrant commits every crime at once -- robbery, kidnapping, fraud, theft -- but on such a scale that it transforms from disgrace to glory. Small-scale injustice is punished; large-scale injustice is rewarded.
D: Summary: Thrasymachus argues that tyranny is injustice perfected: the tyrant commits every crime simultaneously and on a massive scale. Individual crimes bring punishment, but wholesale injustice through tyranny brings the greatest happiness.
D: Distilled: Tyranny is the perfection of injustice: crimes committed on a grand enough scale cease to be punished and become celebrated.
Notes: Thrasymachus speaks to Socrates. -- The claim that the tyrant is 'the happiest of men' is the thesis the entire Republic is written to refute. -- The list of crimes (temple robbery, kidnapping, burglary, fraud, theft) forms a climactic sequence leading to tyranny, which encompasses them all. -- The distinction between small-scale crime (punished) and large-scale crime (celebrated) has striking modern parallels in discussions of white-collar crime, corporate malfeasance, and political corruption. -- The observation that 'things sacred as well as profane' are seized connects to Greek religious law, where temple robbery (hierosylia) was among the most severe offenses. -- This passage directly anticipates Book IX's portrait of the tyrannical soul (571a-580a), where Plato argues that the tyrant is actually the most miserable of men, not the happiest -- the exact inversion of Thrasymachus's claim.

D: Original: But when a man besides taking away the money of the citizens has made slaves of them, then, instead of these names of reproach, he is termed happy and blessed, not only by the citizens but by all who hear of his having achieved the consummation of injustice. For mankind censure injustice, fearing that they may be the victims of it and not because they shrink from committing it. And thus, as I have shown, Socrates, injustice, when on a sufficient scale, has more strength and freedom and mastery than justice; and, as I said at first, justice is the interest of the stronger, whereas injustice is a man's own profit and interest.
D: Narrative: "But when a man besides taking away the money of the citizens has made slaves of them, then, instead of these names of reproach, he is termed happy and blessed, not only by the citizens but by all who hear of his having achieved the consummation of injustice. For mankind censure injustice, fearing that they may be the victims of it and not because they shrink from committing it. And thus, as I have shown, Socrates, injustice, when on a sufficient scale, has more strength and freedom and mastery than justice; and, as I said at first, justice is the interest of the stronger, whereas injustice is a man's own profit and interest."
D: Refashioned: "But when a man not only takes the citizens' money but enslaves them too, he's not called a thief or a criminal -- he's called 'happy' and 'blessed,' not just by his own subjects but by everyone who hears about it. People condemn injustice because they're afraid of being its victims, not because they'd actually refuse to commit it themselves. So as I've demonstrated, Socrates, injustice on a large enough scale has more power, freedom, and mastery than justice. Justice, as I said at the start, is the interest of the stronger. Injustice is a man's own true profit and interest."
D: Elegant: The tyrant who enslaves a nation is called blessed, not criminal. People condemn injustice only from fear of suffering it, not from moral conviction. Thrasymachus's conclusion: large-scale injustice surpasses justice in power, freedom, and mastery. Justice serves others; injustice alone serves oneself.
D: Summary: Thrasymachus completes his argument: the successful tyrant is admired, not condemned. People oppose injustice only from fear of being its victims. Injustice at scale has more power and freedom than justice. Justice serves the stronger; injustice serves the self.
D: Distilled: People oppose injustice only because they fear being its victims; the successful tyrant proves that large-scale injustice is more powerful and profitable than justice.
Notes: Thrasymachus speaks to Socrates. -- The claim that people condemn injustice from fear of suffering it, not from moral principle, is one of the most psychologically penetrating claims in the dialogue. -- This exact challenge is restated more carefully by Glaucon in Book II (358e-359b) using the thought experiment of the Ring of Gyges. -- The thesis that injustice has 'more strength and freedom and mastery' directly opposes the Republic's conclusion that justice constitutes the soul's proper order and therefore its maximum power. -- The word 'blessed' (makarios) is loaded: it implies divine favor, suggesting the tyrant enjoys a godlike freedom from constraint. -- Thrasymachus's dual thesis is now complete: (1) justice = the interest of the stronger (a political claim); (2) injustice = one's own profit (a personal claim). The rest of Book I and, implicitly, the entire Republic respond to these two claims. -- The passage anticipates Nietzsche's distinction between master morality and slave morality in Beyond Good and Evil and On the Genealogy of Morals.

D: Original:
Thrasymachus, when he had thus spoken, having, like a bathman, deluged our ears with his words, had a mind to go away. But the company would not let him; they insisted that he should remain and defend his position; and I myself added my own humble request that he would not leave us. Thrasymachus, I said to him, excellent man, how suggestive are your remarks! And are you going to run away before you have fairly taught or learned whether they are true or not? Is the attempt to determine the way of man's life so small a matter in your eyes --to determine how life may be passed by each one of us to the greatest advantage?

And do I differ from you, he said, as to the importance of the enquiry?
D: Narrative:
Thrasymachus, when he had thus spoken, having, like a bathman, deluged our ears with his words, had a mind to go away. But the company would not let him; they insisted that he should remain and defend his position; and I myself added my own humble request that he would not leave us.

"Thrasymachus," I said to him, "excellent man, how suggestive are your remarks! And are you going to run away before you have fairly taught or learned whether they are true or not? Is the attempt to determine the way of man's life so small a matter in your eyes -- to determine how life may be passed by each one of us to the greatest advantage?"

"And do I differ from you," he said, "as to the importance of the enquiry?"
D: Refashioned:
After this speech, Thrasymachus -- having drenched our ears with words like a man dumping a bucket of bathwater -- prepared to leave. But the group wouldn't let him. They insisted he stay and defend his position, and I added my own request.

"Thrasymachus," I said, "those are extraordinary claims. Are you really going to walk away before we've determined whether they're true? Is the question of how to live -- how each of us should spend our lives for the greatest good -- really so trivial to you?"

"Do you think I consider it unimportant?" he replied.
D: Elegant: Thrasymachus tries to leave after his speech, but the group detains him. Socrates raises the stakes: the question at hand is nothing less than how to live. Thrasymachus agrees it matters -- and thereby commits himself to staying for the examination.
D: Summary: After his impassioned speech, Thrasymachus tries to leave, but the group forces him to stay. Socrates elevates the discussion by framing it as the most important question: how should one live? Thrasymachus agrees on its importance.
D: Distilled: Thrasymachus tries to exit after his monologue, but Socrates detains him with the argument that the question of how to live is too important to abandon.
Notes: Socrates narrates and speaks to Thrasymachus. -- The 'bathman' simile compares Thrasymachus's speech to having a bucket of water poured over one's head -- overwhelming but not necessarily cleansing. -- Thrasymachus's attempt to leave after a monologue (rather than staying for dialogue) characterizes the sophistic approach: deliver a performance and depart, versus Socratic dialectic which requires sustained mutual examination. -- Socrates's question 'Is the attempt to determine the way of man's life so small a matter?' states the Republic's fundamental theme. -- The phrase 'how life may be passed by each one of us to the greatest advantage' frames ethics as the practical question of how to live well -- the same question Aristotle opens the Nicomachean Ethics with. -- By getting Thrasymachus to agree on the importance of the inquiry, Socrates secures his participation for the remainder of Book I.

D: Original:
You appear rather, I replied, to have no care or thought about us, Thrasymachus --whether we live better or worse from not knowing what you say you know, is to you a matter of indifference. Prithee, friend, do not keep your knowledge to yourself; we are a large party; and any benefit which you confer upon us will be amply rewarded. For my own part I openly declare that I am not convinced, and that I do not believe injustice to be more gainful than justice, even if uncontrolled and allowed to have free play. For, granting that there may be an unjust man who is able to commit injustice either by fraud or force, still this does not convince me of the superior advantage of injustice, and there may be others who are in the same predicament with myself. Perhaps we may be wrong; if so, you in your wisdom should convince us that we are mistaken in preferring justice to injustice.

And how am I to convince you, he said, if you are not already convinced by what I have just said; what more can I do for you? Would you have me put the proof bodily into your souls?
D: Narrative:
"You appear rather," I replied, "to have no care or thought about us, Thrasymachus -- whether we live better or worse from not knowing what you say you know, is to you a matter of indifference. Prithee, friend, do not keep your knowledge to yourself; we are a large party; and any benefit which you confer upon us will be amply rewarded. For my own part I openly declare that I am not convinced, and that I do not believe injustice to be more gainful than justice, even if uncontrolled and allowed to have free play. For, granting that there may be an unjust man who is able to commit injustice either by fraud or force, still this does not convince me of the superior advantage of injustice, and there may be others who are in the same predicament with myself. Perhaps we may be wrong; if so, you in your wisdom should convince us that we are mistaken in preferring justice to injustice."

"And how am I to convince you," he said, "if you are not already convinced by what I have just said; what more can I do for you? Would you have me put the proof bodily into your souls?"
D: Refashioned:
"On the contrary," I replied, "you seem completely indifferent to whether we live well or badly because of what you claim to know. Please, friend, don't hoard your knowledge -- there are many of us here, and we'll make it worth your while. For my part, I'll say openly that I'm not convinced. I don't believe injustice is more profitable than justice, even when it goes entirely unchecked. Grant that there exists an unjust man who can get away with anything through fraud or force -- that still doesn't prove injustice is better. And I suspect others here feel the same way. If we're wrong, then you, in your wisdom, should be able to show us why preferring justice to injustice is a mistake."

"How am I supposed to convince you," he said, "if my speech didn't do it? What else can I do -- physically insert the proof into your souls?"
D: Elegant: Socrates openly declares himself unconvinced that injustice beats justice, even when unchecked. He challenges Thrasymachus: if you truly have knowledge, share it and persuade us. Thrasymachus, frustrated, asks what more he can do if his speech was not enough -- should he implant the proof directly into their souls?
D: Summary: Socrates publicly states he is not persuaded that injustice is more profitable than justice, even without constraints. He challenges Thrasymachus to convince them rationally. Thrasymachus, exasperated, asks how he can convince those who resist his arguments.
D: Distilled: Socrates openly declares he prefers justice to injustice and challenges Thrasymachus to prove him wrong through argument, not assertion.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- Socrates's open declaration that he is 'not convinced' is significant: it states the moral position the Republic will spend nine books defending. -- The phrase 'even if uncontrolled and allowed to have free play' refers to the unchecked unjust man, whose case Glaucon will later develop with the Ring of Gyges (Book II). -- Thrasymachus's frustrated question about putting proof 'bodily into souls' ironically describes exactly what Plato believes education should do: transform the soul through dialectic. -- The contrast between Thrasymachus's rhetorical monologue and Socrates's demand for dialectical examination highlights the fundamental difference between sophistic and philosophical approaches to truth. -- Socrates's willingness to admit he might be wrong ('perhaps we may be wrong') exemplifies intellectual humility and openness to refutation -- the opposite of Thrasymachus's dogmatic certainty.

D: Original: Heaven forbid! I said; I would only ask you to be consistent; or, if you change, change openly and let there be no deception. For I must remark, Thrasymachus, if you will recall what was previously said, that although you began by defining the true physician in an exact sense, you did not observe a like exactness when speaking of the shepherd; you thought that the shepherd as a shepherd tends the sheep not with a view to their own good, but like a mere diner or banqueter with a view to the pleasures of the table; or, again, as a trader for sale in the market, and not as a shepherd. Yet surely the art of the shepherd is concerned only with the good of his subjects; he has only to provide the best for them, since the perfection of the art is already ensured whenever all the requirements of it are satisfied. And that was what I was saying just now about the ruler. I conceived that the art of the ruler, considered as ruler, whether in a state or in private life, could only regard the good of his flock or subjects; whereas you seem to think that the rulers in states, that is to say, the true rulers, like being in authority.
D: Narrative: "Heaven forbid!" I said. "I would only ask you to be consistent; or, if you change, change openly and let there be no deception. For I must remark, Thrasymachus, if you will recall what was previously said, that although you began by defining the true physician in an exact sense, you did not observe a like exactness when speaking of the shepherd; you thought that the shepherd as a shepherd tends the sheep not with a view to their own good, but like a mere diner or banqueter with a view to the pleasures of the table; or, again, as a trader for sale in the market, and not as a shepherd. Yet surely the art of the shepherd is concerned only with the good of his subjects; he has only to provide the best for them, since the perfection of the art is already ensured whenever all the requirements of it are satisfied. And that was what I was saying just now about the ruler. I conceived that the art of the ruler, considered as ruler, whether in a state or in private life, could only regard the good of his flock or subjects; whereas you seem to think that the rulers in states, that is to say, the true rulers, like being in authority."
D: Refashioned:
"God forbid!" I said. "All I ask is that you be consistent. If you change your position, do it openly -- no deception. Because here's the problem, Thrasymachus. You started by defining the true physician in the strict sense, but you didn't apply the same strict standard to the shepherd. You described the shepherd not as a shepherd but as a dinner guest eyeing the meat -- or as a livestock trader looking at market prices. But the art of shepherding, strictly speaking, is concerned only with the welfare of the sheep. The shepherd's job is to provide the best for his flock, and the art is perfected when those needs are fully met.

"That's exactly what I've been saying about rulers. The art of ruling, strictly conceived, can only be about the good of the subjects -- whether in a state or in private life. But you seem to think true rulers actually enjoy holding power for its own sake."
D: Elegant: Socrates catches Thrasymachus in an inconsistency: he defined the physician strictly (as a healer) but the shepherd loosely (as a profiteer). The true shepherd, like the true physician, serves his subjects' good. The true ruler must do the same. Thrasymachus's error is treating the shepherd as a diner at the table rather than as a practitioner of a craft.
D: Summary: Socrates points out that Thrasymachus applied strict definitions to the physician but not the shepherd. The true shepherd, strictly defined, serves the sheep's welfare -- just as the true ruler serves the subjects' good. Thrasymachus inconsistently treats ruling as self-enrichment rather than a craft of service.
D: Distilled: Thrasymachus defined the doctor strictly but the shepherd loosely -- applying the same strict standard to both shows that ruling, like shepherding, must serve the flock.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Thrasymachus. -- This is a crucial moment where Socrates identifies the logical inconsistency in Thrasymachus's argument: he used 'strict sense' reasoning when it helped him (the infallible ruler) but abandoned it when it didn't (the exploitative shepherd). -- The distinction between the shepherd-as-shepherd and the shepherd-as-businessman anticipates the Republic's later separation of the ruling art from the money-making art (1.48-53). -- The shepherd metaphor is revisited in the Statesman (Politikos 267d-268d), where the Eleatic Stranger argues that the political art is not exactly like shepherding because humans are not animals to be herded. -- Socrates's insistence on consistency ('if you change, change openly') articulates a fundamental norm of dialectic: positions must be held sincerely and revisions acknowledged transparently. -- The claim that rulers 'like being in authority' sets up the next argument about why good people must be compelled or incentivized to rule.

D: Original:
Think! Nay, I am sure of it. Then why in the case of lesser offices do men never take them willingly without payment, unless under the idea that they govern for the advantage not of themselves but of others? Let me ask you a question: Are not the several arts different, by reason of their each having a separate function? And, my dear illustrious friend, do say what you think, that we may make a little progress.

Yes, that is the difference, he replied. And each art gives us a particular good and not merely a general one --medicine, for example, gives us health; navigation, safety at sea, and so on?
D: Narrative:
"Think! Nay, I am sure of it."

"Then why in the case of lesser offices do men never take them willingly without payment, unless under the idea that they govern for the advantage not of themselves but of others? Let me ask you a question: Are not the several arts different, by reason of their each having a separate function? And, my dear illustrious friend, do say what you think, that we may make a little progress."

"Yes, that is the difference," he replied.

"And each art gives us a particular good and not merely a general one -- medicine, for example, gives us health; navigation, safety at sea, and so on?"
D: Refashioned:
"Think? No -- I'm certain of it."

"Then explain this: why do people in minor offices refuse to serve without payment? It must be because they understand that governing benefits others, not themselves. But let me ask you a question -- and please, actually answer, so we can make progress: aren't the various arts different from each other because each has its own distinct function?"

"Yes, that's what distinguishes them."

"And each art produces a specific good, not just a general one -- medicine gives health, navigation gives safety at sea, and so on?"
D: Elegant: Socrates introduces a key observation: people in public office demand payment, proving they understand that ruling benefits others, not themselves. He then begins distinguishing arts by their specific functions and products -- medicine gives health, navigation gives safety -- to separate the art of ruling from the art of earning.
D: Summary: Socrates asks why officeholders demand payment if ruling supposedly serves themselves. This proves they know ruling benefits others. He then establishes that each art has its own distinct function and product, laying the groundwork for separating ruling from money-making.
D: Distilled: The demand for payment in public office proves that ruling serves others, not the ruler -- each art has its own distinct function.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- The argument from the demand for payment is ingenious: if ruling were naturally profitable to the ruler, no one would need to be paid for it. -- This empirical observation grounds the philosophical argument in practical reality. -- The enumeration of arts and their specific goods (health from medicine, safety from navigation) continues the craft analogy. -- The distinction between arts by their functions anticipates the Republic's functional analysis of the soul in Book IV, where each part has its own proper role. -- 'My dear illustrious friend' is ironic courtesy directed at the increasingly frustrated Thrasymachus. -- The passage sets up the crucial distinction between the art of ruling and the art of wage-earning (developed in 1.49-53), which will be one of Socrates's most important arguments in Book I.

D: Original: Yes, he said. And the art of payment has the special function of giving pay: but we do not confuse this with other arts, any more than the art of the pilot is to be confused with the art of medicine, because the health of the pilot may be improved by a sea voyage. You would not be inclined to say, would you, that navigation is the art of medicine, at least if we are to adopt your exact use of language?
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said.

"And the art of payment has the special function of giving pay: but we do not confuse this with other arts, any more than the art of the pilot is to be confused with the art of medicine, because the health of the pilot may be improved by a sea voyage. You would not be inclined to say, would you, that navigation is the art of medicine, at least if we are to adopt your exact use of language?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," he said.

"Now, the art of earning payment has its own special function: giving pay. But we don't confuse it with other arts. We wouldn't say that navigation is medicine just because a sea voyage might improve the pilot's health. At least, not if we're using your standard of precise language."
D: Elegant: Socrates introduces the art of payment as a separate, distinct craft. A pilot's health may improve at sea, but that doesn't make navigation a form of medicine. Similarly, a ruler may earn wages, but that doesn't make ruling a form of money-making.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes that earning payment is a distinct art from whatever craft a person practices. Just as a pilot's improved health at sea doesn't make navigation into medicine, a ruler's wages don't make ruling into money-making.
D: Distilled: The art of payment is distinct from every other art -- a pilot's health improving at sea does not make navigation into medicine.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Thrasymachus. -- The separation of the art of payment from other arts is a key philosophical move: it means that any benefit a practitioner receives as payment comes from a different art than the one they practice. -- The pilot/medicine example is carefully chosen to make the distinction vivid and intuitive. -- The phrase 'your exact use of language' throws Thrasymachus's demand for precision back at him yet again. -- This argument has important implications for political philosophy: if the art of ruling is distinct from the art of earning, then a true ruler governs not for profit but for the governed's benefit. -- The passage anticipates the Republic's later distinction between the philosopher's contemplative activity (which is its own reward) and the practical necessity of governing (which must be incentivized).

D: Original:
Certainly not. Or because a man is in good health when he receives pay you would not say that the art of payment is medicine?

I should say not. Nor would you say that medicine is the art of receiving pay because a man takes fees when he is engaged in healing?

Certainly not. And we have admitted, I said, that the good of each art is specially confined to the art?

Yes. Then, if there be any good which all artists have in common, that is to be attributed to something of which they all have the common use?

True, he replied. And when the artist is benefited by receiving pay the advantage is gained by an additional use of the art of pay, which is not the art professed by him?
D: Narrative:
"Certainly not."

"Or because a man is in good health when he receives pay you would not say that the art of payment is medicine?"

"I should say not."

"Nor would you say that medicine is the art of receiving pay because a man takes fees when he is engaged in healing?"

"Certainly not."

"And we have admitted," I said, "that the good of each art is specially confined to the art?"

"Yes."

"Then, if there be any good which all artists have in common, that is to be attributed to something of which they all have the common use?"

"True," he replied.

"And when the artist is benefited by receiving pay the advantage is gained by an additional use of the art of pay, which is not the art professed by him?"
D: Refashioned:
"Of course not."

"And you wouldn't call the art of payment 'medicine' just because someone happens to be healthy when they receive their wages?"

"No."

"And you wouldn't say medicine is the art of getting paid just because a doctor collects fees while healing?"

"Certainly not."

"We've agreed that each art produces its own specific good?"

"Yes."

"So if there's some benefit that all practitioners share in common, it must come from something separate that they all use in common?"

"True."

"And when a practitioner benefits by receiving pay, that advantage comes from the additional art of earning wages -- which is not the art he actually practices?"
D: Elegant: Through careful elimination, Socrates establishes that payment is always an additional, separate art. A doctor's fee comes from the art of earning, not from medicine. The benefit all practitioners share -- wages -- belongs to the wage-earning art, not to their respective crafts.
D: Summary: Socrates proves through examples that receiving pay is always attributable to the separate art of wage-earning, not to whatever craft a person practices. Medicine produces health; the wage that comes with it belongs to a different art entirely.
D: Distilled: Payment comes from the art of earning, which is distinct from every other art -- the doctor's fee is not a product of medicine.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- The distinction between a craft's proper product and the incidental benefit of payment is crucial to Socrates's refutation of Thrasymachus: it means that the ruler's compensation (money, honor, or avoidance of penalty) is extrinsic to the art of ruling itself. -- This argument has striking parallels to modern debates about intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation in psychology and management theory. -- The logical structure is elegant: each art has one specific good; all practitioners share the good of payment; therefore payment belongs to a universal supplementary art, not to any particular art. -- This prepares the ground for 1.53's conclusion that rulers must be paid separately because ruling itself benefits only the ruled.

D: Original: He gave a reluctant assent to this. Then the pay is not derived by the several artists from their respective arts. But the truth is, that while the art of medicine gives health, and the art of the builder builds a house, another art attends them which is the art of pay. The various arts may be doing their own business and benefiting that over which they preside, but would the artist receive any benefit from his art unless he were paid as well?
D: Narrative:
He gave a reluctant assent to this.

"Then the pay is not derived by the several artists from their respective arts. But the truth is, that while the art of medicine gives health, and the art of the builder builds a house, another art attends them which is the art of pay. The various arts may be doing their own business and benefiting that over which they preside, but would the artist receive any benefit from his art unless he were paid as well?"
D: Refashioned:
He reluctantly agreed.

"So practitioners don't get their pay from their own arts. The reality is: medicine produces health, building produces houses, and a separate art -- the art of earning wages -- accompanies each of them. Each art does its own job and benefits its subject, but the practitioner personally benefits only through the additional art of payment. Would any practitioner gain anything from practicing their art alone, without being paid?"
D: Elegant: Thrasymachus reluctantly concedes the point. Each art produces its own specific good -- health, houses -- while the practitioner's personal benefit comes only from the separate art of wage-earning. The art itself benefits only its subject.
D: Summary: Socrates secures the conclusion: each art benefits its subject, while the practitioner benefits only through the separate art of wage-earning. Medicine produces health for the patient; the doctor's income comes from an entirely different art.
D: Distilled: Arts benefit their subjects; practitioners benefit only through the separate art of earning wages.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Thrasymachus. -- The 'reluctant assent' motif recurs throughout Thrasymachus's engagement with Socrates, showing intellectual defeat overcoming emotional resistance. -- The tripartite structure (medicine/health, building/houses, pay/wages) makes the argument crystalline. -- The question at the end is rhetorical: Socrates is driving toward the conclusion that ruling, like any art, benefits its subject (the governed), while the ruler's personal benefit must come from elsewhere. -- This distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic goods of a practice has been influential in virtue ethics, notably in Alasdair MacIntyre's After Virtue.

D: Original:
I suppose not. But does he therefore confer no benefit when he works for nothing?

Certainly, he confers a benefit. Then now, Thrasymachus, there is no longer any doubt that neither arts nor governments provide for their own interests; but, as we were before saying, they rule and provide for the interests of their subjects who are the weaker and not the stronger --to their good they attend and not to the good of the superior.
D: Narrative:
"I suppose not."

"But does he therefore confer no benefit when he works for nothing?"

"Certainly, he confers a benefit."

"Then now, Thrasymachus, there is no longer any doubt that neither arts nor governments provide for their own interests; but, as we were before saying, they rule and provide for the interests of their subjects who are the weaker and not the stronger -- to their good they attend and not to the good of the superior."
D: Refashioned:
"I suppose not."

"But does that mean a practitioner provides no benefit when working for free?"

"Of course he still provides a benefit."

"Then there's no longer any doubt, Thrasymachus. Neither arts nor governments serve their own interests. As we established earlier, they serve and provide for their subjects -- the weaker, not the stronger. They attend to the good of the governed, not the good of the governor."
D: Elegant: The clinching argument: even unpaid, an art still benefits its subject. This proves that the art's purpose is service, not self-enrichment. Therefore neither arts nor governments serve the stronger -- they serve the weaker.
D: Summary: Socrates delivers the decisive conclusion: since a practitioner still benefits his subject even when working for free, the purpose of any art -- including governance -- is to serve the weaker, not the stronger.
D: Distilled: An art benefits its subject even without payment, proving that arts and governments exist to serve the governed, not the governor.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- The argument that even unpaid work benefits the subject is the knockout blow to Thrasymachus's thesis that justice serves the stronger. -- The reasoning: if payment is what benefits the practitioner, and the art still works without payment, then the art's function is entirely about the subject's good. -- This conclusion directly contradicts Thrasymachus's central claim and completes the first phase of Socrates's refutation. -- The distinction between ruler-as-ruler and ruler-as-earner will be formalized in the next passage (1.53). -- The principle that governance is service anticipates the Republic's philosopher-king, who rules not for personal benefit but because justice demands it.

D: Original: And this is the reason, my dear Thrasymachus, why, as I was just now saying, no one is willing to govern; because no one likes to take in hand the reformation of evils which are not his concern without remuneration. For, in the execution of his work, and in giving his orders to another, the true artist does not regard his own interest, but always that of his subjects; and therefore in order that rulers may be willing to rule, they must be paid in one of three modes of payment: money, or honour, or a penalty for refusing.
D: Narrative: "And this is the reason, my dear Thrasymachus, why, as I was just now saying, no one is willing to govern; because no one likes to take in hand the reformation of evils which are not his concern without remuneration. For, in the execution of his work, and in giving his orders to another, the true artist does not regard his own interest, but always that of his subjects; and therefore in order that rulers may be willing to rule, they must be paid in one of three modes of payment: money, or honour, or a penalty for refusing."
D: Refashioned: "And this, Thrasymachus, is exactly why no one willingly takes on the burden of governing. Nobody wants to fix other people's problems without compensation. The true practitioner, in everything he does and orders, considers only his subjects' good, never his own. That's why rulers need to be paid in one of three ways: money, honor, or a penalty for refusing to serve."
D: Elegant: Socrates explains why no one rules willingly: the true ruler serves others' interests, not his own. To induce people to govern, they must receive money, honor, or face a penalty for refusing. The need for external incentives proves that ruling is self-sacrificial, not self-serving.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that the reluctance to govern proves ruling serves others. True rulers must be compensated through money, honor, or the threat of punishment for refusing, since governance itself offers the ruler nothing.
D: Distilled: No one rules willingly because ruling serves the governed; rulers must be paid, honored, or penalized into service.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Thrasymachus. -- The three modes of payment (money, honor, penalty) form one of the most cited passages in the Republic's political philosophy. -- The third mode -- penalty for refusing -- is the most novel and will be developed in 1.55 as the key incentive for the best rulers. -- The argument parallels the Republic's later claim that philosophers must be compelled to return to the cave and govern (Book VII, 519d-520e). -- The principle that governing is burdensome rather than desirable inverts common assumptions about political ambition and anticipates the Republic's distrust of those who seek power. -- The passage connects to the broader Platonic theme that the best rulers are those who least want to rule -- a principle with echoes in the Taoist concept of wu wei and in modern arguments about the paradox of political leadership.

D: Original:
What do you mean, Socrates? said Glaucon. The first two modes of payment are intelligible enough, but what the penalty is I do not understand, or how a penalty can be a payment.

You mean that you do not understand the nature of this payment which to the best men is the great inducement to rule? Of course you know that ambition and avarice are held to be, as indeed they are, a disgrace?
D: Narrative:
"What do you mean, Socrates?" said Glaucon. "The first two modes of payment are intelligible enough, but what the penalty is I do not understand, or how a penalty can be a payment."

"You mean that you do not understand the nature of this payment which to the best men is the great inducement to rule? Of course you know that ambition and avarice are held to be, as indeed they are, a disgrace?"
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean, Socrates?" asked Glaucon. "I understand money and honor as payments, but how can a penalty be a form of payment?"

"You don't understand the kind of payment that motivates the best people to rule? You know, of course, that ambition and greed are considered disgraceful -- and rightly so?"
D: Elegant: Glaucon asks how a penalty can count as payment for ruling. Socrates begins to explain: the best people are neither greedy nor ambitious, so neither money nor honor motivates them. Something else must compel them to govern.
D: Summary: Glaucon is puzzled by how a penalty can serve as payment for governing. Socrates begins his explanation by noting that the best people are indifferent to money and honor, since both ambition and avarice are disgraceful.
D: Distilled: The best rulers are moved neither by money nor by honor -- so what compels them to govern?
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speak. -- Glaucon's question provides the dramatic occasion for one of the Republic's most important political insights. -- The characterization of ambition and avarice as 'disgraceful' reflects the aristocratic Athenian value system, but Socrates gives it philosophical grounding. -- The conversation shifts from Thrasymachus to Glaucon, signaling that the discussion is moving toward the more constructive phase. -- Glaucon's engagement here foreshadows his major role in Book II, where he will demand a more rigorous defense of justice. -- The idea that the best people are indifferent to conventional incentives connects to the philosopher-king concept: wisdom-lovers rule not for reward but from necessity.

D: Original: Very true. And for this reason, I said, money and honour have no attraction for them; good men do not wish to be openly demanding payment for governing and so to get the name of hirelings, nor by secretly helping themselves out of the public revenues to get the name of thieves. And not being ambitious they do not care about honour. Wherefore necessity must be laid upon them, and they must be induced to serve from the fear of punishment. And this, as I imagine, is the reason why the forwardness to take office, instead of waiting to be compelled, has been deemed dishonourable. Now the worst part of the punishment is that he who refuses to rule is liable to be ruled by one who is worse than himself. And the fear of this, as I conceive, induces the good to take office, not because they would, but because they cannot help --not under the idea that they are going to have any benefit or enjoyment themselves, but as a necessity, and because they are not able to commit the task of ruling to any one who is better than themselves, or indeed as good. For there is reason to think that if a city were composed entirely of good men, then to avoid office would be as much an object of contention as to obtain office is at present; then we should have plain proof that the true ruler is not meant by nature to regard his own interest, but that of his subjects; and every one who knew this would choose rather to receive a benefit from another than to have the trouble of conferring one. So far am I from agreeing with Thrasymachus that justice is the interest of the stronger. This latter question need not be further discussed at present; but when Thrasymachus says that the life of the unjust is more advantageous than that of the just, his new statement appears to me to be of a far more serious character. Which of us has spoken truly? And which sort of life, Glaucon, do you prefer?
D: Narrative:
"Very true."

"And for this reason," I said, "money and honour have no attraction for them; good men do not wish to be openly demanding payment for governing and so to get the name of hirelings, nor by secretly helping themselves out of the public revenues to get the name of thieves. And not being ambitious they do not care about honour. Wherefore necessity must be laid upon them, and they must be induced to serve from the fear of punishment. And this, as I imagine, is the reason why the forwardness to take office, instead of waiting to be compelled, has been deemed dishonourable.

"Now the worst part of the punishment is that he who refuses to rule is liable to be ruled by one who is worse than himself. And the fear of this, as I conceive, induces the good to take office, not because they would, but because they cannot help -- not under the idea that they are going to have any benefit or enjoyment themselves, but as a necessity, and because they are not able to commit the task of ruling to any one who is better than themselves, or indeed as good.

"For there is reason to think that if a city were composed entirely of good men, then to avoid office would be as much an object of contention as to obtain office is at present; then we should have plain proof that the true ruler is not meant by nature to regard his own interest, but that of his subjects; and every one who knew this would choose rather to receive a benefit from another than to have the trouble of conferring one. So far am I from agreeing with Thrasymachus that justice is the interest of the stronger.

"This latter question need not be further discussed at present; but when Thrasymachus says that the life of the unjust is more advantageous than that of the just, his new statement appears to me to be of a far more serious character. Which of us has spoken truly? And which sort of life, Glaucon, do you prefer?"
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly."

"That's why money and honor don't attract good people. They don't want to be seen as mercenaries who demand payment for governing, or as thieves who secretly skim public funds. And since they're not ambitious, honor doesn't motivate them either. So they have to be compelled -- driven by the fear of punishment. That's why eagerness for office has always been considered shameful: the honorable path is to serve only when compelled.

"And here's the key: the greatest punishment for refusing to rule is being governed by someone worse than yourself. This fear, I believe, is what drives good people to take office -- not out of desire or expectation of personal gain, but out of sheer necessity, because they can't find anyone better or even equal to handle the job.

"In fact, if a city were composed entirely of good people, they'd compete to avoid office the way people now compete to hold it. That would prove beyond doubt that the true ruler doesn't serve his own interest but his subjects'. Everyone would rather receive good governance than bear the burden of providing it.

"So I could not disagree more with Thrasymachus's claim that justice is the interest of the stronger. But let's set that aside for now. His claim that the unjust life is more advantageous than the just life is a far more serious challenge. Who is right? Which sort of life do you prefer, Glaucon?"
D: Elegant: Good people shun money and honor, so only one incentive compels them to rule: the penalty of being governed by someone worse. In an ideal city, people would compete to avoid office. This proves the true ruler serves subjects, not self. Socrates firmly rejects Thrasymachus's first thesis but identifies the second -- that injustice is more advantageous than justice -- as the more dangerous claim requiring further examination.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that the best rulers serve only because the alternative -- being ruled by someone worse -- is intolerable. In a city of good people, everyone would avoid office. This proves ruling is service, not self-interest. Socrates declares the first thesis refuted and turns to the more serious second claim: that the unjust life is better than the just.
D: Distilled: Good people rule only to avoid being governed by worse people -- proving that true ruling is reluctant service, not self-interested power.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Glaucon. -- The argument that good people rule to avoid being ruled by inferiors is one of the most celebrated passages in the Republic and directly anticipates the philosopher-king doctrine (Books V-VII). -- The thought experiment of a city of good people competing to avoid office is a philosophical inversion of real political competition that illuminates the true nature of ruling. -- The transition from Thrasymachus's first thesis (justice = interest of the stronger) to his second (injustice is more profitable than justice) marks the structural pivot of Book I. -- The question to Glaucon ('which sort of life do you prefer?') foreshadows Glaucon's pivotal role in Book II, where he will demand a more rigorous defense. -- The principle that eagerness for office is dishonorable has deep roots in Roman as well as Greek republican thought; cf. the Roman ideal of Cincinnatus leaving his plow to serve. -- This passage influenced modern thinkers from Machiavelli to John Rawls on the problem of political motivation.

D: Original:
I for my part deem the life of the just to be the more advantageous, he answered.

Did you hear all the advantages of the unjust which Thrasymachus was rehearsing?

Yes, I heard him, he replied, but he has not convinced me.

Then shall we try to find some way of convincing him, if we can, that he is saying what is not true?
D: Narrative:
"I for my part deem the life of the just to be the more advantageous," he answered.

"Did you hear all the advantages of the unjust which Thrasymachus was rehearsing?"

"Yes, I heard him," he replied, "but he has not convinced me."

"Then shall we try to find some way of convincing him, if we can, that he is saying what is not true?"
D: Refashioned:
"Personally, I think the just life is more advantageous," Glaucon answered.

"Did you hear all the advantages of injustice that Thrasymachus listed?"

"I heard every word," he said, "but he hasn't convinced me."

"Then should we try to find a way to show him he's wrong?"
D: Elegant: Glaucon declares for the just life, unpersuaded by Thrasymachus's case for injustice. Socrates proposes they attempt to prove Thrasymachus wrong.
D: Summary: Glaucon sides with justice over injustice despite Thrasymachus's arguments. Socrates proposes they work together to refute Thrasymachus's claim that the unjust life is more advantageous.
D: Distilled: Glaucon is unconvinced by Thrasymachus and sides with justice; Socrates proposes to prove Thrasymachus wrong.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speak. -- Glaucon's declaration for justice is sincere but, as he will reveal in Book II, intellectually unsatisfied: he wants a deeper argument, not just the refutation of Thrasymachus. -- The question 'shall we try to find some way of convincing him?' frames the enterprise as cooperative persuasion rather than adversarial debate. -- Glaucon's response that he 'heard but was not convinced' shows independent judgment, foreshadowing his role as demanding interlocutor in the constructive books.

D: Original:
Most certainly, he replied. If, I said, he makes a set speech and we make another recounting all the advantages of being just, and he answers and we rejoin, there must be a numbering and measuring of the goods which are claimed on either side, and in the end we shall want judges to decide; but if we proceed in our enquiry as we lately did, by making admissions to one another, we shall unite the offices of judge and advocate in our own persons.

Very good, he said. And which method do I understand you to prefer? I said. That which you propose. Well, then, Thrasymachus, I said, suppose you begin at the beginning and answer me. You say that perfect injustice is more gainful than perfect justice?
D: Narrative:
"Most certainly," he replied.

"If," I said, "he makes a set speech and we make another recounting all the advantages of being just, and he answers and we rejoin, there must be a numbering and measuring of the goods which are claimed on either side, and in the end we shall want judges to decide; but if we proceed in our enquiry as we lately did, by making admissions to one another, we shall unite the offices of judge and advocate in our own persons."

"Very good," he said.

"And which method do I understand you to prefer?" I said.

"That which you propose."

"Well, then, Thrasymachus," I said, "suppose you begin at the beginning and answer me. You say that perfect injustice is more gainful than perfect justice?"
D: Refashioned:
"Absolutely," he said.

"Here's the thing," I said. "If we just trade speeches -- him listing the advantages of injustice, us listing the advantages of justice, back and forth -- we'll end up counting and measuring goods on both sides and needing external judges to decide. But if we proceed through dialogue, making concessions to each other as we go, we become both judge and advocate at once."

"That sounds right."

"Which method do you prefer?"

"The one you're proposing -- dialogue."

"Very well. Thrasymachus, let's start from the top. You say that complete injustice is more profitable than complete justice?"
D: Elegant: Socrates distinguishes two methods: competing speeches requiring judges, or dialectical inquiry where both parties serve as judges. Glaucon prefers dialectic. Socrates turns to Thrasymachus and restates the question at its starkest: is perfect injustice more profitable than perfect justice?
D: Summary: Socrates contrasts rhetorical debate (competing speeches, external judges) with dialectical inquiry (mutual concessions, participants as their own judges). Glaucon prefers dialectic. Socrates then directly asks Thrasymachus whether perfect injustice beats perfect justice.
D: Distilled: Dialectic -- mutual inquiry through shared concessions -- is preferred over competing speeches, and the question is restated: is perfect injustice more profitable than perfect justice?
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speak; Socrates addresses Thrasymachus. -- The contrast between set speeches and dialectical inquiry captures the fundamental difference between rhetoric and philosophy for Plato. -- Rhetoric produces competing narratives judged by external authority; dialectic produces shared understanding through mutual examination. -- This methodological passage is significant because it articulates the Republic's preferred mode of philosophical investigation. -- The phrase 'unite the offices of judge and advocate in our own persons' describes the ideal of rational autonomy: each participant is both presenter and evaluator of arguments. -- The restatement of the question as 'perfect injustice vs. perfect justice' clarifies the scope: this is not about petty dishonesty but about the fundamental orientation of a life.

D: Original:
Yes, that is what I say, and I have given you my reasons.

And what is your view about them? Would you call one of them virtue and the other vice?

Certainly. I suppose that you would call justice virtue and injustice vice?

What a charming notion! So likely too, seeing that I affirm injustice to be profitable and justice not.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, that is what I say, and I have given you my reasons."

"And what is your view about them? Would you call one of them virtue and the other vice?"

"Certainly."

"I suppose that you would call justice virtue and injustice vice?"

"What a charming notion! So likely too, seeing that I affirm injustice to be profitable and justice not."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that's exactly what I say, and I've explained why."

"And how do you categorize them? Would you call one a virtue and the other a vice?"

"Of course."

"I assume you'd call justice the virtue and injustice the vice?"

"What a delightful idea! Very plausible -- given that I just argued injustice is profitable and justice isn't."
D: Elegant: Socrates probes whether Thrasymachus considers justice a virtue. Thrasymachus's sarcastic response makes clear he does not: since injustice is profitable and justice is not, the conventional labels of virtue and vice are reversed.
D: Summary: Socrates asks Thrasymachus to classify justice and injustice as virtue or vice. Thrasymachus sarcastically rejects the conventional pairing, since he considers injustice profitable and justice not.
D: Distilled: Thrasymachus reverses conventional morality: if injustice is profitable and justice is not, then the usual labels of virtue and vice are backwards.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- Thrasymachus's sarcasm ('What a charming notion!') signals his awareness of how radical his position is. -- The inversion of virtue and vice marks a new level in the argument: Thrasymachus is not merely claiming that injustice is expedient but that it represents a kind of excellence. -- This is far more radical than merely saying 'crime pays' -- it challenges the entire moral framework. -- The transvaluation of values anticipates Nietzsche's project in Beyond Good and Evil and On the Genealogy of Morals more than two millennia later. -- Socrates recognizes this escalation (see 1.60) as making the argument 'almost unanswerable' on conventional grounds.

D: Original:
What else then would you say? The opposite, he replied. And would you call justice vice? No, I would rather say sublime simplicity. Then would you call injustice malignity? No; I would rather say discretion. And do the unjust appear to you to be wise and good? Yes, he said; at any rate those of them who are able to be perfectly unjust, and who have the power of subduing states and nations; but perhaps you imagine me to be talking of cutpurses.

Even this profession if undetected has advantages, though they are not to be compared with those of which I was just now speaking.

I do not think that I misapprehend your meaning, Thrasymachus, I replied; but still I cannot hear without amazement that you class injustice with wisdom and virtue, and justice with the opposite.
D: Narrative:
"What else then would you say?"

"The opposite," he replied.

"And would you call justice vice?"

"No, I would rather say sublime simplicity."

"Then would you call injustice malignity?"

"No; I would rather say discretion."

"And do the unjust appear to you to be wise and good?"

"Yes," he said; "at any rate those of them who are able to be perfectly unjust, and who have the power of subduing states and nations; but perhaps you imagine me to be talking of cutpurses."

"Even this profession if undetected has advantages, though they are not to be compared with those of which I was just now speaking."

"I do not think that I misapprehend your meaning, Thrasymachus," I replied; "but still I cannot hear without amazement that you class injustice with wisdom and virtue, and justice with the opposite."
D: Refashioned:
"Then what would you call them?"

"The reverse."

"You'd call justice a vice?"

"Not 'vice' exactly -- I'd call it noble naivety."

"And injustice -- would you call it malice?"

"No -- I'd call it good judgment."

"So the unjust seem to you wise and good?"

"Yes -- at least those who can practice injustice perfectly and have the power to conquer whole cities and nations. Unless you think I'm talking about pickpockets."

"Even pickpocketing has its advantages when you get away with it, though they're nothing compared to what you're describing."

"I understand your meaning, Thrasymachus," I said. "But I'm genuinely astonished to hear you classify injustice as wisdom and virtue, and justice as their opposite."
D: Elegant: Thrasymachus doesn't merely reverse virtue and vice -- he rebrands them. Justice is 'noble naivety'; injustice is 'good judgment.' The truly unjust are wise and good, especially those who conquer nations. Even petty crime has its merits. Socrates expresses genuine amazement at this complete moral inversion.
D: Summary: Thrasymachus calls justice 'noble naivety' and injustice 'good judgment,' classifying the successfully unjust as wise and virtuous. Socrates expresses astonishment at this thoroughgoing moral inversion.
D: Distilled: Thrasymachus rebrands justice as naivety and injustice as wisdom -- the successfully unjust person who conquers nations is truly virtuous.
