Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- 'Sublime simplicity' (gennaian euētheian) could also be translated as 'high-minded foolishness' -- the word euētheia originally meant 'good character' but had come to mean 'simple-mindedness,' a semantic shift that itself reflects the cultural tension Thrasymachus exploits. -- The term 'discretion' (euboulia) means 'good counsel' or 'sound judgment,' implying that injustice is the mark of the prudent person. -- The contemptuous reference to 'cutpurses' (petty thieves) shows Thrasymachus is talking about grand-scale political injustice, not common crime. -- This passage represents the fullest expression of Thrasymachus's moral nihilism: not merely that injustice is expedient but that it constitutes genuine excellence. -- Socrates's 'amazement' may be genuine or performative, but it signals to the reader that a new, more radical challenge has been posed. -- The position resembles what Callicles argues in the Gorgias (482c-486d): that conventional morality is for the weak, while the strong should pursue their own good without constraint.

D: Original: Certainly I do so class them. Now, I said, you are on more substantial and almost unanswerable ground; for if the injustice which you were maintaining to be profitable had been admitted by you as by others to be vice and deformity, an answer might have been given to you on received principles; but now I perceive that you will call injustice honourable and strong, and to the unjust you will attribute all the qualities which were attributed by us before to the just, seeing that you do not hesitate to rank injustice with wisdom and virtue.
D: Narrative:
"Certainly I do so class them."

"Now," I said, "you are on more substantial and almost unanswerable ground; for if the injustice which you were maintaining to be profitable had been admitted by you as by others to be vice and deformity, an answer might have been given to you on received principles; but now I perceive that you will call injustice honourable and strong, and to the unjust you will attribute all the qualities which were attributed by us before to the just, seeing that you do not hesitate to rank injustice with wisdom and virtue."
D: Refashioned:
"I certainly do."

"Now you're on much more formidable -- almost unanswerable -- ground. If you'd admitted, like most people, that injustice is profitable but still a vice and a deformity, I could have answered you using standard moral principles. But you're going further: you're calling injustice honorable and strong, and you're attributing to the unjust all the qualities we normally reserve for the just. You don't hesitate to rank injustice alongside wisdom and virtue."
D: Elegant: Socrates acknowledges the strength of Thrasymachus's position: if he merely said injustice was profitable but vicious, conventional arguments would refute him. But by calling injustice itself a form of wisdom and virtue, he has stepped beyond the reach of conventional morality. A new kind of argument is needed.
D: Summary: Socrates concedes that Thrasymachus's position is nearly unanswerable on conventional grounds, since he doesn't just claim injustice is profitable but that it constitutes a genuine virtue -- wisdom and strength. This requires a fundamentally new approach.
D: Distilled: Thrasymachus's position is nearly unanswerable because he does not merely call injustice profitable but ranks it as a genuine virtue alongside wisdom.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Thrasymachus. -- Socrates's concession that the position is 'almost unanswerable' is important: it shows he takes the challenge seriously and cannot dismiss it with easy arguments. -- The distinction between 'injustice is profitable but vicious' and 'injustice is virtuous' identifies the philosophical escalation: the first position is morally incoherent (why avoid what's profitable?), while the second is morally coherent but radical (injustice is the true good). -- The phrase 'received principles' (homologoumena) refers to commonly accepted moral premises that Socrates normally uses as starting points for the elenchus. -- When an interlocutor rejects those premises, Socrates must find different argumentative resources -- which he will do through the analogy between the wise/ignorant and the just/unjust. -- This passage is methodologically significant because it shows the limits of the elenctic method: it works only when interlocutors share some common moral ground.

D: Original:
You have guessed most infallibly, he replied. Then I certainly ought not to shrink from going through with the argument so long as I have reason to think that you, Thrasymachus, are speaking your real mind; for I do believe that you are now in earnest and are not amusing yourself at our expense.

I may be in earnest or not, but what is that to you? --to refute the argument is your business.

Very true, I said; that is what I have to do: But will you be so good as answer yet one more question? Does the just man try to gain any advantage over the just?

Far otherwise; if he did would not be the simple, amusing creature which he is.

And would he try to go beyond just action? He would not. And how would he regard the attempt to gain an advantage over the unjust; would that be considered by him as just or unjust?

He would think it just, and would try to gain the advantage; but he would not be able.

Whether he would or would not be able, I said, is not to the point. My question is only whether the just man, while refusing to have more than another just man, would wish and claim to have more than the unjust?

Yes, he would. And what of the unjust --does he claim to have more than the just man and to do more than is just

Of course, he said, for he claims to have more than all men.

And the unjust man will strive and struggle to obtain more than the unjust man or action, in order that he may have more than all?

True. We may put the matter thus, I said --the just does not desire more than his like but more than his unlike, whereas the unjust desires more than both his like and his unlike?

Nothing, he said, can be better than that statement. And the unjust is good and wise, and the just is neither?

Good again, he said. And is not the unjust like the wise and good and the just unlike them?
D: Narrative:
"You have guessed most infallibly," he replied.

"Then I certainly ought not to shrink from going through with the argument so long as I have reason to think that you, Thrasymachus, are speaking your real mind; for I do believe that you are now in earnest and are not amusing yourself at our expense."

"I may be in earnest or not, but what is that to you? -- to refute the argument is your business."

"Very true," I said; "that is what I have to do. But will you be so good as answer yet one more question? Does the just man try to gain any advantage over the just?"

"Far otherwise; if he did he would not be the simple, amusing creature which he is."

"And would he try to go beyond just action?"

"He would not."

"And how would he regard the attempt to gain an advantage over the unjust; would that be considered by him as just or unjust?"

"He would think it just, and would try to gain the advantage; but he would not be able."

"Whether he would or would not be able," I said, "is not to the point. My question is only whether the just man, while refusing to have more than another just man, would wish and claim to have more than the unjust?"

"Yes, he would."

"And what of the unjust -- does he claim to have more than the just man and to do more than is just?"

"Of course," he said, "for he claims to have more than all men."

"And the unjust man will strive and struggle to obtain more than the unjust man or action, in order that he may have more than all?"

"True."

"We may put the matter thus," I said -- "the just does not desire more than his like but more than his unlike, whereas the unjust desires more than both his like and his unlike?"

"Nothing," he said, "can be better than that statement."

"And the unjust is good and wise, and the just is neither?"

"Good again," he said.

"And is not the unjust like the wise and good and the just unlike them?"
D: Refashioned:
"You've guessed perfectly," he said.

"Then I shouldn't hesitate to follow the argument wherever it leads, as long as you're being sincere. I believe you genuinely hold this position and aren't just provoking us."

"Whether I'm sincere or not is irrelevant. Your job is to refute the argument."

"Fair enough. But let me ask one more question. Does the just person try to outdo other just people?"

"Of course not -- that's what makes him the charming simpleton he is."

"Does he try to exceed what's just?"

"No."

"And what about exceeding the unjust -- would he consider that just or unjust?"

"He'd consider it just and would try, but he wouldn't succeed."

"Whether he'd succeed isn't the point. I'm asking whether the just person would want to exceed the unjust, while not wanting to exceed other just people?"

"Yes."

"And the unjust person claims to exceed everyone -- both just and unjust?"

"Of course. He wants more than everyone."

"So: the just person wants to outdo only his opposite, while the unjust person wants to outdo both his like and his opposite?"

"Perfectly put."

"And you say the unjust person is wise and good, while the just person is neither?"

"Correct."

"And the unjust person resembles the wise and good, while the just person does not?"
D: Elegant: Socrates maps the logical structure: the just person seeks advantage only over the unjust (his unlike), not over other just people (his like). The unjust person seeks advantage over everyone. Thrasymachus confirms this pattern and affirms that the unjust -- who seeks to exceed all -- resembles the wise and good.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes a key pattern: just people don't try to outdo each other, only the unjust. Unjust people try to outdo everyone. Thrasymachus confirms this and maintains that the unjust person, who seeks to exceed all, is the one who resembles the wise and good.
D: Distilled: The just seek advantage only over the unjust; the unjust seek advantage over everyone -- and Thrasymachus identifies this total grasping with wisdom.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- The concept of pleonexia (wanting more, overreaching) is central to Greek ethical thought. The just person exhibits pleonexia only toward the unjust; the unjust exhibits it universally. -- Socrates is setting a logical trap: he will show that the pattern of the unjust (exceeding both like and unlike) matches the pattern of the ignorant, not the wise. -- The distinction between 'exceeding one's like' and 'exceeding one's unlike' is the key to the argument that follows. -- Thrasymachus's characterization of the just man as 'the simple, amusing creature which he is' echoes his earlier label of 'sublime simplicity' (1.59). -- The passage demonstrates Socrates's technique of getting precise formulations agreed upon before springing the logical consequence.

D: Original:
Of course, he said, he who is of a certain nature, is like those who are of a certain nature; he who is not, not.

Each of them, I said, is such as his like is? Certainly, he replied. Very good, Thrasymachus, I said; and now to take the case of the arts: you would admit that one man is a musician and another not a musician?

Yes. And which is wise and which is foolish? Clearly the musician is wise, and he who is not a musician is foolish.

And he is good in as far as he is wise, and bad in as far as he is foolish?

Yes. And you would say the same sort of thing of the physician?

Yes. And do you think, my excellent friend, that a musician when he adjusts the lyre would desire or claim to exceed or go beyond a musician in the tightening and loosening the strings?

I do not think that he would. But he would claim to exceed the non-musician? Of course. And what would you say of the physician? In prescribing meats and drinks would he wish to go beyond another physician or beyond the practice of medicine?

He would not. But he would wish to go beyond the non-physician? Yes. And about knowledge and ignorance in general; see whether you think that any man who has knowledge ever would wish to have the choice of saying or doing more than another man who has knowledge. Would he not rather say or do the same as his like in the same case?

That, I suppose, can hardly be denied. And what of the ignorant? would he not desire to have more than either the knowing or the ignorant?

I dare say. And the knowing is wise? Yes. And the wise is good? True. Then the wise and good will not desire to gain more than his like, but more than his unlike and opposite?

I suppose so. Whereas the bad and ignorant will desire to gain more than both?

Yes. But did we not say, Thrasymachus, that the unjust goes beyond both his like and unlike? Were not these your words? They were.
D: Narrative:
"Of course," he said, "he who is of a certain nature, is like those who are of a certain nature; he who is not, not."

"Each of them," I said, "is such as his like is?"

"Certainly," he replied.

"Very good, Thrasymachus," I said; "and now to take the case of the arts: you would admit that one man is a musician and another not a musician?"

"Yes."

"And which is wise and which is foolish?"

"Clearly the musician is wise, and he who is not a musician is foolish."

"And he is good in as far as he is wise, and bad in as far as he is foolish?"

"Yes."

"And you would say the same sort of thing of the physician?"

"Yes."

"And do you think, my excellent friend, that a musician when he adjusts the lyre would desire or claim to exceed or go beyond a musician in the tightening and loosening the strings?"

"I do not think that he would."

"But he would claim to exceed the non-musician?"

"Of course."

"And what would you say of the physician? In prescribing meats and drinks would he wish to go beyond another physician or beyond the practice of medicine?"

"He would not."

"But he would wish to go beyond the non-physician?"

"Yes."

"And about knowledge and ignorance in general; see whether you think that any man who has knowledge ever would wish to have the choice of saying or doing more than another man who has knowledge. Would he not rather say or do the same as his like in the same case?"

"That, I suppose, can hardly be denied."

"And what of the ignorant? Would he not desire to have more than either the knowing or the ignorant?"

"I dare say."

"And the knowing is wise?"

"Yes."

"And the wise is good?"

"True."

"Then the wise and good will not desire to gain more than his like, but more than his unlike and opposite?"

"I suppose so."

"Whereas the bad and ignorant will desire to gain more than both?"

"Yes."

"But did we not say, Thrasymachus, that the unjust goes beyond both his like and unlike? Were not these your words?"

"They were."
D: Refashioned:
"Of course," he said. "Like is attracted to like."

"And each person is defined by who they resemble?"

"Certainly."

"Good. Now consider the arts. You'd admit that one person is a musician and another is not?"

"Yes."

"And the musician is wise, the non-musician foolish?"

"Clearly."

"Good in proportion to wisdom, bad in proportion to foolishness?"

"Yes."

"Same for the physician?"

"Yes."

"Now, would a skilled musician, when tuning a lyre, try to outdo another skilled musician in tightening the strings?"

"No."

"But he'd claim to exceed the non-musician?"

"Of course."

"And a physician prescribing treatment -- would he try to outdo another physician, or go beyond medical practice?"

"No."

"But he'd want to exceed the non-physician?"

"Yes."

"In general, does anyone with knowledge wish to exceed or outdo another person with the same knowledge? Or would they say and do the same thing as their equal?"

"They'd do the same -- that can hardly be denied."

"But the ignorant person -- would he want to exceed both the knowledgeable and the ignorant?"

"Probably."

"And the knowledgeable person is wise?"

"Yes."

"And the wise is good?"

"True."

"So the wise and good person wants to exceed only the unlike -- not the like?"

"I suppose so."

"While the bad and ignorant wants to exceed everyone?"

"Yes."

"But didn't we say the unjust person tries to exceed both like and unlike? Weren't those your own words?"

"They were."
D: Elegant: Socrates springs the trap through craft analogies. A skilled musician doesn't try to outdo other musicians in tuning -- only non-musicians. A doctor doesn't try to exceed other doctors -- only non-doctors. In general, the knowledgeable person matches his equals and exceeds only the ignorant. The ignorant person tries to exceed everyone. This pattern exactly matches the unjust person's behavior -- not the just person's.
D: Summary: Using examples from music and medicine, Socrates shows that experts don't try to outdo other experts -- only non-experts. The knowledgeable exceed their unlike; the ignorant try to exceed everyone. Since the unjust person matches the pattern of the ignorant, not the wise, injustice aligns with ignorance rather than wisdom.
D: Distilled: The wise exceed only the unlike (as the just do); the ignorant exceed everyone (as the unjust do) -- so injustice resembles ignorance, not wisdom.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- This is the logical climax of Socrates's argument against Thrasymachus's claim that injustice is a form of wisdom. -- The argument is structural: it compares behavioral patterns rather than making direct moral claims. -- The musician tuning a lyre is the perfect example: there is one correct tuning, and all competent musicians converge on it. Only the incompetent would try to 'exceed' it. -- Similarly, in medicine, there is one correct prescription, and doctors agree on it. -- The general principle: knowledge produces convergence (experts agree with experts), while ignorance produces unlimited grasping (the ignorant try to outdo everyone). -- Since the unjust person tries to outdo everyone (like the ignorant), and the just person tries to outdo only the unlike (like the expert), justice resembles knowledge and injustice resembles ignorance. -- This is one of the most elegant arguments in Plato and anticipates the Republic's identification of justice with the proper ordering of the soul through knowledge (Book IV).

D: Original:
They were. And you also said that the lust will not go beyond his like but his unlike?

Yes. Then the just is like the wise and good, and the unjust like the evil and ignorant?

That is the inference. And each of them is such as his like is? That was admitted. Then the just has turned out to be wise and good and the unjust evil and ignorant.

Thrasymachus made all these admissions, not fluently, as I repeat them, but with extreme reluctance; it was a hot summer's day, and the perspiration poured from him in torrents; and then I saw what I had never seen before, Thrasymachus blushing. As we were now agreed that justice was virtue and wisdom, and injustice vice and ignorance, I proceeded to another point:

Well, I said, Thrasymachus, that matter is now settled; but were we not also saying that injustice had strength; do you remember?

Yes, I remember, he said, but do not suppose that I approve of what you are saying or have no answer; if however I were to answer, you would be quite certain to accuse me of haranguing; therefore either permit me to have my say out, or if you would rather ask, do so, and I will answer 'Very good,' as they say to story-telling old women, and will nod 'Yes' and 'No.'

Certainly not, I said, if contrary to your real opinion.

Yes, he said, I will, to please you, since you will not let me speak. What else would you have?

Nothing in the world, I said; and if you are so disposed I will ask and you shall answer.
D: Narrative:
"And you also said that the just will not go beyond his like but his unlike?"

"Yes."

"Then the just is like the wise and good, and the unjust like the evil and ignorant?"

"That is the inference."

"And each of them is such as his like is?"

"That was admitted."

"Then the just has turned out to be wise and good and the unjust evil and ignorant."

Thrasymachus made all these admissions, not fluently, as I repeat them, but with extreme reluctance; it was a hot summer's day, and the perspiration poured from him in torrents; and then I saw what I had never seen before, Thrasymachus blushing.

As we were now agreed that justice was virtue and wisdom, and injustice vice and ignorance, I proceeded to another point.

"Well," I said, "Thrasymachus, that matter is now settled; but were we not also saying that injustice had strength; do you remember?"

"Yes, I remember," he said, "but do not suppose that I approve of what you are saying or have no answer; if however I were to answer, you would be quite certain to accuse me of haranguing; therefore either permit me to have my say out, or if you would rather ask, do so, and I will answer 'Very good,' as they say to story-telling old women, and will nod 'Yes' and 'No.'"

"Certainly not," I said, "if contrary to your real opinion."

"Yes," he said, "I will, to please you, since you will not let me speak. What else would you have?"

"Nothing in the world," I said; "and if you are so disposed I will ask and you shall answer."
D: Refashioned:
"And you said the just person exceeds only the unlike, not the like?"

"Yes."

"Then the just person matches the pattern of the wise and good, and the unjust matches the pattern of the evil and ignorant?"

"So it seems."

"And each person is defined by who they resemble?"

"That was agreed."

"Then justice turns out to be wisdom and virtue, and injustice turns out to be ignorance and vice."

Thrasymachus conceded all of this -- not smoothly, as I'm retelling it, but with agonizing reluctance. It was a hot summer day, and sweat was pouring off him. Then I witnessed something I'd never seen before: Thrasymachus blushing.

With justice now established as virtue and wisdom, and injustice as vice and ignorance, I moved to the next issue.

"Well, Thrasymachus, that's settled. But weren't we also saying that injustice has strength? Do you remember?"

"I remember," he said. "But don't imagine I accept your conclusions or have nothing to say. If I gave a full response, though, you'd just accuse me of speechifying. So either let me speak freely, or keep asking your questions and I'll sit here nodding like someone humoring a storytelling grandmother -- 'Yes, very nice, go on.'"

"Not if those nods contradict what you actually think," I said.

"Fine. I'll go along with it to keep you happy, since you won't let me speak freely. What more do you want?"

"Nothing at all. If that's how you'd like to proceed, I'll ask and you'll answer."
D: Elegant: The logical trap closes: since the just person matches the pattern of the knowledgeable (exceeding only the unlike) and the unjust matches the pattern of the ignorant (exceeding everyone), justice is wisdom and injustice is ignorance. Thrasymachus concedes with extreme reluctance, visibly sweating and -- for the first time ever -- blushing. Deflated but defiant, he agrees to continue answering, though he protests that Socrates won't let him give a proper speech.
D: Summary: Socrates completes the argument: justice aligns with wisdom and virtue; injustice with ignorance and vice. Thrasymachus concedes reluctantly, sweating and blushing for the first time. Though defeated on this point, he insists he disagrees and sullenly agrees to continue the question-and-answer format.
D: Distilled: Justice is proven to be wisdom and virtue, injustice ignorance and vice -- and Thrasymachus, sweating and blushing, reluctantly concedes.
Notes: Socrates narrates and speaks; Thrasymachus speaks. -- The image of Thrasymachus blushing is one of the most vivid dramatic details in the Republic and signals his recognition of intellectual defeat, even if he cannot bring himself to fully admit it. -- The 'hot summer's day' adds physical atmosphere to the philosophical drama. -- Thrasymachus's comparison of Socrates's method to humoring 'story-telling old women' is dismissive but also revealing: he sees dialectic as a game rigged against the respondent. -- His complaint that Socrates won't let him give a full speech highlights the tension between rhetoric (monologue) and dialectic (dialogue). -- The argument that justice = wisdom and injustice = ignorance is the first major constructive result of Book I and provides the foundation for the Republic's identification of the just soul with the well-ordered, knowledge-guided soul. -- Thrasymachus's protest that he 'has an answer' but is prevented from giving it adds dramatic irony: the reader suspects his answer would not survive cross-examination.

D: Original: Proceed. Then I will repeat the question which I asked before, in order that our examination of the relative nature of justice and injustice may be carried on regularly. A statement was made that injustice is stronger and more powerful than justice, but now justice, having been identified with wisdom and virtue, is easily shown to be stronger than injustice, if injustice is ignorance; this can no longer be questioned by any one. But I want to view the matter, Thrasymachus, in a different way: You would not deny that a state may be unjust and may be unjustly attempting to enslave other states, or may have already enslaved them, and may be holding many of them in subjection?
D: Narrative:
"Proceed."

"Then I will repeat the question which I asked before, in order that our examination of the relative nature of justice and injustice may be carried on regularly. A statement was made that injustice is stronger and more powerful than justice, but now justice, having been identified with wisdom and virtue, is easily shown to be stronger than injustice, if injustice is ignorance; this can no longer be questioned by any one. But I want to view the matter, Thrasymachus, in a different way: You would not deny that a state may be unjust and may be unjustly attempting to enslave other states, or may have already enslaved them, and may be holding many of them in subjection?"
D: Refashioned:
"Go ahead."

"Let me restate the question so we can proceed methodically. It was claimed that injustice is stronger and more powerful than justice. But now that we've identified justice with wisdom and virtue, it easily follows that justice is stronger -- if injustice is ignorance, no one can dispute that. Still, I want to approach this from a different angle. You wouldn't deny that a state can be unjust -- that it might attempt to enslave other states, or might already hold several in subjection?"
D: Elegant: Socrates notes that since justice has been identified with wisdom, it must logically be stronger. But he takes a different approach: can an unjust state actually function effectively? Even Thrasymachus's favorite example -- the conquering state -- requires examination.
D: Summary: Having established that justice is wisdom, Socrates pivots to examine whether injustice truly has power. He approaches from a new angle: can an unjust state, which Thrasymachus admires, actually function as a unified force?
D: Distilled: Justice, now identified with wisdom, should be stronger than injustice -- but Socrates tests this through the practical question of whether unjust states can function.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Thrasymachus. -- The methodical restatement ('in order that our examination may be carried on regularly') shows Socrates's commitment to systematic inquiry. -- The transition from the abstract argument (justice = wisdom, therefore stronger) to the practical argument (can unjust groups actually cooperate?) shows two complementary approaches. -- The mention of states enslaving others would resonate powerfully with Plato's audience, given Athens's own imperial history and the recent trauma of the Peloponnesian War. -- This begins the argument about injustice's self-defeating nature: even a gang of criminals needs internal justice to function.

D: Original:
True, he replied; and I will add the best and perfectly unjust state will be most likely to do so.

I know, I said, that such was your position; but what I would further consider is, whether this power which is possessed by the superior state can exist or be exercised without justice.

If you are right in you view, and justice is wisdom, then only with justice; but if I am right, then without justice.

I am delighted, Thrasymachus, to see you not only nodding assent and dissent, but making answers which are quite excellent.

That is out of civility to you, he replied. You are very kind, I said; and would you have the goodness also to inform me, whether you think that a state, or an army, or a band of robbers and thieves, or any other gang of evil-doers could act at all if they injured one another?

No indeed, he said, they could not. But if they abstained from injuring one another, then they might act together better?

Yes. And this is because injustice creates divisions and hatreds and fighting, and justice imparts harmony and friendship; is not that true, Thrasymachus?

I agree, he said, because I do not wish to quarrel with you.

How good of you, I said; but I should like to know also whether injustice, having this tendency to arouse hatred, wherever existing, among slaves or among freemen, will not make them hate one another and set them at variance and render them incapable of common action?

Certainly. And even if injustice be found in two only, will they not quarrel and fight, and become enemies to one another and to the just

They will. And suppose injustice abiding in a single person, would your wisdom say that she loses or that she retains her natural power?
D: Narrative:
"True," he replied; "and I will add that the best and perfectly unjust state will be most likely to do so."

"I know," I said, "that such was your position; but what I would further consider is, whether this power which is possessed by the superior state can exist or be exercised without justice."

"If you are right in your view, and justice is wisdom, then only with justice; but if I am right, then without justice."

"I am delighted, Thrasymachus, to see you not only nodding assent and dissent, but making answers which are quite excellent."

"That is out of civility to you," he replied.

"You are very kind," I said; "and would you have the goodness also to inform me, whether you think that a state, or an army, or a band of robbers and thieves, or any other gang of evil-doers could act at all if they injured one another?"

"No indeed," he said, "they could not."

"But if they abstained from injuring one another, then they might act together better?"

"Yes."

"And this is because injustice creates divisions and hatreds and fighting, and justice imparts harmony and friendship; is not that true, Thrasymachus?"

"I agree," he said, "because I do not wish to quarrel with you."

"How good of you," I said; "but I should like to know also whether injustice, having this tendency to arouse hatred, wherever existing, among slaves or among freemen, will not make them hate one another and set them at variance and render them incapable of common action?"

"Certainly."

"And even if injustice be found in two only, will they not quarrel and fight, and become enemies to one another and to the just?"

"They will."

"And suppose injustice abiding in a single person, would your wisdom say that she loses or that she retains her natural power?"
D: Refashioned:
"True," he said. "And I'd add that the most perfectly unjust state would be the most successful at conquering."

"I understand your position. But here's what I want to examine: can this power exist without any justice at all?"

"If you're right that justice is wisdom, then no -- it needs justice. If I'm right, then it can operate without it."

"I'm glad to see you giving real answers now, Thrasymachus, not just nodding."

"I'm being polite," he said.

"Very gracious of you. Now tell me: could a state, an army, or even a band of thieves function at all if they were constantly harming each other?"

"Obviously not."

"But if they restrained themselves from harming each other, they could work together more effectively?"

"Yes."

"Because injustice produces division, hatred, and conflict, while justice produces harmony and cooperation. Isn't that right?"

"I agree -- because I don't want to argue with you."

"How generous. But tell me: this tendency of injustice to breed hatred -- wouldn't it work the same way everywhere? Among slaves, among free people? Wouldn't it make any group hate each other and become incapable of joint action?"

"Yes."

"Even between just two people -- wouldn't injustice make them quarrel, fight, and become enemies?"

"It would."

"And what about injustice residing in a single person? Does it lose its destructive power, or retain it?"
D: Elegant: Socrates draws the critical concession: even a band of thieves needs internal justice to function. Injustice breeds division, hatred, and conflict wherever it exists -- among states, armies, gangs, between two people, or even within a single person. Justice produces harmony and cooperation. The question scales down from states to individuals: does injustice destroy unity even within one soul?
D: Summary: Socrates shows that injustice is self-defeating: even criminals need internal justice to cooperate. Injustice creates division at every level -- states, armies, gangs, partnerships, individuals. He scales the argument down to the crucial question: what does injustice do to a single person?
D: Distilled: Even a band of thieves needs internal justice to function; injustice breeds division at every level, down to the individual soul.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- The argument from the band of thieves is one of the most famous in the Republic and demonstrates that injustice is parasitic on justice: it can only succeed where some minimal justice still operates. -- The scaling argument (state -> army -> gang -> pair -> individual) is methodologically characteristic of the Republic, which explicitly parallels the city and the soul. -- Thrasymachus's 'because I do not wish to quarrel with you' is ironic, given that quarreling is exactly what injustice produces. -- The question about injustice 'in a single person' anticipates the Republic's psychology of the soul: internal conflict (injustice within) produces the same dysfunction as external conflict (injustice between). -- The personification of injustice as 'she' follows Greek grammatical gender (adikia is feminine). -- This argument has been compared to Hobbes's state of nature: where there is no justice, there is war of all against all.

D: Original: Let us assume that she retains her power. Yet is not the power which injustice exercises of such a nature that wherever she takes up her abode, whether in a city, in an army, in a family, or in any other body, that body is, to begin with, rendered incapable of united action by reason of sedition and distraction; and does it not become its own enemy and at variance with all that opposes it, and with the just? Is not this the case?
D: Narrative: "Let us assume that she retains her power. Yet is not the power which injustice exercises of such a nature that wherever she takes up her abode, whether in a city, in an army, in a family, or in any other body, that body is, to begin with, rendered incapable of united action by reason of sedition and distraction; and does it not become its own enemy and at variance with all that opposes it, and with the just? Is not this the case?"
D: Refashioned: "Let's assume injustice retains its power. But consider what that power actually does. Wherever injustice takes up residence -- in a city, an army, a family, or any group -- that group is immediately torn apart by internal conflict and division. It becomes its own enemy, incapable of unified action, and hostile to everything that opposes it and to everything just. Isn't that the case?"
D: Elegant: Injustice's power is self-destructive: wherever it resides -- city, army, family -- it produces internal conflict, making the body its own enemy and incapable of unified action.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that injustice, wherever it takes hold, produces sedition and division, making any body -- state, army, or family -- its own enemy and incapable of united action.
D: Distilled: Injustice makes every body it inhabits -- city, army, family -- its own worst enemy through internal division.
Notes: Socrates speaks to Thrasymachus. -- The enumeration of 'city, army, family, or any other body' systematically covers all forms of human association. -- The argument that injustice makes a body 'its own enemy' is crucial: it means injustice is not merely harmful to others but self-destructive. -- The word 'sedition' (stasis) was the Greek term for civil war or factional conflict, one of the most feared political phenomena in the Greek world. -- This passage directly anticipates the Republic's analysis of the four degenerate constitutions (Book VIII), each of which suffers from increasing internal discord. -- The family reference is significant: the Republic will later argue that the ideal city requires communal child-rearing precisely to prevent familial loyalty from creating factional division. -- The argument scales down to the individual in the next passage.

D: Original:
Yes, certainly. And is not injustice equally fatal when existing in a single person; in the first place rendering him incapable of action because he is not at unity with himself, and in the second place making him an enemy to himself and the just? Is not that true, Thrasymachus?

Yes. And O my friend, I said, surely the gods are just? Granted that they are. But if so, the unjust will be the enemy of the gods, and the just will be their friend?

Feast away in triumph, and take your fill of the argument; I will not oppose you, lest I should displease the company.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, certainly."

"And is not injustice equally fatal when existing in a single person; in the first place rendering him incapable of action because he is not at unity with himself, and in the second place making him an enemy to himself and the just? Is not that true, Thrasymachus?"

"Yes."

"And O my friend," I said, "surely the gods are just?"

"Granted that they are."

"But if so, the unjust will be the enemy of the gods, and the just will be their friend?"

"Feast away in triumph, and take your fill of the argument; I will not oppose you, lest I should displease the company."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes."

"And isn't injustice equally destructive within a single person? First, it makes him incapable of action because he's at war with himself. Second, it makes him an enemy to himself and to the just. Isn't that true?"

"Yes."

"And surely, my friend, the gods are just?"

"Let's say they are."

"Then the unjust person is an enemy of the gods, and the just person is their friend?"

"Go ahead, celebrate your victory," he said. "Help yourself to the argument. I won't resist -- I don't want to upset the company."
D: Elegant: Socrates completes the descent: injustice within a single person creates internal war, making him unable to act and an enemy to himself. Adding that the gods are just, the unjust person is also an enemy of the gods. Thrasymachus, defeated and sullen, concedes everything rather than antagonize the group.
D: Summary: Injustice in a single person produces internal conflict, self-enmity, and incapacity for action. Since the gods are just, the unjust person is also their enemy. Thrasymachus surrenders the argument with bitter sarcasm rather than continue fighting.
D: Distilled: Injustice within a person creates internal warfare and self-enmity; the unjust person is an enemy to himself and to the gods.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- The argument that injustice renders a person 'not at unity with himself' is the seed of the Republic's tripartite psychology (Book IV): the unjust soul is one where the parts are in conflict rather than harmony. -- The claim that the unjust person is 'an enemy to himself' directly contradicts Thrasymachus's earlier claim that injustice is 'a man's own profit and interest' (1.44). -- The theological argument (the gods are just, therefore the unjust are their enemies) adds a religious dimension, though Plato's theology is philosophical rather than traditional. -- Thrasymachus's 'Feast away in triumph' is a bitter concession, using the metaphor of the philosophical discussion as a banquet (connecting to the Bendideia festival context). -- His deference to 'the company' rather than to the argument itself shows that social pressure, not logical persuasion, is driving his compliance -- exactly the kind of unreasoned compliance that the Republic argues against.

D: Original: Well then, proceed with your answers, and let me have the remainder of my repast. For we have already shown that the just are clearly wiser and better and abler than the unjust, and that the unjust are incapable of common action; nay ing at more, that to speak as we did of men who are evil acting at any time vigorously together, is not strictly true, for if they had been perfectly evil, they would have laid hands upon one another; but it is evident that there must have been some remnant of justice in them, which enabled them to combine; if there had not been they would have injured one another as well as their victims; they were but half --villains in their enterprises; for had they been whole villains, and utterly unjust, they would have been utterly incapable of action. That, as I believe, is the truth of the matter, and not what you said at first. But whether the just have a better and happier life than the unjust is a further question which we also proposed to consider. I think that they have, and for the reasons which to have given; but still I should like to examine further, for no light matter is at stake, nothing less than the rule of human life.
D: Narrative:
"Well then, proceed with your answers, and let me have the remainder of my repast. For we have already shown that the just are clearly wiser and better and abler than the unjust, and that the unjust are incapable of common action; nay more, that to speak as we did of men who are evil acting at any time vigorously together, is not strictly true, for if they had been perfectly evil, they would have laid hands upon one another; but it is evident that there must have been some remnant of justice in them, which enabled them to combine; if there had not been they would have injured one another as well as their victims; they were but half-villains in their enterprises; for had they been whole villains, and utterly unjust, they would have been utterly incapable of action.

"That, as I believe, is the truth of the matter, and not what you said at first. But whether the just have a better and happier life than the unjust is a further question which we also proposed to consider. I think that they have, and for the reasons which I have given; but still I should like to examine further, for no light matter is at stake, nothing less than the rule of human life."
D: Refashioned:
"Good -- keep answering, and let me finish my meal of arguments. We've established that the just are wiser, better, and more capable than the unjust, and that the unjust can't even cooperate with each other. In fact, when we speak of evil people acting effectively together, that's not strictly accurate. If they were perfectly evil, they'd destroy each other. Any criminal organization that actually functions must contain some remnant of justice -- otherwise they'd attack each other as readily as their victims. They're half-villains at best. Total villains would be totally incapable of action.

"That, I believe, is the truth -- not what you said earlier. But there's still the question of whether the just live better and happier lives than the unjust. I believe they do, based on what we've discussed. But I want to examine this further, because the stakes couldn't be higher -- we're talking about nothing less than how to live a human life."
D: Elegant: Socrates summarizes: the just are wiser, better, and more capable. The unjust cannot cooperate -- even successful criminals depend on some remnant of justice. Total injustice means total incapacity. But the question of whether the just are happier remains open, and Socrates insists on examining it further because the stakes are the highest possible: the right way to live.
D: Summary: Socrates recaps: the just are superior in wisdom and capability, and even criminal cooperation depends on residual justice. But the question of whether the just live happier lives still needs examination, since the stakes are nothing less than the fundamental question of how to live.
D: Distilled: Even criminals need residual justice to function; total injustice means total incapacity -- but whether the just are happier remains to be proven.
Notes: Socrates speaks. -- The 'repast' metaphor continues the banquet theme established by the Bendideia festival and Thrasymachus's 'feast away' comment. -- The observation that criminals are 'half-villains' is philosophically important: it shows that injustice is parasitic on justice and cannot exist in pure form. -- This insight anticipates Augustine's argument in the City of God that evil is a privation of good, not an independent substance. -- The phrase 'nothing less than the rule of human life' (ou gar peri tou epitychontos ho logos, alla peri tou hontina tropon chrē zēn) echoes 1.45 and states the Republic's overarching theme in the most explicit terms. -- Socrates's admission that the argument so far is suggestive but not conclusive shows philosophical honesty and foreshadows Book I's self-critical conclusion.

D: Original:
Proceed. I will proceed by asking a question: Would you not say that a horse has some end?

I should. And the end or use of a horse or of anything would be that which could not be accomplished, or not so well accomplished, by any other thing?

I do not understand, he said. Let me explain: Can you see, except with the eye? Certainly not. Or hear, except with the ear? No. These then may be truly said to be the ends of these organs?

They may. But you can cut off a vine-branch with a dagger or with a chisel, and in many other ways?

Of course. And yet not so well as with a pruning-hook made for the purpose?

True. May we not say that this is the end of a pruning-hook? We may. Then now I think you will have no difficulty in understanding my meaning when I asked the question whether the end of anything would be that which could not be accomplished, or not so well accomplished, by any other thing?

I understand your meaning, he said, and assent. And that to which an end is appointed has also an excellence? Need I ask again whether the eye has an end?

It has. And has not the eye an excellence? Yes. And the ear has an end and an excellence also? True. And the same is true of all other things; they have each of them an end and a special excellence?

That is so. Well, and can the eyes fulfil their end if they are wanting in their own proper excellence and have a defect instead?

How can they, he said, if they are blind and cannot see?

You mean to say, if they have lost their proper excellence, which is sight; but I have not arrived at that point yet. I would rather ask the question more generally, and only enquire whether the things which fulfil their ends fulfil them by their own proper excellence, and fall of fulfilling them by their own defect?

Certainly, he replied. I might say the same of the ears; when deprived of their own proper excellence they cannot fulfil their end?
D: Narrative:
"Proceed."

"I will proceed by asking a question: Would you not say that a horse has some end?"

"I should."

"And the end or use of a horse or of anything would be that which could not be accomplished, or not so well accomplished, by any other thing?"

"I do not understand," he said.

"Let me explain: Can you see, except with the eye?"

"Certainly not."

"Or hear, except with the ear?"

"No."

"These then may be truly said to be the ends of these organs?"

"They may."

"But you can cut off a vine-branch with a dagger or with a chisel, and in many other ways?"

"Of course."

"And yet not so well as with a pruning-hook made for the purpose?"

"True."

"May we not say that this is the end of a pruning-hook?"

"We may."

"Then now I think you will have no difficulty in understanding my meaning when I asked the question whether the end of anything would be that which could not be accomplished, or not so well accomplished, by any other thing?"

"I understand your meaning," he said, "and assent."

"And that to which an end is appointed has also an excellence? Need I ask again whether the eye has an end?"

"It has."

"And has not the eye an excellence?"

"Yes."

"And the ear has an end and an excellence also?"

"True."

"And the same is true of all other things; they have each of them an end and a special excellence?"

"That is so."

"Well, and can the eyes fulfil their end if they are wanting in their own proper excellence and have a defect instead?"

"How can they," he said, "if they are blind and cannot see?"

"You mean to say, if they have lost their proper excellence, which is sight; but I have not arrived at that point yet. I would rather ask the question more generally, and only enquire whether the things which fulfil their ends fulfil them by their own proper excellence, and fail of fulfilling them by their own defect?"

"Certainly," he replied.

"I might say the same of the ears; when deprived of their own proper excellence they cannot fulfil their end?"
D: Refashioned:
"Go ahead."

"Let me start with a question: would you say a horse has a function -- a purpose?"

"Yes."

"And by 'function' I mean something that can only be done, or done best, by that particular thing?"

"I don't follow."

"Let me explain. Can you see with anything other than your eyes?"

"No."

"Or hear with anything other than your ears?"

"No."

"So seeing is the proper function of the eyes, and hearing of the ears?"

"Yes."

"Now, you could cut a vine branch with a knife or a chisel, among other tools?"

"Sure."

"But not as well as with a pruning hook designed for the job?"

"Right."

"So pruning is the function of a pruning hook?"

"Yes."

"Now do you understand what I mean by the 'function' of something -- that which it alone can do, or does best?"

"I understand, and I agree."

"Now: does everything that has a function also have an excellence? Take the eye -- it has a function, right?"

"It does."

"And an excellence?"

"Yes."

"The ear too -- function and excellence?"

"Yes."

"And this applies to everything -- each thing has its function and its proper excellence?"

"Agreed."

"Can the eyes fulfill their function if they lack their proper excellence and have a defect instead?"

"Obviously not -- if they're blind, they can't see."

"You're jumping ahead. I'm not asking about blindness specifically but about the general principle: things fulfill their function through their proper excellence and fail through defect?"

"Yes."

"And the same holds for ears -- without their proper excellence, they can't hear?"
D: Elegant: Socrates introduces the concept of function (ergon) and excellence (aretē). Everything has a function -- what it alone can do or does best -- and a corresponding excellence. Eyes see (function) through sight (excellence); without it, they fail. The pruning hook prunes best because that's its purpose. This framework will be applied to the soul itself.
D: Summary: Socrates builds the function argument: every thing has a specific function (what it alone does or does best) and a corresponding excellence (what enables it to perform that function well). Eyes need sight, ears need hearing. Defects prevent function. This framework leads toward the soul.
D: Distilled: Everything has a function and a corresponding excellence; without that excellence, it cannot fulfill its purpose.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- The function argument (ergon argument) is one of the most important philosophical tools in the Republic and later in Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics (Book I, 1097b-1098a), where it becomes the basis for defining human happiness. -- Ergon means 'function,' 'work,' or 'characteristic activity'; aretē means 'excellence' or 'virtue.' -- The progression from eye to ear to pruning hook establishes the universal principle before applying it to the soul. -- The pruning hook example is characteristically Socratic in its mundane specificity. -- The argument's logic: (1) everything has a function; (2) everything has an excellence that enables it to fulfill its function; (3) the soul has a function; (4) the soul has an excellence; (5) that excellence is justice. -- This will yield the conclusion that the just person lives well and the unjust person does not.

D: Original:
True. And the same observation will apply to all other things?

I agree. Well; and has not the soul an end which nothing else can fulfil? for example, to superintend and command and deliberate and the like. Are not these functions proper to the soul, and can they rightly be assigned to any other?

To no other. And is not life to be reckoned among the ends of the soul?

Assuredly, he said. And has not the soul an excellence also? Yes. And can she or can she not fulfil her own ends when deprived of that excellence?

She cannot. Then an evil soul must necessarily be an evil ruler and superintendent, and the good soul a good ruler?

Yes, necessarily. And we have admitted that justice is the excellence of the soul, and injustice the defect of the soul?

That has been admitted. Then the just soul and the just man will live well, and the unjust man will live ill?

That is what your argument proves. And he who lives well is blessed and happy, and he who lives ill the reverse of happy?

Certainly. Then the just is happy, and the unjust miserable? So be it. But happiness and not misery is profitable. Of course. Then, my blessed Thrasymachus, injustice can never be more profitable than justice.

Let this, Socrates, he said, be your entertainment at the Bendidea.
D: Narrative:
"True."

"And the same observation will apply to all other things?"

"I agree."

"Well; and has not the soul an end which nothing else can fulfil? For example, to superintend and command and deliberate and the like. Are not these functions proper to the soul, and can they rightly be assigned to any other?"

"To no other."

"And is not life to be reckoned among the ends of the soul?"

"Assuredly," he said.

"And has not the soul an excellence also?"

"Yes."

"And can she or can she not fulfil her own ends when deprived of that excellence?"

"She cannot."

"Then an evil soul must necessarily be an evil ruler and superintendent, and the good soul a good ruler?"

"Yes, necessarily."

"And we have admitted that justice is the excellence of the soul, and injustice the defect of the soul?"

"That has been admitted."

"Then the just soul and the just man will live well, and the unjust man will live ill?"

"That is what your argument proves."

"And he who lives well is blessed and happy, and he who lives ill the reverse of happy?"

"Certainly."

"Then the just is happy, and the unjust miserable?"

"So be it."

"But happiness and not misery is profitable."

"Of course."

"Then, my blessed Thrasymachus, injustice can never be more profitable than justice."

"Let this, Socrates," he said, "be your entertainment at the Bendidea."
D: Refashioned:
"True."

"And this applies to everything?"

"Yes."

"Now: doesn't the soul have a function that nothing else can perform? Directing, commanding, deliberating, and so on -- aren't these uniquely the soul's work?"

"They are."

"And isn't living itself one of the soul's functions?"

"Certainly."

"And the soul has an excellence?"

"Yes."

"Can the soul fulfill its functions without that excellence?"

"No."

"Then a defective soul must be a bad ruler and director, while a good soul is a good one?"

"Necessarily."

"And we've agreed that justice is the soul's excellence and injustice its defect?"

"We have."

"Then the just person will live well and the unjust person will live badly?"

"So your argument proves."

"And whoever lives well is happy, and whoever lives badly is miserable?"

"Of course."

"Then the just person is happy and the unjust person is miserable?"

"If you say so."

"And happiness, not misery, is profitable?"

"Obviously."

"Then, my dear Thrasymachus, injustice can never be more profitable than justice."

"Let this be your entertainment at the festival, Socrates," he said.
D: Elegant: Socrates applies the function argument to the soul. The soul's function -- directing, commanding, deliberating, living -- requires its proper excellence, which is justice. Without justice, the soul fails at everything, including living well. The just person is therefore happy and the unjust miserable. Since happiness is profitable and misery is not, injustice can never be more profitable than justice. Thrasymachus concedes with weary sarcasm.
D: Summary: The soul's function is to direct, command, and live. Its excellence is justice. Without justice, the soul fails; with it, the soul thrives. Therefore the just are happy and the unjust miserable, and injustice can never be more profitable than justice. Thrasymachus concedes the point as a gift to Socrates at the festival.
D: Distilled: The soul's excellence is justice; without it, the soul cannot live well -- therefore the just are happy and injustice can never be more profitable.
Notes: Socrates and Thrasymachus speak. -- The function argument (ergon argument) reaches its conclusion: the soul's proper excellence (aretē) is justice, and without it the soul cannot fulfill its essential functions, including living well (eu zēn). -- The chain of reasoning: soul has function -> function requires excellence -> excellence is justice -> just soul lives well -> living well is happiness -> happiness is profitable -> justice is always more profitable than injustice. -- Thrasymachus's 'Let this be your entertainment at the Bendidea' circles back to the festival setting of 1.1-1.2, providing literary closure to the frame narrative. -- The word 'entertainment' (hestiais) means 'feast' or 'treat,' maintaining the banquet metaphor. -- Though the argument seems conclusive, Socrates himself will express dissatisfaction in the next passage. -- The ergon argument is taken up and refined by Aristotle in the Nicomachean Ethics I.7, where he uses it to define eudaimonia (flourishing/happiness) as activity of soul in accordance with virtue.

D: Original: For which I am indebted to you, I said, now that you have grown gentle towards me and have left off scolding. Nevertheless, I have not been well entertained; but that was my own fault and not yours. As an epicure snatches a taste of every dish which is successively brought to table, he not having allowed himself time to enjoy the one before, so have I gone from one subject to another without having discovered what I sought at first, the nature of justice. I left that enquiry and turned away to consider whether justice is virtue and wisdom or evil and folly; and when there arose a further question about the comparative advantages of justice and injustice, I could not refrain from passing on to that. And the result of the whole discussion has been that I know nothing at all. For I know not what justice is, and therefore I am not likely to know whether it is or is not a virtue, nor can I say whether the just man is happy or unhappy.
D: Narrative: "For which I am indebted to you," I said, "now that you have grown gentle towards me and have left off scolding. Nevertheless, I have not been well entertained; but that was my own fault and not yours. As an epicure snatches a taste of every dish which is successively brought to table, he not having allowed himself time to enjoy the one before, so have I gone from one subject to another without having discovered what I sought at first, the nature of justice. I left that enquiry and turned away to consider whether justice is virtue and wisdom or evil and folly; and when there arose a further question about the comparative advantages of justice and injustice, I could not refrain from passing on to that. And the result of the whole discussion has been that I know nothing at all. For I know not what justice is, and therefore I am not likely to know whether it is or is not a virtue, nor can I say whether the just man is happy or unhappy."
D: Refashioned:
"And I'm grateful to you for that," I said, "now that you've calmed down and stopped berating me. Even so, I haven't been well entertained -- but that's my own fault, not yours. Like a greedy diner who grabs a taste of every dish as it comes, never taking time to properly enjoy any single one, I've jumped from topic to topic without discovering what I was looking for in the first place: the nature of justice. I abandoned that inquiry to ask whether justice is virtue and wisdom or vice and folly. And when the question arose of whether justice or injustice is more advantageous, I couldn't resist chasing that instead.

"The result of this entire discussion is that I know nothing. I don't know what justice is, and therefore I can hardly know whether it's a virtue or not, or whether the just person is happy or unhappy."
D: Elegant: Socrates ends with characteristic self-criticism. Like a glutton tasting every dish but savoring none, he jumped from topic to topic without ever answering the fundamental question: what is justice? Without knowing that, all subsequent conclusions -- about virtue, wisdom, happiness -- rest on sand. Book I ends in admitted ignorance, setting the stage for the Republic's constructive project.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes Book I by confessing failure. He compares himself to a greedy diner who samples everything but enjoys nothing. He never answered the central question -- what is justice? -- and therefore all his conclusions about justice's relationship to virtue and happiness are premature. The dialogue ends in Socratic ignorance.
D: Distilled: Book I ends in confessed ignorance: without knowing what justice is, Socrates cannot determine whether it is a virtue or whether the just person is happy.
Notes: Socrates speaks. -- This self-critical conclusion is characteristic of Plato's early 'aporetic' dialogues (Laches, Charmides, Euthyphro), which end in acknowledged ignorance (aporia). -- The 'epicure' (opsophagos) metaphor compares philosophical inquiry to dining: both require patience and focus, not greedy sampling. -- Socrates identifies the logical problem: his arguments about justice's profitability depend on knowing what justice is, but he never established that. The arguments of Book I are therefore provisional. -- This admission of failure is not merely rhetorical humility -- it creates the dramatic and philosophical necessity for Books II-X, where Glaucon and Adeimantus will demand (and receive) a proper constructive account. -- The statement 'I know nothing at all' echoes the Apology's famous claim that Socratic wisdom consists in knowing one's own ignorance (Apology 21d). -- Book I thus functions as a dramatic prologue: it clears the ground by refuting inadequate definitions, establishes the central question, and reveals the depth of the challenge, while leaving the constructive answer for the remainder of the Republic.

D: Original: With these words I was thinking that I had made an end of the discussion; but the end, in truth, proved to be only a beginning. For Glaucon, who is always the most pugnacious of men, was dissatisfied at Thrasymachus' retirement; he wanted to have the battle out. So he said to me: Socrates, do you wish really to persuade us, or only to seem to have persuaded us, that to be just is always better than to be unjust?
D: Narrative:
With these words I was thinking that I had made an end of the discussion; but the end, in truth, proved to be only a beginning. For Glaucon, who is always the most pugnacious of men, was dissatisfied at Thrasymachus' retirement; he wanted to have the battle out.

So he said to me: "Socrates, do you wish really to persuade us, or only to seem to have persuaded us, that to be just is always better than to be unjust?"
D: Refashioned:
I thought the debate was over -- but it turned out to be just getting started. Glaucon, who never backs down from an argument, was unhappy that Thrasymachus had given up so easily. He wanted the fight to continue.

"Socrates," he said to me, "do you actually want to convince us that being just is always better than being unjust -- or do you just want to appear to have convinced us?"
D: Elegant: I thought the discussion was finished, but Glaucon -- always a fighter -- was unsatisfied with Thrasymachus's surrender. He pressed me: "Do you truly want to persuade us that justice is always better than injustice, or merely appear to have done so?"
D: Summary: After Thrasymachus concedes defeat in Book I, Glaucon reopens the debate, challenging Socrates to genuinely prove -- not just seem to prove -- that justice is always preferable to injustice.
D: Distilled: Glaucon challenges Socrates to truly demonstrate, not merely appear to demonstrate, that justice is always superior to injustice.
Notes: Socrates narrates; Glaucon speaks -- Glaucon is Plato's older brother -- Continues directly from the end of Book I where Thrasymachus argued that injustice is more profitable than justice -- The distinction between 'really persuading' and 'seeming to persuade' foreshadows the appearance-vs-reality theme central to Book II -- Thrasymachus: the sophist from Book I who argued justice is 'the advantage of the stronger'

D: Original:
I should wish really to persuade you, I replied, if I could.

Then you certainly have not succeeded. Let me ask you now: --How would you arrange goods --are there not some which we welcome for their own sakes, and independently of their consequences, as, for example, harmless pleasures and enjoyments, which delight us at the time, although nothing follows from them?

I agree in thinking that there is such a class, I replied.

Is there not also a second class of goods, such as knowledge, sight, health, which are desirable not only in themselves, but also for their results?

Certainly, I said. And would you not recognize a third class, such as gymnastic, and the care of the sick, and the physician's art; also the various ways of money-making --these do us good but we regard them as disagreeable; and no one would choose them for their own sakes, but only for the sake of some reward or result which flows from them?

There is, I said, this third class also. But why do you ask?

Because I want to know in which of the three classes you would place justice?

In the highest class, I replied, --among those goods which he who would be happy desires both for their own sake and for the sake of their results.

Then the many are of another mind; they think that justice is to be reckoned in the troublesome class, among goods which are to be pursued for the sake of rewards and of reputation, but in themselves are disagreeable and rather to be avoided.

I know, I said, that this is their manner of thinking, and that this was the thesis which Thrasymachus was maintaining just now, when he censured justice and praised injustice. But I am too stupid to be convinced by him.
D: Narrative:
"I should wish really to persuade you," I replied, "if I could."

"Then you certainly have not succeeded," Glaucon said. "Let me ask you now: How would you arrange goods -- are there not some which we welcome for their own sakes, and independently of their consequences, as, for example, harmless pleasures and enjoyments, which delight us at the time, although nothing follows from them?"

"I agree in thinking that there is such a class," I replied.

"Is there not also a second class of goods, such as knowledge, sight, health, which are desirable not only in themselves, but also for their results?"

"Certainly," I said.

"And would you not recognize a third class, such as gymnastic, and the care of the sick, and the physician's art; also the various ways of money-making -- these do us good but we regard them as disagreeable; and no one would choose them for their own sakes, but only for the sake of some reward or result which flows from them?"

"There is," I said, "this third class also. But why do you ask?"

"Because I want to know in which of the three classes you would place justice?"

"In the highest class," I replied, "among those goods which he who would be happy desires both for their own sake and for the sake of their results."

"Then the many are of another mind; they think that justice is to be reckoned in the troublesome class, among goods which are to be pursued for the sake of rewards and of reputation, but in themselves are disagreeable and rather to be avoided."

"I know," I said, "that this is their manner of thinking, and that this was the thesis which Thrasymachus was maintaining just now, when he censured justice and praised injustice. But I am too stupid to be convinced by him."
D: Refashioned:
"I would genuinely like to persuade you," I replied, "if I could."

"Well, you haven't," Glaucon said. "Let me ask you something. How would you classify different kinds of goods? Aren't there some things we value purely for their own sake -- simple pleasures and enjoyments that are delightful in the moment, even if nothing comes of them?"

"Yes, I agree there is such a category," I said.

"And isn't there a second category -- things like knowledge, sight, and health -- that are valuable both in themselves and for what they produce?"

"Certainly."

"And wouldn't you also recognize a third category -- things like exercise, medical treatment, and various ways of making money? These benefit us, but we find them unpleasant. No one would choose them for their own sake, only for the rewards they bring."

"Yes, there is this third category too. But why do you ask?"

"Because I want to know: in which of these three categories would you place justice?"

"In the highest," I replied -- "among those goods that anyone who wants to be happy values both for their own sake and for their results."

"Most people disagree with you. They put justice in the unpleasant category -- something to be endured for the sake of rewards and reputation, but inherently disagreeable."

"I know that is what people think," I said. "It is exactly what Thrasymachus was arguing when he attacked justice and praised injustice. But I am not convinced by him."
D: Elegant: Glaucon establishes a three-part classification of goods: (1) things valued purely for themselves, like simple pleasures; (2) things valued both for themselves and their consequences, like knowledge and health; (3) things valued only for their consequences, like exercise or money-making. Socrates places justice in the highest class -- valued both intrinsically and for its results. Glaucon counters that most people place justice in the third, disagreeable class, endured only for reputation and rewards.
D: Summary: Glaucon classifies goods into three types: valued for themselves alone, valued both intrinsically and for results, or valued only for results despite being unpleasant. Socrates places justice in the second, highest class, but Glaucon notes most people consider justice merely a necessary burden endured for its rewards.
D: Distilled: Socrates considers justice intrinsically and instrumentally good, but most people view it as an unpleasant necessity endured only for external rewards.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- The three-fold classification of goods is original to this passage and becomes the framework for the entire challenge -- Thrasymachus's position from Book I (338c): justice is the advantage of the stronger -- This taxonomy anticipates Aristotle's later classification of goods in the Nicomachean Ethics -- The 'highest class' (second category) becomes the standard Socrates must meet: proving justice is good both in itself and in its consequences

D: Original:
I wish, he said, that you would hear me as well as him, and then I shall see whether you and I agree. For Thrasymachus seems to me, like a snake, to have been charmed by your voice sooner than he ought to have been; but to my mind the nature of justice and injustice have not yet been made clear. Setting aside their rewards and results, I want to know what they are in themselves, and how they inwardly work in the soul. If you, please, then, I will revive the argument of Thrasymachus. And first I will speak of the nature and origin of justice according to the common view of them. Secondly, I will show that all men who practise justice do so against their will, of necessity, but not as a good. And thirdly, I will argue that there is reason in this view, for the life of the unjust is after all better far than the life of the just --if what they say is true, Socrates, since I myself am not of their opinion. But still I acknowledge that I am perplexed when I hear the voices of Thrasymachus and myriads of others dinning in my ears; and, on the other hand, I have never yet heard the superiority of justice to injustice maintained by any one in a satisfactory way. I want to hear justice praised in respect of itself; then I shall be satisfied, and you are the person from whom I think that I am most likely to hear this; and therefore I will praise the unjust life to the utmost of my power, and my manner of speaking will indicate the manner in which I desire to hear you too praising justice and censuring injustice. Will you say whether you approve of my proposal?

Indeed I do; nor can I imagine any theme about which a man of sense would oftener wish to converse.

I am delighted, he replied, to hear you say so, and shall begin by speaking, as I proposed, of the nature and origin of justice.
D: Narrative:
"I wish," Glaucon said, "that you would hear me as well as him, and then I shall see whether you and I agree. For Thrasymachus seems to me, like a snake, to have been charmed by your voice sooner than he ought to have been; but to my mind the nature of justice and injustice have not yet been made clear. Setting aside their rewards and results, I want to know what they are in themselves, and how they inwardly work in the soul.

"If you please, then, I will revive the argument of Thrasymachus. And first I will speak of the nature and origin of justice according to the common view of them. Secondly, I will show that all men who practise justice do so against their will, of necessity, but not as a good. And thirdly, I will argue that there is reason in this view, for the life of the unjust is after all better far than the life of the just -- if what they say is true, Socrates, since I myself am not of their opinion.

"But still I acknowledge that I am perplexed when I hear the voices of Thrasymachus and myriads of others dinning in my ears; and, on the other hand, I have never yet heard the superiority of justice to injustice maintained by any one in a satisfactory way. I want to hear justice praised in respect of itself; then I shall be satisfied, and you are the person from whom I think that I am most likely to hear this; and therefore I will praise the unjust life to the utmost of my power, and my manner of speaking will indicate the manner in which I desire to hear you too praising justice and censuring injustice. Will you say whether you approve of my proposal?"

"Indeed I do," I replied; "nor can I imagine any theme about which a man of sense would oftener wish to converse."

"I am delighted," he replied, "to hear you say so, and shall begin by speaking, as I proposed, of the nature and origin of justice."
D: Refashioned:
"I wish you would hear my argument too," Glaucon said, "and then we will see if we agree. Thrasymachus, it seems to me, was like a snake charmed by your voice -- he gave in too easily. The true nature of justice and injustice still has not been made clear. Setting aside rewards and consequences entirely, I want to know what justice and injustice are in themselves, and what they do to the soul from within.

"So let me revive Thrasymachus's argument. First, I will lay out the common account of what justice is and where it comes from. Second, I will show that everyone who practices justice does so unwillingly -- out of necessity, not because they see it as a good. Third, I will argue that this view makes sense, because the unjust life really is better than the just life -- if the common view is correct. I do not personally believe this, Socrates.

"But I confess I am troubled. The arguments of Thrasymachus and countless others ring in my ears, and I have never heard anyone defend the superiority of justice over injustice in a way that truly satisfies. I want to hear justice praised for what it is in itself. You are the one most likely to give me that defense. So I will argue for injustice as powerfully as I can, and the way I argue will show you exactly how I want to hear you argue for justice. Do you accept this proposal?"

"Absolutely," I said. "I cannot think of a topic more worthy of serious conversation."

"Wonderful," he replied. "Then let me begin, as I said, with the nature and origin of justice."
D: Elegant: Glaucon declares Thrasymachus surrendered too easily -- like a snake charmed by Socrates' voice. He wants to know what justice and injustice are in themselves, apart from all consequences. He proposes a three-part challenge: first, the social-contract origin of justice; second, that people practice justice only from necessity; third, that the unjust life is rationally preferable. He will argue for injustice at full strength so Socrates can see exactly what kind of defense justice requires. Socrates eagerly accepts.
D: Summary: Glaucon announces he will play devil's advocate, arguing the case for injustice as powerfully as possible, so that Socrates will be forced to defend justice purely on its own merits -- stripped of all rewards, reputation, and consequences. He outlines a three-part challenge covering the origin, practice, and rationality of justice.
D: Distilled: Glaucon will steel-man the case for injustice in three parts, demanding Socrates prove justice is good in itself, apart from all external rewards.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- Glaucon explicitly disclaims belief in the position he will argue (devil's advocate / steel-manning) -- The snake-charming metaphor for Thrasymachus echoes the sophist-as-serpent trope -- 'What they are in themselves and how they inwardly work in the soul' sets the standard for the rest of the Republic -- Glaucon's three-part structure: (1) social contract theory of justice (2.4), (2) Ring of Gyges thought experiment (2.5-2.7), (3) comparison of just and unjust lives (2.8-2.11) -- Pindar and Archilochus will be cited later -- This challenge drives the entire Republic: Books II-X are Socrates' response

D: Original: They say that to do injustice is, by nature, good; to suffer injustice, evil; but that the evil is greater than the good. And so when men have both done and suffered injustice and have had experience of both, not being able to avoid the one and obtain the other, they think that they had better agree among themselves to have neither; hence there arise laws and mutual covenants; and that which is ordained by law is termed by them lawful and just.
