D: Elegant: If shoemaking requires lifelong specialization, warfare -- which is far more important -- demands it even more. A part-time soldier is as absurd as a part-time chess master. The principle of specialization that governs every other craft applies with even greater force to the art of war.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that if shoemaking requires dedicated specialization, warfare demands it even more. Just as no one becomes an expert at board games through casual play, no one can be a competent soldier while also practicing another trade.
D: Distilled: If every craft requires lifelong specialization, warfare -- the most important craft -- demands it most of all.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- The argument from specialization is now applied to create the guardian (phylax) class -- 'Dice or draught player': board games (pessoi, petteia) used as analogies for skill requiring dedicated practice -- The comparison of warfare to shoemaking is deliberately provocative: it elevates craft-knowledge while challenging aristocratic assumptions about natural military prowess -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (374d-376c), where the qualities of the guardian nature are described -- Cross-reference: Laches 181a-184c, where the value of military training is discussed -- This argument is the birth of the professional military class in political philosophy

D: Original: No tools will make a man a skilled workman, or master of defence, nor be of any use to him who has not learned how to handle them, and has never bestowed any attention upon them. How then will he who takes up a shield or other implement of war become a good fighter all in a day, whether with heavy-armed or any other kind of troops?
D: Narrative: "No tools will make a man a skilled workman, or master of defence, nor be of any use to him who has not learned how to handle them, and has never bestowed any attention upon them. How then will he who takes up a shield or other implement of war become a good fighter all in a day, whether with heavy-armed or any other kind of troops?"
D: Refashioned: "No set of tools will make someone a skilled craftsman or a capable defender if they have never learned to use them and have never practiced. How, then, can someone pick up a shield or any other weapon of war and become a competent soldier overnight -- whether as a heavy infantryman or in any other role?"
D: Elegant: Tools alone do not create skill. Without training and practice, picking up a shield does not make a soldier -- no more than picking up a hammer makes a carpenter. Professional warfare requires professional preparation.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that tools alone cannot create competence: without dedicated training, picking up a shield no more makes someone a soldier than picking up tools makes someone a craftsman.
D: Distilled: Tools without training are useless -- you cannot become a soldier by simply picking up a shield.
Notes: Socrates speaking -- This short passage reinforces the specialization argument for a dedicated military class -- 'Heavy-armed' (hoplites): the standard Greek citizen-soldier; Socrates is arguing against the Athenian practice of citizen-militia warfare -- The argument challenges Athenian democratic military practice, where citizens were expected to serve as soldiers -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (374d-e), guardian selection criteria -- Cross-reference: Laches, which is entirely devoted to the question of what courage is and how it relates to military expertise -- This completes the logical case for a professional guardian class distinct from the productive class

Yes, he said, the tools which would teach men their own use would be beyond price.

And the higher the duties of the guardian, I said, the more time, and skill, and art, and application will be needed by him?

No doubt, he replied. Will he not also require natural aptitude for his calling?

Certainly. Then it will be our duty to select, if we can, natures which are fitted for the task of guarding the city?

It will. And the selection will be no easy matter, I said; but we must be brave and do our best.

We must. Is not the noble youth very like a well-bred dog in respect of guarding and watching?

What do you mean? I mean that both of them ought to be quick to see, and swift to overtake the enemy when they see him; and strong too if, when they have caught him, they have to fight with him.

All these qualities, he replied, will certainly be required by them.

Well, and your guardian must be brave if he is to fight well?

Certainly. And is he likely to be brave who has no spirit, whether horse or dog or any other animal? Have you never observed how invincible and unconquerable is spirit and how the presence of it makes the soul of any creature to be absolutely fearless and indomitable?

I have. Then now we have a clear notion of the bodily qualities which are required in the guardian.

True. And also of the mental ones; his soul is to be full of spirit?

Yes. But are not these spirited natures apt to be savage with one another, and with everybody else?

A difficulty by no means easy to overcome, he replied. Whereas, I said, they ought to be dangerous to their enemies, and gentle to their friends; if not, they will destroy themselves without waiting for their enemies to destroy them.

True, he said. What is to be done then? I said; how shall we find a gentle nature which has also a great spirit, for the one is the contradiction of the other?

"All these qualities," he replied, "will certainly be required by them."

"Well, and your guardian must be brave if he is to fight well?"

"Certainly."

"I have."

"Then now we have a clear notion of the bodily qualities which are required in the guardian."

"True."

"And also of the mental ones; his soul is to be full of spirit?"

"Yes."

"But are not these spirited natures apt to be savage with one another, and with everybody else?"

"A difficulty by no means easy to overcome," he replied.

"Whereas," I said, "they ought to be dangerous to their enemies, and gentle to their friends; if not, they will destroy themselves without waiting for their enemies to destroy them."

D: Refashioned:
"Yes," Glaucon said, "tools that could teach their own use would be priceless."

"And the greater the guardian's responsibilities," I said, "the more time, skill, craft, and dedication they require."

"No doubt."

"So guardians must also have a natural aptitude for the role?"

"Of course."

"Then we must select -- if we can -- those natures fitted for guarding the city."

"We must."

"This will not be easy, but we have to try our best."

"Agreed."

"Think of it this way: isn't a noble young guardian very much like a well-bred dog?"

"How so?"

"Both need sharp perception to spot the enemy, speed to pursue, and strength to fight when they catch up."

"Those qualities are certainly necessary."

"And the guardian must be courageous to fight well?"

"Obviously."

"But can anyone be brave who lacks spirit -- whether horse, dog, or any other creature? Have you noticed that spirit makes the soul of any living thing absolutely fearless and unconquerable?"

"I have."

"So we now know the physical requirements: perception, speed, strength. And the mental requirement: a spirited soul."

"Yes."

"But here is the problem: spirited natures tend to be aggressive -- savage toward everyone, not just enemies."

"A serious difficulty," he admitted.

"Yet guardians must be fierce toward enemies and gentle toward friends. Otherwise they will destroy each other before the enemy has a chance to."

"True."

"So how do we find a nature that combines gentleness with great spirit? The two seem to contradict each other."
D: Elegant: The guardian needs natural aptitude: sharp perception, speed, strength, and above all, spirit -- the quality that makes any creature fearless. But spirited natures tend toward savagery. Guardians must be fierce to enemies yet gentle to friends, or they will destroy themselves. The apparent contradiction: how can one nature combine gentleness with great spirit?
D: Summary: Socrates identifies the guardian's required qualities -- keen perception, speed, strength, and spiritedness -- but faces a paradox: spirited natures tend toward aggression against everyone. Guardians must be fierce toward enemies yet gentle toward friends, creating an apparent contradiction that must be resolved.
D: Distilled: The guardian paradox: they must be spirited enough to be fearless in battle yet gentle enough not to destroy their own people.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- The guardian-as-dog analogy: one of the Republic's most sustained metaphors, developed through 2.41-2.43 -- 'Spirit' (thumos): the spirited or passionate element of the soul, which in Book IV becomes the middle part of the tripartite soul -- The fierce-to-enemies, gentle-to-friends formula echoes the traditional Greek ethical code (cf. Republic Book I, 332d) -- The paradox of combining gentleness and spiritedness prepares for the introduction of the philosophical nature -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (439e-441c), the tripartite soul: reason, spirit, appetite -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (375a-376c), continuation of guardian nature discussion

True. He will not be a good guardian who is wanting in either of these two qualities; and yet the combination of them appears to be impossible; and hence we must infer that to be a good guardian is impossible.

I am afraid that what you say is true, he replied. Here feeling perplexed I began to think over what had preceded. My friend, I said, no wonder that we are in a perplexity; for we have lost sight of the image which we had before us.

What do you mean? he said. I mean to say that there do exist natures gifted with those opposite qualities.

And where do you find them? Many animals, I replied, furnish examples of them; our friend the dog is a very good one: you know that well-bred dogs are perfectly gentle to their familiars and acquaintances, and the reverse to strangers.

Yes, I know. Then there is nothing impossible or out of the order of nature in our finding a guardian who has a similar combination of qualities?

Certainly not. Would not he who is fitted to be a guardian, besides the spirited nature, need to have the qualities of a philosopher?

I do not apprehend your meaning. The trait of which I am speaking, I replied, may be also seen in the dog, and is remarkable in the animal.

What trait? Why, a dog, whenever he sees a stranger, is angry; when an acquaintance, he welcomes him, although the one has never done him any harm, nor the other any good. Did this never strike you as curious?

The matter never struck me before; but I quite recognise the truth of your remark.

And surely this instinct of the dog is very charming; --your dog is a true philosopher.

Why? Why, because he distinguishes the face of a friend and of an enemy only by the criterion of knowing and not knowing. And must not an animal be a lover of learning who determines what he likes and dislikes by the test of knowledge and ignorance?

Most assuredly. And is not the love of learning the love of wisdom, which is philosophy?

"And surely this instinct of the dog is very charming -- your dog is a true philosopher."

"Why, because he distinguishes the face of a friend and of an enemy only by the criterion of knowing and not knowing. And must not an animal be a lover of learning who determines what he likes and dislikes by the test of knowledge and ignorance?"

D: Refashioned:
"A guardian lacking either quality -- gentleness or spirit -- will not serve. But combining them seems impossible, which would make a good guardian impossible."

"I am afraid you are right," Glaucon said.

Feeling stuck, I reconsidered the problem.

"Wait," I said. "We have lost sight of something obvious. There are natures that combine these opposite qualities."

"Where?"

"In many animals. Take the well-bred dog: perfectly gentle with people it knows, and hostile to strangers."

"True enough."

"So there is nothing unnatural about finding a guardian with the same combination."

"Agreed."

"But the ideal guardian needs something beyond spirit. They need to be, in a sense, a philosopher."

"What do you mean?"

"Look at the dog again. There is a remarkable trait: a dog is hostile to every stranger and friendly to every acquaintance -- regardless of whether the stranger has ever harmed it or the acquaintance ever helped it. Have you noticed this?"

"Now that you mention it, yes."

"This makes the dog a natural philosopher."

"How?"

"Because the dog distinguishes friend from enemy purely by the criterion of knowing versus not-knowing. Any creature that sorts the world based on knowledge and ignorance must be called a lover of learning. And what is the love of learning if not the love of wisdom -- philosophy?"
D: Elegant: The paradox is resolved by the example of the well-bred dog, which is naturally gentle with those it knows and fierce with strangers. The dog distinguishes friend from foe not by who has helped or harmed it, but by the criterion of knowledge versus ignorance. This makes the dog, in a sense, a philosopher -- a lover of learning. The ideal guardian must likewise combine spirit with a philosophical nature.
D: Summary: Socrates resolves the guardian paradox with the analogy of the dog: well-bred dogs are gentle to those they know and hostile to strangers, distinguishing friend from foe by knowledge alone. Since the dog sorts the world by knowing versus not-knowing, it is a natural philosopher -- and the guardian must similarly combine spiritedness with a love of wisdom.
D: Distilled: The dog resolves the paradox: it is gentle to the known and fierce to the unknown, making it a natural philosopher -- and the model for combining spirit with the love of wisdom.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- The 'philosophical dog' is one of the Republic's most famous (and debated) arguments -- some scholars consider it playful, others see it as a serious point about the connection between knowledge and virtue -- 'Love of learning is love of wisdom (philosophia)': Socrates derives the guardian's need for philosophy from the dog analogy -- The criterion of knowing/not-knowing connects to the Republic's epistemology (Book V, 475e-480a: the philosopher loves knowledge, the non-philosopher loves opinion) -- Cross-reference: Republic Book V (474b-480a), the nature of the philosopher -- Cross-reference: Republic Book VI (485a-487a), further development of the philosophical nature -- The move from thumos (spirit) to philosophia (love of wisdom) anticipates the tripartite soul and the dominance of reason

D: Original:
They are the same, he replied. And may we not say confidently of man also, that he who is likely to be gentle to his friends and acquaintances, must by nature be a lover of wisdom and knowledge?

That we may safely affirm. Then he who is to be a really good and noble guardian of the State will require to unite in himself philosophy and spirit and swiftness and strength?

Undoubtedly. Then we have found the desired natures; and now that we have found them, how are they to be reared and educated? D: Narrative:
"They are the same," he replied.

"And may we not say confidently of man also, that he who is likely to be gentle to his friends and acquaintances, must by nature be a lover of wisdom and knowledge?"

"That we may safely affirm."

"Then he who is to be a really good and noble guardian of the State will require to unite in himself philosophy and spirit and swiftness and strength?"

"Undoubtedly."

"Then we have found the desired natures; and now that we have found them, how are they to be reared and educated? Is not this enquiry which may be expected to throw light on the greater enquiry which is our final end -- How do justice and injustice grow up in States? For we do not want either to omit what is to the point or to draw out the argument to an inconvenient length."
D: Refashioned:
"They are the same," Glaucon agreed.

"And we can say the same about human beings: a person who is naturally gentle toward friends must also be a natural lover of wisdom and knowledge."

"We can safely say that."

"So the ideal guardian must unite in one person: philosophy, spirit, speed, and strength."

"Without question."

"Good -- we have identified the right natures. The next question is: how should they be raised and educated? This inquiry should shed light on the larger question that is our ultimate goal -- how do justice and injustice develop in states? We want to be thorough without being unnecessarily long-winded."
D: Elegant: The ideal guardian must unite four qualities: philosophy (love of wisdom), spirit (courage), swiftness, and strength. Having identified these natures, the question becomes how to educate them -- an inquiry that should illuminate how justice and injustice develop in states.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes that the ideal guardian must combine philosophy, spirit, speed, and strength. With the right natures identified, the investigation now turns to education -- which should reveal how justice and injustice grow within states.
D: Distilled: The guardian must unite philosophy, spirit, speed, and strength -- and the question of how to educate such natures will reveal how justice and injustice develop.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- The four guardian qualities: philosophia (love of wisdom), thumos (spirit/courage), tachos (swiftness), ischus (strength) -- The transition from identifying natures to educating them marks the beginning of the Republic's educational program (Books II-III) -- 'How do justice and injustice grow up in States?': restates the central research question -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (376c-412b), the detailed curriculum for guardians -- Cross-reference: Republic Book VII (521c-541b), the higher education of philosopher-kings -- The move from nature to nurture: good natures must be properly educated, or they can become the worst (cf. Republic Book VI, 491d-492a)

D: Original:
Adeimantus thought that the enquiry would be of great service to us.

Then, I said, my dear friend, the task must not be given up, even if somewhat long.

Certainly not. Come then, and let us pass a leisure hour in story-telling, and our story shall be the education of our heroes.

By all means. And what shall be their education? Can we find a better than the traditional sort? --and this has two divisions, gymnastic for the body, and music for the soul.

True. Shall we begin education with music, and go on to gymnastic afterwards?

By all means. And when you speak of music, do you include literature or not?

I do. And literature may be either true or false? Yes. And the young should be trained in both kinds, and we begin with the false?

I do not understand your meaning, he said. You know, I said, that we begin by telling children stories which, though not wholly destitute of truth, are in the main fictitious; and these stories are told them when they are not of an age to learn gymnastics.

Very true. That was my meaning when I said that we must teach music before gymnastics.

Quite right, he said. You know also that the beginning is the most important part of any work, especially in the case of a young and tender thing; for that is the time at which the character is being formed and the desired impression is more readily taken.

Quite true. And shall we just carelessly allow children to hear any casual tales which may be devised by casual persons, and to receive into their minds ideas for the most part the very opposite of those which we should wish them to have when they are grown up?

We cannot. Then the first thing will be to establish a censorship of the writers of fiction, and let the censors receive any tale of fiction which is good, and reject the bad; and we will desire mothers and nurses to tell their children the authorised ones only. Let them fashion the mind with such tales, even more fondly than they mould the body with their hands; but most of those which are now in use must be discarded.

D: Refashioned:
Adeimantus agreed this inquiry would be very useful.

"Then we must not abandon it, even if it takes time," I said.

"Absolutely not."

"Let us spend some time telling a story -- and the story will be the education of our guardians."

"Let us."

"What should their education look like? The traditional system has two parts: physical training for the body, and music for the soul. Can we improve on this?"

"That seems right."

"Should we start with music and move to physical training later?"

"Yes."

"When I say 'music,' I include literature. And literature can be either true or fictional."

"Right."

"We train the young in both kinds, beginning with fiction."

"What do you mean?" Adeimantus asked.

"We start teaching children with stories -- stories that are mostly fictional, though they contain elements of truth. These stories are told at an age when children are too young for physical training."

"That is true."

"This is why we must begin with music before gymnastics."

"Makes sense."

"And you know that the beginning of any work is the most important part -- especially with something young and impressionable. Early childhood is when character is formed and impressions are most easily stamped."

"Absolutely."

"So should we carelessly let children hear any random stories invented by random people, absorbing ideas that are often the exact opposite of what we want them to believe as adults?"

"We cannot."

"Then the first step is to establish oversight of fiction writers. Censors will approve good stories and reject bad ones. Mothers and nurses should tell children only the approved stories. Let them shape the mind with stories even more carefully than they shape the body with their hands. Most of the stories currently in use must be thrown out."
D: Elegant: Education begins with music (including literature), since stories shape character in early childhood when impressions are most lasting. Children must not be exposed to random tales that instill wrong ideas. Fiction writers must be supervised: good stories approved, bad ones rejected. Stories shape the mind even more powerfully than hands shape the body, and most current stories must be discarded.
D: Summary: Socrates begins designing guardian education with the traditional two-part curriculum: music (including literature) for the soul and gymnastics for the body, starting with music. Since early childhood stories powerfully shape character, fiction must be censored -- only approved stories should be told to children, and most existing tales must be discarded.
D: Distilled: Education begins with stories because early impressions shape character most powerfully -- so fiction must be censored to ensure children absorb the right ideas.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- 'Music' (mousike): encompasses all arts presided over by the Muses, including literature, poetry, and song -- not just instrumental music -- 'Gymnastic' (gymnastike): physical training -- The 'beginning is the most important part': a principle Socrates applies to education, politics, and philosophy -- 'Censorship of fiction': one of the Republic's most controversial proposals -- Plato is serious about controlling narrative -- 'Fashioning the mind with tales': the metaphor of the soul as soft material being shaped -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (386a-403c), detailed censorship guidelines -- Cross-reference: Republic Book X (595a-608b), the famous banishment of poets -- Cross-reference: Laws Book VII (801c-802e), similar proposals for literary censorship

Of what tales are you speaking? he said. You may find a model of the lesser in the greater, I said; for they are necessarily of the same type, and there is the same spirit in both of them.

Very likely, he replied; but I do not as yet know what you would term the greater.

Those, I said, which are narrated by Homer and Hesiod, and the rest of the poets, who have ever been the great story-tellers of mankind.

But which stories do you mean, he said; and what fault do you find with them?

A fault which is most serious, I said; the fault of telling a lie, and, what is more, a bad lie.

But when is this fault committed? Whenever an erroneous representation is made of the nature of gods and heroes, --as when a painter paints a portrait not having the shadow of a likeness to the original.

D: Narrative:
"Of what tales are you speaking?" he said.

"You may find a model of the lesser in the greater," I said; "for they are necessarily of the same type, and there is the same spirit in both of them."

"Very likely," he replied; "but I do not as yet know what you would term the greater."

"Those," I said, "which are narrated by Homer and Hesiod, and the rest of the poets, who have ever been the great story-tellers of mankind."

"But which stories do you mean," he said; "and what fault do you find with them?"

"A fault which is most serious," I said; "the fault of telling a lie, and, what is more, a bad lie."

"But when is this fault committed?"

"Whenever an erroneous representation is made of the nature of gods and heroes -- as when a painter paints a portrait not having the shadow of a likeness to the original."

"Yes," he said, "that sort of thing is certainly very blamable; but what are the stories which you mean?"
D: Refashioned:
"Which tales are you referring to?" Adeimantus asked.

"The smaller stories follow the pattern of the larger ones -- they share the same character and spirit."

"Probably so, but which are the larger stories?"

"The ones told by Homer, Hesiod, and the other great poets -- the master storytellers of the human race."

"What stories specifically, and what is wrong with them?"

"The most serious fault possible: they tell lies -- and bad lies at that."

"When does this happen?"

"Whenever they misrepresent the nature of gods and heroes. It is like a painter creating a portrait that bears no resemblance whatsoever to the original subject."

"That is certainly blameworthy. But which stories do you mean specifically?"
D: Elegant: The problematic stories are those of Homer, Hesiod, and the great poets -- humanity's master storytellers. Their fault: they tell lies about the nature of gods and heroes, creating portraits bearing no resemblance to the originals. This is the most serious kind of falsehood.
D: Summary: Socrates identifies the problematic stories as those of Homer, Hesiod, and the other major poets, whose most serious fault is misrepresenting the nature of gods and heroes -- telling lies that bear no resemblance to the truth, like a portrait nothing like its subject.
D: Distilled: Homer and Hesiod commit the gravest fault: lying about the nature of gods and heroes through false representations.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- Homer and Hesiod: the two foundational authorities on Greek religion and mythology -- 'The fault of telling a lie, and a bad lie': Plato distinguishes between harmless fiction and harmful misrepresentation -- The painter analogy: a false portrait of the gods is worse than no portrait at all -- 'The lesser in the greater': small nursery tales follow the same theological patterns as the great epics -- Cross-reference: Republic Book X (595a-602b), where the mimetic arts (including poetry and painting) are criticized for being 'three removes from truth' -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (386a-392c), where specific censorship guidelines are laid out -- This passage begins Plato's theology: what can and cannot be said about the gods

D: Original: First of all, I said, there was that greatest of all lies, in high places, which the poet told about Uranus, and which was a bad lie too, --I mean what Hesiod says that Uranus did, and how Cronus retaliated on him. The doings of Cronus, and the sufferings which in turn his son inflicted upon him, even if they were true, ought certainly not to be lightly told to young and thoughtless persons; if possible, they had better be buried in silence. But if there is an absolute necessity for their mention, a chosen few might hear them in a mystery, and they should sacrifice not a common [Eleusinian] pig, but some huge and unprocurable victim; and then the number of the hearers will be very few indeed.
D: Narrative:
"First of all," I said, "there was that greatest of all lies, in high places, which the poet told about Uranus, and which was a bad lie too -- I mean what Hesiod says that Uranus did, and how Cronus retaliated on him. The doings of Cronus, and the sufferings which in turn his son inflicted upon him, even if they were true, ought certainly not to be lightly told to young and thoughtless persons; if possible, they had better be buried in silence.

"But if there is an absolute necessity for their mention, a chosen few might hear them in a mystery, and they should sacrifice not a common [Eleusinian] pig, but some huge and unprocurable victim; and then the number of the hearers will be very few indeed."
D: Refashioned:
"The greatest and worst of these lies," I said, "is the story Hesiod tells about Uranus -- what Uranus did and how Cronus retaliated against him. And then what Cronus's son Zeus did to him in turn. Even if these stories were true, they should never be casually told to young, impressionable minds. Ideally, they should be buried in total silence.

"If they absolutely must be mentioned, they should be restricted to a select few, told within the secrecy of a mystery rite -- and the price of admission should not be the common Eleusinian pig, but some enormous and nearly impossible-to-obtain sacrificial animal. That way the audience would be vanishingly small."
D: Elegant: The worst theological lies are Hesiod's stories of divine succession: Uranus's crimes, Cronus's retaliation, Zeus's overthrow of Cronus. Even if true, such stories of divine violence should be suppressed entirely or restricted to the tiniest possible audience through prohibitively expensive mystery rites.
D: Summary: Socrates singles out Hesiod's stories of divine succession -- Uranus, Cronus, and Zeus violently overthrowing each other -- as the worst theological lies. Even if true, they should be suppressed or restricted to the smallest possible audience through exclusive, costly mystery rites.
D: Distilled: Stories of gods violently overthrowing their fathers must be suppressed -- they are the worst possible models for the young.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- Hesiod, Theogony (154-210, 453-506): Uranus imprisoned his children; Cronus castrated Uranus; Zeus overthrew Cronus -- 'Greatest of all lies in high places': theological falsehoods are the most dangerous because they concern the highest subjects -- Eleusinian pig: initiates at the Eleusinian Mysteries sacrificed a pig as the standard offering; Socrates's joke is that the price should be impossibly high -- 'Buried in silence': suppression as the first option, restricted access as the fallback -- Cross-reference: Euthyphro 5e-6a, where Euthyphro cites these divine conflicts to justify his prosecution of his father -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (386a-391e), where more specific censorship rules are developed -- The divine succession myth was central to Greek religion; censoring it would be revolutionary

Why, yes, said he, those stories are extremely objectionable.

Yes, Adeimantus, they are stories not to be repeated in our State; the young man should not be told that in committing the worst of crimes he is far from doing anything outrageous; and that even if he chastises his father when does wrong, in whatever manner, he will only be following the example of the first and greatest among the gods.

I entirely agree with you, he said; in my opinion those stories are quite unfit to be repeated.

"Why, yes," said he, "those stories are extremely objectionable."

"Yes, Adeimantus, they are stories not to be repeated in our State; the young man should not be told that in committing the worst of crimes he is far from doing anything outrageous; and that even if he chastises his father when he does wrong, in whatever manner, he will only be following the example of the first and greatest among the gods."

"I entirely agree with you," he said; "in my opinion those stories are quite unfit to be repeated."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," Adeimantus said, "those stories are deeply objectionable."

"They must not be told in our state. A young person should never hear that committing the worst crimes is no big deal, or that if he punishes his own father -- in any way whatsoever -- he is simply following the example of the first and greatest of the gods."

"I completely agree," he said. "Those stories are entirely unfit to be repeated."
D: Elegant: Stories of divine violence against parents must be banned: they teach young people that the worst crimes are acceptable because even the greatest gods committed them. If Zeus overthrew his father, why shouldn't a son punish his?
D: Summary: Socrates argues that stories of gods committing violence against their parents must be banned from the state, because they teach young people that even the worst crimes are acceptable when modeled by divine example.
D: Distilled: Stories of gods attacking their parents must be banned -- they make the worst crimes seem acceptable by divine precedent.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- The practical danger: if the gods set the example of filial violence, young people will feel justified in similar behavior -- 'Chastises his father': this was a serious crime in Athenian law -- Cross-reference: Euthyphro, where Euthyphro's prosecution of his father is motivated by precisely the kind of reasoning Socrates warns against here -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (390b-d), further examples of harmful divine models -- The principle: stories shape behavior, especially in the young, and divine exemplars carry the most authority

D: Original: Neither, if we mean our future guardians to regard the habit of quarrelling among themselves as of all things the basest, should any word be said to them of the wars in heaven, and of the plots and fightings of the gods against one another, for they are not true. No, we shall never mention the battles of the giants, or let them be embroidered on garments; and we shall be silent about the innumerable other quarrels of gods and heroes with their friends and relatives. If they would only believe us we would tell them that quarrelling is unholy, and that never up to this time has there been any, quarrel between citizens; this is what old men and old women should begin by telling children; and when they grow up, the poets also should be told to compose for them in a similar spirit. But the narrative of Hephaestus binding Here his mother, or how on another occasion Zeus sent him flying for taking her part when she was being beaten, and all the battles of the gods in Homer --these tales must not be admitted into our State, whether they are supposed to have an allegorical meaning or not. For a young person cannot judge what is allegorical and what is literal; anything that he receives into his mind at that age is likely to become indelible and unalterable; and therefore it is most important that the tales which the young first hear should be models of virtuous thoughts.
D: Narrative:
"Neither, if we mean our future guardians to regard the habit of quarrelling among themselves as of all things the basest, should any word be said to them of the wars in heaven, and of the plots and fightings of the gods against one another, for they are not true.

"No, we shall never mention the battles of the giants, or let them be embroidered on garments; and we shall be silent about the innumerable other quarrels of gods and heroes with their friends and relatives.

"If they would only believe us we would tell them that quarrelling is unholy, and that never up to this time has there been any quarrel between citizens; this is what old men and old women should begin by telling children; and when they grow up, the poets also should be told to compose for them in a similar spirit.

"But the narrative of Hephaestus binding Hera his mother, or how on another occasion Zeus sent him flying for taking her part when she was being beaten, and all the battles of the gods in Homer -- these tales must not be admitted into our State, whether they are supposed to have an allegorical meaning or not.

"For a young person cannot judge what is allegorical and what is literal; anything that he receives into his mind at that age is likely to become indelible and unalterable; and therefore it is most important that the tales which the young first hear should be models of virtuous thoughts."
D: Refashioned:
"If we want our guardians to consider internal conflict the most shameful thing possible, they must never hear about wars among the gods or plots of divine beings against each other -- because these stories are not true.

"We will never mention the battles of the giants. They will not appear embroidered on clothing. We will say nothing about the countless quarrels between gods and heroes and their own friends and family.

"Ideally, we would teach children from the very start that quarreling is unholy, and that citizens have never fought among themselves. This is what the elderly should tell children first, and what poets should compose as children grow up.

"The story of Hephaestus binding his mother Hera, Zeus hurling Hephaestus from heaven for defending her, and all the divine battles in Homer -- none of these can be admitted into our state. It does not matter whether they are meant allegorically.

"A young person cannot tell allegory from literal truth. Whatever enters the mind at that age tends to become permanent and unalterable. Therefore the first stories children hear must be models of virtuous thinking."
D: Elegant: Guardians must never hear stories of divine conflict -- wars among gods, plots against family members, the battles of giants. Even allegory is no defense, because children cannot distinguish allegorical from literal meaning. Whatever enters a young mind becomes permanent. The first stories children hear must therefore be models of virtue.
D: Summary: All stories of divine and heroic conflict must be banned from guardian education -- including Homer's battles among the gods -- because young people cannot distinguish allegory from literal truth. Whatever enters a child's mind becomes permanent, so their first stories must model virtuous thinking.
D: Distilled: Children cannot distinguish allegory from literal truth, so their first stories must model virtue -- all tales of divine conflict must be banned.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- Hephaestus binding Hera: Iliad 1.586-594 -- Zeus hurling Hephaestus: Iliad 1.590-594 -- 'Battles of the giants' (Gigantomachy): a popular mythological subject depicted on the Parthenon and on clothing -- 'Embroidered on garments': the Panathenaic peplos (robe) woven for Athena depicted the Gigantomachy -- 'Allegorical meaning': Plato dismisses the allegorical defense of myth, which was common among pre-Socratic philosophers and Sophists -- 'Indelible and unalterable': a strong claim about early childhood impressions -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (386a-392c), continuation of censorship guidelines -- Cross-reference: Republic Book X (595a-608b), the final critique and banishment of poetry -- This passage is foundational for debates about censorship, children's media, and the power of narrative

D: Original:
There you are right, he replied; but if any one asks where are such models to be found and of what tales are you speaking --how shall we answer him?

I said to him, You and I, Adeimantus, at this moment are not poets, but founders of a State: now the founders of a State ought to know the general forms in which poets should cast their tales, and the limits which must be observed by them, but to make the tales is not their business.

Very true, he said; but what are these forms of theology which you mean?

Something of this kind, I replied: --God is always to be represented as he truly is, whatever be the sort of poetry, epic, lyric or tragic, in which the representation is given.

Right. And is he not truly good? and must he not be represented as such?

Certainly. And no good thing is hurtful? No, indeed. And that which is not hurtful hurts not? Certainly not. And that which hurts not does no evil? No. And can that which does no evil be a cause of evil? Impossible. And the good is advantageous? Yes. And therefore the cause of well-being? Yes. It follows therefore that the good is not the cause of all things, but of the good only?

Assuredly. Then God, if he be good, is not the author of all things, as the many assert, but he is the cause of a few things only, and not of most things that occur to men. For few are the goods of human life, and many are the evils, and the good is to be attributed to God alone; of the evils the causes are to be sought elsewhere, and not in him.
D: Narrative:
"There you are right," he replied; "but if any one asks where are such models to be found and of what tales are you speaking -- how shall we answer him?"

I said to him: "You and I, Adeimantus, at this moment are not poets, but founders of a State: now the founders of a State ought to know the general forms in which poets should cast their tales, and the limits which must be observed by them, but to make the tales is not their business."

"Very true," he said; "but what are these forms of theology which you mean?"

"Something of this kind," I replied: "God is always to be represented as he truly is, whatever be the sort of poetry, epic, lyric or tragic, in which the representation is given."

"Right."

"And is he not truly good? And must he not be represented as such?"

"Certainly."

"And no good thing is hurtful?"

"No, indeed."

"And that which is not hurtful hurts not?"

"Certainly not."

"And that which hurts not does no evil?"

"No."

"And can that which does no evil be a cause of evil?"

"Impossible."

"And the good is advantageous?"

"Yes."

"And therefore the cause of well-being?"

"Yes."

"It follows therefore that the good is not the cause of all things, but of the good only?"

"Assuredly."

"Then God, if he be good, is not the author of all things, as the many assert, but he is the cause of a few things only, and not of most things that occur to men. For few are the goods of human life, and many are the evils, and the good is to be attributed to God alone; of the evils the causes are to be sought elsewhere, and not in him."
D: Refashioned:
"You are right," Adeimantus said. "But if someone asks where these model stories can be found, what do we say?"

"You and I are not poets, Adeimantus -- we are founders of a state. Founders need to know the general principles that poets must follow and the boundaries they must respect. Actually writing the stories is not our job."

"Fair enough. But what are these theological principles?"

"This: God must always be represented as he truly is, in every form of poetry -- epic, lyric, or tragic."

"Agreed."

"And God is truly good? And must be represented as such?"

"Of course."

"Now: nothing that is good is harmful."

"Correct."

"What is not harmful does not cause harm."

"Right."

"What causes no harm does no evil."

"True."

"And what does no evil cannot be a cause of evil."

"Impossible."

"The good is beneficial -- the cause of well-being."

"Yes."

"Therefore the good is not the cause of everything, but only of good things."

"Necessarily."

"Then God, being good, is not the author of all things -- as most people believe. God is the cause of only a few things that happen to human beings: the good things. The evil things, which are far more numerous, must have causes elsewhere. They cannot come from God."
D: Elegant: Founders of a state set the theological principles that poets must follow. The first principle: God must be represented as truly good. Through a chain of reasoning -- good is not harmful, what is not harmful does no evil, what does no evil cannot cause evil -- Socrates concludes that God is not the author of all things, but only of the good. Evil, which far outnumbers good in human life, must have other causes.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes the first principle of theology for the ideal state: God is good and must be represented as such. Since the good cannot cause evil, God is the author only of good things. The many evils of human life must have causes other than God.
D: Distilled: First theological principle: God is good and therefore the cause of good only -- evil must come from other sources.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- This is the first of two 'types' (tupoi) of theology Socrates establishes -- 'Not poets but founders': the statesman sets the framework, the artist works within it -- The chain argument (good > not harmful > no evil > not a cause of evil) is a classic Socratic logical chain -- 'God is not the author of all things': this directly contradicts popular Greek theology and Homer's portrayal of Zeus -- 'Few are the goods, many the evils': a pessimistic assessment of human life common in Greek literature -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (379a-383c), where these two theological types are fully developed -- Cross-reference: Timaeus 29e-30a, where the Demiurge creates only good -- Cross-reference: Laws Book X (899d-905d), where Plato argues the gods are good and not negligent -- This passage is foundational for Western philosophical theology and the problem of evil (theodicy)

D: Narrative:
"That appears to me to be most true," he said.

"Then we must not listen to Homer or to any other poet who is guilty of the folly of saying that

'Two casks lie at the threshold of Zeus, full of lots, one of good, the other of evil lots,'

and that he to whom Zeus gives a mixture of the two

D: Refashioned:
"That seems absolutely right," Adeimantus said.

"Then we must reject Homer and every other poet who foolishly claims that 'two jars sit at Zeus's threshold, one filled with good fortunes, the other with bad,' and that whoever receives a mixture from Zeus 'sometimes meets with misfortune, sometimes with good luck,' while whoever receives only from the jar of evil is 'driven by wild hunger across the beautiful earth.'

"And further --"
D: Elegant: Homer's image of Zeus dispensing good and evil from two jars must be rejected. If God is good and causes only good, he cannot be a source of evil fortune -- whether mixed with good or unmixed.
D: Summary: Socrates rejects Homer's famous image of Zeus distributing human fates from two jars -- one of good fortune, one of evil. Since God is good and can only cause good, the idea that Zeus sends evil fortune to mortals contradicts the first theological principle.
D: Distilled: Homer's two jars of Zeus -- dispensing both good and evil fortune -- must be rejected because God cannot be a source of evil.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- Homer, Iliad 24.527-532: Achilles tells Priam about Zeus's two jars, one of good and one of evil -- This passage was central to Greek popular theology and frequently quoted -- Plato's rejection of it is a direct challenge to Homeric authority -- 'Wild hunger drives o'er the beauteous earth': the fate of those who receive only evil from Zeus -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (379c-380c), where this passage is part of the systematic critique -- Cross-reference: Timaeus 29a-30b, where the creator god is purely good -- The argument has implications for the problem of evil in Western theology: if God is good, whence evil?

And if any one asserts that the violation of oaths and treaties, which was really the work of Pandarus, was brought about by Athene and Zeus, or that the strife and contention of the gods was instigated by Themis and Zeus, he shall not have our approval; neither will we allow our young men to hear the words of Aeschylus, that God plants guilt among men when he desires utterly to destroy a house. And if a poet writes of the sufferings of Niobe --the subject of the tragedy in which these iambic verses occur --or of the house of Pelops, or of the Trojan war or on any similar theme, either we must not permit him to say that these are the works of God, or if they are of God, he must devise some explanation of them such as we are seeking; he must say that God did what was just and right, and they were the better for being punished; but that those who are punished are miserable, and that God is the author of their misery --the poet is not to be permitted to say; though he may say that the wicked are miserable because they require to be punished, and are benefited by receiving punishment from God; but that God being good is the author of evil to any one is to be strenuously denied, and not to be said or sung or heard in verse or prose by any one whether old or young in any well-ordered commonwealth. Such a fiction is suicidal, ruinous, impious.

D: Refashioned:
"Nor can we accept the idea of Zeus as the dispenser of both good and evil.

"If anyone claims that Athena and Zeus caused Pandarus to break oaths and treaties -- when it was really Pandarus's own doing -- or that Zeus and Themis instigated strife among the gods, we will not approve. We will not let young people hear Aeschylus say that 'God plants guilt in men when he wishes to utterly destroy a house.'

"If a poet writes about the sufferings of Niobe, the house of Pelops, the Trojan War, or any similar theme, he must not say these calamities are God's doing. Or if he does attribute them to God, he must explain that God acted justly and that the punished were improved by their punishment. But the poet must never say that the punished are miserable and that God caused their misery. He may say the wicked suffer because they need punishment and benefit from it. But the claim that God, being good, is the author of evil to anyone must be vigorously denied. It must never be spoken, sung, or heard -- in verse or prose, by young or old -- in any well-governed state. Such a claim is self-destructive, ruinous, and impious."
D: Elegant: Poets must not attribute human wrongdoing to divine instigation, as Homer does with Pandarus, or claim God plants guilt in men to destroy them, as Aeschylus does. When depicting suffering, poets may say the wicked benefit from divine punishment, but never that God is the author of misery. The claim that God causes evil must be absolutely prohibited in any well-governed state -- it is suicidal, ruinous, and impious.
D: Summary: Socrates prohibits attributing human wrongdoing to divine causation and bars poets from saying God is the author of evil or misery. Punishment may be depicted as beneficial to the punished, but God causing evil must be denied absolutely in every form of expression throughout the state.
D: Distilled: Poets must never claim God causes evil or plants guilt in men -- such a fiction is self-destructive and impious.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- Pandarus: a Trojan who broke the truce (Iliad 4.86-126); Homer has Athena prompt him, but Socrates insists the responsibility was Pandarus's own -- Aeschylus, fragment from a lost play: 'God plants guilt (ate) among men when he wishes to destroy a house utterly' -- Niobe: mythological figure whose children were killed by Apollo and Artemis -- House of Pelops: the cursed house of Atreus (Agamemnon, Orestes, etc.) -- Themis: goddess of divine law and order -- 'Suicidal, ruinous, impious': the strongest language Socrates uses to condemn a theological claim -- The principle: human responsibility must not be displaced onto the divine -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (380a-383c), further development of theological principles -- Cross-reference: Euthyphro 6a-c, on whether the gods cause evil

D: Original:
I agree with you, he replied, and am ready to give my assent to the law.

Let this then be one of our rules and principles concerning the gods, to which our poets and reciters will be expected to conform --that God is not the author of all things, but of good only.

That will do, he said. And what do you think of a second principle? Shall I ask you whether God is a magician, and of a nature to appear insidiously now in one shape, and now in another --sometimes himself changing and passing into many forms, sometimes deceiving us with the semblance of such transformations; or is he one and the same immutably fixed in his own proper image?

I cannot answer you, he said, without more thought. Well, I said; but if we suppose a change in anything, that change must be effected either by the thing itself, or by some other thing?

Most certainly. And things which are at their best are also least liable to be altered or discomposed; for example, when healthiest and strongest, the human frame is least liable to be affected by meats and drinks, and the plant which is in the fullest vigour also suffers least from winds or the heat of the sun or any similar causes.

Of course. And will not the bravest and wisest soul be least confused or deranged by any external influence?

True. And the same principle, as I should suppose, applies to all composite things --furniture, houses, garments; when good and well made, they are least altered by time and circumstances.

Very true. Then everything which is good, whether made by art or nature, or both, is least liable to suffer change from without?

True. But surely God and the things of God are in every way perfect?

Of course they are. Then he can hardly be compelled by external influence to take many shapes?

He cannot. But may he not change and transform himself? Clearly, he said, that must be the case if he is changed at all.

And will he then change himself for the better and fairer, or for the worse and more unsightly?
D: Narrative:
"I agree with you," he replied, "and am ready to give my assent to the law."

"Let this then be one of our rules and principles concerning the gods, to which our poets and reciters will be expected to conform -- that God is not the author of all things, but of good only."

"That will do," he said.

"And what do you think of a second principle? Shall I ask you whether God is a magician, and of a nature to appear insidiously now in one shape, and now in another -- sometimes himself changing and passing into many forms, sometimes deceiving us with the semblance of such transformations; or is he one and the same immutably fixed in his own proper image?"

"I cannot answer you," he said, "without more thought."

"Well," I said; "but if we suppose a change in anything, that change must be effected either by the thing itself, or by some other thing?"

"Most certainly."

"And things which are at their best are also least liable to be altered or discomposed; for example, when healthiest and strongest, the human frame is least liable to be affected by meats and drinks, and the plant which is in the fullest vigour also suffers least from winds or the heat of the sun or any similar causes."

"Of course."

"And will not the bravest and wisest soul be least confused or deranged by any external influence?"

"True."

"And the same principle, as I should suppose, applies to all composite things -- furniture, houses, garments; when good and well made, they are least altered by time and circumstances."

"Very true."

"Then everything which is good, whether made by art or nature, or both, is least liable to suffer change from without?"

"True."

"But surely God and the things of God are in every way perfect?"

"Of course they are."

"Then he can hardly be compelled by external influence to take many shapes?"

"He cannot."

"But may he not change and transform himself?"

"Clearly," he said, "that must be the case if he is changed at all."

"And will he then change himself for the better and fairer, or for the worse and more unsightly?"
D: Refashioned:
"I agree," Adeimantus said. "I am ready to make this a law."

"Then here is our first theological principle for poets and performers: God is not the author of all things, but of good only."

"Agreed."

"Now for a second principle. Is God a shapeshifter -- appearing deceptively in different forms, sometimes actually changing, sometimes creating illusions of change? Or is God one and the same, immutably fixed in his own true form?"

"I need to think about that."

"Consider this: any change in something must be caused either by the thing itself or by something external."

"Obviously."

"And things at their peak condition are least affected by external forces. A body at peak health is least disturbed by food and drink. A plant at full vitality is least damaged by wind and sun."

"Of course."

"The bravest and wisest soul is least disturbed by external influences."

"True."

"This applies to all well-made things -- furniture, buildings, clothing. The better crafted they are, the less they change over time."

"Correct."

"So everything that is good, whether natural or crafted, is least subject to change from outside forces."

"True."

"And God and divine things are perfect in every way."

"They are."

"Then God cannot be compelled by external forces to change shape."

"No."

"Could God change himself voluntarily?"

"That would be the only possibility."

"Would such a self-change be for the better or for the worse?"
D: Elegant: The first theological law is established: God is not the author of all things, but of good only. The second inquiry begins: does God change form? Socrates argues that whatever is most perfect is least subject to change -- bodies at peak health, plants at full vigor, well-crafted objects. Since God is perfect, he cannot be changed by external forces. The only remaining possibility is self-change -- but in which direction?
D: Summary: With the first theological principle established (God causes only good), Socrates turns to the second: does God change form? Through a chain of reasoning -- that the more perfect a thing is, the less susceptible it is to change -- he argues that God cannot be changed externally. The question becomes whether God would voluntarily change himself.
D: Distilled: Second theological inquiry: since perfection is least subject to change, and God is perfect, God cannot be altered from without -- could he change himself?
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- This establishes the second tupos (type/principle) of theology: God is unchangeable -- The argument from perfection: what is best is least changeable -- applies universally from bodies to souls to artifacts to God -- 'God is a magician': refers to the many Homeric stories of gods disguising themselves (Zeus as a swan, Athena as Mentor, etc.) -- The question structure (external change vs. self-change) is methodical -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (380d-383c), completion of the second theological type -- Cross-reference: Parmenides fragments on the unchangeability of Being -- Cross-reference: Aristotle, Metaphysics XII (Unmoved Mover) -- This argument prefigures medieval philosophical theology on divine immutability (Aquinas, Summa Theologica I.9)

That necessarily follows, he said, in my judgment. Then, I said, my dear friend, let none of the poets tell us that

The gods, taking the disguise of strangers from other lands, walk up and down cities in all sorts of forms; and let no one slander Proteus and Thetis, neither let any one, either in tragedy or in any other kind of poetry, introduce Here disguised in the likeness of a priestess asking an alms
D: Narrative:
"If he change at all he can only change for the worse, for we cannot suppose him to be deficient either in virtue or beauty."

"Very true, Adeimantus; but then, would any one, whether God or man, desire to make himself worse?"

"Impossible."

"Then it is impossible that God should ever be willing to change; being, as is supposed, the fairest and best that is conceivable, every god remains absolutely and for ever in his own form."

"That necessarily follows," he said, "in my judgment."

"Then," I said, "my dear friend, let none of the poets tell us that

'The gods, taking the disguise of strangers from other lands, walk up and down cities in all sorts of forms;'

and let no one slander Proteus and Thetis, neither let any one, either in tragedy or in any other kind of poetry, introduce Hera disguised in the likeness of a priestess asking an alms --"
D: Refashioned:
"If God changed at all, it could only be for the worse -- since we cannot suppose God is deficient in either virtue or beauty."

"True, Adeimantus. But would anyone -- God or human -- willingly make themselves worse?"

"Impossible."

"Then it is impossible that God would ever willingly change. Being the fairest and best conceivable, every god remains absolutely and forever in his own true form."

"That follows necessarily," he agreed.

"Then let no poet tell us that 'the gods, disguised as strangers from foreign lands, walk through cities in every kind of shape.' Let no one defame Proteus and Thetis with tales of their transformations. Let no one, in tragedy or any other form of poetry, depict Hera disguised as a priestess begging for alms --"
D: Elegant: Since God is already perfect in virtue and beauty, any change could only be for the worse -- and no being willingly degrades itself. Therefore God is absolutely and eternally unchanging. All poetic stories of divine shapeshifting -- gods disguised as strangers, Proteus's transformations, Hera begging in disguise -- must be banned.
D: Summary: Socrates completes the argument for divine immutability: since God is already perfect, any change would be for the worse, and no being willingly becomes worse. Therefore God never changes form. All poetic tales of divine shapeshifting and disguise must be prohibited.
D: Distilled: God, being perfect, can only change for the worse -- but no being willingly degrades itself, so God is eternally unchanging and all stories of divine shapeshifting are false.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- The argument: (1) change from perfection can only be degradation; (2) no being willingly degrades itself; (3) therefore God never changes -- Homer, Odyssey 17.485-487: 'The gods, in the likeness of strangers from other lands, walk through cities in every shape' -- Proteus: the shape-shifting sea god (Odyssey 4.349-570) -- Thetis: sea-nymph, mother of Achilles, known for shapeshifting -- Hera disguised as a priestess: from a lost tragedy, possibly by Aeschylus -- 'Daughters of Inachus': reference continues in 2.54 -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (381b-382a), completion of the divine immutability argument -- Cross-reference: Symposium 211a-b, the Form of Beauty as unchanging -- The divine immutability principle becomes central to later Western theology

Neither must we have mothers under the influence of the poets scaring their children with a bad version of these myths --telling how certain gods, as they say, 'Go about by night in the likeness of so many strangers and in divers forms'; but let them take heed lest they make cowards of their children, and at the same time speak blasphemy against the gods.

"'For the life-giving daughters of Inachus the river of Argos;'

-- let us have no more lies of that sort. Neither must we have mothers under the influence of the poets scaring their children with a bad version of these myths -- telling how certain gods, as they say, 'Go about by night in the likeness of so many strangers and in divers forms;' but let them take heed lest they make cowards of their children, and at the same time speak blasphemy against the gods."

"-- 'for the life-giving daughters of Inachus, river of Argos.' No more lies like these.

"And mothers must not be influenced by poets into frightening their children with distorted versions of these myths -- telling them that gods 'wander by night in the guise of strangers and in all manner of shapes.' Such stories make cowards of children while committing blasphemy against the gods."
D: Elegant: Stories of gods wandering in disguise at night must end. Mothers who use such myths to frighten their children simultaneously create cowards and commit blasphemy against the gods.
D: Summary: Socrates condemns the practice of mothers using tales of shape-shifting gods to frighten children, arguing it both produces cowardice and constitutes blasphemy against the divine nature.
D: Distilled: Frightening children with tales of gods in disguise creates cowards and blasphemes the gods simultaneously.
Notes: Socrates speaking -- 'Daughters of Inachus': the Argive priestesses; this continues the Hera-as-priestess reference from 2.53 -- Inachus: the river god of Argos, father of Io -- 'Go about by night': Homer, Odyssey 17.485-487 again, now applied to the context of mothers scaring children -- The dual harm: psychological damage to children (cowardice) and theological error (blasphemy) -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (381d-e), completing the ban on stories of divine transformation -- The practical dimension: Plato is concerned not just with elite poetry but with everyday storytelling in the household -- Mothers and nurses are identified as primary vectors of harmful mythology

D: Original:
Heaven forbid, he said. But although the gods are themselves unchangeable, still by witchcraft and deception they may make us think that they appear in various forms?

Perhaps, he replied. Well, but can you imagine that God will be willing to lie, whether in word or deed, or to put forth a phantom of himself?

I cannot say, he replied. Do you not know, I said, that the true lie, if such an expression may be allowed, is hated of gods and men?

What do you mean? he said. I mean that no one is willingly deceived in that which is the truest and highest part of himself, or about the truest and highest matters; there, above all, he is most afraid of a lie having possession of him.

Still, he said, I do not comprehend you. The reason is, I replied, that you attribute some profound meaning to my words; but I am only saying that deception, or being deceived or uninformed about the highest realities in the highest part of themselves, which is the soul, and in that part of them to have and to hold the lie, is what mankind least like; --that, I say, is what they utterly detest.

There is nothing more hateful to them. And, as I was just now remarking, this ignorance in the soul of him who is deceived may be called the true lie; for the lie in words is only a kind of imitation and shadowy image of a previous affection of the soul, not pure unadulterated falsehood. Am I not right?

Perfectly right. The true lie is hated not only by the gods, but also by men?

"Perfectly right."

"The true lie is hated not only by the gods, but also by men?"
D: Refashioned:
"Heaven forbid," Adeimantus said.

"But even if the gods themselves do not actually change, could they use illusion or deception to make us think they appear in different forms?"

"Perhaps."

"But can you imagine God willingly lying -- in word, in deed, or by projecting a false image of himself?"

"I am not sure."

"Consider this: do you not know that the 'true lie' -- if I may use that phrase -- is hated by both gods and men?"

"What do you mean by 'true lie'?"

"I mean that no one wants to be deceived about the most important things in the most important part of themselves. What people fear most is having a lie take hold of their soul regarding the highest realities."

"I still do not follow."

"I am saying something quite simple: to be deceived or ignorant about the highest truths in the highest part of yourself -- the soul -- and to harbor falsehood there, is the thing human beings hate most in the world. They absolutely detest it."

"Nothing is more hateful to them."

"This ignorance lodged in the deceived person's soul is what I call the 'true lie.' A lie in words is merely an imitation, a shadowy copy of the deeper falsehood already residing in the soul. The verbal lie is not the real falsehood -- it is a secondary reflection."

"That is exactly right."

"And this true lie -- falsehood in the soul -- is hated by gods and men alike?"
D: Elegant: Socrates introduces the crucial distinction between the 'true lie' and the 'lie in words.' The true lie is falsehood lodged in the soul -- being deceived about the highest realities in one's deepest self. This is universally and utterly detested by both gods and men. Verbal lies are merely secondary shadows of this deeper soul-deception. Could God, who is unchanging, willingly deceive us even through illusion?
D: Summary: Socrates distinguishes the 'true lie' -- falsehood residing in the soul about the highest realities -- from the mere 'lie in words,' which is only a secondary reflection. The true lie is what both gods and men hate most. This distinction addresses whether God could deceive through illusion even without actually changing form.
D: Distilled: The 'true lie' is falsehood lodged in the soul about the highest realities -- universally detested by gods and men alike, far worse than any verbal deception.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- 'True lie' (alethos pseudos) vs. 'lie in words' (en tois logois pseudos): one of the Republic's most important philosophical distinctions -- The true lie = ignorance in the soul about ultimate realities; the verbal lie = a mere reflection or imitation of soul-falsehood -- This distinction parallels the Republic's metaphysics: reality vs. appearance, Forms vs. images -- Cross-reference: Republic Book V (476d-480a), knowledge vs. opinion -- Cross-reference: Republic Book VI (508d-509b), the Sun analogy and the Good as the source of truth -- Cross-reference: Republic Book VII (514a-521b), the Cave allegory -- 'The highest part of themselves, which is the soul': prefigures the tripartite soul with reason as its highest element -- The distinction between true and verbal lies becomes crucial for understanding the 'noble lie' (414b-415d)

D: Original: Yes. Whereas the lie in words is in certain cases useful and not hateful; in dealing with enemies --that would be an instance; or again, when those whom we call our friends in a fit of madness or illusion are going to do some harm, then it is useful and is a sort of medicine or preventive; also in the tales of mythology, of which we were just now speaking --because we do not know the truth about ancient times, we make falsehood as much like truth as we can, and so turn it to account.
D: Narrative:
"Yes."

"Whereas the lie in words is in certain cases useful and not hateful; in dealing with enemies -- that would be an instance; or again, when those whom we call our friends in a fit of madness or illusion are going to do some harm, then it is useful and is a sort of medicine or preventive; also in the tales of mythology, of which we were just now speaking -- because we do not know the truth about ancient times, we make falsehood as much like truth as we can, and so turn it to account."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes."

"But verbal lies, unlike the true lie, can sometimes be useful and not hateful. Against enemies, for example -- deception in warfare is legitimate. Or when friends, in a fit of madness or delusion, are about to do something harmful -- then a lie serves as medicine or prevention. And in mythology, which we were just discussing: since we do not know the truth about ancient times, we make our fictional accounts as close to truth as possible, and put them to good use."
D: Elegant: While the true lie in the soul is always hateful, verbal lies have legitimate uses: deceiving enemies, preventing harm from delusional friends (as a kind of medicine), and crafting mythology about unknown ancient events to approximate truth as closely as possible.
D: Summary: Socrates identifies three legitimate uses of verbal lies: deceiving enemies, preventing harm from delusional friends (as a form of medicine), and constructing mythology about ancient times whose truth is unknown -- making fiction as truth-like as possible.
D: Distilled: Verbal lies, unlike soul-falsehood, can be useful: against enemies, as medicine for delusional friends, and in mythology approximating unknown truths.
Notes: Socrates speaking -- Three legitimate uses of verbal falsehood: (1) deception of enemies, (2) therapeutic deception of deranged friends, (3) mythological approximation of unknown history -- 'Medicine or preventive' (pharmakon): the medicinal lie foreshadows the 'noble lie' of Republic Book III (414b-415d) -- 'Make falsehood as much like truth as we can': the criterion for acceptable mythology -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (389b-c), where lying is permitted to rulers as medicine for the state -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (414b-415d), the Noble Lie (Phoenician tale) -- Cross-reference: Hippias Minor 365b-372a, Socrates' paradoxical argument that the voluntary liar is better than the involuntary one -- The distinction anticipates modern debates about justified deception, 'white lies,' and propaganda
