D: Narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"But can any of these reasons apply to God? Can we suppose that he is ignorant of antiquity, and therefore has recourse to invention?"

"That would be ridiculous," he said.

"Then the lying poet has no place in our idea of God?"

"I should say not."

"Or perhaps he may tell a lie because he is afraid of enemies?"

"That is inconceivable."

"But he may have friends who are senseless or mad?"

"But no mad or senseless person can be a friend of God."

"Then no motive can be imagined why God should lie?"

"None whatever."

"Then the superhuman and divine is absolutely incapable of falsehood?"

"Yes."

"Then is God perfectly simple and true both in word and deed; he changes not; he deceives not, either by sign or word, by dream or waking vision."

"Your thoughts," he said, "are the reflection of my own."

"You agree with me then," I said, "that this is the second type or form in which we should write and speak about divine things. The gods are not magicians who transform themselves, neither do they deceive mankind in any way."

"I grant that."

"Then, although we are admirers of Homer, we do not admire the lying dream which Zeus sends to Agamemnon; neither will we praise the verses of Aeschylus in which Thetis says that Apollo at her nuptials --"
D: Refashioned:
"True," Adeimantus said.

"But do any of these justifications for lying apply to God? Is God ignorant about ancient events and therefore forced to invent?"

"That is absurd."

"So God has no need for poetic invention. Does God lie out of fear of enemies?"

"Inconceivable."

"Does God have friends who are mad or delusional and need therapeutic deception?"

"No mad or senseless person can be a friend of God."

"Then there is no conceivable reason for God to lie?"

"None at all."

"Then the divine nature is absolutely incapable of falsehood."

"Yes."

"God is perfectly simple and truthful in both word and deed. He does not change. He does not deceive -- not by signs, words, dreams, or waking visions."

"My thoughts exactly," Adeimantus said.

"Then this is our second theological principle: the gods are not shapeshifters who transform themselves, nor do they deceive human beings in any way."

"I agree."

"So even though we admire Homer, we reject the false dream Zeus sends to Agamemnon. And we will not praise the verses of Aeschylus in which Thetis says that Apollo at her wedding --"
D: Elegant: None of the legitimate reasons for lying apply to God: he is not ignorant, not fearful, and has no mad friends needing therapeutic deception. Therefore God is absolutely incapable of falsehood -- perfectly simple and true in word and deed, unchanging, never deceiving by any means. This second theological principle bans all stories of divine deception, including Zeus's false dream sent to Agamemnon and Apollo's false prophecies to Thetis.
D: Summary: Socrates eliminates every possible justification for divine lying and establishes the second theological principle: God is absolutely truthful, unchanging, and incapable of deception in any form. This requires rejecting Homer's story of Zeus sending a false dream to Agamemnon and Aeschylus's account of Apollo's broken promises to Thetis.
D: Distilled: God has no reason to lie and is absolutely incapable of falsehood -- the second theological principle: the gods neither change form nor deceive in any way.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- Second tupos (theological type): God is unchanging and truthful -- 'Perfectly simple and true': divine simplicity and truthfulness become foundational concepts in later theology -- Zeus's lying dream: Iliad 2.1-34, where Zeus sends a false dream to Agamemnon promising victory -- Aeschylus, from a lost play (probably the lost Bassarai or another work about Thetis): Thetis accuses Apollo of prophesying falsely about her son Achilles -- 'By sign or word, by dream or waking vision': a comprehensive list covering every mode of possible divine communication -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (382c-383c), where the two types of theology are summarized -- Cross-reference: Timaeus 29e, where the Demiurge is described as free from jealousy and deception -- The two theological types together: (1) God causes only good; (2) God is unchanging and truthful

D: Original: Was celebrating in song her fair progeny whose days were to he long, and to know no sickness. And when he had spoken of my lot as in all things blessed of heaven he raised a note of triumph and cheered my soul. And I thought that the word of Phoebus being divine and full of prophecy, would not fail. And now he himself who uttered the strain, he who was present at the banquet, and who said this --he it is who has slain my son.
D: Narrative:
Socrates quoted Aeschylus, in which Thetis says of Apollo: "He was celebrating in song her fair progeny whose days were to be long, and to know no sickness. And when he had spoken of my lot as in all things blessed of heaven he raised a note of triumph and cheered my soul.

"And I thought that the word of Phoebus being divine and full of prophecy, would not fail. And now he himself who uttered the strain, he who was present at the banquet, and who said this -- he it is who has slain my son."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates quoted Aeschylus, in which Thetis accuses Apollo: "He sang of my beautiful children -- how they would live long lives free from sickness. He described my fate as blessed by heaven in every way, and raised a song of triumph that filled my heart with joy.

"I trusted that the words of Phoebus Apollo, being divine and filled with prophetic truth, could not fail. And yet it is he himself -- the one who sang that song, who sat at my wedding banquet, who made those promises -- it is he who has killed my son."
D: Elegant: In Aeschylus's lost play, Thetis accuses Apollo of the cruelest deception: at her wedding he prophesied that her children would live long, healthy, blessed lives. She trusted his divine word completely. Yet Apollo himself killed her son Achilles. The god who promised happiness delivered death.
D: Summary: Socrates quotes Aeschylus's devastating accusation by Thetis against Apollo: the god prophesied at her wedding that her children would enjoy long, blessed lives, then himself killed her son Achilles -- the ultimate example of divine deception.
D: Distilled: Thetis accuses Apollo of the worst divine lie: promising her son a blessed life at her wedding, then killing him.
Notes: Socrates speaking -- Aeschylus, from a lost play (possibly the Psychostasia or a work in the Achilles trilogy): Thetis's lament for Achilles -- 'Phoebus' = Apollo, god of prophecy, music, and the sun -- Thetis: sea-nymph, mother of Achilles; her wedding to Peleus was attended by the gods -- Apollo's role in Achilles' death: in the Iliad, Apollo guides Paris's arrow to kill Achilles -- The accusation is devastating: the god of truth and prophecy is presented as the ultimate deceiver -- This is precisely the kind of story Plato's second theological principle would ban: a god who deceives and then destroys -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (383a-c), where this passage is cited as the culmination of the censorship argument -- The emotional power of Thetis's accusation makes it the most compelling example of why such stories are dangerous

D: Original:
These are the kind of sentiments about the gods which will arouse our anger; and he who utters them shall be refused a chorus; neither shall we allow teachers to make use of them in the instruction of the young, meaning, as we do, that our guardians, as far as men can be, should be true worshippers of the gods and like them.

I entirely agree, be said, in these principles, and promise to make them my laws.
D: Narrative:
"These are the kind of sentiments about the gods which will arouse our anger; and he who utters them shall be refused a chorus; neither shall we allow teachers to make use of them in the instruction of the young, meaning, as we do, that our guardians, as far as men can be, should be true worshippers of the gods and like them."

"I entirely agree," he said, "in these principles, and promise to make them my laws."
D: Refashioned:
"These are exactly the kind of claims about the gods that will provoke our anger. Any poet who makes them will be denied a chorus for their plays. We will not allow teachers to use such stories in educating the young. Our intention is that the guardians should be, as far as humanly possible, true worshippers of the gods and godlike themselves."

"I completely agree with these principles," Adeimantus said, "and I pledge to make them my laws."
D: Elegant: Poets who misrepresent the gods will be denied the right to produce their plays. Teachers may not use such stories. The goal: guardians who are true worshippers of the gods and, as far as humanly possible, godlike themselves. Adeimantus pledges to make these principles law.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes Book II's theological argument: poets who misrepresent the gods will be censored, teachers forbidden from using such stories, and guardians raised to be true worshippers who aspire to godlikeness. Adeimantus fully endorses these principles as law.
D: Distilled: Poets who misrepresent the gods will be banned, teachers restricted, and guardians raised as true worshippers who strive to be godlike.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- 'Refused a chorus': in Athens, dramatic productions required a wealthy sponsor (choregus) and official approval; denying a chorus meant the play could not be performed at public festivals -- This is practical censorship: controlling the means of artistic production -- 'True worshippers of the gods and like them': the guardians' education aims at homoiosis theoi (becoming like god), a concept central to Platonic ethics -- 'Promise to make them my laws': Adeimantus's pledge signals the transition from theoretical discussion to practical legislation -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (386a-403c), where censorship guidelines continue in detail -- Cross-reference: Republic Book X (595a-608b), the final reckoning with poetry -- Cross-reference: Theaetetus 176a-b, 'becoming like God as far as possible' (homoiosis theoi kata to dunaton) -- This concludes Book II; Book III continues with specific censorship of Homer, Hesiod, and the tragedians -- The two theological types established: (1) God is the cause of good only; (2) God is unchanging and truthful

D: Original:
Such then, I said, are our principles of theology --some tales are to be told, and others are not to be told to our disciples from their youth upwards, if we mean them to honour the gods and their parents, and to value friendship with one another.

Yes; and I think that our principles are right, he said.

But if they are to be courageous, must they not learn other lessons besides these, and lessons of such a kind as will take away the fear of death? Can any man be courageous who has the fear of death in him?

Certainly not, he said. And can he be fearless of death, or will he choose death in battle rather than defeat and slavery, who believes the world below to be real and terrible?

Impossible. Then we must assume a control over the narrators of this class of tales as well as over the others, and beg them not simply to but rather to commend the world below, intimating to them that their descriptions are untrue, and will do harm to our future warriors.

That will be our duty, he said. Then, I said, we shall have to obliterate many obnoxious passages, beginning with the verses,

I would rather he a serf on the land of a poor and portionless man than rule over all the dead who have come to nought. We must also expunge the verse, which tells us how Pluto feared,

Lest the mansions grim and squalid which the gods abhor should he seen both of mortals and immortals. And again:

O heavens! verily in the house of Hades there is soul and ghostly form but no mind at all! Again of Tiresias: --

[To him even after death did Persephone grant mind,] that he alone should be wise; but the other souls are flitting shades. Again: --

The soul flying from the limbs had gone to Hades, lamentng her fate, leaving manhood and youth. Again: --

And the soul, with shrilling cry, passed like smoke beneath the earth. And, --
D: Narrative:
"Such then," I said, "are our principles of theology -- some tales are to be told, and others are not to be told to our disciples from their youth upwards, if we mean them to honour the gods and their parents, and to value friendship with one another."

"Yes; and I think that our principles are right," he said.

"But if they are to be courageous, must they not learn other lessons besides these, and lessons of such a kind as will take away the fear of death? Can any man be courageous who has the fear of death in him?"

"Certainly not," he said.

"And can he be fearless of death, or will he choose death in battle rather than defeat and slavery, who believes the world below to be real and terrible?"

"Impossible."

"Then we must assume a control over the narrators of this class of tales as well as over the others, and beg them not simply to but rather to commend the world below, intimating to them that their descriptions are untrue, and will do harm to our future warriors."

"That will be our duty," he said.

"Then," I said, "we shall have to obliterate many obnoxious passages, beginning with the verses:

'I would rather be a serf on the land of a poor and portionless man than rule over all the dead who have come to nought.'

We must also expunge the verse, which tells us how Pluto feared,

'Lest the mansions grim and squalid which the gods abhor should be seen both of mortals and immortals.'

And again:

'O heavens! verily in the house of Hades there is soul and ghostly form but no mind at all!'

Again of Tiresias:

'To him even after death did Persephone grant mind, that he alone should be wise; but the other souls are flitting shades.'

Again:

'The soul flying from the limbs had gone to Hades, lamenting her fate, leaving manhood and youth.'

Again:

'And the soul, with shrilling cry, passed like smoke beneath the earth.'

And --"
D: Refashioned:
"So those are our theological principles," I said. "Certain stories should be told to our students from childhood, and others should not -- assuming we want them to honor the gods, respect their parents, and value friendship."

"Yes, and I think those principles are sound," he said.

"But if they're also to be brave, don't they need additional lessons -- the kind that eliminate the fear of death? Can anyone be truly courageous while fearing death?"

"Of course not," he said.

"And can someone be fearless of death -- willing to choose death in battle over defeat and slavery -- if he believes the underworld is real and terrifying?"

"Impossible."

"Then we need to regulate the storytellers who deal with the afterlife, just as we regulate the others. We should ask them not to disparage but rather to speak well of the world below, pointing out that their frightening descriptions are false and harmful to future soldiers."

"That will be our duty," he said.

"So we'll have to cut many problematic passages. Starting with lines like: 'I would rather be a servant on a poor man's farm than king over all the dead.' We must also remove the verse about Pluto fearing that his grim halls would be exposed to mortals and immortals alike. And that line: 'In Hades there is soul and ghostly form, but no mind at all!' And the passage about Tiresias being the only shade granted intelligence by Persephone, while all other souls are mere flickering shadows. And: 'The soul flew from the body to Hades, mourning its fate, leaving behind strength and youth.' And: 'The soul passed shrieking like smoke beneath the earth.' And --"
D: Elegant: Our theological principles are established. Now we must address courage. No one can be brave who fears death, and no one can be fearless who believes the underworld is a place of real horror. Therefore, we must censor poets who depict the afterlife as terrifying -- passages like Achilles preferring serfdom to ruling the dead, or souls described as mindless shades shrieking into darkness. These descriptions, however poetically compelling, undermine the warrior spirit we need to cultivate.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that after establishing theological principles, they must also censor poetic depictions of the afterlife that make death seem terrifying, since courage requires freedom from the fear of death. He cites multiple passages from Homer that portray the underworld as grim and horrifying.
D: Distilled: Poetic depictions of a terrifying afterlife must be censored because courage requires freedom from the fear of death.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Continues from Book II's discussion of theological censorship -- The quoted verse 'I would rather be a serf...' is from Homer's Odyssey (11.489-91), spoken by the shade of Achilles to Odysseus -- The Pluto/Hades passage references Iliad 20.64-65 -- The Tiresias reference is from Odyssey 10.494-95 -- 'The soul flying from the limbs' references Iliad 16.856-57 (death of Patroclus) and 22.362 (death of Hector) -- 'Shrilling cry, passed like smoke' references Iliad 23.100-01 -- Cross-reference: Book II (376e-383c) established principles of theology; Book X returns to the censorship of poetry

D: Original: As bats in hollow of mystic cavern, whenever any of the has dropped out of the string and falls from the rock, fly shrilling and cling to one another, so did they with shrilling cry hold together as they moved. And we must beg Homer and the other poets not to be angry if we strike out these and similar passages, not because they are unpoetical, or unattractive to the popular ear, but because the greater the poetical charm of them, the less are they meet for the ears of boys and men who are meant to be free, and who should fear slavery more than death.
D: Narrative:
"As bats in hollow of mystic cavern, whenever any of them has dropped out of the string and falls from the rock, fly shrilling and cling to one another, so did they with shrilling cry hold together as they moved."

"And we must beg Homer and the other poets not to be angry if we strike out these and similar passages, not because they are unpoetical, or unattractive to the popular ear, but because the greater the poetical charm of them, the less are they meet for the ears of boys and men who are meant to be free, and who should fear slavery more than death."
D: Refashioned:
"'As bats in the hollow of some mystic cave, when one drops from the cluster on the rock, fly shrieking and clinging to one another -- so did those souls move together with piercing cries.'

We must ask Homer and the other poets not to take offense if we remove these passages and others like them. It's not that they lack poetic power or popular appeal -- quite the opposite. The more poetically charming they are, the less suitable they become for boys and men who are being raised to be free, and who should fear slavery more than death."
D: Elegant: We must ask Homer not to be angry when we remove such passages. It is precisely because they are so poetically powerful that they are dangerous -- the greater their charm, the less fit they are for those being raised to value freedom above life itself.
D: Summary: Socrates continues citing Homeric passages to be censored, noting that the very poetic excellence of these descriptions makes them more harmful, not less, to young men being educated for freedom and courage.
D: Distilled: The greater a poem's artistic power in depicting death as terrible, the more dangerous it is to the education of free men.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates (continuing his argument to Adeimantus) -- The bat simile is from Homer's Odyssey 24.6-9, describing the souls of the slain suitors being led to Hades by Hermes -- Key aesthetic principle introduced: artistic excellence can increase harmfulness -- 'Fear slavery more than death' echoes Spartan military values -- Cross-reference: Book X (595a-608b) returns to this tension between poetry's beauty and its harmful effects

I do not say that these horrible stories may not have a use of some kind; but there is a danger that the nerves of our guardians may be rendered too excitable and effeminate by them.
D: Narrative:
"Undoubtedly."

"Also we shall have to reject all the terrible and appalling names which describe the world below -- Cocytus and Styx, ghosts under the earth, and sapless shades, and any similar words of which the very mention causes a shudder to pass through the inmost soul of him who hears them. I do not say that these horrible stories may not have a use of some kind; but there is a danger that the nerves of our guardians may be rendered too excitable and effeminate by them."
D: Refashioned:
"Undoubtedly."

"We'll also need to reject all the terrifying names used to describe the underworld -- Cocytus, Styx, ghosts beneath the earth, 'sapless shades,' and any similar language that sends a shudder through the very soul of anyone who hears it. I'm not saying these horrifying stories have no use whatsoever. But there's a real danger that they'll make our guardians too emotionally fragile and soft."
D: Elegant: We must also ban the terrifying nomenclature of the underworld -- Cocytus, Styx, sapless shades -- any words that make listeners shudder. Such stories may serve some purpose, but they risk making our guardians emotionally fragile.
D: Summary: Socrates extends the censorship to the very names and vocabulary associated with the underworld, arguing that even individual words like 'Cocytus' and 'Styx' can produce harmful emotional effects on future guardians.
D: Distilled: Even the terrifying vocabulary of the underworld must be censored to prevent emotional fragility in future guardians.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates (with Adeimantus assenting) -- Cocytus ('river of wailing') and Styx ('river of hatred') are rivers in the Greek underworld -- 'Sapless shades' (amenaena karena) is a Homeric formula for the dead -- The concern about guardians becoming 'effeminate' (malthakos) connects to the later discussion of music and softness (Book III, 398e-399c) -- Cross-reference: Phaedo (113a-114c) where Plato gives his own detailed geography of the underworld rivers

D: Original:
There is a real danger, he said. Then we must have no more of them. True. Another and a nobler strain must be composed and sung by us.

Clearly. And shall we proceed to get rid of the weepings and wailings of famous men?

They will go with the rest. But shall we be right in getting rid of them? Reflect: our principle is that the good man will not consider death terrible to any other good man who is his comrade.

Yes; that is our principle. And therefore he will not sorrow for his departed friend as though he had suffered anything terrible?

He will not. Such an one, as we further maintain, is sufficient for himself and his own happiness, and therefore is least in need of other men.

True, he said. And for this reason the loss of a son or brother, or the deprivation of fortune, is to him of all men least terrible.

Assuredly. And therefore he will be least likely to lament, and will bear with the greatest equanimity any misfortune of this sort which may befall him.

Yes, he will feel such a misfortune far less than another.

Then we shall be right in getting rid of the lamentations of famous men, and making them over to women (and not even to women who are good for anything), or to men of a baser sort, that those who are being educated by us to be the defenders of their country may scorn to do the like.
D: Narrative:
"There is a real danger," he said.

"Then we must have no more of them."

"True."

"Another and a nobler strain must be composed and sung by us."

"Clearly."

"And shall we proceed to get rid of the weepings and wailings of famous men?"

"They will go with the rest."

"But shall we be right in getting rid of them? Reflect: our principle is that the good man will not consider death terrible to any other good man who is his comrade."

"Yes; that is our principle."

"And therefore he will not sorrow for his departed friend as though he had suffered anything terrible?"

"He will not."

"Such an one, as we further maintain, is sufficient for himself and his own happiness, and therefore is least in need of other men."

"True," he said.

"And for this reason the loss of a son or brother, or the deprivation of fortune, is to him of all men least terrible."

"Assuredly."

"And therefore he will be least likely to lament, and will bear with the greatest equanimity any misfortune of this sort which may befall him."

"Yes, he will feel such a misfortune far less than another."

"Then we shall be right in getting rid of the lamentations of famous men, and making them over to women (and not even to women who are good for anything), or to men of a baser sort, that those who are being educated by us to be the defenders of their country may scorn to do the like."
D: Refashioned:
"There's a real danger," he said.

"Then we must eliminate such things."

"Agreed."

"A nobler kind of poetry must be composed to replace them."

"Clearly."

"And shall we also remove the scenes of famous men weeping and wailing?"

"Yes, they should go with the rest."

"But are we justified? Consider our principle: a good man will not regard death as something terrible when it comes to a fellow good man."

"Yes, that's our principle."

"So he won't grieve for a departed friend as if something terrible has happened?"

"He won't."

"We also maintain that such a person is self-sufficient in his own happiness, and therefore least dependent on others."

"True," he said.

"Which means losing a son, a brother, or his fortune is less devastating to him than to anyone else."

"Absolutely."

"So he'll be the least likely to lament, bearing any such misfortune with the greatest composure."

"Yes, he'll feel such a loss far less than others would."

"Then we're right to remove the lamentations of famous heroes and assign such behavior to women of no particular merit, or to inferior men -- so that those we're educating to defend the state will be ashamed to act similarly."
D: Elegant: A good man considers death no tragedy for a fellow good man, is self-sufficient in happiness, and therefore bears loss with equanimity. Consequently, we should remove all scenes of famous heroes lamenting -- such displays belong to lesser characters, not to models for our future defenders.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that since good men are self-sufficient and do not fear death for themselves or their comrades, literary depictions of famous heroes weeping and wailing should be removed from the educational curriculum, as such behavior is beneath the dignity of those being trained as guardians.
D: Distilled: The self-sufficient good man bears loss with equanimity, so poetic depictions of heroic lamentation must be censored as unfit models for guardians.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The principle of self-sufficiency (autarkeia) is central to Greek ethics, especially Stoicism, but originates here -- The dismissal of lamentation to women reflects ancient Greek gender norms but also Plato's philosophical point about emotional control -- Cross-reference: Book II (381e-382a) on the self-sufficiency of the divine; Apology (40c-41c) where Socrates himself models fearlessness before death -- The claim that the good man is 'sufficient for himself' anticipates Aristotle's criticism in Nicomachean Ethics Book I

Nor should he describe Priam the kinsman of the gods as praying and beseeching,
D: Narrative: "Then we will once more entreat Homer and the other poets not to depict Achilles, who is the son of a goddess, first lying on his side, then on his back, and then on his face; then starting up and sailing in a frenzy along the shores of the barren sea; now taking the sooty ashes in both his hands and pouring them over his head, or weeping and wailing in the various modes which Homer has delineated. Nor should he describe Priam the kinsman of the gods as praying and beseeching --"
D: Refashioned: "So once again we must ask Homer and the other poets not to show Achilles -- the son of a goddess, no less -- lying first on his side, then on his back, then face down; then jumping up and pacing wildly along the barren shore; then scooping up handfuls of sooty ash and pouring them over his head, weeping and wailing in all the ways Homer describes. Nor should they depict Priam, kinsman of the gods, groveling in prayer and supplication --"
D: Elegant: We must ask Homer not to show Achilles, son of a goddess, rolling in grief and pouring ashes over his head, or Priam groveling in supplication -- such displays of excessive emotion are unworthy models for those we are educating.
D: Summary: Socrates specifically targets Homer's depictions of Achilles' extravagant grief after Patroclus's death and Priam's desperate supplication as inappropriate models of behavior for future guardians.
D: Distilled: Homer's depictions of Achilles wallowing in grief and Priam groveling in supplication must be removed as unworthy models for guardians.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- Achilles' grief refers to Iliad 18.23-27 (his reaction to the death of Patroclus) and Iliad 24.3-12 (his continued mourning) -- 'Son of a goddess': Achilles' mother is the sea-nymph Thetis -- Priam 'kinsman of the gods' references the Trojan royal family's divine ancestry through Dardanus, son of Zeus -- The 'barren sea' (atrugetoio) is a standard Homeric epithet -- Cross-reference: Iliad 22.414ff (Priam's lamentation for Hector)

D: Original:
Rolling in the dirt, calling each man loudly by his name. Still more earnestly will we beg of him at all events not to introduce the gods lamenting and saying,

Alas! my misery! Alas! that I bore the harvest to my sorrow. But if he must introduce the gods, at any rate let him not dare so completely to misrepresent the greatest of the gods, as to make him say --

O heavens! with my eyes verily I behold a dear friend of mine chased round and round the city, and my heart is sorrowful. Or again: --
D: Narrative:
"Rolling in the dirt, calling each man loudly by his name."

"Still more earnestly will we beg of him at all events not to introduce the gods lamenting and saying,

'Alas! my misery! Alas! that I bore the harvest to my sorrow.'

But if he must introduce the gods, at any rate let him not dare so completely to misrepresent the greatest of the gods, as to make him say --

'O heavens! with my eyes verily I behold a dear friend of mine chased round and round the city, and my heart is sorrowful.'

Or again --"
D: Refashioned:
"...rolling in the dirt, calling each man loudly by name.

Even more urgently, we must insist that Homer not portray the gods themselves lamenting, saying things like: 'Alas, my misery! That I should have borne such a child, only to grieve!'

And if he must include the gods at all, he absolutely must not misrepresent the greatest of them -- Zeus -- by making him say: 'O heavens! I can see with my own eyes a man dear to me being chased around the city, and my heart aches.'

Or again --"
D: Elegant: Most urgently, Homer must not show gods lamenting, and must never make Zeus himself express grief and helplessness at the fate of mortals -- this is the worst possible misrepresentation of divine nature.
D: Summary: Socrates escalates his critique from mortal heroes to divine characters, insisting that Homer must never depict the gods -- especially Zeus -- expressing grief, sorrow, or helplessness, as this fundamentally misrepresents divine nature.
D: Distilled: Depicting gods, especially Zeus, as grief-stricken and helpless fundamentally misrepresents the divine nature and must be prohibited.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- 'Rolling in the dirt' continues the description of Priam from Iliad 22.414 -- 'Alas! that I bore the harvest to my sorrow' is Thetis lamenting Achilles' fate, Iliad 18.54 -- The Zeus quotation refers to Iliad 22.168-69, where Zeus watches Hector being pursued by Achilles around Troy -- 'The greatest of the gods' = Zeus -- Cross-reference: Book II (380d-383c) on the immutability and perfection of the gods

D: Original: Woe is me that I am fated to have Sarpedon, dearest of men to me, subdued at the hands of Patroclus the son of Menoetius. For if, my sweet Adeimantus, our youth seriously listen to such unworthy representations of the gods, instead of laughing at them as they ought, hardly will any of them deem that he himself, being but a man, can be dishonoured by similar actions; neither will he rebuke any inclination which may arise in his mind to say and do the like. And instead of having any shame or self-control, he will be always whining and lamenting on slight occasions.
D: Narrative:
"'Woe is me that I am fated to have Sarpedon, dearest of men to me, subdued at the hands of Patroclus the son of Menoetius.'

For if, my sweet Adeimantus, our youth seriously listen to such unworthy representations of the gods, instead of laughing at them as they ought, hardly will any of them deem that he himself, being but a man, can be dishonoured by similar actions; neither will he rebuke any inclination which may arise in his mind to say and do the like. And instead of having any shame or self-control, he will be always whining and lamenting on slight occasions."
D: Refashioned:
"'Woe is me, that fate decrees my beloved Sarpedon must fall at the hands of Patroclus, son of Menoetius.'

Because here's the thing, my dear Adeimantus: if our young people take these unworthy portrayals of the gods seriously -- instead of laughing at them as they should -- then none of them will think it beneath himself, a mere mortal, to behave the same way. He won't resist any impulse to whine and lament in similar fashion. And so, instead of developing shame and self-control, he'll be forever weeping and complaining over trivial matters."
D: Elegant: If Zeus himself laments, what mortal would think such behavior beneath him? Youth who take these divine portrayals seriously will lose all shame and self-control, whining over every trivial setback instead of bearing hardship with dignity.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that if young people see even Zeus lamenting the fate of his son Sarpedon, they will feel no shame about their own emotional outbursts, losing the self-control essential to their development as guardians.
D: Distilled: When gods are shown lamenting, mortals lose their shame about emotional excess, undermining the self-control guardians need.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates, addressing Adeimantus directly ('my sweet Adeimantus') -- The Zeus-Sarpedon quotation is from Iliad 16.433-34; Sarpedon was Zeus's mortal son, a Lycian ally of Troy killed by Patroclus -- 'My sweet Adeimantus' is a rare term of endearment showing the intimacy of the dialogue -- Key psychological principle: people model their behavior on those they admire, including fictional characters -- Cross-reference: Book X (606a-b) returns to this idea that poetic representations license emotional indulgence

D: Original:
Yes, he said, that is most true. Yes, I replied; but that surely is what ought not to be, as the argument has just proved to us; and by that proof we must abide until it is disproved by a better.

It ought not to be. Neither ought our guardians to be given to laughter. For a fit of laughter which has been indulged to excess almost always produces a violent reaction.

So I believe. Then persons of worth, even if only mortal men, must not be represented as overcome by laughter, and still less must such a representation of the gods be allowed.

Still less of the gods, as you say, he replied. Then we shall not suffer such an expression to be used about the gods as that of Homer when he describes how

Inextinguishable laughter arose among the blessed gods, when they saw Hephaestus bustling about the mansion. On your views, we must not admit them.

On my views, if you like to father them on me; that we must not admit them is certain.

Again, truth should be highly valued; if, as we were saying, a lie is useless to the gods, and useful only as a medicine to men, then the use of such medicines should be restricted to physicians; private individuals have no business with them.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is most true."

"Yes," I replied; "but that surely is what ought not to be, as the argument has just proved to us; and by that proof we must abide until it is disproved by a better."

"It ought not to be."

"Neither ought our guardians to be given to laughter. For a fit of laughter which has been indulged to excess almost always produces a violent reaction."

"So I believe."

"Then persons of worth, even if only mortal men, must not be represented as overcome by laughter, and still less must such a representation of the gods be allowed."

"Still less of the gods, as you say," he replied.

"Then we shall not suffer such an expression to be used about the gods as that of Homer when he describes how

'Inextinguishable laughter arose among the blessed gods, when they saw Hephaestus bustling about the mansion.'

On your views, we must not admit them."

"On my views, if you like to father them on me; that we must not admit them is certain."

"Again, truth should be highly valued; if, as we were saying, a lie is useless to the gods, and useful only as a medicine to men, then the use of such medicines should be restricted to physicians; private individuals have no business with them."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," he said, "that's absolutely true."

"And it's clearly wrong, as our argument has demonstrated. We must stand by that conclusion until someone offers a better one."

"Agreed."

"Our guardians also shouldn't be prone to excessive laughter. When laughter is indulged to excess, it almost always provokes a violent emotional reaction."

"I believe so."

"Then worthy people, even ordinary mortals, must not be shown overcome by laughter -- and this applies even more to the gods."

"Even more so, as you say," he replied.

"So we can't allow Homer's description of 'unquenchable laughter' arising among the gods when they saw Hephaestus hobbling around the hall. By your principles, we must reject this."

"By my principles, if you want to attribute them to me -- but we certainly must reject it."

"Now, truth should be held in the highest regard. If, as we said, lies are useless to the gods and useful to humans only as medicine, then their use should be restricted to physicians. Private citizens have no business administering them."
D: Elegant: Excessive laughter is as dangerous as excessive grief -- both represent loss of self-control. Homer's depiction of 'unquenchable laughter' among the gods must be rejected. Furthermore, truth must be highly valued. If falsehood serves only as medicine for humans, then only the rulers -- as physicians of the state -- should be permitted to use it.
D: Summary: Socrates extends the censorship to excessive laughter, citing Homer's depiction of the gods laughing uncontrollably at Hephaestus. He then transitions to the value of truth, arguing that if lies are useful only as medicine, their use should be restricted to the rulers of the state, just as medicine is restricted to physicians.
D: Distilled: Excessive laughter must be censored alongside excessive grief, and the use of falsehood must be restricted to rulers as a medicine of state.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Inextinguishable laughter' (asbestos gelos) is from Iliad 1.599-600, the famous scene where the lame Hephaestus serves as cupbearer -- The 'noble lie' doctrine is introduced here: falsehood as pharmakon (medicine/drug) -- Cross-reference: Book II (382c-d) established that gods have no need of lies; the 'noble lie' appears fully in Book III (414b-415d) -- Adeimantus's wry response ('if you like to father them on me') shows Plato's humor -- The physician/medicine analogy for lying anticipates the fuller treatment in 389b-c

D: Original: Clearly not, he said. Then if any one at all is to have the privilege of lying, the rulers of the State should be the persons; and they, in their dealings either with enemies or with their own citizens, may be allowed to lie for the public good. But nobody else should meddle with anything of the kind; and although the rulers have this privilege, for a private man to lie to them in return is to be deemed a more heinous fault than for the patient or the pupil of a gymnasium not to speak the truth about his own bodily illnesses to the physician or to the trainer, or for a sailor not to tell the captain what is happening about the ship and the rest of the crew, and how things are going with himself or his fellow sailors.
D: Narrative:
"Clearly not," he said.

"Then if any one at all is to have the privilege of lying, the rulers of the State should be the persons; and they, in their dealings either with enemies or with their own citizens, may be allowed to lie for the public good. But nobody else should meddle with anything of the kind; and although the rulers have this privilege, for a private man to lie to them in return is to be deemed a more heinous fault than for the patient or the pupil of a gymnasium not to speak the truth about his own bodily illnesses to the physician or to the trainer, or for a sailor not to tell the captain what is happening about the ship and the rest of the crew, and how things are going with himself or his fellow sailors."
D: Refashioned:
"Clearly not," he said.

"If anyone is to have the privilege of lying, it should be the rulers of the state. They may lie to enemies or to their own citizens when it serves the public good. But no one else should have anything to do with falsehood. And if a private citizen lies to the rulers, that should be considered an even worse offense than a patient lying to his doctor about his symptoms, or an athlete hiding injuries from his trainer, or a sailor concealing problems on the ship from his captain."
D: Elegant: Only rulers may lie, and only for the public good. A private citizen lying to rulers is worse than a patient deceiving his doctor, an athlete hiding injuries from his trainer, or a sailor withholding critical information from his captain.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes that only rulers of the state should have the privilege of lying, and only for the public good. A citizen lying to the rulers is compared to a patient deceiving his physician -- a dangerous inversion of the proper relationship between those who govern and those who are governed.
D: Distilled: Only rulers may use falsehood, as medicine for the state; citizens lying to rulers is a graver offense than patients deceiving their physicians.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- This passage establishes the doctrine that Plato's critics call the 'noble lie' or 'royal lie' (gennaion pseudos) -- Three analogies for the ruler-citizen relationship: physician-patient, trainer-athlete, captain-sailor -- All three analogies emphasize that those being governed must be transparent with their governors -- Cross-reference: Book II (382a-d) on divine truthfulness; Laws 663d-e on beneficial falsehood -- Karl Popper's The Open Society and Its Enemies famously criticizes this passage as proto-totalitarian

D: Original:
Most true, he said. If, then, the ruler catches anybody beside himself lying in the State,

Any of the craftsmen, whether he priest or physician or carpenter. he will punish him for introducing a practice which is equally subversive and destructive of ship or State.

Most certainly, he said, if our idea of the State is ever carried out.

In the next place our youth must be temperate? Certainly. Are not the chief elements of temperance, speaking generally, obedience to commanders and self-control in sensual pleasures?

Friend, sit still and obey my word, and the verses which follow,

O heavy with wine, who hast the eyes of a dog and the heart of a stag, and of the words which follow? Would you say that these, or any similar impertinences which private individuals are supposed to address to their rulers, whether in verse or prose, are well or ill spoken?

They are ill spoken. They may very possibly afford some amusement, but they do not conduce to temperance. And therefore they are likely to do harm to our young men --you would agree with me there?
