When the tables are full of bread and meat, and the cup-bearer carries round wine which he draws from the bowl and pours into the cups, is it fit or conducive to temperance for a young man to hear such words? Or the verse
D: Narrative:
"Most true," he said.

"If, then, the ruler catches anybody beside himself lying in the State --

'Any of the craftsmen, whether he be priest or physician or carpenter' --

he will punish him for introducing a practice which is equally subversive and destructive of ship or State."

"Most certainly," he said, "if our idea of the State is ever carried out."

"In the next place our youth must be temperate?"

"Certainly."

"Are not the chief elements of temperance, speaking generally, obedience to commanders and self-control in sensual pleasures?"

"True."

"Then we shall approve such language as that of Diomede in Homer:

'Friend, sit still and obey my word,'

and the verses which follow:

'The Greeks marched breathing prowess, ...in silent awe of their leaders,'

and other sentiments of the same kind."

"We shall."

"What of this line:

'O heavy with wine, who hast the eyes of a dog and the heart of a stag,'

and of the words which follow? Would you say that these, or any similar impertinences which private individuals are supposed to address to their rulers, whether in verse or prose, are well or ill spoken?"

"They are ill spoken."

"They may very possibly afford some amusement, but they do not conduce to temperance. And therefore they are likely to do harm to our young men -- you would agree with me there?"

"Yes."

"And then, again, to make the wisest of men say that nothing in his opinion is more glorious than

'When the tables are full of bread and meat, and the cup-bearer carries round wine which he draws from the bowl and pours into the cups,'

is it fit or conducive to temperance for a young man to hear such words? Or the verse --"
D: Refashioned:
"Absolutely true," he said.

"So if a ruler catches anyone else lying in the state -- 'whether priest, physician, or carpenter' -- he will punish that person for introducing a practice as destructive to the state as it would be to a ship."

"Most certainly," he said, "if our vision of the state is ever realized."

"Next: our young people must be temperate?"

"Certainly."

"And the chief elements of temperance are obedience to authority and self-control over physical pleasures?"

"True."

"Then we'll approve lines like Diomede's in Homer: 'Friend, sit still and obey my word,' and the passages that follow about the Greeks marching in disciplined silence, in awe of their leaders."

"We will."

"But what about the line where someone says to a ruler: 'You drunkard, with the eyes of a dog and the heart of a deer' -- and the insults that follow? Would you say such language, addressed by a private citizen to his leader, is well or poorly spoken?"

"Poorly spoken."

"It may be entertaining, but it doesn't promote temperance. And therefore it's likely to harm our young people -- agreed?"

"Yes."

"And then consider: the wisest of men declares that the most glorious thing he can imagine is 'tables loaded with bread and meat, and a cupbearer pouring wine from the bowl into every cup.' Is it proper or conducive to self-control for young people to hear such praise of feasting? Or this verse --"
D: Elegant: Lying by citizens must be punished as subversive to the state. Temperance requires obedience and self-control, so we approve poetry showing disciplined soldiers obeying commanders, but reject verses where subordinates insult leaders or where wise men glorify feasting -- such passages are entertaining but undermine the self-discipline we are trying to cultivate.
D: Summary: Socrates turns to temperance in education. He approves Homeric verses showing obedience and discipline, but rejects passages where characters insult their leaders or glorify sensual pleasures like feasting, as these undermine the temperance required of guardians.
D: Distilled: Poetry must model temperance by showing obedience to authority and self-control, not insubordination toward leaders or glorification of sensual pleasures.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Any of the craftsmen, whether he be priest or physician or carpenter' adapts Odyssey 17.383-84 -- 'Friend, sit still and obey my word' is Diomede speaking in Iliad 4.412 -- 'The Greeks marched breathing prowess' from Iliad 3.8 and 4.431 -- 'O heavy with wine, who hast the eyes of a dog and the heart of a stag' is Achilles insulting Agamemnon, Iliad 1.225 -- 'The wisest of men' praising feasting is Odysseus in Odyssey 9.8-10 -- Temperance (sophrosyne) is one of the four cardinal virtues -- Cross-reference: Book IV (430d-432a) for the full definition of temperance

D: Original: The saddest of fates is to die and meet destiny from hunger? What would you say again to the tale of Zeus, who, while other gods and men were asleep and he the only person awake, lay devising plans, but forgot them all in a moment through his lust, and was so completely overcome at the sight of Here that he would not even go into the hut, but wanted to lie with her on the ground, declaring that he had never been in such a state of rapture before, even when they first met one another
D: Narrative:
"'The saddest of fates is to die and meet destiny from hunger?'

What would you say again to the tale of Zeus, who, while other gods and men were asleep and he the only person awake, lay devising plans, but forgot them all in a moment through his lust, and was so completely overcome at the sight of Here that he would not even go into the hut, but wanted to lie with her on the ground, declaring that he had never been in such a state of rapture before, even when they first met one another --"
D: Refashioned:
"Or: 'The worst fate of all is to die of hunger.'

And what about the story of Zeus? While all the other gods and mortals slept, he alone lay awake devising plans -- only to forget them all in an instant because of his lust. He was so overwhelmed at the sight of Hera that he wouldn't even go inside, but wanted to lie with her right there on the ground, declaring he had never felt such desire, not even when they first fell in love --"
D: Elegant: To declare hunger the worst death glorifies physical comfort over honor. And the story of Zeus forgetting his plans because he was overcome with lust for Hera -- unable even to wait for privacy -- is a catastrophic model of temperance for young people.
D: Summary: Socrates continues listing passages unfit for education: one declaring death by hunger the worst fate (glorifying bodily comfort), and Homer's story of Zeus being so overwhelmed by lust for Hera that he forgot all his plans and lost all self-control.
D: Distilled: Passages glorifying bodily comfort or depicting Zeus overcome by lust must be censored as destructive to temperance.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- 'The saddest of fates is to die from hunger' is from Odyssey 12.342 -- The Zeus and Hera scene is from Iliad 14.294-351, the famous 'Deception of Zeus' (Dios apate) where Hera seduces Zeus to distract him from the Trojan War -- 'Here' is Jowett's spelling of 'Hera' -- This scene was celebrated in antiquity for its erotic beauty but here condemned for its moral implications -- Cross-reference: Book II (378d) on inappropriate sexual stories about the gods

D: Original:
Without the knowledge of their parents; or that other tale of how Hephaestus, because of similar goings on, cast a chain around Ares and Aphrodite?

Indeed, he said, I am strongly of opinion that they ought not to hear that sort of thing.

But any deeds of endurance which are done or told by famous men, these they ought to see and hear; as, for example, what is said in the verses,

He smote his breast, and thus reproached his heart, Endure, my heart; far worse hast thou endured!

Certainly, he said. In the next place, we must not let them be receivers of gifts or lovers of money.

Certainly not. Neither must we sing to them of
D: Narrative:
"Without the knowledge of their parents; or that other tale of how Hephaestus, because of similar goings on, cast a chain around Ares and Aphrodite?"

"Indeed," he said, "I am strongly of opinion that they ought not to hear that sort of thing."

"But any deeds of endurance which are done or told by famous men, these they ought to see and hear; as, for example, what is said in the verses:

'He smote his breast, and thus reproached his heart, Endure, my heart; far worse hast thou endured!'"

"Certainly," he said.

"In the next place, we must not let them be receivers of gifts or lovers of money."

"Certainly not."

"Neither must we sing to them of --"
D: Refashioned:
"...without their parents' knowledge. Or that other story about Hephaestus catching Ares and Aphrodite in a chain-net because of their affair?"

"I strongly agree," he said, "that young people should not hear such things."

"But deeds of endurance by famous men -- those they absolutely should see and hear. For example, when Odysseus strikes his chest and tells his heart: 'Endure, my heart! You have endured far worse than this!'"

"Certainly," he said.

"Next, we must not allow them to become greedy or lovers of money."

"Certainly not."

"Nor must we let them hear --"
D: Elegant: Stories of divine adultery must be excluded, but tales of endurance -- like Odysseus commanding his own heart to bear hardship -- should be held up as models. Additionally, guardians must be trained against greed and love of money.
D: Summary: Socrates contrasts harmful stories (divine adultery between Ares and Aphrodite) with beneficial ones (Odysseus commanding himself to endure), and transitions to the need to prevent guardians from becoming lovers of money.
D: Distilled: Stories of divine adultery corrupt while tales of heroic endurance inspire; guardians must also be guarded against greed.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Without the knowledge of their parents' continues the Zeus-Hera story from Iliad 14.296 -- Hephaestus trapping Ares and Aphrodite is from Odyssey 8.266-366 -- 'Endure, my heart' is from Odyssey 20.17-18, where Odysseus restrains himself from killing the disloyal maidservants prematurely -- This Odyssey passage was widely admired in antiquity as a model of self-mastery (enkrateia) -- Aristotle cites this same passage in De Anima 432b -- The transition to greed moves from physical temperance to material temperance

D: Original:
Gifts persuading gods, and persuading reverend kings. Neither is Phoenix, the tutor of Achilles, to be approved or deemed to have given his pupil good counsel when he told him that he should take the gifts of the Greeks and assist them; but that without a gift he should not lay aside his anger. Neither will we believe or acknowledge Achilles himself to have been such a lover of money that he took Agamemnon's or that when he had received payment he restored the dead body of Hector, but that without payment he was unwilling to do so.

Undoubtedly, he said, these are not sentiments which can be approved.

Loving Homer as I do, I hardly like to say that in attributing these feelings to Achilles, or in believing that they are truly to him, he is guilty of downright impiety. As little can I believe the narrative of his insolence to Apollo, where he says,
D: Narrative:
"'Gifts persuading gods, and persuading reverend kings.'

Neither is Phoenix, the tutor of Achilles, to be approved or deemed to have given his pupil good counsel when he told him that he should take the gifts of the Greeks and assist them; but that without a gift he should not lay aside his anger. Neither will we believe or acknowledge Achilles himself to have been such a lover of money that he took Agamemnon's gifts, or that when he had received payment he restored the dead body of Hector, but that without payment he was unwilling to do so."

"Undoubtedly," he said, "these are not sentiments which can be approved."

"Loving Homer as I do, I hardly like to say that in attributing these feelings to Achilles, or in believing that they are truly attributed to him, he is guilty of downright impiety. As little can I believe the narrative of his insolence to Apollo, where he says --"
D: Refashioned:
"'Gifts persuade the gods, and gifts persuade mighty kings.'

We cannot approve of Phoenix, Achilles' tutor, for advising his pupil to accept the Greeks' gifts as a condition for returning to battle -- implying that without payment, he should keep nursing his anger. Nor will we accept the portrayal of Achilles as so money-hungry that he took Agamemnon's gifts as a bribe, or that he only returned Hector's body after receiving a ransom and would have refused otherwise."

"Undoubtedly," he said, "these are sentiments we cannot approve."

"Loving Homer as I do, I can hardly bring myself to say that by attributing such mercenary feelings to Achilles, Homer is guilty of outright impiety. Equally, I cannot accept the story of Achilles' insolence toward Apollo, where he says --"
D: Elegant: We cannot approve the idea that 'gifts persuade gods and kings,' nor the portrayal of Achilles as motivated by money -- accepting bribes to fight and ransoms to return the dead. Though I love Homer, attributing such greed to a demigod hero borders on impiety.
D: Summary: Socrates condemns the maxim that gifts can persuade gods and kings, and rejects Homer's portrayal of Achilles as motivated by greed -- both in accepting Agamemnon's gifts to return to battle and in ransoming Hector's body. Despite his love for Homer, Socrates considers such characterizations near-impious.
D: Distilled: The idea that gifts persuade gods and heroes is impious; Achilles must not be portrayed as motivated by greed.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- 'Gifts persuading gods' is a proverbial saying, possibly from Hesiod -- Phoenix's advice to Achilles is from Iliad 9.515ff (the Embassy to Achilles) -- Achilles accepting Agamemnon's gifts: Iliad 19.278ff -- Achilles ransoming Hector's body: Iliad 24.502ff -- 'Loving Homer as I do' is one of Plato's most famous expressions of the tension between philosophical truth and poetic beauty -- Phoenix was Achilles' childhood tutor, sent by his father Peleus -- Cross-reference: Book X (595b-c) echoes this love-hate relationship with Homer

D: Original: Thou hast wronged me, O far-darter, most abominable of deities. Verily I would he even with thee, if I had only the power, or his insubordination to the river-god, on whose divinity he is ready to lay hands; or his offering to the dead Patroclus of his own hair, which had been previously dedicated to the other river-god Spercheius, and that he actually performed this vow; or that he dragged Hector round the tomb of Patroclus, and slaughtered the captives at the pyre; of all this I cannot believe that he was guilty, any more than I can allow our citizens to believe that he, the wise Cheiron's pupil, the son of a goddess and of Peleus who was the gentlest of men and third in descent from Zeus, was so disordered in his wits as to be at one time the slave of two seemingly inconsistent passions, meanness, not untainted by avarice, combined with overweening contempt of gods and men.
D: Narrative:
"'Thou hast wronged me, O far-darter, most abominable of deities. Verily I would be even with thee, if I had only the power.'

Or his insubordination to the river-god, on whose divinity he is ready to lay hands; or his offering to the dead Patroclus of his own hair, which had been previously dedicated to the other river-god Spercheius, and that he actually performed this vow; or that he dragged Hector round the tomb of Patroclus, and slaughtered the captives at the pyre; of all this I cannot believe that he was guilty, any more than I can allow our citizens to believe that he, the wise Cheiron's pupil, the son of a goddess and of Peleus who was the gentlest of men and third in descent from Zeus, was so disordered in his wits as to be at one time the slave of two seemingly inconsistent passions, meanness, not untainted by avarice, combined with overweening contempt of gods and men."
D: Refashioned:
"'You have wronged me, Apollo, most despicable of gods. I would take my revenge if I only had the power.'

Or his defiance of the river-god Scamander, whose divinity he was ready to assault. Or his cutting of his own hair -- previously dedicated to the river-god Spercheius -- and offering it instead to the dead Patroclus. Or his dragging of Hector's body around Patroclus's tomb and slaughtering prisoners at the funeral pyre.

I refuse to believe Achilles was guilty of any of this. And I won't allow our citizens to believe it either. This is a man raised by the wise centaur Cheiron, born to a goddess and to Peleus -- the gentlest of men, third in descent from Zeus. How could such a person be enslaved by two contradictory vices at once: petty greed and arrogant contempt for both gods and men?"
D: Elegant: Achilles threatening Apollo, fighting a river-god, desecrating Hector's corpse, slaughtering prisoners -- none of this is believable of a man trained by Cheiron, son of a goddess, grandson of Zeus's line. We cannot accept that such a hero was simultaneously enslaved by greed and contempt for the divine.
D: Summary: Socrates catalogs Achilles' worst behaviors in the Iliad -- threatening Apollo, fighting the river Scamander, desecrating Hector's body, killing captives at Patroclus's pyre -- and declares them unbelievable for someone of his noble lineage and education under the centaur Cheiron.
D: Distilled: The worst acts attributed to Achilles are incompatible with his divine lineage and wise education, and must not be believed.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- 'Thou hast wronged me, O far-darter' is from Iliad 22.15-20, Achilles chasing what he thinks is a Trojan but is actually Apollo in disguise -- The river-god is Scamander/Xanthus, Iliad 21.212-382 -- The hair offering: Iliad 23.141-53 (Spercheius was a river in Achilles' homeland of Thessaly) -- Dragging Hector: Iliad 24.14-18 -- Slaughtering captives at the pyre: Iliad 23.175-76 (twelve Trojan prisoners sacrificed) -- Cheiron: the wise centaur who tutored many Greek heroes -- Peleus 'third in descent from Zeus': Zeus > Aeacus > Peleus -- Cross-reference: Iliad 21 for the entire river-battle sequence

D: Original: You are quite right, he replied. And let us equally refuse to believe, or allow to be repeated, the tale of Theseus son of Poseidon, or of Peirithous son of Zeus, going forth as they did to perpetrate a horrid rape; or of any other hero or son of a god daring to do such impious and dreadful things as they falsely ascribe to them in our day: and let us further compel the poets to declare either that these acts were not done by them, or that they were not the sons of gods; --both in the same breath they shall not be permitted to affirm. We will not have them trying to persuade our youth that the gods are the authors of evil, and that heroes are no better than men-sentiments which, as we were saying, are neither pious nor true, for we have already proved that evil cannot come from the gods.
D: Narrative:
"You are quite right," he replied.

"And let us equally refuse to believe, or allow to be repeated, the tale of Theseus son of Poseidon, or of Peirithous son of Zeus, going forth as they did to perpetrate a horrid rape; or of any other hero or son of a god daring to do such impious and dreadful things as they falsely ascribe to them in our day: and let us further compel the poets to declare either that these acts were not done by them, or that they were not the sons of gods; -- both in the same breath they shall not be permitted to affirm. We will not have them trying to persuade our youth that the gods are the authors of evil, and that heroes are no better than men -- sentiments which, as we were saying, are neither pious nor true, for we have already proved that evil cannot come from the gods."
D: Refashioned:
"You're quite right," he replied.

"And we must equally refuse to believe or repeat the story of Theseus, son of Poseidon, and Peirithous, son of Zeus, setting out to commit a terrible abduction. Or any similar tale of heroes or sons of gods performing the impious and dreadful acts falsely attributed to them. We must force the poets to choose: either these acts were never committed, or the characters who committed them were not sons of gods. They cannot have it both ways.

We will not let poets persuade our young people that the gods produce evil or that heroes are no better than ordinary men. As we've already established, evil cannot originate from the gods."
D: Elegant: Poets must choose: either heroes did not commit evil acts, or they were not sons of gods. Both claims cannot stand together. We have already proved evil cannot come from the gods, so divine parentage and wicked deeds are logically incompatible.
D: Summary: Socrates demands that poets choose between attributing evil deeds to heroes and claiming those heroes are sons of gods, since the two claims are logically incompatible given the established principle that evil cannot originate from the divine.
D: Distilled: Divine parentage and wicked deeds are logically incompatible; poets must not attribute both to the same character.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Theseus and Peirithous attempted to abduct Persephone from the underworld; earlier, Theseus helped Peirithous abduct Helen -- Theseus was son of Poseidon (or Aegeus); Peirithous was son of Zeus (or Ixion) -- The logical disjunction ('either...or') shows Plato's philosophical method applied to literary criticism -- 'Evil cannot come from the gods' was established in Book II (379b-c) -- Cross-reference: Euthyphro (5e-6a) on whether the gods can be authors of evil -- This passage summarizes the entire censorship program for theological content

D: Original:
Assuredly not. And further they are likely to have a bad effect on those who hear them; for everybody will begin to excuse his own vices when he is convinced that similar wickednesses are always being perpetrated by --

The kindred of the gods, the relatives of Zeus, whose ancestral altar, the attar of Zeus, is aloft in air on the peak of Ida, and who have

the blood of deities yet flowing in their veins. And therefore let us put an end to such tales, lest they engender laxity of morals among the young.

By all means, he replied. But now that we are determining what classes of subjects are or are not to be spoken of, let us see whether any have been omitted by us. The manner in which gods and demigods and heroes and the world below should be treated has been already laid down.

Very true. And what shall we say about men? That is clearly the remaining portion of our subject.

Clearly so. But we are not in a condition to answer this question at present, my friend.

Why not? Because, if I am not mistaken, we shall have to say that about men poets and story-tellers are guilty of making the gravest misstatements when they tell us that wicked men are often happy, and the good miserable; and that injustice is profitable when undetected, but that justice is a man's own loss and another's gain --these things we shall forbid them to utter, and command them to sing and say the opposite.

To be sure we shall, he replied. But if you admit that I am right in this, then I shall maintain that you have implied the principle for which we have been all along contending.

I grant the truth of your inference. That such things are or are not to be said about men is a question which we cannot determine until we have discovered what justice is, and how naturally advantageous to the possessor, whether he seems to be just or not.

Most true, he said. Enough of the subjects of poetry: let us now speak of the style; and when this has been considered, both matter and manner will have been completely treated.
D: Narrative:
"Assuredly not."

"And further they are likely to have a bad effect on those who hear them; for everybody will begin to excuse his own vices when he is convinced that similar wickednesses are always being perpetrated by --

'The kindred of the gods, the relatives of Zeus, whose ancestral altar, the altar of Zeus, is aloft in air on the peak of Ida, and who have the blood of deities yet flowing in their veins.'

And therefore let us put an end to such tales, lest they engender laxity of morals among the young."

"By all means," he replied.

"But now that we are determining what classes of subjects are or are not to be spoken of, let us see whether any have been omitted by us. The manner in which gods and demigods and heroes and the world below should be treated has been already laid down."

"Very true."

"And what shall we say about men? That is clearly the remaining portion of our subject."

"Clearly so."

"But we are not in a condition to answer this question at present, my friend."

"Why not?"

"Because, if I am not mistaken, we shall have to say that about men, poets and story-tellers are guilty of making the gravest misstatements when they tell us that wicked men are often happy, and the good miserable; and that injustice is profitable when undetected, but that justice is a man's own loss and another's gain -- these things we shall forbid them to utter, and command them to sing and say the opposite."

"To be sure we shall," he replied.

"But if you admit that I am right in this, then I shall maintain that you have implied the principle for which we have been all along contending."

"I grant the truth of your inference."

"That such things are or are not to be said about men is a question which we cannot determine until we have discovered what justice is, and how naturally advantageous to the possessor, whether he seems to be just or not."

"Most true," he said.

"Enough of the subjects of poetry: let us now speak of the style; and when this has been considered, both matter and manner will have been completely treated."
D: Refashioned:
"Absolutely not."

"Furthermore, such stories have a corrosive effect on listeners. Everyone starts excusing their own vices once they're convinced that the same wickedness is routinely committed by 'the kindred of the gods, relatives of Zeus, whose ancestral altar stands atop Mount Ida, and in whose veins the blood of deities still flows.' So we must end such tales before they breed moral laxity in the young."

"By all means," he replied.

"Now let's review whether we've covered all the categories of content. We've established how to treat gods, demigods, heroes, and the underworld."

"True."

"What remains? The portrayal of human beings."

"Clearly."

"But we can't settle that question yet."

"Why not?"

"Because we'd have to say that poets commit their gravest error when they claim that wicked people are often happy while good people suffer, or that injustice is profitable when it goes undetected, while justice benefits others at one's own expense. We'd forbid such claims and require the opposite message."

"Of course we would," he replied.

"But if you agree with that, then you've already accepted the principle we've been arguing for throughout."

"I accept your reasoning."

"However, we can't fully determine what should or shouldn't be said about human beings until we've defined justice and shown that it naturally benefits its possessor, regardless of appearances."

"Very true," he said.

"Enough about the content of poetry. Let's turn to its style -- and once we've covered that, we'll have addressed both subject matter and method."
D: Elegant: People excuse their own vices when they see divine heroes committing the same ones. We've now covered what should be said about gods, demigods, heroes, and the underworld. The portrayal of humans remains, but that question depends on defining justice itself -- which we haven't yet done. For now, we turn from the content of poetry to its style.
D: Summary: Socrates completes the discussion of poetic content by noting that the portrayal of human beings cannot be settled until justice is properly defined. He flags the key falsehood to combat -- that injustice is profitable and justice is a loss -- but defers this question. The discussion now shifts from the subject matter of poetry to its style and mode of presentation.
D: Distilled: The proper portrayal of human beings in poetry depends on first defining justice; meanwhile, the discussion shifts from content to style.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The quoted verse about 'kindred of the gods' is from Aeschylus (possibly the Niobe) -- Mount Ida is near Troy, associated with Zeus worship -- This passage is a crucial structural pivot: it links the censorship discussion to the main argument of the Republic (what is justice?) -- The claim that 'injustice is profitable when undetected' is the central challenge posed by Glaucon and Adeimantus in Book II -- Cross-reference: Books II-IV develop the definition of justice; Book IX (580b-588a) proves justice benefits the just man -- The shift from 'matter' to 'manner' (content to style) introduces the theory of mimesis (imitation)

D: Original:
I do not understand what you mean, said Adeimantus. Then I must make you understand; and perhaps I may be more intelligible if I put the matter in this way. You are aware, I suppose, that all mythology and poetry is a narration of events, either past, present, or to come?

Certainly, he replied. And narration may be either simple narration, or imitation, or a union of the two?
D: Narrative:
"I do not understand what you mean," said Adeimantus.

"Then I must make you understand; and perhaps I may be more intelligible if I put the matter in this way. You are aware, I suppose, that all mythology and poetry is a narration of events, either past, present, or to come?"

"Certainly," he replied.

"And narration may be either simple narration, or imitation, or a union of the two?"
D: Refashioned:
"I don't understand what you mean," said Adeimantus.

"Then let me explain. Perhaps I can make it clearer by putting it this way. You're aware, I assume, that all mythology and poetry consists of narrating events -- past, present, or future?"

"Certainly," he replied.

"And narration can take three forms: simple narration, imitation, or a combination of both?"
D: Elegant: All poetry narrates events. Narration takes three forms: the poet speaking in his own voice (simple narration), the poet speaking as a character (imitation), or a combination of both.
D: Summary: Socrates introduces the theory of poetic modes, explaining that all poetry narrates events and can do so through three methods: simple narration (the poet's own voice), imitation (the poet speaking as a character), or a mixture of both.
D: Distilled: Poetry narrates through three modes: the poet's own voice, imitation of characters, or a combination of both.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- This begins Plato's theory of mimesis (imitation), one of the most influential ideas in Western aesthetics -- The three modes: diegesis (simple narration), mimesis (imitation/dramatic presentation), and mixed -- Adeimantus's confusion ('I do not understand') is a dramatic device allowing Socrates to explain systematically -- Cross-reference: Book X (595a-608b) develops the theory of mimesis much further; Aristotle's Poetics responds directly to this classification

D: Original: That again, he said, I do not quite understand. I fear that I must be a ridiculous teacher when I have so much difficulty in making myself apprehended. Like a bad speaker, therefore, I will not take the whole of the subject, but will break a piece off in illustration of my meaning. You know the first lines of the Iliad, in which the poet says that Chryses prayed Agamemnon to release his daughter, and that Agamemnon flew into a passion with him; whereupon Chryses, failing of his object, invoked the anger of the God against the Achaeans. Now as far as these lines,
D: Narrative:
"That again," he said, "I do not quite understand."

"I fear that I must be a ridiculous teacher when I have so much difficulty in making myself apprehended. Like a bad speaker, therefore, I will not take the whole of the subject, but will break a piece off in illustration of my meaning. You know the first lines of the Iliad, in which the poet says that Chryses prayed Agamemnon to release his daughter, and that Agamemnon flew into a passion with him; whereupon Chryses, failing of his object, invoked the anger of the God against the Achaeans. Now as far as these lines --"
D: Refashioned:
"That I still don't quite understand," he said.

"I'm afraid I must be a terrible teacher if I'm having this much difficulty getting my point across. So like a bad speaker, instead of taking on the whole subject at once, I'll break off a piece to illustrate my meaning.

You know the opening of the Iliad, where Homer tells us that Chryses came to beg Agamemnon to release his daughter, that Agamemnon reacted with fury, and that Chryses, having failed, called down Apollo's wrath upon the Greeks. Now, up to those lines --"
D: Elegant: To illustrate the three modes: consider the Iliad's opening. Homer narrates that Chryses begged Agamemnon, was refused, and then invoked Apollo's anger against the Greeks. In this portion, Homer speaks in his own voice -- this is simple narration.
D: Summary: Socrates uses the opening of Homer's Iliad as a concrete example to illustrate his theory of narrative modes, beginning with the passage where the poet narrates in his own voice the events surrounding Chryses, Agamemnon, and the invocation of Apollo.
D: Distilled: The Iliad's opening demonstrates simple narration, where Homer tells us what happened in his own voice rather than speaking as a character.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- Chryses is a priest of Apollo whose daughter Chryseis was taken as a war prize by Agamemnon (Iliad 1.12ff) -- Socrates' self-deprecation ('a ridiculous teacher') is characteristic Socratic irony -- The pedagogical method of using a concrete example from a well-known text is itself a model of good teaching -- Cross-reference: Iliad Book 1 is the source text being analyzed; Aristotle's Poetics Chapter 3 refines this same distinction between narrative and dramatic modes

D: Original: And he prayed all the Greeks, but especially the two sons of Atreus, the chiefs of the people, the poet is speaking in his own person; he never leads us to suppose that he is any one else. But in what follows he takes the person of Chryses, and then he does all that he can to make us believe that the speaker is not Homer, but the aged priest himself. And in this double form he has cast the entire narrative of the events which occurred at Troy and in Ithaca and throughout the Odyssey.
D: Narrative:
"'And he prayed all the Greeks, but especially the two sons of Atreus, the chiefs of the people' --

the poet is speaking in his own person; he never leads us to suppose that he is any one else. But in what follows he takes the person of Chryses, and then he does all that he can to make us believe that the speaker is not Homer, but the aged priest himself. And in this double form he has cast the entire narrative of the events which occurred at Troy and in Ithaca and throughout the Odyssey."
D: Refashioned:
"'And he prayed to all the Greeks, but especially to the two sons of Atreus, the commanders of the army' -- here the poet is speaking in his own person. He never tries to make us think he is anyone else.

But in what follows, Homer takes on the character of Chryses. He does everything he can to make us believe the speaker is not Homer, but the aged priest himself.

And in this double form -- alternating between his own voice and the voices of his characters -- Homer has cast the entire narrative of events at Troy, in Ithaca, and throughout the Odyssey."
D: Elegant: When Homer says 'he prayed to all the Greeks,' the poet speaks in his own voice. When Homer then speaks as Chryses, he tries to make us forget the poet and believe we hear the priest himself. The entire Iliad and Odyssey alternate between these two modes.
D: Summary: Socrates demonstrates how Homer alternates between speaking in his own voice (simple narration) and speaking as his characters (imitation), showing that this mixed mode characterizes the entire Iliad and Odyssey.
D: Distilled: Homer's epics alternate between the poet's own voice and dramatic impersonation of characters, exemplifying the mixed narrative mode.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- 'The two sons of Atreus' are Agamemnon and Menelaus -- The analysis shows Homer as the paradigmatic 'mixed mode' poet -- Plato's insight that Homer 'does all that he can to make us believe' the speaker is the character anticipates modern narratological concepts of focalization and free indirect discourse -- 'Throughout the Odyssey' -- Socrates extends the observation beyond the Iliad to encompass all of Homeric epic -- Cross-reference: Aristotle Poetics 1448a19-24 credits Homer with being exceptional among epic poets for using dramatic speeches

D: Original:
Yes. And a narrative it remains both in the speeches which the poet recites from time to time and in the intermediate passages?

Quite true. But when the poet speaks in the person of another, may we not say that he assimilates his style to that of the person who, as he informs you, is going to speak?

Certainly. And this assimilation of himself to another, either by the use of voice or gesture, is the imitation of the person whose character he assumes?

Of course. Then in this case the narrative of the poet may be said to proceed by way of imitation?
D: Narrative:
"Yes."

"And a narrative it remains both in the speeches which the poet recites from time to time and in the intermediate passages?"

"Quite true."

"But when the poet speaks in the person of another, may we not say that he assimilates his style to that of the person who, as he informs you, is going to speak?"

"Certainly."

"And this assimilation of himself to another, either by the use of voice or gesture, is the imitation of the person whose character he assumes?"

"Of course."

"Then in this case the narrative of the poet may be said to proceed by way of imitation?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes."

"And it remains narration both in the direct speeches and in the connecting passages between them?"

"Quite true."

"But when the poet speaks as one of his characters, wouldn't we say he adapts his style to match the person he's about to voice?"

"Certainly."

"And this adapting of himself to another person -- through voice, gesture, or manner -- is the imitation of that person's character?"

"Of course."

"So in those passages, the poet's narration proceeds by way of imitation?"
D: Elegant: When a poet speaks as a character, adapting his voice and manner, that is imitation. The narrative then proceeds not through the poet's own voice but through dramatic impersonation.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes that when a poet speaks in the person of a character, adapting voice and style to match that character, the narration becomes imitation -- the poet assumes another's identity rather than speaking in his own voice.
D: Distilled: When a poet adapts his voice to speak as a character, narration becomes imitation -- the poet assumes another's identity.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The definition of mimesis as 'assimilation of oneself to another' is crucial -- it encompasses voice, gesture, and character -- This anticipates modern acting theory and Stanislavski's concept of 'becoming' the character -- The passage builds the logical chain: speaking as another = adapting style = imitating character -- Cross-reference: Book X (598b-599a) where mimesis is defined more critically as being 'three removes from the truth'

D: Original:
Very true. Or, if the poet everywhere appears and never conceals himself, then again the imitation is dropped, and his poetry becomes simple narration. However, in order that I may make my meaning quite clear, and that you may no more say, I don't understand,' I will show how the change might be effected. If Homer had said, 'The priest came, having his daughter's ransom in his hands, supplicating the Achaeans, and above all the kings;' and then if, instead of speaking in the person of Chryses, he had continued in his own person, the words would have been, not imitation, but simple narration. The passage would have run as follows (I am no poet, and therefore I drop the metre), 'The priest came and prayed the gods on behalf of the Greeks that they might capture Troy and return safely home, but begged that they would give him back his daughter, and take the ransom which he brought, and respect the God. Thus he spoke, and the other Greeks revered the priest and assented. But Agamemnon was wroth, and bade him depart and not come again, lest the staff and chaplets of the God should be of no avail to him --the daughter of Chryses should not be released, he said --she should grow old with him in Argos. And then he told him to go away and not to provoke him, if he intended to get home unscathed. And the old man went away in fear and silence, and, when he had left the camp, he called upon Apollo by his many names, reminding him of everything which he had done pleasing to him, whether in building his temples, or in offering sacrifice, and praying that his good deeds might be returned to him, and that the Achaeans might expiate his tears by the arrows of the god,' --and so on. In this way the whole becomes simple narrative.

I understand, he said. Or you may suppose the opposite case --that the intermediate passages are omitted, and the dialogue only left.

That also, he said, I understand; you mean, for example, as in tragedy.

"I understand," he said.

D: Refashioned:
"Exactly.

Or, if the poet always speaks in his own voice and never hides behind characters, then there's no imitation at all -- just simple narration.

Let me make this crystal clear so you won't say 'I don't understand' again. If Homer had written: 'The priest came with his daughter's ransom, supplicating the Greeks, especially the kings' -- and then continued in his own voice instead of speaking as Chryses, it would have been simple narration, not imitation. Something like this (and since I'm no poet, I'll drop the meter):

'The priest came and prayed that the Greeks might take Troy and return home safely. He begged them to return his daughter and accept the ransom he brought, out of respect for the god. The other Greeks agreed, but Agamemnon was furious and ordered him to leave and never return, warning that his priestly insignia would not protect him. The daughter would not be released -- she would grow old with him in Argos. He told the priest to leave without provoking him if he wanted to get home safely. The old man departed in fear and silence. Once outside the camp, he prayed to Apollo, invoking the god's many names, reminding him of temples built and sacrifices offered, asking that his service be repaid and that the Greeks pay for his tears through the god's arrows.'

And so on. The whole thing becomes simple narrative."

"I understand," he said.

"Now imagine the opposite: the connecting passages are removed and only the dialogue remains."

"I understand that too -- you mean like in tragedy."
D: Elegant: If Homer had told the entire Chryses story in his own voice -- 'the priest came, prayed, was refused by Agamemnon, and then called on Apollo' -- it would be pure narration with no imitation. Conversely, if we removed everything except the characters' direct speech, we would have pure dramatic imitation, like tragedy.
D: Summary: Socrates demonstrates the difference between narrative modes by rewriting Homer's opening in pure narration (the poet reporting everything in his own voice, without character speech), and then noting that pure imitation (only character dialogue, no narrator) is what we find in tragedy.
D: Distilled: Pure narration means the poet reports everything in his own voice; pure imitation means only characters speak, as in tragedy.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- Socrates' prose paraphrase of Iliad 1.12-42 is a remarkable early example of literary criticism through creative rewriting -- 'I am no poet, and therefore I drop the metre' shows characteristic Socratic modesty -- The 'staff and chaplets of the God' are Apollo's priestly insignia -- Adeimantus's recognition that 'the opposite case' = tragedy shows the classification becoming clear -- Cross-reference: Aristotle Poetics 1449b develops the theory of tragedy as pure dramatic imitation -- The three modes are now fully illustrated: pure narration (dithyramb), pure imitation (tragedy/comedy), mixed (epic)

D: Original: You have conceived my meaning perfectly; and if I mistake not, what you failed to apprehend before is now made clear to you, that poetry and mythology are, in some cases, wholly imitative --instances of this are supplied by tragedy and comedy; there is likewise the opposite style, in which the my poet is the only speaker --of this the dithyramb affords the best example; and the combination of both is found in epic, and in several other styles of poetry. Do I take you with me?
D: Narrative: "You have conceived my meaning perfectly; and if I mistake not, what you failed to apprehend before is now made clear to you, that poetry and mythology are, in some cases, wholly imitative -- instances of this are supplied by tragedy and comedy; there is likewise the opposite style, in which the poet is the only speaker -- of this the dithyramb affords the best example; and the combination of both is found in epic, and in several other styles of poetry. Do I take you with me?"
D: Refashioned: "You've grasped my meaning perfectly. And if I'm not mistaken, what was unclear before is now evident: poetry and mythology come in three forms. Some are wholly imitative -- tragedy and comedy, where characters speak for themselves. Then there's the opposite style, where the poet alone speaks -- the dithyramb is the best example. And the combination of both is found in epic poetry and several other forms. Are you following me?"
D: Elegant: Poetry takes three forms: wholly imitative (tragedy and comedy), wholly narrative (dithyramb), and mixed (epic). The distinction depends on whether characters, the poet, or both do the speaking.
D: Summary: Socrates summarizes the three modes of poetry: wholly imitative (tragedy and comedy), wholly narrative with the poet as sole speaker (dithyramb), and the mixed mode combining both (epic poetry).
D: Distilled: The three modes of poetry are pure imitation (tragedy/comedy), pure narration (dithyramb), and their combination (epic).
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The dithyramb was a choral hymn to Dionysus, performed without dramatic characters -- Tragedy and comedy are 'wholly imitative' because they consist entirely of characters speaking in their own voices (no narrator) -- Epic (Homer) is 'mixed' because the poet narrates and characters speak -- 'Several other styles' may include lyric poetry -- Cross-reference: Aristotle Poetics Chapter 3 (1448a19-b3) refines this classification; Book X (595c) revisits the classification

D: Original:
Yes, he said; I see now what you meant. I will ask you to remember also what I began by saying, that we had done with the subject and might proceed to the style.

Yes, I remember. In saying this, I intended to imply that we must come to an understanding about the mimetic art, --whether the poets, in narrating their stories, are to be allowed by us to imitate, and if so, whether in whole or in part, and if the latter, in what parts; or should all imitation be prohibited?

You mean, I suspect, to ask whether tragedy and comedy shall be admitted into our State?

Yes, I said; but there may be more than this in question: I really do not know as yet, but whither the argument may blow, thither we go.

And go we will, he said. Then, Adeimantus, let me ask you whether our guardians ought to be imitators; or rather, has not this question been decided by the rule already laid down that one man can only do one thing well, and not many; and that if he attempt many, he will altogether fall of gaining much reputation in any?

Certainly. And this is equally true of imitation; no one man can imitate many things as well as he would imitate a single one?

He cannot. Then the same person will hardly be able to play a serious part in life, and at the same time to be an imitator and imitate many other parts as well; for even when two species of imitation are nearly allied, the same persons cannot succeed in both, as, for example, the writers of tragedy and comedy --did you not just now call them imitations?

Yes, I did; and you are right in thinking that the same persons cannot succeed in both.

Any more than they can be rhapsodists and actors at once?

True. Neither are comic and tragic actors the same; yet all these things are but imitations.

They are so. And human nature, Adeimantus, appears to have been coined into yet smaller pieces, and to be as incapable of imitating many things well, as of performing well the actions of which the imitations are copies.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said; "I see now what you meant."

"I will ask you to remember also what I began by saying, that we had done with the subject and might proceed to the style."

"Yes, I remember."

"In saying this, I intended to imply that we must come to an understanding about the mimetic art, -- whether the poets, in narrating their stories, are to be allowed by us to imitate, and if so, whether in whole or in part, and if the latter, in what parts; or should all imitation be prohibited?"

"You mean, I suspect, to ask whether tragedy and comedy shall be admitted into our State?"

"Yes," I said; "but there may be more than this in question: I really do not know as yet, but whither the argument may blow, thither we go."

"And go we will," he said.

"Then, Adeimantus, let me ask you whether our guardians ought to be imitators; or rather, has not this question been decided by the rule already laid down that one man can only do one thing well, and not many; and that if he attempt many, he will altogether fail of gaining much reputation in any?"

"Certainly."

"And this is equally true of imitation; no one man can imitate many things as well as he would imitate a single one?"

"He cannot."

"Then the same person will hardly be able to play a serious part in life, and at the same time to be an imitator and imitate many other parts as well; for even when two species of imitation are nearly allied, the same persons cannot succeed in both, as, for example, the writers of tragedy and comedy -- did you not just now call them imitations?"

"Yes, I did; and you are right in thinking that the same persons cannot succeed in both."

"Any more than they can be rhapsodists and actors at once?"

"True."

"Neither are comic and tragic actors the same; yet all these things are but imitations."

"They are so."

"And human nature, Adeimantus, appears to have been coined into yet smaller pieces, and to be as incapable of imitating many things well, as of performing well the actions of which the imitations are copies."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," he said, "I see what you meant now."

"Good. And recall what I said at the beginning: that we'd finished with content and could move to style."

"I remember."

"By that I meant we need to reach an understanding about imitation. Should poets be allowed to use it in their storytelling? If so, should they imitate wholly or only in part? And if only in part, what kinds of imitation are acceptable? Or should all imitation be forbidden?"

"I suspect you're really asking whether tragedy and comedy should be admitted into our state."

"Yes, but the question may go even further than that. I don't know yet where the argument will lead -- but wherever it goes, we follow."

"And follow we will," he said.

"Then, Adeimantus, should our guardians be imitators? Actually, hasn't this already been settled by our earlier principle that one person can only do one thing well -- and that attempting many things means excelling at none?"

"Certainly."

"The same applies to imitation: no one can imitate many things as well as a single thing."

"Agreed."

"So a person can hardly play a serious role in real life while also being a skilled imitator of many different characters. Even closely related forms of imitation -- writing tragedy and comedy, for instance -- can't be mastered by the same person. You did call those imitations, didn't you?"

"I did, and you're right that the same person can't succeed at both."

"Nor can one be both a reciter and an actor."

"True."

"Comic and tragic actors aren't the same people either, yet both are doing imitation."

"They are."

"Human nature, Adeimantus, seems to have been minted in even smaller denominations than we thought -- as incapable of imitating many things well as of actually performing the many actions those imitations represent."
D: Elegant: Should guardians be imitators? Our principle of specialization answers this: one person does one thing well. Since even closely related forms of imitation -- tragedy and comedy -- cannot be mastered by the same person, human nature is too specialized to allow guardians to be skilled imitators of many characters while also performing their real duties.
D: Summary: Socrates applies the principle of specialization to imitation, arguing that since one person can only do one thing well, guardians should not be imitators. He illustrates this with examples: writers of tragedy cannot also write comedy, and comic and tragic actors are never the same people.
D: Distilled: The principle of specialization means guardians cannot be skilled imitators of many characters while also fulfilling their real role as defenders of the state.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The principle of specialization (one person, one craft) was established in Book II (369e-370c) as the founding principle of the city -- 'Whither the argument may blow, thither we go' expresses Socratic commitment to following the logos wherever it leads -- Rhapsodists recited epic poetry; actors performed in dramatic productions -- The metaphor of human nature being 'coined into small pieces' compares human capacities to currency -- Cross-reference: Ion (535a-536d) on the rhapsode's claim to universal expertise; Republic Book II (370a-b) on specialization

D: Original: Quite true, he replied. If then we adhere to our original notion and bear in mind that our guardians, setting aside every other business, are to dedicate themselves wholly to the maintenance of freedom in the State, making this their craft, and engaging in no work which does not bear on this end, they ought not to practise or imitate anything else; if they imitate at all, they should imitate from youth upward only those characters which are suitable to their profession --the courageous, temperate, holy, free, and the like; but they should not depict or be skilful at imitating any kind of illiberality or baseness, lest from imitation they should come to be what they imitate. Did you never observe how imitations, beginning in early youth and continuing far into life, at length grow into habits and become a second nature, affecting body, voice, and mind?
D: Narrative:
"Quite true," he replied.

"If then we adhere to our original notion and bear in mind that our guardians, setting aside every other business, are to dedicate themselves wholly to the maintenance of freedom in the State, making this their craft, and engaging in no work which does not bear on this end, they ought not to practise or imitate anything else; if they imitate at all, they should imitate from youth upward only those characters which are suitable to their profession -- the courageous, temperate, holy, free, and the like; but they should not depict or be skilful at imitating any kind of illiberality or baseness, lest from imitation they should come to be what they imitate. Did you never observe how imitations, beginning in early youth and continuing far into life, at length grow into habits and become a second nature, affecting body, voice, and mind?"
D: Refashioned:
"Quite true," he replied.

"So if we hold to our original principle -- that guardians must dedicate themselves entirely to maintaining freedom in the state, making this their sole craft -- then they should not practice or imitate anything unrelated to that purpose. If they imitate at all, they should imitate only from youth onward characters appropriate to their calling: the courageous, the disciplined, the reverent, the free-spirited, and similar types. They should never practice imitating anything base or ignoble, for fear that imitation may become reality.

Haven't you noticed how imitations, when they begin in childhood and continue through life, eventually harden into habits and become second nature -- affecting body, voice, and mind alike?"
D: Elegant: Guardians must imitate only characters suitable to their calling -- the brave, temperate, holy, and free. They must never practice imitating baseness, because imitation begun in youth hardens into habit and becomes second nature, transforming body, voice, and mind.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that guardians should imitate only virtuous characters suitable to their role, never base ones, because sustained imitation becomes habit and transforms a person's entire nature -- body, voice, and mind.
D: Distilled: We become what we imitate: sustained practice of imitating virtue or vice transforms habit, body, voice, and character.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The principle 'we become what we imitate' is one of Plato's most psychologically penetrating claims -- It anticipates modern research on behavioral modeling and neuroplasticity -- 'Affecting body, voice, and mind' shows Plato understood imitation as holistic transformation, not mere surface mimicry -- The list of acceptable qualities (courageous, temperate, holy, free) maps onto the cardinal virtues -- Cross-reference: Book X (606b) on how poetic imitation strengthens the lower parts of the soul; Laws 655d-656b on habituation through imitation

D: Original:
Yes, certainly, he said. Then, I said, we will not allow those for whom we profess a care and of whom we say that they ought to be good men, to imitate a woman, whether young or old, quarrelling with her husband, or striving and vaunting against the gods in conceit of her happiness, or when she is in affliction, or sorrow, or weeping; and certainly not one who is in sickness, love, or labour.

Very right, he said. Neither must they represent slaves, male or female, performing the offices of slaves?
D: Narrative:
"Yes, certainly," he said.

"Then," I said, "we will not allow those for whom we profess a care and of whom we say that they ought to be good men, to imitate a woman, whether young or old, quarrelling with her husband, or striving and vaunting against the gods in conceit of her happiness, or when she is in affliction, or sorrow, or weeping; and certainly not one who is in sickness, love, or labour."

"Very right," he said.

"Neither must they represent slaves, male or female, performing the offices of slaves?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, certainly," he said.

"Then we will not allow those we're raising to be good men to imitate a woman -- whether young or old -- quarreling with her husband, boasting against the gods in proud confidence, or falling into grief, sorrow, and weeping. And certainly not a woman who is sick, lovesick, or in labor."

"Very right," he said.

"Nor should they play the part of slaves, male or female, doing the work of slaves?"
D: Elegant: Those being raised as guardians must not imitate women quarreling, lamenting, or in states of physical distress, nor should they play the parts of slaves performing servile tasks.
D: Summary: Socrates specifies that guardians must not imitate women in various emotional states (quarreling, lamenting, boasting against gods) or physical conditions (sickness, love, childbirth), nor should they imitate slaves performing servile duties.
D: Distilled: Guardians must not imitate women in emotional distress or slaves in servitude, as such imitation degrades the imitator's character.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The prohibition on imitating women reflects both Plato's concern about emotional excess (women were stereotyped as more emotional in Greek culture) and his later, more radical position in Book V that women can be guardians -- 'Striving against the gods' may reference Niobe, who boasted of her children and was punished by Apollo and Artemis -- 'Labour' refers to childbirth -- Cross-reference: Book V (451d-457b) where Plato argues women can serve as guardians, creating tension with this passage's assumptions

D: Original: They must not. And surely not bad men, whether cowards or any others, who do the reverse of what we have just been prescribing, who scold or mock or revile one another in drink or out of in drink or, or who in any other manner sin against themselves and their neighbours in word or deed, as the manner of such is. Neither should they be trained to imitate the action or speech of men or women who are mad or bad; for madness, like vice, is to be known but not to be practised or imitated.
D: Narrative:
"They must not."

"And surely not bad men, whether cowards or any others, who do the reverse of what we have just been prescribing, who scold or mock or revile one another in drink or out of drink, or who in any other manner sin against themselves and their neighbours in word or deed, as the manner of such is. Neither should they be trained to imitate the action or speech of men or women who are mad or bad; for madness, like vice, is to be known but not to be practised or imitated."
D: Refashioned:
"They must not."

"And certainly not bad people -- cowards or anyone else who does the opposite of what we've prescribed: people who scold, mock, or insult one another, whether drunk or sober, or who in any way offend against themselves and their neighbors in word or deed, as such people do. Nor should guardians be trained to imitate the speech or actions of men or women who are mad or evil. Madness, like vice, should be understood but never practiced or imitated."
D: Elegant: Guardians must not imitate cowards, drunkards, slanderers, or anyone who sins against others. Madness and vice should be understood but never practiced or imitated.
D: Summary: Socrates prohibits guardians from imitating bad people of any kind -- cowards, drunkards, those who insult others -- and establishes the principle that madness and vice should be recognized and understood but never practiced or imitated.
D: Distilled: Madness and vice should be known but never practiced or imitated; understanding evil does not require performing it.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The principle 'known but not practiced' (gnosteon, ou mimeteon) is a key educational maxim -- It distinguishes between intellectual understanding of vice and experiential practice of it -- 'In drink or out of drink' covers both intoxicated and sober misbehavior -- Cross-reference: Book III (409a-c) applies a similar principle to judges, who should know evil through observation, not personal experience -- This passage also anticipates modern debates about violent media and behavioral modeling

D: Original:
Very true, he replied. Neither may they imitate smiths or other artificers, or oarsmen, or boatswains, or the like?

How can they, he said, when they are not allowed to apply their minds to the callings of any of these?

Nor may they imitate the neighing of horses, the bellowing of bulls, the murmur of rivers and roll of the ocean, thunder, and all that sort of thing?

Nay, he said, if madness be forbidden, neither may they copy the behaviour of madmen.

You mean, I said, if I understand you aright, that there is one sort of narrative style which may be employed by a truly good man when he has anything to say, and that another sort will be used by a man of an opposite character and education.
D: Narrative:
"Very true," he replied.

"Neither may they imitate smiths or other artificers, or oarsmen, or boatswains, or the like?"

"How can they," he said, "when they are not allowed to apply their minds to the callings of any of these?"

"Nor may they imitate the neighing of horses, the bellowing of bulls, the murmur of rivers and roll of the ocean, thunder, and all that sort of thing?"

"Nay," he said, "if madness be forbidden, neither may they copy the behaviour of madmen."

"You mean," I said, "if I understand you aright, that there is one sort of narrative style which may be employed by a truly good man when he has anything to say, and that another sort will be used by a man of an opposite character and education."
D: Refashioned:
"Very true," he replied.

"Nor may they imitate blacksmiths, other craftsmen, oarsmen, boatswains, or the like?"

"How could they," he said, "when they're not allowed to concern themselves with any of those trades?"

"And may they imitate the neighing of horses, the bellowing of bulls, the murmur of rivers, the crash of the sea, thunder, and all that sort of thing?"

"No," he said. "If we've forbidden madness, we can hardly allow them to imitate madmen."

"So you're saying, if I understand you correctly, that there's one kind of narrative style suited to a truly good person, and another kind suited to someone of the opposite character and upbringing."
D: Elegant: Guardians may not imitate craftsmen's labor or nature's sounds -- horses, bulls, rivers, thunder. As Adeimantus observes, if imitating madness is forbidden, so is imitating the behavior of madmen. This implies two distinct narrative styles: one suited to the good person, another to the bad.
D: Summary: Socrates extends the prohibition to imitating craftsmen at work and natural sounds, and Adeimantus adds that if madness is forbidden, imitating madmen must be too. This leads Socrates to propose that there are two fundamentally different narrative styles corresponding to two types of character.
D: Distilled: The restrictions on imitation reveal two fundamental narrative styles: one suited to good character, another to bad.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The prohibition on imitating craftsmen follows from the specialization principle -- Adeimantus's witty response about madmen shows him actively engaging with the argument -- The list of natural sounds (horses, bulls, rivers, thunder) suggests vocal mimicry in performance -- The two narrative styles will be developed as the 'simple' (mostly narration) and the 'imitative' (mostly dramatic) -- Cross-reference: Book II (370a-c) on specialization; the two styles are developed in 396c-397d

D: Original: And which are these two sorts? he asked. Suppose, I answered, that a just and good man in the course of a narration comes on some saying or action of another good man, --I should imagine that he will like to personate him, and will not be ashamed of this sort of imitation: he will be most ready to play the part of the good man when he is acting firmly and wisely; in a less degree when he is overtaken by illness or love or drink, or has met with any other disaster. But when he comes to a character which is unworthy of him, he will not make a study of that; he will disdain such a person, and will assume his likeness, if at all, for a moment only when he is performing some good action; at other times he will be ashamed to play a part which he has never practised, nor will he like to fashion and frame himself after the baser models; he feels the employment of such an art, unless in jest, to be beneath him, and his mind revolts at it.
D: Narrative:
"And which are these two sorts?" he asked.

"Suppose," I answered, "that a just and good man in the course of a narration comes on some saying or action of another good man, -- I should imagine that he will like to personate him, and will not be ashamed of this sort of imitation: he will be most ready to play the part of the good man when he is acting firmly and wisely; in a less degree when he is overtaken by illness or love or drink, or has met with any other disaster. But when he comes to a character which is unworthy of him, he will not make a study of that; he will disdain such a person, and will assume his likeness, if at all, for a moment only when he is performing some good action; at other times he will be ashamed to play a part which he has never practised, nor will he like to fashion and frame himself after the baser models; he feels the employment of such an art, unless in jest, to be beneath him, and his mind revolts at it."
D: Refashioned:
"What are these two kinds?" he asked.

"Imagine a just and good man telling a story where he encounters the words or deeds of another good man. He'll happily take on that role -- he'll feel no shame in that kind of imitation. He'll be most willing to play the good character when that character acts with firmness and wisdom, and somewhat less willing when the character is weakened by illness, love, drink, or some other misfortune.

But when the story involves an unworthy character, he'll refuse to study that role. He'll look down on such a person and adopt the likeness only briefly, if the character happens to be doing something good. Otherwise, he'll be ashamed to play a part he's never practiced, and he'll refuse to mold himself after inferior models. Unless it's done in jest, such imitation feels beneath him -- his very mind rebels against it."
D: Elegant: A good man willingly imitates other good men acting wisely. He reluctantly imitates good men in moments of weakness, and only briefly imitates base characters when they happen to act well. His mind revolts at sustained imitation of inferior models -- it feels beneath him.
D: Summary: Socrates describes how a good person approaches imitation: willingly and without shame when portraying another good person, reluctantly when that person shows weakness, and with revulsion when asked to portray a base character, refusing to mold himself after inferior models.
D: Distilled: A good person willingly imitates virtue, reluctantly imitates weakness, and revolts at sustained imitation of vice.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- This passage reveals the psychological dynamics of imitation: willingness correlates with moral affinity -- 'He feels the employment of such an art to be beneath him' shows that good character creates natural resistance to imitating vice -- The gradations (willing/less willing/ashamed) show Plato's nuanced understanding of how identity shapes artistic engagement -- 'Unless in jest' allows for parody and humor -- Cross-reference: Book X (605c-606b) on how even good people can be seduced by imitation of emotional excess

Certainly, he said; that is the model which such a speaker must necessarily take.

D: Refashioned:
"That's what I'd expect," he replied.

"So this good person will adopt a narrative mode like what we saw in Homer -- a style that combines both imitation and narration. But there will be very little imitation and a great deal of narration. Do you agree?"

"Certainly," he said. "That's the model such a speaker would naturally follow."
D: Elegant: The good storyteller uses a mixed style like Homer's, but with very little imitation and much narration -- reflecting his reluctance to impersonate characters other than virtuous ones.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes that the good person's narrative style will be mostly narration with very little imitation, reflecting his natural reluctance to impersonate anyone other than virtuous characters.
D: Distilled: The good narrator's style is predominantly narration with minimal imitation, reflecting moral selectivity about whose character to assume.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- This establishes the normative style for the ideal state: mostly diegesis (narration) with limited mimesis (imitation) -- The good person's narrative style directly reflects his moral character -- Homer serves as the model despite being 'mixed,' because the approved style permits some imitation -- Cross-reference: 397d-398b where this style is formally adopted for the ideal state

D: Original: But there is another sort of character who will narrate anything, and, the worse lie is, the more unscrupulous he will be; nothing will be too bad for him: and he will be ready to imitate anything, not as a joke, but in right good earnest, and before a large company. As I was just now saying, he will attempt to represent the roll of thunder, the noise of wind and hall, or the creaking of wheels, and pulleys, and the various sounds of flutes; pipes, trumpets, and all sorts of instruments: he will bark like a dog, bleat like a sheep, or crow like a cock; his entire art will consist in imitation of voice and gesture, and there will be very little narration.
D: Narrative: "But there is another sort of character who will narrate anything, and, the worse he is, the more unscrupulous he will be; nothing will be too bad for him: and he will be ready to imitate anything, not as a joke, but in right good earnest, and before a large company. As I was just now saying, he will attempt to represent the roll of thunder, the noise of wind and hail, or the creaking of wheels, and pulleys, and the various sounds of flutes; pipes, trumpets, and all sorts of instruments: he will bark like a dog, bleat like a sheep, or crow like a cock; his entire art will consist in imitation of voice and gesture, and there will be very little narration."
D: Refashioned:
"But there's another type of person who will narrate anything at all, and the worse his character, the less discriminating he'll be. Nothing will be too base for him. He'll be ready to imitate anything -- not as a joke, but in dead seriousness, in front of large audiences.

As I was saying, he'll try to reproduce the rumble of thunder, the howl of wind and hail, the creaking of wheels and pulleys, the sounds of flutes, pipes, trumpets, and every kind of instrument. He'll bark like a dog, bleat like a sheep, crow like a rooster. His entire art will consist of vocal and gestural imitation, with almost no narration at all."
D: Elegant: The opposite type of narrator will imitate anything without discrimination -- thunder, animals, instruments -- performing every sound and gesture before large audiences in complete earnest. His art is all imitation and almost no narration, reflecting a character without moral boundaries.
D: Summary: Socrates describes the opposite type of narrator: one who imitates everything without discrimination, from thunder to animal sounds to musical instruments, performing entirely through vocal and gestural mimicry with almost no narration, reflecting a total lack of moral selectivity.
D: Distilled: The undiscriminating narrator imitates everything -- sounds, animals, instruments -- reflecting a character with no moral boundaries about what roles to assume.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- This describes what we might call the 'total performer' or pantomime -- someone whose art is pure imitation -- The detail about imitating dogs, sheep, and roosters suggests actual Greek performance practices -- 'Not as a joke, but in right good earnest' contrasts with the good man's allowance for jest (3.28) -- The moral argument: artistic indiscriminateness reflects moral indiscriminateness -- Cross-reference: Ion (535b-e) on the rhapsode who claims to become every character; Book X (598c-d) on the imitator who produces 'appearances' of everything

D: Original: That, he said, will be his mode of speaking. These, then, are the two kinds of style? Yes.
