D: Narrative:
"That," he said, "will be his mode of speaking."

"These, then, are the two kinds of style?"

"Yes."

"And you would agree with me in saying that one of them is simple and has but slight changes; and if the harmony and rhythm are also chosen for their simplicity, the result is that the speaker, if he speaks correctly, is always pretty much the same in style, and he will keep within the limits of a single harmony (for the changes are not great), and in like manner he will make use of nearly the same rhythm?"
D: Refashioned:
"That will be his mode of speaking," he said.

"So these are the two kinds of style?"

"Yes."

"And would you agree that one of them is simple, with only minor variations? If the harmony and rhythm are also chosen for simplicity, the result is that the speaker maintains a consistent style throughout. He stays within the range of a single musical mode (since the variations are slight) and uses a nearly uniform rhythm."
D: Elegant: There are two styles: the simple style has minimal variation, uses a single harmony and consistent rhythm, and produces a uniform effect. This reflects the narrator who imitates only virtuous characters.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes that the simple narrative style, corresponding to the good person's mode, involves minimal variation and can be accompanied by a single harmony and a consistent rhythm, producing a uniform and restrained aesthetic.
D: Distilled: The good narrator's simple style requires only a single harmony and consistent rhythm, reflecting its moral and aesthetic restraint.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The connection between narrative mode and musical accompaniment is crucial: style determines what harmonies and rhythms are needed -- 'A single harmony' anticipates the discussion of approved musical modes (Dorian and Phrygian) -- The aesthetic ideal of simplicity reflects the moral ideal of consistency -- Cross-reference: 398c-399e on the specific harmonies and rhythms to be permitted

D: Original:
That is quite true, he said. Whereas the other requires all sorts of harmonies and all sorts of rhythms, if the music and the style are to correspond, because the style has all sorts of changes.

That is also perfectly true, he replied. And do not the two styles, or the mixture of the two, comprehend all poetry, and every form of expression in words? No one can say anything except in one or other of them or in both together.

They include all, he said. And shall we receive into our State all the three styles, or one only of the two unmixed styles? or would you include the mixed?

I should prefer only to admit the pure imitator of virtue.

Yes, I said, Adeimantus, but the mixed style is also very charming: and indeed the pantomimic, which is the opposite of the one chosen by you, is the most popular style with children and their attendants, and with the world in general.

I do not deny it. But I suppose you would argue that such a style is unsuitable to our State, in which human nature is not twofold or manifold, for one man plays one part only?

Yes; quite unsuitable. And this is the reason why in our State, and in our State only, we shall find a shoemaker to be a shoemaker and not a pilot also, and a husbandman to be a husbandman and not a dicast also, and a soldier a soldier and not a trader also, and the same throughout?
D: Narrative:
"That is quite true," he said.

"Whereas the other requires all sorts of harmonies and all sorts of rhythms, if the music and the style are to correspond, because the style has all sorts of changes."

"That is also perfectly true," he replied.

"And do not the two styles, or the mixture of the two, comprehend all poetry, and every form of expression in words? No one can say anything except in one or other of them or in both together."

"They include all," he said.

"And shall we receive into our State all the three styles, or one only of the two unmixed styles? or would you include the mixed?"

"I should prefer only to admit the pure imitator of virtue."

"Yes," I said, "Adeimantus, but the mixed style is also very charming: and indeed the pantomimic, which is the opposite of the one chosen by you, is the most popular style with children and their attendants, and with the world in general."

"I do not deny it."

"But I suppose you would argue that such a style is unsuitable to our State, in which human nature is not twofold or manifold, for one man plays one part only?"

"Yes; quite unsuitable."

"And this is the reason why in our State, and in our State only, we shall find a shoemaker to be a shoemaker and not a pilot also, and a husbandman to be a husbandman and not a dicast also, and a soldier a soldier and not a trader also, and the same throughout?"
D: Refashioned:
"That's quite true," he said.

"The other style, by contrast, requires every kind of harmony and every kind of rhythm to match its constant shifts and variations."

"Perfectly true," he replied.

"And don't these two styles, or their combination, account for all poetry and every form of verbal expression? Everything that can be said falls into one or the other, or a mixture of both."

"They cover everything," he said.

"So shall we admit all three into our state -- or only one of the two unmixed styles? Or the mixed one?"

"I would prefer to admit only the pure imitator of virtue."

"Yes, Adeimantus, but the mixed style has its charms. And the pantomimic style -- the opposite of your choice -- is the most popular with children, their caretakers, and the general public."

"I don't deny it."

"But you'd argue it's unsuitable for our state, where human nature is not divided or multifaceted -- each person plays only one role?"

"Exactly -- quite unsuitable."

"And that's why in our state, and ours alone, a shoemaker is just a shoemaker and not a pilot too; a farmer is a farmer and not a judge too; a soldier is a soldier and not a merchant too -- and so on throughout?"
D: Elegant: The imitative style demands every harmony and rhythm; the simple style needs only one of each. All expression falls into one style or the other or a mixture. For our state, where each person plays one role only, we admit only the style of the virtuous narrator -- though the pantomimic style is admittedly the most popular with audiences.
D: Summary: Socrates contrasts the two styles (simple requiring one harmony, imitative requiring many), notes that all expression falls into these categories, and concludes that the ideal state should admit only the virtuous narrator's simple style, even though the pantomimic style is more popular with audiences.
D: Distilled: The ideal state admits only the simple style of virtuous narration, consistent with the principle that each citizen plays one role only.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Adeimantus choosing 'the pure imitator of virtue' shows him slightly misunderstanding: Socrates means the narrator who mostly narrates and rarely imitates -- The pantomimic style's popularity anticipates Book X's concern with art that appeals to the masses -- 'One man plays one part only' restates the specialization principle from Book II -- The examples (shoemaker/pilot, farmer/judge, soldier/trader) illustrate how specialization pervades the ideal state -- Cross-reference: Book II (369d-370c) on the origin of the city through specialization; Book IV (433a-b) on justice as each doing his own work

D: Original: True, he said. And therefore when any one of these pantomimic gentlemen, who are so clever that they can imitate anything, comes to us, and makes a proposal to exhibit himself and his poetry, we will fall down and worship him as a sweet and holy and wonderful being; but we must also inform him that in our State such as he are not permitted to exist; the law will not allow them. And so when we have anointed him with myrrh, and set a garland of wool upon his head, we shall send him away to another city. For we mean to employ for our souls' health the rougher and severer poet or story-teller, who will imitate the style of the virtuous only, and will follow those models which we prescribed at first when we began the education of our soldiers.
D: Narrative:
"True," he said.

"And therefore when any one of these pantomimic gentlemen, who are so clever that they can imitate anything, comes to us, and makes a proposal to exhibit himself and his poetry, we will fall down and worship him as a sweet and holy and wonderful being; but we must also inform him that in our State such as he are not permitted to exist; the law will not allow them. And so when we have anointed him with myrrh, and set a garland of wool upon his head, we shall send him away to another city. For we mean to employ for our souls' health the rougher and severer poet or story-teller, who will imitate the style of the virtuous only, and will follow those models which we prescribed at first when we began the education of our soldiers."
D: Refashioned:
"True," he said.

"So when one of these brilliant pantomimic artists arrives -- someone so talented he can imitate anything -- and offers to perform his poetry for us, we'll bow down and honor him as a sweet, holy, and wonderful being. But we'll also inform him that our state has no place for him; the law won't allow it. And after anointing him with perfumed oil and crowning him with a garland of wool, we'll escort him to another city.

For ourselves, we'll employ the rougher, more austere poet or storyteller -- one who imitates only virtuous characters and follows the models we established when we first designed the education of our soldiers."
D: Elegant: When a brilliantly versatile performer arrives, we will honor him as a wonderful being -- then politely escort him out of our city. We will employ instead the rougher, more austere poet who imitates only virtue, the kind our soldiers' education requires.
D: Summary: Socrates describes the famous scene of honoring and then banishing the versatile poet: they will anoint him with myrrh, crown him with wool, praise him as wonderful -- and then send him away, because the ideal state requires the rougher, more austere poet who imitates only virtue.
D: Distilled: The versatile poet is honored as wonderful but banished; the ideal state needs the austere poet who imitates only virtue.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- This is one of the Republic's most famous passages: the honoring-and-banishing of the gifted poet -- The irony is deliberate: Plato himself is one of the most versatile literary artists in Western history -- Anointing with myrrh and crowning with wool were ritual acts of religious honor -- 'For our souls' health' uses medical language, connecting to the medicine/pharmakon metaphor -- Cross-reference: Book X (607a-b) the more severe banishment of imitative poets; Ion on the limits of the performer's knowledge -- The 'rougher poet' recalls Sparta's preference for austere art

D: Original:
We certainly will, he said, if we have the power. Then now, my friend, I said, that part of music or literary education which relates to the story or myth may be considered to be finished; for the matter and manner have both been discussed.

I think so too, he said. Next in order will follow melody and song. That is obvious. Every one can see already what we ought to say about them, if we are to be consistent with ourselves.
D: Narrative:
"We certainly will," he said, "if we have the power."

"Then now, my friend," I said, "that part of music or literary education which relates to the story or myth may be considered to be finished; for the matter and manner have both been discussed."

"I think so too," he said.

"That is obvious."

D: Refashioned:
"We certainly will," he said, "if we have the power."

"Then, my friend, we can consider the literary portion of musical education complete -- we've covered both the content and the style of stories."

"I think so too," he said.

"Next comes melody and song."

"Obviously."

"And everyone can already see what we should say about them, if we're to be consistent with our principles."
D: Elegant: The literary portion of education is complete -- both content and style have been addressed. Next comes melody and song, and our principles already dictate what we should say about them.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes the discussion of literary education, having covered both content (what stories to tell) and style (how to tell them), and transitions to the topic of melody and song, noting that their established principles already point toward the answers.
D: Distilled: Literary education (content and style) is complete; the discussion now turns to melody and song.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Music' (mousike) in Greek encompasses all arts presided over by the Muses: literature, music, dance -- The transition from logos (words/stories) to melos (melody) and rhythmos (rhythm) follows the Greek educational curriculum -- 'If we have the power' from Adeimantus is a realistic caveat about implementation -- Cross-reference: The discussion of harmonies and rhythms that follows (398c-400d) shifts the interlocutor from Adeimantus to Glaucon, who is more musically knowledgeable

D: Original:
I fear, said Glaucon, laughing, that the words 'every one' hardly includes me, for I cannot at the moment say what they should be; though I may guess.

At any rate you can tell that a song or ode has three parts --the words, the melody, and the rhythm; that degree of knowledge I may presuppose?

Yes, he said; so much as that you may. And as for the words, there surely be no difference words between words which are and which are not set to music; both will conform to the same laws, and these have been already determined by us?

Yes. And the melody and rhythm will depend upon the words? Certainly. We were saying, when we spoke of the subject-matter, that we had no need of lamentations and strains of sorrow?

True. And which are the harmonies expressive of sorrow? You are musical, and can tell me.

The harmonies which you mean are the mixed or tenor Lydian, and the full-toned or bass Lydian, and such like.

These then, I said, must be banished; even to women who have a character to maintain they are of no use, and much less to men. Certainly.

In the next place, drunkenness and softness and indolence are utterly unbecoming the character of our guardians.

Utterly unbecoming. And which are the soft or drinking harmonies? The Ionian, he replied, and the Lydian; they are termed 'relaxed.'

Well, and are these of any military use? Quite the reverse, he replied; and if so the Dorian and the Phrygian are the only ones which you have left.
D: Narrative:
"I fear," said Glaucon, laughing, "that the words 'every one' hardly includes me, for I cannot at the moment say what they should be; though I may guess."

"At any rate you can tell that a song or ode has three parts -- the words, the melody, and the rhythm; that degree of knowledge I may presuppose?"

"Yes," he said; "so much as that you may."

"And as for the words, there will surely be no difference between words which are and which are not set to music; both will conform to the same laws, and these have been already determined by us?"

"Yes."

"And the melody and rhythm will depend upon the words?"

"Certainly."

"We were saying, when we spoke of the subject-matter, that we had no need of lamentations and strains of sorrow?"

"True."

"And which are the harmonies expressive of sorrow? You are musical, and can tell me."

"The harmonies which you mean are the mixed or tenor Lydian, and the full-toned or bass Lydian, and such like."

"These then," I said, "must be banished; even to women who have a character to maintain they are of no use, and much less to men."

"Certainly."

"In the next place, drunkenness and softness and indolence are utterly unbecoming the character of our guardians."

"Utterly unbecoming."

"And which are the soft or drinking harmonies?"

"The Ionian," he replied, "and the Lydian; they are termed 'relaxed.'"

"Well, and are these of any military use?"

"Quite the reverse," he replied; "and if so the Dorian and the Phrygian are the only ones which you have left."
D: Refashioned:
"I'm afraid," said Glaucon with a laugh, "that 'everyone' doesn't quite include me. I can't say exactly what we should prescribe about melody, though I can guess."

"Well, at minimum you know that a song has three components: the words, the melody, and the rhythm?"

"Yes, that much I know."

"And the words in a song are governed by the same rules as words not set to music -- rules we've already established?"

"Yes."

"And the melody and rhythm should follow the words?"

"Certainly."

"We've already said we have no need for lamentations and mournful strains?"

"True."

"So which musical modes express sorrow? You're musical -- you can tell me."

"The ones you mean are the mixed Lydian and the bass Lydian, and modes like those."

"Then those must be banned. They're useless even for women who are supposed to have character, let alone for men."

"Certainly."

"And drunkenness, softness, and laziness are completely inappropriate for guardians."

"Completely."

"Which are the soft, drinking-song modes?"

"The Ionian and the Lydian -- they're called the 'relaxed' modes."

"Are those of any military use?"

"Quite the opposite," he replied. "Which means you're left with only the Dorian and the Phrygian."
D: Elegant: A song has three parts: words, melody, and rhythm. The words follow our established rules. Since we've banned lamentation, we ban the Lydian modes that express sorrow. Since we've banned softness and indolence, we ban the 'relaxed' Ionian and Lydian modes. Only the Dorian and Phrygian remain -- modes of courage and temperance.
D: Summary: Glaucon replaces Adeimantus as interlocutor for the musical discussion. Socrates establishes that song has three components (words, melody, rhythm) and that melody must follow the words. Since lamentations and softness are banned, the sorrowful Lydian modes and the relaxed Ionian modes must go, leaving only the Dorian and Phrygian.
D: Distilled: Since lamentation and indolence are banned, only the Dorian and Phrygian musical modes remain -- expressing courage and temperance respectively.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon (who replaces Adeimantus as the musically knowledgeable interlocutor) -- The Greek musical modes (harmoniai) were associated with specific emotional characters: Lydian = mournful, Ionian = relaxed/sensual, Dorian = martial/steadfast, Phrygian = enthusiastic/ecstatic -- Glaucon's musical expertise is noted at 398e -- 'Even to women who have a character to maintain' echoes 3.25's concern about gendered imitation -- Cross-reference: Aristotle Politics 1340a-1342b discusses the same musical modes and partially disagrees with Plato's restrictions

D: Original: I answered: Of the harmonies I know nothing, but I want to have one warlike, to sound the note or accent which a brave man utters in the hour of danger and stern resolve, or when his cause is failing, and he is going to wounds or death or is overtaken by some other evil, and at every such crisis meets the blows of fortune with firm step and a determination to endure; and another to be used by him in times of peace and freedom of action, when there is no pressure of necessity, and he is seeking to persuade God by prayer, or man by instruction and admonition, or on the other hand, when he is expressing his willingness to yield to persuasion or entreaty or admonition, and which represents him when by prudent conduct he has attained his end, not carried away by his success, but acting moderately and wisely under the circumstances, and acquiescing in the event. These two harmonies I ask you to leave; the strain of necessity and the strain of freedom, the strain of the unfortunate and the strain of the fortunate, the strain of courage, and the strain of temperance; these, I say, leave.
D: Narrative: "I answered: Of the harmonies I know nothing, but I want to have one warlike, to sound the note or accent which a brave man utters in the hour of danger and stern resolve, or when his cause is failing, and he is going to wounds or death or is overtaken by some other evil, and at every such crisis meets the blows of fortune with firm step and a determination to endure; and another to be used by him in times of peace and freedom of action, when there is no pressure of necessity, and he is seeking to persuade God by prayer, or man by instruction and admonition, or on the other hand, when he is expressing his willingness to yield to persuasion or entreaty or admonition, and which represents him when by prudent conduct he has attained his end, not carried away by his success, but acting moderately and wisely under the circumstances, and acquiescing in the event. These two harmonies I ask you to leave; the strain of necessity and the strain of freedom, the strain of the unfortunate and the strain of the fortunate, the strain of courage, and the strain of temperance; these, I say, leave."
D: Refashioned:
"I know nothing about the technical details of harmonies," I answered, "but I want two. One should be warlike -- capturing the tone of a brave man in the hour of danger and firm resolve, when his cause is failing and he faces wounds, death, or any other disaster, yet meets every blow of fortune with steady step and determination to endure.

The other should be for times of peace and free action, when there's no pressure of necessity: a man persuading the gods through prayer, or instructing and advising other people; or on the other hand, yielding willingly to the persuasion of others. It should capture the man who has achieved his goal through prudent conduct -- not carried away by success, but acting with moderation and wisdom, accepting the outcome gracefully.

These two harmonies I want preserved: the strain of necessity and the strain of freedom, the strain of adversity and the strain of good fortune, the strain of courage and the strain of temperance."
D: Elegant: Two harmonies are needed: one for courage in adversity -- the brave man meeting danger with firm resolve; another for temperance in peace -- the wise man acting with moderation whether persuading, yielding, or accepting outcomes. These are the strain of necessity and freedom, courage and temperance.
D: Summary: Socrates, despite claiming no musical expertise, describes exactly what two harmonies the state needs: one for courage in times of danger and adversity, another for temperance in times of peace and prosperity. He names these the strains of necessity and freedom, courage and temperance.
D: Distilled: The ideal state needs only two harmonies: one for courage in adversity, another for temperance in peace.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- 'Of the harmonies I know nothing' is characteristic Socratic irony, since he then describes them with great precision -- The two harmonies map onto two of the four cardinal virtues: courage (andreia) and temperance (sophrosyne) -- 'The strain of necessity and the strain of freedom' is a memorable formulation that pairs adversity/prosperity -- The peaceful harmony covers multiple social roles: prayer, teaching, learning, accepting outcomes -- Cross-reference: Laches (190d-191e) on courage as endurance; Book IV (429c-430c) on courage as preserving right opinion about what is truly fearful

D: Original:
And these, he replied, are the Dorian and Phrygian harmonies of which I was just now speaking.

Then, I said, if these and these only are to be used in our songs and melodies, we shall not want multiplicity of notes or a panharmonic scale?

I suppose not. Then we shall not maintain the artificers of lyres with three corners and complex scales, or the makers of any other many-stringed curiously-harmonised instruments?

Certainly not. But what do you say to flute-makers and flute-players? Would you admit them into our State when you reflect that in this composite use of harmony the flute is worse than all the stringed instruments put together; even the panharmonic music is only an imitation of the flute?

Clearly not. There remain then only the lyre and the harp for use in the city, and the shepherds may have a pipe in the country.

That is surely the conclusion to be drawn from the argument.

The preferring of Apollo and his instruments to Marsyas and his instruments is not at all strange, I said.

Not at all, he replied. And so, by the dog of Egypt, we have been unconsciously purging the State, which not long ago we termed luxurious.
D: Narrative:
"And these," he replied, "are the Dorian and Phrygian harmonies of which I was just now speaking."

"Then," I said, "if these and these only are to be used in our songs and melodies, we shall not want multiplicity of notes or a panharmonic scale?"

"I suppose not."

"Then we shall not maintain the artificers of lyres with three corners and complex scales, or the makers of any other many-stringed curiously-harmonised instruments?"

"Certainly not."

"But what do you say to flute-makers and flute-players? Would you admit them into our State when you reflect that in this composite use of harmony the flute is worse than all the stringed instruments put together; even the panharmonic music is only an imitation of the flute?"

"Clearly not."

"There remain then only the lyre and the harp for use in the city, and the shepherds may have a pipe in the country."

"That is surely the conclusion to be drawn from the argument."

"The preferring of Apollo and his instruments to Marsyas and his instruments is not at all strange," I said.

"Not at all," he replied.

"And so, by the dog of Egypt, we have been unconsciously purging the State, which not long ago we termed luxurious."
D: Refashioned:
"Those," he replied, "are the Dorian and Phrygian harmonies I just mentioned."

"Then if only these two are to be used in our songs, we won't need a wide range of notes or a panharmonic scale?"

"I suppose not."

"So we won't support makers of triangular lyres, complex-scaled instruments, or any other elaborate multi-stringed devices?"

"Certainly not."

"And what about flute-makers and flute-players? Should we admit them? Consider that the flute is worse than all stringed instruments combined when it comes to harmonic complexity -- even panharmonic music is really just an imitation of what the flute can do."

"Clearly not."

"Then only the lyre and the harp are permitted in the city. Shepherds can keep their pipes in the countryside."

"That's the logical conclusion."

"In preferring Apollo and his instruments over Marsyas and his, we're doing nothing strange," I said.

"Not at all," he replied.

"And by the dog of Egypt, we've been unconsciously purging the very state we recently called luxurious!"
D: Elegant: With only two modes permitted, we need no complex instruments. The flute, the most harmonically versatile instrument, must go. Only Apollo's lyre and harp remain in the city; shepherds may keep their pipes. We are choosing Apollo over Marsyas -- reason over excess -- and purging the luxurious state.
D: Summary: With only the Dorian and Phrygian modes allowed, complex instruments are unnecessary. Socrates bans the flute as too harmonically versatile and retains only the lyre and harp, framing this as choosing Apollo (reason) over Marsyas (excess). He realizes they have been unconsciously purging the state of its luxury.
D: Distilled: Only Apollo's lyre and harp are permitted in the city; the flute and all complex instruments are banned as enabling excess.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Marsyas was a satyr who challenged Apollo to a musical contest with his flute and was flayed alive for his hubris -- Apollo's lyre vs. Marsyas's flute symbolizes reason vs. passion, order vs. wildness -- 'By the dog of Egypt' is Socrates' characteristic mild oath (referencing the dog-headed god Anubis) -- 'Panharmonic scale' means a scale capable of playing in all modes -- The 'luxurious' or 'feverish' state was described in Book II (372e-373d) -- Cross-reference: Aristotle Politics 1341a-b also discusses banning the flute but for somewhat different reasons

D: Original: And we have done wisely, he replied. Then let us now finish the purgation, I said. Next in order to harmonies, rhythms will naturally follow, and they should be subject to the same rules, for we ought not to seek out complex systems of metre, or metres of every kind, but rather to discover what rhythms are the expressions of a courageous and harmonious life; and when we have found them, we shall adapt the foot and the melody to words having a like spirit, not the words to the foot and melody. To say what these rhythms are will be your duty --you must teach me them, as you have already taught me the harmonies.
D: Narrative:
"And we have done wisely," he replied.

"Then let us now finish the purgation," I said. "Next in order to harmonies, rhythms will naturally follow, and they should be subject to the same rules, for we ought not to seek out complex systems of metre, or metres of every kind, but rather to discover what rhythms are the expressions of a courageous and harmonious life; and when we have found them, we shall adapt the foot and the melody to words having a like spirit, not the words to the foot and melody. To say what these rhythms are will be your duty -- you must teach me them, as you have already taught me the harmonies."
D: Refashioned:
"And wisely done," he replied.

"Then let's complete the purification. After harmonies, rhythms naturally follow, and the same principles apply. We shouldn't seek out complex metrical systems or every possible rhythm. Instead, we should discover which rhythms express a courageous and well-ordered life. Once we've found them, we adapt the meter and melody to words of similar spirit -- not the words to the meter and melody. Identifying those rhythms will be your task -- you must teach me, just as you taught me about the harmonies."
D: Elegant: After harmonies, rhythms. The same principle applies: we seek not complexity but the rhythms that express courage and order. And crucially, the meter and melody must serve the words, not the other way around.
D: Summary: Socrates turns from harmonies to rhythms, applying the same principle of simplicity and virtue. He establishes the crucial rule that rhythm and melody must serve the words, not dominate them, and asks Glaucon to identify which rhythms express a courageous and well-ordered life.
D: Distilled: Rhythms, like harmonies, must express courage and order, and must serve the words rather than the other way around.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The primacy of words (logos) over melody (melos) and rhythm (rhythmos) is a key Platonic principle -- It means content determines form, not vice versa -- This was controversial in Greek musical practice, where composers often subordinated text to melody -- 'Courageous and harmonious life' links aesthetic form directly to moral character -- Cross-reference: Laws 669d-670a reiterates that melody and rhythm must follow the words; Aristotle Poetics 1449b-1450a also prioritizes plot (content) over spectacle (form)

D: Original: But, indeed, he replied, I cannot tell you. I only know that there are some three principles of rhythm out of which metrical systems are framed, just as in sounds there are four notes out of which all the harmonies are composed; that is an observation which I have made. But of what sort of lives they are severally the imitations I am unable to say.
D: Narrative: "But, indeed," he replied, "I cannot tell you. I only know that there are some three principles of rhythm out of which metrical systems are framed, just as in sounds there are four notes out of which all the harmonies are composed; that is an observation which I have made. But of what sort of lives they are severally the imitations I am unable to say."
D: Refashioned: "I'm afraid I can't tell you," he replied. "All I know is that there are three basic principles of rhythm from which all metrical systems are constructed, just as there are four notes from which all harmonies are composed. That's an observation I've made on my own. But which kinds of lives these rhythms respectively imitate -- that I cannot say."
D: Elegant: Glaucon knows the technical structure -- three principles of rhythm, four notes underlying all harmonies -- but cannot say which rhythms correspond to which kinds of life. Technical knowledge without philosophical understanding is incomplete.
D: Summary: Glaucon admits he knows the technical structure of rhythm (three basic principles from which all meters are built) but cannot say which rhythms correspond to which types of moral life, revealing the gap between technical musical knowledge and philosophical understanding.
D: Distilled: Technical musical knowledge without philosophical understanding of what rhythms express is incomplete.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The 'three principles of rhythm' likely refer to the three basic rhythmic ratios: equal (dactyl, 2:2), sesquialteran (paean, 3:2), and double (iamb/trochee, 2:1) -- The 'four notes' are the four strings of the basic lyre tetrachord -- Glaucon's honest admission of ignorance contrasts with the rhapsode Ion's pretension to universal knowledge -- The gap between technical expertise and philosophical understanding is a recurring Platonic theme -- Cross-reference: Ion (536d-542b) on expertise without understanding; Gorgias (462b-466a) on the difference between craft and knack

D: Original: Then, I said, we must take Damon into our counsels; and he will tell us what rhythms are expressive of meanness, or insolence, or fury, or other unworthiness, and what are to be reserved for the expression of opposite feelings. And I think that I have an indistinct recollection of his mentioning a complex Cretic rhythm; also a dactylic or heroic, and he arranged them in some manner which I do not quite understand, making the rhythms equal in the rise and fall of the foot, long and short alternating; and, unless I am mistaken, he spoke of an iambic as well as of a trochaic rhythm, and assigned to them short and long quantities. Also in some cases he appeared to praise or censure the movement of the foot quite as much as the rhythm; or perhaps a combination of the two; for I am not certain what he meant. These matters, however, as I was saying, had better be referred to Damon himself, for the analysis of the subject would be difficult, you know.
D: Narrative: "Then," I said, "we must take Damon into our counsels; and he will tell us what rhythms are expressive of meanness, or insolence, or fury, or other unworthiness, and what are to be reserved for the expression of opposite feelings. And I think that I have an indistinct recollection of his mentioning a complex Cretic rhythm; also a dactylic or heroic, and he arranged them in some manner which I do not quite understand, making the rhythms equal in the rise and fall of the foot, long and short alternating; and, unless I am mistaken, he spoke of an iambic as well as of a trochaic rhythm, and assigned to them short and long quantities. Also in some cases he appeared to praise or censure the movement of the foot quite as much as the rhythm; or perhaps a combination of the two; for I am not certain what he meant. These matters, however, as I was saying, had better be referred to Damon himself, for the analysis of the subject would be difficult, you know."
D: Refashioned:
"Then we'll need to consult Damon," I said. "He can tell us which rhythms express meanness, insolence, fury, and other vices, and which are reserved for the opposite qualities.

I have a vague recollection of him discussing a complex Cretic rhythm and a dactylic or heroic rhythm. He arranged them in some way I don't fully understand, balancing the rise and fall of the foot with alternating long and short syllables. He also discussed iambic and trochaic rhythms, if I'm not mistaken, assigning them specific long and short values. In some cases he seemed to praise or criticize the movement of the foot as much as the rhythm itself -- or perhaps some combination of both. I'm not entirely sure what he meant.

But as I said, these technical questions should be referred to Damon himself. A thorough analysis would be quite difficult."
D: Elegant: For the technical details of rhythm, we need the expert Damon, who can distinguish rhythms expressing vice from those expressing virtue. Socrates recalls his discussions of Cretic, dactylic, iambic, and trochaic rhythms but admits the details are beyond his expertise.
D: Summary: Socrates defers to Damon, the Athenian music theorist, for the technical analysis of which rhythms express which moral qualities, admitting his own recollection of Damon's teachings on Cretic, dactylic, iambic, and trochaic rhythms is vague and uncertain.
D: Distilled: Technical questions about which rhythms express virtue or vice should be referred to the expert Damon, as thorough analysis requires specialized knowledge.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- Damon of Athens was a real historical figure, a music theorist and advisor to Pericles -- He believed that musical innovation could destabilize political order -- Cretic rhythm: long-short-long; Dactylic: long-short-short (used in epic hexameter); Iambic: short-long; Trochaic: long-short -- Socrates' professed uncertainty may be genuine or ironic -- 'The rise and fall of the foot' (arsis and thesis) are technical terms for the stressed and unstressed parts of a metrical foot -- Cross-reference: Laws 700a-701b echoes Damon's view that musical innovation leads to political revolution; Aristotle also references Damon's theories

D: Original:
Rather so, I should say. But there is no difficulty in seeing that grace or the absence of grace is an effect of good or bad rhythm.

None at all. And also that good and bad rhythm naturally assimilate to a good and bad style; and that harmony and discord in like manner follow style; for our principle is that rhythm and harmony are regulated by the words, and not the words by them.

Just so, he said, they should follow the words. And will not the words and the character of the style depend on the temper of the soul?

Yes. And everything else on the style? Yes. Then beauty of style and harmony and grace and good rhythm depend on simplicity, --I mean the true simplicity of a rightly and nobly ordered mind and character, not that other simplicity which is only an euphemism for folly?

Very true, he replied. And if our youth are to do their work in life, must they not make these graces and harmonies their perpetual aim?

They must. And surely the art of the painter and every other creative and constructive art are full of them, --weaving, embroidery, architecture, and every kind of manufacture; also nature, animal and vegetable, --in all of them there is grace or the absence of grace. And ugliness and discord and inharmonious motion are nearly allied to ill words and ill nature, as grace and harmony are the twin sisters of goodness and virtue and bear their likeness.
D: Narrative:
"Rather so, I should say."

"But there is no difficulty in seeing that grace or the absence of grace is an effect of good or bad rhythm."

"None at all."

"And also that good and bad rhythm naturally assimilate to a good and bad style; and that harmony and discord in like manner follow style; for our principle is that rhythm and harmony are regulated by the words, and not the words by them."

"Just so," he said, "they should follow the words."

"And will not the words and the character of the style depend on the temper of the soul?"

"Yes."

"And everything else on the style?"

"Yes."

"Then beauty of style and harmony and grace and good rhythm depend on simplicity, -- I mean the true simplicity of a rightly and nobly ordered mind and character, not that other simplicity which is only an euphemism for folly?"

"Very true," he replied.

"And if our youth are to do their work in life, must they not make these graces and harmonies their perpetual aim?"

"They must."

"And surely the art of the painter and every other creative and constructive art are full of them, -- weaving, embroidery, architecture, and every kind of manufacture; also nature, animal and vegetable, -- in all of them there is grace or the absence of grace. And ugliness and discord and inharmonious motion are nearly allied to ill words and ill nature, as grace and harmony are the twin sisters of goodness and virtue and bear their likeness."
D: Refashioned:
"I'd say so."

"But it's easy to see that grace -- or its absence -- results from good or bad rhythm."

"Obvious."

"And that good and bad rhythm naturally correspond to good and bad style, and that harmony and discord likewise follow style. Our principle is that rhythm and harmony are governed by the words, not the words by them."

"Exactly," he said. "They should follow the words."

"And the words and character of the style depend on the quality of the soul?"

"Yes."

"And everything else follows from style?"

"Yes."

"Then beauty of style, harmony, grace, and good rhythm all depend on simplicity -- and I mean true simplicity, the kind that comes from a rightly and nobly ordered mind and character, not the other kind that's just a polite word for foolishness."

"Very true," he replied.

"If our young people are to fulfill their purpose in life, they must make these graces and harmonies their constant aim."

"They must."

"And these qualities pervade every art -- painting, weaving, embroidery, architecture, every kind of craft -- as well as all of nature, animal and plant alike. Everything contains either grace or its absence. Ugliness, discord, and awkward motion are close kin to ugly words and ugly character, just as grace and harmony are the twin sisters of goodness and virtue, bearing their likeness."
D: Elegant: Grace comes from good rhythm, good rhythm from good style, good style from a well-ordered soul. Beauty of style depends on true simplicity -- the simplicity of noble character, not the simplicity of foolishness. This principle extends beyond poetry to all arts and all of nature: grace and harmony are the twin sisters of goodness and virtue.
D: Summary: Socrates traces an unbroken chain from the soul's quality to artistic beauty: soul determines style, style determines words, words determine rhythm and harmony. True simplicity of character -- not foolishness -- produces grace in every domain: painting, weaving, architecture, and even nature itself.
D: Distilled: Beauty of style depends on simplicity of soul; grace and harmony are the twin sisters of goodness and virtue, pervading all art and nature.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The chain of dependence (soul > style > words > rhythm/harmony) is a key aesthetic principle -- 'True simplicity vs. euphemism for folly' is a sharp distinction: genuine simplicity is sophisticated, not naive -- The extension to all arts (painting, weaving, architecture) and to nature makes this a universal aesthetic theory -- 'Grace and harmony are the twin sisters of goodness and virtue' is one of the Republic's most beautiful formulations -- Cross-reference: Symposium (210a-212a) on the ascent from beautiful bodies to Beauty itself; Philebus (64e-65a) on beauty, proportion, and truth

D: Original: That is quite true, he said. But shall our superintendence go no further, and are the poets only to be required by us to express the image of the good in their works, on pain, if they do anything else, of expulsion from our State? Or is the same control to be extended to other artists, and are they also to be prohibited from exhibiting the opposite forms of vice and intemperance and meanness and indecency in sculpture and building and the other creative arts; and is he who cannot conform to this rule of ours to be prevented from practising his art in our State, lest the taste of our citizens be corrupted by him? We would not have our guardians grow up amid images of moral deformity, as in some noxious pasture, and there browse and feed upon many a baneful herb and flower day by day, little by little, until they silently gather a festering mass of corruption in their own soul. Let our artists rather be those who are gifted to discern the true nature of the beautiful and graceful; then will our youth dwell in a land of health, amid fair sights and sounds, and receive the good in everything; and beauty, the effluence of fair works, shall flow into the eye and ear, like a health-giving breeze from a purer region, and insensibly draw the soul from earliest years into likeness and sympathy with the beauty of reason.
D: Narrative:
"That is quite true," he said.

"But shall our superintendence go no further, and are the poets only to be required by us to express the image of the good in their works, on pain, if they do anything else, of expulsion from our State? Or is the same control to be extended to other artists, and are they also to be prohibited from exhibiting the opposite forms of vice and intemperance and meanness and indecency in sculpture and building and the other creative arts; and is he who cannot conform to this rule of ours to be prevented from practising his art in our State, lest the taste of our citizens be corrupted by him? We would not have our guardians grow up amid images of moral deformity, as in some noxious pasture, and there browse and feed upon many a baneful herb and flower day by day, little by little, until they silently gather a festering mass of corruption in their own soul. Let our artists rather be those who are gifted to discern the true nature of the beautiful and graceful; then will our youth dwell in a land of health, amid fair sights and sounds, and receive the good in everything; and beauty, the effluence of fair works, shall flow into the eye and ear, like a health-giving breeze from a purer region, and insensibly draw the soul from earliest years into likeness and sympathy with the beauty of reason."
D: Refashioned:
"That's quite true," he said.

"But should our oversight stop with poets? Should only they be required to express the image of the good -- on pain of expulsion? Or should the same control extend to all artists? Should sculptors, architects, and every kind of craftsman be prohibited from depicting vice, intemperance, meanness, and indecency? Should anyone who can't follow this standard be barred from practicing in our state, so that our citizens' taste isn't corrupted?

We can't let our guardians grow up surrounded by images of moral ugliness, as if in some poisonous pasture, grazing day after day on harmful plants until they unknowingly accumulate a festering mass of corruption in their souls.

Instead, let our artists be those who can discern the true nature of beauty and grace. Then our young people will live in a healthy environment, surrounded by beautiful sights and sounds, absorbing goodness from everything around them. Beauty -- the emanation of fine works -- will flow into their eyes and ears like a healing breeze from a purer region, drawing their souls from earliest childhood into harmony and kinship with the beauty of reason."
D: Elegant: The regulation of art must extend beyond poetry to sculpture, architecture, and every craft. We cannot let guardians grow up in a poisonous pasture of moral ugliness, unknowingly accumulating corruption. Instead, surrounded by beautiful works, beauty will flow into their eyes and ears like a healing breeze, drawing their souls from childhood into kinship with the beauty of reason.
D: Summary: Socrates extends artistic regulation beyond poetry to all creative arts, using the powerful metaphor of a 'noxious pasture' where moral ugliness is absorbed unconsciously. Instead, guardians should be surrounded by beautiful works so that beauty flows into them like a healing breeze, drawing their souls into harmony with reason from childhood.
D: Distilled: All art must express the good, so that beauty flows into young souls like a healing breeze, drawing them into kinship with reason.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The 'noxious pasture' metaphor is one of Plato's most vivid images for the environmental theory of moral development -- The idea that aesthetic environment shapes character unconsciously ('insensibly') is remarkably modern -- 'The effluence of fair works' uses the language of physical emanation (aporrhoe), connecting to Presocratic physics -- 'The beauty of reason' (to kalon tou logou) links aesthetic and intellectual beauty -- Cross-reference: Symposium (210a-212a) on ascending from beautiful particulars to Beauty itself; Laws 656c-657b on regulating all art in the state -- This passage has influenced environmental aesthetics and arguments about public art

D: Original: There can be no nobler training than that, he replied. And therefore, I said, Glaucon, musical training is a more potent instrument than any other, because rhythm and harmony find their way into the inward places of the soul, on which they mightily fasten, imparting grace, and making the soul of him who is rightly educated graceful, or of him who is ill-educated ungraceful; and also because he who has received this true education of the inner being will most shrewdly perceive omissions or faults in art and nature, and with a true taste, while he praises and rejoices over and receives into his soul the good, and becomes noble and good, he will justly blame and hate the bad, now in the days of his youth, even before he is able to know the reason why; and when reason comes he will recognise and salute the friend with whom his education has made him long familiar.
D: Narrative:
"There can be no nobler training than that," he replied.

"And therefore," I said, "Glaucon, musical training is a more potent instrument than any other, because rhythm and harmony find their way into the inward places of the soul, on which they mightily fasten, imparting grace, and making the soul of him who is rightly educated graceful, or of him who is ill-educated ungraceful; and also because he who has received this true education of the inner being will most shrewdly perceive omissions or faults in art and nature, and with a true taste, while he praises and rejoices over and receives into his soul the good, and becomes noble and good, he will justly blame and hate the bad, now in the days of his youth, even before he is able to know the reason why; and when reason comes he will recognise and salute the friend with whom his education has made him long familiar."
D: Refashioned:
"There can be no nobler education than that," he replied.

"And this is precisely why, Glaucon, musical training is the most powerful educational instrument of all. Rhythm and harmony penetrate the deepest recesses of the soul. They take hold there powerfully, imparting grace to the properly educated and gracelessness to the poorly educated.

Moreover, the person who receives this true education of the inner self will develop the keenest perception of flaws and omissions in art and nature. With genuine taste, he will praise and joyfully absorb what is good, letting it nourish his soul and make him noble. He will rightly blame and hate what is bad -- and he'll do all this in his youth, even before he can articulate the reasons why.

Then when rational understanding finally arrives, he will recognize and welcome it like a long-familiar friend, because his education has already prepared him for the meeting."
D: Elegant: Musical training is the most potent educational instrument because rhythm and harmony penetrate the deepest parts of the soul, imparting grace. The musically educated person perceives beauty and ugliness instinctively, loving the good and hating the bad even before understanding why. When reason finally arrives, it is recognized as a familiar friend.
D: Summary: Socrates declares musical education the most powerful instrument of all because rhythm and harmony penetrate the soul's deepest places. The musically educated person develops instinctive good taste, loving beauty and hating ugliness even before rational understanding develops -- and when reason does arrive, it is welcomed as a long-familiar friend.
D: Distilled: Musical education is supreme because rhythm and harmony penetrate the soul, cultivating instinctive love of the good that prepares the way for rational understanding.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'The inward places of the soul' makes rhythm and harmony operate at a pre-rational level -- The developmental sequence is crucial: aesthetic sensitivity precedes and prepares for philosophical reasoning -- 'He will recognise and salute the friend' (reason) = aesthetic education is propaedeutic to philosophy -- This is one of the most influential passages in Western educational philosophy -- Cross-reference: Book VII (521c-522b) on the role of mathematical education as preparation for dialectic; Symposium (210a-e) on the ascent from particular beauties to the Form of Beauty -- Aristotle's concept of habituation (ethismos) in Nicomachean Ethics II.1 echoes this developmental model

D: Original:
Yes, he said, I quite agree with you in thinking that our youth should be trained in music and on the grounds which you mention.

Just as in learning to read, I said, we were satisfied when we knew the letters of the alphabet, which are very few, in all their recurring sizes and combinations; not slighting them as unimportant whether they occupy a space large or small, but everywhere eager to make them out; and not thinking ourselves perfect in the art of reading until we recognise them wherever they are found:

True -- Or, as we recognise the reflection of letters in the water, or in a mirror, only when we know the letters themselves; the same art and study giving us the knowledge of both:

Exactly -- Even so, as I maintain, neither we nor our guardians, whom we have to educate, can ever become musical until we and they know the essential forms, in all their combinations, and can recognise them and their images wherever they are found, not slighting them either in small things or great, but believing them all to be within the sphere of one art and study.

Most assuredly. And when a beautiful soul harmonises with a beautiful form, and the two are cast in one mould, that will be the fairest of sights to him who has an eye to see it?

The fairest indeed. And the fairest is also the loveliest? That may be assumed. And the man who has the spirit of harmony will be most in love with the loveliest; but he will not love him who is of an inharmonious soul?

That is true, he replied, if the deficiency be in his soul; but if there be any merely bodily defect in another he will be patient of it, and will love all the same.

I perceive, I said, that you have or have had experiences of this sort, and I agree. But let me ask you another question: Has excess of pleasure any affinity to temperance?

How can that be? he replied; pleasure deprives a man of the use of his faculties quite as much as pain.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "I quite agree with you in thinking that our youth should be trained in music and on the grounds which you mention."

"Just as in learning to read," I said, "we were satisfied when we knew the letters of the alphabet, which are very few, in all their recurring sizes and combinations; not slighting them as unimportant whether they occupy a space large or small, but everywhere eager to make them out; and not thinking ourselves perfect in the art of reading until we recognise them wherever they are found:"

"True --"

"Or, as we recognise the reflection of letters in the water, or in a mirror, only when we know the letters themselves; the same art and study giving us the knowledge of both:"

"Exactly --"

"Even so, as I maintain, neither we nor our guardians, whom we have to educate, can ever become musical until we and they know the essential forms, in all their combinations, and can recognise them and their images wherever they are found, not slighting them either in small things or great, but believing them all to be within the sphere of one art and study."

"Most assuredly."

"And when a beautiful soul harmonises with a beautiful form, and the two are cast in one mould, that will be the fairest of sights to him who has an eye to see it?"

"The fairest indeed."

"And the fairest is also the loveliest?"

"That may be assumed."

"And the man who has the spirit of harmony will be most in love with the loveliest; but he will not love him who is of an inharmonious soul?"

"That is true," he replied, "if the deficiency be in his soul; but if there be any merely bodily defect in another he will be patient of it, and will love all the same."

"I perceive," I said, "that you have or have had experiences of this sort, and I agree. But let me ask you another question: Has excess of pleasure any affinity to temperance?"

"How can that be?" he replied; "pleasure deprives a man of the use of his faculties quite as much as pain."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," he said, "I completely agree that our youth should be trained in music, for the reasons you describe."

"Think of it like learning to read," I said. "We aren't satisfied until we know every letter of the alphabet -- few as they are -- in all their sizes and combinations. We don't dismiss any letter as unimportant just because it's large or small. We want to recognize them everywhere, and we don't consider ourselves literate until we can."

"True."

"And just as we recognize the reflection of letters in water or a mirror only because we first know the letters themselves -- the same knowledge serving for both --"

"Exactly."

"-- so too, neither we nor our guardians can truly become 'musical' until we know the essential forms of virtue -- temperance, courage, generosity, and their kin -- in all their combinations, and can recognize both the forms and their reflections wherever they appear, in matters small and great, understanding them all as belonging to one unified art and study."

"Most assuredly."

"And when a beautiful soul harmonizes with a beautiful body, both cast in the same mold -- isn't that the most beautiful sight for anyone with eyes to see?"

"The most beautiful indeed."

"And the most beautiful is also the most lovable?"

"We may assume so."

"And the person with a harmonious spirit will be drawn to what is most lovable. But he won't love someone whose soul is inharmonious?"

"True," he replied, "if the deficiency is in the soul. But if there's only a bodily imperfection, he'll be patient with it and love all the same."

"I see you speak from experience," I said, "and I agree. But let me ask you this: does excessive pleasure have any connection to temperance?"

"How could it?" he replied. "Pleasure robs a person of his faculties just as much as pain does."
D: Elegant: Just as literacy requires recognizing letters everywhere -- even their reflections -- true musical education means recognizing the essential forms of virtue in all their manifestations, large and small. When a beautiful soul harmonizes with a beautiful form, it is the fairest of sights. The harmonious person loves what is truly lovable, tolerates bodily imperfections, but cannot love a disharmonious soul. And excessive pleasure undermines temperance as surely as pain does.
D: Summary: Socrates uses the analogy of literacy (recognizing letters everywhere, even in reflections) to explain musical education as recognizing the forms of virtue in all their manifestations. He then discusses the beauty of soul-body harmony, the nature of love for the harmonious person, and establishes that excessive pleasure undermines temperance just as much as pain.
D: Distilled: Musical education means recognizing virtue's essential forms everywhere, just as literacy means recognizing letters; excessive pleasure undermines temperance as much as pain does.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The literacy analogy is important: just as letters combine into words, moral qualities combine into character -- 'The essential forms' (eide) uses language that anticipates the Theory of Forms, though here applied to moral qualities -- Glaucon's personal experience with love ('you have or have had experiences') adds biographical depth -- The claim that excessive pleasure impairs as much as pain is a key anti-hedonist argument -- Cross-reference: Phaedrus (250d-e) on the soul's recognition of Beauty; Philebus (63d-64a) on the mixture of pleasure and intelligence; Book IX (583b-584c) on true vs. illusory pleasure

D: Original:
Or any affinity to virtue in general? None whatever. Any affinity to wantonness and intemperance? Yes, the greatest. And is there any greater or keener pleasure than that of sensual love?

No, nor a madder. Whereas true love is a love of beauty and order --temperate and harmonious?

Quite true, he said. Then no intemperance or madness should be allowed to approach true love?

Certainly not. Then mad or intemperate pleasure must never be allowed to come near the lover and his beloved; neither of them can have any part in it if their love is of the right sort?
D: Narrative:
"Or any affinity to virtue in general?"

"None whatever."

"Any affinity to wantonness and intemperance?"

"Yes, the greatest."

"And is there any greater or keener pleasure than that of sensual love?"

"No, nor a madder."

"Whereas true love is a love of beauty and order -- temperate and harmonious?"

"Quite true," he said.

"Then no intemperance or madness should be allowed to approach true love?"

"Certainly not."

"Then mad or intemperate pleasure must never be allowed to come near the lover and his beloved; neither of them can have any part in it if their love is of the right sort?"
D: Refashioned:
"Does excessive pleasure have any connection to virtue in general?"

"None at all."

"But it does have a connection to wantonness and intemperance?"

"The strongest possible."

"Is there any pleasure greater or more intense than sexual desire?"

"No -- and none madder."

"But true love is a love of beauty and order -- temperate and harmonious?"

"Exactly," he said.

"So nothing intemperate or maddening should be allowed near true love?"

"Certainly not."

"Then wild, intemperate pleasure must never come near the lover and his beloved. Neither should have any part in such pleasure if their love is genuine."
D: Elegant: Excessive pleasure has no affinity to virtue but the strongest affinity to vice. Sexual desire is the keenest and maddest pleasure. True love, by contrast, is a love of beauty and order -- temperate and harmonious. Therefore intemperate pleasure has no place in genuine love.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes that excessive pleasure is antithetical to virtue and allied to vice, that sexual desire is the most intense and maddest pleasure, and that true love -- being a love of beauty, order, and temperance -- must exclude all intemperate and excessive pleasure.
D: Distilled: True love is temperate love of beauty and order; intemperate sexual pleasure is its opposite and must be excluded from genuine relationships.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The distinction between true love (eros oriented toward beauty and order) and mere sexual desire is central to Platonic philosophy -- 'Nor a madder' (manikoteron) links excessive sexual pleasure to literal madness -- Cross-reference: Phaedrus (244a-245c) on divine madness, which complicates this negative view of erotic madness; Symposium (210a-212a) on eros as the desire for beauty leading upward to the Form of Beauty -- This passage reflects the Socratic/Platonic ideal of sublimated eros

D: Original:
No, indeed, Socrates, it must never come near them. Then I suppose that in the city which we are founding you would make a law to the effect that a friend should use no other familiarity to his love than a father would use to his son, and then only for a noble purpose, and he must first have the other's consent; and this rule is to limit him in all his intercourse, and he is never to be seen going further, or, if he exceeds, he is to be deemed guilty of coarseness and bad taste.

I quite agree, he said. Thus much of music, which makes a fair ending; for what should be the end of music if not the love of beauty?

I agree, he said. After music comes gymnastic, in which our youth are next to be trained.

"No, indeed, Socrates, it must never come near them."

"Then I suppose that in the city which we are founding you would make a law to the effect that a friend should use no other familiarity to his love than a father would use to his son, and then only for a noble purpose, and he must first have the other's consent; and this rule is to limit him in all his intercourse, and he is never to be seen going further, or, if he exceeds, he is to be deemed guilty of coarseness and bad taste."

"I quite agree," he said.

"I agree," he said.

"After music comes gymnastic, in which our youth are next to be trained."
D: Refashioned:
"No, indeed, Socrates, it must never come near them."

"Then in the city we're founding, I suppose you'd make a law that a lover's physical familiarity with his beloved should go no further than what a father would show a son -- and only for noble purposes, with the other's consent. This rule limits all physical contact. Anyone who exceeds it should be considered guilty of vulgarity and bad taste."

"I quite agree," he said.

"And so our discussion of music reaches its proper conclusion. For what should be the ultimate purpose of musical education if not the love of beauty?"

"I agree," he said.

"After music comes gymnastic -- the next stage of our youth's training."
D: Elegant: In our city, physical intimacy between lovers should be no more than what a father shows a son -- noble in purpose and with consent. Anything beyond this is vulgarity. Musical education finds its proper end in the love of beauty. Now we turn to gymnastic training.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes the discussion of musical education by legislating that physical intimacy between lovers should be limited and noble, declaring that the proper end of all musical education is the love of beauty, and transitions to the next topic: gymnastic training.
D: Distilled: Musical education culminates in the love of beauty; physical intimacy must be restrained and noble; gymnastic training comes next.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The restriction on physical love to paternal familiarity is remarkably strict even by ancient Greek standards -- The context is male same-sex relationships (erotic pederasty was a significant part of Greek aristocratic culture) -- 'What should be the end of music if not the love of beauty?' is a perfect encapsulation of Plato's aesthetics -- The transition from mousike (music/culture) to gymnastike (physical training) follows the traditional Greek educational curriculum -- Cross-reference: Symposium (211b-c) on the love of beauty as philosophical aspiration; Laws 636b-637a on sexual regulation

D: Original: Certainly. Gymnastic as well as music should begin in early years; the training in it should be careful and should continue through life. Now my belief is, --and this is a matter upon which I should like to have your opinion in confirmation of my own, but my own belief is, --not that the good body by any bodily excellence improves the soul, but, on the contrary, that the good soul, by her own excellence, improves the body as far as this may be possible. What do you say?
D: Narrative:
"Certainly."

"Gymnastic as well as music should begin in early years; the training in it should be careful and should continue through life. Now my belief is, -- and this is a matter upon which I should like to have your opinion in confirmation of my own, but my own belief is, -- not that the good body by any bodily excellence improves the soul, but, on the contrary, that the good soul, by her own excellence, improves the body as far as this may be possible. What do you say?"
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly."

"Gymnastic training, like music, should begin in childhood and continue throughout life. Now here's my belief -- and I'd like your opinion to confirm it -- I don't believe that a good body improves the soul through its physical excellence. On the contrary, I believe a good soul, through its own excellence, improves the body as far as that's possible. What do you think?"
D: Elegant: Physical training should begin early and last a lifetime. But the body does not improve the soul; rather, the good soul improves the body. The direction of causation is from soul to body, not body to soul.
D: Summary: Socrates introduces gymnastic education with a counterintuitive principle: it is not physical fitness that improves the soul, but rather the excellence of the soul that improves the body. The causal direction runs from soul to body, not the reverse.
D: Distilled: The good soul improves the body through its own excellence, not the other way around; physical training serves the soul.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This principle reverses the common assumption that physical health leads to mental well-being -- The primacy of soul over body is a fundamental Platonic doctrine -- 'As far as this may be possible' is an important qualification: the soul's power over the body has limits -- Cross-reference: Phaedo (64c-67b) on the soul's superiority to the body; Timaeus (87c-88b) on the harmony of soul and body; Charmides (156e-157a) where Socrates argues one must cure the soul to cure the body

D: Original:
Yes, I agree. Then, to the mind when adequately trained, we shall be right in handing over the more particular care of the body; and in order to avoid prolixity we will now only give the general outlines of the subject.

Very good. That they must abstain from intoxication has been already remarked by us; for of all persons a guardian should be the last to get drunk and not know where in the world he is.

Yes, he said; that a guardian should require another guardian to take care of him is ridiculous indeed.

But next, what shall we say of their food; for the men are in training for the great contest of all --are they not?

Yes, he said. And will the habit of body of our ordinary athletes be suited to them?

Why not? I am afraid, I said, that a habit of body such as they have is but a sleepy sort of thing, and rather perilous to health. Do you not observe that these athletes sleep away their lives, and are liable to most dangerous illnesses if they depart, in ever so slight a degree, from their customary regimen?

Yes, I do. Then, I said, a finer sort of training will be required for our warrior athletes, who are to be like wakeful dogs, and to see and hear with the utmost keenness; amid the many changes of water and also of food, of summer heat and winter cold, which they will have to endure when on a campaign, they must not be liable to break down in health.

That is my view. The really excellent gymnastic is twin sister of that simple music which we were just now describing.

How so? Why, I conceive that there is a gymnastic which, like our music, is simple and good; and especially the military gymnastic.

What do you mean? My meaning may be learned from Homer; he, you know, feeds his heroes at their feasts, when they are campaigning, on soldiers' fare; they have no fish, although they are on the shores of the Hellespont, and they are not allowed boiled meats but only roast, which is the food most convenient for soldiers, requiring only that they should light a fire, and not involving the trouble of carrying about pots and pans.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, I agree."

"Then, to the mind when adequately trained, we shall be right in handing over the more particular care of the body; and in order to avoid prolixity we will now only give the general outlines of the subject."

"Very good."

"That they must abstain from intoxication has been already remarked by us; for of all persons a guardian should be the last to get drunk and not know where in the world he is."

"Yes," he said; "that a guardian should require another guardian to take care of him is ridiculous indeed."

"But next, what shall we say of their food; for the men are in training for the great contest of all -- are they not?"

"Yes," he said.

"And will the habit of body of our ordinary athletes be suited to them?"

"Why not?"

"I am afraid," I said, "that a habit of body such as they have is but a sleepy sort of thing, and rather perilous to health. Do you not observe that these athletes sleep away their lives, and are liable to most dangerous illnesses if they depart, in ever so slight a degree, from their customary regimen?"

"Yes, I do."

"Then," I said, "a finer sort of training will be required for our warrior athletes, who are to be like wakeful dogs, and to see and hear with the utmost keenness; amid the many changes of water and also of food, of summer heat and winter cold, which they will have to endure when on a campaign, they must not be liable to break down in health."

"That is my view."

"The really excellent gymnastic is twin sister of that simple music which we were just now describing."

"How so?"

"Why, I conceive that there is a gymnastic which, like our music, is simple and good; and especially the military gymnastic."

"What do you mean?"

"My meaning may be learned from Homer; he, you know, feeds his heroes at their feasts, when they are campaigning, on soldiers' fare; they have no fish, although they are on the shores of the Hellespont, and they are not allowed boiled meats but only roast, which is the food most convenient for soldiers, requiring only that they should light a fire, and not involving the trouble of carrying about pots and pans."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, I agree."

"Then once the mind is properly trained, we can entrust it with the particular care of the body. To avoid going on too long, I'll just sketch the general outlines."

"Very good."

"We've already established that guardians must avoid intoxication. A guardian should be the last person to get drunk and lose track of where he is."

"Yes," he said, "a guardian who needs his own guardian is ridiculous."

"Now, what about their diet? These men are training for the greatest contest of all, aren't they?"

"They are."

"Will the typical athlete's physical regimen work for them?"

"Why not?"

"Because that kind of regimen produces a sluggish constitution and is actually dangerous to health. Haven't you noticed that athletes sleep their lives away and become seriously ill if they deviate even slightly from their routine?"

"Yes, I have."

"Our warrior-athletes need a superior kind of training. They must be like alert watchdogs -- with the sharpest sight and hearing. They'll face constant changes in water, food, heat, and cold while on campaign, and they can't afford to break down."

"I agree."

"The truly excellent gymnastic is the twin sister of the simple music we just described."

"How so?"

"There's a kind of physical training that, like our music, is simple and good -- especially the military kind."

"What do you mean?"

"Look at Homer. He feeds his heroes plain soldiers' food when they're on campaign. No fish, even though they're camped on the shores of the Hellespont. No boiled meat -- only roasted, which is the most practical for soldiers. All you need is a fire; no pots and pans to carry around."
D: Elegant: Once the mind is trained, it can oversee the body. Athletes' regimens produce sluggish, fragile constitutions -- our warrior-guardians need something tougher. Like the simple music we approved, the best gymnastic is simple and practical. Homer's heroes ate plain roasted meat on campaign, with no elaborate cookery -- a model of military simplicity.
D: Summary: Socrates outlines gymnastic education for guardians, rejecting the typical athlete's regimen as producing sluggish, fragile bodies. Warrior-guardians need robust health that withstands campaign conditions. The ideal gymnastic, like ideal music, is simple -- illustrated by Homer's heroes eating plain roasted food on campaign without elaborate cookery.
D: Distilled: The best physical training, like the best music, is simple and robust; professional athletes' fragile regimens are unsuitable for warrior-guardians.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'The great contest of all' = the contest of life and civic duty, vs. mere athletic competition -- The critique of professional athletes anticipates modern concerns about overspecialized training -- 'Wakeful dogs' continues the guardian-dog analogy from Book II (375a) -- Homer's heroes eating roast meat: Iliad 9.206-214 (the feast at Achilles' tent) -- No fish on the Hellespont: despite being on the coast, Homeric heroes eat only meat -- 'Twin sister' echoes the earlier 'grace and harmony are twin sisters of goodness' (3.41) -- Cross-reference: Book II (372a-373c) on simple vs. luxurious diet in the city

D: Original:
True. And I can hardly be mistaken in saying that sweet sauces are nowhere mentioned in Homer. In proscribing them, however, he is not singular; all professional athletes are well aware that a man who is to be in good condition should take nothing of the kind.

Yes, he said; and knowing this, they are quite right in not taking them.

Then you would not approve of Syracusan dinners, and the refinements of Sicilian cookery?

I think not. Nor, if a man is to be in condition, would you allow him to have a Corinthian girl as his fair friend?

Certainly not. Neither would you approve of the delicacies, as they are thought, of Athenian confectionery?

Certainly not. All such feeding and living may be rightly compared by us to melody and song composed in the panharmonic style, and in all the rhythms. Exactly.

There complexity engendered license, and here disease; whereas simplicity in music was the parent of temperance in the soul; and simplicity in gymnastic of health in the body.

Most true, he said. But when intemperance and disease multiply in a State, halls of justice and medicine are always being opened; and the arts of the doctor and the lawyer give themselves airs, finding how keen is the interest which not only the slaves but the freemen of a city take about them.
D: Narrative:
"True."

"And I can hardly be mistaken in saying that sweet sauces are nowhere mentioned in Homer. In proscribing them, however, he is not singular; all professional athletes are well aware that a man who is to be in good condition should take nothing of the kind."

"Yes," he said; "and knowing this, they are quite right in not taking them."

"Then you would not approve of Syracusan dinners, and the refinements of Sicilian cookery?"

"I think not."

"Nor, if a man is to be in condition, would you allow him to have a Corinthian girl as his fair friend?"

"Certainly not."

"Neither would you approve of the delicacies, as they are thought, of Athenian confectionery?"

"Certainly not."

"All such feeding and living may be rightly compared by us to melody and song composed in the panharmonic style, and in all the rhythms."

"Exactly."

"There complexity engendered license, and here disease; whereas simplicity in music was the parent of temperance in the soul; and simplicity in gymnastic of health in the body."

"Most true," he said.

"But when intemperance and disease multiply in a State, halls of justice and medicine are always being opened; and the arts of the doctor and the lawyer give themselves airs, finding how keen is the interest which not only the slaves but the freemen of a city take about them."
D: Refashioned:
"True."

"And you'll notice Homer never mentions sweet sauces. He's not alone in this -- all professional athletes know that someone in training should avoid such things."

"Yes, and they're right to avoid them."
