"So you wouldn't approve of Syracusan feasts or the elaborate refinements of Sicilian cuisine?"

"I would not."

"Nor would you allow a man in training to have a Corinthian courtesan as a companion?"

"Certainly not."

"Or the famous delicacies of Athenian pastry-making?"

"Certainly not."

"All this elaborate eating and living can be compared to music composed in the panharmonic style with every possible rhythm."

"Exactly."

"There, complexity bred license; here, it breeds disease. Simplicity in music produces temperance of soul; simplicity in physical training produces health of body."

"Very true," he said.

"And when intemperance and disease multiply in a state, courtrooms and hospitals spring up everywhere. The legal and medical professions start putting on airs, because even free citizens -- not just slaves -- are desperately seeking their services."
D: Elegant: Homer omits sweet sauces; Syracusan feasts, Corinthian courtesans, and Athenian pastries are all analogous to panharmonic music -- elaborate, indulgent, corrupting. Simplicity in music breeds temperance; simplicity in training breeds health. When a society abandons simplicity, courtrooms and hospitals multiply as intemperance and disease take hold.
D: Summary: Socrates draws a parallel between dietary excess (Sicilian cookery, Corinthian courtesans, Athenian pastries) and musical excess (panharmonic complexity). Simplicity in music produces temperance; simplicity in diet produces health. When either kind of simplicity is abandoned, the proliferation of lawyers and doctors signals a sick society.
D: Distilled: Simplicity in music produces temperance of soul; simplicity in training produces health of body; their absence breeds a society overrun by lawyers and doctors.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Syracuse and Sicily were famous for elaborate cuisine in the ancient Greek world -- 'Corinthian girl' = a hetaira (courtesan); Corinth was associated with sexual commerce -- Athenian confectionery (pemma) was apparently renowned -- The parallel between dietary and musical excess is structurally important: both are instances of complexity breeding corruption -- The proliferation of courtrooms and hospitals as signs of social decay is a striking diagnostic -- Cross-reference: Book II (372e-373d) on the 'feverish' or luxurious city; Gorgias (464b-465e) on the analogy between medicine/gymnastics and justice/legislation

D: Original: Of course. And yet what greater proof can there be of a bad and disgraceful state of education than this, that not only artisans and the meaner sort of people need the skill of first-rate physicians and judges, but also those who would profess to have had a liberal education? Is it not disgraceful, and a great sign of want of good-breeding, that a man should have to go abroad for his law and physic because he has none of his own at home, and must therefore surrender himself into the hands of other men whom he makes lords and judges over him?
D: Narrative:
"Of course."

"And yet what greater proof can there be of a bad and disgraceful state of education than this, that not only artisans and the meaner sort of people need the skill of first-rate physicians and judges, but also those who would profess to have had a liberal education? Is it not disgraceful, and a great sign of want of good-breeding, that a man should have to go abroad for his law and physic because he has none of his own at home, and must therefore surrender himself into the hands of other men whom he makes lords and judges over him?"
D: Refashioned: "And yet, what greater proof of a failed education system could there be than this: not just laborers and common people, but even those who claim to have received a liberal education need the services of top physicians and lawyers? Isn't it disgraceful -- a clear sign of poor upbringing -- that a person has to seek law and medicine from others because he has none of his own within himself? He surrenders himself to the authority of other men, making them lords and judges over him."
D: Elegant: The surest sign of failed education is when even the supposedly well-educated need doctors and lawyers. A properly raised person should carry justice and health within himself, not surrender to the authority of others for what he should possess internally.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that the need for external doctors and lawyers, even among the supposedly well-educated, is the surest proof of educational failure. A properly educated person should possess internal resources of health and justice rather than depending on external authorities.
D: Distilled: Needing doctors and lawyers is a sign of educational failure; the well-educated should carry health and justice within themselves.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The ideal of self-sufficiency extends from health to justice -- the educated person needs neither external medicine nor external law -- 'Going abroad for law and physic' means seeking externally what should be cultivated internally -- This anticipates the Stoic ideal of the self-sufficient sage -- The passage critiques not just individuals but the educational system that produced them -- Cross-reference: Gorgias (464b-466a) on the four arts of care for body and soul (gymnastics, medicine, legislation, justice); Crito (47c-48a) on the expert in justice

D: Original: Of all things, he said, the most disgraceful. Would you say 'most,' I replied, when you consider that there is a further stage of the evil in which a man is not only a life-long litigant, passing all his days in the courts, either as plaintiff or defendant, but is actually led by his bad taste to pride himself on his litigiousness; he imagines that he is a master in dishonesty; able to take every crooked turn, and wriggle into and out of every hole, bending like a withy and getting out of the way of justice: and all for what? --in order to gain small points not worth mentioning, he not knowing that so to order his life as to be able to do without a napping judge is a far higher and nobler sort of thing. Is not that still more disgraceful?
D: Narrative:
"Of all things," he said, "the most disgraceful."

"Would you say 'most,'" I replied, "when you consider that there is a further stage of the evil in which a man is not only a life-long litigant, passing all his days in the courts, either as plaintiff or defendant, but is actually led by his bad taste to pride himself on his litigiousness; he imagines that he is a master in dishonesty; able to take every crooked turn, and wriggle into and out of every hole, bending like a withy and getting out of the way of justice: and all for what? -- in order to gain small points not worth mentioning, he not knowing that so to order his life as to be able to do without a napping judge is a far higher and nobler sort of thing. Is not that still more disgraceful?"
D: Refashioned:
"The most disgraceful of all," he said.

"'Most'? Consider an even worse stage: a man who is not just a lifelong litigant, spending all his days in court as plaintiff or defendant, but who actually takes pride in his litigiousness. He thinks he's a master of cunning -- able to take every crooked turn, wriggle through every loophole, bending like a willow branch to evade justice. And for what? To score petty points not worth mentioning.

He doesn't realize that ordering one's life so as to never need a drowsy judge is a far nobler achievement. Isn't that even more disgraceful?"
D: Elegant: Worse than needing lawyers is being proud of your litigiousness -- imagining yourself a master of legal cunning, wriggling through loopholes for petty gains, never realizing that a life that needs no judge is far nobler than a life spent in court.
D: Summary: Socrates describes something worse than simply needing lawyers: the person who takes pride in being a skilled litigant, boasting of his ability to manipulate the legal system for trivial advantages, never understanding that a life requiring no courts is far nobler.
D: Distilled: Pride in legal cunning for trivial gains is more disgraceful than merely needing lawyers; the noble life needs no courts at all.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- 'Bending like a withy' (withy = flexible willow twig) is a vivid image of moral spinelessness -- The 'napping judge' suggests judges bored by trivial cases -- The critique of litigiousness was topical in Plato's Athens, which was famous for its love of lawsuits -- This passage echoes critiques of sophistic rhetoric, which trained people to win arguments rather than pursue justice -- Cross-reference: Gorgias (471e-472c) on the contrast between legal victory and true justice; Apology (17a-18a) where Socrates contrasts his plain speech with courtroom rhetoric

D: Original:
Yes, he said, that is still more disgraceful. Well, I said, and to require the help of medicine, not when a wound has to be cured, or on occasion of an epidemic, but just because, by indolence and a habit of life such as we have been describing, men fill themselves with waters and winds, as if their bodies were a marsh, compelling the ingenious sons of Asclepius to find more names for diseases, such as flatulence and catarrh; is not this, too, a disgrace?

Yes, he said, they do certainly give very strange and newfangled names to diseases.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is still more disgraceful."

"Well," I said, "and to require the help of medicine, not when a wound has to be cured, or on occasion of an epidemic, but just because, by indolence and a habit of life such as we have been describing, men fill themselves with waters and winds, as if their bodies were a marsh, compelling the ingenious sons of Asclepius to find more names for diseases, such as flatulence and catarrh; is not this, too, a disgrace?"

"Yes," he said, "they do certainly give very strange and newfangled names to diseases."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that's even more disgraceful," he said.

"And isn't it equally disgraceful to need medicine -- not for treating wounds or epidemics, but simply because through laziness and the indulgent lifestyle we've been describing, people fill their bodies with fluids and gases as if they were marshes? They force the ingenious descendants of Asclepius to invent ever more names for diseases -- things like 'flatulence' and 'catarrh.' Isn't that a disgrace?"

"Yes," he said, "they certainly do come up with strange and newfangled disease names."
D: Elegant: It is disgraceful to need medicine not for wounds or epidemics but because an indulgent lifestyle has turned the body into a swamp, forcing physicians to invent new names for self-inflicted ailments like flatulence and catarrh.
D: Summary: Socrates ridicules the need for medicine that arises not from genuine injuries or epidemics but from self-indulgent lifestyles that fill the body with excess, forcing doctors to invent new names for diseases that are essentially the result of laziness and overindulgence.
D: Distilled: Medicine needed for self-inflicted ailments of indulgence is as disgraceful as law needed for self-inflicted disputes.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Sons of Asclepius' = physicians (Asclepius being the divine patron of medicine) -- 'Waters and winds' (humors and gases) reflects ancient Greek medical theory about bodily fluids -- The body-as-marsh metaphor is comic and vivid -- 'Flatulence and catarrh' as newfangled diseases suggests Plato's skepticism about medical specialization -- The parallel to legal proliferation is clear: both result from moral and physical self-indulgence -- Cross-reference: Timaeus (82a-86a) on the causes of disease; Gorgias (464a-465d) on medicine and gymnastics as true arts vs. cookery as flattery

D: Original: Yes, I said, and I do not believe that there were any such diseases in the days of Asclepius; and this I infer from the circumstance that the hero Eurypylus, after he has been wounded in Homer, drinks a posset of Pramnian wine well besprinkled with barley-meal and grated cheese, which are certainly inflammatory, and yet the sons of Asclepius who were at the Trojan war do not blame the damsel who gives him the drink, or rebuke Patroclus, who is treating his case.
D: Narrative: "Yes," I said, "and I do not believe that there were any such diseases in the days of Asclepius; and this I infer from the circumstance that the hero Eurypylus, after he has been wounded in Homer, drinks a posset of Pramnian wine well besprinkled with barley-meal and grated cheese, which are certainly inflammatory, and yet the sons of Asclepius who were at the Trojan war do not blame the damsel who gives him the drink, or rebuke Patroclus, who is treating his case."
D: Refashioned: "Yes, and I don't believe such diseases existed in Asclepius's day. My evidence? In Homer, after the hero Eurypylus is wounded, he drinks a mixture of Pramnian wine heavily sprinkled with barley meal and grated cheese -- ingredients that are certainly inflammatory. Yet Asclepius's sons, who were present at Troy, don't criticize the woman who serves the drink or rebuke Patroclus for administering it."
D: Elegant: Such lifestyle diseases didn't exist in Asclepius's time. Homer's Eurypylus drinks an inflammatory mixture of wine, barley, and cheese after being wounded, yet Asclepius's sons at Troy raise no objection -- because in that simpler age, such elaborate dietary concerns were unnecessary.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that the lifestyle diseases of his own era didn't exist in the simpler time of Asclepius, citing Homer: the wounded Eurypylus drinks an inflammatory concoction without any objection from the physicians present, suggesting a healthier baseline constitution that didn't require dietary micromanagement.
D: Distilled: In the simpler age of Homer, lifestyle diseases didn't exist and elaborate dietary restrictions were unnecessary because people were fundamentally healthier.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- Eurypylus is wounded in Iliad 11.824-48; the treatment is described at 11.638-41 -- Pramnian wine was a strong, dark wine -- The 'sons of Asclepius' at Troy were Machaon and Podalirius (Iliad 2.731-32) -- 'The damsel' is Hecamede, who prepares the drink (Iliad 11.624-41) -- Patroclus treating the wound: Iliad 11.844-48 -- The argument is primitivist: earlier ages were healthier because of simpler lifestyles -- Cross-reference: Statesman (271d-272b) on the simplicity of earlier ages; the 'simple city' of Book II (369d-372d)

D: Original:
Well, he said, that was surely an extraordinary drink to be given to a person in his condition.

Not so extraordinary, I replied, if you bear in mind that in former days, as is commonly said, before the time of Herodicus, the guild of Asclepius did not practise our present system of medicine, which may be said to educate diseases. But Herodicus, being a trainer, and himself of a sickly constitution, by a combination of training and doctoring found out a way of torturing first and chiefly himself, and secondly the rest of the world.

How was that? he said. By the invention of lingering death; for he had a mortal disease which he perpetually tended, and as recovery was out of the question, he passed his entire life as a valetudinarian; he could do nothing but attend upon himself, and he was in constant torment whenever he departed in anything from his usual regimen, and so dying hard, by the help of science he struggled on to old age.
D: Narrative:
"Well," he said, "that was surely an extraordinary drink to be given to a person in his condition."

"Not so extraordinary," I replied, "if you bear in mind that in former days, as is commonly said, before the time of Herodicus, the guild of Asclepius did not practise our present system of medicine, which may be said to educate diseases. But Herodicus, being a trainer, and himself of a sickly constitution, by a combination of training and doctoring found out a way of torturing first and chiefly himself, and secondly the rest of the world."

"How was that?" he said.

"By the invention of lingering death; for he had a mortal disease which he perpetually tended, and as recovery was out of the question, he passed his entire life as a valetudinarian; he could do nothing but attend upon himself, and he was in constant torment whenever he departed in anything from his usual regimen, and so dying hard, by the help of science he struggled on to old age."
D: Refashioned:
"Well," he said, "that's surely an extraordinary drink for someone in that condition."

"Not so extraordinary," I replied, "when you consider that before Herodicus, the medical tradition of Asclepius didn't practice our modern system of medicine -- which basically educates diseases into existence. Herodicus was a trainer with a sickly constitution who combined training with doctoring and discovered a way of torturing first himself, then the rest of the world."

"How so?" he said.

"He invented the lingering death. He had a terminal condition that he constantly managed. Since recovery was impossible, he spent his entire life as an invalid, doing nothing but tend to his own health. He was in constant torment whenever he deviated even slightly from his regimen. Through the help of medical science, he struggled painfully all the way to old age."
D: Elegant: Before Herodicus, medicine was simple. Herodicus -- a sickly trainer -- invented the art of managing terminal illness through perpetual medical attention, creating a life of pure invalidism. He didn't cure himself; he merely prolonged his dying, torturing himself and establishing a model that tortured the rest of the world.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that before Herodicus, Asclepian medicine was simple and effective. Herodicus, a trainer with a chronically weak constitution, invented the modern system of perpetual medical management, spending his entire life as an invalid, unable to do anything but tend to his own condition -- a model of lingering death rather than cure.
D: Distilled: Herodicus invented the lingering death: a life spent entirely managing illness rather than living, which corrupted medicine from cure to chronic maintenance.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Herodicus of Selymbria was a historical figure, a gymnastics trainer who combined athletic training with medical regimen -- 'Educate diseases' is a brilliant Platonic coinage: the medical system creates the conditions for the diseases it then treats -- The 'valetudinarian' (someone excessively preoccupied with health) is treated as a cautionary example -- Plato's critique anticipates modern debates about medicalization -- Cross-reference: Protagoras (316d-e) mentions Herodicus; Phaedrus (227d) references his walking regimen -- The Greek medical tradition is here contrasted with itself: early Asclepian medicine (simple, curative) vs. Herodicean medicine (complex, maintenance-based)

D: Original: A rare reward of his skill! Yes, I said; a reward which a man might fairly expect who never understood that, if Asclepius did not instruct his descendants in valetudinarian arts, the omission arose, not from ignorance or inexperience of such a branch of medicine, but because he knew that in all well-ordered states every individual has an occupation to which he must attend, and has therefore no leisure to spend in continually being ill. This we remark in the case of the artisan, but, ludicrously enough, do not apply the same rule to people of the richer sort.
D: Narrative:
"A rare reward of his skill!"

"Yes," I said; "a reward which a man might fairly expect who never understood that, if Asclepius did not instruct his descendants in valetudinarian arts, the omission arose, not from ignorance or inexperience of such a branch of medicine, but because he knew that in all well-ordered states every individual has an occupation to which he must attend, and has therefore no leisure to spend in continually being ill. This we remark in the case of the artisan, but, ludicrously enough, do not apply the same rule to people of the richer sort."
D: Refashioned:
"What a fine reward for his expertise!"

"Indeed -- the kind of reward you'd expect for someone who never understood why Asclepius didn't teach his descendants the art of managing chronic illness. The omission wasn't from ignorance or lack of medical skill. It was because Asclepius understood that in a well-ordered society, every person has a job to do and therefore no time to spend being perpetually ill.

We readily accept this principle when it comes to laborers and craftsmen, but absurdly, we don't apply the same standard to the wealthy."
D: Elegant: Asclepius didn't teach chronic illness management not from ignorance but from wisdom: in a well-ordered society, everyone has work to do and no time for perpetual sickness. We accept this for laborers but absurdly exempt the rich.
D: Summary: Socrates explains that Asclepius deliberately omitted teaching chronic illness management because he understood that in a well-ordered state, everyone has work to do and cannot afford to be perpetually ill. This principle is accepted for workers but absurdly not applied to the wealthy.
D: Distilled: Asclepius wisely omitted chronic illness management because a well-ordered society has no room for perpetual invalids, whether rich or poor.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The class distinction is important: the rich are judged more leniently for health-obsession than workers, which Socrates finds 'ludicrous' -- Asclepius is here presented as a statesman-physician who considers social utility -- 'In all well-ordered states every individual has an occupation' restates the specialization principle -- The implicit argument: chronic self-care is a form of idleness that harms the state -- Cross-reference: Book II (369d-370c) on the division of labor; the artisan analogy continues in 3.56

D: Original: How do you mean? he said. I mean this: When a carpenter is ill he asks the physician for a rough and ready cure; an emetic or a purge or a cautery or the knife, --these are his remedies. And if some one prescribes for him a course of dietetics, and tells him that he must swathe and swaddle his head, and all that sort of thing, he replies at once that he has no time to be ill, and that he sees no good in a life which is spent in nursing his disease to the neglect of his customary employment; and therefore bidding good-bye to this sort of physician, he resumes his ordinary habits, and either gets well and lives and does his business, or, if his constitution falls, he dies and has no more trouble.
D: Narrative:
"How do you mean?" he said.

"I mean this: When a carpenter is ill he asks the physician for a rough and ready cure; an emetic or a purge or a cautery or the knife, -- these are his remedies. And if some one prescribes for him a course of dietetics, and tells him that he must swathe and swaddle his head, and all that sort of thing, he replies at once that he has no time to be ill, and that he sees no good in a life which is spent in nursing his disease to the neglect of his customary employment; and therefore bidding good-bye to this sort of physician, he resumes his ordinary habits, and either gets well and lives and does his business, or, if his constitution falls, he dies and has no more trouble."
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean?" he said.

"I mean this: when a carpenter gets sick, he asks the doctor for a quick, decisive cure -- an emetic, a purge, cauterization, or surgery. Those are his remedies. If someone prescribes a prolonged dietary regimen and tells him to wrap and coddle his head and follow an elaborate treatment plan, he says at once that he has no time to be sick. He sees no point in a life spent nursing his illness while neglecting his work.

So he says goodbye to that kind of doctor, goes back to his normal habits, and either recovers and gets on with his life, or -- if his body gives out -- he dies and is done with it."
D: Elegant: The carpenter's approach to medicine is admirable in its simplicity: give me a quick cure or let me be. He has no time for prolonged treatment and no interest in a life spent managing illness. He either recovers through normal living or dies without prolonging the inevitable.
D: Summary: Socrates praises the carpenter's pragmatic approach to medicine: he demands a quick, decisive cure and refuses prolonged treatment because he has work to do. If a quick cure doesn't work, he returns to his normal life and either recovers naturally or dies -- either way, he doesn't waste his life nursing his disease.
D: Distilled: The carpenter models the right attitude toward illness: demand a quick cure, refuse prolonged treatment, and get back to work -- or accept death without prolonging the inevitable.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The carpenter is the model patient: pragmatic, no-nonsense, focused on productive work -- The four remedies (emetic, purge, cautery, knife) represent aggressive, decisive intervention -- 'Swathe and swaddle his head' satirizes the elaborate regimens of Herodicean medicine -- The binary outcome (get well or die) is presented as preferable to indefinite invalidism -- This passage has been influential in medical ethics discussions about quality of life vs. length of life -- Cross-reference: Gorgias (478a-479c) on the analogy between medicine for the body and justice for the soul

D: Original:
Yes, he said, and a man in his condition of life ought to use the art of medicine thus far only.

Has he not, I said, an occupation; and what profit would there be in his life if he were deprived of his occupation?

Quite true, he said. But with the rich man this is otherwise; of him we do not say that he has any specially appointed work which he must perform, if he would live.

He is generally supposed to have nothing to do. Then you never heard of the saying of Phocylides, that as soon as a man has a livelihood he should practise virtue?

Nay, he said, I think that he had better begin somewhat sooner.

Let us not have a dispute with him about this, I said; but rather ask ourselves: Is the practice of virtue obligatory on the rich man, or can he live without it? And if obligatory on him, then let us raise a further question, whether this dieting of disorders which is an impediment to the application of the mind t in carpentering and the mechanical arts, does not equally stand in the way of the sentiment of Phocylides?

Of that, he replied, there can be no doubt; such excessive care of the body, when carried beyond the rules of gymnastic, is most inimical to the practice of virtue.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "and a man in his condition of life ought to use the art of medicine thus far only."

"Has he not," I said, "an occupation; and what profit would there be in his life if he were deprived of his occupation?"

"Quite true," he said.

"But with the rich man this is otherwise; of him we do not say that he has any specially appointed work which he must perform, if he would live."

"He is generally supposed to have nothing to do."

"Then you never heard of the saying of Phocylides, that as soon as a man has a livelihood he should practise virtue?"

"Nay," he said, "I think that he had better begin somewhat sooner."

"Let us not have a dispute with him about this," I said; "but rather ask ourselves: Is the practice of virtue obligatory on the rich man, or can he live without it? And if obligatory on him, then let us raise a further question, whether this dieting of disorders which is an impediment to the application of the mind in carpentering and the mechanical arts, does not equally stand in the way of the sentiment of Phocylides?"

"Of that," he replied, "there can be no doubt; such excessive care of the body, when carried beyond the rules of gymnastic, is most inimical to the practice of virtue."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," he said, "a man in the carpenter's position should use medicine only to that extent."

"Because he has a job to do," I said. "What good is his life if he can't work?"

"Quite true," he said.

"But the rich man's case is different. We don't say he has any particular work he must perform in order to justify his existence."

"He's generally assumed to have nothing to do."

"Haven't you heard Phocylides' saying: 'Once a man has a livelihood, he should practice virtue'?"

"Actually," he said, "I think he should start somewhat sooner than that."

"Let's not argue with Phocylides about the timing. Instead, let's ask ourselves: is the practice of virtue obligatory for the rich? And if it is, then doesn't this obsessive health management that prevents carpenters from working equally prevent the rich from practicing virtue -- the very thing Phocylides says is their duty?"

"Without a doubt," he replied. "Excessive preoccupation with the body, beyond what good gymnastic requires, is absolutely hostile to the practice of virtue."
D: Elegant: The carpenter can't afford prolonged illness because he has work to do. The rich man supposedly has no work -- but Phocylides says his work is to practice virtue. And excessive health management impedes virtue just as much as it impedes carpentry. Therefore the rich have even less excuse for chronic invalidism.
D: Summary: Socrates extends the argument to the rich: if the carpenter can't afford to be perpetually ill because he has work, the rich person's work is to practice virtue (as Phocylides says). Excessive health management impedes the practice of virtue just as it impedes carpentry, so the wealthy have no excuse for chronic invalidism either.
D: Distilled: The rich man's occupation is virtue; excessive preoccupation with bodily health is as hostile to virtue as it is to carpentry.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Phocylides of Miletus was a 6th-century BC elegiac poet known for gnomic maxims -- His saying implies virtue is a luxury that follows material security -- Glaucon's response ('somewhat sooner') suggests virtue should precede wealth, not follow it -- The key move: even the rich have an 'occupation' (practicing virtue), so they too cannot afford perpetual illness -- Cross-reference: Aristotle Nicomachean Ethics I.8 (1098b-1099a) on external goods needed for virtue; Protagoras (326a-b) on wealth and virtue

D: Original: Yes, indeed, I replied, and equally incompatible with the management of a house, an army, or an office of state; and, what is most important of all, irreconcilable with any kind of study or thought or self-reflection --there is a constant suspicion that headache and giddiness are to be ascribed to philosophy, and hence all practising or making trial of virtue in the higher sense is absolutely stopped; for a man is always fancying that he is being made ill, and is in constant anxiety about the state of his body.
D: Narrative: "Yes, indeed," I replied, "and equally incompatible with the management of a house, an army, or an office of state; and, what is most important of all, irreconcilable with any kind of study or thought or self-reflection -- there is a constant suspicion that headache and giddiness are to be ascribed to philosophy, and hence all practising or making trial of virtue in the higher sense is absolutely stopped; for a man is always fancying that he is being made ill, and is in constant anxiety about the state of his body."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly," I replied. "And it's equally incompatible with managing a household, commanding an army, or holding public office. Most importantly, it's irreconcilable with any kind of serious study, thought, or self-reflection.

The chronic invalid constantly suspects that every headache or dizzy spell is caused by his philosophical exertions. This shuts down any attempt to practice or test virtue at the highest level, because the person is perpetually convinced he's making himself sick, and lives in constant anxiety about his body."
D: Elegant: Chronic health anxiety is incompatible with every serious endeavor -- household management, military command, statecraft. Worst of all, it kills philosophy, because the hypochondriac attributes every headache to thinking and abandons intellectual effort entirely.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that excessive health preoccupation is incompatible with every serious human endeavor -- managing a household, commanding an army, governing, and especially philosophy. The chronic invalid blames every headache on intellectual effort, making serious thought and self-reflection impossible.
D: Distilled: Excessive bodily preoccupation kills philosophy because the hypochondriac blames every discomfort on thinking and abandons intellectual effort.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The progression (household > army > state > philosophy) moves from practical to theoretical, culminating in self-reflection -- 'Headache and giddiness ascribed to philosophy' is both humorous and psychologically acute: psychosomatic symptoms become excuses to avoid intellectual effort -- The passage critiques what moderns might call 'wellness culture' when it displaces productive activity -- Cross-reference: Phaedo (66b-67b) on the body as an obstacle to philosophy; Theaetetus (173c-175b) on the philosopher's indifference to bodily concerns

D: Original:
Yes, likely enough. And therefore our politic Asclepius may be supposed to have exhibited the power of his art only to persons who, being generally of healthy constitution and habits of life, had a definite ailment; such as these he cured by purges and operations, and bade them live as usual, herein consulting the interests of the State; but bodies which disease had penetrated through and through he would not have attempted to cure by gradual processes of evacuation and infusion: he did not want to lengthen out good-for-nothing lives, or to have weak fathers begetting weaker sons; --if a man was not able to live in the ordinary way he had no business to cure him; for such a cure would have been of no use either to himself, or to the State.

Then, he said, you regard Asclepius as a statesman. Clearly; and his character is further illustrated by his sons. Note that they were heroes in the days of old and practised the medicines of which I am speaking at the siege of Troy: You will remember how, when Pandarus wounded Menelaus, they
D: Narrative:
"Yes, likely enough."

"And therefore our politic Asclepius may be supposed to have exhibited the power of his art only to persons who, being generally of healthy constitution and habits of life, had a definite ailment; such as these he cured by purges and operations, and bade them live as usual, herein consulting the interests of the State; but bodies which disease had penetrated through and through he would not have attempted to cure by gradual processes of evacuation and infusion: he did not want to lengthen out good-for-nothing lives, or to have weak fathers begetting weaker sons; -- if a man was not able to live in the ordinary way he had no business to cure him; for such a cure would have been of no use either to himself, or to the State."

"Then," he said, "you regard Asclepius as a statesman."

"Clearly; and his character is further illustrated by his sons. Note that they were heroes in the days of old and practised the medicines of which I am speaking at the siege of Troy: You will remember how, when Pandarus wounded Menelaus, they --"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, quite likely."

"So our politically minded Asclepius would have used his art only on people who were generally healthy in constitution and habits but had a specific ailment. He would cure them with decisive treatments and tell them to resume their normal lives -- always keeping the state's interests in mind.

But bodies thoroughly penetrated by disease he would not try to cure through prolonged courses of treatment. He had no desire to extend worthless lives or produce weak fathers begetting even weaker sons. If a person couldn't live in the ordinary way, Asclepius saw no point in curing him -- such a cure would benefit neither the patient nor the state."

"So you regard Asclepius as a statesman," he said.

"Clearly. And his sons confirm this. They were heroes of the old days who practiced the kind of medicine I'm describing at the siege of Troy. Remember when Pandarus wounded Menelaus -- they --"
D: Elegant: Asclepius the statesman-physician treated only those who were fundamentally healthy but had specific ailments, curing them decisively and sending them back to work. He refused to prolong the lives of the chronically ill, seeing no benefit to patient or state in extending worthless lives or producing ever-weaker offspring.
D: Summary: Socrates presents Asclepius as a statesman-physician who treated only fundamentally healthy people with specific ailments, using decisive cures. He refused to prolong the lives of the chronically ill, considering such treatment useless to both the individual and the state, and undesirable for producing weak offspring.
D: Distilled: Asclepius as statesman-physician treated only the fundamentally healthy, refusing to prolong worthless lives that served neither the individual nor the state.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage contains Plato's most controversial medical-political argument: that the state has an interest in not prolonging chronically ill lives -- 'Weak fathers begetting weaker sons' introduces a eugenic dimension -- Pandarus wounding Menelaus: Iliad 4.127-49 -- Machaon treats Menelaus: Iliad 4.190-219 -- Asclepius as 'statesman' (politikon) means his medicine serves political/social ends -- Cross-reference: Book V (459d-461c) on selective breeding in the guardian class; Laws 720a-720e on different types of physicians -- This passage has been cited in bioethics debates and is historically significant in discussions of medical rationing

D: Original: Sucked the blood out of the wound, and sprinkled soothing remedies, but they never prescribed what the patient was afterwards to eat or drink in the case of Menelaus, any more than in the case of Eurypylus; the remedies, as they conceived, were enough to heal any man who before he was wounded was healthy and regular in habits; and even though he did happen to drink a posset of Pramnian wine, he might get well all the same. But they would have nothing to do with unhealthy and intemperate subjects, whose lives were of no use either to themselves or others; the art of medicine was not designed for their good, and though they were as rich as Midas, the sons of Asclepius would have declined to attend them.
D: Narrative: "Sucked the blood out of the wound, and sprinkled soothing remedies, but they never prescribed what the patient was afterwards to eat or drink in the case of Menelaus, any more than in the case of Eurypylus; the remedies, as they conceived, were enough to heal any man who before he was wounded was healthy and regular in habits; and even though he did happen to drink a posset of Pramnian wine, he might get well all the same. But they would have nothing to do with unhealthy and intemperate subjects, whose lives were of no use either to themselves or others; the art of medicine was not designed for their good, and though they were as rich as Midas, the sons of Asclepius would have declined to attend them."
D: Refashioned:
"...sucked the blood from the wound and applied soothing remedies. But they never prescribed a special diet for Menelaus afterward, any more than for Eurypylus. They believed their treatments were enough to heal anyone who was already healthy and disciplined before being wounded -- even if he happened to drink an inflammatory wine posset, he'd recover just fine.

But chronically unhealthy and intemperate people? They wanted nothing to do with them. Such people's lives were of no use to themselves or to anyone else. Medicine wasn't designed for them, and even if they were as rich as Midas, the sons of Asclepius would have refused to treat them."
D: Elegant: The sons of Asclepius treated wounds with simple remedies and no special diets, trusting that healthy people heal naturally. They refused to treat the chronically unhealthy and intemperate, regardless of wealth -- medicine was not designed for those whose lives serve neither themselves nor others.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes his account of Asclepian medicine: the sons of Asclepius used simple wound treatment without dietary prescriptions, trusting healthy constitutions to heal. They categorically refused to treat chronically unhealthy or intemperate people, regardless of their wealth, because medicine was not designed for those whose lives serve no purpose.
D: Distilled: True medicine treats the fundamentally healthy; it refuses the chronically intemperate regardless of wealth, for their lives serve no purpose to themselves or society.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Sucked the blood and sprinkled remedies' describes Machaon's treatment of Menelaus, Iliad 4.218-19 -- The Eurypylus reference connects back to 3.53 -- 'As rich as Midas' is proverbial: King Midas of Phrygia was legendary for his wealth -- The refusal to treat the rich and intemperate is the most provocative claim: wealth cannot purchase medical treatment in Plato's ideal -- Cross-reference: Gorgias (464b-466a) on the distinction between genuine medicine and flattery; Book III (407d-408b) for the full context of this medical philosophy

D: Original: They were very acute persons, those sons of Asclepius. Naturally so, I replied. Nevertheless, the tragedians and Pindar disobeying our behests, although they acknowledge that Asclepius was the son of Apollo, say also that he was bribed into healing a rich man who was at the point of death, and for this reason he was struck by lightning. But we, in accordance with the principle already affirmed by us, will not believe them when they tell us both; --if he was the son of a god, we maintain that hd was not avaricious; or, if he was avaricious he was not the son of a god.
D: Narrative:
"They were very acute persons, those sons of Asclepius."

"Naturally so," I replied. "Nevertheless, the tragedians and Pindar disobeying our behests, although they acknowledge that Asclepius was the son of Apollo, say also that he was bribed into healing a rich man who was at the point of death, and for this reason he was struck by lightning. But we, in accordance with the principle already affirmed by us, will not believe them when they tell us both; -- if he was the son of a god, we maintain that he was not avaricious; or, if he was avaricious, he was not the son of a god."
D: Refashioned:
"Those sons of Asclepius were very shrewd."

"Naturally," I replied. "But the tragedians and Pindar defy our principles. Even though they acknowledge Asclepius was the son of Apollo, they also claim he was bribed into healing a rich man who was dying, and that Zeus struck him with lightning for it.

But according to our established principle, we won't accept both claims at once. If he was the son of a god, then he wasn't greedy. If he was greedy, then he wasn't the son of a god. You can't have it both ways."
D: Elegant: The tragedians and Pindar claim Asclepius was both Apollo's son and a corrupt physician bribed into healing the dying rich. By our principle, these claims are incompatible: a god's son cannot be avaricious, and an avaricious man cannot be a god's son.
D: Summary: Socrates applies the earlier principle (divine parentage and vice are incompatible) to the myth of Asclepius: the tragedians and Pindar cannot simultaneously claim he was Apollo's son and that he was bribed into healing a rich man -- these claims are logically incompatible.
D: Distilled: If Asclepius was Apollo's son, he was not greedy; if he was greedy, he was not Apollo's son -- poets cannot maintain both claims.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Pindar tells the story of Asclepius's death in Pythian 3.54-58 -- The tragedians who told this story may include Euripides -- Asclepius was struck by Zeus's thunderbolt for raising the dead (specifically Hippolytus, according to some versions) -- The logical disjunction echoes 3.15 (Theseus and Peirithous) -- Apollo then killed the Cyclopes in retaliation for Zeus killing his son -- Cross-reference: Book II (379b-c) on the principle that evil cannot come from the gods; Pindar Pythian 3 for the full myth

D: Original:
All that, Socrates, is excellent; but I should like to put a question to you: Ought there not to be good physicians in a State, and are not the best those who have treated the greatest number of constitutions good and bad? and are not the best judges in like manner those who are acquainted with all sorts of moral natures?

Yes, I said, I too would have good judges and good physicians. But do you know whom I think good?

Will you tell me? I will, if I can. Let me however note that in the same question you join two things which are not the same.
D: Narrative:
"All that, Socrates, is excellent; but I should like to put a question to you: Ought there not to be good physicians in a State, and are not the best those who have treated the greatest number of constitutions good and bad? and are not the best judges in like manner those who are acquainted with all sorts of moral natures?"

"Yes," I said, "I too would have good judges and good physicians. But do you know whom I think good?"

"Will you tell me?"

"I will, if I can. Let me however note that in the same question you join two things which are not the same."
D: Refashioned:
"All that is excellent, Socrates. But I have a question: shouldn't a state have good physicians? And aren't the best physicians those who've treated the widest range of constitutions, healthy and unhealthy? And similarly, aren't the best judges those who've dealt with every kind of moral character?"

"Yes," I said, "I want good judges and good physicians too. But do you know who I consider truly good?"

"Tell me."

"I will, if I can. But first notice that your question conflates two things that aren't the same."
D: Elegant: Glaucon asks whether the best physicians are those with the widest experience of illness, and the best judges those who know every kind of moral character. Socrates agrees in principle but warns that the analogy between physicians and judges is misleading -- the two cases differ fundamentally.
D: Summary: Glaucon asks whether the best physicians and judges are those with the widest experience of their respective domains. Socrates agrees that both are needed but warns that Glaucon's question conflates two fundamentally different cases -- the requirements for good physicians and good judges are not parallel.
D: Distilled: The best physicians and the best judges are both needed, but the qualifications for each differ fundamentally despite apparent parallels.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Glaucon's question is intelligent: it challenges the idea that physicians and judges should be limited in their experience -- Socrates' response ('two things which are not the same') sets up the distinction developed in 3.63-3.65 -- The physician-judge analogy is important throughout Greek philosophy -- Cross-reference: Gorgias (464b-465d) on the parallel between body and soul arts; Protagoras (313d-314b) on physicians of the soul

D: Original: How so? he asked. Why, I said, you join physicians and judges. Now the most skilful physicians are those who, from their youth upwards, have combined with the knowledge of their art the greatest experience of disease; they had better not be robust in health, and should have had all manner of diseases in their own persons. For the body, as I conceive, is not the instrument with which they cure the body; in that case we could not allow them ever to be or to have been sickly; but they cure the body with the mind, and the mind which has become and is sick can cure nothing.
D: Narrative:
"How so?" he asked.

"Why," I said, "you join physicians and judges. Now the most skilful physicians are those who, from their youth upwards, have combined with the knowledge of their art the greatest experience of disease; they had better not be robust in health, and should have had all manner of diseases in their own persons. For the body, as I conceive, is not the instrument with which they cure the body; in that case we could not allow them ever to be or to have been sickly; but they cure the body with the mind, and the mind which has become and is sick can cure nothing."
D: Refashioned:
"How so?" he asked.

"Because the cases of physicians and judges are fundamentally different. The most skilled physicians are those who've combined knowledge of their art with the widest experience of disease from youth onward. It's actually better if they aren't robustly healthy themselves -- they should have experienced all kinds of illness in their own bodies.

This makes sense because physicians don't cure the body with their body. If they did, we could never allow them to be or to have been sick. They cure the body with their mind. And a mind that has become sick is incapable of curing anything."
D: Elegant: The best physicians have personal experience of disease because they cure the body with the mind, not with their own body. A physician's bodily sickness enriches his understanding; but a sick mind cannot cure anything.
D: Summary: Socrates explains that physicians benefit from personal experience of disease because they cure the body with the mind, not with their own body. Having been sick themselves enriches their understanding. But this principle does not extend to judges, because a corrupted mind cannot serve justice.
D: Distilled: Physicians cure with the mind, not the body, so personal experience of illness helps them; but a morally corrupted mind cannot serve justice.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The principle 'they cure the body with the mind' is crucial: it justifies physicians having personal experience of illness while setting up the contrast with judges -- A sick body can still house a healthy mind, but a morally sick mind cannot judge rightly -- This passage distinguishes between bodily experience (useful for physicians) and moral experience (harmful for judges) -- Cross-reference: Charmides (156d-157a) on treating the soul to cure the body; Phaedo (64c-67b) on the soul's independence from the body

D: Original:
That is very true, he said. But with the judge it is otherwise; since he governs mind by mind; he ought not therefore to have been trained among vicious minds, and to have associated with them from youth upwards, and to have gone through the whole calendar of crime, only in order that he may quickly infer the crimes of others as he might their bodily diseases from his own self-consciousness; the honourable mind which is to form a healthy judgment should have had no experience or contamination of evil habits when young. And this is the reason why in youth good men often appear to be simple, and are easily practised upon by the dishonest, because they have no examples of what evil is in their own souls.

Yes, he said, they are far too apt to be deceived. Therefore, I said, the judge should not be young; he should have learned to know evil, not from his own soul, but from late and long observation of the nature of evil in others: knowledge should be his guide, not personal experience.
D: Narrative:
"That is very true," he said.

"But with the judge it is otherwise; since he governs mind by mind; he ought not therefore to have been trained among vicious minds, and to have associated with them from youth upwards, and to have gone through the whole calendar of crime, only in order that he may quickly infer the crimes of others as he might their bodily diseases from his own self-consciousness; the honourable mind which is to form a healthy judgment should have had no experience or contamination of evil habits when young. And this is the reason why in youth good men often appear to be simple, and are easily practised upon by the dishonest, because they have no examples of what evil is in their own souls."

"Yes," he said, "they are far too apt to be deceived."

"Therefore," I said, "the judge should not be young; he should have learned to know evil, not from his own soul, but from late and long observation of the nature of evil in others: knowledge should be his guide, not personal experience."
D: Refashioned:
"Very true," he said.

"But with judges it's different. A judge governs mind by mind. Therefore he should not have been raised among corrupt people, associating with criminals from youth, going through the entire catalog of crimes just so he can quickly diagnose the wrongdoing of others the way a physician diagnoses illness from personal experience.

The honorable mind that is to form sound judgments should have had no experience or contamination of evil habits in youth. This is why good young people often seem naive and are easily deceived by the dishonest -- they have no model of evil within their own souls."

"Yes," he said, "they are far too easily deceived."

"Therefore the judge should not be young. He should learn to recognize evil not from his own soul but from long observation of evil in others over time. Knowledge should be his guide, not personal experience."
D: Elegant: A judge governs mind by mind and must therefore not have been contaminated by personal experience of evil. Young good people seem naive because they have no inner model of wickedness. The judge should be old enough to have learned evil through long observation of others, guided by knowledge rather than personal experience.
D: Summary: Socrates explains why judges, unlike physicians, must not learn through personal experience of evil. Since judges govern mind by mind, a corrupted soul cannot judge rightly. Good young people appear naive because they lack inner models of evil. Therefore judges should be mature, having learned about evil through long observation rather than personal moral contamination.
D: Distilled: Judges must learn evil through long observation of others, not personal experience, because a mind corrupted by vice cannot govern other minds justly.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The physician/judge asymmetry is now clear: physicians need personal experience of bodily illness; judges need observational knowledge of moral evil without personal corruption -- 'Good men appear simple' (euetheis) in youth because they lack inner patterns of vice -- This connects to the theory of moral perception: we recognize in others what we know in ourselves -- The principle that judges should be mature anticipates modern judicial appointment practices -- Cross-reference: Meno (77b-78b) on whether anyone desires evil knowingly; Protagoras (352a-358d) on knowledge vs. experience of vice

D: Original: Yes, he said, that is the ideal of a judge. Yes, I replied, and he will be a good man (which is my answer to your question); for he is good who has a good soul. But the cunning and suspicious nature of which we spoke, --he who has committed many crimes, and fancies himself to be a master in wickedness, when he is amongst his fellows, is wonderful in the precautions which he takes, because he judges of them by himself: but when he gets into the company of men of virtue, who have the experience of age, he appears to be a fool again, owing to his unseasonable suspicions; he cannot recognise an honest man, because he has no pattern of honesty in himself; at the same time, as the bad are more numerous than the good, and he meets with them oftener, he thinks himself, and is by others thought to be, rather wise than foolish.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is the ideal of a judge."

"Yes," I replied, "and he will be a good man (which is my answer to your question); for he is good who has a good soul. But the cunning and suspicious nature of which we spoke, -- he who has committed many crimes, and fancies himself to be a master in wickedness, when he is amongst his fellows, is wonderful in the precautions which he takes, because he judges of them by himself: but when he gets into the company of men of virtue, who have the experience of age, he appears to be a fool again, owing to his unseasonable suspicions; he cannot recognise an honest man, because he has no pattern of honesty in himself; at the same time, as the bad are more numerous than the good, and he meets with them oftener, he thinks himself, and is by others thought to be, rather wise than foolish."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that's the ideal judge," he said.

"And he will be a good man -- which answers your original question. A person is good who has a good soul. Now consider the opposite type: the cunning, suspicious person who has committed many crimes and thinks himself a master of wickedness. Among his fellow criminals, he seems brilliant because he judges them by his own nature and takes every precaution.

But put him among virtuous, experienced people, and he looks like a fool. His suspicions are misplaced. He can't recognize an honest person because he has no pattern of honesty within himself. Yet because bad people outnumber good ones and he encounters them more often, he thinks himself wise -- and others think so too."
D: Elegant: The good judge has a good soul. The criminal who fancies himself a master of wickedness is shrewd among criminals but foolish among the virtuous -- he cannot recognize honesty, having no inner pattern for it. Since the bad outnumber the good, he mistakes his cunning for wisdom.
D: Summary: Socrates completes the physician-judge comparison: the good judge is simply a good person with a good soul. The criminal who considers himself a master of wickedness is only clever among fellow criminals; among the virtuous he appears foolish, unable to recognize honesty because he has no internal model for it.
D: Distilled: The criminal is clever among criminals but foolish among the virtuous, because we can only recognize in others what we carry within ourselves.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'He judges of them by himself' is the key psychological principle: we use ourselves as the template for understanding others -- The criminal's 'unseasonable suspicions' among the virtuous = projecting his own dishonesty onto honest people -- 'The bad are more numerous than the good' is a pessimistic empirical claim -- The inability to recognize virtue without possessing it connects to the Platonic principle that like knows like -- Cross-reference: Book VI (490a-b) on the philosopher's love of truth; Theaetetus (176a-b) on evil being more common than good in human affairs

D: Original:
Most true, he said. Then the good and wise judge whom we are seeking is not this man, but the other; for vice cannot know virtue too, but a virtuous nature, educated by time, will acquire a knowledge both of virtue and vice: the virtuous, and not the vicious, man has wisdom --in my opinion.

And in mine also. This is the sort of medicine, and this is the sort of law, which you sanction in your State. They will minister to better natures, giving health both of soul and of body; but those who are diseased in their bodies they will leave to die, and the corrupt and incurable souls they will put an end to themselves.

That is clearly the best thing both for the patients and for the State.

And thus our youth, having been educated only in that simple music which, as we said, inspires temperance, will be reluctant to go to law.

Clearly. And the musician, who, keeping to the same track, is content to practise the simple gymnastic, will have nothing to do with medicine unless in some extreme case.

That I quite believe. The very exercises and tolls which he undergoes are intended to stimulate the spirited element of his nature, and not to increase his strength; he will not, like common athletes, use exercise and regimen to develop his muscles.

Very right, he said. Neither are the two arts of music and gymnastic really designed, as is often supposed, the one for the training of the soul, the other fir the training of the body.

What then is the real object of them? I believe, I said, that the teachers of both have in view chiefly the improvement of the soul.

How can that be? he asked. Did you never observe, I said, the effect on the mind itself of exclusive devotion to gymnastic, or the opposite effect of an exclusive devotion to music?

In what way shown? he said. The one producing a temper of hardness and ferocity, the other of softness and effeminacy, I replied.

Yes, he said, I am quite aware that the mere athlete becomes too much of a savage, and that the mere musician is melted and softened beyond what is good for him.
D: Narrative:
"Most true," he said.

"Then the good and wise judge whom we are seeking is not this man, but the other; for vice cannot know virtue too, but a virtuous nature, educated by time, will acquire a knowledge both of virtue and vice: the virtuous, and not the vicious, man has wisdom -- in my opinion."

"And in mine also."

"This is the sort of medicine, and this is the sort of law, which you sanction in your State. They will minister to better natures, giving health both of soul and of body; but those who are diseased in their bodies they will leave to die, and the corrupt and incurable souls they will put an end to themselves."

"That is clearly the best thing both for the patients and for the State."

"And thus our youth, having been educated only in that simple music which, as we said, inspires temperance, will be reluctant to go to law."

"Clearly."

"And the musician, who, keeping to the same track, is content to practise the simple gymnastic, will have nothing to do with medicine unless in some extreme case."

"That I quite believe."

"The very exercises and toils which he undergoes are intended to stimulate the spirited element of his nature, and not to increase his strength; he will not, like common athletes, use exercise and regimen to develop his muscles."

"Very right," he said.

"Neither are the two arts of music and gymnastic really designed, as is often supposed, the one for the training of the soul, the other for the training of the body."

"What then is the real object of them?"

"I believe," I said, "that the teachers of both have in view chiefly the improvement of the soul."

"How can that be?" he asked.

"Did you never observe," I said, "the effect on the mind itself of exclusive devotion to gymnastic, or the opposite effect of an exclusive devotion to music?"

"In what way shown?" he said.

"The one producing a temper of hardness and ferocity, the other of softness and effeminacy," I replied.

"Yes," he said, "I am quite aware that the mere athlete becomes too much of a savage, and that the mere musician is melted and softened beyond what is good for him."
D: Refashioned:
"Most true," he said.

"So the good and wise judge we're seeking is not the criminal, but the virtuous man. Vice cannot truly know virtue, but a virtuous nature, educated by time and experience, acquires knowledge of both virtue and vice. Wisdom belongs to the virtuous, not the vicious -- in my opinion."

"And in mine."

"This, then, is the kind of medicine and the kind of law our state sanctions. They will serve better natures, providing health of both soul and body. Those whose bodies are hopelessly diseased will be left to die, and the corrupt and incurable souls will be put to death."

"That is clearly best for both the patients and the state."

"And our youth, educated in the simple music that inspires temperance, will rarely feel the need for courts."

"Clearly."

"And the person who follows the same path with simple gymnastic will rarely need medicine, except in emergencies."

"I quite believe it."

"His exercises and exertions are designed to stimulate the spirited element of his nature, not merely to build muscle. He won't train like an ordinary athlete, using diet and exercise just to develop his body."

"Very right," he said.

"In fact, music and gymnastic are not really designed -- as people commonly think -- one for the soul and the other for the body."

"Then what are they really for?"

"I believe both arts aim primarily at improving the soul."

"How can that be?" he asked.

"Haven't you noticed the effect on the mind of exclusive devotion to gymnastic? Or the opposite effect of exclusive devotion to music?"

"In what way?"

"One produces hardness and ferocity; the other, softness and weakness."

"Yes, I'm well aware that the pure athlete becomes too savage, and the pure musician becomes soft beyond what's healthy."
D: Elegant: Vice cannot know virtue, but virtue, educated by time, knows both -- so wisdom belongs to the virtuous. In our state, medicine and law serve better natures; the hopelessly diseased and incurably corrupt are let go. Both music and gymnastic aim at the soul, not (as commonly supposed) one at the soul and the other at the body. Exclusive gymnastic produces savagery; exclusive music produces weakness. The balance of both is needed.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes that wisdom belongs to the virtuous (who can understand both virtue and vice), outlines the state's harsh policy toward the incurably sick and corrupt, and reveals a surprising thesis: both music and gymnastic are designed for the soul, not (as commonly assumed) one for the soul and the other for the body. Exclusive devotion to either produces imbalance -- savagery or weakness.
D: Distilled: Wisdom belongs to the virtuous; both music and gymnastic aim at the soul; exclusive devotion to either produces dangerous imbalance.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Vice cannot know virtue' but virtue can know vice: asymmetric moral epistemology -- The harsh policy toward the incurable (bodily: leave to die; morally: put to death) is one of the Republic's most disturbing passages -- The revelation that both music and gymnastic serve the soul is a major philosophical insight, overturning conventional Greek educational theory -- The savage/soft dichotomy maps onto the spirited/philosophical duality in the soul -- Cross-reference: Book IV (439d-441c) on the three parts of the soul; Book IX (571c-572b) on the spirited element; Timaeus (87c-88b) on soul-body harmony

D: Original:
Yet surely, I said, this ferocity only comes from spirit, which, if rightly educated, would give courage, but, if too much intensified, is liable to become hard and brutal.

That I quite think. On the other hand the philosopher will have the quality of gentleness. And this also, when too much indulged, will turn to softness, but, if educated rightly, will be gentle and moderate.

True. And in our opinion the guardians ought to have both these qualities?

Assuredly. And both should be in harmony? Beyond question. And the harmonious soul is both temperate and courageous?
D: Narrative:
"Yet surely," I said, "this ferocity only comes from spirit, which, if rightly educated, would give courage, but, if too much intensified, is liable to become hard and brutal."

"That I quite think."

"On the other hand the philosopher will have the quality of gentleness. And this also, when too much indulged, will turn to softness, but, if educated rightly, will be gentle and moderate."

"True."

"And in our opinion the guardians ought to have both these qualities?"

"Assuredly."

"And both should be in harmony?"

"Beyond question."

"And the harmonious soul is both temperate and courageous?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yet this ferocity," I said, "comes from spirit -- which, properly educated, produces courage, but when overstimulated becomes hard and brutal."

"I agree."

"The philosophical nature, on the other hand, tends toward gentleness. Properly educated, this becomes genuine moderation; overindulged, it turns to weakness."

"True."

"And our guardians need both qualities?"

"Absolutely."

"And both must be in harmony?"

"Without question."

"And the harmonious soul is both temperate and courageous?"
D: Elegant: Spirit rightly educated becomes courage; overintensified, it becomes brutality. Gentleness rightly educated becomes moderation; overindulged, it becomes weakness. Guardians need both qualities in harmony, producing a soul that is both temperate and courageous.
D: Summary: Socrates shows that the spirited element, properly educated, becomes courage (but overstimulated becomes brutality), while the philosophical/gentle element, properly educated, becomes moderation (but overindulged becomes weakness). Guardians need both in harmony, producing a soul that is both temperate and courageous.
D: Distilled: Spirit rightly educated becomes courage; gentleness rightly educated becomes moderation; guardians need both in harmony.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The spirited (thumoeides) and philosophical (philosophon) elements correspond to two parts of the soul developed more fully in Book IV -- 'Hard and brutal' vs. 'soft' = the extremes that balanced education prevents -- The harmonious soul combining temperance and courage is the educational ideal -- The two virtues (courage and temperance) are the ones earlier associated with the Dorian and Phrygian harmonies (3.36) -- Cross-reference: Book IV (441e-442d) on the harmonious soul; Book II (375a-376c) on the guardian's need for both spiritedness and gentleness

D: Original: Yes. And the inharmonious is cowardly and boorish? Very true. And, when a man allows music to play upon him and to pour into his soul through the funnel of his ears those sweet and soft and melancholy airs of which we were just now speaking, and his whole life is passed in warbling and the delights of song; in the first stage of the process the passion or spirit which is in him is tempered like iron, and made useful, instead of brittle and useless. But, if he carries on the softening and soothing process, in the next stage he begins to melt and waste, until he has wasted away his spirit and cut out the sinews of his soul; and he becomes a feeble warrior.
D: Narrative:
"Yes."

"And the inharmonious is cowardly and boorish?"

"Very true."

"And, when a man allows music to play upon him and to pour into his soul through the funnel of his ears those sweet and soft and melancholy airs of which we were just now speaking, and his whole life is passed in warbling and the delights of song; in the first stage of the process the passion or spirit which is in him is tempered like iron, and made useful, instead of brittle and useless. But, if he carries on the softening and soothing process, in the next stage he begins to melt and waste, until he has wasted away his spirit and cut out the sinews of his soul; and he becomes a feeble warrior."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes."

"And the disharmonious soul is cowardly and crude?"

"Very true."

"Now, when a person gives himself over to music -- letting those sweet, soft, melancholy airs pour into his soul through the funnel of his ears, spending his whole life immersed in singing and the pleasures of song -- at first the process is beneficial. The passion or spirit within him is tempered like iron, made useful rather than brittle.

But if the softening continues unchecked, in the next stage he begins to melt and waste away. His spirit dissolves. The sinews of his soul are cut. And he becomes a feeble warrior."
D: Elegant: Music first tempers the spirit like iron, making it useful. But unrestrained musical indulgence continues the softening until spirit dissolves entirely, the sinews of the soul are severed, and the person becomes a feeble warrior incapable of action.
D: Summary: Socrates describes how music initially tempers the spirit beneficially, like the tempering of iron. But if musical softening continues unchecked, it melts and wastes the spirit, cutting the sinews of the soul and producing a feeble warrior incapable of courage.
D: Distilled: Music first tempers the soul's spirit like iron; continued unchecked, it melts spirit away entirely, producing a feeble warrior.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The iron-tempering metaphor is precise: the right amount of heat makes iron useful; too much makes it weak -- 'Through the funnel of his ears' is a vivid physical image of musical absorption -- 'Cut out the sinews of his soul' (neura ektemnei tes psyches) is one of Plato's most powerful metaphors -- The process has two stages: beneficial tempering, then destructive over-softening -- Cross-reference: Book X (606a-b) on how poetry 'waters' the emotions that should be dried; Phaedrus (245a) on divine madness and inspiration

D: Original: Very true. If the element of spirit is naturally weak in him the change is speedily accomplished, but if he have a good deal, then the power of music weakening the spirit renders him excitable; --on the least provocation he flames up at once, and is speedily extinguished; instead of having spirit he grows irritable and passionate and is quite impracticable.
D: Narrative:
"Very true."

"If the element of spirit is naturally weak in him the change is speedily accomplished, but if he have a good deal, then the power of music weakening the spirit renders him excitable; -- on the least provocation he flames up at once, and is speedily extinguished; instead of having spirit he grows irritable and passionate and is quite impracticable."
D: Refashioned:
"Very true."

"If his spirit is naturally weak, the transformation happens quickly. But if he has a strong spirit to begin with, the weakening effect of excessive music makes him volatile. At the slightest provocation he flares up, then immediately burns out. Instead of genuine spirit, he develops irritability and irrational passion -- he becomes completely impossible to deal with."
D: Elegant: Those with weak spirits are quickly ruined by musical excess. Those with strong spirits are made volatile -- flaring up at the slightest provocation, then immediately burning out, replacing genuine courage with empty irritability.
D: Summary: Socrates distinguishes two effects of musical excess depending on natural temperament: those with weak spirits are quickly made feeble, while those with naturally strong spirits become volatile and irritable -- flaring up and burning out quickly, replacing genuine courage with empty emotional reactivity.
D: Distilled: Musical excess makes the weak-spirited feeble and the strong-spirited volatile, replacing genuine courage with empty irritability in both cases.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The distinction between weak and strong spirits facing the same corrupting influence shows Plato's psychological sophistication -- 'Flames up and is speedily extinguished' = the signature of a corrupted spirit: intense but unsustainable emotional reactions -- 'Irritable and passionate' (akracholos kai thumoeides) replaces genuine courage (andreia) -- The difference between genuine spirit and mere irritability is crucial -- Cross-reference: Book IV (440c-441a) on the spirited element of the soul; Aristotle Nicomachean Ethics IV.5 on appropriate vs. inappropriate anger

D: Original:
Exactly. And so in gymnastics, if a man takes violent exercise and is a great feeder, and the reverse of a great student of music and philosophy, at first the high condition of his body fills him with pride and spirit, and lie becomes twice the man that he was.

Certainly. And what happens? if he do nothing else, and holds no con-a verse with the Muses, does not even that intelligence which there may be in him, having no taste of any sort of learning or enquiry or thought or culture, grow feeble and dull and blind, his mind never waking up or receiving nourishment, and his senses not being purged of their mists?

True, he said. And he ends by becoming a hater of philosophy, uncivilized, never using the weapon of persuasion, --he is like a wild beast, all violence and fierceness, and knows no other way of dealing; and he lives in all ignorance and evil conditions, and has no sense of propriety and grace.

That is quite true, he said. And as there are two principles of human nature, one the spirited and the other the philosophical, some God, as I should say, has given mankind two arts answering to them (and only indirectly to the soul and body), in order that these two principles (like the strings of an instrument) may be relaxed or drawn tighter until they are duly harmonised.

That appears to be the intention. And he who mingles music with gymnastic in the fairest proportions, and best attempers them to the soul, may be rightly called the true musician and harmonist in a far higher sense than the tuner of the strings.

You are quite right, Socrates. And such a presiding genius will be always required in our State if the government is to last.

Yes, he will be absolutely necessary. Such, then, are our principles of nurture and education: Where would be the use of going into further details about the dances of our citizens, or about their hunting and coursing, their gymnastic and equestrian contests? For these all follow the general principle, and having found that, we shall have no difficulty in discovering them.
D: Narrative:
"Exactly."

"And so in gymnastics, if a man takes violent exercise and is a great feeder, and the reverse of a great student of music and philosophy, at first the high condition of his body fills him with pride and spirit, and he becomes twice the man that he was."

"Certainly."

"And what happens? if he do nothing else, and holds no converse with the Muses, does not even that intelligence which there may be in him, having no taste of any sort of learning or enquiry or thought or culture, grow feeble and dull and blind, his mind never waking up or receiving nourishment, and his senses not being purged of their mists?"

"True," he said.

"And he ends by becoming a hater of philosophy, uncivilized, never using the weapon of persuasion, -- he is like a wild beast, all violence and fierceness, and knows no other way of dealing; and he lives in all ignorance and evil conditions, and has no sense of propriety and grace."

"That is quite true," he said.

"And as there are two principles of human nature, one the spirited and the other the philosophical, some God, as I should say, has given mankind two arts answering to them (and only indirectly to the soul and body), in order that these two principles (like the strings of an instrument) may be relaxed or drawn tighter until they are duly harmonised."

"That appears to be the intention."

"And he who mingles music with gymnastic in the fairest proportions, and best attempers them to the soul, may be rightly called the true musician and harmonist in a far higher sense than the tuner of the strings."

"You are quite right, Socrates."

"And such a presiding genius will be always required in our State if the government is to last."

"Yes, he will be absolutely necessary."

"Such, then, are our principles of nurture and education: Where would be the use of going into further details about the dances of our citizens, or about their hunting and coursing, their gymnastic and equestrian contests? For these all follow the general principle, and having found that, we shall have no difficulty in discovering them."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly."

"Now consider the opposite: gymnastics without music. If a man exercises violently, eats heavily, and never studies music or philosophy, at first his superb physical condition fills him with pride and energy. He feels twice the man he was."

"Certainly."

"But if he does nothing else -- never engages with the Muses, never tastes learning, inquiry, thought, or culture -- whatever intelligence he has grows feeble, dull, and blind. His mind never wakes up, never receives nourishment, his perceptions are never cleared of their fog."

"True," he said.

"He ends up hating philosophy. He becomes uncivilized, never using the weapon of persuasion. He's like a wild beast -- all violence and ferocity, knowing no other way. He lives in ignorance and degradation, with no sense of propriety or grace."

"Quite true," he said.
