"So there are two principles in human nature: the spirited and the philosophical. Some god, I would say, has given mankind two corresponding arts -- music and gymnastic -- aimed not directly at soul and body, but at these two principles. Like the strings of an instrument, the two principles must be loosened or tightened until they are properly harmonized."

"That seems to be the purpose."

"And the person who blends music and gymnastic in the best proportions, tuning them to the soul, may rightly be called the true musician and harmonist -- in a far higher sense than someone who merely tunes strings."

"You're quite right, Socrates."

"Such a person will always be needed in our state if the government is to endure."

"Absolutely necessary."

"These, then, are our principles of education. Why go into details about dances, hunting, riding, or athletic competitions? They all follow from the general principles we've established, and won't be hard to work out."
D: Elegant: Gymnastics without music produces a savage who hates philosophy and knows only violence. Music and gymnastic correspond to the two principles of human nature -- the philosophical and the spirited -- and must be harmonized like strings on an instrument. The person who achieves this blend is the true musician in the highest sense. Such a person will always be needed if the state is to endure.
D: Summary: Socrates shows that exclusive gymnastics produces an uncivilized brute who hates philosophy and uses only force. He reveals that music and gymnastic correspond to the two principles of human nature (philosophical and spirited) and must be harmonized. The person who achieves this balance is the true musician in the deepest sense, and such a person is essential for the state's survival.
D: Distilled: Music and gymnastic serve the two principles of human nature -- philosophical and spirited; their proper harmony produces the true musician, essential for the state's survival.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The symmetry is complete: excess music = soft/weak; excess gymnastics = hard/brutal; proper blend = harmonious soul -- 'Like the strings of an instrument' uses musical language to describe the educational goal -- 'The true musician and harmonist' = not a player of instruments but a tuner of souls -- 'A presiding genius' needed for the state = the philosopher-ruler anticipated -- The details (dances, hunting, equestrian) are deferred as derivative -- Cross-reference: Book IV (441e-442d) on psychic harmony; Book VII (521c-540b) on the philosopher-ruler's education; Statesman (305e-306a) on the weaving of spirited and gentle natures

D: Original:
I dare say that there will be no difficulty. Very good, I said; then what is the next question? Must we not ask who are to be rulers and who subjects?

Certainly. There can be no doubt that the elder must rule the younger.

Clearly. And that the best of these must rule. That is also clear. Now, are not the best husbandmen those who are most devoted to husbandry?

Yes. And as we are to have the best of guardians for our city, must they not be those who have most the character of guardians?

Yes. And to this end they ought to be wise and efficient, and to have a special care of the State?

True. And a man will be most likely to care about that which he loves?

To be sure. And he will be most likely to love that which he regards as having the same interests with himself, and that of which the good or evil fortune is supposed by him at any time most to affect his own?

Very true, he replied. Then there must be a selection. Let us note among the guardians those who in their whole life show the greatest eagerness to do what is for the good of their country, and the greatest repugnance to do what is against her interests.

Those are the right men. And they will have to be watched at every age, in order that we may see whether they preserve their resolution, and never, under the influence either of force or enchantment, forget or cast off their sense of duty to the State.

How cast off? he said. I will explain to you, I replied. A resolution may go out of a man's mind either with his will or against his will; with his will when he gets rid of a falsehood and learns better, against his will whenever he is deprived of a truth.

I understand, he said, the willing loss of a resolution; the meaning of the unwilling I have yet to learn.

Why, I said, do you not see that men are unwillingly deprived of good, and willingly of evil? Is not to have lost the truth an evil, and to possess the truth a good? and you would agree that to conceive things as they are is to possess the truth?

D: Refashioned:
"I expect not."

"Good. What comes next? We need to determine who will be rulers and who will be ruled."

"Certainly."

"Elders must rule the younger."

"Obviously."

"And the best among them must rule."

"Also clear."

"Just as the best farmers are the most devoted to farming, the best guardians must be those most devoted to guarding."

"Yes."

"They should be wise, capable, and genuinely care about the state."

"True."

"And a person cares most about what he loves."

"Of course."

"And he loves most what he believes shares his own interests -- whose fortune and misfortune he feels as his own."

"Very true," he replied.

"Those are the right people."

"And they must be observed at every age to see whether they maintain their commitment and never, through force or enchantment, abandon their sense of duty."

"What do you mean by 'cast off'?" he said.

"A conviction can leave a person's mind either willingly or unwillingly. Willingly, when he abandons a falsehood upon learning the truth. Unwillingly, when he is robbed of a truth."

"I understand the willing loss," he said, "but the unwilling loss needs explanation."

"Don't you see that people are unwillingly deprived of good things and willingly give up bad ones? Losing the truth is an evil. Possessing the truth is a good. And you'd agree that conceiving things as they are means possessing the truth?"
D: Elegant: The best rulers are those who care most about the state, identify their interests with its interests, and maintain their convictions throughout life. We must test them at every age against force and enchantment. A conviction can be lost willingly (abandoning falsehood for truth) or unwillingly (being robbed of truth) -- and no one is willingly deprived of what is good.
D: Summary: Socrates turns to selecting rulers from among the guardians. The best rulers are those who identify their interests completely with the state's, showing lifelong devotion. They must be tested at every age to ensure they maintain their convictions against force and enchantment. Socrates distinguishes between voluntary loss of conviction (correcting a falsehood) and involuntary loss (being robbed of truth).
D: Distilled: The best rulers identify their interests with the state's and must be tested throughout life to ensure they never lose their true convictions through force, deception, or enchantment.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The transition from education to selection of rulers marks a new phase of the argument -- The principle that people love what shares their interests grounds political authority in alignment of interests -- Three threats to conviction: force (bia), enchantment (goeteia), and theft (klope) -- developed in 3.72 -- 'No one is willingly deprived of good' connects to the Socratic paradox that no one errs willingly -- Cross-reference: Book V (473c-d) on philosopher-rulers; Book VII (519c-521b) on rulers who don't want to rule; Protagoras (345d-e) on the involuntary nature of error

D: Original:
Yes, he replied; I agree with you in thinking that mankind are deprived of truth against their will.

And is not this involuntary deprivation caused either by theft, or force, or enchantment?

Still, he replied, I do not understand you. I fear that I must have been talking darkly, like the tragedians. I only mean that some men are changed by persuasion and that others forget; argument steals away the hearts of one class, and time of the other; and this I call theft. Now you understand me?

Yes. Those again who are forced are those whom the violence of some pain or grief compels to change their opinion.

I understand, he said, and you are quite right. And you would also acknowledge that the enchanted are those who change their minds either under the softer influence of pleasure, or the sterner influence of fear?

Yes, he said; everything that deceives may be said to enchant.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he replied; "I agree with you in thinking that mankind are deprived of truth against their will."

"And is not this involuntary deprivation caused either by theft, or force, or enchantment?"

"Still," he replied, "I do not understand you."

"I fear that I must have been talking darkly, like the tragedians. I only mean that some men are changed by persuasion and that others forget; argument steals away the hearts of one class, and time of the other; and this I call theft. Now you understand me?"

"Yes."

"Those again who are forced are those whom the violence of some pain or grief compels to change their opinion."

"I understand," he said, "and you are quite right."

"And you would also acknowledge that the enchanted are those who change their minds either under the softer influence of pleasure, or the sterner influence of fear?"

"Yes," he said; "everything that deceives may be said to enchant."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," he replied, "I agree that people are deprived of truth against their will."

"And this involuntary deprivation happens through theft, force, or enchantment."

"I still don't follow," he said.

"I'm afraid I'm being obscure, like the tragedians. Let me be plain. Some people lose their convictions through persuasion, others through forgetting. Bad arguments steal the hearts of the first group; time steals from the second. That's what I mean by 'theft.' Do you understand now?"

"Yes."

"Those who lose convictions through 'force' are people whom intense pain or grief compels to change their minds."

"I understand, and you're right."

"And the 'enchanted' are those who change their minds under the seductive influence of pleasure or the intimidating influence of fear."

"Yes," he said. "Everything that deceives can be called enchantment."
D: Elegant: Truth is lost in three ways: by theft (persuasion or forgetting steal it), by force (pain or grief compel change), or by enchantment (pleasure seduces or fear intimidates). Everything that deceives may be called enchantment.
D: Summary: Socrates explains the three ways people involuntarily lose true convictions: theft (persuasion or time steals them away), force (pain or grief compels a change of mind), and enchantment (pleasure seduces or fear intimidates into abandoning truth). Glaucon adds that everything which deceives may be called enchantment.
D: Distilled: True convictions are lost through three forms of involuntary deprivation: theft by persuasion, force through pain, and enchantment through pleasure or fear.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The three-fold classification (theft/force/enchantment) provides the framework for testing future rulers -- 'Talking darkly, like the tragedians' is self-deprecating humor about his own obscurity -- Theft = argument or time eroding conviction -- Force = pain/grief overwhelming conviction -- Enchantment = pleasure/fear seducing conviction -- 'Everything that deceives may be said to enchant' (Glaucon's contribution) is an important philosophical insight -- Cross-reference: Sophist (228c-d) on the types of ignorance; Phaedrus (261a-262c) on rhetoric as enchantment

D: Original:
Therefore, as I was just now saying, we must enquire who are the best guardians of their own conviction that what they think the interest of the State is to be the rule of their lives. We must watch them from their youth upwards, and make them perform actions in which they are most likely to forget or to be deceived, and he who remembers and is not deceived is to be selected, and he who falls in the trial is to be rejected. That will be the way?

Yes. And there should also be toils and pains and conflicts prescribed for them, in which they will be made to give further proof of the same qualities.
D: Narrative:
"Therefore, as I was just now saying, we must enquire who are the best guardians of their own conviction that what they think the interest of the State is to be the rule of their lives. We must watch them from their youth upwards, and make them perform actions in which they are most likely to forget or to be deceived, and he who remembers and is not deceived is to be selected, and he who falls in the trial is to be rejected. That will be the way?"

"Yes."

"And there should also be toils and pains and conflicts prescribed for them, in which they will be made to give further proof of the same qualities."
D: Refashioned:
"So, as I was saying, we must find who are the best guardians of their own conviction that the state's interest must govern their lives. We must observe them from youth, putting them through situations where they're most likely to forget their principles or be deceived. Those who remember and resist deception are selected; those who fail are rejected."

"Yes."

"We should also prescribe hardships, suffering, and competitive struggles to test these same qualities further."
D: Elegant: Future rulers must be tested from youth in situations designed to make them forget or abandon their principles. Those who remain steadfast through trials of deception, hardship, and competition are selected; those who fail are rejected.
D: Summary: Socrates outlines the testing process for selecting rulers: they must be observed from youth through situations designed to make them forget or betray their principles, including hardships, pains, and competitive struggles. Only those who maintain their convictions are selected.
D: Distilled: Future rulers must be tested from youth through trials of deception and hardship; only those who maintain their convictions are selected.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The testing program is comprehensive: cognitive (deception), physical (toils and pains), and competitive (conflicts) -- Selection by elimination through testing anticipates modern assessment processes -- The emphasis on lifelong observation ('from their youth upwards') makes this a continuous evaluation, not a one-time test -- Cross-reference: Book VII (537a-540a) on the extended education and testing of philosopher-rulers; Laws 751c-752a on testing officials

D: Original: Very right, he replied. And then, I said, we must try them with enchantments that is the third sort of test --and see what will be their behaviour: like those who take colts amid noise and tumult to see if they are of a timid nature, so must we take our youth amid terrors of some kind, and again pass them into pleasures, and prove them more thoroughly than gold is proved in the furnace, that we may discover whether they are armed against all enchantments, and of a noble bearing always, good guardians of themselves and of the music which they have learned, and retaining under all circumstances a rhythmical and harmonious nature, such as will be most serviceable to the individual and to the State. And he who at every age, as boy and youth and in mature life, has come out of the trial victorious and pure, shall be appointed a ruler and guardian of the State; he shall be honoured in life and death, and shall receive sepulture and other memorials of honour, the greatest that we have to give. But him who fails, we must reject. I am inclined to think that this is the sort of way in which our rulers and guardians should be chosen and appointed. I speak generally, and not with any pretension to exactness.
D: Narrative:
"Very right," he replied.

"And then," I said, "we must try them with enchantments -- that is the third sort of test -- and see what will be their behaviour: like those who take colts amid noise and tumult to see if they are of a timid nature, so must we take our youth amid terrors of some kind, and again pass them into pleasures, and prove them more thoroughly than gold is proved in the furnace, that we may discover whether they are armed against all enchantments, and of a noble bearing always, good guardians of themselves and of the music which they have learned, and retaining under all circumstances a rhythmical and harmonious nature, such as will be most serviceable to the individual and to the State. And he who at every age, as boy and youth and in mature life, has come out of the trial victorious and pure, shall be appointed a ruler and guardian of the State; he shall be honoured in life and death, and shall receive sepulture and other memorials of honour, the greatest that we have to give. But him who fails, we must reject. I am inclined to think that this is the sort of way in which our rulers and guardians should be chosen and appointed. I speak generally, and not with any pretension to exactness."
D: Refashioned:
"Very right," he replied.

"Then we must test them with enchantments -- the third type. Like breeders who take young horses into noise and chaos to see if they're skittish, we must expose our youth to terrors and then to pleasures, testing them more rigorously than gold in a furnace.

We need to discover whether they're proof against all enchantments, maintaining noble composure in every situation -- true guardians of themselves and of the musical education they've received, keeping a rhythmical and harmonious nature under all circumstances, the kind most useful to themselves and to the state.

Whoever emerges victorious and pure from these trials at every age -- as boy, as young man, and in full maturity -- shall be appointed ruler and guardian of the state. He shall be honored in life and in death, receiving the greatest memorials of honor we can bestow. But whoever fails, we reject.

This is roughly how I think our rulers should be chosen. I'm speaking in general terms, without claiming precision."
D: Elegant: Like testing colts in chaos, we must expose candidates to terrors and pleasures, proving them more thoroughly than gold in a furnace. Those who emerge from every trial -- in boyhood, youth, and maturity -- maintaining their harmonious nature and noble bearing, are appointed rulers. Those who fail are rejected.
D: Summary: Socrates completes the selection process for rulers with the test of enchantment: candidates are exposed to terrors and pleasures at every stage of life, tested more rigorously than gold in a furnace. Only those who maintain their principles, composure, and harmonious nature through every trial are appointed as rulers and honored in life and death.
D: Distilled: Rulers are selected by lifelong testing through terrors and pleasures, more rigorously than gold in a furnace; only those who maintain their principles at every age are appointed.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The colt metaphor is vivid: young horses are tested for temperament in chaotic conditions -- 'More thoroughly than gold is proved in the furnace' = a standard of purity testing -- The testing at three ages (boy, youth, maturity) establishes a lifelong evaluation process -- 'Good guardians of themselves and of the music they have learned' links self-mastery to cultural education -- The modesty of 'I speak generally' is characteristic Socratic qualification -- Cross-reference: Book VII (537a-540a) provides a more detailed timeline for philosophical education and testing; Book VI (503b-504a) on the qualities needed for rulers

D: Original: And, speaking generally, I agree with you, he said. And perhaps the word 'guardian' in the fullest sense ought to be applied to this higher class only who preserve us against foreign enemies and maintain peace among our citizens at home, that the one may not have the will, or the others the power, to harm us. The young men whom we before called guardians may be more properly designated auxiliaries and supporters of the principles of the rulers.
D: Narrative:
"And, speaking generally, I agree with you," he said.

"And perhaps the word 'guardian' in the fullest sense ought to be applied to this higher class only who preserve us against foreign enemies and maintain peace among our citizens at home, that the one may not have the will, or the others the power, to harm us. The young men whom we before called guardians may be more properly designated auxiliaries and supporters of the principles of the rulers."
D: Refashioned:
"Speaking generally, I agree with you," he said.

"And perhaps the title 'guardian' in its fullest sense should be reserved for this higher class alone -- those who protect us from external enemies and maintain domestic peace, ensuring that outsiders lack the power and insiders lack the will to harm us. The young warriors we previously called guardians would be more properly termed 'auxiliaries' -- supporters and enforcers of the rulers' principles."
D: Elegant: The title 'guardian' properly belongs only to the tested rulers who protect against external enemies and maintain internal peace. The younger warriors are more accurately called 'auxiliaries' -- enforcers of the rulers' principles.
D: Summary: Socrates refines the terminology: 'guardian' in its fullest sense applies only to the proven rulers who protect against external threats and maintain internal order. The younger warriors previously called guardians are properly 'auxiliaries' -- supporters and enforcers of the rulers' principles.
D: Distilled: True 'guardians' are the tested rulers; the younger warriors are 'auxiliaries' who support and enforce the rulers' principles.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage establishes the crucial distinction between guardians proper (phylakes) and auxiliaries (epikouroi) -- The guardian class is now split into two: rulers (who have passed all tests) and warriors (who support them) -- External enemies + internal order = the two functions of the ruling class -- 'The one may not have the will' (internal) and 'the others the power' (external) to harm = different threats require different responses -- Cross-reference: Book IV (428a-429a) where the guardian/auxiliary distinction is important for defining wisdom and courage in the state

D: Original:
I agree with you, he said. How then may we devise one of those needful falsehoods of which we lately spoke --just one royal lie which may deceive the rulers, if that be possible, and at any rate the rest of the city?

What sort of lie? he said. Nothing new, I replied; only an old Phoenician tale of what has often occurred before now in other places, (as the poets say, and have made the world believe,) though not in our time, and I do not know whether such an event could ever happen again, or could now even be made probable, if it did.

How your words seem to hesitate on your lips! You will not wonder, I replied, at my hesitation when you have heard.
D: Narrative:
"I agree with you," he said.

"How then may we devise one of those needful falsehoods of which we lately spoke -- just one royal lie which may deceive the rulers, if that be possible, and at any rate the rest of the city?"

"What sort of lie?" he said.

"Nothing new," I replied; "only an old Phoenician tale of what has often occurred before now in other places, (as the poets say, and have made the world believe,) though not in our time, and I do not know whether such an event could ever happen again, or could now even be made probable, if it did."

"How your words seem to hesitate on your lips!"

"You will not wonder," I replied, "at my hesitation when you have heard."
D: Refashioned:
"I agree," he said.

"Now, how might we craft one of those necessary falsehoods we discussed earlier -- a single 'royal lie' that could convince the rulers themselves, if possible, and certainly the rest of the city?"

"What kind of lie?" he said.

"Nothing original," I replied. "Just an old Phoenician story about something that has supposedly happened in other places -- or so the poets say, and they've made people believe it -- though not in our time. I don't know whether such a thing could ever happen again or be made believable if it did."

"You seem to be hesitating!"

"You won't be surprised at my hesitation once you hear what it is."
D: Elegant: Socrates proposes devising a 'royal lie' -- a necessary falsehood to unite the city. He presents it as an old Phoenician tale, already hesitating at the audacity of what he's about to suggest.
D: Summary: Socrates introduces the famous 'noble lie' or 'royal lie,' presenting it hesitantly as an old Phoenician tale that must be devised to unite the city. His visible discomfort signals the audacity of what follows -- a founding myth designed to convince even the rulers, if possible, and certainly the rest of the citizenry.
D: Distilled: Socrates hesitantly introduces the 'noble lie' -- a necessary founding myth to unite the city, presented as an old Phoenician tale.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The 'noble lie' (gennaion pseudos) or 'royal lie' (literally 'well-born falsehood') is one of the most debated concepts in political philosophy -- 'Phoenician tale' may allude to Cadmus founding Thebes with earth-born soldiers -- Socrates' dramatic hesitation ('How your words seem to hesitate') makes the passage theatrical -- The lie is described as 'needful' (anagkaion), connecting to the earlier permission for rulers to lie for the public good (3.9) -- Cross-reference: Book II (382c-d) on the 'lie in the soul' vs. 'lie in words'; Laws 663d-e on beneficial falsehood; Karl Popper and Leo Strauss debate the political implications

D: Original:
Speak, he said, and fear not. Well then, I will speak, although I really know not how to look you in the face, or in what words to utter the audacious fiction, which I propose to communicate gradually, first to the rulers, then to the soldiers, and lastly to the people. They are to be told that their youth was a dream, and the education and training which they received from us, an appearance only; in reality during all that time they were being formed and fed in the womb of the earth, where they themselves and their arms and appurtenances were manufactured; when they were completed, the earth, their mother, sent them up; and so, their country being their mother and also their nurse, they are bound to advise for her good, and to defend her against attacks, and her citizens they are to regard as children of the earth and their own brothers.

You had good reason, he said, to be ashamed of the lie which you were going to tell.
D: Narrative:
"Speak," he said, "and fear not."

"Well then, I will speak, although I really know not how to look you in the face, or in what words to utter the audacious fiction, which I propose to communicate gradually, first to the rulers, then to the soldiers, and lastly to the people. They are to be told that their youth was a dream, and the education and training which they received from us, an appearance only; in reality during all that time they were being formed and fed in the womb of the earth, where they themselves and their arms and appurtenances were manufactured; when they were completed, the earth, their mother, sent them up; and so, their country being their mother and also their nurse, they are bound to advise for her good, and to defend her against attacks, and her citizens they are to regard as children of the earth and their own brothers."

"You had good reason," he said, "to be ashamed of the lie which you were going to tell."
D: Refashioned:
"Speak," he said, "and don't be afraid."

"Well, I'll speak, though I hardly know how to look you in the eye or find words for this audacious fiction. I propose to communicate it gradually -- first to the rulers, then to the soldiers, and finally to the people.

They are to be told that their youth was a dream and their education merely an illusion. In reality, during all that time they were being formed and nourished in the womb of the earth, where they and their weapons and equipment were fashioned. When they were complete, the earth -- their mother -- sent them up to the surface.

Therefore, since the earth is their mother and nurse, they are bound to protect her and defend her against all attacks. And they must regard their fellow citizens as brothers -- children of the same earth."

"You had good reason to be embarrassed about this lie," he said.
D: Elegant: The noble lie: everyone is told their education was a dream; in reality they were formed in the earth's womb and sent up complete. Therefore the earth is their mother, the country their nurse, and all citizens are brothers born from the same soil. This myth binds citizens to their land and to each other.
D: Summary: Socrates reveals the first part of the noble lie: citizens are to believe their education was a dream and that they were actually formed in the earth's womb, making the country their mother. This creates a mythic bond between citizens and their land, and establishes all citizens as siblings -- children of the earth.
D: Distilled: The noble lie's first part: all citizens were born from the earth, making their country their mother and all citizens brothers, bonding them to land and to each other.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The earth-born myth (autochthony) was a real element of Athenian civic ideology: Athenians believed they were 'autochthonous,' born from the soil of Attica -- The myth serves three functions: (1) bonds citizens to the land, (2) creates fraternity among citizens, (3) erases the artificiality of the social order -- Glaucon's response ('good reason to be ashamed') shows the lie's audacity even to a sympathetic listener -- 'Gradually, first to the rulers' = the lie must percolate down the social hierarchy -- Cross-reference: Menexenus (237b-238a) where Plato parodies Athenian autochthony myths; Timaeus (23d-24a) on Egyptian criticism of Greek origin stories

D: Original:
True, I replied, but there is more coming; I have only told you half. Citizens, we shall say to them in our tale, you are brothers, yet God has framed you differently. Some of you have the power of command, and in the composition of these he has mingled gold, wherefore also they have the greatest honour; others he has made of silver, to be auxillaries; others again who are to be husbandmen and craftsmen he has composed of brass and iron; and the species will generally be preserved in the children. But as all are of the same original stock, a golden parent will sometimes have a silver son, or a silver parent a golden son. And God proclaims as a first principle to the rulers, and above all else, that there is nothing which should so anxiously guard, or of which they are to be such good guardians, as of the purity of the race. They should observe what elements mingle in their off spring; for if the son of a golden or silver parent has an admixture of brass and iron, then nature orders a transposition of ranks, and the eye of the ruler must not be pitiful towards the child because he has to descend in the scale and become a husbandman or artisan, just as there may be sons of artisans who having an admixture of gold or silver in them are raised to honour, and become guardians or auxiliaries. For an oracle says that when a man of brass or iron guards the State, it will be destroyed. Such is the tale; is there any possibility of making our citizens believe in it?

Not in the present generation, he replied; there is no way of accomplishing this; but their sons may be made to believe in the tale, and their sons' sons, and posterity after them.

And God proclaims as a first principle to the rulers, and above all else, that there is nothing which they should so anxiously guard, or of which they are to be such good guardians, as of the purity of the race. D: Refashioned:
"True," I replied, "but there's more. I've only told you half.

In our tale, we'll say to the citizens: 'You are all brothers, but God has made you differently. Some of you have the capacity for leadership -- in their composition God mixed gold, which is why they have the greatest honor. Others are made of silver and serve as auxiliaries. Still others -- the farmers and craftsmen -- are composed of brass and iron.

The metal will generally pass from parent to child. But since everyone comes from the same stock, a golden parent may sometimes have a silver child, or a silver parent a golden one.

God's first commandment to the rulers, above all else, is this: nothing is more important for them to guard than the mixture of metals in the next generation. If a golden or silver parent produces a child with brass or iron, the child must be demoted without pity to the rank of farmer or craftsman. And if a craftsman's child shows gold or silver, that child must be elevated to guardian or auxiliary.

For an oracle declares that the state will be destroyed when it is guarded by a man of brass or iron.'

That's the tale. Is there any chance our citizens would believe it?"

"Not the present generation," he replied. "There's no way to accomplish that. But their children might believe it, and their children's children, and all posterity after them."
D: Elegant: The second part of the noble lie: God has mixed gold into rulers, silver into auxiliaries, brass and iron into workers. The metals generally pass to children, but mobility between classes must be maintained based on each child's nature. An oracle warns that the state falls when guarded by men of brass or iron. The present generation won't believe it, but future generations might.
D: Summary: Socrates reveals the second part of the noble lie: a myth of metals in which God has composed rulers of gold, auxiliaries of silver, and workers of brass and iron. While the metals usually pass to children, social mobility must be maintained based on each individual's nature. An oracle warns that the state will fall if the wrong metal guards it. Glaucon says the present generation won't believe it, but future ones might.
D: Distilled: The myth of metals: rulers are gold, auxiliaries silver, workers brass and iron, with mobility between classes based on individual nature; the state falls when guarded by the wrong metal.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The 'Myth of the Metals' or 'Allegory of the Metals' is the central component of the noble lie -- It serves two purposes: (1) justify the class structure, (2) mandate social mobility based on merit -- The possibility of a golden parent having a bronze child, and vice versa, makes this a meritocratic rather than hereditary system -- 'The eye of the ruler must not be pitiful' = hard-nosed assessment of actual capability -- The oracle about brass/iron guardians destroying the state echoes Hesiod's myth of the ages (Works and Days 106-201) -- Cross-reference: Book VIII (546a-547a) where the mixing of metals leads to the decline of the ideal state; Statesman (269a-274e) on cosmic myth and political order

D: Original: I see the difficulty, I replied; yet the fostering of such a belief will make them care more for the city and for one another. Enough, however, of the fiction, which may now fly abroad upon the wings of rumour, while we arm our earth-born heroes, and lead them forth under the command of their rulers. Let them look round and select a spot whence they can best suppress insurrection, if any prove refractory within, and also defend themselves against enemies, who like wolves may come down on the fold from without; there let them encamp, and when they have encamped, let them sacrifice to the proper Gods and prepare their dwellings.
D: Narrative: "I see the difficulty," I replied; "yet the fostering of such a belief will make them care more for the city and for one another. Enough, however, of the fiction, which may now fly abroad upon the wings of rumour, while we arm our earth-born heroes, and lead them forth under the command of their rulers. Let them look round and select a spot whence they can best suppress insurrection, if any prove refractory within, and also defend themselves against enemies, who like wolves may come down on the fold from without; there let them encamp, and when they have encamped, let them sacrifice to the proper Gods and prepare their dwellings."
D: Refashioned:
"I see the difficulty," I replied. "But fostering this belief will make citizens care more deeply for the city and for each other. But enough of the myth -- let it spread on the wings of rumor.

Meanwhile, let us arm our earth-born heroes and lead them forth under their rulers' command. Let them survey the land and choose a strategic position from which they can both suppress any internal rebellion and defend against external enemies who might descend on the fold like wolves. There they should make camp, offer sacrifices to the proper gods, and build their quarters."
D: Elegant: Though difficult to establish, the myth will make citizens care more for each other and for the city. Now let the myth spread while we turn to practical matters: arming the guardians, choosing a strategic position for defense against both internal rebellion and external attack, and establishing their camp.
D: Summary: Socrates acknowledges the difficulty of establishing the noble lie but argues it will foster civic devotion. He then turns to practical matters: the guardians must be armed, positioned strategically to handle both internal insurrection and external invasion, and settled in proper encampments.
D: Distilled: The noble lie fosters civic devotion; now the guardians must be strategically positioned to suppress internal rebellion and defend against external threats.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates -- 'Fly abroad upon the wings of rumour' = let the myth propagate naturally through oral culture -- 'Earth-born heroes' refers back to the autochthony myth just established -- The dual function of the guardian position (internal order + external defense) reflects the dual nature of their role -- 'Like wolves' threatening the fold continues the guardian-dog/sheep metaphor from Book II -- Cross-reference: Book II (374d-376c) on the guardian as watchdog; Book IV (419a-421c) on the guardians' living arrangements

D: Original:
Just so, he said. And their dwellings must be such as will shield them against the cold of winter and the heat of summer.

I suppose that you mean houses, he replied. Yes, I said; but they must be the houses of soldiers, and not of shop-keepers.

What is the difference? he said. That I will endeavour to explain, I replied. To keep watchdogs, who, from want of discipline or hunger, or some evil habit, or evil habit or other, would turn upon the sheep and worry them, and behave not like dogs but wolves, would be a foul and monstrous thing in a shepherd?

Truly monstrous, he said. And therefore every care must be taken that our auxiliaries, being stronger than our citizens, may not grow to be too much for them and become savage tyrants instead of friends and allies?

Yes, great care should be taken. And would not a really good education furnish the best safeguard?

But they are well-educated already, he replied. I cannot be so confident, my dear Glaucon, I said; I am much certain that they ought to be, and that true education, whatever that may be, will have the greatest tendency to civilize and humanize them in their relations to one another, and to those who are under their protection.

Very true, he replied. And not only their education, but their habitations, and all that belongs to them, should be such as will neither impair their virtue as guardians, nor tempt them to prey upon the other citizens. Any man of sense must acknowledge that.
D: Narrative:
"Just so," he said.

"And their dwellings must be such as will shield them against the cold of winter and the heat of summer."

"I suppose that you mean houses," he replied.

"Yes," I said; "but they must be the houses of soldiers, and not of shop-keepers."

"What is the difference?" he said.

"That I will endeavour to explain," I replied. "To keep watchdogs, who, from want of discipline or hunger, or some evil habit or other, would turn upon the sheep and worry them, and behave not like dogs but wolves, would be a foul and monstrous thing in a shepherd?"

"Truly monstrous," he said.

"And therefore every care must be taken that our auxiliaries, being stronger than our citizens, may not grow to be too much for them and become savage tyrants instead of friends and allies?"

"Yes, great care should be taken."

"And would not a really good education furnish the best safeguard?"

"But they are well-educated already," he replied.

"I cannot be so confident, my dear Glaucon," I said; "I am much certain that they ought to be, and that true education, whatever that may be, will have the greatest tendency to civilize and humanize them in their relations to one another, and to those who are under their protection."

"Very true," he replied.

"And not only their education, but their habitations, and all that belongs to them, should be such as will neither impair their virtue as guardians, nor tempt them to prey upon the other citizens. Any man of sense must acknowledge that."
D: Refashioned:
"Just so," he said.

"Their quarters must protect them from winter cold and summer heat."

"You mean houses," he replied.

"Yes, but soldiers' houses, not shopkeepers'."

"What's the difference?" he said.

"Let me explain with an analogy. If watchdogs -- through poor discipline, hunger, or bad habits -- turned on the sheep they were guarding, behaving like wolves instead of dogs, that would be a monstrous betrayal by the shepherd, wouldn't it?"

"Truly monstrous," he said.

"Then we must take every precaution to prevent our auxiliaries -- who are stronger than ordinary citizens -- from becoming savage tyrants instead of friends and protectors."

"Yes, great care is needed."

"Wouldn't a truly good education provide the best safeguard?"

"But they're already well educated," he replied.

"I'm not so sure about that, my dear Glaucon. I'm certain they ought to be, and that genuine education -- whatever its precise form -- will do the most to civilize them in their relations with each other and with those they protect."

"Very true," he replied.

"And not just their education -- their housing and possessions must be designed so as neither to undermine their virtue as guardians nor to tempt them to prey on other citizens. Any sensible person would agree."
D: Elegant: Watchdogs that turn on the sheep are monstrous. Our auxiliaries, being stronger than citizens, must not become tyrants. Education is the primary safeguard, but their housing and material conditions must also be designed to preserve their virtue and prevent them from preying on those they protect.
D: Summary: Socrates uses the watchdog analogy to warn that auxiliaries who prey on citizens are like dogs that attack sheep -- a monstrous betrayal. Education is the primary safeguard against this, but their living conditions must also be designed to preserve virtue and prevent the temptation to become tyrants over those they are meant to protect.
D: Distilled: Guardians who prey on citizens are watchdogs turned wolves; education and material conditions must prevent this monstrous betrayal.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The watchdog/wolf distinction from Book II (375a-376c) returns with greater urgency -- 'Soldiers' houses, not shopkeepers' distinguishes military austerity from commercial comfort -- Glaucon's confidence ('they're well-educated already') is gently corrected by Socrates' uncertainty -- 'True education, whatever that may be' shows characteristic Socratic humility about the details -- The principle that material conditions shape moral behavior is remarkably modern (structural/institutional approaches to ethics) -- Cross-reference: Book IV (419a-421c) develops the guardians' communal living arrangements; Book VIII (545d-547a) shows what happens when guardians acquire private property

D: Original:
He must. Then let us consider what will be their way of life, if they are to realize our idea of them. In the first place, none of them should have any property of his own beyond what is absolutely necessary; neither should they have a private house or store closed against any one who has a mind to enter; their provisions should be only such as are required by trained warriors, who are men of temperance and courage; they should agree to receive from the citizens a fixed rate of pay, enough to meet the expenses of the year and no more; and they will go and live together like soldiers in a camp. Gold and silver we will tell them that they have from God; the diviner metal is within them, and they have therefore no need of the dross which is current among men, and ought not to pollute the divine by any such earthly admixture; for that commoner metal has been the source of many unholy deeds, but their own is undefiled. And they alone of all the citizens may not touch or handle silver or gold, or be under the same roof with them, or wear them, or drink from them. And this will be their salvation, and they will be the saviours of the State. But should they ever acquire homes or lands or moneys of their own, they will become housekeepers and husbandmen instead of guardians, enemies and tyrants instead of allies of the other citizens; hating and being hated, plotting and being plotted against, they will pass their whole life in much greater terror of internal than of external enemies, and the hour of ruin, both to themselves and to the rest of the State, will be at hand. For all which reasons may we not say that thus shall our State be ordered, and that these shall be the regulations appointed by us for guardians concerning their houses and all other matters? other

Yes, said Glaucon.
D: Narrative:
"He must."

"Then let us consider what will be their way of life, if they are to realize our idea of them. In the first place, none of them should have any property of his own beyond what is absolutely necessary; neither should they have a private house or store closed against any one who has a mind to enter; their provisions should be only such as are required by trained warriors, who are men of temperance and courage; they should agree to receive from the citizens a fixed rate of pay, enough to meet the expenses of the year and no more; and they will go and live together like soldiers in a camp. Gold and silver we will tell them that they have from God; the diviner metal is within them, and they have therefore no need of the dross which is current among men, and ought not to pollute the divine by any such earthly admixture; for that commoner metal has been the source of many unholy deeds, but their own is undefiled. And they alone of all the citizens may not touch or handle silver or gold, or be under the same roof with them, or wear them, or drink from them. And this will be their salvation, and they will be the saviours of the State. But should they ever acquire homes or lands or moneys of their own, they will become housekeepers and husbandmen instead of guardians, enemies and tyrants instead of allies of the other citizens; hating and being hated, plotting and being plotted against, they will pass their whole life in much greater terror of internal than of external enemies, and the hour of ruin, both to themselves and to the rest of the State, will be at hand. For all which reasons may we not say that thus shall our State be ordered, and that these shall be the regulations appointed by us for guardians concerning their houses and all other matters?"

"Yes," said Glaucon.
D: Refashioned:
"He must."

"Then let's determine their way of life if they're to fulfill our vision. First, none of them should own any property beyond the barest necessities. They should have no private houses or storerooms closed to anyone who wishes to enter. Their food should be only what trained warriors of temperance and courage require. They should receive from the citizens a fixed annual stipend -- enough to cover their needs and nothing more. They will live communally, like soldiers in a camp.

As for gold and silver, we'll tell them they already possess the divine metal within themselves, placed there by God. They have no need of the common metal that circulates among ordinary people and should not pollute their inner gold with that earthly alloy -- for common gold and silver have been the source of countless unholy acts, while theirs is pure and undefiled.

They alone, of all citizens, may not touch, handle, or possess silver or gold, nor live under the same roof with it, nor wear it, nor drink from golden or silver cups.

This prohibition will be their salvation, and they will be the saviors of the state. But should they ever acquire private homes, land, or money, they will transform from guardians into landowners, from allies into enemies and tyrants over the other citizens. Hating and being hated, plotting and being plotted against, they will live in far greater fear of internal enemies than external ones -- and ruin, for themselves and the entire state, will be at hand.

For all these reasons, shall we establish these as the regulations governing our guardians' housing and all other material arrangements?"

"Yes," said Glaucon.
D: Elegant: Guardians must own nothing, live communally, accept only a modest stipend, and never touch gold or silver. They already possess the divine metal within. If they ever acquire private property, they transform from guardians into tyrants, from allies into enemies -- and the state's ruin becomes inevitable.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes the guardians' radical communal lifestyle: no private property, no closed houses, communal living like soldiers, a modest fixed stipend, and an absolute prohibition on possessing gold or silver. Their inner divine metal makes external wealth unnecessary. If they ever acquire private property, they will inevitably become tyrants rather than guardians, and the state will be destroyed.
D: Distilled: Guardians must live communally without private property or gold; if they ever acquire wealth, they transform from protectors into tyrants and the state is destroyed.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage establishes the famous communist arrangements for the guardian class -- The prohibition on gold connects to the Myth of the Metals: guardians have gold within, so external gold would 'pollute' the divine -- 'Enemies and tyrants instead of allies' describes the corruption cycle that begins the decline of regimes in Books VIII-IX -- The fear of 'internal enemies' greater than external ones describes the psychology of tyranny -- 'Like soldiers in a camp' emphasizes the military origins of guardian life -- Cross-reference: Book IV (419a-421c) where Adeimantus challenges these arrangements as making guardians unhappy; Book V (457c-461e) extends communism to wives and children; Book VIII (547b-c) where guardians begin acquiring property, triggering the state's decline

D: Original: Here Adeimantus interposed a question: How would you answer, Socrates, said he, if a person were to say that you are making these people miserable, and that they are the cause of their own unhappiness; the city in fact belongs to them, but they are none the better for it; whereas other men acquire lands, and build large and handsome houses, and have everything handsome about them, offering sacrifices to the gods on their own account, and practising hospitality; moreover, as you were saying just now, they have gold and silver, and all that is usual among the favourites of fortune; but our poor citizens are no better than mercenaries who are quartered in the city and are always mounting guard?
D: Narrative: Here Adeimantus interposed a question: "How would you answer, Socrates," said he, "if a person were to say that you are making these people miserable, and that they are the cause of their own unhappiness; the city in fact belongs to them, but they are none the better for it; whereas other men acquire lands, and build large and handsome houses, and have everything handsome about them, offering sacrifices to the gods on their own account, and practising hospitality; moreover, as you were saying just now, they have gold and silver, and all that is usual among the favourites of fortune; but our poor citizens are no better than mercenaries who are quartered in the city and are always mounting guard?"
D: Refashioned: At this point, Adeimantus jumped in with a question: "How would you respond, Socrates, if someone said you're making these guardians miserable -- that they're the cause of their own unhappiness? The city belongs to them, yet they get nothing out of it. Other people acquire land, build grand houses, furnish them beautifully, make sacrifices to the gods, and entertain guests. As you yourself just pointed out, they have gold and silver and all the trappings of good fortune. But our citizens? They're nothing more than hired soldiers garrisoned in the city, perpetually standing watch."
D: Elegant: Adeimantus raises an objection: aren't we making the guardians miserable? The city belongs to them, yet they gain nothing from it. Other men acquire property, live in luxury, and enjoy every advantage of wealth. Our guardians are no better than mercenaries permanently stationed on guard duty.
D: Summary: Adeimantus challenges Socrates by asking how he would respond to the charge that the guardians are being made miserable -- they own the city but enjoy none of its benefits, living like mercenaries while others accumulate wealth and luxury.
D: Distilled: Adeimantus objects that the guardians, despite being the city's protectors, live like impoverished mercenaries while others enjoy wealth and comfort.
Notes: Speakers: Adeimantus poses the question, Socrates will respond -- Adeimantus is Plato's brother, along with Glaucon -- This objection follows directly from Book III's establishment of the guardians' austere communal lifestyle -- The mercenary comparison anticipates the distinction between true guardians and mere soldiers -- Cross-ref: Book III 416d-417b on the prohibition of private property for guardians -- The concern about guardian happiness recurs in Book VII when discussing the philosopher's reluctant return to the cave

D: Original:
Yes, I said; and you may add that they are only fed, and not paid in addition to their food, like other men; and therefore they cannot, if they would, take a journey of pleasure; they have no money to spend on a mistress or any other luxurious fancy, which, as the world goes, is thought to be happiness; and many other accusations of the same nature might be added.

But, said he, let us suppose all this to be included in the charge.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," I said; "and you may add that they are only fed, and not paid in addition to their food, like other men; and therefore they cannot, if they would, take a journey of pleasure; they have no money to spend on a mistress or any other luxurious fancy, which, as the world goes, is thought to be happiness; and many other accusations of the same nature might be added."

"But," said he, "let us suppose all this to be included in the charge."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," I said, "and you could add that they're only given food -- no salary on top of it, like other men receive. So even if they wanted to, they couldn't take a vacation. They have no money to spend on a lover or any other indulgence that the world considers happiness. And plenty of similar accusations could be piled on."

"Well," he said, "let's assume all of that is included in the charge."
D: Elegant: Socrates concedes the objection can be pushed even further: the guardians receive only food, no wages. They cannot travel for pleasure or spend on luxuries the world considers happiness. Adeimantus agrees to include all such charges.
D: Summary: Socrates actually strengthens Adeimantus's objection by noting that the guardians receive only food with no pay, cannot travel for pleasure, and have no money for any indulgence the world considers happiness. Adeimantus accepts all this as part of the charge.
D: Distilled: Socrates amplifies the objection -- guardians receive only food, no wages, and cannot partake in any pleasure the world associates with happiness.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates amplifies the charge, Adeimantus accepts it -- Socrates' rhetorical strategy of strengthening the opponent's case before answering is characteristic of his dialectical method -- The list of pleasures (travel, mistresses, luxury) represents conventional Greek happiness (eudaimonia) -- Cross-ref: This anticipates the fuller treatment of pleasure in Book IX -- The word 'happiness' here reflects the common understanding Plato will challenge

D: Original: You mean to ask, I said, what will be our answer? Yes. If we proceed along the old path, my belief, I said, is that we shall find the answer. And our answer will be that, even as they are, our guardians may very likely be the happiest of men; but that our aim in founding the State was not the disproportionate happiness of any one class, but the greatest happiness of the whole; we thought that in a State which is ordered with a view to the good of the whole we should be most likely to find Justice, and in the ill-ordered State injustice: and, having found them, we might then decide which of the two is the happier. At present, I take it, we are fashioning the happy State, not piecemeal, or with a view of making a few happy citizens, but as a whole; and by-and-by we will proceed to view the opposite kind of State. Suppose that we were painting a statue, and some one came up to us and said, Why do you not put the most beautiful colours on the most beautiful parts of the body --the eyes ought to be purple, but you have made them black --to him we might fairly answer, Sir, you would not surely have us beautify the eyes to such a degree that they are no longer eyes; consider rather whether, by giving this and the other features their due proportion, we make the whole beautiful. And so I say to you, do not compel us to assign to the guardians a sort of happiness which will make them anything but guardians; for we too can clothe our husbandmen in royal apparel, and set crowns of gold on their heads, and bid them till the ground as much as they like, and no more. Our potters also might be allowed to repose on couches, and feast by the fireside, passing round the winecup, while their wheel is conveniently at hand, and working at pottery only as much as they like; in this way we might make every class happy-and then, as you imagine, the whole State would be happy.
D: Narrative:
"You mean to ask," I said, "what will be our answer?"

"Yes."

"If we proceed along the old path, my belief," I said, "is that we shall find the answer. And our answer will be that, even as they are, our guardians may very likely be the happiest of men; but that our aim in founding the State was not the disproportionate happiness of any one class, but the greatest happiness of the whole; we thought that in a State which is ordered with a view to the good of the whole we should be most likely to find Justice, and in the ill-ordered State injustice: and, having found them, we might then decide which of the two is the happier. At present, I take it, we are fashioning the happy State, not piecemeal, or with a view of making a few happy citizens, but as a whole; and by-and-by we will proceed to view the opposite kind of State.

Suppose that we were painting a statue, and some one came up to us and said, 'Why do you not put the most beautiful colours on the most beautiful parts of the body -- the eyes ought to be purple, but you have made them black' -- to him we might fairly answer, 'Sir, you would not surely have us beautify the eyes to such a degree that they are no longer eyes; consider rather whether, by giving this and the other features their due proportion, we make the whole beautiful.'

And so I say to you, do not compel us to assign to the guardians a sort of happiness which will make them anything but guardians; for we too can clothe our husbandmen in royal apparel, and set crowns of gold on their heads, and bid them till the ground as much as they like, and no more. Our potters also might be allowed to repose on couches, and feast by the fireside, passing round the winecup, while their wheel is conveniently at hand, and working at pottery only as much as they like; in this way we might make every class happy -- and then, as you imagine, the whole State would be happy."
D: Refashioned:
"You want to know how we'd answer that?" I said.

"Yes."

"If we stay on the path we've been following, I believe we'll find the answer. Our guardians, even as they are, may well be the happiest of men. But our goal in founding the city was never the lopsided happiness of any single class -- it was the greatest happiness of the whole. We believed that in a city ordered for the good of everyone, we'd be most likely to find justice, and in a disordered city, injustice. Having found them, we could then judge which produces more happiness. Right now, we're designing the happy city as a whole -- not piecemeal, not to make a few citizens happy at the expense of others.

Imagine we were painting a statue, and someone came up and said, 'Why haven't you used the most beautiful colors on the most beautiful parts? The eyes should be purple, but you've made them black.' We'd rightly answer: 'Surely you wouldn't have us beautify the eyes to the point where they're no longer recognizable as eyes. Consider instead whether, by giving each feature its proper proportion, we make the whole thing beautiful.'

So I say to you: don't force us to give the guardians a kind of happiness that would make them anything but guardians. We could just as easily dress our farmers in royal robes, crown them with gold, and tell them to farm only when they feel like it. Our potters could lounge on couches, feast by the fire, pass around the wine, keep the wheel handy, and work only as much as they please. We could make every class 'happy' this way -- and then, as you imagine, the whole city would be happy."
D: Elegant: The aim in founding the city was not the disproportionate happiness of one class, but the greatest happiness of the whole. In a city ordered for the common good, justice is most likely to be found. Just as a painter would not use the most beautiful colors on the eyes if it made them cease to look like eyes -- each part must have its proper proportion for the whole to be beautiful. Give every class self-indulgent happiness and none will perform its function; the city will collapse.
D: Summary: Socrates answers that the city was founded not for the happiness of any single class but for the greatest happiness of the whole. He uses the analogy of painting a statue: you don't make the eyes the most beautiful color if it ruins the whole. Making every class indulgently happy would destroy each class's function and the city itself.
D: Distilled: The city's aim is the happiness of the whole, not any single class; each part must fulfill its proper function for the whole to flourish.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates delivers the core response to Adeimantus's challenge -- The statue-painting analogy is one of Plato's most famous images for organic unity -- 'Greatest happiness of the whole' is sometimes read as proto-utilitarian, but Plato's meaning is structural, not aggregative -- Cross-ref: The organic unity principle returns in Book VIII when describing how cities degenerate -- The farmer and potter examples show how indulging any class destroys its function -- Cross-ref: The principle of specialization established in Book II (369b-370c)

D: Original: But do not put this idea into our heads; for, if we listen to you, the husbandman will be no longer a husbandman, the potter will cease to be a potter, and no one will have the character of any distinct class in the State. Now this is not of much consequence where the corruption of society, and pretension to be what you are not, is confined to cobblers; but when the guardians of the laws and of the government are only seemingly and not real guardians, then see how they turn the State upside down; and on the other hand they alone have the power of giving order and happiness to the State. We mean our guardians to be true saviours and not the destroyers of the State, whereas our opponent is thinking of peasants at a festival, who are enjoying a life of revelry, not of citizens who are doing their duty to the State. But, if so, we mean different things, and he is speaking of something which is not a State. And therefore we must consider whether in appointing our guardians we would look to their greatest happiness individually, or whether this principle of happiness does not rather reside in the State as a whole.
D: Narrative:
"But do not put this idea into our heads; for, if we listen to you, the husbandman will be no longer a husbandman, the potter will cease to be a potter, and no one will have the character of any distinct class in the State. Now this is not of much consequence where the corruption of society, and pretension to be what you are not, is confined to cobblers; but when the guardians of the laws and of the government are only seemingly and not real guardians, then see how they turn the State upside down; and on the other hand they alone have the power of giving order and happiness to the State.

We mean our guardians to be true saviours and not the destroyers of the State, whereas our opponent is thinking of peasants at a festival, who are enjoying a life of revelry, not of citizens who are doing their duty to the State. But, if so, we mean different things, and he is speaking of something which is not a State. And therefore we must consider whether in appointing our guardians we would look to their greatest happiness individually, or whether this principle of happiness does not rather reside in the State as a whole."
D: Refashioned:
"But don't let this line of thinking take hold -- because if we listen to it, the farmer will stop being a farmer, the potter will stop being a potter, and no class in the city will retain its distinct character. Now, when this kind of corruption -- pretending to be what you're not -- is limited to cobblers, it doesn't matter much. But when the guardians of the laws and government are only pretending to be guardians, watch how they turn the whole city upside down. And yet, they alone have the power to bring the city order and happiness.

We intend our guardians to be the city's true protectors, not its destroyers. Our critic is imagining peasants at a festival, enjoying a life of revelry -- not citizens performing their duty. If that's his vision, we're talking about completely different things. What he's describing isn't a city at all. So the real question is: in appointing our guardians, should we aim at their individual happiness, or does true happiness reside in the city as a whole?"
D: Elegant: If every class abandons its function for pleasure, the city loses its structure. When corruption is confined to cobblers, it matters little -- but when guardians become merely apparent guardians, they destroy the city. True guardians are protectors, not revelers. The question is whether happiness belongs to individual classes or to the whole.
D: Summary: Socrates warns that if classes abandon their roles for pleasure, the city's structure collapses. False guardians are especially dangerous since they alone have the power to bring order or ruin. The real question is whether happiness resides in individual classes or in the city as a whole.
D: Distilled: When guardians abandon their true function for personal happiness, they become the city's destroyers rather than its protectors.

That is evident. Here, then, is a discovery of new evils, I said, against which the guardians will have to watch, or they will creep into the city unobserved.

D: Refashioned:
"If this is true, then the guardians, auxiliaries, and everyone else must be compelled or encouraged to do their own work as well as possible. This way, the whole city will develop in proper order, and each class will receive the share of happiness that nature assigns to it."

"True."

"Clearly."

As for war against wealthy enemies, trained warriors will always outfight rich amateurs.

The guardians must prevent both from creeping in. When Adeimantus worries about fighting wealthy enemies, Socrates argues trained warriors will always defeat rich amateurs.
D: Distilled: Both wealth and poverty corrupt excellence in any craft; the guardians must prevent both extremes to maintain the city's order and strength.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus in rapid dialogue -- The potter example illustrates the corrupting effects of both extremes of wealth -- 'Sinews of war' is a proverbial expression -- The argument that trained warriors beat wealthy amateurs reflects Greek military values and Spartan-style thinking -- Cross-ref: Book II's principle of specialization -- Cross-ref: Book VIII on how wealth-accumulation leads to oligarchic degeneration -- Wealth and poverty as twin evils anticipates Aristotle's doctrine of the mean

"That is true," he said.

"Hardly, if they came upon him at once."

"Certainly," he said, "there would be nothing wonderful in that."

"Likely enough."

"Then we may assume that our athletes will be able to fight with two or three times their own number?"
D: Refashioned:
"True," he said.

"Consider this, Adeimantus: wouldn't a single expert boxer easily handle two well-fed gentlemen who don't know how to fight?"

"Not if they attacked him simultaneously."

"But what if he could retreat and then turn to strike whoever came forward first? If he repeated this tactic under a scorching sun, couldn't an expert take down more than one out-of-shape opponent?"

"Certainly -- there'd be nothing surprising about that."

"And rich men probably have a greater advantage in boxing skill than they do in military training."

"Probably so."

"Then we can assume our trained warriors could fight opponents two or three times their number."
D: Elegant: An expert boxer can defeat multiple untrained wealthy opponents by using skill and tactics -- retreating, picking them off one by one, exploiting their poor conditioning. If this holds in boxing, it holds even more in warfare. Trained warriors can fight far superior numbers of undisciplined rich men.
D: Summary: Socrates uses a boxing analogy: a skilled boxer can defeat multiple wealthy but untrained opponents through superior technique and conditioning. Since the advantage of training over wealth is even greater in warfare than boxing, their warriors can fight enemies two or three times their number.
D: Distilled: Expert training defeats superior numbers of wealthy but undisciplined opponents, in boxing as in warfare.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The boxing analogy reflects the Greek gymnasium culture where military and athletic training overlapped -- The tactical description (retreat, turn, strike) resembles actual Greek light infantry tactics -- This argument parallels Spartan military ideology: a small, well-trained force defeats larger, wealthier armies -- Cross-ref: The Battle of Thermopylae as a historical example Plato's audience would have known -- The 'scorching sun' detail emphasizes the advantage of physical conditioning

D: Narrative:
"I agree with you, for I think you right. And suppose that, before engaging, our citizens send an embassy to one of the two cities, telling them what is the truth: 'Silver and gold we neither have nor are permitted to have, but you may; do you therefore come and help us in war, and take the spoils of the other city.' Who, on hearing these words, would choose to fight against lean wiry dogs, rather than, with the dogs on their side, against fat and tender sheep?"

"That is not likely; and yet there might be a danger to the poor State if the wealth of many States were to be gathered into one."

"But how simple of you to use the term 'State' at all of any but our own!"
D: Refashioned:
"I agree -- you're right. And imagine that before battle, our citizens send ambassadors to one of the two enemy cities with this message: 'We have no silver or gold, and we're forbidden to possess any -- but you may have all you want. Come fight alongside us, and you can keep all the spoils from the other city.' Who would choose to fight against lean, wiry dogs when they could fight alongside those dogs against fat, tender sheep?"

"That seems right. But wouldn't there be a danger if many wealthy cities combined their resources against our poor one?"

"How naive of you to use the word 'city' for any of those places but our own!"
D: Elegant: A city without gold can form alliances by offering wealthy allies the spoils of their enemies. No one would choose to fight lean, hardened warriors when they could fight alongside them against soft, wealthy opponents. As for coalitions of wealthy cities -- they are not true cities at all.
D: Summary: Socrates proposes a diplomatic strategy: offer wealthy allies all war spoils in exchange for their help, since no one would rather fight trained warriors than fight alongside them. When Adeimantus worries about coalitions of wealthy enemies, Socrates challenges whether such places deserve the name 'city' at all.
D: Distilled: A disciplined city without wealth can win allies by offering spoils, and wealthy but divided cities are not true cities at all.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Lean wiry dogs vs. fat tender sheep' is a vivid Platonic image contrasting discipline with self-indulgence -- The diplomatic strategy of offering spoils reflects real Greek alliance practices -- Socrates' correction about the term 'State/city' (polis) sets up the next passage's argument that internally divided cities are not true political communities -- Cross-ref: This anticipates the argument in 4.8 about every city being divided into rich and poor

D: Original: Why so? You ought to speak of other States in the plural number; not one of them is a city, but many cities, as they say in the game. For indeed any city, however small, is in fact divided into two, one the city of the poor, the other of the rich; these are at war with one another; and in either there are many smaller divisions, and you would be altogether beside the mark if you treated them all as a single State. But if you deal with them as many, and give the wealth or power or persons of the one to the others, you will always have a great many friends and not many enemies. And your State, while the wise order which has now been prescribed continues to prevail in her, will be the greatest of States, I do not mean to say in reputation or appearance, but in deed and truth, though she number not more than a thousand defenders. A single State which is her equal you will hardly find, either among Hellenes or barbarians, though many that appear to be as great and many times greater.
D: Narrative:
"Why so?"

"You ought to speak of other States in the plural number; not one of them is a city, but many cities, as they say in the game. For indeed any city, however small, is in fact divided into two, one the city of the poor, the other of the rich; these are at war with one another; and in either there are many smaller divisions, and you would be altogether beside the mark if you treated them all as a single State.

But if you deal with them as many, and give the wealth or power or persons of the one to the others, you will always have a great many friends and not many enemies. And your State, while the wise order which has now been prescribed continues to prevail in her, will be the greatest of States, I do not mean to say in reputation or appearance, but in deed and truth, though she number not more than a thousand defenders. A single State which is her equal you will hardly find, either among Hellenes or barbarians, though many that appear to be as great and many times greater."
D: Refashioned:
"Why do you say that?"

"Because you should speak of those other places in the plural. None of them is really one city -- each is many cities, as the saying goes. Every city, no matter how small, is actually divided into at least two: the city of the poor and the city of the rich, and these two are at war with each other. Within each, there are further divisions. You'd be completely wrong to treat any of them as a single unified state.

But if you treat them as the many factions they are, and offer the wealth, power, or people of one faction to another, you'll always have plenty of allies and few enemies. And our city, as long as its wise order holds, will be the greatest of all cities -- not in appearance or reputation, but in reality and truth, even if it has no more than a thousand defenders. You'll hardly find its equal among Greeks or foreigners, though many cities may appear as great or far greater."
D: Elegant: Every conventional city is actually divided into at least two warring cities -- the rich and the poor -- with further divisions within each. Treat these factions as separate entities and you can always find allies among them. A truly unified city, even with only a thousand defenders, will be the greatest in reality if not in appearance.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that every conventional city is really multiple cities at war with each other -- at minimum, the city of the rich versus the city of the poor. By exploiting these divisions, a unified city can always find allies. Their city, though small, will be the greatest in truth because it alone is truly one.
D: Distilled: Every city is really divided into warring factions of rich and poor; only a truly unified city, however small, is genuinely great.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates explains to Adeimantus -- 'As they say in the game' refers to a board game (petteia) where one piece represents many -- The rich-poor division as civil war is a profound sociological observation that influenced Marx and others -- Cross-ref: Book VIII extensively develops how class conflict drives political degeneration (oligarchy to democracy to tyranny) -- 'Hellenes or barbarians' encompasses the entire known world -- The contrast between appearance and reality is a central Platonic theme -- Cross-ref: Thucydides' account of stasis (civil conflict) in Corcyra
