That is most true, he said. And what, I said, will be the best limit for our rulers to fix when they are considering the size of the State and the amount of territory which they are to include, and beyond which they will not go?

What limit would you propose? I would allow the State to increase so far as is consistent with unity; that, I think, is the proper limit.

Very good, he said. Here then, I said, is another order which will have to be conveyed to our guardians: Let our city be accounted neither large nor small, but one and self-sufficing.

And surely, said he, this is not a very severe order which we impose upon them.
D: Narrative:
"That is most true," he said.

"And what," I said, "will be the best limit for our rulers to fix when they are considering the size of the State and the amount of territory which they are to include, and beyond which they will not go?"

"What limit would you propose?"

"I would allow the State to increase so far as is consistent with unity; that, I think, is the proper limit."

"Very good," he said.

"Here then," I said, "is another order which will have to be conveyed to our guardians: Let our city be accounted neither large nor small, but one and self-sufficing."

"And surely," said he, "this is not a very severe order which we impose upon them."
D: Refashioned:
"That's absolutely true," he said.

"So what should be the limit our rulers set when deciding the city's size and territory -- beyond which they won't expand?"

"What limit would you suggest?"

"The city should grow only as far as it can while remaining unified. That's the proper limit."

"Good."

"So here's another directive for our guardians: let the city be considered neither large nor small, but one and self-sufficient."

"That doesn't seem like a particularly harsh requirement," he said.
D: Elegant: The city should expand only so far as is consistent with its unity. The proper directive for the guardians: the city should be neither large nor small, but one and self-sufficient.
D: Summary: Socrates proposes that the city's growth should be limited by what preserves its unity. The directive to the guardians is simple: the city should be one and self-sufficient, neither too large nor too small.
D: Distilled: The city should grow only as far as it can remain unified and self-sufficient.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Unity as the limit of growth reflects Greek suspicion of imperial expansion -- 'One and self-sufficing' (autarkeia) is a key concept in Greek political thought, later central to Aristotle's Politics -- Cross-ref: Book II's founding of the city based on mutual need and self-sufficiency -- The idea contrasts with Athenian imperial ambitions that Plato witnessed -- Aristotle (Politics 1326b) later discusses optimal city size in detail

D: Refashioned: "And the other duty we mentioned earlier is lighter still," I said, "-- the responsibility of demoting the children of guardians when they prove inferior, and promoting the children of lower classes when they show natural superiority. The whole point is that every individual should be assigned the role nature designed them for -- one person, one job. When each person does their own work, each becomes unified in purpose rather than scattered. And so the whole city becomes one rather than many."
D: Elegant: Children of guardians who prove inferior must be demoted; children of lower classes who show natural ability must be elevated. Each person should do the one thing nature fitted them for. When individuals are unified in purpose, the city itself becomes one.
D: Summary: Socrates describes the principle of social mobility based on natural ability: guardian children who prove inferior are demoted while talented children from lower classes are elevated. The goal is that each person does the one work nature intended, making both individual and city unified rather than fragmented.
D: Distilled: Social mobility based on natural ability ensures each person does their proper work, making both individual and city truly unified.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates -- This is the meritocratic principle of the Republic, remarkable for ancient Greece -- 'One to one work' restates the specialization principle from Book II -- The phrase 'be one and not many' connects individual unity to civic unity, foreshadowing the tripartite soul argument later in Book IV -- Cross-ref: Book III 415a-c, the Allegory of the Metals (gold, silver, bronze souls) -- Cross-ref: The 'Noble Lie' that justifies this social mobility -- This principle is both progressive (mobility) and restrictive (fixed roles)

"Yes," he said, "that's not too difficult."

"Education and upbringing. A well-started city builds momentum like a wheel -- good education produces good character, which produces better education, improving with each generation. Above all, rulers must preserve the established forms of music and gymnastic education without innovation.

A well-started city gains momentum: good education produces good character, which improves education further across generations. The rulers' supreme task is preserving music and gymnastic education in their original form.

D: Original:
Yes, said Adeimantus; and you may add my suffrage to Damon's and your own.

Then, I said, our guardians must lay the foundations of their fortress in music?

Yes, he said; the lawlessness of which you speak too easily steals in.

Yes, I replied, in the form of amusement; and at first sight it appears harmless.

Why, yes, he said, and there is no harm; were it not that little by little this spirit of licence, finding a home, imperceptibly penetrates into manners and customs; whence, issuing with greater force, it invades contracts between man and man, and from contracts goes on to laws and constitutions, in utter recklessness, ending at last, Socrates, by an overthrow of all rights, private as well as public.

Is that true? I said. That is my belief, he replied. Then, as I was saying, our youth should be trained from the first in a stricter system, for if amusements become lawless, and the youths themselves become lawless, they can never grow up into well-conducted and virtuous citizens.

Very true, he said. And when they have made a good beginning in play, and by the help of music have gained the habit of good order, then this habit of order, in a manner how unlike the lawless play of the others! will accompany them in all their actions and be a principle of growth to them, and if there be any fallen places a principle in the State will raise them up again.

Very true, he said. Thus educated, they will invent for themselves any lesser rules which their predecessors have altogether neglected.

What do you mean? I mean such things as these: --when the young are to be silent before their elders; how they are to show respect to them by standing and making them sit; what honour is due to parents; what garments or shoes are to be worn; the mode of dressing the hair; deportment and manners in general. You would agree with me?

Yes. But there is, I think, small wisdom in legislating about such matters, --I doubt if it is ever done; nor are any precise written enactments about them likely to be lasting.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," said Adeimantus; "and you may add my suffrage to Damon's and your own."

"Then," I said, "our guardians must lay the foundations of their fortress in music?"

"Yes," he said; "the lawlessness of which you speak too easily steals in."

"Yes," I replied, "in the form of amusement; and at first sight it appears harmless."

"Why, yes," he said, "and there is no harm; were it not that little by little this spirit of licence, finding a home, imperceptibly penetrates into manners and customs; whence, issuing with greater force, it invades contracts between man and man, and from contracts goes on to laws and constitutions, in utter recklessness, ending at last, Socrates, by an overthrow of all rights, private as well as public."

"Is that true?" I said.

"That is my belief," he replied.

"Then, as I was saying, our youth should be trained from the first in a stricter system, for if amusements become lawless, and the youths themselves become lawless, they can never grow up into well-conducted and virtuous citizens."

"Very true," he said.

"And when they have made a good beginning in play, and by the help of music have gained the habit of good order, then this habit of order, in a manner how unlike the lawless play of the others! will accompany them in all their actions and be a principle of growth to them, and if there be any fallen places in the State will raise them up again."

"Very true," he said.

"Thus educated, they will invent for themselves any lesser rules which their predecessors have altogether neglected."

"What do you mean?"

"I mean such things as these: -- when the young are to be silent before their elders; how they are to show respect to them by standing and making them sit; what honour is due to parents; what garments or shoes are to be worn; the mode of dressing the hair; deportment and manners in general. You would agree with me?"

"Yes."

"But there is, I think, small wisdom in legislating about such matters -- I doubt if it is ever done; nor are any precise written enactments about them likely to be lasting."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," said Adeimantus, "add my vote to Damon's and yours."

"Then our guardians must build their fortress on the foundation of music?"

"Yes -- the lawlessness you describe creeps in all too easily."

"It does, disguised as harmless amusement."

"Exactly. It seems harmless at first. But little by little, this spirit of license finds a foothold and imperceptibly seeps into manners and customs. From there, with growing force, it invades business dealings and contracts. From contracts it moves on to laws and constitutions, with complete recklessness, until finally, Socrates, it overthrows all rights, both private and public."

"Is that really how it works?"

"That is what I believe."

"Then our youth must be trained from the start in a stricter system. If their amusements become lawless, they themselves become lawless, and they can never grow into well-conducted, virtuous citizens."

"True."

"But when they make a good beginning in play and through music develop habits of order, those habits -- so different from lawless play -- will accompany them in everything they do, becoming a principle of growth. If any part of the city has fallen into disrepair, those habits will restore it."

"True."

"Citizens educated this way will figure out for themselves all the minor rules their predecessors neglected."

"Such as?"

"Things like: when the young should be silent before their elders, how to show respect by standing and offering seats, what honor is owed to parents, appropriate clothing and shoes, hairstyles, deportment and manners generally."

"Yes, I'd agree."

"But there's little wisdom in trying to legislate such things. I doubt it's ever been done successfully, and precise written rules about them never last."
D: Elegant: Lawlessness enters the city disguised as harmless amusement. It begins in play, seeps into manners and customs, invades contracts and laws, and ultimately overthrows all rights. The antidote is strict early education in music: youth who develop habits of order through disciplined play carry those habits into every sphere of life. Well-educated citizens will discover proper manners for themselves -- such things cannot be legislated.
D: Summary: Lawlessness enters disguised as harmless amusement in music and play, gradually corrupting manners, contracts, laws, and eventually all rights. The solution is strict early education: youth trained in orderly music develop habits of order that pervade their entire lives. Well-educated citizens will discover proper social manners on their own -- legislating such details is futile.
D: Distilled: Lawlessness creeps in through amusement and gradually corrupts everything; only disciplined early education in music can instill the habits of order that prevent this decay.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus in extended dialogue -- Adeimantus unusually delivers the key argument about lawlessness spreading from amusement to constitutions -- This 'slippery slope' from cultural to political corruption is one of Plato's most influential ideas -- Cross-ref: Book III on education in music and gymnastic -- Cross-ref: Book VIII on the progression of political degeneration -- The list of manners (silence before elders, standing, hairstyles) reflects actual Greek social norms -- The claim that manners cannot be legislated is notable given the Republic's extensive legislation elsewhere -- 'Fallen places in the State' suggests music education has restorative, not just preventive, power

D: Original:
Impossible. It would seem, Adeimantus, that the direction in which education starts a man, will determine his future life. Does not like always attract like?

To be sure. Until some one rare and grand result is reached which may be good, and may be the reverse of good?

That is not to be denied. And for this reason, I said, I shall not attempt to legislate further about them.

"That is not to be denied."

D: Refashioned:
"Impossible to legislate."

"It seems, Adeimantus, that the direction education gives a person at the start determines the course of their entire life. Like attracts like, doesn't it?"

"Of course."

"Until some rare and significant outcome is reached -- which may be good, or may be the opposite?"

"That can't be denied."

"And for this reason, I won't attempt to legislate further on such matters."
D: Elegant: The direction education gives a person at the start determines their entire future. Like attracts like, building momentum toward some grand result -- for good or ill. Therefore, further legislation on minor matters is unnecessary.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes that education's initial direction determines the entire course of a person's life, as like attracts like, building toward some significant result -- good or bad. This is why further legislation on manners and customs is unnecessary.
D: Distilled: The initial direction of education determines a person's entire life trajectory, making detailed legislation on conduct unnecessary.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Like attracts like' echoes Homer and is a common Greek proverb -- The idea of education as determining trajectory connects to the wheel metaphor in 4.11 -- The acknowledgment that the grand result 'may be the reverse of good' shows Plato's awareness that education can compound in negative directions too -- Cross-ref: The allegory of the cave (Book VII) as the ultimate statement about education's transformative power -- This passage justifies the Republic's focus on education over legislation

D: Refashioned:
"Naturally," he replied.

"And what about marketplace business, everyday dealings between people, contracts with tradesmen, matters of insult and injury, filing lawsuits, appointing juries? There are also questions about market taxes, harbor fees, and regulations for markets, police, and harbors generally. D: Elegant: What about marketplace regulations, contracts, lawsuits, taxes, harbor fees, and policing? Should we legislate on any of these details? Surely not.
D: Summary: Socrates lists the mundane details of civic life -- marketplace dealings, contracts, lawsuits, juries, taxes, harbor regulations -- and asks rhetorically whether they should bother legislating about any of them.
D: Distilled: Mundane civic regulations about markets, courts, and commerce should not need explicit legislation in a well-educated city.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates poses the rhetorical question to Adeimantus -- The agora (marketplace) was the commercial and social center of Greek cities -- The list covers commercial law, tort law, procedural law, and regulatory law -- Plato's dismissal of detailed legislation reflects his belief that education, not rules, produces good behavior -- Cross-ref: This contrasts with the Laws, Plato's later dialogue, which legislates precisely these kinds of details -- The exclamation 'oh heavens!' conveys Socrates' exasperation at the thought of micromanaging civic life

D: Original:
I think, he said, that there is no need to impose laws about them on good men; what regulations are necessary they will find out soon enough for themselves.

Yes, I said, my friend, if God will only preserve to them the laws which we have given them.

And without divine help, said Adeimantus, they will go on for ever making and mending their laws and their lives in the hope of attaining perfection.

You would compare them, I said, to those invalids who, having no self-restraint, will not leave off their habits of intemperance?

Exactly. Yes, I said; and what a delightful life they lead! they are always doctoring and increasing and complicating their disorders, and always fancying that they will be cured by any nostrum which anybody advises them to try.

Such cases are very common, he said, with invalids of this sort.

Yes, I replied; and the charming thing is that they deem him their worst enemy who tells them the truth, which is simply that, unless they give up eating and drinking and wenching and idling, neither drug nor cautery nor spell nor amulet nor any other remedy will avail.

Charming! he replied. I see nothing charming in going into a passion with a man who tells you what is right.

These gentlemen, I said, do not seem to be in your good graces.
D: Narrative:
"I think," he said, "that there is no need to impose laws about them on good men; what regulations are necessary they will find out soon enough for themselves."

"Yes," I said, "my friend, if God will only preserve to them the laws which we have given them."

"And without divine help," said Adeimantus, "they will go on for ever making and mending their laws and their lives in the hope of attaining perfection."

"You would compare them," I said, "to those invalids who, having no self-restraint, will not leave off their habits of intemperance?"

"Exactly."

"Yes," I said; "and what a delightful life they lead! they are always doctoring and increasing and complicating their disorders, and always fancying that they will be cured by any nostrum which anybody advises them to try."

"Such cases are very common," he said, "with invalids of this sort."

"Yes," I replied; "and the charming thing is that they deem him their worst enemy who tells them the truth, which is simply that, unless they give up eating and drinking and wenching and idling, neither drug nor cautery nor spell nor amulet nor any other remedy will avail."

"Charming!" he replied. "I see nothing charming in going into a passion with a man who tells you what is right."

"These gentlemen," I said, "do not seem to be in your good graces."
D: Refashioned:
"I don't think," he said, "there's any need to impose such laws on good people. They'll figure out whatever regulations are necessary on their own."

"Yes, my friend -- provided God preserves the fundamental laws we've given them."

"And without divine help," said Adeimantus, "they'll go on forever tinkering with their laws and their lives, always hoping to reach perfection."

"You'd compare them to those chronic invalids who lack self-restraint and refuse to give up their unhealthy habits?"

"Exactly."

"What a delightful life they lead! Always doctoring themselves, making their conditions more complicated, and forever believing the next remedy someone recommends will finally cure them."

"That's extremely common with such people."

"And here's the charming part: they consider their worst enemy the person who tells them the truth -- that unless they stop overeating, overdrinking, and overindulging, no drug, surgery, spell, or charm will do any good."

"Charming indeed! I see nothing charming about getting angry at someone who tells you what's right."

"These people don't seem to be in your good graces," I said.
D: Elegant: Good people need no detailed laws -- they'll figure things out themselves. But without sound foundations, people endlessly tinker with laws hoping for perfection, like invalids who refuse to change their habits yet constantly try new remedies. The ultimate irony: they despise whoever tells them the truth -- that no cure will work until they change their way of life.
D: Summary: Good people don't need detailed laws. But without sound foundations, citizens endlessly tinker with legislation hoping for perfection, like invalids who refuse to change their habits yet keep trying new cures. Most tellingly, they hate whoever tells them the truth: that no remedy works without changing one's way of life.
D: Distilled: Endlessly reforming laws without changing fundamental habits is like an intemperate invalid trying every remedy while refusing to change the lifestyle causing the disease.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The invalid analogy is one of Plato's favorites; medicine-as-metaphor for politics runs throughout the Republic -- Cross-ref: Book III 405a-410a on medicine and the healthy city -- 'Eating and drinking and wenching and idling' covers the four main forms of intemperance -- The idea that people hate truth-tellers anticipates the philosopher's reception in the cave allegory (Book VII) -- Cross-ref: Socrates' own fate as a truth-teller Athens rejected -- 'Cautery, spell, amulet' juxtaposes rational and irrational medicine, as Plato often does

D: Original: Assuredly not. Nor would you praise the behaviour of States which act like the men whom I was just now describing. For are there not ill-ordered States in which the citizens are forbidden under pain of death to alter the constitution; and yet he who most sweetly courts those who live under this regime and indulges them and fawns upon them and is skilful in anticipating and gratifying their humours is held to be a great and good statesman --do not these States resemble the persons whom I was describing?
D: Narrative:
"Assuredly not."

"Nor would you praise the behaviour of States which act like the men whom I was just now describing. For are there not ill-ordered States in which the citizens are forbidden under pain of death to alter the constitution; and yet he who most sweetly courts those who live under this regime and indulges them and fawns upon them and is skilful in anticipating and gratifying their humours is held to be a great and good statesman -- do not these States resemble the persons whom I was describing?"
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly not."

"Then you wouldn't praise states that behave the same way. Aren't there badly governed states where citizens are forbidden on pain of death from altering the constitution -- yet the person who most skillfully flatters those in power, indulges them, fawns over them, and anticipates their every whim is celebrated as a great statesman? Don't these states resemble the invalids I was just describing?"
D: Elegant: Badly governed states forbid constitutional change on pain of death, yet celebrate as great statesmen those who most skillfully flatter and indulge the powerful. Such states are like invalids who refuse to change their habits while trying every superficial remedy.
D: Summary: Socrates extends the invalid analogy to states: badly governed cities forbid constitutional change yet celebrate as great statesmen those who most skillfully flatter and indulge the ruling class. Such states, like chronic invalids, refuse fundamental change while rewarding those who enable their dysfunction.
D: Distilled: States that forbid constitutional reform while celebrating skilled flatterers as great statesmen are like invalids who refuse to change their harmful habits.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates poses the analogy to Adeimantus -- The description of flattering statesmen recalls the Gorgias, where Socrates distinguishes genuine statesmanship from flattery (kolakeia) -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 463a-466a on rhetoric as flattery versus true political art -- The 'pain of death' for constitutional change may reference actual Greek laws (e.g., Spartan prohibition on changing Lycurgan laws) -- This passage anticipates Book VIII's critique of democratic and tyrannical politicians -- The word 'sweetly' (hedeos) connects political flattery to the pleasure-driven life Plato opposes

D: Original:
Yes, he said; the States are as bad as the men; and I am very far from praising them.

But do you not admire, I said, the coolness and dexterity of these ready ministers of political corruption?

Yes, he said, I do; but not of all of them, for there are some whom the applause of the multitude has deluded into the belief that they are really statesmen, and these are not much to be admired.

What do you mean? I said; you should have more feeling for them. When a man cannot measure, and a great many others who cannot measure declare that he is four cubits high, can he help believing what they say?

Nay, he said, certainly not in that case. Well, then, do not be angry with them; for are they not as good as a play, trying their hand at paltry reforms such as I was describing; they are always fancying that by legislation they will make an end of frauds in contracts, and the other rascalities which I was mentioning, not knowing that they are in reality cutting off the heads of a hydra?

Yes, he said; that is just what they are doing. I conceive, I said, that the true legislator will not trouble himself with this class of enactments whether concerning laws or the constitution either in an ill-ordered or in a well-ordered State; for in the former they are quite useless, and in the latter there will be no difficulty in devising them; and many of them will naturally flow out of our previous regulations.

What, then, he said, is still remaining to us of the work of legislation?

Nothing to us, I replied; but to Apollo, the God of Delphi, there remains the ordering of the greatest and noblest and chiefest things of all.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said; "the States are as bad as the men; and I am very far from praising them."

"But do you not admire," I said, "the coolness and dexterity of these ready ministers of political corruption?"

"Yes," he said, "I do; but not of all of them, for there are some whom the applause of the multitude has deluded into the belief that they are really statesmen, and these are not much to be admired."

"What do you mean?" I said; "you should have more feeling for them. When a man cannot measure, and a great many others who cannot measure declare that he is four cubits high, can he help believing what they say?"

"Nay," he said, "certainly not in that case."

"Well, then, do not be angry with them; for are they not as good as a play, trying their hand at paltry reforms such as I was describing; they are always fancying that by legislation they will make an end of frauds in contracts, and the other rascalities which I was mentioning, not knowing that they are in reality cutting off the heads of a hydra?"

"Yes," he said; "that is just what they are doing."

"I conceive," I said, "that the true legislator will not trouble himself with this class of enactments whether concerning laws or the constitution either in an ill-ordered or in a well-ordered State; for in the former they are quite useless, and in the latter there will be no difficulty in devising them; and many of them will naturally flow out of our previous regulations."

"What, then," he said, "is still remaining to us of the work of legislation?"

"Nothing to us," I replied; "but to Apollo, the God of Delphi, there remains the ordering of the greatest and noblest and chiefest things of all."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," he said, "those states are as bad as those invalids, and I'm far from praising them."

"But don't you admire the coolness and skill of these willing agents of political corruption?"

"I do -- though not all of them. Some have been deluded by popular applause into genuinely believing they're real statesmen. Those aren't much to admire."

"You should feel sorry for them! When a man can't measure, and a crowd of equally ignorant people tell him he's four cubits tall, how can he help believing them?"

"He can't, I suppose."

"So don't be angry with them. They're really quite entertaining -- trying their hand at petty reforms, always imagining they can legislate away fraud and corruption, not realizing they're cutting off the heads of a hydra."

"That's exactly what they're doing."

"The true legislator won't bother with this kind of legislation, whether in a well-ordered or poorly ordered state. In a bad state, such laws are useless. In a good state, they're unnecessary -- most of them will flow naturally from the foundational laws we've already established."

"Then what legislative work remains for us?"

"Nothing for us. But for Apollo, the god of Delphi, the greatest, noblest, and most important matters of all remain."
D: Elegant: Politicians deluded by popular applause into thinking they're statesmen try endlessly to legislate away corruption -- cutting off the heads of a hydra. The true legislator avoids such pettiness: in a bad state, detailed laws are useless; in a good state, they flow naturally from sound foundations. Only the greatest matters -- religion and the sacred -- remain, and these belong to Apollo at Delphi.
D: Summary: Politicians deluded by popular applause attempt endless piecemeal reforms, not realizing they're fighting a hydra. The true legislator ignores such details: in bad states they're useless, in good states they're unnecessary. The only remaining legislative task -- ordering religion and sacred rites -- belongs not to human legislators but to Apollo at Delphi.
D: Distilled: Piecemeal legal reforms are like cutting off a hydra's heads; the true legislator relies on foundational education and defers sacred matters to the divine authority of Delphi.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The hydra reference is to the Lernaean Hydra slain by Heracles, whose heads regrew when cut -- 'Four cubits high' (about six feet) -- the joke is that collective ignorance reinforces individual delusion -- Apollo at Delphi (the Oracle) was the supreme religious authority in Greece -- Cross-ref: The Apology, where Socrates' philosophical mission begins with the Delphic oracle -- Deferring to Delphi on religious matters shows Plato's caution about legislating religion -- Cross-ref: Laws, Book V, where Plato similarly defers religious legislation to Delphi -- The distinction between true and apparent statesmen parallels the true vs. apparent guardians of 4.4

These are matters of which we are ignorant ourselves, and as founders of a city we should be unwise in trusting them to any interpreter but our ancestral deity. D: Narrative:
"Which are they?" he said.

"The institution of temples and sacrifices, and the entire service of gods, demigods, and heroes; also the ordering of the repositories of the dead, and the rites which have to be observed by him who would propitiate the inhabitants of the world below. These are matters of which we are ignorant ourselves, and as founders of a city we should be unwise in trusting them to any interpreter but our ancestral deity. He is the god who sits in the centre, on the navel of the earth, and he is the interpreter of religion to all mankind."
D: Refashioned:
"What matters are those?" he asked.

"The establishment of temples and sacrifices, and the entire worship of gods, demigods, and heroes. Also the proper treatment of the dead, and the rites required to honor those in the underworld. These are matters we know nothing about ourselves, and as founders of a city, we'd be foolish to entrust them to any authority other than our ancestral god. Apollo sits at the center, on the navel of the earth, and serves as the interpreter of religion for all humanity."
D: Elegant: The greatest matters of legislation -- temples, sacrifices, worship of gods and heroes, funeral rites, and the honoring of the dead -- belong to Apollo at Delphi. As founders, we are ignorant of such things and must defer to the god who sits at the earth's navel, the interpreter of religion for all mankind.
D: Summary: The greatest legislative matters -- establishing temples, sacrifices, divine worship, funerary rites, and honoring the dead -- must be left to Apollo at Delphi, the ancestral god who sits at the earth's navel and interprets religion for all humanity. Human founders are too ignorant to legislate on sacred matters.
D: Distilled: Sacred matters -- temples, sacrifices, worship, and rites for the dead -- must be left to Apollo at Delphi, the divine interpreter of religion.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates responds to Adeimantus -- The 'navel of the earth' (omphalos) was a sacred stone at Delphi believed to mark the earth's center -- Apollo was considered the 'ancestral deity' (patrios theos) of the Ionians, including Athenians -- 'Demigods and heroes' (daimones and heroi) were important categories in Greek religion -- 'Inhabitants of the world below' refers to the dead in Hades -- Cross-ref: Book III 386a-387c on proper representation of the afterlife -- Cross-ref: Phaedo on the soul's fate after death -- This passage shows Plato's conservative stance on traditional religion, even within his revolutionary political theory

D: Original: You are right, and we will do as you propose. But where, amid all this, is justice? son of Ariston, tell me where. Now that our city has been made habitable, light a candle and search, and get your brother and Polemarchus and the rest of our friends to help, and let us see where in it we can discover justice and where injustice, and in what they differ from one another, and which of them the man who would be happy should have for his portion, whether seen or unseen by gods and men.
D: Narrative:
"You are right, and we will do as you propose."

"But where, amid all this, is justice? Son of Ariston, tell me where. Now that our city has been made habitable, light a candle and search, and get your brother and Polemarchus and the rest of our friends to help, and let us see where in it we can discover justice and where injustice, and in what they differ from one another, and which of them the man who would be happy should have for his portion, whether seen or unseen by gods and men."
D: Refashioned:
"You're right, and we'll follow your proposal."

"But where in all of this is justice? Son of Ariston, tell me! Now that our city has been made livable, light a torch and search. Get your brother and Polemarchus and the rest of our friends to help. Let's see where in this city we can find justice and where injustice lies, how they differ from each other, and which of them the person who wants to be happy should choose -- whether or not gods and men are watching."
D: Elegant: Now that the city is built, the hunt for justice begins. Socrates calls on Glaucon (son of Ariston), his brother, Polemarchus, and the others to search the city and find where justice and injustice reside, how they differ, and which leads to happiness -- whether observed by gods and men or not.
D: Summary: With the city now constructed, Socrates pivots to the central question: where is justice? He calls on Glaucon (son of Ariston), Adeimantus, Polemarchus, and the others to search the city for justice and injustice, to understand how they differ, and to determine which leads to happiness regardless of whether gods and men observe it.
D: Distilled: With the city built, Socrates turns to the central quest: finding where justice and injustice reside and which leads to true happiness.
Notes: Speakers: Adeimantus agrees, then Socrates addresses Glaucon directly as 'Son of Ariston' -- Ariston was the father of both Glaucon and Adeimantus (and Plato himself) -- Polemarchus was the host of the gathering in Book I and gave the initial definition of justice -- 'Whether seen or unseen by gods and men' directly references Glaucon's challenge in Book II (360b-362c), where he asked whether justice is good in itself or only when observed -- The candle/torch metaphor for philosophical inquiry is characteristic of Plato -- This passage marks the major transition from city-building to the analysis of the four virtues -- Cross-ref: Book I's initial definitions of justice by Cephalus, Polemarchus, and Thrasymachus

D: Original:
Nonsense, said Glaucon: did you not promise to search yourself, saying that for you not to help justice in her need would be an impiety?

I do not deny that I said so, and as you remind me, I will be as good as my word; but you must join.

We will, he replied. Well, then, I hope to make the discovery in this way: I mean to begin with the assumption that our State, if rightly ordered, is perfect.

That is most certain. And being perfect, is therefore wise and valiant and temperate and just.

That is likewise clear. And whichever of these qualities we find in the State, the one which is not found will be the residue?

Very good. If there were four things, and we were searching for one of them, wherever it might be, the one sought for might be known to us from the first, and there would be no further trouble; or we might know the other three first, and then the fourth would clearly be the one left.

Very true, he said. And is not a similar method to be pursued about the virtues, which are also four in number?

Clearly. First among the virtues found in the State, wisdom comes into view, and in this I detect a certain peculiarity.

What is that? The State which we have been describing is said to be wise as being good in counsel?

Very true. And good counsel is clearly a kind of knowledge, for not by ignorance, but by knowledge, do men counsel well?

Clearly. And the kinds of knowledge in a State are many and diverse?

Of course. There is the knowledge of the carpenter; but is that the sort of knowledge which gives a city the title of wise and good in counsel?

Certainly not; that would only give a city the reputation of skill in carpentering.

Then a city is not to be called wise because possessing a knowledge which counsels for the best about wooden implements?

Certainly not. Nor by reason of a knowledge which advises about brazen pots, I said, nor as possessing any other similar knowledge?

"If there were four things, and we were searching for one of them, wherever it might be, the one sought for might be known to us from the first, and there would be no further trouble; or we might know the other three first, and then the fourth would clearly be the one left."

"Very true," he said.

"And is not a similar method to be pursued about the virtues, which are also four in number?"

"Clearly."

"First among the virtues found in the State, wisdom comes into view, and in this I detect a certain peculiarity."

"What is that?"

"The State which we have been describing is said to be wise as being good in counsel?"

"Very true."

"And good counsel is clearly a kind of knowledge, for not by ignorance, but by knowledge, do men counsel well?"

"Clearly."

"And the kinds of knowledge in a State are many and diverse?"

"Of course."

"There is the knowledge of the carpenter; but is that the sort of knowledge which gives a city the title of wise and good in counsel?"

"Certainly not; that would only give a city the reputation of skill in carpentering."

"Then a city is not to be called wise because possessing a knowledge which counsels for the best about wooden implements?"

"Certainly not."

"Nor by reason of a knowledge which advises about brazen pots," I said, "nor as possessing any other similar knowledge?"
D: Refashioned:
"Nonsense," said Glaucon. "Didn't you promise to search for justice yourself? You said it would be impious not to help justice in her hour of need."

"I don't deny saying that, and since you remind me, I'll keep my word. But you must join the search."

"We will."

"Good. Here's my approach: I'll begin with the assumption that our city, if rightly ordered, is perfect."

"That's certain."

"And being perfect, it must be wise, courageous, temperate, and just."

"Clearly."

"So whichever of these four virtues we identify, the ones remaining will be whatever we haven't found yet."

"Agreed."

"If we're searching for one thing among four, we might recognize it immediately. Or we might identify the other three first, and the fourth would be whatever is left."

"True."

"We can use the same method with the virtues, which are also four."

"Clearly."

"Wisdom comes into view first, and I notice something peculiar about it."

"What's that?"

"Our city is called wise because it possesses good counsel, correct?"

"Yes."

"And good counsel is a form of knowledge -- people counsel well through knowledge, not ignorance."

"Obviously."

"But there are many kinds of knowledge in a city."

"Of course."

"Take carpentry -- does that kind of knowledge make a city wise and good in counsel?"

"No, it would only make the city skilled in carpentry."

"So a city isn't called wise because it has knowledge about the best use of wooden implements?"

"Certainly not."

"Or knowledge about bronze pots, or any other specialized craft?"
D: Elegant: The method: our perfect city must be wise, courageous, temperate, and just. Find three virtues, and the fourth is whatever remains. Wisdom comes first. The city is wise through good counsel, which requires knowledge -- but not the knowledge of any particular craft like carpentry or metalwork. Those make a city skilled, not wise.
D: Summary: Socrates proposes a method of elimination: the perfect city must possess four virtues -- wisdom, courage, temperance, and justice. Find three, and the fourth is the remainder. Beginning with wisdom, he argues that the city's wisdom lies not in any specialized craft knowledge (carpentry, metalwork) but in something else entirely.
D: Distilled: The city's wisdom is not the specialized knowledge of any craft; it must be a higher form of knowledge -- good counsel about the city as a whole.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon take over the dialogue from Adeimantus -- The four cardinal virtues (wisdom, courage, temperance, justice) are a Platonic formulation that became foundational in Western ethics -- The method of elimination (finding justice as the residue after identifying the other three) is a distinctive logical strategy -- The distinction between craft-knowledge (techne) and political wisdom (sophia) is central to Plato's epistemology -- Cross-ref: Book I where Thrasymachus compares justice to a craft -- Cross-ref: The Protagoras on whether virtue is one or many -- 'Good counsel' (euboulia) is later important in Aristotle's Ethics

D: Original:
Not by reason of any of them, he said. Nor yet by reason of a knowledge which cultivates the earth; that would give the city the name of agricultural?

Yes. Well, I said, and is there any knowledge in our recently founded State among any of the citizens which advises, not about any particular thing in the State, but about the whole, and considers how a State can best deal with itself and with other States?

There certainly is. And what is knowledge, and among whom is it found? I asked.

It is the knowledge of the guardians, he replied, and found among those whom we were just now describing as perfect guardians.

And what is the name which the city derives from the possession of this sort of knowledge?

The name of good in counsel and truly wise. And will there be in our city more of these true guardians or more smiths?

The smiths, he replied, will be far more numerous. Will not the guardians be the smallest of all the classes who receive a name from the profession of some kind of knowledge?

Much the smallest. And so by reason of the smallest part or class, and of the knowledge which resides in this presiding and ruling part of itself, the whole State, being thus constituted according to nature, will be wise; and this, which has the only knowledge worthy to be called wisdom, has been ordained by nature to be of all classes the least.

Most true. Thus, then, I said, the nature and place in the State of one of the four virtues has somehow or other been discovered.

And, in my humble opinion, very satisfactorily discovered, he replied.

Again, I said, there is no difficulty in seeing the nature of courage; and in what part that quality resides which gives the name of courageous to the State.

How do you mean? Why, I said, every one who calls any State courageous or cowardly, will be thinking of the part which fights and goes out to war on the State's behalf.

No one, he replied, would ever think of any other. Certainly not. The rest of the citizens may be courageous or may be cowardly but their courage or cowardice will not, as I conceive, have the effect of making the city either the one or the other.

"Most true."

"And, in my humble opinion, very satisfactorily discovered," he replied.

"No one," he replied, "would ever think of any other."

D: Refashioned:
"No, none of those," he said.

"Nor knowledge of farming -- that would only make the city agricultural."

"Right."

"Then is there any knowledge in our newly founded city that advises not about any particular matter, but about the city as a whole -- how it can best manage its internal affairs and its relations with other cities?"

"There certainly is."

"What is this knowledge, and who possesses it?"

"It's the knowledge of the guardians -- those we described as the perfect guardians."

"And what does this knowledge make the city?"

"Good in counsel and truly wise."

"Are there more of these true guardians in our city, or more smiths?"

"Far more smiths."

"Are the guardians the smallest class defined by any kind of knowledge?"

"By far the smallest."

"Exactly right."

"So we've discovered the nature and location of the first of our four virtues."

"And quite satisfactorily, in my opinion."

"Next, courage. It's not hard to see where this virtue resides."

"How so?"

D: Elegant: The city's wisdom resides not in any specialized craft but in the knowledge of the guardians -- knowledge of the city as a whole, how it should manage itself and its relations. This ruling knowledge, found in the smallest class, makes the whole city wise. Similarly, the city's courage belongs to the warrior class -- the part that fights on its behalf. Individual citizens' bravery doesn't determine the city's character.
D: Summary: The city's wisdom comes from the guardians' unique knowledge -- not about any craft, but about the city as a whole and its dealings with other cities. Though the guardians are the smallest class, their knowledge makes the entire city wise. Moving to courage, this virtue resides in the warrior class: the city is called courageous based on its soldiers, not the personal bravery of other citizens.
D: Distilled: Wisdom belongs to the smallest ruling class who know the city as a whole; courage belongs to the warrior class who fight on its behalf.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The distinction between knowledge of parts and knowledge of the whole is fundamental to Platonic philosophy -- The smallest class possessing the highest knowledge mirrors the soul's structure (reason is smallest but should rule) -- Cross-ref: The philosopher-king argument in Books V-VII develops this idea that the wisest, not the most numerous, should rule -- The transition from wisdom to courage follows the hierarchical structure: guardians (wisdom), auxiliaries (courage) -- Cross-ref: This maps onto the tripartite soul: reason (wisdom), spirit (courage), appetite (temperance) -- The civic courage of the warrior class anticipates the distinction from individual courage in 4.23-4.26

D: Original:
The city will be courageous in virtue of a portion of herself which preserves under all circumstances that opinion about the nature of things to be feared and not to be feared in which our legislator educated them; and this is what you term courage.

I should like to hear what you are saying once more, for I do not think that I perfectly understand you.

I mean that courage is a kind of salvation. Salvation of what? Of the opinion respecting things to be feared, what they are and of what nature, which the law implants through education; and I mean by the words 'under all circumstances' to intimate that in pleasure or in pain, or under the influence of desire or fear, a man preserves, and does not lose this opinion. Shall I give you an illustration?
D: Narrative:
"The city will be courageous in virtue of a portion of herself which preserves under all circumstances that opinion about the nature of things to be feared and not to be feared in which our legislator educated them; and this is what you term courage."

"I should like to hear what you are saying once more, for I do not think that I perfectly understand you."

"I mean that courage is a kind of salvation."

"Salvation of what?"

"Of the opinion respecting things to be feared, what they are and of what nature, which the law implants through education; and I mean by the words 'under all circumstances' to intimate that in pleasure or in pain, or under the influence of desire or fear, a man preserves, and does not lose this opinion. Shall I give you an illustration?"
D: Refashioned:
"The city is courageous because one part of it preserves, under all circumstances, the correct opinion about what should and shouldn't be feared -- the opinion instilled by the lawgiver through education. That is what I mean by courage."

"I'd like to hear that again. I don't think I fully understand."

"Courage is a kind of preservation."

"Preservation of what?"

"Of the opinion about what is truly to be feared and what is not -- the opinion that law implants through education. When I say 'under all circumstances,' I mean that a person holds onto this opinion whether experiencing pleasure or pain, whether gripped by desire or fear. Would an illustration help?"
D: Elegant: Courage is the preservation of the law-educated opinion about what truly should and should not be feared -- maintained under all circumstances, whether in pleasure, pain, desire, or fear.
D: Summary: Socrates defines civic courage as the preservation of correct opinion about what should and should not be feared -- an opinion implanted by law through education -- maintained under all circumstances, whether in pleasure, pain, desire, or fear.
D: Distilled: Courage is the steadfast preservation of educated opinion about what is truly to be feared, regardless of pleasure, pain, desire, or fear.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates defines courage, Glaucon asks for clarification -- The definition of courage as 'preservation of opinion' (soteria tes doxes) is distinctive to the Republic -- This is civic, not philosophical, courage -- it's right opinion, not knowledge -- Cross-ref: The Laches, Plato's dialogue dedicated entirely to defining courage -- Cross-ref: Protagoras 349d-360e where courage is discussed as knowledge of what is and isn't to be feared -- The four threats to courage (pleasure, pain, desire, fear) correspond to the four solvents in the dye analogy that follows -- Glaucon's request for clarification is genuine, not Socratic irony

D: Original:
If you please. You know, I said, that dyers, when they want to dye wool for making the true sea-purple, begin by selecting their white colour first; this they prepare and dress with much care and pains, in order that the white ground may take the purple hue in full perfection. The dyeing then proceeds; and whatever is dyed in this manner becomes a fast colour, and no washing either with lyes or without them can take away the bloom. But, when the ground has not been duly prepared, you will have noticed how poor is the look either of purple or of any other colour.

Yes, he said; I know that they have a washed-out and ridiculous appearance.
D: Narrative:
"If you please."

"You know," I said, "that dyers, when they want to dye wool for making the true sea-purple, begin by selecting their white colour first; this they prepare and dress with much care and pains, in order that the white ground may take the purple hue in full perfection. The dyeing then proceeds; and whatever is dyed in this manner becomes a fast colour, and no washing either with lyes or without them can take away the bloom. But, when the ground has not been duly prepared, you will have noticed how poor is the look either of purple or of any other colour."

"Yes," he said; "I know that they have a washed-out and ridiculous appearance."
D: Refashioned:
"Please do."

"You know how dyers work when they want to produce true sea-purple wool. First, they carefully select white wool. Then they prepare and treat it with great care so the white ground will absorb the purple dye perfectly. Once dyed this way, the color becomes permanent -- no amount of washing, with lye or without, can fade the bloom. But when the wool hasn't been properly prepared, the result, whether purple or any other color, looks washed-out and ridiculous."

"Yes, I've noticed that."
D: Elegant: To produce true sea-purple, dyers first carefully prepare white wool so it absorbs the dye perfectly, producing a color that no washing can fade. Without proper preparation, the result is washed-out and ridiculous.
D: Summary: Socrates uses the analogy of dyeing wool: to achieve lasting sea-purple, dyers must first carefully prepare white wool so it absorbs the dye permanently. Without proper preparation, the color washes out. This parallels how education must prepare the soul to hold courage fast.
D: Distilled: Just as proper preparation of wool makes dye permanent, proper education makes courageous opinion permanent in the soul.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates illustrates, Glaucon confirms -- Sea-purple (Tyrian purple) was the most expensive dye in the ancient world, produced from murex sea snails -- The analogy maps precisely: white wool = the right natural character, careful preparation = education in music and gymnastic, the dye = correct opinion about what to fear, washing/lyes = pleasure, pain, desire, and fear -- Cross-ref: Book III's emphasis on selecting the right natures for guardian education -- The 'washed-out and ridiculous appearance' describes what happens when untrained people try to be courageous -- This is one of Plato's most vivid craft analogies

D: Original: Then now, I said, you will understand what our object was in selecting our soldiers, and educating them in music and gymnastic; we were contriving influences which would prepare them to take the dye of the laws in perfection, and the colour of their opinion about dangers and of every other opinion was to be indelibly fixed by their nurture and training, not to be washed away by such potent lyes as pleasure --mightier agent far in washing the soul than any soda or lye; or by sorrow, fear, and desire, the mightiest of all other solvents. And this sort of universal saving power of true opinion in conformity with law about real and false dangers I call and maintain to be courage, unless you disagree.
D: Narrative: "Then now," I said, "you will understand what our object was in selecting our soldiers, and educating them in music and gymnastic; we were contriving influences which would prepare them to take the dye of the laws in perfection, and the colour of their opinion about dangers and of every other opinion was to be indelibly fixed by their nurture and training, not to be washed away by such potent lyes as pleasure -- mightier agent far in washing the soul than any soda or lye; or by sorrow, fear, and desire, the mightiest of all other solvents. And this sort of universal saving power of true opinion in conformity with law about real and false dangers I call and maintain to be courage, unless you disagree."
D: Refashioned: "Now you can understand our purpose in selecting soldiers and educating them in music and gymnastic. We were creating the conditions for them to absorb the dye of the laws perfectly. Their opinions about danger -- and every other important matter -- were to be indelibly fixed by their upbringing and training, impossible to wash away. Not by pleasure, which is a far more powerful solvent of the soul than any lye. Not by sorrow, fear, or desire -- the mightiest solvents of all. This universal power to preserve true, law-conforming opinion about real and false dangers is what I call courage -- unless you disagree."
D: Elegant: Education in music and gymnastic prepares the soul to absorb the dye of law so deeply that no solvent can wash it away -- not pleasure (the most potent), nor sorrow, fear, or desire. Courage is the power to preserve true opinion about what is and isn't to be feared, in conformity with law.
D: Summary: Socrates completes the dye analogy: education in music and gymnastic prepares soldiers to absorb law's teachings so deeply they become permanent. Neither pleasure (the most powerful solvent of the soul), nor sorrow, fear, nor desire can wash away their trained opinions about danger. This preservation of law-educated opinion about real and false dangers is courage.
D: Distilled: Courage is the power to preserve law-educated opinion about danger against all solvents -- pleasure, sorrow, fear, and desire.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates delivers the formal definition of courage -- Pleasure is identified as the most potent threat to courage, more dangerous than fear itself -- a counterintuitive insight -- The four solvents (pleasure, sorrow, fear, desire) map onto the four threats mentioned in 4.23 -- 'Universal saving power' (soteria dia pantos) emphasizes that courage must hold under all conditions -- This definition is explicitly civic courage (conformity with law), not philosophical courage -- Cross-ref: Laches 194e-199e where Nicias defines courage as knowledge of what is to be feared -- Cross-ref: Book III 412e-413c on testing guardians with pleasures and pains

D: Original:
But I agree, he replied; for I suppose that you mean to exclude mere uninstructed courage, such as that of a wild beast or of a slave --this, in your opinion, is not the courage which the law ordains, and ought to have another name.

Most certainly. Then I may infer courage to be such as you describe? Why, yes, said I, you may, and if you add the words 'of a citizen,' you will not be far wrong; --hereafter, if you like, we will carry the examination further, but at present we are we w seeking not for courage but justice; and for the purpose of our enquiry we have said enough.

You are right, he replied. Two virtues remain to be discovered in the State-first temperance, and then justice which is the end of our search.

Very true. Now, can we find justice without troubling ourselves about temperance?

I do not know how that can be accomplished, he said, nor do I desire that justice should be brought to light and temperance lost sight of; and therefore I wish that you would do me the favour of considering temperance first.

Certainly, I replied, I should not be justified in refusing your request.

Then consider, he said. Yes, I replied; I will; and as far as I can at present see, the virtue of temperance has more of the nature of harmony and symphony than the preceding.

How so? he asked. Temperance, I replied, is the ordering or controlling of certain pleasures and desires; this is curiously enough implied in the saying of 'a man being his own master' and other traces of the same notion may be found in language.

No doubt, he said. There is something ridiculous in the expression 'master of himself'; for the master is also the servant and the servant the master; and in all these modes of speaking the same person is denoted.
D: Narrative:
"But I agree," he replied; "for I suppose that you mean to exclude mere uninstructed courage, such as that of a wild beast or of a slave -- this, in your opinion, is not the courage which the law ordains, and ought to have another name."

"Most certainly."

"Then I may infer courage to be such as you describe?"

"Why, yes," said I, "you may, and if you add the words 'of a citizen,' you will not be far wrong; -- hereafter, if you like, we will carry the examination further, but at present we are seeking not for courage but justice; and for the purpose of our enquiry we have said enough."

"You are right," he replied.

"Two virtues remain to be discovered in the State -- first temperance, and then justice which is the end of our search."

"Very true."

"Now, can we find justice without troubling ourselves about temperance?"

"I do not know how that can be accomplished," he said, "nor do I desire that justice should be brought to light and temperance lost sight of; and therefore I wish that you would do me the favour of considering temperance first."

"Certainly," I replied, "I should not be justified in refusing your request."

"Then consider," he said.

"Yes," I replied; "I will; and as far as I can at present see, the virtue of temperance has more of the nature of harmony and symphony than the preceding."

"How so?" he asked.

"Temperance," I replied, "is the ordering or controlling of certain pleasures and desires; this is curiously enough implied in the saying of 'a man being his own master'; and other traces of the same notion may be found in language."

"No doubt," he said.

"There is something ridiculous in the expression 'master of himself'; for the master is also the servant and the servant the master; and in all these modes of speaking the same person is denoted."
D: Refashioned:
"I agree," he replied, "because I assume you're excluding mere untrained courage -- the kind shown by wild animals or slaves. That isn't the courage ordained by law and deserves a different name."

"Exactly."

"So courage is what you've described?"

"Yes -- and if you add 'of a citizen,' you won't be far off. We could examine this further later, but right now we're searching for justice, and we've said enough about courage."

"Fair enough."

"Two virtues remain: first temperance, then justice, which is our ultimate goal."

"True."

"Can we find justice without examining temperance?"

"I don't see how," he said, "and I wouldn't want justice to come to light while temperance is ignored. Please consider temperance first."

"I'd be wrong to refuse. Let me think... As far as I can see, temperance has more the nature of harmony and symphony than the virtues we've examined so far."

"How so?"

"Temperance is the ordering or controlling of certain pleasures and desires. This is curiously implied in the expression 'being master of oneself.' You can find similar hints in language."

"No doubt."

"But there's something absurd about 'master of oneself' -- the master is also the servant, and the servant is the master. The same person is both."
D: Elegant: Mere animal fearlessness or slave endurance is not true courage -- courage is specifically a citizen's educated preservation of right opinion. Moving to temperance: it resembles harmony more than the other virtues. It is the ordering of pleasures and desires, implied in the paradoxical expression 'master of oneself' -- where the same person is both master and servant.
D: Summary: Glaucon notes that uninstructed courage (of beasts or slaves) is excluded from this definition -- true courage is a citizen's virtue. Two virtues remain: temperance and justice. Temperance, unlike the previous virtues, has the nature of harmony. It means ordering pleasures and desires, as hinted by the paradoxical expression 'master of oneself,' where the same person is both ruler and ruled.
D: Distilled: True courage is educated civic virtue, not animal fearlessness; temperance is the harmonious ordering of pleasures and desires, where one part of the self masters the other.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The distinction between civic courage and animal/slave courage is important: Plato values educated virtue, not mere toughness -- Cross-ref: Laches where different kinds of courage are distinguished -- The pivot to temperance (sophrosyne) introduces one of the most important Greek ethical concepts -- 'Master of oneself' (kreitto hautou) was a common Greek expression -- The paradox of self-mastery implies that the self has parts -- this is the first hint of the tripartite soul theory that will follow -- Cross-ref: Charmides, Plato's dialogue on temperance -- Temperance as 'harmony' differentiates it from wisdom and courage, which each reside in one part

D: Original: Certainly. The meaning is, I believe, that in the human soul there is a better and also a worse principle; and when the better has the worse under control, then a man is said to be master of himself; and this is a term of praise: but when, owing to evil education or association, the better principle, which is also the smaller, is overwhelmed by the greater mass of the worse --in this case he is blamed and is called the slave of self and unprincipled.
D: Narrative:
"Certainly."

"The meaning is, I believe, that in the human soul there is a better and also a worse principle; and when the better has the worse under control, then a man is said to be master of himself; and this is a term of praise: but when, owing to evil education or association, the better principle, which is also the smaller, is overwhelmed by the greater mass of the worse -- in this case he is blamed and is called the slave of self and unprincipled."
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly."

"Here's what I think the expression means: in the human soul, there's a better part and a worse part. When the better part controls the worse, a person is called 'master of himself' -- a term of praise. But when bad education or bad company allows the worse part, which is also the larger, to overwhelm the better part, which is smaller -- then the person is blamed and called 'a slave to himself' and 'unprincipled.'"
D: Elegant: The soul has a better and a worse principle. When the better controls the worse, a person is praised as 'master of himself.' When bad education lets the worse (larger) part overwhelm the better (smaller) part, the person is called a slave to self and unprincipled.
D: Summary: The expression 'master of oneself' means that the better part of the soul controls the worse. When bad education or association lets the worse part -- which is larger -- overwhelm the smaller better part, a person becomes a slave to their own appetites.
D: Distilled: Self-mastery means the soul's better but smaller principle controls its worse but larger one; bad education reverses this natural order.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates explains to Glaucon -- This is a crucial passage establishing that the soul has distinct parts with a natural hierarchy -- The 'better principle' is reason, the 'worse' is appetite -- The observation that the better is also the smaller anticipates the tripartite soul: reason is the smallest part, appetite the largest -- Cross-ref: 4.22 where wisdom resides in the smallest class -- Evil education and association as causes of inversion connect back to 4.11-4.14 on education's paramount importance -- 'Slave of self' inverts the praise of self-mastery and anticipates Book IX's portrait of the tyrant as the ultimate slave to appetite

Yes, there is reason in that. And now, I said, look at our newly created State, and there you will find one of these two conditions realised; for the State, as you will acknowledge, may be justly called master of itself, if the words 'temperance' and 'self-mastery' truly express the rule of the better part over the worse.

Yes, he said, I see that what you say is true. Let me further note that the manifold and complex pleasures and desires and pains are generally found in children and women and servants, and in the freemen so called who are of the lowest and more numerous class.

Certainly, he said. Whereas the simple and moderate desires which follow reason, and are under the guidance of mind and true opinion, are to be found only in a few, and those the best born and best educated.

Very true. These two, as you may perceive, have a place in our State; and the meaner desires of the are held down by the virtuous desires and wisdom of the few.

That I perceive, he said. Then if there be any city which may be described as master of its own pleasures and desires, and master of itself, ours may claim such a designation?

Certainly, he replied. It may also be called temperate, and for the same reasons?

Yes. And if there be any State in which rulers and subjects will be agreed as to the question who are to rule, that again will be our State?

Undoubtedly. And the citizens being thus agreed among themselves, in which class will temperance be found --in the rulers or in the subjects?

In both, as I should imagine, he replied. Do you observe that we were not far wrong in our guess that temperance was a sort of harmony?
