"I agree with you."

"We will not," I said, "be over-positive as yet; but if, on trial, this conception of justice be verified in the individual as well as in the State, there will be no longer any room for doubt; if it be not verified, we must have a fresh enquiry. First let us complete the old investigation, which we began, as you remember, under the impression that, if we could previously examine justice on the larger scale, there would be less difficulty in discerning her in the individual. That larger example appeared to be the State, and accordingly we constructed as good a one as we could, knowing well that in the good State justice would be found. Let the discovery which we made be now applied to the individual -- if they agree, we shall be satisfied; or, if there be a difference in the individual, we will come back to the State and have another trial of the theory. The friction of the two when rubbed together may possibly strike a light in which justice will shine forth, and the vision which is then revealed we will fix in our souls."
D: Refashioned:
"I agree."

"Let's not be overconfident yet. If this conception of justice holds true for the individual as well as the city, there will be no room for doubt. If it doesn't, we'll need a fresh inquiry. Remember our original plan: we decided to examine justice on the larger scale first -- the city -- because it would be easier to see. So we built the best city we could, confident justice would be found in it. Now let's apply what we've discovered to the individual. If city and individual match, we're satisfied. If not, we'll return to the city and test the theory again. By rubbing the two together, we may strike a spark that reveals justice clearly, and that vision we'll fix permanently in our souls."
D: Elegant: The conception of justice must now be tested in the individual. If city and individual agree, the theory is confirmed. If not, rubbing the two together like flint may strike a spark that illuminates justice clearly enough to fix the vision permanently in their souls.
D: Summary: Socrates cautions against premature certainty. The definition of justice found in the city must now be tested in the individual. If city and soul agree, the theory is confirmed; if not, they'll try again. By comparing the two -- like rubbing flint -- they may strike a spark that illuminates justice clearly enough to fix it permanently in their souls.
D: Distilled: The definition of justice in the city must be verified in the individual soul; comparing the two may strike the spark of true understanding.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The 'larger letters' analogy from Book II (368d) is recalled here: they examined justice in the city first because it would be easier to see at a larger scale -- The fire-striking metaphor (friction producing light) is a powerful image for dialectical discovery -- Cross-ref: Seventh Letter 344b where Plato describes knowledge flashing forth like fire from rubbed sticks -- 'Fix in our souls' suggests the vision of justice becomes permanent knowledge -- This passage marks the major transition from political to psychological analysis -- The method of comparing city and soul is the Republic's distinctive philosophical strategy

D: Original:
That will be in regular course; let us do as you say. I proceeded to ask: When two things, a greater and less, are called by the same name, are they like or unlike in so far as they are called the same?

Like, he replied. The just man then, if we regard the idea of justice only, will be like the just State?

He will. And a State was thought by us to be just when the three classes in the State severally did their own business; and also thought to be temperate and valiant and wise by reason of certain other affections and qualities of these same classes?

True, he said. And so of the individual; we may assume that he has the same three principles in his own soul which are found in the State; and he may be rightly described in the same terms, because he is affected in the same manner?

Certainly, he said. Once more then, O my friend, we have alighted upon an easy question --whether the soul has these three principles or not?

An easy question! Nay, rather, Socrates, the proverb holds that hard is the good.

Very true, I said; and I do not think that the method which we are employing is at all adequate to the accurate solution of this question; the true method is another and a longer one. Still we may arrive at a solution not below the level of the previous enquiry.

May we not be satisfied with that? he said; --under the circumstances, I am quite content.
D: Narrative:
"That will be in regular course; let us do as you say."

"I proceeded to ask: When two things, a greater and less, are called by the same name, are they like or unlike in so far as they are called the same?"

"Like," he replied.

"The just man then, if we regard the idea of justice only, will be like the just State?"

"He will."

"And a State was thought by us to be just when the three classes in the State severally did their own business; and also thought to be temperate and valiant and wise by reason of certain other affections and qualities of these same classes?"

"True," he said.

"And so of the individual; we may assume that he has the same three principles in his own soul which are found in the State; and he may be rightly described in the same terms, because he is affected in the same manner?"

"Certainly," he said.

"Once more then, O my friend, we have alighted upon an easy question -- whether the soul has these three principles or not?"

"An easy question! Nay, rather, Socrates, the proverb holds that hard is the good."

"Very true," I said; "and I do not think that the method which we are employing is at all adequate to the accurate solution of this question; the true method is another and a longer one. Still we may arrive at a solution not below the level of the previous enquiry."

"May we not be satisfied with that?" he said; "-- under the circumstances, I am quite content."
D: Refashioned:
"Let's proceed as you suggest."

"When two things, one larger and one smaller, share the same name, are they alike insofar as they share that name?"

"They are."

"Then the just person, considered purely in terms of justice, will resemble the just city."

"Yes."

"We said the city is just when its three classes each do their own work, and wise, courageous, and temperate because of certain qualities in those same classes."

"True."

"So the individual must have the same three principles in their soul as exist in the city, and can be described in the same terms for the same reasons."

"Certainly."

"So here's another easy question: does the soul actually have these three principles?"

"Easy? Hardly, Socrates! The proverb says 'the good is hard.'"

"You're right. And I'll admit that the method we're using isn't really adequate for a precise answer to this question -- the true method would be longer and more rigorous. But we can reach an answer that matches the level of our previous inquiry."

"That's good enough for me, given the circumstances."
D: Elegant: If the just person resembles the just city, the soul must contain the same three principles found in the three classes of the city. But proving the soul has three parts is not easy -- 'the good is hard.' The method here is not fully adequate; a longer, more rigorous approach would be needed for precision. Still, a satisfactory answer is within reach.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes the key analogy: the just person must resemble the just city, so the soul must contain three principles matching the city's three classes. But proving this is harder than it seems. Socrates acknowledges their method is inadequate for full precision -- a longer approach would be needed -- but they can reach a satisfactory answer.
D: Distilled: The soul must have three principles matching the city's three classes, but proving this requires more rigorous methods than they will employ here.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Hard is the good' (chalepa ta kala) is a well-known Greek proverb -- Socrates' admission that 'the true method is another and a longer one' is significant: he's acknowledging that the tripartite soul argument here is preliminary -- Cross-ref: The Phaedrus (246a-249d) and Timaeus (69c-72d) give more elaborate accounts of the soul's parts -- The 'larger and smaller sharing the same name' argument relies on the assumption that city and soul are structurally analogous -- This assumption is fundamental to the Republic but is philosophically debatable -- Cross-ref: Aristotle (Politics I.2) questions whether the analogy between city and soul is valid

D: Original: I too, I replied, shall be extremely well satisfied. Then faint not in pursuing the speculation, he said. Must we not acknowledge, I said, that in each of us there are the same principles and habits which there are in the State; and that from the individual they pass into the State? --how else can they come there? Take the quality of passion or spirit; --it would be ridiculous to imagine that this quality, when found in States, is not derived from the individuals who are supposed to possess it, e.g. the Thracians, Scythians, and in general the northern nations; and the same may be said of the love of knowledge, which is the special characteristic of our part of the world, or of the love of money, which may, with equal truth, be attributed to the Phoenicians and Egyptians.
D: Narrative:
"I too," I replied, "shall be extremely well satisfied."

"Then faint not in pursuing the speculation," he said.

"Must we not acknowledge," I said, "that in each of us there are the same principles and habits which there are in the State; and that from the individual they pass into the State? -- how else can they come there? Take the quality of passion or spirit; -- it would be ridiculous to imagine that this quality, when found in States, is not derived from the individuals who are supposed to possess it, e.g. the Thracians, Scythians, and in general the northern nations; and the same may be said of the love of knowledge, which is the special characteristic of our part of the world, or of the love of money, which may, with equal truth, be attributed to the Phoenicians and Egyptians."
D: Refashioned:
"I'll be satisfied too."

"Then don't lose heart -- keep pursuing the argument," he said.

"We must acknowledge that the same principles and dispositions found in the city exist first in its individual citizens -- where else would the city get them? Take spirit or passion: it would be absurd to claim this quality in certain states doesn't come from their individual people. The Thracians, Scythians, and northern peoples generally are known for their spiritedness. The love of knowledge is the special characteristic of our Greek world. The love of money can be attributed to the Phoenicians and Egyptians."
D: Elegant: The qualities found in a city derive from its individual citizens. Spiritedness characterizes northern peoples (Thracians, Scythians), love of knowledge characterizes Greeks, and love of money characterizes Phoenicians and Egyptians. The same principles that structure the city must first exist in the individual soul.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that a city's character derives from its individual citizens, not the reverse. He illustrates with national stereotypes: Thracians and Scythians are spirited, Greeks love knowledge, Phoenicians and Egyptians love money. These three qualities in different peoples confirm that the same three principles exist in individuals.
D: Distilled: The city's character comes from its citizens; the spiritedness, love of knowledge, and love of money found in different nations prove these principles exist in individual souls.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The national stereotypes (Thracian spirit, Greek reason, Phoenician commerce) were commonplace in Greek ethnography -- The three national characters map onto the three parts of the soul: spirit (thymos), reason (logos), appetite (epithymia) -- Cross-ref: Herodotus and other Greek historians who characterized peoples by dominant traits -- The argument that the city's character derives from individuals, not vice versa, is an important methodological claim -- 'Our part of the world' means Greece, specifically Athens as the philosophical center -- The Phoenicians were the great traders of the ancient Mediterranean -- This argument uses empirical observation (national character) to support the tripartite soul theory

D: Original: Exactly so, he said. There is no difficulty in understanding this. None whatever. But the question is not quite so easy when we proceed to ask whether these principles are three or one; whether, that is to say, we learn with one part of our nature, are angry with another, and with a third part desire the satisfaction of our natural appetites; or whether the whole soul comes into play in each sort of action --to determine that is the difficulty.
D: Narrative:
"Exactly so," he said.

"There is no difficulty in understanding this."

"None whatever."

"But the question is not quite so easy when we proceed to ask whether these principles are three or one; whether, that is to say, we learn with one part of our nature, are angry with another, and with a third part desire the satisfaction of our natural appetites; or whether the whole soul comes into play in each sort of action -- to determine that is the difficulty."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly," he said.

"That's easy enough to understand."

"No difficulty at all."

"But here's where it gets harder: are these principles actually three distinct parts, or one? Do we learn with one part of our nature, get angry with another, and desire with a third? Or does the whole soul engage in each type of activity? Determining that is the real difficulty."
D: Elegant: The real difficulty: are reason, spirit, and appetite three distinct parts of the soul, or does the whole soul engage in each activity? Do we learn with one part, get angry with another, and desire with a third?
D: Summary: The easy part is recognizing that the same qualities exist in individuals and cities. The hard question is whether these are three distinct parts of the soul or one: do we reason with one part, feel anger with another, and desire with a third? Or does the whole soul participate in every action?
D: Distilled: The crucial question is whether reason, spirit, and appetite are three distinct parts of the soul or whether the whole soul engages in each activity.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the formal statement of the problem that the tripartite soul theory will answer -- The three activities (learning, anger, appetite) correspond to the three proposed parts (reason, spirit, appetite) -- Cross-ref: The Phaedrus chariot allegory (246a-254e) presents the same tripartition as charioteer, noble horse, and base horse -- Cross-ref: De Anima where Aristotle critiques the idea of soul-parts -- The question 'three or one' has vast implications for ethics, psychology, and the possibility of inner conflict -- Plato will use the principle of non-contradiction (same thing cannot do opposites simultaneously in the same respect) to argue for distinct parts

D: Original:
Yes, he said; there lies the difficulty. Then let us now try and determine whether they are the same or different.

How can we? he asked. I replied as follows: The same thing clearly cannot act or be acted upon in the same part or in relation to the same thing at the same time, in contrary ways; and therefore whenever this contradiction occurs in things apparently the same, we know that they are really not the same, but different.

Good. For example, I said, can the same thing be at rest and in motion at the same time in the same part?
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said; "there lies the difficulty."

"Then let us now try and determine whether they are the same or different."

"How can we?" he asked.

"I replied as follows: The same thing clearly cannot act or be acted upon in the same part or in relation to the same thing at the same time, in contrary ways; and therefore whenever this contradiction occurs in things apparently the same, we know that they are really not the same, but different."

"Good."

"For example," I said, "can the same thing be at rest and in motion at the same time in the same part?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that's exactly where the difficulty lies."

"Then let's try to determine whether these are the same or different."

"How?"

"Here's the principle: the same thing clearly cannot act or be acted upon in contrary ways, at the same time, in the same part, in relation to the same thing. So whenever we find this contradiction in something that appears to be one, we know it's actually not one but many."

"Good principle."

"For instance: can the same thing be at rest and in motion at the same time, in the same part of itself?"
D: Elegant: The key principle: the same thing cannot act in contrary ways at the same time, in the same part, in relation to the same thing. When contradictory impulses appear in a single entity, that entity must contain distinct parts.
D: Summary: Socrates introduces the foundational logical principle: the same thing cannot act or be acted upon in contrary ways simultaneously, in the same respect, regarding the same thing. Whenever apparent contradiction occurs in what seems to be one thing, that thing must actually contain distinct parts.
D: Distilled: The principle of non-contradiction -- the same thing cannot do opposite things at the same time in the same respect -- will prove the soul has distinct parts.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is Plato's statement of the principle of non-contradiction, one of the most important logical principles in Western philosophy -- Cross-ref: Aristotle formalized this as the Law of Non-Contradiction in Metaphysics IV.3 -- The qualifications 'same part,' 'same time,' 'same relation' are crucial: they prevent easy counterexamples -- The principle will be applied to the soul: if the soul simultaneously desires and rejects the same thing, it must have distinct parts -- Cross-ref: Physics I where Aristotle discusses motion and rest -- This passage establishes the logical foundation for everything that follows in the tripartite soul argument

D: Original: Impossible. Still, I said, let us have a more precise statement of terms, lest we should hereafter fall out by the way. Imagine the case of a man who is standing and also moving his hands and his head, and suppose a person to say that one and the same person is in motion and at rest at the same moment-to such a mode of speech we should object, and should rather say that one part of him is in motion while another is at rest.
D: Narrative:
"Impossible."

"Still," I said, "let us have a more precise statement of terms, lest we should hereafter fall out by the way. Imagine the case of a man who is standing and also moving his hands and his head, and suppose a person to say that one and the same person is in motion and at rest at the same moment -- to such a mode of speech we should object, and should rather say that one part of him is in motion while another is at rest."
D: Refashioned:
"Impossible."

"But let's be more precise, so we don't run into trouble later. Imagine someone standing still while moving their hands and head. If someone said this person is simultaneously in motion and at rest, we'd object. We'd say one part of them is moving while another part is still."
D: Elegant: Precision is needed: a person standing still while moving their hands is not simultaneously in motion and at rest. One part is moving, another is still. The principle of non-contradiction must be applied to the same part.
D: Summary: Socrates insists on precision: when a person stands still while moving their hands, they are not contradicting the principle. One part of them is moving while another is at rest. The principle of non-contradiction applies to the same part, not the whole person.
D: Distilled: Non-contradiction must be precisely applied: different parts of the same thing can be in different states simultaneously without contradiction.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The example of standing while moving hands is an everyday illustration of the part/whole distinction -- This precision is essential: without it, any complex entity would trivially violate non-contradiction -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's more rigorous treatment in Physics VI on parts and wholes in motion -- The insistence on precise terms ('lest we should hereafter fall out by the way') shows Socratic care with definitions -- This sets up the spinning top example that follows

D: Original: Very true. And suppose the objector to refine still further, and to draw the nice distinction that not only parts of tops, but whole tops, when they spin round with their pegs fixed on the spot, are at rest and in motion at the same time (and he may say the same of anything which revolves in the same spot), his objection would not be admitted by us, because in such cases things are not at rest and in motion in the same parts of themselves; we should rather say that they have both an axis and a circumference, and that the axis stands still, for there is no deviation from the perpendicular; and that the circumference goes round. But if, while revolving, the axis inclines either to the right or left, forwards or backwards, then in no point of view can they be at rest.
D: Narrative:
"Very true."

"And suppose the objector to refine still further, and to draw the nice distinction that not only parts of tops, but whole tops, when they spin round with their pegs fixed on the spot, are at rest and in motion at the same time (and he may say the same of anything which revolves in the same spot), his objection would not be admitted by us, because in such cases things are not at rest and in motion in the same parts of themselves; we should rather say that they have both an axis and a circumference, and that the axis stands still, for there is no deviation from the perpendicular; and that the circumference goes round. But if, while revolving, the axis inclines either to the right or left, forwards or backwards, then in no point of view can they be at rest."
D: Refashioned:
"True."

"And if a clever objector pressed further, arguing that a spinning top with its peg fixed in one spot is simultaneously at rest and in motion -- and the same could be said of anything revolving on a fixed point -- we wouldn't accept that objection. Because in these cases, rest and motion don't apply to the same parts. The top has an axis and a circumference: the axis stands still (there's no deviation from the vertical), while the circumference spins. But if the axis tilts in any direction while the top revolves, then it isn't at rest in any sense."
D: Elegant: A spinning top is not simultaneously at rest and in motion in the same respect: its axis is still while its circumference rotates. But if the axis tilts, nothing is at rest. The principle of non-contradiction holds when properly applied to parts.
D: Summary: Socrates addresses the spinning top objection: a top spinning on a fixed point seems both at rest and in motion, but actually its axis is still while its circumference rotates -- different parts are in different states. If the axis tilts, nothing is at rest. Properly applied, non-contradiction holds.
D: Distilled: A spinning top resolves the apparent contradiction: its axis rests while its circumference moves -- the same thing is not at rest and in motion in the same part.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The spinning top (or gyroscope) example is a famous passage in the history of physics and philosophy -- The distinction between axis and circumference resolves the apparent paradox through part-analysis -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Physics VIII.8 discusses circular motion similarly -- 'Deviation from the perpendicular' is a precise geometric description -- This example demonstrates that apparent violations of non-contradiction can always be resolved by identifying the relevant parts or aspects -- The intellectual rigor here is characteristic of Plato's middle-period metaphysics

D: Original:
That is the correct mode of describing them, he replied.

Then none of these objections will confuse us, or incline us to believe that the same thing at the same time, in the same part or in relation to the same thing, can act or be acted upon in contrary ways.

Certainly not, according to my way of thinking. Yet, I said, that we may not be compelled to examine all such objections, and prove at length that they are untrue, let us assume their absurdity, and go forward on the understanding that hereafter, if this assumption turn out to be untrue, all the consequences which follow shall be withdrawn.

Yes, he said, that will be the best way. Well, I said, would you not allow that assent and dissent, desire and aversion, attraction and repulsion, are all of them opposites, whether they are regarded as active or passive (for that makes no difference in the fact of their opposition)?
D: Narrative:
"That is the correct mode of describing them," he replied.

"Then none of these objections will confuse us, or incline us to believe that the same thing at the same time, in the same part or in relation to the same thing, can act or be acted upon in contrary ways."

"Certainly not, according to my way of thinking."

"Yet," I said, "that we may not be compelled to examine all such objections, and prove at length that they are untrue, let us assume their absurdity, and go forward on the understanding that hereafter, if this assumption turn out to be untrue, all the consequences which follow shall be withdrawn."

"Yes," he said, "that will be the best way."

"Well," I said, "would you not allow that assent and dissent, desire and aversion, attraction and repulsion, are all of them opposites, whether they are regarded as active or passive (for that makes no difference in the fact of their opposition)?"
D: Refashioned:
"That's the right way to describe it."

"Good. Then none of these objections will confuse us or make us doubt that the same thing cannot act in contrary ways at the same time, in the same part, in relation to the same thing."

"Not from my perspective."

"Still, rather than addressing every possible objection at length, let's proceed on this assumption. If it turns out to be wrong, we'll retract everything that follows from it."

"That's the best approach."

"Now: would you agree that assent and dissent, desire and aversion, attraction and repulsion are all pairs of opposites, whether we consider them as active or passive? That distinction doesn't affect the fact of their opposition."
D: Elegant: The principle of non-contradiction stands firm. Moving forward on this basis: assent and dissent, desire and aversion, attraction and repulsion are genuine opposites, whether active or passive.
D: Summary: Having established the principle of non-contradiction, Socrates proposes to assume its truth and proceed, promising to retract conclusions if the assumption fails. He then identifies the key psychological opposites: assent/dissent, desire/aversion, attraction/repulsion -- all genuine pairs of contraries regardless of whether they're active or passive.
D: Distilled: With non-contradiction established, Socrates identifies the psychological opposites -- desire and aversion, assent and dissent, attraction and repulsion -- that will prove the soul's division.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The conditional assumption ('if this turns out untrue, we'll retract') shows Platonic philosophical honesty -- Moving from physical examples (tops, standing man) to psychological opposites (desire/aversion) bridges the transition to soul analysis -- The active/passive distinction is a subtle philosophical point: wanting and being drawn to something are both forms of desire -- Cross-ref: These psychological opposites will be used in 4.46-4.51 to demonstrate the soul has distinct parts -- The list of opposites (assent/dissent, desire/aversion, attraction/repulsion) covers cognitive, conative, and emotional domains

D: Original: Yes, he said, they are opposites. Well, I said, and hunger and thirst, and the desires in general, and again willing and wishing, --all these you would refer to the classes already mentioned. You would say --would you not? --that the soul of him who desires is seeking after the object of his desires; or that he is drawing to himself the thing which he wishes to possess: or again, when a person wants anything to be given him, his mind, longing for the realisation of his desires, intimates his wish to have it by a nod of assent, as if he had been asked a question?
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "they are opposites."

"Well," I said, "and hunger and thirst, and the desires in general, and again willing and wishing -- all these you would refer to the classes already mentioned. You would say -- would you not? -- that the soul of him who desires is seeking after the object of his desires; or that he is drawing to himself the thing which he wishes to possess: or again, when a person wants anything to be given him, his mind, longing for the realisation of his desires, intimates his wish to have it by a nod of assent, as if he had been asked a question?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, they are opposites."

"Now, hunger, thirst, and desires generally -- as well as willing and wishing -- all belong to the category of attraction and assent. When someone desires something, their soul reaches toward the object of desire, drawing it to themselves. When someone wants something given to them, their mind signals its longing with a kind of inner nod of assent, as if answering 'yes' to an unspoken question."
D: Elegant: Hunger, thirst, and all desires are forms of attraction: the soul reaches toward what it wants, drawing objects to itself. Desire is fundamentally an assent -- the soul nodding 'yes' to its objects.
D: Summary: Socrates classifies hunger, thirst, and all desires as forms of attraction and assent. When the soul desires, it reaches toward its object, drawing it inward. Desire functions like a nod of assent -- the soul saying 'yes' to what it wants.
D: Distilled: All desire is a form of attraction and assent -- the soul reaching toward and affirming its objects.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Hunger and thirst are chosen as paradigmatic desires because they are the simplest and most obviously bodily -- The metaphor of desire as a 'nod of assent' connects appetitive and rational language -- Cross-ref: This analysis of desire anticipates 4.48's detailed examination of thirst as 'pure' desire -- The idea of the soul 'drawing to itself' its objects has Platonic metaphysical implications about the relationship between soul and world -- Cross-ref: Symposium 200a-201c where desire is analyzed as desire for what one lacks

D: Original:
Very true. And what would you say of unwillingness and dislike and the absence of desire; should not these be referred to the opposite class of repulsion and rejection?

Certainly. Admitting this to be true of desire generally, let us suppose a particular class of desires, and out of these we will select hunger and thirst, as they are termed, which are the most obvious of them?
D: Narrative:
"Very true."

"And what would you say of unwillingness and dislike and the absence of desire; should not these be referred to the opposite class of repulsion and rejection?"

"Certainly."

"Admitting this to be true of desire generally, let us suppose a particular class of desires, and out of these we will select hunger and thirst, as they are termed, which are the most obvious of them?"
D: Refashioned:
"True."

"And unwillingness, dislike, the absence of desire -- wouldn't these all belong to the opposite category of repulsion and rejection?"

"Certainly."

"If this holds for desire generally, let's examine a specific class. We'll select hunger and thirst, the most obvious examples."
D: Elegant: Unwillingness and dislike are forms of repulsion, the opposite of desire's attraction. To test the theory, hunger and thirst are selected as the most obvious specific desires.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes that unwillingness and dislike belong to the opposite category of repulsion and rejection. To examine desire more precisely, he selects hunger and thirst as the clearest, most obvious examples.
D: Distilled: Desire attracts, aversion repels; hunger and thirst provide the clearest test cases for analyzing how desire works in the soul.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The opposition between desire (attraction) and aversion (repulsion) sets up the key argument: if the soul simultaneously desires and rejects the same thing, it must have parts -- Hunger and thirst are strategically chosen: they are undeniably bodily appetites, making them the clearest cases of 'pure' desire -- Cross-ref: 4.48 will analyze thirst as desire for drink 'pure and simple' -- This methodological move from general to specific is characteristic of Platonic dialectic -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 496c-497a also analyzes hunger and thirst

D: Original: Let us take that class, he said. The object of one is food, and of the other drink? Yes. And here comes the point: is not thirst the desire which the soul has of drink, and of drink only; not of drink qualified by anything else; for example, warm or cold, or much or little, or, in a word, drink of any particular sort: but if the thirst be accompanied by heat, then the desire is of cold drink; or, if accompanied by cold, then of warm drink; or, if the thirst be excessive, then the drink which is desired will be excessive; or, if not great, the quantity of drink will also be small: but thirst pure and simple will desire drink pure and simple, which is the natural satisfaction of thirst, as food is of hunger?
D: Narrative:
"Let us take that class," he said.

"The object of one is food, and of the other drink?"

"Yes."

"And here comes the point: is not thirst the desire which the soul has of drink, and of drink only; not of drink qualified by anything else; for example, warm or cold, or much or little, or, in a word, drink of any particular sort: but if the thirst be accompanied by heat, then the desire is of cold drink; or, if accompanied by cold, then of warm drink; or, if the thirst be excessive, then the drink which is desired will be excessive; or, if not great, the quantity of drink will also be small: but thirst pure and simple will desire drink pure and simple, which is the natural satisfaction of thirst, as food is of hunger?"
D: Refashioned:
"Let's examine those."

"Hunger desires food, thirst desires drink?"

"Yes."

"Now here's the crucial point: isn't thirst simply the desire for drink -- drink and nothing more? Not warm drink or cold drink, not a lot or a little, not any particular kind. If the thirst is accompanied by heat, then the desire becomes specific -- cold drink. If accompanied by cold, then warm drink. If the thirst is extreme, the desire is for a large quantity; if mild, for a small amount. But thirst in itself -- pure, unqualified thirst -- desires nothing but drink in itself. Just as hunger in itself desires nothing but food."
D: Elegant: Thirst in itself is simply the desire for drink, unqualified. Only additional conditions (heat, cold, excess) make the desire specific. Pure thirst desires pure drink; pure hunger desires pure food. The basic desire is for its basic object, nothing more.
D: Summary: Socrates makes a crucial distinction: thirst in its pure form is desire for drink and nothing else -- not warm, cold, much, or little. Only accompanying conditions (heat, cold, excess) qualify the desire further. Pure thirst desires pure drink, just as pure hunger desires pure food.
D: Distilled: Pure thirst is desire for drink and nothing else; qualifications arise only from additional conditions, not from thirst itself.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This analysis of 'pure' desire versus 'qualified' desire is a key philosophical move -- It establishes that basic appetites are for basic objects, without rational qualification -- Cross-ref: 4.49 where an objector might say we always desire 'good' drink -- The distinction between unqualified and qualified desire maps onto the distinction between appetite (which just wants) and reason (which evaluates) -- This passage is foundational for Plato's psychology: the appetitive part desires blindly; only reason adds evaluation -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's De Anima III.10 on the objects of desire -- The food/drink analysis anticipates modern philosophy of mind discussions about the intentionality of desire

D: Original:
Yes, he said; the simple desire is, as you say, in every case of the simple object, and the qualified desire of the qualified object.

But here a confusion may arise; and I should wish to guard against an opponent starting up and saying that no man desires drink only, but good drink, or food only, but good food; for good is the universal object of desire, and thirst being a desire, will necessarily be thirst after good drink; and the same is true of every other desire.

Yes, he replied, the opponent might have something to say.

Nevertheless I should still maintain, that of relatives some have a quality attached to either term of the relation; others are simple and have their correlatives simple.

I do not know what you mean. Well, you know of course that the greater is relative to the less?

Certainly. And the much greater to the much less? Yes. And the sometime greater to the sometime less, and the greater that is to be to the less that is to be?

Certainly, he said. And so of more and less, and of other correlative terms, such as the double and the half, or again, the heavier and the lighter, the swifter and the slower; and of hot and cold, and of any other relatives; --is not this true of all of them?

Yes. And does not the same principle hold in the sciences? The object of science is knowledge (assuming that to be the true definition), but the object of a particular science is a particular kind of knowledge; I mean, for example, that the science of house-building is a kind of knowledge which is defined and distinguished from other kinds and is therefore termed architecture.

Certainly. Because it has a particular quality which no other has? Yes. And it has this particular quality because it has an object of a particular kind; and this is true of the other arts and sciences?
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said; "the simple desire is, as you say, in every case of the simple object, and the qualified desire of the qualified object."

"But here a confusion may arise; and I should wish to guard against an opponent starting up and saying that no man desires drink only, but good drink, or food only, but good food; for good is the universal object of desire, and thirst being a desire, will necessarily be thirst after good drink; and the same is true of every other desire."

"Yes," he replied, "the opponent might have something to say."

"Nevertheless I should still maintain, that of relatives some have a quality attached to either term of the relation; others are simple and have their correlatives simple."

"I do not know what you mean."

"Well, you know of course that the greater is relative to the less?"

"Certainly."

"And the much greater to the much less?"

"Yes."

"And the sometime greater to the sometime less, and the greater that is to be to the less that is to be?"

"Certainly," he said.

"And so of more and less, and of other correlative terms, such as the double and the half, or again, the heavier and the lighter, the swifter and the slower; and of hot and cold, and of any other relatives; -- is not this true of all of them?"

"Yes."

"And does not the same principle hold in the sciences? The object of science is knowledge (assuming that to be the true definition), but the object of a particular science is a particular kind of knowledge; I mean, for example, that the science of house-building is a kind of knowledge which is defined and distinguished from other kinds and is therefore termed architecture."

"Certainly."

"Because it has a particular quality which no other has?"

"Yes."

"And it has this particular quality because it has an object of a particular kind; and this is true of the other arts and sciences?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes -- simple desire for a simple object, qualified desire for a qualified object."

"But a confusion might arise. An opponent could argue that no one ever desires just drink, but good drink; not just food, but good food. Since 'the good' is the universal object of all desire, thirst must really be thirst for good drink -- and the same for every desire."

"That opponent might have a point."

"Still, I maintain this: in relative terms, some have qualities attached to both sides of the relation, while others are simple with simple correlatives."

"I don't follow."

"You know that 'greater' is relative to 'less'?"

"Of course."

"And 'much greater' to 'much less'?"

"Yes."

"And 'sometimes greater' to 'sometimes less,' and 'going to be greater' to 'going to be less'?"

"Yes."

"The same holds for more and less, double and half, heavier and lighter, swifter and slower, hot and cold, and all other relative terms."

"Yes."

"And the same principle applies to the sciences. Science in general has knowledge as its object. But a particular science has a particular kind of knowledge as its object. Architecture, for instance, is defined and distinguished from other sciences because it's the science of house-building."

"Right."

"It has a unique character because it has a unique object. And this holds for all arts and sciences."
D: Elegant: An objector might claim all desire is really desire for the good, making thirst always desire for good drink. But relative terms show the principle: simple terms have simple correlatives, qualified terms have qualified correlatives. 'Greater' is relative to 'less,' 'much greater' to 'much less.' Similarly in science: science in general seeks knowledge, but particular sciences seek particular knowledge -- architecture is defined by its particular object, house-building.
D: Summary: Socrates anticipates the objection that all desire is really for 'the good' (making thirst desire for good drink). He refutes this through the logic of relative terms: simple terms have simple correlatives, qualified have qualified. Just as 'greater' relates to 'less' and 'much greater' to 'much less,' science in general relates to knowledge in general, while architecture relates specifically to house-building knowledge.
D: Distilled: Simple relatives have simple correlatives; just as 'greater' relates to 'less' and general science to general knowledge, pure thirst relates to pure drink -- not 'good' drink.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The objection that all desire is for 'the good' is a Socratic position from earlier dialogues (Meno 77b-78b) -- Here Plato is refining beyond his earlier view: appetite desires its object regardless of goodness -- The theory of relative terms (pros ti) is an important piece of Platonic logic -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Categories 7 develops the theory of relatives more systematically -- The architecture example shows how the object determines the nature of the knowledge -- This entire argument serves to keep 'pure' appetite separate from rational evaluation, supporting the tripartite soul theory

D: Original: Yes. Now, then, if I have made myself clear, you will understand my original meaning in what I said about relatives. My meaning was, that if one term of a relation is taken alone, the other is taken alone; if one term is qualified, the other is also qualified. I do not mean to say that relatives may not be disparate, or that the science of health is healthy, or of disease necessarily diseased, or that the sciences of good and evil are therefore good and evil; but only that, when the term science is no longer used absolutely, but has a qualified object which in this case is the nature of health and disease, it becomes defined, and is hence called not merely science, but the science of medicine.
D: Narrative: "Now, then, if I have made myself clear, you will understand my original meaning in what I said about relatives. My meaning was, that if one term of a relation is taken alone, the other is taken alone; if one term is qualified, the other is also qualified. I do not mean to say that relatives may not be disparate, or that the science of health is healthy, or of disease necessarily diseased, or that the sciences of good and evil are therefore good and evil; but only that, when the term science is no longer used absolutely, but has a qualified object which in this case is the nature of health and disease, it becomes defined, and is hence called not merely science, but the science of medicine."
D: Refashioned: "If I've been clear, you now understand what I meant about relatives. My point is: when one term of a relation is taken unqualified, the other is also unqualified; when one is qualified, the other is too. I'm not saying that relatives must be alike in every way -- the science of health isn't itself healthy, and the science of disease isn't diseased; sciences of good and evil aren't themselves good or evil. My point is only that when 'science' is no longer used in its general sense but takes a specific object -- say, health and disease -- it becomes defined, and we call it not just 'science' but 'the science of medicine.'"
D: Elegant: The principle of correlatives: unqualified terms relate to unqualified terms, qualified to qualified. This doesn't mean relatives share their qualities -- medical science isn't itself healthy or sick. It means that when a general term (science) takes a specific object (health), it becomes a specific term (medicine).
D: Summary: Socrates clarifies his principle of correlatives: unqualified terms relate to unqualified terms, qualified to qualified. This doesn't mean they share qualities -- medical science isn't itself healthy. It means when a general term takes a specific object, it becomes a specific discipline. Applied to desire: unqualified thirst relates to unqualified drink.
D: Distilled: Unqualified terms have unqualified correlatives, qualified have qualified -- so pure thirst relates to pure drink, not to 'good' or 'bad' drink.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates explains to Glaucon -- The clarification that 'the science of health is not healthy' prevents a common fallacy in reasoning about relatives -- This is an important logical point: the relation between knower and known, desirer and desired, doesn't transfer qualities between the terms -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Categories 7 and Topics I make similar distinctions about relatives -- The medical science example is well-chosen because Plato frequently uses medicine as an analogy -- The upshot for psychology: thirst-in-itself relates to drink-in-itself; only additional rational qualifications produce desire for 'good' drink

D: Original:
I quite understand, and I think as you do. Would you not say that thirst is one of these essentially relative terms, having clearly a relation --

Yes, thirst is relative to drink. And a certain kind of thirst is relative to a certain kind of drink; but thirst taken alone is neither of much nor little, nor of good nor bad, nor of any particular kind of drink, but of drink only?

Certainly. Then the soul of the thirsty one, in so far as he is thirsty, desires only drink; for this he yearns and tries to obtain it?

That is plain. And if you suppose something which pulls a thirsty soul away from drink, that must be different from the thirsty principle which draws him like a beast to drink; for, as we were saying, the same thing cannot at the same time with the same part of itself act in contrary ways about the same.

Impossible. No more than you can say that the hands of the archer push and pull the bow at the same time, but what you say is that one hand pushes and the other pulls.

Exactly so, he replied. And might a man be thirsty, and yet unwilling to drink? Yes, he said, it constantly happens. And in such a case what is one to say? Would you not say that there was something in the soul bidding a man to drink, and something else forbidding him, which is other and stronger than the principle which bids him?

I should say so. And the forbidding principle is derived from reason, and that which bids and attracts proceeds from passion and disease?

Clearly. Then we may fairly assume that they are two, and that they differ from one another; the one with which man reasons, we may call the rational principle of the soul, the other, with which he loves and hungers and thirsts and feels the flutterings of any other desire, may be termed the irrational or appetitive, the ally of sundry pleasures and satisfactions?

Yes, he said, we may fairly assume them to be different.

Then let us finally determine that there are two principles existing in the soul. And what of passion, or spirit? Is it a third, or akin to one of the preceding?
D: Narrative:
"I quite understand, and I think as you do."

"Would you not say that thirst is one of these essentially relative terms, having clearly a relation --"

"Yes, thirst is relative to drink."

"And a certain kind of thirst is relative to a certain kind of drink; but thirst taken alone is neither of much nor little, nor of good nor bad, nor of any particular kind of drink, but of drink only?"

"Certainly."

"Then the soul of the thirsty one, in so far as he is thirsty, desires only drink; for this he yearns and tries to obtain it?"

"That is plain."

"And if you suppose something which pulls a thirsty soul away from drink, that must be different from the thirsty principle which draws him like a beast to drink; for, as we were saying, the same thing cannot at the same time with the same part of itself act in contrary ways about the same."

"Impossible."

"No more than you can say that the hands of the archer push and pull the bow at the same time, but what you say is that one hand pushes and the other pulls."

"Exactly so," he replied.

"And might a man be thirsty, and yet unwilling to drink?"

"Yes," he said, "it constantly happens."

"And in such a case what is one to say? Would you not say that there was something in the soul bidding a man to drink, and something else forbidding him, which is other and stronger than the principle which bids him?"

"I should say so."

"And the forbidding principle is derived from reason, and that which bids and attracts proceeds from passion and disease?"

"Clearly."

"Then we may fairly assume that they are two, and that they differ from one another; the one with which a man reasons, we may call the rational principle of the soul, the other, with which he loves and hungers and thirsts and feels the flutterings of any other desire, may be termed the irrational or appetitive, the ally of sundry pleasures and satisfactions?"

"Yes," he said, "we may fairly assume them to be different."

"Then let us finally determine that there are two principles existing in the soul. And what of passion, or spirit? Is it a third, or akin to one of the preceding?"
D: Refashioned:
"I understand completely and agree."

"Thirst is one of these relative terms -- clearly related to..."

"Drink. Yes."

"And a particular kind of thirst relates to a particular kind of drink. But thirst in itself is neither for much nor little, good nor bad, nor any specific kind -- just drink."

"Right."

"So the soul of a thirsty person, insofar as they're thirsty, desires only drink. That's what they crave and try to obtain."

"Obviously."

"But if something pulls a thirsty person away from drink, that must be different from the thirsty impulse that draws them to drink like an animal. As we established, the same thing cannot simultaneously act in contrary ways about the same object with the same part of itself."

"Impossible."

"Just as you can't say the archer's hands push and pull the bow at once -- one hand pushes while the other pulls."

"Exactly."

"Can a person be thirsty yet unwilling to drink?"

"Yes, that happens all the time."

"Then what do we say? There must be something in the soul urging the person to drink, and something else forbidding it -- something different and stronger than the urging impulse."

"There must be."

"The forbidding comes from reason; the urging and attracting comes from appetite and passion."

"Clearly."

"So we can fairly conclude these are two distinct principles: the one we reason with, we'll call the rational part of the soul. The other, with which we love, hunger, thirst, and feel the fluttering of every desire, we'll call the irrational or appetitive part -- the ally of various pleasures and satisfactions."

"Yes, we can fairly call them different."

"So two principles are established. But what about passion or spirit? Is it a third, or does it belong to one of these two?"
D: Elegant: Thirst in itself desires only drink. But a person can be thirsty yet refuse to drink -- something in the soul urges while something else forbids. Since the same thing cannot simultaneously do opposites in the same respect, these must be two distinct principles: reason (which forbids) and appetite (which urges). The rational part reasons; the appetitive part hungers, thirsts, and desires. But is spirit a third part, or merely a form of one of these two?
D: Summary: The proof of the soul's division: thirst desires only drink, yet a person can be thirsty and refuse to drink. Since the same thing cannot simultaneously do opposites, two principles must exist: reason (which forbids) and appetite (which urges). The rational part reasons; the appetitive part desires. This establishes two parts of the soul -- but the question remains: is spirit/passion a third part, or does it belong to one of these two?
D: Distilled: A thirsty person who refuses to drink proves the soul has at least two parts -- reason that forbids and appetite that urges -- and the question arises whether spirit is a third.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the central argument for the bipartite (and eventually tripartite) soul -- The thirsty person refusing to drink is the key empirical observation -- The archer analogy (one hand pushes, the other pulls) brilliantly illustrates how one entity can contain opposing forces -- 'Draws him like a beast to drink' characterizes appetite as animal-like -- 'Flutterings of desire' is vivid psychological language -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 246a-254e develops the soul's parts as charioteer and two horses -- The question about spirit (thymos) sets up 4.52-4.58 -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's De Anima III.9-10 on the desiring and reasoning parts of the soul -- 'Irrational or appetitive' (alogiston kai epithymetikon) becomes standard terminology in Greek philosophy

D: Original:
I should be inclined to say --akin to desire. Well, I said, there is a story which I remember to have heard, and in which I put faith. The story is, that Leontius, the son of Aglaion, coming up one day from the Piraeus, under the north wall on the outside, observed some dead bodies lying on the ground at the place of execution. He felt a desire to see them, and also a dread and abhorrence of them; for a time he struggled and covered his eyes, but at length the desire got the better of him; and forcing them open, he ran up to the dead bodies, saying, Look, ye wretches, take your fill of the fair sight.

I have heard the story myself, he said. The moral of the tale is, that anger at times goes to war with desire, as though they were two distinct things.
D: Narrative:
"I should be inclined to say -- akin to desire."

"Well," I said, "there is a story which I remember to have heard, and in which I put faith. The story is, that Leontius, the son of Aglaion, coming up one day from the Piraeus, under the north wall on the outside, observed some dead bodies lying on the ground at the place of execution. He felt a desire to see them, and also a dread and abhorrence of them; for a time he struggled and covered his eyes, but at length the desire got the better of him; and forcing them open, he ran up to the dead bodies, saying, 'Look, ye wretches, take your fill of the fair sight.'"

"I have heard the story myself," he said.

"The moral of the tale is, that anger at times goes to war with desire, as though they were two distinct things."
D: Refashioned:
"I'd be inclined to say spirit is akin to desire."

"Let me tell you a story I believe to be true. Leontius, the son of Aglaion, was walking up from the Piraeus along the outside of the north wall when he noticed corpses lying at the place of execution. He felt a desire to look at them, but also dread and revulsion. For a while he struggled, covering his eyes. But finally the desire overpowered him. He forced his eyes open, ran toward the bodies, and cried out: 'Look, you wretches -- feast your eyes on this fine spectacle!'"

"I've heard that story too."

"The lesson is that anger sometimes goes to war against desire, as if they were two distinct things."
D: Elegant: Leontius felt desire to look at corpses and revulsion that forbade it. When desire won, he cursed his own eyes in anger. The moral: anger (spirit) wars against desire as a separate force. Spirit is not akin to appetite -- it opposes it.
D: Summary: Glaucon suggests spirit is akin to desire, but Socrates tells the story of Leontius: passing corpses at an execution site, he felt both desire to look and revulsion. When desire overpowered him, he cursed his own eyes in anger. The moral: anger (spirit) fights against desire as a distinct force, not an ally.
D: Distilled: The story of Leontius proves that spirit (anger at oneself) wars against desire as a separate force, not as another form of appetite.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The story of Leontius is one of the most famous passages in the Republic and in the history of psychology -- Piraeus was Athens' port city; the north wall was part of the Long Walls connecting Athens to Piraeus -- The 'place of execution' was likely near the Barathron, a pit where condemned criminals were thrown -- Leontius's self-address ('Look, ye wretches') shows spirit (anger at himself) treating his eyes/desires as separate entities -- This is a vivid case of inner conflict: appetite pulls toward the corpses, spirit recoils -- Cross-ref: Freud's concept of the superego punishing the id bears structural similarity -- The story is the key evidence that spirit is not appetite -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 253c-254e where the dark horse (appetite) is restrained by the charioteer and noble horse (reason and spirit)

D: Original: Yes; that is the meaning, he said. And are there not many other cases in which we observe that when a man's desires violently prevail over his reason, he reviles himself, and is angry at the violence within him, and that in this struggle, which is like the struggle of factions in a State, his spirit is on the side of his reason; --but for the passionate or spirited element to take part with the desires when reason that she should not be opposed, is a sort of thing which thing which I believe that you never observed occurring in yourself, nor, as I should imagine, in any one else?
D: Narrative:
"Yes; that is the meaning," he said.

"And are there not many other cases in which we observe that when a man's desires violently prevail over his reason, he reviles himself, and is angry at the violence within him, and that in this struggle, which is like the struggle of factions in a State, his spirit is on the side of his reason; -- but for the passionate or spirited element to take part with the desires when reason decides that she should not be opposed, is a sort of thing which I believe that you never observed occurring in yourself, nor, as I should imagine, in any one else?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that's the meaning."

"And aren't there many other cases where a person's desires violently overpower their reason, and they curse themselves, angry at the violence within? In this inner struggle -- like a civil war between factions in a city -- spirit always sides with reason. But have you ever observed spirit siding with the desires against reason's judgment? I don't think you've ever seen that in yourself or in anyone else."
D: Elegant: When desires violently overpower reason, a person reviles themselves -- spirit always sides with reason in this inner civil war. Spirit never allies with appetite against reason's judgment. This is a universal observation.
D: Summary: In cases of inner conflict where desires overpower reason, people revile themselves -- their spirit sides with reason, angry at the appetitive violence within. Socrates claims this is universal: spirit never takes the side of desire against reason's judgment, functioning like a political faction that always allies with the ruling class.
D: Distilled: Spirit always sides with reason against desire in inner conflict; it never allies with appetite against reason's judgment.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The inner conflict as 'struggle of factions in a State' (stasis) explicitly connects the soul's structure to political structure -- Spirit's alliance with reason against appetite is the key structural claim of the tripartite theory -- Cross-ref: In the city, the auxiliaries (spirit) support the guardians (reason) against the producers (appetite) -- The claim that spirit never sides with appetite against reason is strong and arguably overdrawn -- Cross-ref: Book IX's analysis of the tyrannical soul where all parts are in disorder -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Rhetoric II.2 on anger (orge) as a response to perceived injustice -- The self-reviling person shows spirit as a kind of moral indignation turned inward

D: Original: Certainly not. Suppose that a man thinks he has done a wrong to another, the nobler he is the less able is he to feel indignant at any suffering, such as hunger, or cold, or any other pain which the injured person may inflict upon him --these he deems to be just, and, as I say, his anger refuses to be excited by them.
D: Narrative:
"Certainly not."

"Suppose that a man thinks he has done a wrong to another, the nobler he is the less able is he to feel indignant at any suffering, such as hunger, or cold, or any other pain which the injured person may inflict upon him -- these he deems to be just, and, as I say, his anger refuses to be excited by them."
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly not."

"Consider this: when a person believes they have wronged another, the nobler they are, the less able they are to feel indignant at any suffering the wronged person inflicts on them -- hunger, cold, or any other pain. They consider the suffering just, and their anger refuses to rise against it."
D: Elegant: A noble person who knows they've done wrong cannot feel indignant at the suffering inflicted by the wronged party. Their spirit accepts the punishment as just and refuses to be aroused.
D: Summary: When a noble person recognizes they have wronged someone, they cannot feel indignant at any retaliatory suffering. They accept hunger, cold, and pain as just consequences, and their spirit refuses to rebel against deserved punishment.
D: Distilled: A noble person's spirit accepts suffering inflicted by someone they have wronged, recognizing it as just and refusing to feel indignant.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates to Glaucon -- This shows spirit (thymos) as responsive to justice: it flares when one is wronged but subsides when one has done wrong -- Spirit is thus a moral faculty, not merely emotional -- Cross-ref: 4.55 completes the picture with the opposite case -- The nobler the person, the more responsive their spirit is to moral truth -- This connects spirit to the virtue of courage (preservation of right opinion about what to fear) -- Cross-ref: Crito 49a-e where Socrates argues one should never return wrong for wrong -- The example shows that spirit is educated by reason's judgments about justice

D: Original: True, he said. But when he thinks that he is the sufferer of the wrong, then he boils and chafes, and is on the side of what he believes to be justice; and because he suffers hunger or cold or other pain he is only the more determined to persevere and conquer. His noble spirit will not be quelled until he either slays or is slain; or until he hears the voice of the shepherd, that is, reason, bidding his dog bark no more.
D: Narrative:
"True," he said.

"But when he thinks that he is the sufferer of the wrong, then he boils and chafes, and is on the side of what he believes to be justice; and because he suffers hunger or cold or other pain he is only the more determined to persevere and conquer. His noble spirit will not be quelled until he either slays or is slain; or until he hears the voice of the shepherd, that is, reason, bidding his dog bark no more."
D: Refashioned:
"True."

"But when someone believes they are the one being wronged, they boil with indignation, siding with what they believe to be justice. Hunger, cold, and pain only make them more determined to persevere and prevail. Their noble spirit refuses to be quelled until they either conquer or die -- unless they hear the voice of the shepherd, that is, reason, telling its dog to stop barking."
D: Elegant: When wronged, spirit boils with indignation, siding with justice. Suffering only strengthens its resolve. It fights until victory or death -- unless reason, like a shepherd, calls its dog to heel.
D: Summary: When someone believes they've been wronged, their spirit boils with indignation, siding with justice. Suffering only strengthens their determination. Spirit fights to the death unless reason -- like a shepherd commanding its dog -- calls it to heel.
D: Distilled: Spirit fights relentlessly for justice when one is wronged, yielding only to the calming voice of reason -- the shepherd commanding its dog.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates to Glaucon -- The shepherd-dog metaphor is powerful and precise: reason is the shepherd, spirit is the dog -- Cross-ref: Book III 375a-376c where the guardian's nature is compared to a well-bred dog -- 'Slays or is slain' recalls Homeric warrior values -- The image of spirit 'boiling' (zeo) uses the same heat metaphor found in Homer's descriptions of anger -- Cross-ref: Iliad where Achilles' wrath is the paradigmatic case of thymos in action -- Spirit responds to perceived justice/injustice, confirming it as a moral faculty distinct from appetite -- The contrast between 4.54 (spirit quiescent when one has done wrong) and 4.55 (spirit aroused when one is wronged) demonstrates spirit's moral sensitivity

D: Original:
The illustration is perfect, he replied; and in our State, as we were saying, the auxiliaries were to be dogs, and to hear the voice of the rulers, who are their shepherds.

I perceive, I said, that you quite understand me; there is, however, a further point which I wish you to consider.

What point? You remember that passion or spirit appeared at first sight to be a kind of desire, but now we should say quite the contrary; for in the conflict of the soul spirit is arrayed on the side of the rational principle.
D: Narrative:
"The illustration is perfect," he replied; "and in our State, as we were saying, the auxiliaries were to be dogs, and to hear the voice of the rulers, who are their shepherds."

"I perceive," I said, "that you quite understand me; there is, however, a further point which I wish you to consider."

"What point?"

"You remember that passion or spirit appeared at first sight to be a kind of desire, but now we should say quite the contrary; for in the conflict of the soul spirit is arrayed on the side of the rational principle."
D: Refashioned:
"Perfect illustration," he replied. "And in our city, the auxiliaries were to be like dogs, obeying the voice of the rulers, their shepherds."

"You understand me well. But there's a further point to consider."

"What's that?"

Certainly, he said, that is the true account of temperance whether in the State or individual.

There is no difference in my opinion, he said. Because, if any doubt is still lingering in our minds, a few commonplace instances will satisfy us of the truth of what I am saying.

Wisdom is reason ruling with knowledge of each part's interest. Temperance is all three parts in harmony, agreeing reason should rule. Justice is all parts doing their own work. D: Summary: The four virtues are now fully defined in the individual: courage is spirit preserving reason's commands; wisdom is reason ruling with knowledge of each part's interest; temperance is all parts harmoniously agreeing reason should rule; justice is each part doing its own work. Practical proof: the just person will not steal, commit sacrilege, or betray friends or country.
D: Distilled: In the just individual, reason rules wisely, spirit enforces courageously, all parts agree temperately, and each performs its function -- producing a person who will never steal, betray, or commit sacrilege.

"Clearly."

"But true justice concerns not the outward person but the inward -- the true self. The just person does not allow the elements within to interfere with one another or do each other's work. They set their inner life in order, becoming their own master and their own law, at peace with themselves. When they have bound together the three principles within -- comparable to the high, low, and middle notes of a musical scale, with all the intervals between -- when these are bound into unity, and the person is no longer many but has become one entirely temperate, perfectly harmonized nature, then they act.
