They bind the three principles together -- like high, low, and middle notes of a scale -- becoming one harmonized nature rather than many. Then every action that preserves this harmony is called just; whatever impairs it, unjust. The knowledge guiding harmony is wisdom; the opinion guiding discord is ignorance.
D: Summary: Justice concerns the inner person, not the outer. The just person orders their soul so no part interferes with another, binding reason, spirit, and appetite together like the notes of a musical scale into one harmonized nature. Every action preserving this inner harmony is just and wise; every action disrupting it is unjust and ignorant. The just person and the just city have been discovered.
D: Distilled: True justice is inner harmony -- the soul's three parts bound together like musical notes, producing a unified nature where every action preserves internal order.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is arguably the most important passage in the Republic -- the definitive statement of justice as inner harmony -- 'Not the outward man, but the inward' distinguishes Plato's justice from conventional Greek justice (which focused on external actions) -- 'His own master and his own law' (autos hautou archon) echoes the self-mastery discussion of 4.26-4.28 -- The musical scale analogy (high, low, middle notes plus intervals) creates a rich image of the harmonized soul -- 'No longer many, but has become one' fulfills the unity theme from 4.9-4.10 -- Cross-ref: Pythagorean musical-mathematical philosophy deeply influences this passage -- The definitions of just action (preserving harmony) and unjust action (impairing it) are beautifully simple -- Cross-ref: This is Plato's answer to Thrasymachus (Book I) and to Glaucon's challenge (Book II)

D: Original: Most certainly not. May we say so, then? Let us say so. And now, I said, injustice has to be considered. Clearly. Must not injustice be a strife which arises among the three principles --a meddlesomeness, and interference, and rising up of a part of the soul against the whole, an assertion of unlawful authority, which is made by a rebellious subject against a true prince, of whom he is the natural vassal, --what is all this confusion and delusion but injustice, and intemperance and cowardice and ignorance, and every form of vice?
D: Narrative:
"Most certainly not."

"May we say so, then?"

"Let us say so."

"And now," I said, "injustice has to be considered."

"Clearly."

"Must not injustice be a strife which arises among the three principles -- a meddlesomeness, and interference, and rising up of a part of the soul against the whole, an assertion of unlawful authority, which is made by a rebellious subject against a true prince, of whom he is the natural vassal -- what is all this confusion and delusion but injustice, and intemperance and cowardice and ignorance, and every form of vice?"
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly not a falsehood."

"Then let us say so."

"Now we must consider injustice."

"Clearly."

"Injustice must be the conflict that arises among the three principles -- the meddling and interference, the rebellion of one part of the soul against the whole, the assertion of unlawful authority by a part that is naturally subordinate against the part that is its natural ruler. What is all this confusion and disorder but injustice itself? And is it not also intemperance, cowardice, ignorance -- every form of vice?"
D: Elegant: Injustice is strife among the soul's three parts: meddling, interference, and the rebellion of a subordinate part against its natural ruler. This inner disorder is not only injustice but simultaneously intemperance, cowardice, ignorance -- every form of vice.
D: Summary: Injustice is defined as internal strife: the soul's parts meddling with each other, a subordinate part rebelling against its natural ruler and asserting unlawful authority over the whole. This disorder is simultaneously injustice, intemperance, cowardice, and ignorance -- all vices are forms of inner disharmony.
D: Distilled: Injustice is the rebellion of a subordinate soul-part against the whole, and this single disorder encompasses every vice -- intemperance, cowardice, and ignorance alike.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Political language describes psychological disorder: 'rebellious subject against a true prince,' 'unlawful authority,' 'natural vassal' -- This makes injustice a kind of inner tyranny or civil war -- Cross-ref: Book IX's portrait of the tyrannical soul as the ultimate case of inner rebellion -- The claim that all vices are forms of one disorder (injustice as inner disharmony) is a powerful unifying thesis -- Cross-ref: The Protagoras on the unity of virtue -- each vice corresponds to one of the four virtues failing: intemperance (loss of temperance), cowardice (loss of courage), ignorance (loss of wisdom), injustice (loss of justice) -- Cross-ref: Book VIII's sequence of degenerate constitutions mirrors the progressive breakdown of inner order

D: Original:
Exactly so. And if the nature of justice and injustice be known, then the meaning of acting unjustly and being unjust, or, again, of acting justly, will also be perfectly clear?

What do you mean? he said. Why, I said, they are like disease and health; being in the soul just what disease and health are in the body.

How so? he said. Why, I said, that which is healthy causes health, and that which is unhealthy causes disease.

Yes. And just actions cause justice, and unjust actions cause injustice?

That is certain. And the creation of health is the institution of a natural order and government of one by another in the parts of the body; and the creation of disease is the production of a state of things at variance with this natural order?

True. And is not the creation of justice the institution of a natural order and government of one by another in the parts of the soul, and the creation of injustice the production of a state of things at variance with the natural order?

Exactly so, he said. Then virtue is the health and beauty and well-being of the soul, and vice the disease and weakness and deformity of the same?

True. And do not good practices lead to virtue, and evil practices to vice?

Assuredly. Still our old question of the comparative advantage of justice and injustice has not been answered: Which is the more profitable, to be just and act justly and practise virtue, whether seen or unseen of gods and men, or to be unjust and act unjustly, if only unpunished and unreformed?
D: Narrative:
"Exactly so."

"And if the nature of justice and injustice be known, then the meaning of acting unjustly and being unjust, or, again, of acting justly, will also be perfectly clear?"

"What do you mean?" he said.

"Why," I said, "they are like disease and health; being in the soul just what disease and health are in the body."

"How so?" he said.

"Why," I said, "that which is healthy causes health, and that which is unhealthy causes disease."

"Yes."

"And just actions cause justice, and unjust actions cause injustice?"

"That is certain."

"And the creation of health is the institution of a natural order and government of one by another in the parts of the body; and the creation of disease is the production of a state of things at variance with this natural order?"

"True."

"And is not the creation of justice the institution of a natural order and government of one by another in the parts of the soul, and the creation of injustice the production of a state of things at variance with the natural order?"

"Exactly so," he said.

"Then virtue is the health and beauty and well-being of the soul, and vice the disease and weakness and deformity of the same?"

"True."

"And do not good practices lead to virtue, and evil practices to vice?"

"Assuredly."

"Still our old question of the comparative advantage of justice and injustice has not been answered: Which is the more profitable, to be just and act justly and practise virtue, whether seen or unseen of gods and men, or to be unjust and act unjustly, if only unpunished and unreformed?"
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly."

"So if we understand the nature of justice and injustice, the meaning of acting justly or unjustly becomes clear."

"How so?"

"Justice and injustice are to the soul what health and disease are to the body."

"Explain."

"What is healthy produces health; what is unhealthy produces disease."

"Yes."

"Just actions produce justice in the soul; unjust actions produce injustice."

"Certainly."

"Health is the establishment of natural order among the body's parts, where each governs and is governed according to nature. Disease is a state at variance with this natural order."

"True."

"Then justice is the establishment of natural order among the soul's parts, and injustice is disorder at variance with that natural order."

"Exactly."

"So virtue is the health, beauty, and well-being of the soul, and vice is its disease, weakness, and deformity."

"True."

"And good practices lead to virtue, evil practices to vice."

"Certainly."

"But our original question remains unanswered: which is more profitable -- to be just and practice virtue, whether or not gods and men observe it, or to be unjust and act unjustly, so long as one escapes punishment and correction?"
D: Elegant: Justice is to the soul what health is to the body: the natural order of parts governing and being governed according to nature. Virtue is the soul's health, beauty, and well-being; vice is its disease, weakness, and deformity. Good practices produce virtue; evil practices produce vice. The original question returns: which is more profitable -- justice observed or unobserved, or unpunished injustice?
D: Summary: Socrates draws the analogy between justice and health: both are the natural ordering of parts, where each element governs and is governed according to nature. Virtue is the soul's health and beauty; vice is its disease and deformity. This brings back the original question: which is more profitable -- practicing justice whether or not anyone sees it, or practicing injustice if one can escape punishment?
D: Distilled: Justice is the soul's health -- natural order among its parts; vice is its disease -- and the question remains whether justice or unpunished injustice is more profitable.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The health-disease analogy for justice-injustice is one of Plato's most powerful arguments -- it makes the question of justice's profitability seem absurd, since no one would ask whether health is profitable -- Cross-ref: Book III 405a-410a on medicine and the healthy city -- The phrase 'whether seen or unseen of gods and men' directly recalls Glaucon's challenge in Book II (360b-362c) about the Ring of Gyges -- Cross-ref: The Ring of Gyges asked whether a person invisible to gods and men would still choose justice -- The answer is now obvious: who would choose to have a diseased soul? -- 'Unpunished and unreformed' (me didontos diken) adds the condition that makes injustice seem maximally attractive -- This sets up the final reductio in 4.65

D: Original: In my judgment, Socrates, the question has now become ridiculous. We know that, when the bodily constitution is gone, life is no longer endurable, though pampered with all kinds of meats and drinks, and having all wealth and all power; and shall we be told that when the very essence of the vital principle is undermined and corrupted, life is still worth having to a man, if only he be allowed to do whatever he likes with the single exception that he is not to acquire justice and virtue, or to escape from injustice and vice; assuming them both to be such as we have described?
D: Narrative: "In my judgment, Socrates, the question has now become ridiculous. We know that, when the bodily constitution is gone, life is no longer endurable, though pampered with all kinds of meats and drinks, and having all wealth and all power; and shall we be told that when the very essence of the vital principle is undermined and corrupted, life is still worth having to a man, if only he be allowed to do whatever he likes with the single exception that he is not to acquire justice and virtue, or to escape from injustice and vice; assuming them both to be such as we have described?"
D: Refashioned: "In my judgment, Socrates, the question is now ridiculous. We know that when the body's constitution is ruined, life becomes unendurable -- no matter how much food, drink, wealth, and power you have. So are we really supposed to believe that when the soul's vital principle is undermined and corrupted, life is still worth living -- as long as the person can do whatever they want, with the sole restriction that they cannot acquire justice and virtue or escape from injustice and vice? Given what we've now shown justice and injustice to be, the question answers itself."
D: Elegant: The question is now absurd. When bodily health is destroyed, no amount of wealth or pleasure makes life endurable. How then could life be worth living when the soul's very essence is corrupted -- when one possesses everything except justice and virtue?
D: Summary: Glaucon declares the question of justice's profitability has become ridiculous. Just as no amount of wealth or pleasure makes life worth living when the body's health is destroyed, life cannot be worth living when the soul's essential principle -- justice -- is corrupted. The health analogy has made the answer self-evident.
D: Distilled: The question of whether injustice can be profitable is now absurd -- just as no one would choose bodily ruin for any amount of wealth, no one should choose the corruption of their soul.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon delivers the judgment, confirming Socrates' argument -- This is the dramatic payoff of the entire Republic so far -- Glaucon, who posed the challenge in Book II (that injustice might be more profitable), now declares the question 'ridiculous' -- The body-soul parallel is complete: health of body :: justice of soul, therefore justice is obviously preferable -- 'All kinds of meats and drinks, all wealth and all power' represents the maximum imaginable external goods -- even these cannot compensate for inner corruption -- Cross-ref: Book II's Ring of Gyges and the invisible unjust man -- the answer is now clear: even invisible, the unjust person is miserable because their soul is diseased -- Cross-ref: Book IX will further quantify the superiority of the just life

D: Original:
Yes, I said, the question is, as you say, ridiculous. Still, as we are near the spot at which we may see the truth in the clearest manner with our own eyes, let us not faint by the way.

Certainly not, he replied. Come up hither, I said, and behold the various forms of vice, those of them, I mean, which are worth looking at.

I am following you, he replied: proceed. I said, The argument seems to have reached a height from which, as from some tower of speculation, a man may look down and see that virtue is one, but that the forms of vice are innumerable; there being four special ones which are deserving of note.

What do you mean? he said. I mean, I replied, that there appear to be as many forms of the soul as there are distinct forms of the State.

How many? There are five of the State, and five of the soul, I said.

What are they? The first, I said, is that which we have been describing, and which may be said to have two names, monarchy and aristocracy, accordingly as rule is exercised by one distinguished man or by many.

True, he replied. But I regard the two names as describing one form only; for whether the government is in the hands of one or many, if the governors have been trained in the manner which we have supposed, the fundamental laws of the State will be maintained.

That is true, he replied.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," I said, "the question is, as you say, ridiculous. Still, as we are near the spot at which we may see the truth in the clearest manner with our own eyes, let us not faint by the way."

"Certainly not," he replied.

"Come up hither," I said, "and behold the various forms of vice, those of them, I mean, which are worth looking at."

"I am following you," he replied: "proceed."

"I said, The argument seems to have reached a height from which, as from some tower of speculation, a man may look down and see that virtue is one, but that the forms of vice are innumerable; there being four special ones which are deserving of note."

"What do you mean?" he said.

"I mean," I replied, "that there appear to be as many forms of the soul as there are distinct forms of the State."

"How many?"

"There are five of the State, and five of the soul," I said.

"What are they?"

"The first," I said, "is that which we have been describing, and which may be said to have two names, monarchy and aristocracy, accordingly as rule is exercised by one distinguished man or by many."

"True," he replied.

"But I regard the two names as describing one form only; for whether the government is in the hands of one or many, if the governors have been trained in the manner which we have supposed, the fundamental laws of the State will be maintained."

"That is true," he replied.
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, the question is ridiculous. But since we're close to the point where we can see the truth most clearly, let's not give up now."

"Certainly not."

"Come higher, then, and look at the various forms of vice -- at least the ones worth examining."

"I'm following. Go on."

"From this height in our argument -- as from a tower of speculation -- we can look down and see that virtue is one, while the forms of vice are innumerable. Four in particular deserve attention."

"What do you mean?"

"There are as many forms of the soul as there are distinct forms of government."

"How many?"

"Five forms of government and five forms of the soul."

"What are they?"

"The first is the one we've been describing, which goes by two names: monarchy when one distinguished person rules, aristocracy when several do."

"True."

"But I regard these as one form, not two. Whether one or many govern, as long as the rulers are trained as we've described, the fundamental laws of the city will be maintained."

"That's true."
D: Elegant: From the height of their argument, they can see that virtue is one while the forms of vice are innumerable -- four deserve special attention. There are five forms of government and five corresponding forms of the soul. The first and best -- the one they've been building -- is monarchy or aristocracy, which are really one form: so long as rulers are properly trained, the fundamental laws hold whether one rules or many.
D: Summary: From the summit of their argument, Socrates sees that virtue is one while vice takes innumerable forms -- four especially worth examining. Five forms of government correspond to five forms of the soul. The first and best is what they've been constructing: monarchy or aristocracy, which are fundamentally one form, since properly trained rulers maintain the same laws whether governing alone or in a group. This sets up the analysis of degenerate constitutions in Books VIII-IX.
D: Distilled: Virtue is one, vice innumerable; five forms of government match five forms of the soul, with the best being monarchy/aristocracy -- one form of just rule -- and four forms of degeneration to follow.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Tower of speculation' (skopia) is a memorable image for the philosophical vantage point -- 'Virtue is one, forms of vice innumerable' echoes Tolstoy's later observation about happy and unhappy families -- The five forms of government: aristocracy/monarchy, timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, tyranny -- these will be analyzed in Books VIII-IX -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Politics III develops a different classification of constitutions -- The five soul types correspond to the five constitutions: philosophical/kingly, honor-loving, money-loving, freedom-loving, tyrannical -- Monarchy and aristocracy as one form shows Plato values the quality of rulers over their number -- This passage serves as the bridge to Books VIII-IX, announcing the program of analyzing degenerate constitutions and souls -- Book IV ends here with the major arguments complete: the four virtues of the city, the tripartite soul, justice as inner harmony

D: Original:
Such is the good and true City or State, and the good and man is of the same pattern; and if this is right every other is wrong; and the evil is one which affects not only the ordering of the State, but also the regulation of the individual soul, and is exhibited in four forms.

What are they? he said. I was proceeding to tell the order in which the four evil forms appeared to me to succeed one another, when Pole marchus, who was sitting a little way off, just beyond Adeimantus, began to whisper to him: stretching forth his hand, he took hold of the upper part of his coat by the shoulder, and drew him towards him, leaning forward himself so as to be quite close and saying something in his ear, of which I only caught the words, 'Shall we let him off, or what shall we do?
D: Narrative:
"Such is the good and true City or State, and the good and true man is of the same pattern; and if this is right every other is wrong; and the evil is one which affects not only the ordering of the State, but also the regulation of the individual soul, and is exhibited in four forms."

"What are they?" he said.

I was proceeding to tell the order in which the four evil forms appeared to me to succeed one another, when Polemarchus, who was sitting a little way off, just beyond Adeimantus, began to whisper to him: stretching forth his hand, he took hold of the upper part of his coat by the shoulder, and drew him towards him, leaning forward himself so as to be quite close and saying something in his ear, of which I only caught the words, "Shall we let him off, or what shall we do?"
D: Refashioned:
"So that's the good and true City or State, and a good and true person follows the same pattern. If this model is right, every other is wrong -- and the corruption that ruins a State also ruins the individual soul, manifesting in four distinct forms."

"What are they?" Glaucon asked.

I was about to explain the order in which these four corrupt forms succeed one another, when Polemarchus, sitting a short distance away just past Adeimantus, began whispering to him. He reached out, grabbed the shoulder of Adeimantus's cloak, and pulled him close, leaning in to murmur something in his ear. I caught only the words: "Should we let him off, or what should we do?"
D: Elegant: Socrates declares that the good State and the good individual share the same pattern -- all deviations from this are corrupt, manifesting in four forms. Before he can enumerate them, Polemarchus whispers to Adeimantus, conspiring to press Socrates on a point he has skipped over.
D: Summary: As Socrates prepares to describe four forms of corrupt government, Polemarchus secretly confers with Adeimantus about whether to let Socrates skip over an important topic he has glossed over.
D: Distilled: Socrates is interrupted mid-argument by his companions conspiring to force him back to an unfinished topic.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates (narrator), with Polemarchus and Adeimantus conspiring -- Polemarchus is the son of Cephalus, in whose house the Republic is set (Book I) -- The 'four evil forms' refer to timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, and tyranny, discussed in Books VIII-IX -- This interruption redirects the dialogue to Book V's topics: equality of women, community of families, and philosopher-kings

D: Original: Certainly not, said Adeimantus, raising his voice. Who is it, I said, whom you are refusing to let off? You, he said. I repeated, Why am I especially not to be let off? Why, he said, we think that you are lazy, and mean to cheat us out of a whole chapter which is a very important part of the story; and you fancy that we shall not notice your airy way of proceeding; as if it were self-evident to everybody, that in the matter of women and children 'friends have all things in common.'
D: Narrative:
"Certainly not," said Adeimantus, raising his voice.

"Who is it," I said, "whom you are refusing to let off?"

"You," he said.

I repeated, "Why am I especially not to be let off?"

"Why," he said, "we think that you are lazy, and mean to cheat us out of a whole chapter which is a very important part of the story; and you fancy that we shall not notice your airy way of proceeding; as if it were self-evident to everybody, that in the matter of women and children 'friends have all things in common.'"
D: Refashioned:
"Absolutely not," said Adeimantus, raising his voice.

"Who is it you're refusing to let off?" I asked.

"You," he said.

"Why me in particular?" I pressed.

"Because we think you're being lazy," he replied. "You're trying to cheat us out of an entire chapter that's a crucial part of the argument. You assume we won't notice your breezy way of skipping over it -- as though it were obvious to everyone that when it comes to women and children, 'friends share all things in common.'"
D: Elegant: Adeimantus accuses Socrates of lazily skipping a crucial topic -- the meaning of 'friends have all things in common' as applied to women and children among the guardians.
D: Summary: Adeimantus confronts Socrates, accusing him of trying to skip past the important question of what it means for the guardians to share women and children in common, treating it as self-evident when it requires thorough explanation.
D: Distilled: Adeimantus demands Socrates explain what communal sharing of women and children actually means rather than glossing over it.
Notes: Speakers: Adeimantus and Socrates -- 'Friends have all things in common' is a Pythagorean proverb, attributed in various forms throughout Greek philosophy -- Adeimantus, brother of Glaucon and Plato, serves as a persistent questioner throughout the Republic -- The 'whole chapter' refers to what becomes Book V's central content

D: Original:
And was I not right, Adeimantus? Yes, he said; but what is right in this particular case, like everything else, requires to be explained; for community may be of many kinds. Please, therefore, to say what sort of community you mean. We have been long expecting that you would tell us something about the family life of your citizens --how they will bring children into the world, and rear them when they have arrived, and, in general, what is the nature of this community of women and children-for we are of opinion that the right or wrong management of such matters will have a great and paramount influence on the State for good or for evil. And now, since the question is still undetermined, and you are taking in hand another State, we have resolved, as you heard, not to let you go until you give an account of all this.

To that resolution, said Glaucon, you may regard me as saying Agreed.
D: Narrative:
"And was I not right, Adeimantus?"

"Yes," he said; "but what is right in this particular case, like everything else, requires to be explained; for community may be of many kinds. Please, therefore, to say what sort of community you mean. We have been long expecting that you would tell us something about the family life of your citizens -- how they will bring children into the world, and rear them when they have arrived, and, in general, what is the nature of this community of women and children -- for we are of opinion that the right or wrong management of such matters will have a great and paramount influence on the State for good or for evil. And now, since the question is still undetermined, and you are taking in hand another State, we have resolved, as you heard, not to let you go until you give an account of all this."

"To that resolution," said Glaucon, "you may regard me as saying 'Agreed.'"
D: Refashioned:
"Wasn't I right about that, Adeimantus?"

"Yes," he said, "but being right in principle, like everything else, still requires explanation. 'Community' can mean many things. So please specify what kind of community you have in mind. We've been waiting for you to address the family life of your citizens -- how they'll have children, how they'll raise them, and what this sharing of women and children really looks like in practice. We believe that getting this right or wrong will have an enormous impact on the State, for better or worse. Since you've left this question unanswered and are already moving on to the next topic, we've resolved -- as you overheard -- not to let you proceed until you give a full account."

"Count me in on that resolution," said Glaucon.
D: Elegant: Adeimantus insists that 'community' can take many forms and demands Socrates specify how guardians will bear and raise children, since family arrangements will decisively shape the State. Glaucon seconds the demand.
D: Summary: Adeimantus presses Socrates to explain precisely what communal family life means for the guardians -- how children will be born and raised -- arguing that these arrangements will have paramount influence on the State's success or failure. Glaucon agrees.
D: Distilled: The interlocutors demand a detailed account of how communal child-rearing will work, insisting it is crucial to the State's welfare.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, Adeimantus, and Glaucon -- This passage sets up the three 'waves' of Book V: (1) equality of women, (2) community of families, (3) philosopher-kings -- Glaucon's agreement signals unanimous pressure on Socrates -- The concern about 'right or wrong management' anticipates the eugenics program Socrates will propose

D: Original:
And without more ado, said Thrasymachus, you may consider us all to be equally agreed.

I said, You know not what you are doing in thus assailing me: What an argument are you raising about the State! Just as I thought that I had finished, and was only too glad that I had laid this question to sleep, and was reflecting how fortunate I was in your acceptance of what I then said, you ask me to begin again at the very foundation, ignorant of what a hornet's nest of words you are stirring. Now I foresaw this gathering trouble, and avoided it.

For what purpose do you conceive that we have come here, said Thrasymachus, --to look for gold, or to hear discourse?
D: Narrative:
"And without more ado," said Thrasymachus, "you may consider us all to be equally agreed."

I said, "You know not what you are doing in thus assailing me: What an argument are you raising about the State! Just as I thought that I had finished, and was only too glad that I had laid this question to sleep, and was reflecting how fortunate I was in your acceptance of what I then said, you ask me to begin again at the very foundation, ignorant of what a hornet's nest of words you are stirring. Now I foresaw this gathering trouble, and avoided it."

"For what purpose do you conceive that we have come here," said Thrasymachus, "-- to look for gold, or to hear discourse?"
D: Refashioned:
"You can consider us all unanimously agreed," said Thrasymachus without hesitation.

"You have no idea what you're doing by pressing me on this," I said. "What an argument you're opening up about the State! Just when I thought I was finished -- grateful that I'd put this question to rest and relieved that you'd accepted what I said earlier -- you're asking me to start over from the foundations. You don't realize what a hornet's nest of controversy you're stirring up. I saw this trouble coming, which is exactly why I avoided it."

"What do you think we came here for?" said Thrasymachus. "To look for gold, or to hear philosophical discussion?"
D: Elegant: Thrasymachus confirms universal agreement that Socrates must address the topic. Socrates protests that they are reopening a hornet's nest he deliberately avoided. Thrasymachus retorts that they came for discourse, not gold.
D: Summary: When all interlocutors insist Socrates address communal families, he protests that they are stirring up a hornet's nest of controversy he intentionally sidestepped. Thrasymachus reminds him that philosophical discourse is exactly what they came for.
D: Distilled: Socrates warns his companions they are opening a hornet's nest, but Thrasymachus insists discourse is their purpose.
Notes: Speakers: Thrasymachus and Socrates -- Thrasymachus, the sophist from Chalcedon, was Socrates' main antagonist in Book I -- His reappearance here is notable; he has been largely silent since his defeat in Book I -- 'To look for gold, or to hear discourse' echoes the Greek philosophical tradition of valuing logos over material wealth -- The 'hornet's nest' metaphor anticipates the three controversial 'waves' Socrates must face

D: Original: Yes, but discourse should have a limit. Yes, Socrates, said Glaucon, and the whole of life is the only limit which wise men assign to the hearing of such discourses. But never mind about us; take heart yourself and answer the question in your own way: What sort of community of women and children is this which is to prevail among our guardians? and how shall we manage the period between birth and education, which seems to require the greatest care? Tell us how these things will be.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, but discourse should have a limit."

"Yes, Socrates," said Glaucon, "and the whole of life is the only limit which wise men assign to the hearing of such discourses. But never mind about us; take heart yourself and answer the question in your own way: What sort of community of women and children is this which is to prevail among our guardians? and how shall we manage the period between birth and education, which seems to require the greatest care? Tell us how these things will be."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, but there should be limits to discussion."

"Socrates," said Glaucon, "the only limit wise people set on hearing such discussions is the span of a lifetime. Don't worry about us -- gather your courage and answer the question however you see fit. What kind of community of women and children will exist among our guardians? And how should we handle the period between birth and education, which seems to demand the greatest care? Tell us how all this will work."
D: Elegant: Glaucon declares that a lifetime is the only proper limit for philosophical discourse and urges Socrates to explain the guardians' communal arrangements for women, children, and early education.
D: Summary: When Socrates suggests discourse should have limits, Glaucon counters that wise men devote their whole lives to such discussions and presses him to explain the guardians' system of shared families and child-rearing.
D: Distilled: Glaucon insists philosophy deserves a lifetime's attention and urges Socrates to detail the guardians' communal family system.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Glaucon's enthusiasm for unlimited philosophical inquiry contrasts with Socrates' reluctance -- The 'period between birth and education' anticipates the state-managed nursery system Socrates will describe -- This echoes Socrates' famous claim that 'the unexamined life is not worth living' (Apology 38a)

D: Original:
Yes, my simple friend, but the answer is the reverse of easy; many more doubts arise about this than about our previous conclusions. For the practicability of what is said may be doubted; and looked at in another point of view, whether the scheme, if ever so practicable, would be for the best, is also doubtful. Hence I feel a reluctance to approach the subject, lest our aspiration, my dear friend, should turn out to be a dream only.

Fear not, he replied, for your audience will not be hard upon you; they are not sceptical or hostile.

I said: My good friend, I suppose that you mean to encourage me by these words.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, my simple friend, but the answer is the reverse of easy; many more doubts arise about this than about our previous conclusions. For the practicability of what is said may be doubted; and looked at in another point of view, whether the scheme, if ever so practicable, would be for the best, is also doubtful. Hence I feel a reluctance to approach the subject, lest our aspiration, my dear friend, should turn out to be a dream only."

"Fear not," he replied, "for your audience will not be hard upon you; they are not sceptical or hostile."

I said: "My good friend, I suppose that you mean to encourage me by these words."
D: Refashioned:
"My friend, the answer is far from easy. This raises many more doubts than our previous conclusions. First, the practicability of the proposal can be questioned. Second, even if it were entirely practicable, whether it would actually be for the best is also uncertain. So I'm reluctant to approach the subject, for fear that our aspiration may turn out to be nothing more than a dream."

"Don't be afraid," Glaucon replied. "Your audience won't be hard on you. They're neither skeptical nor hostile."

"I suppose you mean to encourage me by saying that," I said.
D: Elegant: Socrates expresses reluctance -- the proposal raises doubts about both practicability and desirability, and may prove to be mere dreaming. Glaucon assures him the audience is sympathetic.
D: Summary: Socrates hesitates to address the communal family system, fearing it may be impractical or undesirable -- a mere dream. Glaucon reassures him that his audience is friendly and open-minded.
D: Distilled: Socrates fears his proposals about communal families may be impractical dreams, but Glaucon assures a sympathetic hearing.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Socrates' hesitation is partly dramatic and partly genuine philosophical caution -- The distinction between practicability and desirability becomes a recurring methodological theme in Book V -- 'Dream' versus reality anticipates the later epistemological discussion about opinion versus knowledge (5.80-5.88)

D: Original: Yes, he said. Then let me tell you that you are doing just the reverse; the encouragement which you offer would have been all very well had I myself believed that I knew what I was talking about: to declare the truth about matters of high interest which a man honours and loves among wise men who love him need occasion no fear or faltering in his mind; but to carry on an argument when you are yourself only a hesitating enquirer, which is my condition, is a dangerous and slippery thing; and the danger is not that I shall be laughed at (of which the fear would be childish), but that I shall miss the truth where I have most need to be sure of my footing, and drag my friends after me in my fall. And I pray Nemesis not to visit upon me the words which I am going to utter. For I do indeed believe that to be an involuntary homicide is a less crime than to be a deceiver about beauty or goodness or justice in the matter of laws. And that is a risk which I would rather run among enemies than among friends, and therefore you do well to encourage me.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said.

"Then let me tell you that you are doing just the reverse; the encouragement which you offer would have been all very well had I myself believed that I knew what I was talking about: to declare the truth about matters of high interest which a man honours and loves among wise men who love him need occasion no fear or faltering in his mind; but to carry on an argument when you are yourself only a hesitating enquirer, which is my condition, is a dangerous and slippery thing; and the danger is not that I shall be laughed at (of which the fear would be childish), but that I shall miss the truth where I have most need to be sure of my footing, and drag my friends after me in my fall. And I pray Nemesis not to visit upon me the words which I am going to utter. For I do indeed believe that to be an involuntary homicide is a less crime than to be a deceiver about beauty or goodness or justice in the matter of laws. And that is a risk which I would rather run among enemies than among friends, and therefore you do well to encourage me."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," he said.

"Then you're actually doing the opposite of encouraging me," I replied. "Your reassurance would be fine if I were confident I knew what I was talking about. A man who speaks truth on matters he honors and loves, among wise friends who love him, has nothing to fear. But arguing when you're only a hesitant inquirer yourself -- which is my position -- is dangerous and treacherous ground. The danger isn't that I'll be laughed at; that fear would be childish. The danger is that I'll miss the truth precisely where I need it most, and drag my friends down with me in the fall.

I pray that Nemesis won't punish me for what I'm about to say. I genuinely believe that involuntary manslaughter is a lesser crime than deceiving people about beauty, goodness, or justice when it comes to laws. And that's a risk I'd rather take among enemies than among friends -- so in that sense, yes, your encouragement is well-placed."
D: Elegant: Socrates argues that friendly encouragement actually increases the stakes: his real fear is not ridicule but misleading his friends on matters of justice and goodness. He considers intellectual deception worse than involuntary homicide and invokes Nemesis against his own potential errors.
D: Summary: Socrates explains that the danger of philosophical inquiry among friends is not mockery but leading them astray on questions of justice and law. He considers deceiving others about goodness to be a worse crime than involuntary killing.
D: Distilled: Socrates fears misleading his friends about justice more than he fears ridicule, considering intellectual deception worse than involuntary manslaughter.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Nemesis is the Greek goddess of retribution who punishes hubris -- The distinction between involuntary homicide and deliberate deception echoes the Socratic position that wrongdoing stems from ignorance (Protagoras, Meno) -- 'Hesitating enquirer' reflects Socratic irony and the method of aporia -- The passage foreshadows Socrates' controversial claims about philosopher-kings

D: Original:
Glaucon laughed and said: Well then, Socrates, in case you and your argument do us any serious injury you shall be acquitted beforehand of the and shall not be held to be a deceiver; take courage then and speak.

Well, I said, the law says that when a man is acquitted he is free from guilt, and what holds at law may hold in argument.

Then why should you mind? Well, I replied, I suppose that I must retrace my steps and say what I perhaps ought to have said before in the proper place. The part of the men has been played out, and now properly enough comes the turn of the women. Of them I will proceed to speak, and the more readily since I am invited by you.

For men born and educated like our citizens, the only way, in my opinion, of arriving at a right conclusion about the possession and use of women and children is to follow the path on which we originally started, when we said that the men were to be the guardians and watchdogs of the herd.

True. Let us further suppose the birth and education of our women to be subject to similar or nearly similar regulations; then we shall see whether the result accords with our design.
D: Narrative:
Glaucon laughed and said: "Well then, Socrates, in case you and your argument do us any serious injury you shall be acquitted beforehand of the homicide and shall not be held to be a deceiver; take courage then and speak."

"Well," I said, "the law says that when a man is acquitted he is free from guilt, and what holds at law may hold in argument."

"Then why should you mind?"

"Well," I replied, "I suppose that I must retrace my steps and say what I perhaps ought to have said before in the proper place. The part of the men has been played out, and now properly enough comes the turn of the women. Of them I will proceed to speak, and the more readily since I am invited by you.

For men born and educated like our citizens, the only way, in my opinion, of arriving at a right conclusion about the possession and use of women and children is to follow the path on which we originally started, when we said that the men were to be the guardians and watchdogs of the herd."

"True."

"Let us further suppose the birth and education of our women to be subject to similar or nearly similar regulations; then we shall see whether the result accords with our design."
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon laughed. "Well then, Socrates, if you and your argument do us any serious harm, consider yourself acquitted of the homicide in advance. You won't be held a deceiver. Take courage and speak."

"The law does say that an acquitted man is free from guilt," I said, "and what holds in law may hold in argument."

"Then what's stopping you?"

"Very well," I replied. "I suppose I must go back and address what I should have covered earlier, in its proper place. We've dealt with the men's role; now it's the women's turn. And I'll take this up all the more readily since you're urging me to do so.

For people born and educated as our citizens are, the only sound approach to the question of women and children is to follow the path we originally set out on -- when we said that the men were to be guardians and watchdogs of the community."

"True."

"Then let us suppose that the birth and education of women should follow the same or similar regulations as the men's. We'll see whether the result fits our design."
D: Elegant: Glaucon playfully acquits Socrates in advance. Socrates agrees to retrace his steps: since the men are guardians and watchdogs of the State, the question of women must follow the same foundational logic. He proposes giving women the same birth conditions and education as men, to test whether the results accord with the State's design.
D: Summary: After Glaucon grants him advance acquittal for any intellectual harm, Socrates agrees to address the role of women. He argues that since men serve as guardians, the only coherent approach is to apply the same or similar educational regulations to women and see if the results fit the State's design.
D: Distilled: Socrates proposes that women should receive the same education and training as male guardians to maintain consistency in the State's design.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- The 'guardians and watchdogs' metaphor was established in Books II-III -- 'The part of the men has been played out' refers to the guardian class system developed in Books II-IV -- This begins the first 'wave' of Book V: the equality of women -- The legal metaphor of acquittal plays on the earlier comparison of deception to homicide

D: Original: What do you mean? What I mean may be put into the form of a question, I said: Are dogs divided into hes and shes, or do they both share equally in hunting and in keeping watch and in the other duties of dogs? or do we entrust to the males the entire and exclusive care of the flocks, while we leave the females at home, under the idea that the bearing and suckling their puppies is labour enough for them?
D: Narrative:
"What do you mean?"

"What I mean may be put into the form of a question," I said: "Are dogs divided into hes and shes, or do they both share equally in hunting and in keeping watch and in the other duties of dogs? or do we entrust to the males the entire and exclusive care of the flocks, while we leave the females at home, under the idea that the bearing and suckling their puppies is labour enough for them?"
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean?"

"Let me put it as a question," I said. "With dogs, do we divide duties by sex, or do males and females share equally in hunting, guarding, and every other task? Or do we assign all the work of protecting the flocks to the males alone, while keeping the females at home on the assumption that bearing and nursing puppies is labor enough for them?"
D: Elegant: Socrates poses the analogy: do we divide dogs' duties by sex, or do males and females share in hunting and guarding alike? Or do we confine females to breeding while males do all the work?
D: Summary: Socrates introduces his argument for women's equality through an analogy with dogs: do we separate canine duties by sex, or do both male and female dogs share in hunting and guarding?
D: Distilled: Socrates uses the dog analogy to ask whether duties should be divided by sex or shared equally.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- The dog/guardian analogy was established in Book II (375a-376c) where Socrates compared ideal guardians to well-bred watchdogs -- This is a classic Socratic analogical argument: if female dogs can guard and hunt, so can female guardians -- The analogy deliberately challenges Athenian norms where women were confined to the household (oikos)

D: Original:
No, he said, they share alike; the only difference between them is that the males are stronger and the females weaker.

But can you use different animals for the same purpose, unless they are bred and fed in the same way?

You cannot. Then, if women are to have the same duties as men, they must have the same nurture and education?

Yes. The education which was assigned to the men was music and gymnastic. Yes.

Then women must be taught music and gymnastic and also the art of war, which they must practise like the men?

That is the inference, I suppose. I should rather expect, I said, that several of our proposals, if they are carried out, being unusual, may appear ridiculous.

No doubt of it. Yes, and the most ridiculous thing of all will be the sight of women naked in the palaestra, exercising with the men, especially when they are no longer young; they certainly will not be a vision of beauty, any more than the enthusiastic old men who in spite of wrinkles and ugliness continue to frequent the gymnasia.

Yes, indeed, he said: according to present notions the proposal would be thought ridiculous.

But then, I said, as we have determined to speak our minds, we must not fear the jests of the wits which will be directed against this sort of innovation; how they will talk of women's attainments both in music and gymnastic, and above all about their wearing armour and riding upon horseback!
D: Narrative:
"No," he said, "they share alike; the only difference between them is that the males are stronger and the females weaker."

"But can you use different animals for the same purpose, unless they are bred and fed in the same way?"

"You cannot."

"Then, if women are to have the same duties as men, they must have the same nurture and education?"

"Yes."

"The education which was assigned to the men was music and gymnastic."

"Yes."

"Then women must be taught music and gymnastic and also the art of war, which they must practise like the men?"

"That is the inference, I suppose."

"I should rather expect," I said, "that several of our proposals, if they are carried out, being unusual, may appear ridiculous."

"No doubt of it."

"Yes, and the most ridiculous thing of all will be the sight of women naked in the palaestra, exercising with the men, especially when they are no longer young; they certainly will not be a vision of beauty, any more than the enthusiastic old men who in spite of wrinkles and ugliness continue to frequent the gymnasia."

"Yes, indeed," he said: "according to present notions the proposal would be thought ridiculous."

"But then," I said, "as we have determined to speak our minds, we must not fear the jests of the wits which will be directed against this sort of innovation; how they will talk of women's attainments both in music and gymnastic, and above all about their wearing armour and riding upon horseback!"
D: Refashioned:
"No," Glaucon said, "they share duties alike. The only difference is that males are stronger and females weaker."

"But can you use different animals for the same purpose unless they're bred and trained the same way?"

"You can't."

"Then if women are to have the same duties as men, they must receive the same upbringing and education?"

"Yes."

"And the education we assigned to men was music and gymnastics."

"Yes."

"Then women must be taught music, gymnastics, and the art of war, practicing alongside the men?"

"That seems to follow."

"I should warn you," I said, "that several of these proposals, being so unconventional, will appear ridiculous."

"No doubt."

"And the most ridiculous sight of all will be women exercising naked in the gymnasium alongside men -- especially the older women. They won't exactly be a vision of beauty, any more than those enthusiastic old men who keep showing up at the gym despite their wrinkles."

"Yes," he said, "by current standards that would seem absurd."

"But since we've committed to speaking honestly," I said, "we can't let ourselves be intimidated by the mockery this will invite -- jokes about women in music and athletics, and above all, about women wearing armor and riding horses!"
D: Elegant: Since male and female dogs share duties and differ only in strength, the same logic applies to guardians: women must receive the same education in music, gymnastics, and warfare. Socrates acknowledges this will seem ridiculous -- especially women exercising naked in gymnasia -- but insists they must not be deterred by mockery.
D: Summary: Socrates argues from the dog analogy that since equal duties require equal training, women guardians must study music, gymnastics, and warfare alongside men. He acknowledges the proposal will seem ridiculous by current standards but insists on following the argument wherever it leads.
D: Distilled: Equal duties require equal education: women guardians must train in music, gymnastics, and war alongside men, regardless of how ridiculous it appears.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The palaestra was the Greek wrestling school where men exercised naked (gymnos, root of 'gymnastics') -- Greek athletics were performed nude; proposing this for women was genuinely shocking -- Music (mousike) in Greek education included literature, poetry, and intellectual training, not just musical performance -- The education of guardians in music and gymnastic was established in Books II-III

D: Original: Very true, he replied. Yet having begun we must go forward to the rough places of the law; at the same time begging of these gentlemen for once in their life to be serious. Not long ago, as we shall remind them, the Hellenes were of the opinion, which is still generally received among the barbarians, that the sight of a naked man was ridiculous and improper; and when first the Cretans and then the Lacedaemonians introduced the custom, the wits of that day might equally have ridiculed the innovation.
D: Narrative:
"Very true," he replied.

"Yet having begun we must go forward to the rough places of the law; at the same time begging of these gentlemen for once in their life to be serious. Not long ago, as we shall remind them, the Hellenes were of the opinion, which is still generally received among the barbarians, that the sight of a naked man was ridiculous and improper; and when first the Cretans and then the Lacedaemonians introduced the custom, the wits of that day might equally have ridiculed the innovation."
D: Refashioned:
"Very true," he replied.

"Yet having begun, we must press forward through the rough terrain of this argument. At the same time, let us ask our critics to be serious for once in their lives. Not long ago, the Greeks themselves believed -- as most non-Greek peoples still do -- that the sight of a naked man was ridiculous and improper. When the Cretans and then the Spartans first introduced the practice of male nudity in athletics, the comedians of that era could just as easily have mocked the innovation."
D: Elegant: Having begun, they must press forward despite ridicule. Socrates reminds his audience that Greek male nudity in athletics was itself once considered absurd -- the Cretans and Spartans introduced it against mockery, and custom eventually vindicated them.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that what seems ridiculous now may prove wise later, pointing out that Greek male athletic nudity was itself once considered shameful and absurd before being adopted through Cretan and Spartan example.
D: Distilled: What seems ridiculous in custom may prove wise in practice, as the once-mocked Greek adoption of male athletic nudity demonstrates.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The Cretans and Lacedaemonians (Spartans) were credited with introducing gymnastic nudity, probably in the 8th-7th century BCE -- Thucydides (1.6) also notes that athletic nudity was a relatively recent Greek innovation -- 'Barbarians' (barbaroi) meant non-Greek speakers, not necessarily uncivilized -- Spartan women notably did receive physical training, making Sparta a partial real-world precedent for Socrates' proposals

D: Original: No doubt. But when experience showed that to let all things be uncovered was far better than to cover them up, and the ludicrous effect to the outward eye vanished before the better principle which reason asserted, then the man was perceived to be a fool who directs the shafts of his ridicule at any other sight but that of folly and vice, or seriously inclines to weigh the beautiful by any other standard but that of the good.
D: Narrative: "No doubt. But when experience showed that to let all things be uncovered was far better than to cover them up, and the ludicrous effect to the outward eye vanished before the better principle which reason asserted, then the man was perceived to be a fool who directs the shafts of his ridicule at any other sight but that of folly and vice, or seriously inclines to weigh the beautiful by any other standard but that of the good."
D: Refashioned: "No doubt. But when experience demonstrated that exercising uncovered was far better than exercising covered up, the initial absurdity faded as reason revealed the deeper principle. At that point, anyone who directed ridicule at anything other than folly and vice was himself revealed as a fool -- just as anyone who measured beauty by any standard other than the good was shown to be misguided."
D: Elegant: When experience showed that uncovered exercise was superior, reason triumphed over mockery. The true fool is one who ridicules anything other than vice, or who measures beauty by any standard other than the good.
D: Summary: Experience vindicated the practice of athletic nudity, proving that reason outweighs initial ridicule. The real fool is one who mocks anything other than genuine vice, or who judges beauty by any standard other than goodness.
D: Distilled: Reason and experience outweigh mockery: true ridicule should target only vice, and true beauty is measured only by the good.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates -- This establishes a key Platonic aesthetic principle: the beautiful (to kalon) is identified with the good (to agathon) -- This principle recurs throughout the Republic and is central to Books VI-VII -- The argument that custom evolves through reason anticipates the progressive logic of the entire Book V

D: Original:
Very true, he replied. First, then, whether the question is to be put in jest or in earnest, let us come to an understanding about the nature of woman: Is she capable of sharing either wholly or partially in the actions of men, or not at all? And is the art of war one of those arts in which she can or can not share? That will be the best way of commencing the enquiry, and will probably lead to the fairest conclusion.

That will be much the best way. Shall we take the other side first and begin by arguing against ourselves; in this manner the adversary's position will not be undefended.
D: Narrative:
"Very true," he replied.

"First, then, whether the question is to be put in jest or in earnest, let us come to an understanding about the nature of woman: Is she capable of sharing either wholly or partially in the actions of men, or not at all? And is the art of war one of those arts in which she can or can not share? That will be the best way of commencing the enquiry, and will probably lead to the fairest conclusion."

"That will be much the best way."

"Shall we take the other side first and begin by arguing against ourselves; in this manner the adversary's position will not be undefended."
D: Refashioned:
"Very true," he replied.

"First, then -- whether we treat this seriously or playfully -- let us reach an understanding about the nature of women. Are they capable of sharing in men's activities fully, partially, or not at all? And specifically, is warfare one of those activities they can or cannot share in? This will be the best starting point and will likely lead to the fairest conclusion."

"That's by far the best approach."

"Should we begin by arguing against our own position? That way, the opposing case won't go unrepresented."
D: Elegant: Socrates proposes to investigate whether women can share in men's activities fully, partially, or not at all -- especially warfare. He suggests they first argue against their own position so the opposing view is fairly represented.
D: Summary: Socrates frames the central question: can women share in men's activities, including warfare? He proposes a fair inquiry by first arguing against their own position, ensuring the opposition is properly represented.
D: Distilled: The inquiry begins: can women share in men's work and warfare? Socrates proposes to steelman the opposing case first.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Socrates' method of arguing against himself anticipates modern steelmanning and dialectical method -- The question of women's capacity for warfare was radical in Athens, where women had no political or military role -- Sparta, by contrast, did train women physically, though not for direct combat -- This methodological fairness is characteristic of Socratic dialectic

D: Original: Why not? he said. Then let us put a speech into the mouths of our opponents. They will say: 'Socrates and Glaucon, no adversary need convict you, for you yourselves, at the first foundation of the State, admitted the principle that everybody was to do the one work suited to his own nature.' And certainly, if I am not mistaken, such an admission was made by us. 'And do not the natures of men and women differ very much indeed?' And we shall reply: Of course they do. Then we shall be asked, 'Whether the tasks assigned to men and to women should not be different, and such as are agreeable to their different natures?' Certainly they should. 'But if so, have you not fallen into a serious inconsistency in saying that men and women, whose natures are so entirely different, ought to perform the same actions?' --What defence will you make for us, my good Sir, against any one who offers these objections?
D: Narrative:
"Why not?" he said.

"Then let us put a speech into the mouths of our opponents. They will say: 'Socrates and Glaucon, no adversary need convict you, for you yourselves, at the first foundation of the State, admitted the principle that everybody was to do the one work suited to his own nature.' And certainly, if I am not mistaken, such an admission was made by us. 'And do not the natures of men and women differ very much indeed?' And we shall reply: Of course they do. 'Then we shall be asked, Whether the tasks assigned to men and to women should not be different, and such as are agreeable to their different natures?' Certainly they should. 'But if so, have you not fallen into a serious inconsistency in saying that men and women, whose natures are so entirely different, ought to perform the same actions?' -- What defence will you make for us, my good Sir, against any one who offers these objections?"
D: Refashioned:
"Why not?" he said.

"Then let us give voice to our opponents' argument. They would say: 'Socrates and Glaucon, you don't even need an adversary to refute you -- you've done it yourselves. When you first founded the State, you established the principle that everyone should do the one kind of work suited to their own nature.' And indeed, if I'm not mistaken, we did admit that. 'And don't the natures of men and women differ significantly?' We'd have to agree they do. 'Then shouldn't the tasks assigned to men and women be different, matching their different natures?' Of course. 'But then haven't you fallen into a serious contradiction by saying that men and women -- whose natures are so different -- should perform the same work?'

What defense would you offer, Glaucon, against someone who raises these objections?"
D: Elegant: Socrates voices the strongest objection: they themselves established that each person should do work suited to their nature. Since men's and women's natures differ, assigning them the same work appears self-contradictory. He challenges Glaucon to find a defense.
D: Summary: Socrates constructs the opponent's argument: since the State's founding principle is that everyone should do work suited to their nature, and men and women have different natures, isn't it contradictory to assign them the same tasks?
D: Distilled: The apparent contradiction: if different natures should have different work, and men and women differ in nature, how can they share the same duties?
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The principle of specialization ('one person, one work') was established in Book II (369e-370c) as the foundation of the just city -- This is a genuine logical challenge that Socrates must resolve, not merely a straw man -- The resolution will depend on distinguishing relevant from irrelevant natural differences -- This dialectical move exemplifies the elenchus method

D: Original:
That is not an easy question to answer when asked suddenly; and I shall and I do beg of you to draw out the case on our side.

These are the objections, Glaucon, and there are many others of a like kind, which I foresaw long ago; they made me afraid and reluctant to take in hand any law about the possession and nurture of women and children.

By Zeus, he said, the problem to be solved is anything but easy.

Why yes, I said, but the fact is that when a man is out of his depth, whether he has fallen into a little swimming bath or into mid-ocean, he has to swim all the same.

Very true. And must not we swim and try to reach the shore: we will hope that Arion's dolphin or some other miraculous help may save us?

I suppose so, he said. Well then, let us see if any way of escape can be found. We acknowledged --did we not? that different natures ought to have different pursuits, and that men's and women's natures are different. And now what are we saying? --that different natures ought to have the same pursuits, --this is the inconsistency which is charged upon us.

Precisely. Verily, Glaucon, I said, glorious is the power of the art of contradiction!

Why do you say so? Because I think that many a man falls into the practice against his will. When he thinks that he is reasoning he is really disputing, just because he cannot define and divide, and so know that of which he is speaking; and he will pursue a merely verbal opposition in the spirit of contention and not of fair discussion.

Yes, he replied, such is very often the case; but what has that to do with us and our argument?

A great deal; for there is certainly a danger of our getting unintentionally into a verbal opposition.

In what way? Why, we valiantly and pugnaciously insist upon the verbal truth, that different natures ought to have different pursuits, but we never considered at all what was the meaning of sameness or difference of nature, or why we distinguished them when we assigned different pursuits to different natures and the same to the same natures.

"I suppose so," he said.

"Well then, let us see if any way of escape can be found. We acknowledged -- did we not? -- that different natures ought to have different pursuits, and that men's and women's natures are different. And now what are we saying? -- that different natures ought to have the same pursuits -- this is the inconsistency which is charged upon us."

"Precisely."

"Verily, Glaucon," I said, "glorious is the power of the art of contradiction!"

"Why do you say so?"

"Because I think that many a man falls into the practice against his will. When he thinks that he is reasoning he is really disputing, just because he cannot define and divide, and so know that of which he is speaking; and he will pursue a merely verbal opposition in the spirit of contention and not of fair discussion."

"Yes," he replied, "such is very often the case; but what has that to do with us and our argument?"

"A great deal; for there is certainly a danger of our getting unintentionally into a verbal opposition."

"In what way?"

"Why, we valiantly and pugnaciously insist upon the verbal truth, that different natures ought to have different pursuits, but we never considered at all what was the meaning of sameness or difference of nature, or why we distinguished them when we assigned different pursuits to different natures and the same to the same natures."
D: Refashioned:
"That's not easy to answer on the spot. I beg you to make the case on our behalf."

"These objections, Glaucon, along with many others like them, are exactly what I foresaw long ago. They're what made me afraid and reluctant to propose any law about women and children."

"By Zeus," he said, "this is no easy problem."

"True," I said, "but when a man is out of his depth, whether he's fallen into a kiddie pool or the middle of the ocean, he has to swim regardless."

"Very true."

"So we must swim for shore. Perhaps Arion's dolphin or some other miraculous rescue will save us."

"I suppose so."

"Well then, let's look for a way through. We agreed that different natures should have different pursuits, and that men's and women's natures are different. Now we're saying that different natures should have the same pursuits. That's the contradiction we're charged with."

"Precisely."

"How powerful the art of contradiction is, Glaucon!"

"What do you mean?"

"Many people fall into mere contradiction without realizing it. They think they're reasoning when they're actually just arguing -- because they can't properly define and distinguish their terms. They pursue verbal disputes in a spirit of contention rather than genuine inquiry."

"Yes, that happens constantly. But what does it have to do with our argument?"

"Everything. There's a real danger that we've slipped into a merely verbal opposition ourselves."

"How so?"

"We stubbornly insisted on the principle that different natures should have different pursuits, but we never actually examined what we meant by 'sameness' or 'difference' of nature. We never asked which differences matter when assigning different pursuits to different natures."
D: Elegant: Glaucon is stumped. Socrates notes they must swim regardless of depth, then identifies the key error: they have been engaging in verbal contradiction without examining what 'difference in nature' actually means. The crucial unasked question is which differences in nature are relevant to assigning different pursuits.
D: Summary: Socrates identifies the root of the apparent contradiction: they insisted that different natures require different work but never defined what counts as a relevant 'difference in nature.' Many people fall into mere verbal disputes because they fail to define their terms precisely. The real question is which natural differences actually matter for assigning different roles.
D: Distilled: The apparent contradiction dissolves when we ask: which differences in nature are actually relevant to assigning different work?
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Arion was a legendary poet saved from drowning by a dolphin, as told by Herodotus (1.23-24) -- The distinction between eristic (contentious arguing) and dialectic (genuine inquiry) is central to Platonic philosophy, explored extensively in Euthydemus and Sophist -- The key philosophical move here -- distinguishing relevant from irrelevant differences -- is a foundational principle of logic and classification -- 'Define and divide' (diairesis) is the Platonic method of classification, developed further in Phaedrus and Sophist

D: Original: Why, no, he said, that was never considered by us. I said: Suppose that by way of illustration we were to ask the question whether there is not an opposition in nature between bald men and hairy men; and if this is admitted by us, then, if bald men are cobblers, we should forbid the hairy men to be cobblers, and conversely?
D: Narrative:
"Why, no," he said, "that was never considered by us."

I said: "Suppose that by way of illustration we were to ask the question whether there is not an opposition in nature between bald men and hairy men; and if this is admitted by us, then, if bald men are cobblers, we should forbid the hairy men to be cobblers, and conversely?"
D: Refashioned:
"No," he said, "we never considered that."

"Suppose, as an illustration," I said, "we asked whether there's a natural opposition between bald men and hairy men. If we admitted that there was, then -- following our principle -- if bald men are cobblers, we'd have to forbid hairy men from being cobblers, and vice versa."
D: Elegant: Socrates uses a reductio ad absurdum: if any natural difference justified different roles, then bald men and hairy men would have to practice different trades -- an obviously absurd conclusion.
D: Summary: Socrates illustrates the absurdity of treating every natural difference as relevant by asking whether bald men and hairy men should be forbidden from practicing the same trade, since they differ in nature.
D: Distilled: The bald-versus-hairy example shows that not every natural difference is relevant to role assignment.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is a classic reductio ad absurdum, a favorite Socratic technique -- The example is deliberately trivial to expose the logical error of treating all differences as relevant -- This move is crucial to the overall argument: biological sex differences (like baldness versus hairiness) may be similarly irrelevant to the capacity for guardianship

D: Original:
That would be a jest, he said. Yes, I said, a jest; and why? because we never meant when we constructed the State, that the opposition of natures should extend to every difference, but only to those differences which affected the pursuit in which the individual is engaged; we should have argued, for example, that a physician and one who is in mind a physician may be said to have the same nature.

True. Whereas the physician and the carpenter have different natures?
D: Narrative:
"That would be a jest," he said.

"Yes," I said, "a jest; and why? because we never meant when we constructed the State, that the opposition of natures should extend to every difference, but only to those differences which affected the pursuit in which the individual is engaged; we should have argued, for example, that a physician and one who is in mind a physician may be said to have the same nature."

"True."

"Whereas the physician and the carpenter have different natures?"

"Certainly."
D: Refashioned:
"That would be absurd," he said.

"Exactly -- absurd. And why? Because when we founded the State, we never intended the principle of 'different natures, different work' to apply to every conceivable difference between people. We meant it to apply only to differences relevant to the pursuit in question. For example, we'd say that a physician and someone with a physician's aptitude share the same nature."

"True."

"While a physician and a carpenter have genuinely different natures?"

"Certainly."
D: Elegant: The principle of specialization was never meant to apply to every difference -- only to differences relevant to the work in question. A physician and a natural-born healer share the same nature; a physician and a carpenter do not.
D: Summary: Socrates clarifies that the principle of different natures requiring different work applies only to differences relevant to the specific pursuit -- not to all differences. A physician and someone with healing aptitude share the same nature; a physician and carpenter do not.
D: Distilled: Only differences relevant to a specific pursuit matter for role assignment, not all natural differences.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This distinction between relevant and irrelevant differences is the philosophical key to the entire argument for women's equality -- The physician/carpenter example shows that 'nature' in this context means aptitude for a particular activity -- This anticipates modern arguments about the difference between sex (biological) and relevant occupational capacity

D: Original: Certainly. And if, I said, the male and female sex appear to differ in their fitness for any art or pursuit, we should say that such pursuit or art ought to be assigned to one or the other of them; but if the difference consists only in women bearing and men begetting children, this does not amount to a proof that a woman differs from a man in respect of the sort of education she should receive; and we shall therefore continue to maintain that our guardians and their wives ought to have the same pursuits.
D: Narrative: "And if," I said, "the male and female sex appear to differ in their fitness for any art or pursuit, we should say that such pursuit or art ought to be assigned to one or the other of them; but if the difference consists only in women bearing and men begetting children, this does not amount to a proof that a woman differs from a man in respect of the sort of education she should receive; and we shall therefore continue to maintain that our guardians and their wives ought to have the same pursuits."
D: Refashioned: "If men and women prove to differ in their aptitude for a specific art or pursuit," I said, "then we should assign that pursuit to one sex or the other. But if the only difference between them is that women bear children and men beget them, that alone doesn't prove women differ from men in the kind of education they should receive. We should therefore continue to maintain that our guardians and their wives ought to share the same pursuits."
D: Elegant: If men and women differ in aptitude for a specific pursuit, that pursuit should be assigned accordingly. But the mere biological difference of childbearing versus begetting does not prove women differ from men in their capacity for education or guardianship.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that the only proven difference between men and women -- that women bear children and men beget them -- is not relevant to their capacity for education or guardianship. Unless a specific aptitude difference can be shown, women should share men's pursuits.
D: Distilled: The biological difference of childbearing does not prove women are unfit for the same education and pursuits as men.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates -- This is the crux of Socrates' argument for women's equality: reproductive difference does not entail difference in intellectual or martial capacity -- This was a remarkably progressive position for 4th-century BCE Athens -- The argument anticipates modern feminist philosophy's distinction between sex and gender roles -- Compare Aristophanes' Assemblywomen (Ecclesiazusae), a comedy about women taking over government, performed around the same period

D: Original:
Very true, he said. Next, we shall ask our opponent how, in reference to any of the pursuits or arts of civic life, the nature of a woman differs from that of a man?

That will be quite fair. And perhaps he, like yourself, will reply that to give a sufficient answer on the instant is not easy; but after a little reflection there is no difficulty.
