Yes, perhaps. Suppose then that we invite him to accompany us in the argument, and then we may hope to show him that there is nothing peculiar in the constitution of women which would affect them in the administration of the State.
D: Narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"Next, we shall ask our opponent how, in reference to any of the pursuits or arts of civic life, the nature of a woman differs from that of a man?"

"That will be quite fair."

"And perhaps he, like yourself, will reply that to give a sufficient answer on the instant is not easy; but after a little reflection there is no difficulty."

"Yes, perhaps."

"Suppose then that we invite him to accompany us in the argument, and then we may hope to show him that there is nothing peculiar in the constitution of women which would affect them in the administration of the State."
D: Refashioned:
"Very true," he said.

"Next, let's challenge our opponent: in what specific civic art or pursuit does a woman's nature actually differ from a man's?"

"That's a fair question."

"And perhaps, like you, he'll say it's not easy to answer on the spot -- but with a little thought, it shouldn't be difficult."

"Perhaps."

"Then let's invite him to follow the argument with us. We may be able to show him that there is nothing in women's constitution that disqualifies them from participating in the administration of the State."
D: Elegant: Socrates challenges opponents to identify any specific civic pursuit where women's nature differs from men's, confident the argument will show nothing in women's constitution disqualifies them from governing.
D: Summary: Socrates challenges the opponent to name any specific civic art where women's nature genuinely differs from men's, confident that careful examination will reveal nothing in women's constitution that bars them from governing.
D: Distilled: Socrates challenges opponents to identify any civic pursuit where women's nature actually differs from men's in a relevant way.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The burden of proof is shifted to the opponent: they must identify a specific relevant difference, not just assert general inferiority -- 'Administration of the State' (politeia) is the same word as the dialogue's Greek title -- This methodological move -- demanding specific evidence rather than accepting general assumptions -- is characteristically Socratic

D: Original: By all means. Let us say to him: Come now, and we will ask you a question: --when you spoke of a nature gifted or not gifted in any respect, did you mean to say that one man will acquire a thing easily, another with difficulty; a little learning will lead the one to discover a great deal; whereas the other, after much study and application, no sooner learns than he forgets; or again, did you mean, that the one has a body which is a good servant to his mind, while the body of the other is a hindrance to him?-would not these be the sort of differences which distinguish the man gifted by nature from the one who is ungifted?
D: Narrative:
"By all means."

"Let us say to him: Come now, and we will ask you a question: -- when you spoke of a nature gifted or not gifted in any respect, did you mean to say that one man will acquire a thing easily, another with difficulty; a little learning will lead the one to discover a great deal; whereas the other, after much study and application, no sooner learns than he forgets; or again, did you mean, that the one has a body which is a good servant to his mind, while the body of the other is a hindrance to him? -- would not these be the sort of differences which distinguish the man gifted by nature from the one who is ungifted?"
D: Refashioned:
"By all means."

"Let us ask him this: When you talk about someone being naturally gifted or ungifted for something, what do you mean? Do you mean that one person picks things up easily while another struggles; that a little learning leads one to great discoveries while the other forgets everything despite long study? Or do you mean that one person's body serves their mind well while the other's body holds them back? Aren't these the kinds of differences that distinguish natural talent from its absence?"
D: Elegant: Socrates defines natural aptitude: ease of learning, retention of knowledge, and having a body that serves rather than hinders the mind. These are the relevant criteria for 'nature,' not sex.
D: Summary: Socrates defines what it means to be naturally gifted: learning easily, retaining knowledge, and having a body that supports rather than hinders mental activity. These are the relevant criteria for determining who is suited for a pursuit.
D: Distilled: Natural aptitude means ease of learning, good retention, and a body that serves the mind -- not biological sex.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This definition of natural aptitude focuses on cognitive and practical criteria, deliberately excluding sex as a category -- The body-mind relationship here anticipates later discussions in Book VII about the soul's education -- 'A body which is a good servant to his mind' reflects the Greek ideal of physical-intellectual harmony (kalokagathia)

D: Original: No one will deny that. And can you mention any pursuit of mankind in which the male sex has not all these gifts and qualities in a higher degree than the female? Need I waste time in speaking of the art of weaving, and the management of pancakes and preserves, in which womankind does really appear to be great, and in which for her to be beaten by a man is of all things the most absurd?
D: Narrative:
"No one will deny that."

"And can you mention any pursuit of mankind in which the male sex has not all these gifts and qualities in a higher degree than the female? Need I waste time in speaking of the art of weaving, and the management of pancakes and preserves, in which womankind does really appear to be great, and in which for her to be beaten by a man is of all things the most absurd?"
D: Refashioned:
"No one would deny that."

"And can you name any human pursuit in which men don't generally surpass women in these aptitudes? I won't waste time discussing weaving or cooking, where women do genuinely excel and where it would be absurd for a man to outperform them."
D: Elegant: Socrates concedes that men generally surpass women in degree of aptitude across most pursuits, while acknowledging women's superiority in domestic arts like weaving and cooking -- though he treats these as trivial.
D: Summary: Socrates concedes that men generally exceed women in aptitude across most pursuits, though he acknowledges women's superiority in domestic arts like weaving and cooking, dismissing these as unworthy of extended discussion.
D: Distilled: Men generally surpass women in degree of aptitude, though women excel in domestic arts -- but this difference of degree does not justify exclusion.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The concession about male superiority in degree is important: Socrates argues for equal opportunity despite unequal average ability -- 'Pancakes and preserves' reflects the Greek domestic sphere assigned to women -- The humor here is intentional -- Socrates is both acknowledging and gently mocking gendered assumptions -- This nuanced position distinguishes Socrates from both modern egalitarianism and ancient patriarchy

D: Original:
You are quite right, he replied, in maintaining the general inferiority of the female sex: although many women are in many things superior to many men, yet on the whole what you say is true.

And if so, my friend, I said, there is no special faculty of administration in a state which a woman has because she is a woman, or which a man has by virtue of his sex, but the gifts of nature are alike diffused in both; all the pursuits of men are the pursuits of women also, but in all of them a woman is inferior to a man.

Very true. Then are we to impose all our enactments on men and none of them on women?

That will never do. One woman has a gift of healing, another not; one is a musician, and another has no music in her nature?

Very true. And one woman has a turn for gymnastic and military exercises, and another is unwarlike and hates gymnastics?

Certainly. And one woman is a philosopher, and another is an enemy of philosophy; one has spirit, and another is without spirit?

That is also true. Then one woman will have the temper of a guardian, and another not. Was not the selection of the male guardians determined by differences of this sort?

Yes. Men and women alike possess the qualities which make a guardian; they differ only in their comparative strength or weakness.

Obviously. And those women who have such qualities are to be selected as the companions and colleagues of men who have similar qualities and whom they resemble in capacity and in character?

Very true. And ought not the same natures to have the same pursuits?

They ought. Then, as we were saying before, there is nothing unnatural in assigning music and gymnastic to the wives of the guardians --to that point we come round again.

Certainly not. The law which we then enacted was agreeable to nature, and therefore not an impossibility or mere aspiration; and the contrary practice, which prevails at present, is in reality a violation of nature.
D: Narrative:
"You are quite right," he replied, "in maintaining the general inferiority of the female sex: although many women are in many things superior to many men, yet on the whole what you say is true."

"And if so, my friend," I said, "there is no special faculty of administration in a state which a woman has because she is a woman, or which a man has by virtue of his sex, but the gifts of nature are alike diffused in both; all the pursuits of men are the pursuits of women also, but in all of them a woman is inferior to a man."

"Very true."

"Then are we to impose all our enactments on men and none of them on women?"

"That will never do."

"One woman has a gift of healing, another not; one is a musician, and another has no music in her nature?"

"Very true."

"And one woman has a turn for gymnastic and military exercises, and another is unwarlike and hates gymnastics?"

"Certainly."

"And one woman is a philosopher, and another is an enemy of philosophy; one has spirit, and another is without spirit?"

"That is also true."

"Then one woman will have the temper of a guardian, and another not. Was not the selection of the male guardians determined by differences of this sort?"

"Yes."

"Men and women alike possess the qualities which make a guardian; they differ only in their comparative strength or weakness."

"Obviously."

"And those women who have such qualities are to be selected as the companions and colleagues of men who have similar qualities and whom they resemble in capacity and in character?"

"Very true."

"And ought not the same natures to have the same pursuits?"

"They ought."

"Then, as we were saying before, there is nothing unnatural in assigning music and gymnastic to the wives of the guardians -- to that point we come round again."

"Certainly not."

"The law which we then enacted was agreeable to nature, and therefore not an impossibility or mere aspiration; and the contrary practice, which prevails at present, is in reality a violation of nature."
D: Refashioned:
"You're right," Glaucon replied, "about the general inferiority of women in degree -- though many individual women are superior to many individual men in various things. On the whole, your point holds."

"Then there is no governing ability that a woman possesses because she is a woman, or that a man possesses by virtue of being a man," I said. "Natural gifts are distributed across both sexes. All of men's pursuits are also women's pursuits, though women are generally weaker in each."

"Very true."

"So should we impose all our laws on men and none on women?"

"Of course not."

"One woman has a gift for healing, another does not. One is musical, another has no music in her."

"True."

"One woman is inclined toward athletics and military training, while another is unwarlike and hates exercise."

"Certainly."

"One woman is a philosopher; another despises philosophy. One has spirit; another lacks it."

"Also true."

"So some women will have the temperament of a guardian, and others won't. Weren't the male guardians selected on exactly this basis?"

"Yes."

"Then men and women alike possess the qualities that make a guardian. They differ only in relative strength."

"Obviously."

"Women with these qualities should be selected as companions and colleagues of men with similar qualities, whom they resemble in capacity and character?"

"True."

"And shouldn't the same natures pursue the same activities?"

"They should."

"Then there is nothing unnatural about assigning music and gymnastics to the wives of guardians. We've come full circle on this point."

"Nothing unnatural at all."

"The law we enacted is in harmony with nature -- not an impossibility or wishful thinking. The current practice of excluding women is actually the violation of nature."
D: Elegant: No governing ability belongs to one sex by nature. Natural gifts are distributed across both sexes; individuals of both sexes vary in aptitude for healing, music, athletics, philosophy, and spiritedness. Women with guardian temperaments should be selected as colleagues of similarly qualified men. The current exclusion of women, not their inclusion, violates nature.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes that no political capacity belongs inherently to either sex. Natural talents are distributed across both, with individual variation within each sex. Women with guardian temperaments should serve alongside similarly qualified men. The current practice of excluding women from public life is the true violation of nature.
D: Distilled: Natural gifts are distributed across both sexes; excluding qualified women from guardianship violates nature, while including them fulfills it.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the culmination of the first 'wave' argument -- The key distinction is between average group differences (women generally weaker) and individual variation (some women are stronger/wiser than some men) -- 'The contrary practice is a violation of nature' is a radical inversion of conventional Greek thinking -- Compare Aristotle's opposing view in Politics 1.13, where he argues women are naturally subordinate -- The phrase 'companions and colleagues' suggests genuine partnership, not subordination

D: Original:
That appears to be true. We had to consider, first, whether our proposals were possible, and secondly whether they were the most beneficial?

Yes. And the possibility has been acknowledged? Yes. The very great benefit has next to be established? Quite so. You will admit that the same education which makes a man a good guardian will make a woman a good guardian; for their original nature is the same?

Yes. I should like to ask you a question. What is it? Would you say that all men are equal in excellence, or is one man better than another?

The latter. And in the commonwealth which we were founding do you conceive the guardians who have been brought up on our model system to be more perfect men, or the cobblers whose education has been cobbling?

What a ridiculous question! You have answered me, I replied: Well, and may we not further say that our guardians are the best of our citizens?

By far the best. And will not their wives be the best women? Yes, by far the best. And can there be anything better for the interests of the State than that the men and women of a State should be as good as possible?

There can be nothing better. And this is what the arts of music and gymnastic, when present in such manner as we have described, will accomplish?

Certainly. Then we have made an enactment not only possible but in the highest degree beneficial to the State?
D: Narrative:
"We had to consider, first, whether our proposals were possible, and secondly whether they were the most beneficial?"

"Yes."

"And the possibility has been acknowledged?"

"Yes."

"The very great benefit has next to be established?"

"Quite so."

"You will admit that the same education which makes a man a good guardian will make a woman a good guardian; for their original nature is the same?"

"Yes."

"I should like to ask you a question."

"What is it?"

"Would you say that all men are equal in excellence, or is one man better than another?"

"The latter."

"And in the commonwealth which we were founding do you conceive the guardians who have been brought up on our model system to be more perfect men, or the cobblers whose education has been cobbling?"

"What a ridiculous question!"

"You have answered me," I replied: "Well, and may we not further say that our guardians are the best of our citizens?"

"By far the best."

"And will not their wives be the best women?"

"Yes, by far the best."

"And can there be anything better for the interests of the State than that the men and women of a State should be as good as possible?"

"There can be nothing better."

"And this is what the arts of music and gymnastic, when present in such manner as we have described, will accomplish?"

"Certainly."

"Then we have made an enactment not only possible but in the highest degree beneficial to the State?"
D: Refashioned:
"We needed to establish two things: first, whether our proposals are possible, and second, whether they are maximally beneficial."

"Yes."

"We've established possibility. Now we must demonstrate the great benefit."

"Agreed."

"You'd admit that the same education that makes a man a good guardian will make a woman a good guardian, since their fundamental nature is the same?"

"Yes."

"Let me ask you something. Are all men equal in excellence, or are some better than others?"

"Some are better."

"In the State we're founding, are the guardians raised under our educational system more excellent, or the cobblers whose education has been cobbling?"

"What a ridiculous question!"

"You've answered it. So our guardians are the best citizens?"

"By far."

"And their wives will be the best women?"

"By far the best."

"And is there anything better for a State than having its men and women be as excellent as possible?"

"Nothing."

"And this is exactly what our program of music and gymnastics will achieve?"

"Certainly."

"Then our law is not only possible but supremely beneficial to the State."
D: Elegant: Having established possibility, Socrates proves benefit: the same education produces excellent guardians regardless of sex. The best-educated guardians will be the best citizens, and having the best possible men and women serves the State's highest interest. The law is both possible and supremely beneficial.
D: Summary: Socrates completes the argument for women's equality by establishing its benefit: equal education produces excellent guardians of both sexes, and the State's highest interest is served by having its men and women be as good as possible.
D: Distilled: Equal education for women guardians is not only possible but maximally beneficial, producing the best possible citizens of both sexes.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The two-step method (possibility then benefit) structures Book V's argumentation -- The cobbler comparison recalls Book II's division of labor -- The conclusion that current practice violates nature while equal education fulfills it completes the first 'wave' of Book V -- This sets up the transition to the second wave: community of wives and children

D: Original: True. Then let the wives of our guardians strip, for their virtue will be their robe, and let them share in the toils of war and the defence of their country; only in the distribution of labours the lighter are to be assigned to the women, who are the weaker natures, but in other respects their duties are to be the same. And as for the man who laughs at naked women exercising their bodies from the best of motives, in his laughter he is plucking
D: Narrative: "True. Then let the wives of our guardians strip, for their virtue will be their robe, and let them share in the toils of war and the defence of their country; only in the distribution of labours the lighter are to be assigned to the women, who are the weaker natures, but in other respects their duties are to be the same. And as for the man who laughs at naked women exercising their bodies from the best of motives, in his laughter he is plucking"
D: Refashioned: "Then let the wives of our guardians train unclothed, for their virtue will be their garment. Let them share in the hardships of war and the defense of their country. In distributing tasks, the lighter duties should go to women, who are physically weaker, but in all other respects their responsibilities are to be the same. And the man who laughs at women exercising naked for the noblest reasons is, in his laughter, plucking --"
D: Elegant: Women guardians should train and fight alongside men, clothed in virtue rather than fabric. Lighter physical tasks may be assigned to women given their lesser strength, but duties are otherwise identical. Anyone who mocks women exercising for noble purposes is a fool.
D: Summary: Socrates declares that women guardians should exercise and serve in war alongside men, with virtue as their covering. Lighter tasks may accommodate physical differences, but duties are otherwise equal. Mockery of women training for noble ends is foolish.
D: Distilled: Women guardians will share in warfare and training, with virtue as their robe and only lighter physical tasks as concession to lesser strength.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates -- 'Their virtue will be their robe' is one of the most famous lines from Book V -- The sentence is cut off mid-thought; it continues in 5.25 ('a fruit of unripe wisdom') -- The accommodation for physical strength differences shows Socrates' position is equal opportunity, not identical treatment -- This concludes the first wave with a practical policy statement

D: Original:
A fruit of unripe wisdom, and he himself is ignorant of what he is laughing at, or what he is about; --for that is, and ever will be, the best of sayings, That the useful is the noble and the hurtful is the base.

Very true. Here, then, is one difficulty in our law about women, which we may say that we have now escaped; the wave has not swallowed us up alive for enacting that the guardians of either sex should have all their pursuits in common; to the utility and also to the possibility of this arrangement the consistency of the argument with itself bears witness.

Yes, that was a mighty wave which you have escaped. Yes, I said, but a greater is coming; you will of this when you see the next.

Go on; let me see. The law, I said, which is the sequel of this and of all that has preceded, is to the following effect, --'that the wives of our guardians are to be common, and their children are to be common, and no parent is to know his own child, nor any child his parent.'

Yes, he said, that is a much greater wave than the other; and the possibility as well as the utility of such a law are far more questionable.

I do not think, I said, that there can be any dispute about the very great utility of having wives and children in common; the possibility is quite another matter, and will be very much disputed.

I think that a good many doubts may be raised about both.

You imply that the two questions must be combined, I replied. Now I meant that you should admit the utility; and in this way, as I thought; I should escape from one of them, and then there would remain only the possibility.

But that little attempt is detected, and therefore you will please to give a defence of both.
D: Narrative:
"A fruit of unripe wisdom, and he himself is ignorant of what he is laughing at, or what he is about; -- for that is, and ever will be, the best of sayings, That the useful is the noble and the hurtful is the base."

"Very true."

"Here, then, is one difficulty in our law about women, which we may say that we have now escaped; the wave has not swallowed us up alive for enacting that the guardians of either sex should have all their pursuits in common; to the utility and also to the possibility of this arrangement the consistency of the argument with itself bears witness."

"Yes, that was a mighty wave which you have escaped."

"Yes," I said, "but a greater is coming; you will not think much of this when you see the next."

"Go on; let me see."

"The law," I said, "which is the sequel of this and of all that has preceded, is to the following effect, -- 'that the wives of our guardians are to be common, and their children are to be common, and no parent is to know his own child, nor any child his parent.'"

"Yes," he said, "that is a much greater wave than the other; and the possibility as well as the utility of such a law are far more questionable."

"I do not think," I said, "that there can be any dispute about the very great utility of having wives and children in common; the possibility is quite another matter, and will be very much disputed."

"I think that a good many doubts may be raised about both."

"You imply that the two questions must be combined," I replied. "Now I meant that you should admit the utility; and in this way, as I thought, I should escape from one of them, and then there would remain only the possibility."

"But that little attempt is detected, and therefore you will please to give a defence of both."
D: Refashioned:
"-- a fruit of unripe wisdom. He doesn't even know what he's laughing at or why. For this is, and always will be, the best of maxims: the useful is the noble, and the harmful is the base."

"Very true."

"So we've survived the first difficulty in our law about women. The wave hasn't swallowed us for declaring that guardians of both sexes should share all pursuits in common. The argument's internal consistency confirms both the utility and possibility of the arrangement."

"That was indeed a mighty wave you escaped."

"Yes, but a greater one is coming. You won't think much of this first wave when you see the next."

"Go on -- let me see it."

"The next law, which follows from everything we've established, is this: the wives of our guardians are to be held in common, their children are to be held in common, and no parent is to know their own child, nor any child their parent."

"That is indeed a much greater wave! Both its possibility and utility are far more questionable."

"I don't think the utility of communal wives and children can seriously be disputed," I said. "The possibility is another matter entirely and will be hotly debated."

"I think serious doubts can be raised about both."

"You're insisting I address both questions together," I replied. "I was hoping you'd concede the utility so I'd only have to defend the possibility."

"Nice try -- but that little maneuver has been detected. You'll need to defend both."
D: Elegant: The first wave -- equality of women -- is survived. Now comes the greater second wave: wives, children, and families held entirely in common, with no parent knowing their own child. Socrates claims the utility is obvious but the possibility debatable. Glaucon insists he must defend both.
D: Summary: Having survived the first wave (women's equality), Socrates introduces the second and more controversial wave: complete community of wives and children, with no parent knowing their own child. He tries to separate utility from possibility, but Glaucon demands he defend both claims.
D: Distilled: The second wave proposes abolishing the family: wives and children held in common, with no parent-child recognition.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'The useful is the noble and the hurtful is the base' connects the Greek concepts of to kalon (noble/beautiful) and to agathon (good) through utility -- The 'three waves' metaphor structures Book V: (1) women's equality, (2) community of families, (3) philosopher-kings -- The communal family proposal was influenced by Spartan practices but goes far beyond them -- This proposal was controversial even in antiquity; Aristotle criticizes it extensively in Politics II.1-5

D: Original: Well, I said, I submit to my fate. Yet grant me a little favour: let me feast my mind with the dream as day dreamers are in the habit of feasting themselves when they are walking alone; for before they have discovered any means of effecting their wishes --that is a matter which never troubles them --they would rather not tire themselves by thinking about possibilities; but assuming that what they desire is already granted to them, they proceed with their plan, and delight in detailing what they mean to do when their wish has come true --that is a way which they have of not doing much good to a capacity which was never good for much. Now I myself am beginning to lose heart, and I should like, with your permission, to pass over the question of possibility at present. Assuming therefore the possibility of the proposal, I shall now proceed to enquire how the rulers will carry out these arrangements, and I shall demonstrate that our plan, if executed, will be of the greatest benefit to the State and to the guardians. First of all, then, if you have no objection, I will endeavour with your help to consider the advantages of the measure; and hereafter the question of possibility.
D: Narrative: "Well," I said, "I submit to my fate. Yet grant me a little favour: let me feast my mind with the dream as day dreamers are in the habit of feasting themselves when they are walking alone; for before they have discovered any means of effecting their wishes -- that is a matter which never troubles them -- they would rather not tire themselves by thinking about possibilities; but assuming that what they desire is already granted to them, they proceed with their plan, and delight in detailing what they mean to do when their wish has come true -- that is a way which they have of not doing much good to a capacity which was never good for much. Now I myself am beginning to lose heart, and I should like, with your permission, to pass over the question of possibility at present. Assuming therefore the possibility of the proposal, I shall now proceed to enquire how the rulers will carry out these arrangements, and I shall demonstrate that our plan, if executed, will be of the greatest benefit to the State and to the guardians. First of all, then, if you have no objection, I will endeavour with your help to consider the advantages of the measure; and hereafter the question of possibility."
D: Refashioned:
"Very well, I submit. But grant me one favor: let me indulge in the dream the way daydreamers do when walking alone. They never bother figuring out how to achieve their wishes -- that's a concern that doesn't trouble them. They simply assume their desire is already fulfilled and proceed to happily detail what they'll do once it comes true. It's a habit that doesn't do much good for minds that weren't good for much to begin with.

I'm beginning to lose heart myself, so with your permission, I'd like to set aside the question of possibility for now. Assuming the proposal is possible, I'll examine how the rulers would carry out these arrangements and demonstrate that the plan, if executed, would bring the greatest benefit to the State and its guardians. Let me first address the advantages, and we'll tackle possibility afterward."
D: Elegant: Socrates asks permission to assume possibility for now and focus on benefits -- like a daydreamer who skips over practical obstacles to envision ideal outcomes. He will show how rulers would implement the communal system and why it would benefit the State.
D: Summary: Socrates asks to temporarily set aside the question of whether communal families are possible and instead demonstrate their benefits, comparing himself to a daydreamer who assumes his wish is granted and works out the details from there.
D: Distilled: Socrates sets aside the question of possibility to first demonstrate the benefits of communal families, reasoning like a daydreamer.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The daydreamer metaphor is self-deprecating but also methodologically deliberate: Socrates separates the normative question (is this good?) from the practical question (is this possible?) -- This methodological move anticipates the later discussion of ideals versus reality (5.70-5.71) -- The question of possibility will eventually be addressed through the philosopher-king proposal (the third wave)

D: Original: I have no objection; proceed. First, I think that if our rulers and their auxiliaries are to be worthy of the name which they bear, there must be willingness to obey in the one and the power of command in the other; the guardians must themselves obey the laws, and they must also imitate the spirit of them in any details which are entrusted to their care.
D: Narrative:
"I have no objection; proceed."

"First, I think that if our rulers and their auxiliaries are to be worthy of the name which they bear, there must be willingness to obey in the one and the power of command in the other; the guardians must themselves obey the laws, and they must also imitate the spirit of them in any details which are entrusted to their care."
D: Refashioned:
"No objection. Proceed."

"First, if our rulers and their auxiliaries are to deserve their titles, the auxiliaries must be willing to obey and the rulers must have the capacity to command. The guardians must obey the laws themselves, and in any matters entrusted to their discretion, they must act in the spirit of those laws."
D: Elegant: Rulers must have the power to command and auxiliaries the willingness to obey. Guardians must both follow the laws and embody their spirit in discretionary matters.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes the foundational principle for implementing communal families: rulers must command and auxiliaries must obey, with all guardians both following the laws and embodying their spirit.
D: Distilled: The communal system requires rulers who command wisely and auxiliaries who obey willingly, all acting in the spirit of the laws.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The distinction between rulers (archontes) and auxiliaries (epikouroi) was established in Book III -- Rulers are the philosopher-guardians; auxiliaries are the warrior class -- 'Imitate the spirit' of the laws suggests principled discretion rather than rigid rule-following -- This sets up the need for the 'noble lie' about the marriage lottery

D: Original: That is right, he said. You, I said, who are their legislator, having selected the men, will now select the women and give them to them; --they must be as far as possible of like natures with them; and they must live in common houses and meet at common meals, None of them will have anything specially his or her own; they will be together, and will be brought up together, and will associate at gymnastic exercises. And so they will be drawn by a necessity of their natures to have intercourse with each other --necessity is not too strong a word, I think?
D: Narrative:
"That is right," he said.

"You," I said, "who are their legislator, having selected the men, will now select the women and give them to them; -- they must be as far as possible of like natures with them; and they must live in common houses and meet at common meals. None of them will have anything specially his or her own; they will be together, and will be brought up together, and will associate at gymnastic exercises. And so they will be drawn by a necessity of their natures to have intercourse with each other -- necessity is not too strong a word, I think?"
D: Refashioned:
"That's right," he said.

"You, as legislator, have already selected the men. Now you'll select women of similar natures and pair them together. They'll live in common houses and eat at common meals. No one will have private property. They'll live together, train together, and exercise together. And naturally, being together constantly, they'll be drawn by the necessity of their natures into sexual relationships -- and I don't think 'necessity' is too strong a word here."
D: Elegant: The legislator selects men and women of like natures to live together in common houses with common meals, no private property, and shared training. Their constant proximity will naturally lead to sexual unions.
D: Summary: Socrates outlines the communal living arrangements: men and women of similar natures selected to live in common houses, share meals, own nothing privately, and train together. Their close association will naturally lead to sexual relationships.
D: Distilled: Guardians of both sexes will live communally with shared housing, meals, and training, naturally drawn into sexual unions by constant proximity.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Common meals (syssitia) were a real Spartan institution -- The abolition of private property for guardians was established in Book III (416d-417b) -- 'Necessity of their natures' (anagke) acknowledges eros as a natural force that must be channeled, not suppressed -- The legislator's role in pairing partners anticipates the regulated breeding program

D: Original:
Yes, he said; --necessity, not geometrical, but another sort of necessity which lovers know, and which is far more convincing and constraining to the mass of mankind.

True, I said; and this, Glaucon, like all the rest, must proceed after an orderly fashion; in a city of the blessed, licentiousness is an unholy thing which the rulers will forbid.

Yes, he said, and it ought not to be permitted. Then clearly the next thing will be to make matrimony sacred in the highest degree, and what is most beneficial will be deemed sacred?

Exactly. And how can marriages be made most beneficial? --that is a question which I put to you, because I see in your house dogs for hunting, and of the nobler sort of birds not a few. Now, I beseech you, do tell me, have you ever attended to their pairing and breeding?

In what particulars? Why, in the first place, although they are all of a good sort, are not some better than others?

True. And do you breed from them all indifferently, or do you take care to breed from the best only?

From the best. And do you take the oldest or the youngest, or only those of ripe age?

I choose only those of ripe age. And if care was not taken in the breeding, your dogs and birds would greatly deteriorate?

Certainly. And the same of horses and animals in general? Undoubtedly. Good heavens! my dear friend, I said, what consummate skill will our rulers need if the same principle holds of the human species!

Certainly, the same principle holds; but why does this involve any particular skill?

Because, I said, our rulers will often have to practise upon the body corporate with medicines. Now you know that when patients do not require medicines, but have only to be put under a regimen, the inferior sort of practitioner is deemed to be good enough; but when medicine has to be given, then the doctor should be more of a man.

That is quite true, he said; but to what are you alluding?
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said; "-- necessity, not geometrical, but another sort of necessity which lovers know, and which is far more convincing and constraining to the mass of mankind."

"True," I said; "and this, Glaucon, like all the rest, must proceed after an orderly fashion; in a city of the blessed, licentiousness is an unholy thing which the rulers will forbid."

"Yes," he said, "and it ought not to be permitted."

"Then clearly the next thing will be to make matrimony sacred in the highest degree, and what is most beneficial will be deemed sacred?"

"Exactly."

"And how can marriages be made most beneficial? -- that is a question which I put to you, because I see in your house dogs for hunting, and of the nobler sort of birds not a few. Now, I beseech you, do tell me, have you ever attended to their pairing and breeding?"

"In what particulars?"

"Why, in the first place, although they are all of a good sort, are not some better than others?"

"True."

"And do you breed from them all indifferently, or do you take care to breed from the best only?"

"From the best."

"And do you take the oldest or the youngest, or only those of ripe age?"

"I choose only those of ripe age."

"And if care was not taken in the breeding, your dogs and birds would greatly deteriorate?"

"Certainly."

"And the same of horses and animals in general?"

"Undoubtedly."

"Good heavens! my dear friend," I said, "what consummate skill will our rulers need if the same principle holds of the human species!"

"Certainly, the same principle holds; but why does this involve any particular skill?"

"Because," I said, "our rulers will often have to practise upon the body corporate with medicines. Now you know that when patients do not require medicines, but have only to be put under a regimen, the inferior sort of practitioner is deemed to be good enough; but when medicine has to be given, then the doctor should be more of a man."

"That is quite true," he said; "but to what are you alluding?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes -- necessity," he said, "but not the geometric kind. This is the kind of necessity that lovers understand, and it's far more compelling to most people than any mathematical proof."

"True," I said. "And this natural attraction, Glaucon, must be regulated in an orderly fashion. In a city of the blessed, licentiousness is unholy and the rulers will forbid it."

"As they should."

"So the next step is to make marriage sacred to the highest degree. And what is most beneficial will be deemed most sacred?"

"Exactly."

"How can marriages be made most beneficial? I ask you this, Glaucon, because I know you keep hunting dogs and fine birds in your household. Tell me -- have you paid attention to their breeding?"

"In what way?"

"Though they're all good stock, aren't some better than others?"

"True."

"Do you breed from all of them indiscriminately, or only from the best?"

"Only from the best."

"And do you breed from the oldest or youngest, or only those in their prime?"

"Only those in their prime."

"And if you didn't breed carefully, your dogs and birds would deteriorate badly?"

"Certainly."

"The same goes for horses and all other animals?"

"Without question."

"Good heavens, my friend!" I said. "What extraordinary skill our rulers will need if the same principle applies to human beings!"

"The principle certainly applies. But why would this require special skill?"

"Because our rulers will frequently need to administer medicine to the body politic. When patients only need a healthy regimen, an ordinary practitioner will do. But when actual medicine must be administered, you need a far more capable physician."

"That's true. But what exactly are you getting at?"
D: Elegant: Sexual attraction among communal guardians must be regulated, not left to license. Socrates draws an analogy to animal breeding: just as Glaucon breeds only from his best dogs and birds in their prime, the State must apply selective breeding to its guardians. This will require extraordinary skill from rulers -- the skill of a physician administering powerful medicine to the body politic.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that the guardians' sexual unions must be carefully regulated, drawing an analogy to selective animal breeding. Just as a breeder mates only the best animals in their prime, the State must apply the same principle to human reproduction. This will require rulers of extraordinary skill, like physicians administering potent medicine.
D: Distilled: Guardians' unions must be selectively regulated like animal breeding -- mating only the best in their prime -- requiring rulers of extraordinary skill.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The animal breeding analogy is central to Plato's eugenics proposal, which has been both influential and deeply controversial throughout history -- 'Necessity which lovers know' distinguishes erotic necessity (anagke erotike) from logical/mathematical necessity -- The 'medicine' metaphor foreshadows the 'noble lie' about the marriage lottery -- Glaucon's experience with hunting dogs and birds suggests his aristocratic background -- The breeding analogy was critiqued by Aristotle (Politics II.3-4) and has been controversial in modern reception

D: Original:
I mean, I replied, that our rulers will find a considerable dose of falsehood and deceit necessary for the good of their subjects: we were saying that the use of all these things regarded as medicines might be of advantage.

And we were very right. And this lawful use of them seems likely to be often needed in the regulations of marriages and births.
D: Narrative:
"I mean," I replied, "that our rulers will find a considerable dose of falsehood and deceit necessary for the good of their subjects: we were saying that the use of all these things regarded as medicines might be of advantage."

"And we were very right."

"And this lawful use of them seems likely to be often needed in the regulations of marriages and births."
D: Refashioned:
"I mean," I replied, "that our rulers will need a considerable amount of falsehood and deception for the good of their subjects. We established earlier that such things, used as medicines, can be beneficial."

"And we were right to say so."

"And this legitimate use of deception will likely be needed quite often in regulating marriages and births."
D: Elegant: Rulers will need falsehood and deception -- used as medicine -- to regulate marriages and births for the State's benefit. This 'noble lie' was previously justified as beneficial when administered by rulers.
D: Summary: Socrates reveals that rulers will need to employ deception as a kind of medicine in regulating marriages and births, recalling the earlier justification for rulers' use of falsehood for the common good.
D: Distilled: Rulers will use deception as beneficial medicine to regulate the guardians' marriages and breeding.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The justification of rulers' deception was established in Book III (389b-c) with the 'noble lie' (gennaion pseudos) -- This is one of the most controversial aspects of Plato's political philosophy -- The medical metaphor justifies paternalistic deception: just as doctors may deceive patients for their health, rulers may deceive citizens for the State's health -- Karl Popper's 'The Open Society and Its Enemies' famously attacked this Platonic doctrine -- The deception specifically involves rigging the marriage lottery to appear random

D: Original: How so? Why, I said, the principle has been already laid down that the best of either sex should be united with the best as often, and the inferior with the inferior, as seldom as possible; and that they should rear the offspring of the one sort of union, but not of the other, if the flock is to be maintained in first-rate condition. Now these goings on must be a secret which the rulers only know, or there will be a further danger of our herd, as the guardians may be termed, breaking out into rebellion.
D: Narrative:
"How so?"

"Why," I said, "the principle has been already laid down that the best of either sex should be united with the best as often, and the inferior with the inferior, as seldom as possible; and that they should rear the offspring of the one sort of union, but not of the other, if the flock is to be maintained in first-rate condition. Now these goings on must be a secret which the rulers only know, or there will be a further danger of our herd, as the guardians may be termed, breaking out into rebellion."
D: Refashioned:
"How so?"

"The principle has already been established: the best men and women should be paired together as often as possible, and the inferior paired as seldom as possible. The offspring of the superior unions should be raised, but not those of the inferior -- if we want to maintain the guardian class in peak condition. These arrangements must remain secret, known only to the rulers; otherwise, the guardians -- our 'herd,' so to speak -- might rebel."
D: Elegant: The best should breed with the best frequently, the inferior with the inferior rarely. Only superior offspring should be raised. These eugenic arrangements must remain secret to prevent rebellion among the guardians.
D: Summary: Socrates states the eugenic principle plainly: the best guardians should be paired most frequently, the inferior least, with only superior offspring raised. This selective breeding must be kept secret from the guardians themselves to prevent unrest.
D: Distilled: The best must breed with the best, inferior offspring must be removed, and the entire system must be kept secret to prevent rebellion.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage contains Plato's most explicit eugenics proposal -- 'Not rear the offspring of the other' is deliberately vague; it likely implies exposure (abandonment) of infants, a practice that existed in ancient Greece -- The secrecy requirement is essential: the system works only if guardians believe pairings are random -- The 'herd' (poimnion) and 'flock' metaphors reinforce the rulers-as-shepherds imagery -- This passage has been used both to celebrate and condemn Plato's political philosophy

D: Original: Very true. Had we not better appoint certain festivals at which we will bring together the brides and bridegrooms, and sacrifices will be offered and suitable hymeneal songs composed by our poets: the number of weddings is a matter which must be left to the discretion of the rulers, whose aim will be to preserve the average of population? There are many other things which they will have to consider, such as the effects of wars and diseases and any similar agencies, in order as far as this is possible to prevent the State from becoming either too large or too small.
D: Narrative:
"Very true."

"Had we not better appoint certain festivals at which we will bring together the brides and bridegrooms, and sacrifices will be offered and suitable hymeneal songs composed by our poets: the number of weddings is a matter which must be left to the discretion of the rulers, whose aim will be to preserve the average of population? There are many other things which they will have to consider, such as the effects of wars and diseases and any similar agencies, in order as far as this is possible to prevent the State from becoming either too large or too small."
D: Refashioned: "Shouldn't we establish regular festivals to bring together brides and bridegrooms, with sacrifices and wedding hymns composed by our poets? The number of marriages should be left to the rulers' discretion, since their goal will be to maintain a stable population. They'll need to account for many factors -- war casualties, disease, and similar forces -- to keep the State from growing too large or too small."
D: Elegant: Regular marriage festivals with religious ceremony will bring couples together, with rulers controlling the number of marriages to maintain optimal population, accounting for losses from war and disease.
D: Summary: Socrates proposes regular marriage festivals with sacrifices and hymns, where rulers control the number of unions to maintain stable population levels, accounting for losses from war and disease.
D: Distilled: Marriage festivals regulated by rulers will maintain optimal population through controlled pairings, compensating for war and disease.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Hymeneal songs are wedding hymns, named after Hymen, the god of marriage -- Population management (maintaining neither too large nor too small a state) was a practical concern in Greek city-states -- This anticipates Aristotle's similar concerns about optimal population in Politics VII.4 -- The religious trappings (sacrifices, hymns) give legitimacy to what is essentially a state-managed breeding program

D: Original:
Certainly, he replied. We shall have to invent some ingenious kind of lots which the less worthy may draw on each occasion of our bringing them together, and then they will accuse their own ill-luck and not the rulers.

To be sure, he said. And I think that our braver and better youth, besides their other honours and rewards, might have greater facilities of intercourse with women given them; their bravery will be a reason, and such fathers ought to have as many sons as possible.

True. And the proper officers, whether male or female or both, for offices are to be held by women as well as by men --

Yes -- The proper officers will take the offspring of the good parents to the pen or fold, and there they will deposit them with certain nurses who dwell in a separate quarter; but the offspring of the inferior, or of the better when they chance to be deformed, will be put away in some mysterious, unknown place, as they should be.

Yes, he said, that must be done if the breed of the guardians is to be kept pure.
D: Narrative:
"Certainly," he replied.

"We shall have to invent some ingenious kind of lots which the less worthy may draw on each occasion of our bringing them together, and then they will accuse their own ill-luck and not the rulers."

"To be sure," he said.

"And I think that our braver and better youth, besides their other honours and rewards, might have greater facilities of intercourse with women given them; their bravery will be a reason, and such fathers ought to have as many sons as possible."

"True."

"And the proper officers, whether male or female or both, for offices are to be held by women as well as by men --"

"Yes --"

"The proper officers will take the offspring of the good parents to the pen or fold, and there they will deposit them with certain nurses who dwell in a separate quarter; but the offspring of the inferior, or of the better when they chance to be deformed, will be put away in some mysterious, unknown place, as they should be."

"Yes," he said, "that must be done if the breed of the guardians is to be kept pure."
D: Refashioned:
"We'll need to devise an ingenious lottery system so that the less worthy draw unfavorable lots at each mating festival. That way, they'll blame their own bad luck rather than the rulers."

"Of course," he said.

"And the braver and more excellent young guardians should receive, among their other honors and rewards, more frequent opportunities to mate. Their valor deserves it, and such fathers should have as many children as possible."

"True."

"The appropriate officials -- male, female, or both, since women hold office alongside men --"

"Yes --"

"These officials will take the children of the best parents to a communal nursery in a separate quarter, where designated nurses will care for them. But the offspring of inferior parents, or those of superior parents who are born deformed, will be quietly removed to some undisclosed place."

"Yes," he said, "that must be done to keep the guardian class pure."
D: Elegant: A rigged lottery conceals the eugenic selection, making inferior guardians blame luck rather than rulers. The best warriors get more mating opportunities. Superior offspring go to communal nurseries; inferior or deformed children are removed to an unknown place.
D: Summary: The rulers will rig a lottery so that inferior guardians blame bad luck for fewer mating opportunities. Brave warriors receive more chances to reproduce. Superior children are raised in communal nurseries, while inferior or deformed offspring are secretly removed.
D: Distilled: A rigged lottery conceals eugenic selection; the bravest breed most, superior children are communally raised, and inferior offspring are secretly removed.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Put away in some mysterious, unknown place' almost certainly refers to infant exposure, a practiced (if controversial) reality in ancient Greece -- The rigged lottery is the specific mechanism of the 'noble lie' applied to breeding -- Officers 'male or female' maintains the principle of women's equality established in wave one -- 'Keeping the breed pure' uses animal husbandry language deliberately -- This is perhaps the most morally troubling passage in the Republic for modern readers

D: Original: They will provide for their nurture, and will bring the mothers to the fold when they are full of milk, taking the greatest possible care that no mother recognizes her own child; and other wet-nurses may be engaged if more are required. Care will also be taken that the process of suckling shall not be protracted too long; and the mothers will have no getting up at night or other trouble, but will hand over all this sort of thing to the nurses and attendants.
D: Narrative: "They will provide for their nurture, and will bring the mothers to the fold when they are full of milk, taking the greatest possible care that no mother recognizes her own child; and other wet-nurses may be engaged if more are required. Care will also be taken that the process of suckling shall not be protracted too long; and the mothers will have no getting up at night or other trouble, but will hand over all this sort of thing to the nurses and attendants."
D: Refashioned: "The officials will manage the children's care, bringing mothers to the nursery when their milk comes in, but taking the greatest care to ensure no mother recognizes her own child. Additional wet-nurses can be hired if needed. Nursing won't be prolonged unnecessarily, and the mothers will be spared night feedings and other burdens -- all of that will be handed over to the nurses and attendants."
D: Elegant: Mothers will nurse infants at the communal nursery but must not recognize their own children. Wet-nurses supplement as needed. Mothers are freed from night feedings and childcare burdens, which fall to professional attendants.
D: Summary: Mothers will be brought to the nursery to breastfeed but will be prevented from identifying their own children. Wet-nurses will supplement as needed, and mothers will be relieved of nighttime care and other burdens by professional attendants.
D: Distilled: Mothers nurse at the communal nursery without knowing which child is theirs, freed from childcare burdens by professional nurses.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates -- The deliberate prevention of maternal recognition is essential to the communal family system: if mothers don't know their children, they'll treat all children as their own -- The relief of mothers from nighttime care could be read as either progressive (freeing women for guardian duties) or disturbing (severing the maternal bond) -- Aristotle criticizes this proposal in Politics II.3, arguing that 'watered-down' affection spread across all children is worse than concentrated natural affection

D: Original:
You suppose the wives of our guardians to have a fine easy time of it when they are having children.

Why, said I, and so they ought. Let us, however, proceed with our scheme. We were saying that the parents should be in the prime of life?

Very true. And what is the prime of life? May it not be defined as a period of about twenty years in a woman's life, and thirty in a man's?

Which years do you mean to include? A woman, I said, at twenty years of age may begin to bear children to the State, and continue to bear them until forty; a man may begin at five-and-twenty, when he has passed the point at which the pulse of life beats quickest, and continue to beget children until he be fifty-five.

Certainly, he said, both in men and women those years are the prime of physical as well as of intellectual vigour.
D: Narrative:
"You suppose the wives of our guardians to have a fine easy time of it when they are having children."

"Why," said I, "and so they ought. Let us, however, proceed with our scheme. We were saying that the parents should be in the prime of life?"

"Very true."

"And what is the prime of life? May it not be defined as a period of about twenty years in a woman's life, and thirty in a man's?"

"Which years do you mean to include?"

"A woman," I said, "at twenty years of age may begin to bear children to the State, and continue to bear them until forty; a man may begin at five-and-twenty, when he has passed the point at which the pulse of life beats quickest, and continue to beget children until he be fifty-five."

"Certainly," he said, "both in men and women those years are the prime of physical as well as of intellectual vigour."
D: Refashioned:
"It sounds like the guardian wives will have a pretty easy time of motherhood."

"And so they should," I said. "But let's continue. We were saying that parents should be in their prime?"

"Yes."

"And what is the prime of life? Roughly a twenty-year window for women and thirty years for men?"

"Which years exactly?"

"A woman may begin bearing children for the State at twenty and continue until forty. A man may begin at twenty-five -- once past the peak of youthful energy -- and continue until fifty-five."

"Certainly," he said. "For both men and women, those are the years of peak physical and intellectual vigor."
D: Elegant: Women should bear children between ages twenty and forty, men between twenty-five and fifty-five -- the prime years of physical and intellectual vigor.
D: Summary: Socrates defines the optimal childbearing period: women from age twenty to forty, men from twenty-five to fifty-five, corresponding to the prime of both physical and intellectual vigor.
D: Distilled: The State prescribes childbearing ages: women from 20-40, men from 25-55, the years of peak physical and mental vigor.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- These age ranges roughly correspond to ancient Greek medical understanding of peak fertility -- 'The pulse of life beats quickest' (akme) refers to the Greek concept of physical/intellectual peak -- The different age ranges for men and women reflect ancient Greek assumptions about maturation -- 'Bear children to the State' emphasizes that reproduction is a civic duty, not a private matter -- Compare Aristotle's similar but different age recommendations in Politics VII.16

D: Original:
Any one above or below the prescribed ages who takes part in the public hymeneals shall be said to have done an unholy and unrighteous thing; the child of which he is the father, if it steals into life, will have been conceived under auspices very unlike the sacrifices and prayers, which at each hymeneal priestesses and priest and the whole city will offer, that the new generation may be better and more useful than their good and useful parents, whereas his child will be the offspring of darkness and strange lust.

Very true, he replied. And the same law will apply to any one of those within the prescribed age who forms a connection with any woman in the prime of life without the sanction of the rulers; for we shall say that he is raising up a bastard to the State, uncertified and unconsecrated.
D: Narrative:
"Any one above or below the prescribed ages who takes part in the public hymeneals shall be said to have done an unholy and unrighteous thing; the child of which he is the father, if it steals into life, will have been conceived under auspices very unlike the sacrifices and prayers, which at each hymeneal priestesses and priest and the whole city will offer, that the new generation may be better and more useful than their good and useful parents, whereas his child will be the offspring of darkness and strange lust."

"Very true," he replied.

"And the same law will apply to any one of those within the prescribed age who forms a connection with any woman in the prime of life without the sanction of the rulers; for we shall say that he is raising up a bastard to the State, uncertified and unconsecrated."
D: Refashioned:
"Anyone outside the prescribed age range who participates in the public marriage rites will have committed an unholy and unrighteous act. Any child born from such a union -- if it manages to come into existence -- will have been conceived under omens entirely unlike the sacrifices and prayers that priestesses, priests, and the entire city offer at each marriage ceremony, praying that the next generation surpass their parents in goodness and usefulness. Such a child will be the offspring of darkness and illicit desire."

"Very true," he replied.

"And the same law applies to anyone of proper age who has relations with a woman in her prime without the rulers' approval. We'll declare that he is fathering an illegitimate child upon the State -- uncertified and unconsecrated."
D: Elegant: Reproduction outside prescribed ages or without rulers' sanction is declared unholy. Authorized unions receive religious blessing -- prayers that children surpass their parents. Unauthorized children are deemed illegitimate offspring of 'darkness and strange lust,' uncertified by the State.
D: Summary: Socrates declares that mating outside the prescribed ages or without rulers' approval is an unholy act. Sanctioned unions receive religious ceremony and prayers for superior offspring; unsanctioned children are considered illegitimate and unconsecrated.
D: Distilled: Reproduction outside prescribed ages or without state sanction is declared unholy, producing illegitimate 'children of darkness.'
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The religious language (unholy, unconsecrated, sacrifices, prayers) gives eugenic regulation sacred authority -- 'Offspring of darkness and strange lust' contrasts with the blessed children of sanctioned unions -- The concept of state-certified reproduction centralizes reproductive control in the ruling class -- 'Uncertified and unconsecrated' (anenguon kai anieron) uses legal and religious terminology to stigmatize unauthorized reproduction

D: Original:
Very true, he replied. This applies, however, only to those who are within the specified age: after that we allow them to range at will, except that a man may not marry his daughter or his daughter's daughter, or his mother or his mother's mother; and women, on the other hand, are prohibited from marrying their sons or fathers, or son's son or father's father, and so on in either direction. And we grant all this, accompanying the permission with strict orders to prevent any embryo which may come into being from seeing the light; and if any force a way to the birth, the parents must understand that the offspring of such an union cannot be maintained, and arrange accordingly.

That also, he said, is a reasonable proposition. But how will they know who are fathers and daughters, and so on?
D: Narrative:
"Very true," he replied.

"This applies, however, only to those who are within the specified age: after that we allow them to range at will, except that a man may not marry his daughter or his daughter's daughter, or his mother or his mother's mother; and women, on the other hand, are prohibited from marrying their sons or fathers, or son's son or father's father, and so on in either direction. And we grant all this, accompanying the permission with strict orders to prevent any embryo which may come into being from seeing the light; and if any force a way to the birth, the parents must understand that the offspring of such an union cannot be maintained, and arrange accordingly."

"That also," he said, "is a reasonable proposition. But how will they know who are fathers and daughters, and so on?"
D: Refashioned:
"This restriction applies only during the prescribed childbearing years. After that, people may have relations freely -- except that a man may not be with his daughter, granddaughter, mother, or grandmother, and a woman may not be with her son, grandson, father, or grandfather. Even with this freedom, we'll give strict orders that no pregnancy resulting from these post-childbearing unions should come to term. And if a child is born despite these measures, the parents must understand that it cannot be raised."

"That seems reasonable," he said. "But how will they know who their fathers and daughters actually are?"
D: Elegant: After the prescribed childbearing years, guardians may have free sexual relations with only incest prohibitions. Any resulting pregnancies must be terminated; any births must not be raised. Glaucon asks the obvious question: without family knowledge, how will they identify relatives?
D: Summary: After childbearing age, guardians may have relations freely except with direct ancestors or descendants. Pregnancies from these unions must not come to term, and any resulting births must not be raised. Glaucon asks the critical question: how will anyone know who their relatives are?
D: Distilled: Post-childbearing guardians may mate freely except for incest, but no resulting children may be raised.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Prevent any embryo from seeing the light' refers to abortion or infanticide -- The incest prohibition is complicated by the communal system where no one knows their actual parents -- Glaucon's question ('how will they know?') is astute and leads to the next passage's solution -- The tension between sexual freedom after childbearing age and strict control during it reveals the system's purely eugenic purpose -- Ancient Greek attitudes toward abortion and exposure differed significantly from modern ones

D: Original: They will never know. The way will be this: --dating from the day of the hymeneal, the bridegroom who was then married will call all the male children who are born in the seventh and tenth month afterwards his sons, and the female children his daughters, and they will call him father, and he will call their children his grandchildren, and they will call the elder generation grandfathers and grandmothers. All who were begotten at the time when their fathers and mothers came together will be called their brothers and sisters, and these, as I was saying, will be forbidden to inter-marry. This, however, is not to be understood as an absolute prohibition of the marriage of brothers and sisters; if the lot favours them, and they receive the sanction of the Pythian oracle, the law will allow them.
D: Narrative: "They will never know. The way will be this: -- dating from the day of the hymeneal, the bridegroom who was then married will call all the male children who are born in the seventh and tenth month afterwards his sons, and the female children his daughters, and they will call him father, and he will call their children his grandchildren, and they will call the elder generation grandfathers and grandmothers. All who were begotten at the time when their fathers and mothers came together will be called their brothers and sisters, and these, as I was saying, will be forbidden to inter-marry. This, however, is not to be understood as an absolute prohibition of the marriage of brothers and sisters; if the lot favours them, and they receive the sanction of the Pythian oracle, the law will allow them."
D: Refashioned: "They won't ever know for certain. Here's how it works: counting from the day of the marriage festival, the bridegroom will call all male children born in the seventh through tenth months his sons, and all female children his daughters. They will call him father, their children will be his grandchildren, and they will call the older generation grandfathers and grandmothers. Everyone conceived during the same mating period will be considered brothers and sisters, and will normally be forbidden to marry each other -- though this isn't an absolute prohibition. If the lottery favors them and the oracle at Delphi gives its sanction, the law will permit it."
D: Elegant: Family identity is assigned by timing: all children born seven to ten months after a marriage festival are considered children of that cohort's parents. The resulting 'siblings' are generally forbidden to marry, unless approved by lot and the Delphic oracle.
D: Summary: Since no one can know their actual parents, family relationships are assigned by timing: all children born within a certain window after a marriage festival are considered offspring of that cohort. These 'siblings' generally cannot intermarry, though exceptions can be made through lottery and oracle.
D: Distilled: Family identity is assigned by birth timing relative to marriage festivals, creating cohort-based kinship without actual biological knowledge.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The seventh and tenth month window covers the range of normal gestation -- The Pythian oracle is the oracle at Delphi, dedicated to Apollo, the most authoritative religious institution in ancient Greece -- This system creates artificial but functionally real kinship bonds across the entire guardian class -- The exception for 'siblings' marrying with oracle approval provides flexibility -- Aristotle criticizes this as producing 'diluted' family bonds (Politics II.3-4)

D: Original:
Quite right, he replied. Such is the scheme, Glaucon, according to which the guardians of our State are to have their wives and families in common. And now you would have the argument show that this community is consistent with the rest of our polity, and also that nothing can be better --would you not?

Yes, certainly. Shall we try to find a common basis by asking of ourselves what ought to be the chief aim of the legislator in making laws and in the organization of a State, --what is the greatest I good, and what is the greatest evil, and then consider whether our previous description has the stamp of the good or of the evil?

By all means. Can there be any greater evil than discord and distraction and plurality where unity ought to reign? or any greater good than the bond of unity?

There cannot. And there is unity where there is community of pleasures and pains --where all the citizens are glad or grieved on the same occasions of joy and sorrow?

No doubt. Yes; and where there is no common but only private feeling a State is disorganized --when you have one half of the world triumphing and the other plunged in grief at the same events happening to the city or the citizens?

Certainly. Such differences commonly originate in a disagreement about the use of the terms 'mine' and 'not mine,' 'his' and 'not his.'

Exactly so. And is not that the best-ordered State in which the greatest number of persons apply the terms 'mine' and 'not mine' in the same way to the same thing?
D: Narrative:
"Quite right," he replied.

"Such is the scheme, Glaucon, according to which the guardians of our State are to have their wives and families in common. And now you would have the argument show that this community is consistent with the rest of our polity, and also that nothing can be better -- would you not?"

"Yes, certainly."

"Shall we try to find a common basis by asking of ourselves what ought to be the chief aim of the legislator in making laws and in the organization of a State, -- what is the greatest good, and what is the greatest evil, and then consider whether our previous description has the stamp of the good or of the evil?"

"By all means."

"Can there be any greater evil than discord and distraction and plurality where unity ought to reign? or any greater good than the bond of unity?"

"There cannot."

"And there is unity where there is community of pleasures and pains -- where all the citizens are glad or grieved on the same occasions of joy and sorrow?"

"No doubt."

"Yes; and where there is no common but only private feeling a State is disorganized -- when you have one half of the world triumphing and the other plunged in grief at the same events happening to the city or the citizens?"

"Certainly."

"Such differences commonly originate in a disagreement about the use of the terms 'mine' and 'not mine,' 'his' and 'not his.'"

"Exactly so."

"And is not that the best-ordered State in which the greatest number of persons apply the terms 'mine' and 'not mine' in the same way to the same thing?"
D: Refashioned:
"Quite right," he replied.

"That's the scheme, Glaucon: the guardians will share wives and families in common. Now you want to see that this arrangement is consistent with our overall political system and that nothing could be better?"

"Yes."

"Let's start by asking: what should be the legislator's chief aim? What is the greatest good for a State, and what is the greatest evil? Then we can judge whether our proposal bears the mark of good or evil."

"By all means."

"Is there any greater evil than discord, division, and fragmentation where unity should reign? Or any greater good than the bond of unity?"

"No."

"And unity exists where pleasures and pains are shared -- where all citizens rejoice and grieve together over the same events?"

"No doubt."

"Whereas a State is disorganized when feelings are private rather than shared -- when half the population celebrates while the other half mourns the very same events?"

"Certainly."

"Such divisions typically originate in disagreements about the words 'mine' and 'not mine,' 'his' and 'not his.'"

"Exactly."

"Then isn't the best-ordered State one in which the greatest number of people apply 'mine' and 'not mine' to the same things in the same way?"
D: Elegant: The greatest evil in a State is discord where unity should reign; the greatest good is the bond of unity. Unity exists where pleasures and pains are communal. Division arises from private conceptions of 'mine' and 'not mine.' The best State is one where the most citizens share the same sense of 'mine.'
D: Summary: Socrates argues that the State's greatest good is unity and greatest evil is discord. Unity means shared pleasures and pains; discord arises when citizens have competing private interests expressed through 'mine' and 'not mine.' The best-ordered State maximizes shared ownership and feeling.
D: Distilled: The greatest good is civic unity through shared pleasures and pains; the greatest evil is discord from competing claims of 'mine' and 'not mine.'
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This argument for unity as the supreme political good is central to Plato's political philosophy -- The analysis of 'mine' and 'not mine' (to emon / to ouk emon) as the source of political division is remarkably modern -- Aristotle directly counters this in Politics II.2, arguing that some private ownership is necessary for generosity and that shared ownership leads to neglect -- The organic unity model (State as body) is developed in the next passage (5.40) -- This justification of communal property echoes the earlier abolition of private property for guardians (Book III)

D: Original: Quite true. Or that again which most nearly approaches to the condition of the individual --as in the body, when but a finger of one of us is hurt, the whole frame, drawn towards the soul as a center and forming one kingdom under the ruling power therein, feels the hurt and sympathizes all together with the part affected, and we say that the man has a pain in his finger; and the same expression is used about any other part of the body, which has a sensation of pain at suffering or of pleasure at the alleviation of suffering.
D: Narrative:
"Quite true."

"Or that again which most nearly approaches to the condition of the individual -- as in the body, when but a finger of one of us is hurt, the whole frame, drawn towards the soul as a center and forming one kingdom under the ruling power therein, feels the hurt and sympathizes all together with the part affected, and we say that the man has a pain in his finger; and the same expression is used about any other part of the body, which has a sensation of pain at suffering or of pleasure at the alleviation of suffering."
D: Refashioned: "Or consider which State most resembles an individual person. When a finger is injured, the entire body -- organized as a single system under the soul's command -- feels the hurt and sympathizes with the affected part.
