D: Refashioned:
"One more question: is knowledge a faculty, or would you classify it differently?"

"Knowledge is certainly a faculty -- the most powerful of all."

"And opinion is also a faculty?"

"Yes -- opinion is the power by which we form opinions."

"But you agreed earlier that knowledge is not the same as opinion?"

"Of course -- how could any reasonable person equate something infallible with something that makes mistakes?"

"An excellent answer, which proves we clearly distinguish between them."

"Yes."

"Then since knowledge and opinion have distinct powers, they must have distinct objects?"

"Certainly."

"Being -- what truly exists -- is the object of knowledge?"

"Yes."

"And opinion is the power to form opinions. But is its object the same as knowledge's object?"

"No -- we've already shown that different faculties must have different objects. Since opinion and knowledge are distinct faculties, their objects cannot be the same."

"So if being is the object of knowledge, something else must be the object of opinion?"

"Yes."

"Is non-being the object of opinion? Actually, can there even be an opinion about non-being? Think about it: when someone has an opinion, it must be an opinion about something. Can you have an opinion about nothing?"

"Impossible."

"Someone with an opinion has an opinion about something that is one thing?"

"Yes."

"But non-being isn't one thing -- properly speaking, it's nothing at all."

"True."

"And we established that ignorance is the correlate of non-being, just as knowledge is the correlate of being?"

"True."

"Then opinion is concerned with neither being nor non-being?"

"Neither."

"So opinion is neither ignorance nor knowledge?"

"It seems so."

"Does opinion lie outside and beyond both of them -- clearer than knowledge, or darker than ignorance?"

"Neither."

"Then opinion strikes you as darker than knowledge but lighter than ignorance?"
D: Elegant: Knowledge is infallible and grasps being; ignorance corresponds to non-being. Opinion, being fallible but about something, cannot correspond to either pure being or pure non-being. It lies between them -- darker than knowledge but lighter than ignorance -- corresponding to an intermediate realm between being and non-being.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes the epistemological hierarchy: knowledge is infallible and grasps being (what fully exists); ignorance corresponds to non-being (what doesn't exist). Opinion, which is fallible but directed at something real, occupies the middle ground -- darker than knowledge but lighter than ignorance -- and must correspond to something between being and non-being.
D: Distilled: Knowledge grasps being infallibly; ignorance corresponds to non-being; opinion falls between them, darker than knowledge but lighter than ignorance.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The epistemological hierarchy (knowledge > opinion > ignorance) corresponds to the ontological hierarchy (being > becoming > non-being) -- 'Infallible' (anamarteton) versus 'errs' (hamartanon) is the key distinction between knowledge and opinion -- The argument that different faculties must have different objects is controversial and has been extensively debated -- This tripartite structure anticipates the Divided Line (Book VI, 509d-511e) where the visible and intelligible worlds are further subdivided -- The claim that opinion is 'about something' but not about being or non-being creates the logical space for the intermediate realm of becoming -- This is one of the most technically rigorous philosophical arguments in the Republic

D: Original: Both; and in no small degree. And also to be within and between them? Yes. Then you would infer that opinion is intermediate? No question. But were we not saying before, that if anything appeared to be of a sort which is and is not at the same time, that sort of thing would appear also to lie in the interval between pure being and absolute not-being; and that the corresponding faculty is neither knowledge nor ignorance, but will be found in the interval between them?
D: Narrative:
"Both; and in no small degree."

"And also to be within and between them?"

"Yes."

"Then you would infer that opinion is intermediate?"

"No question."

"But were we not saying before, that if anything appeared to be of a sort which is and is not at the same time, that sort of thing would appear also to lie in the interval between pure being and absolute not-being; and that the corresponding faculty is neither knowledge nor ignorance, but will be found in the interval between them?"
D: Refashioned:
"Both -- significantly darker than knowledge and significantly lighter than ignorance."

"And it lies within and between them?"

"Yes."

"So opinion is intermediate between knowledge and ignorance."

"Without question."

"But didn't we say earlier that if anything appeared to both exist and not exist simultaneously, it would occupy the interval between pure being and absolute non-being? And the corresponding faculty would be neither knowledge nor ignorance, but something found in the interval between them?"
D: Elegant: Opinion is confirmed as intermediate between knowledge and ignorance. Whatever both exists and does not exist simultaneously occupies the interval between being and non-being, grasped by the intermediate faculty of opinion.
D: Summary: Opinion is definitively placed as intermediate between knowledge and ignorance. This confirms the earlier principle: whatever both exists and does not exist simultaneously occupies the space between pure being and absolute non-being, apprehended by the corresponding intermediate faculty.
D: Distilled: Opinion is the intermediate faculty between knowledge and ignorance, grasping the intermediate reality that both is and is not.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The argument reaches its structural conclusion: three levels of cognition (knowledge, opinion, ignorance) map onto three levels of reality (being, becoming, non-being) -- 'Is and is not at the same time' characterizes the sensible world: a beautiful thing is also ugly in some respect, a large thing is also small relative to something else -- This framework is the philosophical foundation of the Divided Line and the Cave allegory -- The 'interval' (metaxy) between being and non-being is the realm of becoming (genesis), which is the sensible/physical world -- Plato's claim that the physical world is not fully real but not nothing either is one of the most influential and debated ideas in Western philosophy

D: Original:
True. And in that interval there has now been discovered something which we call opinion?

There has. Then what remains to be discovered is the object which partakes equally of the nature of being and not-being, and cannot rightly be termed either, pure and simple; this unknown term, when discovered, we may truly call the subject of opinion, and assign each to its proper faculty, -the extremes to the faculties of the extremes and the mean to the faculty of the mean.
D: Narrative:
"True."

"And in that interval there has now been discovered something which we call opinion?"

"There has."

"Then what remains to be discovered is the object which partakes equally of the nature of being and not-being, and cannot rightly be termed either, pure and simple; this unknown term, when discovered, we may truly call the subject of opinion, and assign each to its proper faculty, -- the extremes to the faculties of the extremes and the mean to the faculty of the mean."
D: Refashioned:
"And in that interval we've now identified something we call opinion."

"We have."

"What remains is to discover the object that partakes equally of being and non-being -- that cannot properly be called either one, purely and simply. Once we identify this object, we can rightfully call it the subject of opinion and assign each level of reality to its corresponding faculty: the extremes (being and non-being) to their respective faculties (knowledge and ignorance), and the intermediate to its faculty (opinion)."
D: Elegant: With opinion identified as the intermediate faculty, what remains is to find its object: something that partakes equally of being and non-being. This will complete the mapping of faculties to realities -- knowledge to being, ignorance to non-being, opinion to the intermediate.
D: Summary: Having established opinion as the intermediate faculty, Socrates seeks its corresponding object: something that partakes equally of being and non-being. This will complete the systematic mapping: knowledge corresponds to being, ignorance to non-being, and opinion to the intermediate realm.
D: Distilled: The object of opinion is whatever partakes equally of being and non-being, completing the faculty-reality mapping.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The systematic mapping (faculty to object) creates a complete epistemological-ontological framework -- 'Partakes equally of being and not-being' describes the sensible world: physical things come into being and pass away, are beautiful and ugly, large and small -- The 'extremes and the mean' language reflects mathematical proportionality, connecting to the geometric structure of the Divided Line -- This passage sets up the final argument of Book V (5.86-5.88), which identifies the many beautiful things of the sensible world as the proper objects of opinion -- The complete framework: Forms (being) -> Knowledge; Sensible things (becoming) -> Opinion; Nothing (non-being) -> Ignorance

D: Original: True. This being premised, I would ask the gentleman who is of opinion that there is no absolute or unchangeable idea of beauty --in whose opinion the beautiful is the manifold --he, I say, your lover of beautiful sights, who cannot bear to be told that the beautiful is one, and the just is one, or that anything is one --to him I would appeal, saying, Will you be so very kind, sir, as to tell us whether, of all these beautiful things, there is one which will not be found ugly; or of the just, which will not be found unjust; or of the holy, which will not also be unholy?
D: Narrative:
"True."

"This being premised, I would ask the gentleman who is of opinion that there is no absolute or unchangeable idea of beauty -- in whose opinion the beautiful is the manifold -- he, I say, your lover of beautiful sights, who cannot bear to be told that the beautiful is one, and the just is one, or that anything is one -- to him I would appeal, saying, Will you be so very kind, sir, as to tell us whether, of all these beautiful things, there is one which will not be found ugly; or of the just, which will not be found unjust; or of the holy, which will not also be unholy?"
D: Refashioned:
"With this established, let me address the person who believes there is no absolute or unchanging Form of Beauty -- who holds that beauty is simply the manifold of beautiful things. This lover of beautiful sights, who cannot tolerate being told that Beauty is one, or that Justice is one, or that anything is one -- to this person I would say:

'Would you be so kind as to tell us: among all these beautiful things you love, is there one that won't also be found ugly from some perspective? Among all things you call just, is there one that won't also appear unjust? Among all things holy, is there one that won't also seem unholy?'"
D: Elegant: Socrates challenges the sight-lover who denies the Forms: among all the beautiful things in the sensible world, can you find even one that won't also appear ugly? Among the just, one that won't seem unjust? The sensible world's particulars always exhibit contradictory properties.
D: Summary: Socrates challenges those who deny the existence of absolute Forms: can any particular beautiful thing never appear ugly? Can anything just never seem unjust? The sensible world's objects always exhibit contradictory properties, proving they are not absolute being.
D: Distilled: Every beautiful thing also appears ugly from some perspective -- proving sensible particulars are not absolute being but the intermediate objects of opinion.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates -- This is the Allegory of the Cave in miniature: those who see only the shadows (sensible world) deny the existence of the light (Forms) -- 'The beautiful is the manifold' represents the anti-Platonic position that there are only particular beautiful things, not Beauty itself -- The argument that sensible particulars always exhibit opposite properties (beautiful and ugly, just and unjust) is called the 'argument from relativity' or 'compresence of opposites' -- This argument appears in multiple Platonic dialogues: Hippias Major, Symposium, Phaedo -- The sight-lover (philotheamon) is contrasted with the wisdom-lover (philosophos) -- Aristotle's response is that particulars can be understood through universals abstracted from experience, not separate Forms

D: Original:
No, he replied; the beautiful will in some point of view be found ugly; and the same is true of the rest.

And may not the many which are doubles be also halves? --doubles, that is, of one thing, and halves of another?

Quite true. And things great and small, heavy and light, as they are termed, will not be denoted by these any more than by the opposite names?

True; both these and the opposite names will always attach to all of them.

And can any one of those many things which are called by particular names be said to be this rather than not to be this?

He replied: They are like the punning riddles which are asked at feasts or the children's puzzle about the eunuch aiming at the bat, with what he hit him, as they say in the puzzle, and upon what the bat was sitting. The individual objects of which I am speaking are also a riddle, and have a double sense: nor can you fix them in your mind, either as being or not-being, or both, or neither.

Then what will you do with them? I said. Can they have a better place than between being and not-being? For they are clearly not in greater darkness or negation than not-being, or more full of light and existence than being.

That is quite true, he said. Thus then we seem to have discovered that the many ideas which the multitude entertain about the beautiful and about all other things are tossing about in some region which is halfway between pure being and pure not-being?

We have. Yes; and we had before agreed that anything of this kind which we might find was to be described as matter of opinion, and not as matter of knowledge; being the intermediate flux which is caught and detained by the intermediate faculty.

Quite true. Then those who see the many beautiful, and who yet neither see absolute beauty, nor can follow any guide who points the way thither; who see the many just, and not absolute justice, and the like, --such persons may be said to have opinion but not knowledge?
D: Narrative:
"No," he replied; "the beautiful will in some point of view be found ugly; and the same is true of the rest."

"And may not the many which are doubles be also halves? -- doubles, that is, of one thing, and halves of another?"

"Quite true."

The individual objects of which I am speaking are also a riddle, and have a double sense: nor can you fix them in your mind, either as being or not-being, or both, or neither."

"Then what will you do with them?" I said. "Can they have a better place than between being and not-being? For they are clearly not in greater darkness or negation than not-being, or more full of light and existence than being."

"That is quite true," he said.

"Thus then we seem to have discovered that the many ideas which the multitude entertain about the beautiful and about all other things are tossing about in some region which is halfway between pure being and pure not-being?"

"We have."

"Yes; and we had before agreed that anything of this kind which we might find was to be described as matter of opinion, and not as matter of knowledge; being the intermediate flux which is caught and detained by the intermediate faculty."

"Quite true."

"Then those who see the many beautiful, and who yet neither see absolute beauty, nor can follow any guide who points the way thither; who see the many just, and not absolute justice, and the like, -- such persons may be said to have opinion but not knowledge?"
D: Refashioned:
"No," he replied. "The beautiful will always be found ugly from some perspective, and the same is true of everything else."

"And can't things that are double also be half? Double one thing and half another?"

"True."

"And things called great or small, heavy or light -- don't they equally deserve the opposite descriptions?"

"True -- both the name and its opposite will always apply to all of them."

"So can any of these particular things be said definitively to be this rather than not be this?"

"They're like those punning riddles at dinner parties," he replied, "or the children's puzzle about the eunuch throwing at a bat -- what did he hit it with, and what was it sitting on? The particular objects I'm talking about are the same kind of riddle: they have a double meaning. You can't pin them down in your mind as being or not-being, as both or neither."

"Then where do they belong?" I asked. "Can they have a better place than between being and non-being? They're not darker or more negative than non-being, nor brighter or more real than being."

"That's exactly right."

"So we've discovered that the multitude's ideas about beauty and all other things are tossing about in a region halfway between pure being and pure non-being."

"We have."

"And we agreed earlier that anything found in this intermediate zone would be matter for opinion, not knowledge -- an intermediate flux grasped by the intermediate faculty."

"Quite true."

"Then people who see the many beautiful things but never see Beauty itself -- who cannot follow anyone who tries to lead them toward it; who see many just things but never absolute Justice -- such people have opinion but not knowledge?"
D: Elegant: Every particular thing is a riddle: doubles are also halves, great things are also small, beautiful things are also ugly. Sensible particulars cannot be fixed as being or not-being -- they toss about between the two. This intermediate flux is the proper object of opinion, not knowledge. Those who see many beautiful things but never Beauty itself have opinion, not knowledge.
D: Summary: Particular things are riddles that cannot be pinned down: what is double is also half, what is beautiful is also ugly. They exist in an intermediate flux between being and non-being, making them objects of opinion rather than knowledge. Those who see many beautiful things but never absolute Beauty possess opinion, not genuine knowledge.
D: Distilled: Sensible particulars are riddles -- always both what they are and what they are not -- tossing in the flux between being and non-being as objects of opinion.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The eunuch riddle is a famous ancient Greek puzzle: 'A eunuch who did not see well saw a bat sitting on a reed and threw a pumice stone at it and missed' -- every term is ambiguous -- The 'compresence of opposites' argument (things are both F and not-F) is the main argument for Forms in the Republic -- 'Intermediate flux' (metaxy rheonta) perfectly describes the Heraclitean world of becoming -- 'Tossing about' (kylindeitai) suggests instability, contrasting with the stability of the Forms -- The argument moves from metaphysics to epistemology: the nature of sensible objects determines what kind of cognition grasps them -- This completes the philosophical argument that distinguishes philosophers (who grasp Forms through knowledge) from sight-lovers (who grasp particulars through opinion)

D: Original:
That is certain. But those who see the absolute and eternal and immutable may be said to know, and not to have opinion only?

Neither can that be denied. The one loves and embraces the subjects of knowledge, the other those of opinion? The latter are the same, as I dare say will remember, who listened to sweet sounds and gazed upon fair colours, but would not tolerate the existence of absolute beauty.

Yes, I remember. Shall we then be guilty of any impropriety in calling them lovers of opinion rather than lovers of wisdom, and will they be very angry with us for thus describing them?

I shall tell them not to be angry; no man should be angry at what is true.

But those who love the truth in each thing are to be called lovers of wisdom and not lovers of opinion.

Assuredly.

"That is certain."

"But those who see the absolute and eternal and immutable may be said to know, and not to have opinion only?"

"Neither can that be denied."

"The one loves and embraces the subjects of knowledge, the other those of opinion? The latter are the same, as I dare say you will remember, who listened to sweet sounds and gazed upon fair colours, but would not tolerate the existence of absolute beauty."

"Yes, I remember."

"Shall we then be guilty of any impropriety in calling them lovers of opinion rather than lovers of wisdom, and will they be very angry with us for thus describing them?"

"I shall tell them not to be angry; no man should be angry at what is true."

D: Refashioned:
"That's certain."

"But those who see the absolute, eternal, and unchanging -- they can be said to have knowledge, not merely opinion?"

"That can't be denied either."

"So the philosopher loves and embraces the objects of knowledge, while the other group loves the objects of opinion. The latter are the same people -- as you'll recall -- who delight in beautiful sounds and colors but refuse to acknowledge the existence of absolute Beauty."

"Yes, I remember."

"Then would it be improper to call them 'lovers of opinion' rather than 'lovers of wisdom'? Will they be angry with us for this description?"

"I'd tell them not to be angry. No one should be angry at the truth."

"But those who love the truth in each thing -- they are to be called lovers of wisdom, not lovers of opinion."

"Without question."
D: Elegant: Those who grasp the absolute, eternal, and immutable have knowledge; those who embrace only sensible particulars have opinion. The sight-lovers are properly called 'lovers of opinion' (philodoxoi), not 'lovers of wisdom' (philosophoi). Only those who love truth itself deserve the name philosopher.
D: Summary: Book V concludes with the definitive distinction: those who see the absolute and eternal have knowledge and are true philosophers (lovers of wisdom). Those who love only sensible things have opinion and are properly called lovers of opinion, not lovers of wisdom. No one should be angry at this truth.
D: Distilled: True philosophers love the absolute and eternal (knowledge); sight-lovers love only sensible particulars (opinion) and are lovers of opinion, not wisdom.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage completes Book V's philosophical argument and sets up Books VI-VII -- Philodoxoi (lovers of opinion) versus philosophoi (lovers of wisdom) is a key Platonic coinage -- 'Absolute, eternal, and immutable' describes the Forms: they do not change, come into being, or pass away -- 'No man should be angry at what is true' encapsulates the Socratic commitment to truth over comfort -- The book ends where it began: with a distinction that justifies philosopher-kings. If only philosophers grasp truth while others grasp mere opinion, then only philosophers should rule -- Book VI will explore why most people distrust philosophers and what makes the philosophical nature rare and easily corrupted -- The entire arc of Book V: women's equality -> communal families -> philosopher-kings -> the nature of the philosopher -> knowledge versus opinion

D: Original:
And thus, Glaucon, after the argument has gone a weary way, the true and the false philosophers have at length appeared in view.

I do not think, he said, that the way could have been shortened.

I suppose not, I said; and yet I believe that we might have had a better view of both of them if the discussion could have been confined to this one subject and if there were not many other questions awaiting us, which he who desires to see in what respect the life of the just differs from that of the unjust must consider.

And what is the next question? he asked. Surely, I said, the one which follows next in order. Inasmuch as philosophers only are able to grasp the eternal and unchangeable, and those who wander in the region of the many and variable are not philosophers, I must ask you which of the two classes should be the rulers of our State?

And how can we rightly answer that question? Whichever of the two are best able to guard the laws and institutions of our State --let them be our guardians.

Very good. Neither, I said, can there be any question that the guardian who is to keep anything should have eyes rather than no eyes?
D: Narrative:
"And thus, Glaucon, after the argument has gone a weary way, the true and the false philosophers have at length appeared in view."

"I do not think," he said, "that the way could have been shortened."

"I suppose not," I said; "and yet I believe that we might have had a better view of both of them if the discussion could have been confined to this one subject and if there were not many other questions awaiting us, which he who desires to see in what respect the life of the just differs from that of the unjust must consider."

"And what is the next question?" he asked.

"Surely," I said, "the one which follows next in order. Inasmuch as philosophers only are able to grasp the eternal and unchangeable, and those who wander in the region of the many and variable are not philosophers, I must ask you which of the two classes should be the rulers of our State?"

"And how can we rightly answer that question?"

"Whichever of the two are best able to guard the laws and institutions of our State -- let them be our guardians."

"Very good."

"Neither," I said, "can there be any question that the guardian who is to keep anything should have eyes rather than no eyes?"
D: Refashioned:
"So, Glaucon, after this long and winding road, we've finally gotten a clear look at both the genuine and the counterfeit philosophers."

"I don't think there was any shortcut," he said.

"Probably not," I replied, "though we might have seen them more clearly if we could have focused on this one topic alone. But there are still many questions waiting for us -- questions that anyone who wants to understand how the just life differs from the unjust life must eventually face."

"So what's the next question?" he asked.

"The natural follow-up," I said. "Since only philosophers can grasp what is eternal and unchanging, while those who drift among constantly shifting particulars are not philosophers -- which of these two groups should rule our state?"

"How do we decide that?"

"Whichever group is best equipped to safeguard the laws and institutions of our state -- they should be our guardians."

"That makes sense."

"And surely," I said, "no one would argue that a guardian meant to protect something should be blind rather than sighted?"
D: Elegant: After a long journey through argument, the true philosophers are finally distinguished from the false. The next question follows naturally: since only philosophers grasp the eternal and unchanging, while others wander among shifting particulars, philosophers should be the rulers. The guardians of a state must have vision, not blindness.
D: Summary: Socrates and Glaucon conclude their distinction between true and false philosophers. Socrates argues that since only philosophers grasp eternal truths, they are the natural candidates to rule as guardians of the state.
D: Distilled: Only those who can perceive eternal truths should govern, for a guardian must have sight rather than blindness.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Continues from Book V's discussion of true vs. false philosophers -- 'The eternal and unchangeable' refers to the Forms (Platonic metaphysics) -- 'The many and variable' refers to the world of appearances/becoming -- Guardians as rulers established in Books II-III -- The sight/blindness metaphor anticipates the Sun analogy and Cave allegory (Book VII)

D: Original: There can be no question of that. And are not those who are verily and indeed wanting in the knowledge of the true being of each thing, and who have in their souls no clear pattern, and are unable as with a painter's eye to look at the absolute truth and to that original to repair, and having perfect vision of the other world to order the laws about beauty, goodness, justice in this, if not already ordered, and to guard and preserve the order of them --are not such persons, I ask, simply blind?
D: Narrative: "And are not those who are verily and indeed wanting in the knowledge of the true being of each thing, and who have in their souls no clear pattern, and are unable as with a painter's eye to look at the absolute truth and to that original to repair, and having perfect vision of the other world to order the laws about beauty, goodness, justice in this, if not already ordered, and to guard and preserve the order of them -- are not such persons, I ask, simply blind?"
D: Refashioned: "And what about those who truly lack knowledge of what each thing really is -- who have no clear model in their minds, who cannot look toward absolute truth as a painter looks at a subject, and who cannot use that perfect vision of the higher reality to establish and maintain laws about beauty, goodness, and justice in this world? Aren't such people essentially blind?"
D: Elegant: Those who lack knowledge of true being, who carry no clear pattern of reality in their souls, and who cannot look to absolute truth as a model for ordering laws about beauty, goodness, and justice -- such people are simply blind.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that anyone without knowledge of the true nature of things -- without a clear model of absolute truth to guide their lawmaking about beauty, goodness, and justice -- is effectively blind.
D: Distilled: Without knowledge of absolute truth as a guiding pattern, those who would order laws about beauty, goodness, and justice are simply blind.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates (rhetorical question to Glaucon) -- The 'painter's eye' metaphor: the philosopher-ruler must have a model (paradeigma) of the Forms to copy from -- 'True being of each thing' = the Form or Idea of each thing -- Anticipates the philosopher-as-artist metaphor developed later in Book VI (6.47-48) -- The blindness metaphor connects to the Cave allegory in Book VII

D: Original:
Truly, he replied, they are much in that condition. And shall they be our guardians when there are others who, besides being their equals in experience and falling short of them in no particular of virtue, also know the very truth of each thing?

There can be no reason, he said, for rejecting those who have this greatest of all great qualities; they must always have the first place unless they fail in some other respect.

Suppose then, I said, that we determine how far they can unite this and the other excellences.

By all means. In the first place, as we began by observing, the nature of the philosopher has to be ascertained. We must come to an understanding about him, and, when we have done so, then, if I am not mistaken, we shall also acknowledge that such an union of qualities is possible, and that those in whom they are united, and those only, should be rulers in the State.

What do you mean? Let us suppose that philosophical minds always love knowledge of a sort which shows them the eternal nature not varying from generation and corruption.

Agreed. And further, I said, let us agree that they are lovers of all true being; there is no part whether greater or less, or more or less honourable, which they are willing to renounce; as we said before of the lover and the man of ambition.

True. And if they are to be what we were describing, is there not another quality which they should also possess?

What quality? Truthfulness: they will never intentionally receive into their mind falsehood, which is their detestation, and they will love the truth.

Yes, that may be safely affirmed of them. 'May be,' my friend, I replied, is not the word; say rather 'must be affirmed:' for he whose nature is amorous of anything cannot help loving all that belongs or is akin to the object of his affections.

Right, he said. And is there anything more akin to wisdom than truth? How can there be? Can the same nature be a lover of wisdom and a lover of falsehood?
D: Narrative:
"Truly," he replied, "they are much in that condition."

"And shall they be our guardians when there are others who, besides being their equals in experience and falling short of them in no particular of virtue, also know the very truth of each thing?"

"There can be no reason," he said, "for rejecting those who have this greatest of all great qualities; they must always have the first place unless they fail in some other respect."

"Suppose then," I said, "that we determine how far they can unite this and the other excellences."

"By all means."

"In the first place, as we began by observing, the nature of the philosopher has to be ascertained. We must come to an understanding about him, and, when we have done so, then, if I am not mistaken, we shall also acknowledge that such an union of qualities is possible, and that those in whom they are united, and those only, should be rulers in the State."

"What do you mean?"

"Let us suppose that philosophical minds always love knowledge of a sort which shows them the eternal nature not varying from generation and corruption."

"Agreed."

"And further," I said, "let us agree that they are lovers of all true being; there is no part whether greater or less, or more or less honourable, which they are willing to renounce; as we said before of the lover and the man of ambition."

"True."

"And if they are to be what we were describing, is there not another quality which they should also possess?"

"What quality?"

"Truthfulness: they will never intentionally receive into their mind falsehood, which is their detestation, and they will love the truth."

"Yes, that may be safely affirmed of them."

"'May be,' my friend," I replied, "is not the word; say rather 'must be affirmed:' for he whose nature is amorous of anything cannot help loving all that belongs or is akin to the object of his affections."

"Right," he said.

"And is there anything more akin to wisdom than truth?"

"How can there be?"

"Can the same nature be a lover of wisdom and a lover of falsehood?"

"Never."
D: Refashioned:
"Indeed," he replied, "they really are in that condition."

"Then should they be our guardians when there are others who match them in experience, equal them in every virtue, and also possess knowledge of the true nature of each thing?"

"There's no reason," he said, "to reject those who have this greatest of all qualities. They should always hold first place, unless they fall short in some other way."

"Then let's work out how far they can combine this quality with the other excellences," I said.

"Absolutely."

"First, we need to define the philosopher's nature. Once we've done that, I believe we'll see that such a combination of qualities is indeed possible -- and that only those who possess them all should rule."

"What do you mean?"

"Let's start with this: philosophical minds are drawn to knowledge that reveals the eternal -- what doesn't shift with generation and decay."

"Agreed."

"And they are lovers of all true being -- they won't renounce any part of it, whether great or small, honored or humble. Like the true lover or the truly ambitious person, they want the whole of what they desire."

"True."

"And there's another quality they must have. What is it? Truthfulness. They will never willingly admit falsehood into their minds. They detest it and love truth."

"That can safely be said of them."

"Not just 'safely said,' my friend -- it must be said. Anyone whose nature is passionate about something inevitably loves everything connected to it."

"Right."

"And what is more closely connected to wisdom than truth?"

"Nothing."

"Can the same person be a lover of wisdom and a lover of falsehood?"

"Never."
D: Elegant: Those who know the truth of each thing and also possess every other virtue should be the guardians -- no one else has a stronger claim. The philosophical nature must be defined: philosophers love knowledge of the eternal, embrace all true being without exception, and are devoted to truth. Since wisdom and truth are intimately connected, a lover of wisdom cannot also be a lover of falsehood.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that philosopher-guardians must combine knowledge of eternal truth with all other virtues. He begins defining the philosopher's nature: they love knowledge of the unchanging, embrace all being, and are inherently truthful, since wisdom and truth are inseparable.
D: Distilled: The philosopher's nature requires love of eternal knowledge, embrace of all being, and devotion to truth -- for wisdom and falsehood are incompatible.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Eternal nature not varying from generation and corruption' = the Forms, as opposed to the sensible world of becoming -- The analogy to the lover: as an erotic lover desires everything about the beloved, so the philosopher desires all truth (cf. Book V, 474c-475c) -- Truthfulness as a necessary trait of the philosopher anticipates the corruption argument later in Book VI -- This passage begins the systematic enumeration of the philosopher's virtues

D: Original:
Never. The true lover of learning then must from his earliest youth, as far as in him lies, desire all truth?

Assuredly. But then again, as we know by experience, he whose desires are strong in one direction will have them weaker in others; they will be like a stream which has been drawn off into another channel.

True. He whose desires are drawn towards knowledge in every form will be absorbed in the pleasures of the soul, and will hardly feel bodily pleasure --I mean, if he be a true philosopher and not a sham one.

That is most certain. Such an one is sure to be temperate and the reverse of covetous; for the motives which make another man desirous of having and spending, have no place in his character.

Very true. Another criterion of the philosophical nature has also to be considered.

What is that? There should be no secret corner of illiberality; nothing can more antagonistic than meanness to a soul which is ever longing after the whole of things both divine and human.

Most true, he replied. Then how can he who has magnificence of mind and is the spectator of all time and all existence, think much of human life?

He cannot. Or can such an one account death fearful? No indeed. Then the cowardly and mean nature has no part in true philosophy?

Certainly not. Or again: can he who is harmoniously constituted, who is not covetous or mean, or a boaster, or a coward-can he, I say, ever be unjust or hard in his dealings?

Impossible. Then you will soon observe whether a man is just and gentle, or rude and unsociable; these are the signs which distinguish even in youth the philosophical nature from the unphilosophical.

True. There is another point which should be remarked. What point? Whether he has or has not a pleasure in learning; for no one will love that which gives him pain, and in which after much toil he makes little progress.

Certainly not. And again, if he is forgetful and retains nothing of what he learns, will he not be an empty vessel?
D: Narrative:
"The true lover of learning then must from his earliest youth, as far as in him lies, desire all truth?"

"Assuredly."

"But then again, as we know by experience, he whose desires are strong in one direction will have them weaker in others; they will be like a stream which has been drawn off into another channel."

"True."

"He whose desires are drawn towards knowledge in every form will be absorbed in the pleasures of the soul, and will hardly feel bodily pleasure -- I mean, if he be a true philosopher and not a sham one."

"That is most certain."

"Such an one is sure to be temperate and the reverse of covetous; for the motives which make another man desirous of having and spending, have no place in his character."

"Very true."

"Another criterion of the philosophical nature has also to be considered."

"What is that?"

"There should be no secret corner of illiberality; nothing can be more antagonistic than meanness to a soul which is ever longing after the whole of things both divine and human."

"Most true," he replied.

"Then how can he who has magnificence of mind and is the spectator of all time and all existence, think much of human life?"

"He cannot."

"Or can such an one account death fearful?"

"No indeed."

"Then the cowardly and mean nature has no part in true philosophy?"

"Certainly not."

"Or again: can he who is harmoniously constituted, who is not covetous or mean, or a boaster, or a coward -- can he, I say, ever be unjust or hard in his dealings?"

"Impossible."

"Then you will soon observe whether a man is just and gentle, or rude and unsociable; these are the signs which distinguish even in youth the philosophical nature from the unphilosophical."

"True."

"There is another point which should be remarked."

"What point?"

"Whether he has or has not a pleasure in learning; for no one will love that which gives him pain, and in which after much toil he makes little progress."

"Certainly not."

"And again, if he is forgetful and retains nothing of what he learns, will he not be an empty vessel?"
D: Refashioned:
"So the true lover of learning must, from earliest youth, desire all truth?"

"Of course."

"But we know from experience that when someone's desires flow strongly in one direction, they flow more weakly in others -- like a stream diverted into a new channel."

"True."

"Someone whose desires are drawn entirely toward knowledge will be absorbed in the pleasures of the mind and will barely notice bodily pleasures -- if he's a genuine philosopher, not a pretender."

"Absolutely."

"Such a person will naturally be temperate and free from greed. The drives that make others obsess over acquiring and spending simply don't exist in his character."

"Very true."

"There's another criterion we need to examine."

"What's that?"

"There must be no hidden corner of small-mindedness. Nothing is more opposed to a soul that constantly reaches toward the whole of things, both divine and human, than pettiness."

"Absolutely," he replied.

"And how could someone with greatness of mind, who contemplates all of time and all of existence, place much importance on human life?"

"He couldn't."

"Could such a person consider death something to fear?"

"Not at all."

"So cowardice and small-mindedness have no place in genuine philosophy?"

"Certainly not."

"And can someone who is harmoniously composed -- neither greedy, nor petty, nor boastful, nor cowardly -- ever be unjust or cruel?"

"Impossible."

"So you can tell early on whether someone is just and gentle or rough and antisocial. These are the markers that distinguish the philosophical nature from the unphilosophical, even in youth."

"True."

"One more thing to consider."

"What?"

"Whether he takes pleasure in learning. No one will love what causes him pain and where much effort yields little progress."

"Of course not."

"And if he's forgetful and retains nothing of what he learns -- won't he be an empty vessel?"
D: Elegant: The true philosopher's desires flow toward knowledge, leaving little current for bodily pleasures -- making him naturally temperate and free from greed. He must be free of pettiness, for a soul reaching toward the whole of existence cannot be small-minded. Such greatness of mind makes him fearless before death and incapable of injustice. He must also take pleasure in learning and have a strong memory, or his efforts will be fruitless.
D: Summary: Socrates continues cataloguing the philosopher's traits: desires channeled toward knowledge produce natural temperance; greatness of mind yields courage before death and freedom from injustice; and genuine philosophers must take pleasure in learning and possess strong memories.
D: Distilled: The philosopher's desires, flowing entirely toward knowledge, naturally produce temperance, magnanimity, courage, justice, and a strong memory.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The 'stream' metaphor for desire: psychic energy directed toward knowledge leaves less for bodily appetites -- 'Spectator of all time and all existence' (theoros) -- a famous phrase describing philosophical contemplation -- The enumeration of virtues (temperance, courage, justice, magnanimity) parallels the cardinal virtues discussed in Book IV -- 'Empty vessel' = a mind that cannot retain knowledge is useless for philosophy -- These traits will later be shown to make philosophers vulnerable to corruption (6.17)

D: Original:
That is certain. Labouring in vain, he must end in hating himself and his fruitless occupation? Yes.

Then a soul which forgets cannot be ranked among genuine philosophic natures; we must insist that the philosopher should have a good memory?

Certainly. And once more, the inharmonious and unseemly nature can only tend to disproportion?

Undoubtedly. And do you consider truth to be akin to proportion or to disproportion?

To proportion. Then, besides other qualities, we must try to find a naturally well-proportioned and gracious mind, which will move spontaneously towards the true being of everything.

Certainly. Well, and do not all these qualities, which we have been enumerating, go together, and are they not, in a manner, necessary to a soul, which is to have a full and perfect participation of being?

They are absolutely necessary, he replied. And must not that be a blameless study which he only can pursue who has the gift of a good memory, and is quick to learn, --noble, gracious, the friend of truth, justice, courage, temperance, who are his kindred?

The god of jealousy himself, he said, could find no fault with such a study.

And to men like him, I said, when perfected by years and education, and to these only you will entrust the State.
D: Narrative:
"That is certain. Labouring in vain, he must end in hating himself and his fruitless occupation?"

"Yes."

"Then a soul which forgets cannot be ranked among genuine philosophic natures; we must insist that the philosopher should have a good memory?"

"Certainly."

"And once more, the inharmonious and unseemly nature can only tend to disproportion?"

"Undoubtedly."

"And do you consider truth to be akin to proportion or to disproportion?"

"To proportion."

"Then, besides other qualities, we must try to find a naturally well-proportioned and gracious mind, which will move spontaneously towards the true being of everything."

"Certainly."

"Well, and do not all these qualities, which we have been enumerating, go together, and are they not, in a manner, necessary to a soul, which is to have a full and perfect participation of being?"

"They are absolutely necessary," he replied.

"And must not that be a blameless study which he only can pursue who has the gift of a good memory, and is quick to learn -- noble, gracious, the friend of truth, justice, courage, temperance, who are his kindred?"

"The god of jealousy himself," he said, "could find no fault with such a study."

"And to men like him," I said, "when perfected by years and education, and to these only you will entrust the State."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly. Laboring in vain, he'd end up hating both himself and his fruitless work."

"Yes."

"So a forgetful soul doesn't belong among genuine philosophers. We must insist the philosopher have a good memory."

"Certainly."

"And a disharmonious, graceless nature can only tend toward disproportion."

"Without a doubt."

"And is truth more akin to proportion or to disproportion?"

"To proportion."

"Then we must also look for a naturally well-proportioned and graceful mind -- one that moves spontaneously toward the true nature of everything."

"Of course."

"Now, don't all these qualities we've been listing go together? Aren't they all necessary for a soul that is to fully participate in being?"

"They are absolutely necessary," he replied.

"And isn't it a blameless pursuit -- one that can only be followed by someone with a good memory, quick to learn, noble, graceful, a friend of truth, justice, courage, and temperance?"

"Even the god of jealousy," he said, "couldn't find fault with such a pursuit."

"And to people like this," I said, "when they've been perfected by years and education -- to these alone you will entrust the state."
D: Elegant: A forgetful soul is no philosopher. Truth is akin to proportion, so the philosophical mind must be naturally harmonious and graceful, drawn spontaneously toward true being. All these qualities -- memory, quick learning, nobility, grace, and friendship with truth, justice, courage, and temperance -- are necessary for full participation in being. Only those perfected by years and education should be entrusted with the state.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes the portrait of the philosophical nature: good memory, natural proportion, and graceful intelligence are essential. All these virtues form an inseparable whole, and only those who possess them all, refined through education, should be entrusted with governance.
D: Distilled: The complete philosophical nature -- harmonious, well-memoried, graceful, and allied with every virtue -- is the only nature fit to be entrusted with the state.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'The god of jealousy' (Momus) = Greek god of blame and criticism -- even he could find no fault -- Truth as akin to proportion (symmetria) reflects Pythagorean and Platonic aesthetics -- 'Full and perfect participation of being' = methexis, the soul's relation to the Forms -- This concludes Chapter 6.1's portrait of the ideal philosopher -- The list of virtues (memory, quick learning, nobility, truth, justice, courage, temperance) will be ironically revisited when Socrates explains how these same qualities lead to corruption (6.17)

D: Original: Here Adeimantus interposed and said: To these statements, Socrates, no one can offer a reply; but when you talk in this way, a strange feeling passes over the minds of your hearers: They fancy that they are led astray a little at each step in the argument, owing to their own want of skill in asking and answering questions; these littles accumulate, and at the end of the discussion they are found to have sustained a mighty overthrow and all their former notions appear to be turned upside down. And as unskilful players of draughts are at last shut up by their more skilful adversaries and have no piece to move, so they too find themselves shut up at last; for they have nothing to say in this new game of which words are the counters; and yet all the time they are in the right. The observation is suggested to me by what is now occurring. For any one of us might say, that although in words he is not able to meet you at each step of the argument, he sees as a fact that the votaries of philosophy, when they carry on the study, not only in youth as a part of education, but as the pursuit of their maturer years, most of them become strange monsters, not to say utter rogues, and that those who may be considered the best of them are made useless to the world by the very study which you extol.
D: Narrative: Here Adeimantus interposed and said: "To these statements, Socrates, no one can offer a reply; but when you talk in this way, a strange feeling passes over the minds of your hearers: They fancy that they are led astray a little at each step in the argument, owing to their own want of skill in asking and answering questions; these littles accumulate, and at the end of the discussion they are found to have sustained a mighty overthrow and all their former notions appear to be turned upside down. And as unskilful players of draughts are at last shut up by their more skilful adversaries and have no piece to move, so they too find themselves shut up at last; for they have nothing to say in this new game of which words are the counters; and yet all the time they are in the right. The observation is suggested to me by what is now occurring. For any one of us might say, that although in words he is not able to meet you at each step of the argument, he sees as a fact that the votaries of philosophy, when they carry on the study, not only in youth as a part of education, but as the pursuit of their maturer years, most of them become strange monsters, not to say utter rogues, and that those who may be considered the best of them are made useless to the world by the very study which you extol."
D: Refashioned:
At this point Adeimantus broke in: "No one can argue with what you're saying, Socrates, but your listeners get a strange feeling when you talk like this. They sense they're being led slightly off course at each step -- not through any trick of yours, but because they lack the skill to challenge you properly. These small concessions pile up, and by the end they find their entire position has been overturned. It's like being beaten at checkers by a more skilled player -- eventually you have no moves left. They're checkmated in this game of words, and yet they feel they were right all along.

"This is exactly what's happening now. Any of us could point out that, whatever the logic says, the facts tell a different story: people who pursue philosophy not just in youth but as a lifelong vocation mostly turn out to be cranks, if not outright scoundrels, and even the best of them are made useless to society by the very study you're praising."
D: Elegant: Adeimantus objects: no one can refute Socrates in argument, but listeners feel led astray step by step, like unskilled players checkmated in a word-game. Yet the facts remain stubborn -- most lifelong philosophers become eccentric or corrupt, and even the best are rendered useless by the very study Socrates champions.
D: Summary: Adeimantus interrupts to voice the common objection: while no one can defeat Socrates in argument, observable reality shows that most devoted philosophers become either strange or corrupt, and even the best are made useless to society.
D: Distilled: However compelling the argument for philosophy, the facts show that most philosophers become either corrupt or useless.
Notes: Speakers: Adeimantus interrupts Socrates -- Adeimantus is Plato's brother and frequent interlocutor -- The 'draughts' (pessoi/checkers) metaphor illustrates how dialectical skill can win arguments even against correct intuitions -- This objection is crucial: it forces Socrates to explain why philosophy gets a bad reputation -- The distinction between 'useless' philosophers and 'corrupt' ones structures the rest of Book VI -- Echoes the charge against Socrates himself at his trial (Apology)

D: Original:
Well, and do you think that those who say so are wrong? I cannot tell, he replied; but I should like to know what is your opinion.

Hear my answer; I am of opinion that they are quite right.

Then how can you be justified in saying that cities will not cease from evil until philosophers rule in them, when philosophers are acknowledged by us to be of no use to them?

You ask a question, I said, to which a reply can only be given in a parable.

Yes, Socrates; and that is a way of speaking to which you are not at all accustomed, I suppose.
D: Narrative:
"Well, and do you think that those who say so are wrong?"

"I cannot tell," he replied; "but I should like to know what is your opinion."

"Hear my answer; I am of opinion that they are quite right."

"Then how can you be justified in saying that cities will not cease from evil until philosophers rule in them, when philosophers are acknowledged by us to be of no use to them?"

"You ask a question," I said, "to which a reply can only be given in a parable."

"Yes, Socrates; and that is a way of speaking to which you are not at all accustomed, I suppose."
D: Refashioned:
"And do you think the people who say this are wrong?"

"I'm not sure," he replied. "But I'd like to hear your view."

"Here's my answer: I think they're entirely right."

"Then how can you justify claiming that cities won't be free of evil until philosophers rule them -- when you've just agreed that philosophers are useless to cities?"

"That question," I said, "can only be answered through a parable."

"A parable, Socrates? That's certainly not your usual style," he said with a hint of irony.
D: Elegant: Socrates agrees that philosophers are indeed seen as useless -- then faces the paradox: how can philosopher-kings save cities if philosophers are useless to them? He says this can only be answered through a parable.
D: Summary: Socrates surprisingly agrees that philosophers are viewed as useless. When Adeimantus asks how he can then advocate philosopher-kings, Socrates says the answer requires a parable.
D: Distilled: Socrates concedes philosophers appear useless, but says a parable is needed to explain why the fault lies not with philosophy but with society.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Socrates' agreement that philosophers are useless is a dialectical move -- he will shift the blame from philosophy to the state -- The 'parable' introduces the famous Ship of State allegory -- Adeimantus' sarcasm ('not at all accustomed') is ironic, since Socrates frequently uses analogies and myths -- This sets up one of the Republic's most famous extended metaphors

D: Original: I perceive, I said, that you are vastly amused at having plunged me into such a hopeless discussion; but now hear the parable, and then you will be still more amused at the meagreness of my imagination: for the manner in which the best men are treated in their own States is so grievous that no single thing on earth is comparable to it; and therefore, if I am to plead their cause, I must have recourse to fiction, and put together a figure made up of many things, like the fabulous unions of goats and stags which are found in pictures. Imagine then a fleet or a ship in which there is a captain who is taller and stronger than any of the crew, but he is a little deaf and has a similar infirmity in sight, and his knowledge of navigation is not much better. The sailors are quarrelling with one another about the steering --every one is of opinion that he has a right to steer, though he has never learned the art of navigation and cannot tell who taught him or when he learned, and will further assert that it cannot be taught, and they are ready to cut in pieces any one who says the contrary. They throng about the captain, begging and praying him to commit the helm to them; and if at any time they do not prevail, but others are preferred to them, they kill the others or throw them overboard, and having first chained up the noble captain's senses with drink or some narcotic drug, they mutiny and take possession of the ship and make free with the stores; thus, eating and drinking, they proceed on their voyage in such a manner as might be expected of them. Him who is their partisan and cleverly aids them in their plot for getting the ship out of the captain's hands into their own whether by force or persuasion, they compliment with the name of sailor, pilot, able seaman, and abuse the other sort of man, whom they call a good-for-nothing; but that the true pilot must pay attention to the year and seasons and sky and stars and winds, and whatever else belongs to his art, if he intends to be really qualified for the command of a ship, and that he must and will be the steerer, whether other people like or not-the possibility of this union of authority with the steerer's art has never seriously entered into their thoughts or been made part of their calling.
D: Narrative:
"I perceive," I said, "that you are vastly amused at having plunged me into such a hopeless discussion; but now hear the parable, and then you will be still more amused at the meagreness of my imagination: for the manner in which the best men are treated in their own States is so grievous that no single thing on earth is comparable to it; and therefore, if I am to plead their cause, I must have recourse to fiction, and put together a figure made up of many things, like the fabulous unions of goats and stags which are found in pictures.

Imagine then a fleet or a ship in which there is a captain who is taller and stronger than any of the crew, but he is a little deaf and has a similar infirmity in sight, and his knowledge of navigation is not much better. The sailors are quarrelling with one another about the steering -- every one is of opinion that he has a right to steer, though he has never learned the art of navigation and cannot tell who taught him or when he learned, and will further assert that it cannot be taught, and they are ready to cut in pieces any one who says the contrary. They throng about the captain, begging and praying him to commit the helm to them; and if at any time they do not prevail, but others are preferred to them, they kill the others or throw them overboard, and having first chained up the noble captain's senses with drink or some narcotic drug, they mutiny and take possession of the ship and make free with the stores; thus, eating and drinking, they proceed on their voyage in such a manner as might be expected of them.

Him who is their partisan and cleverly aids them in their plot for getting the ship out of the captain's hands into their own whether by force or persuasion, they compliment with the name of sailor, pilot, able seaman, and abuse the other sort of man, whom they call a good-for-nothing; but that the true pilot must pay attention to the year and seasons and sky and stars and winds, and whatever else belongs to his art, if he intends to be really qualified for the command of a ship, and that he must and will be the steerer, whether other people like or not -- the possibility of this union of authority with the steerer's art has never seriously entered into their thoughts or been made part of their calling."
D: Refashioned:
"I can see you're quite amused at having cornered me," I said, "but hear the parable, and you'll be even more amused by my limited imagination. The way the best people are treated in their own states is so terrible that nothing else compares, so I have to resort to a composite image -- like those mythical paintings of goat-stag hybrids.

Picture a ship whose captain is bigger and stronger than anyone in the crew, but somewhat deaf, somewhat near-sighted, and not much of a navigator. The sailors are all fighting over who should steer, each one convinced he has the right to the helm -- despite never having learned navigation, being unable to name any teacher or time of study, and insisting that navigation can't even be taught. They're ready to tear apart anyone who disagrees.

They crowd around the captain, begging and pleading for control of the helm. When they don't get their way, they kill or throw overboard whoever does. They drug the captain into a stupor, seize the ship, raid the stores, and sail on in a drunken party -- exactly as you'd expect.

Anyone who helps them scheme to take control -- whether through force or manipulation -- they honor with titles like 'skilled sailor,' 'true navigator,' 'master seaman.' Meanwhile, they mock the person who actually understands navigation as a 'useless stargazer.' The idea that a real pilot must study the seasons, the sky, the stars, and the winds -- that genuine navigational knowledge and authority over the ship must go together -- this has never seriously crossed their minds."
D: Elegant: Socrates offers the Ship of State parable: a ship's captain is strong but impaired in sight, hearing, and navigational knowledge. The crew fights over the helm despite knowing nothing of navigation, drugs the captain, seizes control, and rewards schemers as 'true sailors' while mocking anyone who studies the stars and winds as useless. The notion that genuine navigational knowledge should confer authority has never occurred to them.
D: Summary: Socrates introduces the Ship of State allegory: an impaired captain, ignorant mutinous sailors who seize control, and a true navigator dismissed as a useless stargazer -- illustrating why genuine philosophers are scorned in democratic societies.
D: Distilled: In the Ship of State, ignorant sailors seize the helm and mock the true navigator as useless -- just as democracies dismiss genuine philosophers.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates (extended parable, with Adeimantus listening) -- The Ship of State: one of the Republic's most famous allegories -- The captain = the demos/people (strong but impaired in judgment) -- The quarreling sailors = democratic politicians competing for power -- The true pilot = the philosopher who studies reality -- 'Good-for-nothing' and 'star-gazer' = common insults against philosophers (cf. Aristophanes' Clouds mocking Socrates) -- The 'fabulous unions of goats and stags' = chimeric composite images -- This parable directly addresses why philosophers appear useless in democratic states

D: Original:
Now in vessels which are in a state of mutiny and by sailors who are mutineers, how will the true pilot be regarded? Will he not be called by them a prater, a star-gazer, a good-for-nothing?

Of course, said Adeimantus. Then you will hardly need, I said, to hear the interpretation of the figure, which describes the true philosopher in his relation to the State; for you understand already.

Certainly. Then suppose you now take this parable to the gentleman who is surprised at finding that philosophers have no honour in their cities; explain it to him and try to convince him that their having honour would be far more extraordinary.
D: Narrative:
"Now in vessels which are in a state of mutiny and by sailors who are mutineers, how will the true pilot be regarded? Will he not be called by them a prater, a star-gazer, a good-for-nothing?"

"Of course," said Adeimantus.

"Then you will hardly need," I said, "to hear the interpretation of the figure, which describes the true philosopher in his relation to the State; for you understand already."

"Certainly."

"Then suppose you now take this parable to the gentleman who is surprised at finding that philosophers have no honour in their cities; explain it to him and try to convince him that their having honour would be far more extraordinary."
D: Refashioned:
"Now, on a ship in the grip of mutiny, how would the true navigator be regarded by the mutinous sailors? Wouldn't they call him a babbler, a stargazer, a waste of space?"

"Of course," said Adeimantus.

"Then I hardly need to spell out what this parable means for the philosopher's relationship to the state -- you already understand."

"I do."

"So take this parable to anyone who's surprised that philosophers receive no honor in their cities. Try to convince him that it would be far more surprising if they did."
D: Elegant: On a mutinous ship, the true navigator will inevitably be called a useless stargazer. The parable's meaning is clear: in a state run by the ignorant, it would be far more extraordinary if philosophers were honored than if they were not.
D: Summary: Socrates draws the lesson from the Ship of State: in a mutinous political order, the true philosopher will naturally be dismissed. The real surprise would be if philosophers were honored in such cities.
D: Distilled: On a mutinous ship, the true navigator is scorned -- and in a disordered state, it would be astonishing if philosophers were honored.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- This is the interpretive key to the Ship of State allegory -- 'Prater' and 'star-gazer' = terms of abuse for intellectuals in Athenian democracy -- Socrates reverses the expectation: dishonor is the norm, honor would be the anomaly -- The argument shifts blame from philosophers to the political order -- Cf. Socrates' own treatment by Athens, culminating in his trial and execution (Apology, Crito, Phaedo)

D: Original: I will. Say to him, that, in deeming the best votaries of philosophy to be useless to the rest of the world, he is right; but also tell him to attribute their uselessness to the fault of those who will not use them, and not to themselves. The pilot should not humbly beg the sailors to be commanded by him --that is not the order of nature; neither are 'the wise to go to the doors of the rich' --the ingenious author of this saying told a lie --but the truth is, that, when a man is ill, whether he be rich or poor, to the physician he must go, and he who wants to be governed, to him who is able to govern. The ruler who is good for anything ought not to beg his subjects to be ruled by him; although the present governors of mankind are of a different stamp; they may be justly compared to the mutinous sailors, and the true helmsmen to those who are called by them good-for-nothings and star-gazers.
D: Narrative: "I will. Say to him, that, in deeming the best votaries of philosophy to be useless to the rest of the world, he is right; but also tell him to attribute their uselessness to the fault of those who will not use them, and not to themselves. The pilot should not humbly beg the sailors to be commanded by him -- that is not the order of nature; neither are 'the wise to go to the doors of the rich' -- the ingenious author of this saying told a lie -- but the truth is, that, when a man is ill, whether he be rich or poor, to the physician he must go, and he who wants to be governed, to him who is able to govern. The ruler who is good for anything ought not to beg his subjects to be ruled by him; although the present governors of mankind are of a different stamp; they may be justly compared to the mutinous sailors, and the true helmsmen to those who are called by them good-for-nothings and star-gazers."
D: Refashioned:
"Tell him this: he's right that the best philosophers appear useless to society. But tell him to blame those who refuse to use them, not the philosophers themselves.

A pilot shouldn't have to beg the sailors to accept his command -- that's not how nature works. And the old saying that 'the wise should go knocking on the doors of the rich' is a lie. The truth is the opposite: when you're sick, whether rich or poor, you go to the doctor. And when you need governance, you go to the one who knows how to govern.

A ruler who is truly capable shouldn't have to beg people to accept his rule. Yet today's rulers are exactly like those mutinous sailors, and the true helmsmen are the ones they dismiss as useless stargazers."
D: Elegant: The best philosophers are indeed useless -- but the fault lies with those who refuse to use them. A pilot doesn't beg sailors to obey; a patient goes to the doctor, not the reverse. The competent ruler shouldn't beg to rule. Today's rulers are the mutinous sailors; the true helmsmen are those they dismiss as stargazers.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that philosophers' uselessness is society's fault, not philosophy's. Just as patients must go to doctors and not vice versa, those who need governance must seek out those who can govern. Current rulers resemble mutinous sailors, while true philosophers are the dismissed helmsmen.
D: Distilled: Philosophers appear useless because society refuses to use them -- the sick must go to the doctor, not the doctor to the sick.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates (continuing to Adeimantus) -- 'The wise to go to the doors of the rich' = a proverb attributed to Simonides, which Socrates rejects -- The doctor analogy: governance requires expertise, just as medicine does (a recurring Platonic argument, cf. Gorgias) -- This inverts the democratic assumption that rulers should court the people -- The philosopher should not campaign for power; those who need governance should seek the philosopher -- Anticipates the 'reluctant ruler' theme: the best rulers are those who don't want to rule (cf. Book VII, 520d-521b)

D: Original: Precisely so, he said. For these reasons, and among men like these, philosophy, the noblest pursuit of all, is not likely to be much esteemed by those of the opposite faction; not that the greatest and most lasting injury is done to her by her opponents, but by her own professing followers, the same of whom you suppose the accuser to say, that the greater number of them are arrant rogues, and the best are useless; in which opinion I agreed.
D: Narrative:
"Precisely so," he said.

"For these reasons, and among men like these, philosophy, the noblest pursuit of all, is not likely to be much esteemed by those of the opposite faction; not that the greatest and most lasting injury is done to her by her opponents, but by her own professing followers, the same of whom you suppose the accuser to say, that the greater number of them are arrant rogues, and the best are useless; in which opinion I agreed."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly," he said.

"So for these reasons, and among people like these, philosophy -- the noblest pursuit there is -- won't be valued by its opponents. But here's the crucial point: the greatest and most lasting damage to philosophy isn't done by its enemies. It's done by its own self-proclaimed followers -- the very people the accuser describes as mostly rogues, with even the best being useless. And as I said, I agree with that assessment."
D: Elegant: Philosophy, the noblest pursuit, is damaged most not by its enemies but by its own professing followers -- most of whom are rogues, and even the best of whom appear useless.
D: Summary: Socrates makes a key distinction: philosophy's greatest injury comes not from its opponents but from those who falsely claim to practice it, most of whom are corrupt and even the best of whom seem useless.
D: Distilled: Philosophy is harmed most not by its enemies but by its own unworthy practitioners.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Philosophy personified as a noble figure being dishonored -- The distinction between external opponents and internal pretenders is central to Book VI -- This sets up the later discussion of how philosophical natures are corrupted (6.17ff) and how unworthy people enter philosophy (6.31-32) -- The 'arrant rogues' = those who use philosophical training for sophistry or personal gain

D: Original:
Yes. And the reason why the good are useless has now been explained?

True. Then shall we proceed to show that the corruption of the majority is also unavoidable, and that this is not to be laid to the charge of philosophy any more than the other?

By all means. And let us ask and answer in turn, first going back to the description of the gentle and noble nature. Truth, as you will remember, was his leader, whom he followed always and in all things; failing in this, he was an impostor, and had no part or lot in true philosophy.

Yes, that was said. Well, and is not this one quality, to mention no others, greatly at variance with present notions of him?
D: Narrative:
"Yes. And the reason why the good are useless has now been explained?"

"True."

"Then shall we proceed to show that the corruption of the majority is also unavoidable, and that this is not to be laid to the charge of philosophy any more than the other?"

"By all means."

"And let us ask and answer in turn, first going back to the description of the gentle and noble nature. Truth, as you will remember, was his leader, whom he followed always and in all things; failing in this, he was an impostor, and had no part or lot in true philosophy."

"Yes, that was said."

"Well, and is not this one quality, to mention no others, greatly at variance with present notions of him?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes. So we've now explained why the good philosophers appear useless?"

"We have."

"Then shall we move on to show that the corruption of the majority is also unavoidable -- and that this too cannot be blamed on philosophy itself?"

"By all means."

"Let's return to our description of the gentle and noble nature. You'll recall that truth was his guide, whom he followed in everything. Without that, he was a fraud with no claim to genuine philosophy."

"Yes, that's what we said."

"And isn't even this one quality -- to say nothing of the others -- completely at odds with what people currently expect of philosophers?"
D: Elegant: Having explained why good philosophers appear useless, Socrates turns to why so many become corrupt -- a corruption that cannot be blamed on philosophy itself. The key trait of the true philosopher is that truth is his guide in all things; without it, he is an impostor. Yet even this quality contradicts popular expectations of philosophers.
D: Summary: Having explained the apparent uselessness of good philosophers, Socrates turns to explaining the corruption of the majority. He recalls that truth-following is the philosopher's essential trait, and notes this already contradicts public expectations.
D: Distilled: The corruption of most philosophers is unavoidable but cannot be blamed on philosophy itself -- it stems from the conflict between the philosophical nature and society.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Transitional passage: from explaining 'uselessness' to explaining 'corruption' -- Truth as the philosopher's leader recalls the earlier portrait (6.3) -- 'Present notions of him' = contemporary Athenian views of philosophers as impractical or dishonest -- Socrates will argue that the very virtues of the philosophical nature make it vulnerable to corruption by society

D: Original: Certainly, he said. And have we not a right to say in his defence, that the true lover of knowledge is always striving after being --that is his nature; he will not rest in the multiplicity of individuals which is an appearance only, but will go on --the keen edge will not be blunted, nor the force of his desire abate until he have attained the knowledge of the true nature of every essence by a sympathetic and kindred power in the soul, and by that power drawing near and mingling and becoming incorporate with very being, having begotten mind and truth, he will have knowledge and will live and grow truly, and then, and not till then, will he cease from his travail.
D: Narrative:
"Certainly," he said.

"And have we not a right to say in his defence, that the true lover of knowledge is always striving after being -- that is his nature; he will not rest in the multiplicity of individuals which is an appearance only, but will go on -- the keen edge will not be blunted, nor the force of his desire abate until he have attained the knowledge of the true nature of every essence by a sympathetic and kindred power in the soul, and by that power drawing near and mingling and becoming incorporate with very being, having begotten mind and truth, he will have knowledge and will live and grow truly, and then, and not till then, will he cease from his travail."
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly," he said.

"And can't we say in the philosopher's defense that the true lover of knowledge is always striving toward being -- that's simply his nature? He won't rest among the multiplicity of particular things, which are mere appearances. His sharp edge won't be dulled, nor will his desire weaken, until he reaches knowledge of the true nature of every essence.

He does this through a sympathetic, kindred power in his soul. Drawing near to being, mingling with it, becoming one with it, he gives birth to mind and truth. Only then does he truly know, truly live, truly grow. And only then -- not a moment before -- does his labor cease."
