D: Elegant: The true lover of knowledge strives always toward being itself, never resting among mere appearances. Through a kindred power in the soul, he draws near to true being, mingles with it, becomes one with it, and gives birth to mind and truth. Only then does he truly know, truly live, and find rest from his labor.
D: Summary: Socrates defends the philosopher: the genuine lover of knowledge relentlessly pursues being itself, never settling for appearances. Through a sympathetic power in the soul, he unites with true being and gives birth to mind and truth -- only then finding peace.
D: Distilled: The true philosopher never rests until his soul unites with being itself and gives birth to genuine knowledge and truth.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- One of Plato's most lyrical descriptions of philosophical eros -- 'Striving after being' (to on) vs. 'multiplicity of individuals' (appearances/becoming) -- The erotic/generative language ('drawing near,' 'mingling,' 'becoming incorporate,' 'begotten') echoes Diotima's speech in the Symposium (210a-212a) -- 'Sympathetic and kindred power in the soul' = the soul's rational part, which is akin to the Forms -- 'Begotten mind and truth' = genuine knowledge as the offspring of the soul's union with being -- Cf. Symposium: philosophical eros gives birth to wisdom rather than physical children

D: Original:
Nothing, he said, can be more just than such a description of him.

And will the love of a lie be any part of a philosopher's nature? Will he not utterly hate a lie?

He will. And when truth is the captain, we cannot suspect any evil of the band which he leads?

Impossible. Justice and health of mind will be of the company, and temperance will follow after?
D: Narrative:
"Nothing," he said, "can be more just than such a description of him."

"And will the love of a lie be any part of a philosopher's nature? Will he not utterly hate a lie?"

"He will."

"And when truth is the captain, we cannot suspect any evil of the band which he leads?"

"Impossible."

"Justice and health of mind will be of the company, and temperance will follow after?"
D: Refashioned:
"Nothing could be a more fitting description of him," he said.

"And would a love of falsehood be any part of a philosopher's nature? Won't he despise lies?"

"He will."

"And when truth leads the way, can we suspect any corruption in the qualities that follow?"

"Impossible."

"Justice and mental health will be in the company, with temperance close behind?"
D: Elegant: The philosopher utterly hates falsehood. When truth leads, justice and health of mind follow naturally, with temperance close behind.
D: Summary: Socrates reaffirms that the philosopher despises falsehood. Where truth leads, justice, mental health, and temperance naturally follow.
D: Distilled: Where truth leads the soul, justice, health of mind, and temperance inevitably follow.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Truth personified as a captain leading a band of virtues -- 'Health of mind' (hugieia) = psychological wholeness, connecting to Book IV's definition of justice as psychic health -- The unity of the virtues: truth, justice, temperance travel together -- This recapitulates the portrait from 6.3-6.5 in compressed form

D: Original: True, he replied. Neither is there any reason why I should again set in array the philosopher's virtues, as you will doubtless remember that courage, magnificence, apprehension, memory, were his natural gifts. And you objected that, although no one could deny what I then said, still, if you leave words and look at facts, the persons who are thus described are some of them manifestly useless, and the greater number utterly depraved; we were then led to enquire into the grounds of these accusations, and have now arrived at the point of asking why are the majority bad, which question of necessity brought us back to the examination and definition of the true philosopher.
D: Narrative:
"True," he replied.

"Neither is there any reason why I should again set in array the philosopher's virtues, as you will doubtless remember that courage, magnificence, apprehension, memory, were his natural gifts. And you objected that, although no one could deny what I then said, still, if you leave words and look at facts, the persons who are thus described are some of them manifestly useless, and the greater number utterly depraved; we were then led to enquire into the grounds of these accusations, and have now arrived at the point of asking why are the majority bad, which question of necessity brought us back to the examination and definition of the true philosopher."
D: Refashioned:
"True," he replied.

"There's no need for me to list the philosopher's virtues again -- you'll remember that courage, magnificence, quick comprehension, and strong memory were among his natural gifts. Your objection was that, however unanswerable this sounds in theory, when you look at the facts, some of these supposed philosophers are clearly useless and the majority are thoroughly corrupt.

That objection led us to investigate the basis for these accusations. And now we've arrived at the central question: why are the majority bad? Which has necessarily brought us back to examining and defining what a true philosopher actually is."
D: Elegant: The philosopher's virtues -- courage, magnificence, quick comprehension, memory -- are established. But the objection remains: in practice, most philosophers are corrupt and the best are useless. This forces the investigation back to its core question: why are the majority bad?
D: Summary: Socrates summarizes the argument so far: the philosopher's natural virtues have been catalogued, but the practical objection -- that most philosophers are corrupt and the best useless -- has led them back to the fundamental question of why the majority turn bad.
D: Distilled: The philosopher's virtues are established in theory, but the practical objection -- why are most philosophers corrupt? -- drives the inquiry forward.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- This is a structural summary passage, recapping the argument's trajectory -- 'Courage, magnificence, apprehension, memory' = the natural gifts catalogued in 6.3-6.5 -- 'Leave words and look at facts' = Adeimantus' empirical objection from 6.6 -- The dialectical structure: ideal portrait -> objection from reality -> investigation of corruption -- Sets up the crucial argument about how society corrupts philosophical natures (6.17ff)

D: Original: Exactly. And we have next to consider the of the philosophic nature, why so many are spoiled and so few escape spoiling --I am speaking of those who were said to be useless but not wicked --and, when we have done with them, we will speak of the imitators of philosophy, what manner of men are they who aspire after a profession which is above them and of which they are unworthy, and then, by their manifold inconsistencies, bring upon philosophy, and upon all philosophers, that universal reprobation of which we speak.
D: Narrative: "Exactly. And we have next to consider the corruptions of the philosophic nature, why so many are spoiled and so few escape spoiling -- I am speaking of those who were said to be useless but not wicked -- and, when we have done with them, we will speak of the imitators of philosophy, what manner of men are they who aspire after a profession which is above them and of which they are unworthy, and then, by their manifold inconsistencies, bring upon philosophy, and upon all philosophers, that universal reprobation of which we speak."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly. Now we need to examine how the philosophical nature gets corrupted -- why so many are ruined and so few escape unscathed. I'm talking about those we called useless rather than wicked.

Once we've dealt with that, we'll turn to the imitators of philosophy -- what kind of people aspire to a calling that's above them and that they don't deserve? These are the ones who, through their many contradictions and failures, bring philosophy and all genuine philosophers into the universal disrepute we've been discussing."
D: Elegant: Two questions remain: first, how the philosophical nature is corrupted, explaining why so many are ruined and so few survive intact; second, who are the unworthy imitators of philosophy whose inconsistencies bring universal disgrace upon genuine philosophers.
D: Summary: Socrates outlines two remaining inquiries: why genuinely philosophical natures get corrupted, and how unworthy imitators of philosophy bring disgrace upon the entire discipline through their inconsistencies.
D: Distilled: The investigation must explain both how genuine philosophical natures are corrupted and how unworthy imitators bring philosophy into disrepute.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- This passage sets the agenda for the next major section of Book VI -- 'Useless but not wicked' = the best philosophers who withdraw from public life -- 'Imitators of philosophy' = the sophists and pretenders who give philosophy a bad name -- The two-part structure: (1) corruption of genuine natures, (2) invasion by unworthy pretenders -- Anticipates the marriage metaphor of philosophy abandoned by her true suitors (6.30)

D: Original:
What are these corruptions? he said. I will see if I can explain them to you. Every one will admit that a nature having in perfection all the qualities which we required in a philosopher, is a rare plant which is seldom seen among men.

Rare indeed. And what numberless and powerful causes tend to destroy these rare natures!

What causes? In the first place there are their own virtues, their courage, temperance, and the rest of them, every one of which praise worthy qualities (and this is a most singular circumstance) destroys and distracts from philosophy the soul which is the possessor of them.

That is very singular, he replied. Then there are all the ordinary goods of life --beauty, wealth, strength, rank, and great connections in the State --you understand the sort of things --these also have a corrupting and distracting effect.

I understand; but I should like to know more precisely what you mean about them.

Grasp the truth as a whole, I said, and in the right way; you will then have no difficulty in apprehending the preceding remarks, and they will no longer appear strange to you.

And how am I to do so? he asked. Why, I said, we know that all germs or seeds, whether vegetable or animal, when they fail to meet with proper nutriment or climate or soil, in proportion to their vigour, are all the more sensitive to the want of a suitable environment, for evil is a greater enemy to what is good than what is not.

Very true. There is reason in supposing that the finest natures, when under alien conditions, receive more injury than the inferior, because the contrast is greater.

Certainly. And may we not say, Adeimantus, that the most gifted minds, when they are ill-educated, become pre-eminently bad? Do not great crimes and the spirit of pure evil spring out of a fulness of nature ruined by education rather than from any inferiority, whereas weak natures are scarcely capable of any very great good or very great evil?
D: Narrative:
"What are these corruptions?" he said.

"I will see if I can explain them to you. Every one will admit that a nature having in perfection all the qualities which we required in a philosopher, is a rare plant which is seldom seen among men."

"Rare indeed."

"And what numberless and powerful causes tend to destroy these rare natures!"

"What causes?"

"In the first place there are their own virtues, their courage, temperance, and the rest of them, every one of which praiseworthy qualities (and this is a most singular circumstance) destroys and distracts from philosophy the soul which is the possessor of them."

"That is very singular, he replied."

"Then there are all the ordinary goods of life -- beauty, wealth, strength, rank, and great connections in the State -- you understand the sort of things -- these also have a corrupting and distracting effect."

"I understand; but I should like to know more precisely what you mean about them."

"Grasp the truth as a whole," I said, "and in the right way; you will then have no difficulty in apprehending the preceding remarks, and they will no longer appear strange to you."

"And how am I to do so?" he asked.

"Why," I said, "we know that all germs or seeds, whether vegetable or animal, when they fail to meet with proper nutriment or climate or soil, in proportion to their vigour, are all the more sensitive to the want of a suitable environment, for evil is a greater enemy to what is good than what is not."

"Very true."

"There is reason in supposing that the finest natures, when under alien conditions, receive more injury than the inferior, because the contrast is greater."

"Certainly."

"And may we not say, Adeimantus, that the most gifted minds, when they are ill-educated, become pre-eminently bad? Do not great crimes and the spirit of pure evil spring out of a fulness of nature ruined by education rather than from any inferiority, whereas weak natures are scarcely capable of any very great good or very great evil?"
D: Refashioned:
"What are these corrupting forces?" he asked.

"Let me try to explain. Everyone would agree that a nature possessing all the qualities we require in a philosopher is a rare plant, seldom found among people."

"Rare indeed."

"And countless powerful forces work to destroy these rare natures!"

"What forces?"

"First -- and this is the most paradoxical thing -- their own virtues. Their courage, their temperance, and every other praiseworthy quality actually works to destroy and distract the philosophical soul that possesses them."

"That is very strange," he replied.

"Then there are the ordinary goods of life -- beauty, wealth, physical strength, social rank, powerful connections. You know the kind of thing. These too corrupt and distract."

"I understand the list, but I'd like to know more precisely what you mean."

"Grasp the principle as a whole," I said, "and the details will follow. Think of it this way: all seeds, whether plant or animal, when they don't receive the right nourishment, climate, or soil, suffer in proportion to their vigor. The stronger the seed, the more it suffers from the wrong conditions. Evil is a greater enemy to what is good than to what is mediocre."

"Very true."

"So it makes sense that the finest natures, placed in hostile conditions, suffer more damage than inferior ones -- precisely because the gap between what they are and what they could be is so much greater."

"Certainly."

"Then can't we say, Adeimantus, that the most gifted minds, when badly educated, become exceptionally bad? Don't the greatest crimes and the purest evil spring from a powerful nature ruined by bad upbringing, rather than from inherent weakness? Weak natures are barely capable of great good or great evil."
D: Elegant: The philosophical nature is rare, and paradoxically, its very virtues -- courage, temperance, and the rest -- along with worldly advantages like beauty and wealth, work to corrupt it. Like a vigorous seed that suffers most from poor soil, the strongest natures are most damaged by hostile conditions. The most gifted minds, when badly educated, become the most dangerous -- for great evil springs from powerful natures ruined, not from weak ones.
D: Summary: Socrates reveals the paradox of philosophical corruption: the philosopher's own virtues and worldly advantages are what destroy him. Like vigorous seeds needing the right soil, the strongest natures suffer most from bad conditions. The most gifted minds, when corrupted, produce the greatest evil.
D: Distilled: The philosopher's own virtues and advantages paradoxically become the instruments of his corruption -- the finest natures, badly nurtured, produce the greatest evil.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The paradox of virtue becoming corruption is central to Book VI's argument -- The seed/plant analogy: philosophical natures need the right 'soil' (education, political environment) -- 'Evil is a greater enemy to what is good than what is not' = a principle of proportional vulnerability -- This explains why democracies produce both the greatest tyrants and the greatest philosophers -- Cf. Book VIII-IX: the tyrant often emerges from the most gifted nature -- The argument anticipates Alcibiades, the gifted youth corrupted by Athens (though not named here)

D: Original: There I think that you are right. And our philosopher follows the same analogy-he is like a plant which, having proper nurture, must necessarily grow and mature into all virtue, but, if sown and planted in an alien soil, becomes the most noxious of all weeds, unless he be preserved by some divine power. Do you really think, as people so often say, that our youth are corrupted by Sophists, or that private teachers of the art corrupt them in any degree worth speaking of? Are not the public who say these things the greatest of all Sophists? And do they not educate to perfection young and old, men and women alike, and fashion them after their own hearts?
D: Narrative:
"There I think that you are right."

"And our philosopher follows the same analogy -- he is like a plant which, having proper nurture, must necessarily grow and mature into all virtue, but, if sown and planted in an alien soil, becomes the most noxious of all weeds, unless he be preserved by some divine power. Do you really think, as people so often say, that our youth are corrupted by Sophists, or that private teachers of the art corrupt them in any degree worth speaking of? Are not the public who say these things the greatest of all Sophists? And do they not educate to perfection young and old, men and women alike, and fashion them after their own hearts?"
D: Refashioned:
"I think you're right about that."

"And our philosopher follows the same pattern -- he's like a plant that, with proper care, will inevitably grow into full virtue. But plant him in hostile soil, and he becomes the most poisonous of weeds -- unless some divine power preserves him.

And do you really believe, as people constantly say, that it's the Sophists who corrupt the young? That private teachers do any corruption worth mentioning? Aren't the very people who make this complaint the greatest Sophists of all? Isn't the public itself the ultimate educator -- shaping young and old, men and women alike, molding them into its own image?"
D: Elegant: The philosopher is like a plant: properly nurtured, he grows into full virtue; planted in hostile soil, he becomes the most noxious weed. The real corrupters are not the Sophists but the public itself -- the greatest Sophist of all -- which educates everyone, young and old, in its own image.
D: Summary: Socrates extends the plant analogy: the philosopher becomes virtuous with proper nurture but turns into the worst weed in hostile conditions. He provocatively argues that the real corrupters of youth are not the Sophists but the general public, which is the greatest Sophist of all.
D: Distilled: The public itself is the greatest Sophist -- not private teachers, but society at large corrupts philosophical natures by molding everyone in its own image.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Corrupted by Sophists' = a common Athenian complaint, notably the charge against Socrates himself (Apology 19d-20c) -- Socrates reverses the charge: the demos is the real Sophist -- 'Divine power' (theia moira) = the rare cases where a philosophical nature survives corruption -- 'Fashion them after their own hearts' = social conformity as the deepest form of education -- This is a radical critique of democratic culture: the majority's values are the true curriculum -- Cf. Gorgias 463a-466a on rhetoric as the art of flattering the crowd

D: Original: When is this accomplished? he said. When they meet together, and the world sits down at an assembly, or in a court of law, or a theatre, or a camp, or in any other popular resort, and there is a great uproar, and they praise some things which are being said or done, and blame other things, equally exaggerating both, shouting and clapping their hands, and the echo of the rocks and the place in which they are assembled redoubles the sound of the praise or blame --at such a time will not a young man's heart, as they say, leap within him? Will any private training enable him to stand firm against the overwhelming flood of popular opinion? or will he be carried away by the stream? Will he not have the notions of good and evil which the public in general have --he will do as they do, and as they are, such will he be?
D: Narrative:
"When is this accomplished?" he said.

"When they meet together, and the world sits down at an assembly, or in a court of law, or a theatre, or a camp, or in any other popular resort, and there is a great uproar, and they praise some things which are being said or done, and blame other things, equally exaggerating both, shouting and clapping their hands, and the echo of the rocks and the place in which they are assembled redoubles the sound of the praise or blame -- at such a time will not a young man's heart, as they say, leap within him? Will any private training enable him to stand firm against the overwhelming flood of popular opinion? or will he be carried away by the stream? Will he not have the notions of good and evil which the public in general have -- he will do as they do, and as they are, such will he be?"
D: Refashioned:
"When does this happen?" he asked.

"Whenever people gather together -- in an assembly, a courtroom, a theater, a military camp, or any public gathering. There's a tremendous uproar as they praise certain things and condemn others, exaggerating in both directions, shouting and clapping while the echo of the walls amplifies everything into a deafening roar.

At such a moment, won't a young person's heart race? Can any private education hold firm against that overwhelming flood of popular opinion, or will he be swept away? Won't he end up adopting whatever notions of good and evil the crowd holds? He'll do as they do, and become what they are."
D: Elegant: The public educates through the overwhelming force of collective emotion -- in assemblies, courts, theaters, and camps, the crowd's praise and blame drown out all private training. No young person can stand firm against this flood; he will inevitably adopt the crowd's values and become what they are.
D: Summary: Socrates describes how public gatherings -- assemblies, courts, theaters -- create an overwhelming tide of collective emotion that drowns out any private philosophical training. No young person can resist this flood; they inevitably absorb the crowd's values.
D: Distilled: The overwhelming emotional force of public assemblies drowns out private philosophical education, making the young adopt whatever the crowd values.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- A vivid description of crowd psychology and social pressure in Athenian democracy -- The list of venues (assembly, court, theatre, camp) = all major sites of Athenian public life -- 'Echo of the rocks' = the physical amplification of crowd noise, symbolizing how groupthink is reinforced -- The 'flood' or 'stream' metaphor for popular opinion (cf. the stream metaphor for desire in 6.4) -- This anticipates modern crowd psychology (cf. Gustave Le Bon, The Crowd) -- The young person's 'heart leaping' = the emotional pull of belonging to the group

D: Original:
Yes, Socrates; necessity will compel him. And yet, I said, there is a still greater necessity, which has not been mentioned.

What is that? The gentle force of attainder or confiscation or death which, as you are aware, these new Sophists and educators who are the public, apply when their words are powerless.

Indeed they do; and in right good earnest. Now what opinion of any other Sophist, or of any private person, can be expected to overcome in such an unequal contest?
D: Narrative:
"Yes, Socrates; necessity will compel him."

"And yet," I said, "there is a still greater necessity, which has not been mentioned."

"What is that?"

"The gentle force of attainder or confiscation or death which, as you are aware, these new Sophists and educators who are the public, apply when their words are powerless."

"Indeed they do; and in right good earnest."

"Now what opinion of any other Sophist, or of any private person, can be expected to overcome in such an unequal contest?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, Socrates -- he'll be forced to conform."

"And yet," I said, "there's an even greater compulsion we haven't mentioned."

"What's that?"

"The 'gentle' force of legal penalties, property seizure, and execution -- which the public, this great Sophist and educator, applies when its words aren't persuasive enough."

"Indeed they do -- and they mean it."

"So what private teacher or individual Sophist can hope to prevail in such a lopsided contest?"
D: Elegant: Beyond social pressure, the public enforces conformity through legal penalties, confiscation, and death. When persuasion fails, force follows. No private teacher can compete with this power.
D: Summary: Socrates adds that beyond social pressure, the public enforces its values through legal punishments, confiscation, and death. No private Sophist or teacher can compete against such overwhelming coercive power.
D: Distilled: The public enforces its education not only through social pressure but through the ultimate sanctions of punishment and death -- no private teacher can compete.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Gentle force' is deeply ironic -- attainder, confiscation, and death are anything but gentle -- 'These new Sophists...who are the public' = the crowd as the real educator, using both persuasion and coercion -- A clear allusion to Socrates' own fate: the Athenian public executed him for 'corrupting the youth' -- The argument: the real corruption comes from the same public that accuses philosophers of corruption -- Cf. Apology 24b-26b: the trial of Socrates on charges of corrupting youth

D: Original:
None, he replied. No, indeed, I said, even to make the attempt is a great piece of folly; there neither is, nor has been, nor is ever likely to be, any different type of character which has had no other training in virtue but that which is supplied by public opinion --I speak, my friend, of human virtue only; what is more than human, as the proverb says, is not included: for I would not have you ignorant that, in the present evil state of governments, whatever is saved and comes to good is saved by the power of God, as we may truly say.

I quite assent, he replied. Then let me crave your assent also to a further observation.
D: Narrative:
"None," he replied.

"No, indeed," I said, "even to make the attempt is a great piece of folly; there neither is, nor has been, nor is ever likely to be, any different type of character which has had no other training in virtue but that which is supplied by public opinion -- I speak, my friend, of human virtue only; what is more than human, as the proverb says, is not included: for I would not have you ignorant that, in the present evil state of governments, whatever is saved and comes to good is saved by the power of God, as we may truly say."

"I quite assent," he replied.

"Then let me crave your assent also to a further observation."
D: Refashioned:
"None," he replied.

"No indeed," I said. "Even attempting to resist is folly. There has never been, is not now, and never will be a character formed in virtue by anything other than public opinion -- at least as far as human virtue goes. The exception, as the proverb says, is whatever is more than human. And I want to be clear: in the present corrupt state of government, anything that is saved and turns out well is saved, as we may truly say, by divine intervention alone."

"I completely agree," he replied.

"Then let me ask you to consider one more point."
D: Elegant: No character can be formed in virtue against the overwhelming force of public opinion -- only divine intervention can preserve anyone in the current corrupt political order. Human virtue shaped independently of the crowd is essentially impossible.
D: Summary: Socrates declares that no human character can develop virtue independently of public opinion's overwhelming influence. In the present corrupt political order, only divine power can preserve a philosophical nature.
D: Distilled: Under current corrupt governments, no human virtue can develop independently of public opinion -- only divine intervention can save a philosophical nature.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'What is more than human' = a proverbial exception for divine or superhuman cases -- 'Saved by the power of God' (theia moira) = recurring Platonic concept of divine dispensation -- This echoes Socrates' own daimonion (inner divine sign) mentioned in the Apology -- The pessimism about existing governments anticipates the claim that no current state is suitable for philosophy (6.35) -- A rare moment of near-despair in the Republic's argument

D: Original: What are you going to say? Why, that all those mercenary individuals, whom the many call Sophists and whom they deem to be their adversaries, do, in fact, teach nothing but the opinion of the many, that is to say, the opinions of their assemblies; and this is their wisdom. I might compare them to a man who should study the tempers and desires of a mighty strong beast who is fed by him-he would learn how to approach and handle him, also at what times and from what causes he is dangerous or the reverse, and what is the meaning of his several cries, and by what sounds, when another utters them, he is soothed or infuriated; and you may suppose further, that when, by continually attending upon him, he has become perfect in all this, he calls his knowledge wisdom, and makes of it a system or art, which he proceeds to teach, although he has no real notion of what he means by the principles or passions of which he is speaking, but calls this honourable and that dishonourable, or good or evil, or just or unjust, all in accordance with the tastes and tempers of the great brute. Good he pronounces to be that in which the beast delights and evil to be that which he dislikes; and he can give no other account of them except that the just and noble are the necessary, having never himself seen, and having no power of explaining to others the nature of either, or the difference between them, which is immense. By heaven, would not such an one be a rare educator?
D: Narrative:
"What are you going to say?"

"Why, that all those mercenary individuals, whom the many call Sophists and whom they deem to be their adversaries, do, in fact, teach nothing but the opinion of the many, that is to say, the opinions of their assemblies; and this is their wisdom. I might compare them to a man who should study the tempers and desires of a mighty strong beast who is fed by him -- he would learn how to approach and handle him, also at what times and from what causes he is dangerous or the reverse, and what is the meaning of his several cries, and by what sounds, when another utters them, he is soothed or infuriated; and you may suppose further, that when, by continually attending upon him, he has become perfect in all this, he calls his knowledge wisdom, and makes of it a system or art, which he proceeds to teach, although he has no real notion of what he means by the principles or passions of which he is speaking, but calls this honourable and that dishonourable, or good or evil, or just or unjust, all in accordance with the tastes and tempers of the great brute. Good he pronounces to be that in which the beast delights and evil to be that which he dislikes; and he can give no other account of them except that the just and noble are the necessary, having never himself seen, and having no power of explaining to others the nature of either, or the difference between them, which is immense. By heaven, would not such an one be a rare educator?"
D: Refashioned:
"What's your point?"

"This: all those hired intellectuals the public calls Sophists -- the ones they think are their opponents -- actually teach nothing but the public's own opinions. The opinions of their assemblies: that's the Sophists' wisdom.

Imagine someone who tends a large, powerful beast. He studies its moods and appetites -- learning when to approach, what makes it dangerous or calm, what its different cries mean, and which sounds soothe or enrage it. After years of careful observation, he masters all this, calls it 'wisdom,' systematizes it, and starts teaching it.

But he has no actual understanding of the principles behind the beast's reactions. He simply labels whatever pleases the beast 'good' and 'honorable,' and whatever displeases it 'bad' and 'shameful.' He calls what is necessary 'just' and 'noble.' He has never seen the true nature of good or justice, cannot explain the vast difference between genuine goodness and mere crowd-pleasing, and has no capacity to teach that difference to anyone else.

Wouldn't such a person be a remarkable educator?"
D: Elegant: The Sophists teach nothing but the crowd's own opinions, repackaged as wisdom. They are like a keeper who studies a powerful beast's moods -- learning what soothes or enrages it -- then calls this knowledge 'wisdom' and teaches it. He labels whatever pleases the beast 'good' and whatever displeases it 'evil,' having never glimpsed the true nature of either. The difference between genuine goodness and crowd-pleasing is immense, but he cannot see it.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that Sophists merely systematize and teach back the crowd's own opinions. He compares them to a beast-keeper who studies an animal's moods and calls this 'wisdom,' labeling whatever pleases the beast 'good' without any understanding of genuine goodness.
D: Distilled: The Sophists' wisdom is merely a systematized study of the crowd's appetites -- calling whatever pleases the beast 'good' without knowing what goodness truly is.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The Great Beast analogy: one of Plato's most powerful images for democratic culture -- The beast = the demos/public -- The beast-keeper = the Sophist/politician who studies crowd psychology -- 'Calls his knowledge wisdom' = the Sophist's fundamental error: confusing crowd management with genuine knowledge -- The distinction between the necessary/conventional and the genuinely good anticipates the Form of the Good discussion -- Cf. Gorgias 463a-465e: rhetoric as the 'knack' (empeiria) of flattering the crowd vs. genuine political art -- 'The just and noble are the necessary' = the keeper conflates what the beast must tolerate with what is truly good

D: Original: Indeed, he would. And in what way does he who thinks that wisdom is the discernment of the tempers and tastes of the motley multitude, whether in painting or music, or, finally, in politics, differ from him whom I have been describing For when a man consorts with the many, and exhibits to them his poem or other work of art or the service which he has done the State, making them his judges when he is not obliged, the so-called necessity of Diomede will oblige him to produce whatever they praise. And yet the reasons are utterly ludicrous which they give in confirmation of their own notions about the honourable and good. Did you ever hear any of them which were not?
D: Narrative:
"Indeed, he would."

"And in what way does he who thinks that wisdom is the discernment of the tempers and tastes of the motley multitude, whether in painting or music, or, finally, in politics, differ from him whom I have been describing? For when a man consorts with the many, and exhibits to them his poem or other work of art or the service which he has done the State, making them his judges when he is not obliged, the so-called necessity of Diomede will oblige him to produce whatever they praise. And yet the reasons are utterly ludicrous which they give in confirmation of their own notions about the honourable and good. Did you ever hear any of them which were not?"
D: Refashioned:
"A remarkable educator indeed."

"And how is someone who thinks wisdom means understanding the tastes and moods of the diverse crowd -- whether in painting, music, or politics -- any different from the beast-keeper I just described?

When someone takes his work -- a poem, a piece of art, or a political achievement -- before the crowd and makes them his judges when he doesn't have to, a kind of iron necessity forces him to produce whatever they praise. And the reasons the crowd gives for why something is 'honorable' or 'good'? They're utterly absurd. Have you ever heard any that weren't?"
D: Elegant: Anyone who equates wisdom with reading the crowd's tastes -- in art, music, or politics -- is no different from the beast-keeper. Making the crowd your judge creates an iron compulsion to produce whatever they praise, and their reasons for calling things 'good' or 'honorable' are always absurd.
D: Summary: Socrates extends the beast-keeper analogy: anyone in art, music, or politics who makes the crowd their judge becomes enslaved to public taste. The compulsion to produce what the crowd praises is inescapable, and the crowd's justifications for their values are always absurd.
D: Distilled: Making the crowd your judge in art or politics enslaves you to their taste -- and their reasons for what they call 'good' are always absurd.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Necessity of Diomede' = a Greek proverb meaning an absolute, inescapable compulsion (from Diomedes who compelled his guests) -- The argument extends from politics to all arts: painting, music, poetry -- This critiques democratic aesthetics as well as democratic politics -- Cf. Ion and Republic Book X on poets as imitators who please crowds -- The argument anticipates modern critiques of market-driven art and populist politics -- 'Consorts with the many' = the artist/politician who makes popular approval his standard

D: Original: No, nor am I likely to hear. You recognise the truth of what I have been saying? Then let me ask you to consider further whether the world will ever be induced to believe in the existence of absolute beauty rather than of the many beautiful, or of the absolute in each kind rather than of the many in each kind?
D: Narrative:
"No, nor am I likely to hear."

"You recognise the truth of what I have been saying? Then let me ask you to consider further whether the world will ever be induced to believe in the existence of absolute beauty rather than of the many beautiful, or of the absolute in each kind rather than of the many in each kind?"
D: Refashioned:
"No, and I don't expect to."

"So you see the truth of what I'm saying. Then consider this further question: will the world ever be brought to believe in absolute beauty -- as opposed to the many beautiful things? Or in the absolute form of anything, rather than the many particular instances?"
D: Elegant: The crowd will never be brought to believe in absolute beauty rather than many beautiful things, or in the absolute form of anything rather than its many particular instances.
D: Summary: Socrates asks whether the general public can ever be brought to believe in absolute Forms -- such as absolute beauty -- rather than remaining focused on the many particular beautiful things.
D: Distilled: The multitude will never believe in absolute beauty or any Form -- they can only see the many particulars.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Absolute beauty' vs. 'the many beautiful' = the Form of Beauty vs. particular beautiful things -- This restates the fundamental metaphysical divide from Book V (475e-476d) -- The crowd is constitutionally unable to be philosophical because they cannot grasp universals -- This has implications for the Form of the Good discussion to come -- The question is rhetorical: the answer is clearly 'no'

D: Original:
Certainly not. Then the world cannot possibly be a philosopher? Impossible. And therefore philosophers must inevitably fall under the censure of the world?

They must. And of individuals who consort with the mob and seek to please them?

That is evident. Then, do you see any way in which the philosopher can be preserved in his calling to the end? and remember what we were saying of him, that he was to have quickness and memory and courage and magnificence --these were admitted by us to be the true philosopher's gifts.

Yes. Will not such an one from his early childhood be in all things first among all, especially if his bodily endowments are like his mental ones?

Certainly, he said. And his friends and fellow-citizens will want to use him as he gets older for their own purposes?

No question. Falling at his feet, they will make requests to him and do him honour and flatter him, because they want to get into their hands now, the power which he will one day possess.
D: Narrative:
"Certainly not."

"Then the world cannot possibly be a philosopher?"

"Impossible."

"And therefore philosophers must inevitably fall under the censure of the world?"

"They must."

"And of individuals who consort with the mob and seek to please them?"

"That is evident."

"Then, do you see any way in which the philosopher can be preserved in his calling to the end? and remember what we were saying of him, that he was to have quickness and memory and courage and magnificence -- these were admitted by us to be the true philosopher's gifts."

"Yes."

"Will not such an one from his early childhood be in all things first among all, especially if his bodily endowments are like his mental ones?"

"Certainly," he said.

"And his friends and fellow-citizens will want to use him as he gets older for their own purposes?"

"No question."

"Falling at his feet, they will make requests to him and do him honour and flatter him, because they want to get into their hands now, the power which he will one day possess."
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly not."

"Then the world can never be philosophical?"

"Impossible."

"So philosophers will inevitably face the world's disapproval?"

"They must."

"Along with disapproval from individuals who court the mob and try to please them?"

"Obviously."

"Then can you see any way to preserve the philosopher in his calling? Remember, we said his gifts were quickness, strong memory, courage, and magnanimity."

"Yes."

"Won't someone with these qualities stand out from earliest childhood, especially if his physical qualities match his mental ones?"

"Certainly," he said.

"And as he grows older, won't his friends and fellow citizens want to use him for their own purposes?"

"No question."

"They'll fall at his feet, making requests, heaping honors and flattery on him -- because they want to secure for themselves the power he will one day wield."
D: Elegant: The world can never be philosophical, so philosophers will always face public censure. The gifted philosophical youth -- quick, courageous, magnanimous -- will stand out from childhood, and society will immediately try to exploit him, heaping flattery and honors to capture his future power for their own purposes.
D: Summary: Since the crowd can never be philosophical, philosophers will always be censured. The young person with a philosophical nature -- gifted, courageous, magnanimous -- will be targeted early by those who want to exploit his talents, receiving flattery and honors designed to capture his future influence.
D: Distilled: Society inevitably tries to co-opt the gifted philosophical youth through flattery and honor, seeking to capture his future power before philosophy can shape him.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The corruption of the philosophical youth begins: society targets his gifts -- 'Quickness and memory and courage and magnificence' = the very virtues catalogued in 6.3-6.5, now shown as vulnerabilities -- The pattern described fits Alcibiades perfectly: brilliant, beautiful, courted by Athenian politicians from youth -- 'Power which he will one day possess' = political influence that follows from natural gifts -- The argument: the philosopher's gifts make him a target for political exploitation -- Cf. Alcibiades I (if genuine) for Socrates' attempt to save such a youth

D: Original:
That often happens, he said. And what will a man such as he be likely to do under such circumstances, especially if he be a citizen of a great city, rich and noble, and a tall proper youth? Will he not be full of boundless aspirations, and fancy himself able to manage the affairs of Hellenes and of barbarians, and having got such notions into his head will he not dilate and elevate himself in the fulness of vain pomp and senseless pride?

To be sure he will. Now, when he is in this state of mind, if some one gently comes to him and tells him that he is a fool and must get understanding, which can only be got by slaving for it, do you think that, under such adverse circumstances, he will be easily induced to listen?
D: Narrative:
"That often happens," he said.

"And what will a man such as he be likely to do under such circumstances, especially if he be a citizen of a great city, rich and noble, and a tall proper youth? Will he not be full of boundless aspirations, and fancy himself able to manage the affairs of Hellenes and of barbarians, and having got such notions into his head will he not dilate and elevate himself in the fulness of vain pomp and senseless pride?"

"To be sure he will."

"Now, when he is in this state of mind, if some one gently comes to him and tells him that he is a fool and must get understanding, which can only be got by slaving for it, do you think that, under such adverse circumstances, he will be easily induced to listen?"
D: Refashioned:
"That happens all the time," he said.

"And what will such a person do in those circumstances -- especially if he's a citizen of a great city, rich, well-born, and physically impressive? Won't he swell with limitless ambition, convinced he can manage the affairs of Greeks and non-Greeks alike? Won't he inflate himself with empty grandeur and senseless pride?"

"Absolutely."

"Now, while he's in this state of mind, imagine someone approaches him gently and tells him he's a fool -- that real understanding can only be won through hard, grinding work. Do you think, given everything working against it, he'll be easily persuaded to listen?"
D: Elegant: The gifted youth in a great city, rich and well-born, swells with boundless ambition and empty pride, convinced he can manage the world's affairs. When someone gently tells him he's a fool who must slave for real understanding, such a youth will not easily be persuaded to listen.
D: Summary: Socrates describes how a gifted young person in a great city becomes inflated with ambition and pride through flattery. When someone tries to tell him he needs to work hard for genuine understanding, he is unlikely to listen.
D: Distilled: The gifted youth, inflated by flattery and ambition, will not listen when told he must slave for genuine understanding.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'A citizen of a great city, rich and noble, and a tall proper youth' = a clear portrait of Alcibiades, though unnamed -- 'Affairs of Hellenes and barbarians' = pan-Hellenic and imperial ambitions, characteristic of Athenian political culture -- 'Vain pomp and senseless pride' = hubris, the classic Greek vice -- The 'someone who gently comes' = a figure like Socrates himself, who tried to redirect Alcibiades toward philosophy -- 'Slaving for understanding' = the demanding nature of genuine philosophical education -- Cf. Alcibiades I 104a-106a, where Socrates confronts Alcibiades about his ambitions

D: Original: Far otherwise. And even if there be some one who through inherent goodness or natural reasonableness has had his eyes opened a little and is humbled and taken captive by philosophy, how will his friends behave when they think that they are likely to lose the advantage which they were hoping to reap from his companionship? Will they not do and say anything to prevent him from yielding to his better nature and to render his teacher powerless, using to this end private intrigues as well as public prosecutions?
D: Narrative:
"Far otherwise."

"And even if there be some one who through inherent goodness or natural reasonableness has had his eyes opened a little and is humbled and taken captive by philosophy, how will his friends behave when they think that they are likely to lose the advantage which they were hoping to reap from his companionship? Will they not do and say anything to prevent him from yielding to his better nature and to render his teacher powerless, using to this end private intrigues as well as public prosecutions?"
D: Refashioned:
"Far from it."

"And even if someone -- through inherent goodness or natural open-mindedness -- does have his eyes opened a little, is humbled, and begins to be drawn toward philosophy, how will his friends and associates react? When they realize they might lose the political advantages they expected to gain from his friendship, won't they do and say anything to stop him? They'll try to prevent him from following his better nature and to neutralize his teacher -- using private scheming and public prosecution alike."
D: Elegant: Even if a gifted youth is drawn toward philosophy through inherent goodness, his friends and political allies will fight to prevent it -- using both private intrigue and public prosecution -- to preserve the advantages they expect to gain from his power.
D: Summary: Even when a gifted youth begins to turn toward philosophy, his friends and political allies will use every means -- private intrigues and public prosecutions -- to prevent it, since they stand to lose the political advantages they expected from his companionship.
D: Distilled: Society fights to prevent the gifted youth from turning to philosophy, using both private scheming and public prosecution to neutralize his philosophical teachers.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Public prosecutions' = a direct allusion to Socrates' own trial, where he was prosecuted for allegedly corrupting the youth -- 'Private intrigues' = behind-the-scenes pressure from those who benefited from the youth's political ambitions -- The dynamic described: society actively resists philosophical conversion of its most talented members -- Cf. Apology 23d-24a: Socrates' prosecutors acted partly from personal resentment -- The 'teacher' being rendered powerless = Socrates' own fate

D: Original:
There can be no doubt of it. And how can one who is thus circumstanced ever become a philosopher?

Impossible. Then were we not right in saying that even the very qualities which make a man a philosopher may, if he be ill-educated, divert him from philosophy, no less than riches and their accompaniments and the other so-called goods of life?
D: Narrative:
"There can be no doubt of it."

"And how can one who is thus circumstanced ever become a philosopher?"

"Impossible."

"Then were we not right in saying that even the very qualities which make a man a philosopher may, if he be ill-educated, divert him from philosophy, no less than riches and their accompaniments and the other so-called goods of life?"
D: Refashioned:
"There's no doubt about it."

"And how can someone in those circumstances ever become a philosopher?"

"It's impossible."

"Then weren't we right to say that the very qualities that make someone a natural philosopher -- if he's badly educated -- can actually divert him away from philosophy? And that riches, social status, and all the other supposed goods of life work the same way?"
D: Elegant: The very qualities that make someone a natural philosopher, combined with bad education and worldly advantages, become the forces that divert him from philosophy. The philosopher's own gifts become his undoing.
D: Summary: Socrates confirms the paradox: the very qualities that constitute a philosophical nature -- combined with poor education and worldly advantages -- are precisely what drive the gifted person away from philosophy.
D: Distilled: The philosopher's own natural gifts, corrupted by bad education and worldly success, become the very forces that divert him from philosophy.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- This completes the paradox introduced in 6.17: the philosopher's virtues are his vulnerabilities -- 'So-called goods of life' = wealth, beauty, strength, connections (6.17) -- The argument structure: gifts + wrong environment = corruption away from philosophy -- Cf. Book VIII: the decline of constitutions follows a similar logic of corruption through excess -- The conclusion is that philosophical corruption is systematic, not accidental

D: Original: We were quite right. Thus, my excellent friend, is brought about all that ruin and failure which I have been describing of the natures best adapted to the best of all pursuits; they are natures which we maintain to be rare at any time; this being the class out of which come the men who are the authors of the greatest evil to States and individuals; and also of the greatest good when the tide carries them in that direction; but a small man never was the doer of any great thing either to individuals or to States.
D: Narrative:
"We were quite right."

"Thus, my excellent friend, is brought about all that ruin and failure which I have been describing of the natures best adapted to the best of all pursuits; they are natures which we maintain to be rare at any time; this being the class out of which come the men who are the authors of the greatest evil to States and individuals; and also of the greatest good when the tide carries them in that direction; but a small man never was the doer of any great thing either to individuals or to States."
D: Refashioned:
"We were exactly right."

"And so, my friend, this is how the ruin and failure I've been describing comes about -- the destruction of natures best suited for the highest pursuit. These natures are rare in any era. From this class come the people who cause the greatest evil to states and individuals -- and also the greatest good, when the current carries them that way. But a small, mediocre person never accomplished anything truly great, for good or for evil."
D: Elegant: The ruin of the finest natures, best suited for philosophy, produces both the greatest evil and the greatest good. A mediocre person never accomplishes anything truly significant -- only great natures have the capacity for great impact, whether for destruction or salvation.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes that the ruin of the finest philosophical natures is what produces history's greatest villains -- but also its greatest benefactors. Mediocre people never achieve anything of great significance, for good or ill.
D: Distilled: Great natures produce both the greatest good and the greatest evil -- mediocre natures never produce anything of significance.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'A small man never was the doer of any great thing' = an important Platonic principle -- The same nature that could become a philosopher-king becomes a tyrant when corrupted -- Cf. Book VIII-IX: the tyrant emerges from the same gifted class as the philosopher -- Historical examples: Alcibiades (great gifts, great destruction) vs. Socrates (great gifts, great good) -- This principle applies to Plato's theory of political change: transformative leaders come from the same rare class

D: Original: That is most true, he said. And so philosophy is left desolate, with her marriage rite incomplete: for her own have fallen away and forsaken her, and while they are leading a false and unbecoming life, other unworthy persons, seeing that she has no kinsmen to be her protectors, enter in and dishonour her; and fasten upon her the reproaches which, as you say, her reprovers utter, who affirm of her votaries that some are good for nothing, and that the greater number deserve the severest punishment.
D: Narrative:
"That is most true," he said.

"And so philosophy is left desolate, with her marriage rite incomplete: for her own have fallen away and forsaken her, and while they are leading a false and unbecoming life, other unworthy persons, seeing that she has no kinsmen to be her protectors, enter in and dishonour her; and fasten upon her the reproaches which, as you say, her reprovers utter, who affirm of her votaries that some are good for nothing, and that the greater number deserve the severest punishment."
D: Refashioned:
"That is absolutely true," he said.

"And so philosophy is left desolate, her marriage ceremony incomplete. Her true suitors have fallen away and abandoned her to live false, unworthy lives. Meanwhile, other unworthy people -- seeing that she has no family left to protect her -- move in and dishonor her. They fasten onto her all the reproaches you mentioned: that her followers are good for nothing, and the majority deserve the harshest punishment."
D: Elegant: Philosophy is left desolate, her marriage incomplete. Her true suitors have abandoned her for false lives, and unworthy pretenders -- seeing her unprotected -- move in, dishonor her, and bring upon her the reproach that her followers are either useless or deserving of punishment.
D: Summary: Socrates personifies philosophy as a woman abandoned by her rightful suitors, left vulnerable to unworthy pretenders who dishonor her and bring upon her the very reproaches that give philosophy its bad reputation.
D: Distilled: Philosophy is abandoned by her true suitors and dishonored by unworthy pretenders, who bring upon her the reproaches that damage her reputation.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Philosophy personified as a bride or noblewoman (a striking gendered metaphor) -- 'Marriage rite incomplete' = the union of philosophy with its proper practitioners never consummated -- 'Her own have fallen away' = the genuinely gifted who were corrupted -- 'Unworthy persons' = the pretenders and sophists who fill the vacuum -- This answers Adeimantus' original objection (6.6): philosophy's bad reputation comes from its false practitioners, not from philosophy itself -- The marriage metaphor anticipates the 'bald tinker' image (6.32)

D: Original: That is certainly what people say. Yes; and what else would you expect, I said, when you think of the puny creatures who, seeing this land open to them --a land well stocked with fair names and showy titles --like prisoners running out of prison into a sanctuary, take a leap out of their trades into philosophy; those who do so being probably the cleverest hands at their own miserable crafts? For, although philosophy be in this evil case, still there remains a dignity about her which is not to be found in the arts. And many are thus attracted by her whose natures are imperfect and whose souls are maimed and disfigured by their meannesses, as their bodies are by their trades and crafts. Is not this unavoidable?
D: Narrative:
"That is certainly what people say."

"Yes; and what else would you expect," I said, "when you think of the puny creatures who, seeing this land open to them -- a land well stocked with fair names and showy titles -- like prisoners running out of prison into a sanctuary, take a leap out of their trades into philosophy; those who do so being probably the cleverest hands at their own miserable crafts? For, although philosophy be in this evil case, still there remains a dignity about her which is not to be found in the arts. And many are thus attracted by her whose natures are imperfect and whose souls are maimed and disfigured by their meannesses, as their bodies are by their trades and crafts. Is not this unavoidable?"
D: Refashioned:
"That's certainly what people say."

"And what else would you expect," I said, "when you consider the kind of people who see this territory wide open -- a field full of impressive titles and distinguished names -- and leap from their petty trades into philosophy like prisoners escaping into a sanctuary?

These are probably the cleverest operators in their own small-time professions. For despite philosophy's fallen state, she still carries a dignity that no mere trade or craft possesses. So she attracts people with imperfect natures -- souls stunted and deformed by their small-mindedness, just as their bodies are by their manual labor. Isn't this inevitable?"
D: Elegant: Philosophy, despite her fallen state, retains a dignity that trades and crafts lack. This attracts the wrong people -- clever operators from petty professions who leap into philosophy like prisoners into sanctuary, bringing with them souls deformed by small-mindedness, just as their bodies are shaped by manual labor.
D: Summary: Despite philosophy's degraded condition, she retains a dignity that attracts the wrong people -- clever but small-minded tradesmen who leap into philosophy, bringing deformed souls with them, drawn by her prestigious titles and reputation.
D: Distilled: Philosophy's residual dignity attracts the wrong people -- clever tradesmen with small souls who are drawn to her prestigious titles rather than her truth.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Prisoners running into a sanctuary' = the asylum metaphor -- philosophy as a refuge from low-status work -- The social prejudice against manual labor reflects aristocratic Greek values -- 'Fair names and showy titles' = the philosopher's reputation for wisdom -- The critique targets social climbers who use philosophy for status -- 'Souls maimed and disfigured by their meannesses' = the deformation of character by petty concerns -- Cf. the 'bald tinker' metaphor that follows (6.32)

D: Original:
Yes. Are they not exactly like a bald little tinker who has just got out of durance and come into a fortune; he takes a bath and puts on a new coat, and is decked out as a bridegroom going to marry his master's daughter, who is left poor and desolate?

A most exact parallel. What will be the issue of such marriages? Will they not be vile and bastard?

There can be no question of it. And when persons who are unworthy of education approach philosophy and make an alliance with her who is a rank above them what sort of ideas and opinions are likely to be generated? Will they not be sophisms captivating to the ear, having nothing in them genuine, or worthy of or akin to true wisdom?
D: Narrative:
"Yes."

"Are they not exactly like a bald little tinker who has just got out of durance and come into a fortune; he takes a bath and puts on a new coat, and is decked out as a bridegroom going to marry his master's daughter, who is left poor and desolate?"

"A most exact parallel."

"What will be the issue of such marriages? Will they not be vile and bastard?"

"There can be no question of it."

"And when persons who are unworthy of education approach philosophy and make an alliance with her who is a rank above them what sort of ideas and opinions are likely to be generated? Will they not be sophisms captivating to the ear, having nothing in them genuine, or worthy of or akin to true wisdom?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes."

"Aren't they exactly like a bald little tinker who's just been released from prison and come into a fortune? He takes a bath, puts on fine clothes, and gets himself up as a bridegroom to marry his former master's daughter -- who's been left poor and abandoned."

"That's a perfect parallel."

"And what will come of such marriages? Won't the offspring be base and illegitimate?"

"Without question."

"And when people unworthy of real education approach philosophy -- a discipline far above them -- and form an alliance with her, what kind of ideas and opinions will they produce? Won't they be clever-sounding sophisms with nothing genuine in them -- nothing worthy of or akin to true wisdom?"
D: Elegant: The unworthy pretender to philosophy is like a bald tinker who inherits a fortune and dresses up to marry his abandoned master's daughter. Such unions produce only base, illegitimate offspring -- sophisms that sound clever but contain nothing genuine or akin to true wisdom.
D: Summary: Socrates compares unworthy practitioners of philosophy to a freed tinker who marries above his station. Such unions produce only illegitimate ideas -- clever-sounding sophisms with no genuine wisdom in them.
D: Distilled: When unworthy people claim philosophy, they produce only sophisticated-sounding nonsense -- the illegitimate offspring of a mismatched union.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The 'bald tinker' metaphor continues the marriage/bride imagery of 6.30 -- Philosophy = the abandoned noblewoman; the pretender = the social-climbing tinker -- 'Vile and bastard' offspring = the sophisms produced by false philosophy -- This explains why philosophy has a bad reputation: its public face is the products of unworthy practitioners -- Cf. Theaetetus 150c-151d: Socrates as midwife distinguishing genuine philosophical offspring from 'wind eggs'

D: Original: No doubt, he said. Then, Adeimantus, I said, the worthy disciples of philosophy will be but a small remnant: perchance some noble and well-educated person, detained by exile in her service, who in the absence of corrupting influences remains devoted to her; or some lofty soul born in a mean city, the politics of which he contemns and neglects; and there may be a gifted few who leave the arts, which they justly despise, and come to her; --or peradventure there are some who are restrained by our friend Theages' bridle; for everything in the life of Theages conspired to divert him from philosophy; but ill-health kept him away from politics. My own case of the internal sign is hardly worth mentioning, for rarely, if ever, has such a monitor been given to any other man. Those who belong to this small class have tasted how sweet and blessed a possession philosophy is, and have also seen enough of the madness of the multitude; and they know that no politician is honest, nor is there any champion of justice at whose side they may fight and be saved. Such an one may be compared to a man who has fallen among wild beasts --he will not join in the wickedness of his fellows, but neither is he able singly to resist all their fierce natures, and therefore seeing that he would be of no use to the State or to his friends, and reflecting that he would have to throw away his life without doing any good either to himself or others, he holds his peace, and goes his own way. He is like one who, in the storm of dust and sleet which the driving wind hurries along, retires under the shelter of a wall; and seeing the rest of mankind full of wickedness, he is content, if only he can live his own life and be pure from evil or unrighteousness, and depart in peace and good-will, with bright hopes.
D: Narrative:
"No doubt," he said.

"Then, Adeimantus," I said, "the worthy disciples of philosophy will be but a small remnant: perchance some noble and well-educated person, detained by exile in her service, who in the absence of corrupting influences remains devoted to her; or some lofty soul born in a mean city, the politics of which he contemns and neglects; and there may be a gifted few who leave the arts, which they justly despise, and come to her; -- or peradventure there are some who are restrained by our friend Theages' bridle; for everything in the life of Theages conspired to divert him from philosophy; but ill-health kept him away from politics. My own case of the internal sign is hardly worth mentioning, for rarely, if ever, has such a monitor been given to any other man.

Those who belong to this small class have tasted how sweet and blessed a possession philosophy is, and have also seen enough of the madness of the multitude; and they know that no politician is honest, nor is there any champion of justice at whose side they may fight and be saved. Such an one may be compared to a man who has fallen among wild beasts -- he will not join in the wickedness of his fellows, but neither is he able singly to resist all their fierce natures, and therefore seeing that he would be of no use to the State or to his friends, and reflecting that he would have to throw away his life without doing any good either to himself or others, he holds his peace, and goes his own way. He is like one who, in the storm of dust and sleet which the driving wind hurries along, retires under the shelter of a wall; and seeing the rest of mankind full of wickedness, he is content, if only he can live his own life and be pure from evil or unrighteousness, and depart in peace and good-will, with bright hopes."
D: Refashioned:
"No doubt," he said.

"So, Adeimantus, the worthy disciples of philosophy will be just a small remnant. Perhaps someone noble and well-educated who is detained in philosophy's service by exile, and without corrupting influences remains devoted to her. Or a great soul born in a small city whose politics he scorns and ignores. Or a gifted few who leave other professions they rightly despise and come to philosophy. Or perhaps someone restrained by what we might call 'Theages' bridle' -- everything in Theages' life conspired to pull him from philosophy, but chronic illness kept him out of politics. My own case -- my inner divine sign -- is hardly worth mentioning, since such a monitor has rarely, if ever, been given to anyone else.

Those in this small class have tasted how sweet and blessed a thing philosophy is. They've also seen enough of the crowd's madness to know that no politician is honest, and there is no champion of justice at whose side they might fight and survive.

Such a person is like someone who has fallen among wild beasts. He won't join in their savagery, but he can't resist them all alone either. Seeing that he'd be useless to the state or to his friends -- that he'd have to throw his life away without doing anyone any good -- he keeps quiet and goes his own way.

He's like a traveler caught in a storm of dust and driving rain who takes shelter behind a wall. Watching the rest of humanity drown in wickedness, he is content if he can live his own life free from injustice, and depart in peace and goodwill, with bright hopes."
D: Elegant: The true disciples of philosophy are a tiny remnant -- exiles, souls born in small cities, a gifted few who abandon other professions, or those kept from politics by illness or divine signs. They have tasted philosophy's sweetness and seen the crowd's madness. Knowing no politician is honest and no champion of justice exists, they are like travelers sheltering behind a wall during a storm, content to live their own lives free from injustice, departing in peace with bright hopes.
D: Summary: Socrates describes the tiny remnant of genuine philosophers: exiles, great souls in small cities, and a few protected by circumstance. Having tasted philosophy and witnessed the crowd's madness, they withdraw from public life like travelers sheltering from a storm, content to live justly and die in peace.
D: Distilled: The few genuine philosophers, knowing the madness of the crowd and the corruption of politics, withdraw like storm-sheltering travelers -- content to live justly and depart in peace.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Theages' bridle' refers to Theages, a would-be politician kept from politics by illness (cf. the dialogue Theages, possibly spurious) -- 'My own case of the internal sign' = Socrates' daimonion, his famous inner voice that warned him away from certain actions (Apology 31c-d) -- The 'man among wild beasts' = the philosopher in a corrupt society -- The 'wall in the storm' = one of Book VI's most beautiful images for philosophical withdrawal -- This passage is deeply autobiographical, reflecting Socrates' own withdrawal from Athenian politics (Apology 31c-32e) -- 'Bright hopes' (lampras elpidas) = hope for a good afterlife, connecting to the myth of Er (Book X) -- The passage is also melancholic: the philosopher achieves only private virtue, not the public good he could accomplish in a just state

D: Original:
Yes, he said, and he will have done a great work before he departs.

A great work --yes; but not the greatest, unless he find a State suitable to him; for in a State which is suitable to him, he will have a larger growth and be the saviour of his country, as well as of himself.

The causes why philosophy is in such an evil name have now been sufficiently explained: the injustice of the charges against her has been shown-is there anything more which you wish to say?

Nothing more on that subject, he replied; but I should like to know which of the governments now existing is in your opinion the one adapted to her.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "and he will have done a great work before he departs."

"A great work -- yes; but not the greatest, unless he find a State suitable to him; for in a State which is suitable to him, he will have a larger growth and be the saviour of his country, as well as of himself."

"The causes why philosophy is in such an evil name have now been sufficiently explained: the injustice of the charges against her has been shown -- is there anything more which you wish to say?"

"Nothing more on that subject," he replied; "but I should like to know which of the governments now existing is in your opinion the one adapted to her."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," he said, "and he will have accomplished something great before he goes."

"A great thing, yes -- but not the greatest. For if he found a state suited to him, he would grow to his full stature and become the savior of his country as well as of himself."

"The reasons philosophy has such a bad name have now been sufficiently explained. The injustice of the charges against her has been demonstrated. Is there anything more you want to say on this subject?"

"Nothing more about that," he replied. "But I'd like to know: which of the existing governments do you think is suited to philosophy?"
D: Elegant: The philosopher who withdraws does something great -- but not the greatest. In a suitable state, he would grow to full stature and save his country along with himself. The causes of philosophy's bad reputation have been explained. The question now is: which existing government suits philosophy?
D: Summary: Adeimantus acknowledges the withdrawn philosopher's achievement, but Socrates insists it falls short of what would be possible in a suitable state, where the philosopher could save both himself and his country. The causes of philosophy's bad reputation have been explained; Adeimantus asks which existing government suits philosophy.
D: Distilled: The withdrawn philosopher achieves private virtue, but only a suitable state would allow him to reach full stature and save his country.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Not the greatest' = private virtue is inferior to the public good the philosopher could achieve as ruler -- 'Saviour of his country' = the philosopher-king ideal -- This sets up the next question: does any existing state suit philosophy? (The answer will be no -- 6.35) -- The transition from explaining philosophy's bad reputation to the question of implementation -- Cf. Book VII 519c-521b: the philosopher must be compelled to return to the Cave to govern

D: Original: Not any of them, I said; and that is precisely the accusation which I bring against them --not one of them is worthy of the philosophic nature, and hence that nature is warped and estranged; --as the exotic seed which is sown in a foreign land becomes denaturalized, and is wont to be overpowered and to lose itself in the new soil, even so this growth of philosophy, instead of persisting, degenerates and receives another character. But if philosophy ever finds in the State that perfection which she herself is, then will be seen that she is in truth divine, and that all other things, whether natures of men or institutions, are but human; --and now, I know that you are going to ask, what that State is.
D: Narrative: "Not any of them," I said; "and that is precisely the accusation which I bring against them -- not one of them is worthy of the philosophic nature, and hence that nature is warped and estranged; -- as the exotic seed which is sown in a foreign land becomes denaturalized, and is wont to be overpowered and to lose itself in the new soil, even so this growth of philosophy, instead of persisting, degenerates and receives another character. But if philosophy ever finds in the State that perfection which she herself is, then will be seen that she is in truth divine, and that all other things, whether natures of men or institutions, are but human; -- and now, I know that you are going to ask, what that State is."
D: Refashioned:
"Not one of them," I said. "And that is precisely my charge against them -- not a single existing government is worthy of the philosophical nature. That's why the philosophical nature gets warped and alienated.

It's like an exotic seed planted in foreign soil: it becomes denaturalized, overwhelmed, and loses its true character. In the same way, the growth of philosophy degenerates and takes on a different character in an unsuitable state.

But if philosophy ever finds a state as perfect as she herself is, then it will become clear that she is truly divine -- and that everything else, whether human natures or institutions, is merely human.

And now -- I know you're about to ask what that state would be."
D: Elegant: No existing government is worthy of the philosophical nature, which is why it becomes warped -- like an exotic seed that degenerates in foreign soil. But if philosophy ever found a state as perfect as herself, she would be revealed as truly divine, and everything else as merely human.
D: Summary: Socrates declares that no existing government is suitable for philosophy, which is why philosophical natures are warped like exotic seeds in foreign soil. Only a state as perfect as philosophy herself would reveal her divine nature.
D: Distilled: No existing state deserves the philosophical nature -- philosophy degenerates in foreign soil, but in the right state, she would be revealed as truly divine.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The exotic seed metaphor: philosophy requires the right political 'soil' to flourish -- 'Denaturalized' = the philosophical nature takes on alien characteristics in a hostile environment -- 'She is in truth divine' = philosophy's nature is divine when uncorrupted -- The radical claim: all existing governments are inadequate for philosophy -- This leads to the question of whether the ideal state they're constructing is that suitable state -- Cf. the plant/seed metaphor from 6.17-18

D: Original:
No, he said; there you are wrong, for I was going to ask another question --whether it is the State of which. we are the founders and inventors, or some other?

Yes, I replied, ours in most respects; but you may remember my saying before, that some living authority would always be required in the State having the same idea of the constitution which guided you when as legislator you were laying down the laws.

That was said, he replied. Yes, but not in a satisfactory manner; you frightened us by interposing objections, which certainly showed that the discussion would be long and difficult; and what still remains is the reverse of easy.

What is there remaining? The question how the study of philosophy may be so ordered as not to be the ruin of the State: All great attempts are attended with risk; 'hard is the good,' as men say.

Still, he said, let the point be cleared up, and the enquiry will then be complete.
