D: Narrative: "Nay," I said, "ask if you will; but I am certain that you have heard the answer many times, and now you either do not understand me or, as I rather think, you are disposed to be troublesome; for you have often been told that the idea of good is the highest knowledge, and that all other things become useful and advantageous only by their use of this. You can hardly be ignorant that of this I was about to speak, concerning which, as you have often heard me say, we know so little; and, without which, any other knowledge or possession of any kind will profit us nothing. Do you think that the possession of all other things is of any value if we do not possess the good? or the knowledge of all other things if we have no knowledge of beauty and goodness?"
D: Refashioned:
"Go ahead and ask," I said, "but I'm sure you've heard the answer many times. Either you don't understand, or -- more likely -- you're being deliberately difficult. You've been told repeatedly that the Idea of the Good is the highest knowledge, and that everything else becomes useful and beneficial only through its connection to the Good.

You can hardly be unaware that this is what I was about to discuss -- the very thing about which, as I've often said, we know so little. And without knowledge of the Good, no other knowledge or possession of any kind will benefit us in the slightest.

Do you really think that possessing everything else has any value if we don't possess the Good? Or that knowing everything else matters if we have no knowledge of beauty and goodness?"
D: Elegant: The Idea of the Good is the highest knowledge. All other things become useful only through their relation to it. Without knowledge of the Good, every other possession and every other piece of knowledge is worthless.
D: Summary: Socrates reveals what Adeimantus already knows: the Idea of the Good is the highest knowledge, the principle that makes all other knowledge and possessions valuable. Without it, nothing else profits us.
D: Distilled: The Idea of the Good is the highest knowledge -- without it, every other form of knowledge and every possession is worthless.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- THE FORM OF THE GOOD is explicitly named as the highest knowledge -- 'Idea of Good' (he tou agathou idea) = the supreme Form in Plato's metaphysics -- All other Forms derive their value and intelligibility from the Good -- 'We know so little' = Socrates' characteristic profession of ignorance, even about the highest principle -- The Good as the condition of all value: without it, nothing else has worth -- This passage is the metaphysical center of the Republic -- The Good will be illuminated (but not defined) through the Sun analogy (6.63-66), and the Divided Line (6.67-75) -- Cf. Philebus 20d: 'the Good is that which all things that know it pursue'

D: Original:
Assuredly not. You are further aware that most people affirm pleasure to be the good, but the finer sort of wits say it is knowledge

Yes. And you are aware too that the latter cannot explain what they mean by knowledge, but are obliged after all to say knowledge of the good?

How ridiculous! Yes, I said, that they should begin by reproaching us with our ignorance of the good, and then presume our knowledge of it --for the good they define to be knowledge of the good, just as if we understood them when they use the term 'good' --this is of course ridiculous.

Most true, he said. And those who make pleasure their good are in equal perplexity; for they are compelled to admit that there are bad pleasures as well as good.

Certainly. And therefore to acknowledge that bad and good are the same?

True. There can be no doubt about the numerous difficulties in which this question is involved.

There can be none. Further, do we not see that many are willing to do or to have or to seem to be what is just and honourable without the reality; but no one is satisfied with the appearance of good --the reality is what they seek; in the case of the good, appearance is despised by every one.
D: Narrative:
"Assuredly not."

"You are further aware that most people affirm pleasure to be the good, but the finer sort of wits say it is knowledge."

"Yes."

"And you are aware too that the latter cannot explain what they mean by knowledge, but are obliged after all to say knowledge of the good?"

"How ridiculous!"

"Yes," I said, "that they should begin by reproaching us with our ignorance of the good, and then presume our knowledge of it -- for the good they define to be knowledge of the good, just as if we understood them when they use the term 'good' -- this is of course ridiculous."

"Most true," he said.

"And those who make pleasure their good are in equal perplexity; for they are compelled to admit that there are bad pleasures as well as good."

"Certainly."

"And therefore to acknowledge that bad and good are the same?"

"True."

"There can be no doubt about the numerous difficulties in which this question is involved."

"There can be none."

"Further, do we not see that many are willing to do or to have or to seem to be what is just and honourable without the reality; but no one is satisfied with the appearance of good -- the reality is what they seek; in the case of the good, appearance is despised by every one."
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly not."

"You also know that most people identify the Good with pleasure, while the more sophisticated claim it's knowledge."

"Yes."

"And you've noticed that the knowledge camp can't actually explain what kind of knowledge they mean? They end up saying 'knowledge of the Good' -- which is circular."

"How absurd!"

"Exactly," I said. "They scold us for not knowing what the Good is, then define it as 'knowledge of the Good' -- as if we'd understand when they use the very term we're trying to define. It's completely circular."

"Absolutely," he said.

"And those who equate the Good with pleasure are equally stuck. They're forced to admit that some pleasures are bad."

"Certainly."

"Which means they're admitting that the same things can be both good and bad."

"True."

"The difficulties surrounding this question are clearly enormous."

"They are."

"And here's another crucial observation: many people are willing to do, possess, or appear to be what is just and honorable without actually being so. But when it comes to the Good, no one is satisfied with mere appearances -- everyone demands the real thing. With the Good, appearances are universally despised."
D: Elegant: Defining the Good as pleasure fails because some pleasures are bad. Defining it as knowledge is circular -- 'knowledge of the Good' presumes what it tries to define. But one thing is clear: while people accept the appearance of justice, no one accepts the mere appearance of Good. With the Good alone, everyone demands reality over appearance.
D: Summary: Socrates examines two failed definitions of the Good: pleasure (which admits of bad pleasures) and knowledge (which becomes circular). He makes a crucial observation: while people accept appearances of justice, everyone demands the reality of the Good -- no one is satisfied with merely appearing to possess it.
D: Distilled: Neither pleasure nor knowledge adequately defines the Good, but one thing is certain: while people tolerate the appearance of justice, everyone demands the reality of the Good.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Two rival theories of the Good: (1) hedonism (pleasure = good), (2) intellectualism (knowledge = good) -- The hedonist problem: bad pleasures exist, so pleasure cannot simply be the Good -- The intellectualist problem: 'knowledge of the Good' is circular -- The key insight: the Good is the only thing where everyone demands reality over appearance -- This distinguishes the Good from justice and honor, where appearances often suffice -- Cf. Philebus: Plato's extended treatment of whether the Good is pleasure or knowledge (it's neither alone) -- The asymmetry between Good and justice anticipates the Sun analogy: the Good is more fundamental than justice

D: Original: Very true, he said. Of this then, which every soul of man pursues and makes the end of all his actions, having a presentiment that there is such an end, and yet hesitating because neither knowing the nature nor having the same assurance of this as of other things, and therefore losing whatever good there is in other things, --of a principle such and so great as this ought the best men in our State, to whom everything is entrusted, to be in the darkness of ignorance?
D: Narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"Of this then, which every soul of man pursues and makes the end of all his actions, having a presentiment that there is such an end, and yet hesitating because neither knowing the nature nor having the same assurance of this as of other things, and therefore losing whatever good there is in other things, -- of a principle such and so great as this ought the best men in our State, to whom everything is entrusted, to be in the darkness of ignorance?"
D: Refashioned:
"Very true," he said.

"So here we have something that every human soul pursues, making it the goal of all its actions. Every soul has a presentiment that such an ultimate end exists, yet hesitates because it can neither define the Good's nature nor feel the same certainty about it as about other things -- and because of this uncertainty, loses whatever genuine good might be found in other things.

Should the best people in our state -- those to whom everything is entrusted -- be left in darkness about a principle this great?"
D: Elegant: Every soul pursues the Good as the end of all action, sensing its existence but unable to define it -- and this uncertainty causes the loss of whatever good exists in other things. The rulers of the state, to whom everything is entrusted, must not be left in ignorance of such a principle.
D: Summary: Socrates observes that every soul pursues the Good as its ultimate goal, sensing its existence but unable to grasp its nature. This uncertainty undermines all other goods. He argues that the state's rulers absolutely must not remain ignorant of such a fundamental principle.
D: Distilled: Every soul pursues the Good as the goal of all action but cannot define it -- and the rulers of the state must not remain in darkness about something this fundamental.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Every soul pursues and makes the end of all his actions' = the Good as the teleological principle of all human action -- 'Presentiment' (apomanteuomene) = an intuitive, quasi-prophetic sense that the Good exists -- 'Losing whatever good there is in other things' = without knowledge of the Good, even other goods are compromised -- This passage establishes the Good as both (1) the universal object of desire and (2) the condition of all other value -- The argument for philosopher-kings: rulers must know the ultimate principle -- Cf. Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics I.1: 'the Good is that at which all things aim' (a passage influenced by this Republic discussion)

D: Original:
Certainly not, he said. I am sure, I said, that he who does not know now the beautiful and the just are likewise good will be but a sorry guardian of them; and I suspect that no one who is ignorant of the good will have a true knowledge of them.

That, he said, is a shrewd suspicion of yours. And if we only have a guardian who has this knowledge our State will be perfectly ordered?

Of course, he replied; but I wish that you would tell me whether you conceive this supreme principle of the good to be knowledge or pleasure, or different from either.

Aye, I said, I knew all along that a fastidious gentleman like you would not be contented with the thoughts of other people about these matters.

True, Socrates; but I must say that one who like you has passed a lifetime in the study of philosophy should not be always repeating the opinions of others, and never telling his own.

Well, but has any one a right to say positively what he does not know?

Not, he said, with the assurance of positive certainty; he has no right to do that: but he may say what he thinks, as a matter of opinion.

And do you not know, I said, that all mere opinions are bad, and the best of them blind? You would not deny that those who have any true notion without intelligence are only like blind men who feel their way along the road?

Very true. And do you wish to behold what is blind and crooked and base, when others will tell you of brightness and beauty?
D: Narrative:
"Certainly not," he said.

"I am sure," I said, "that he who does not know how the beautiful and the just are likewise good will be but a sorry guardian of them; and I suspect that no one who is ignorant of the good will have a true knowledge of them."

"That," he said, "is a shrewd suspicion of yours."

"And if we only have a guardian who has this knowledge our State will be perfectly ordered?"

"Of course," he replied; "but I wish that you would tell me whether you conceive this supreme principle of the good to be knowledge or pleasure, or different from either."

"Aye," I said, "I knew all along that a fastidious gentleman like you would not be contented with the thoughts of other people about these matters."

"True, Socrates; but I must say that one who like you has passed a lifetime in the study of philosophy should not be always repeating the opinions of others, and never telling his own."

"Well, but has any one a right to say positively what he does not know?"

"Not," he said, "with the assurance of positive certainty; he has no right to do that: but he may say what he thinks, as a matter of opinion."

"And do you not know," I said, "that all mere opinions are bad, and the best of them blind? You would not deny that those who have any true notion without intelligence are only like blind men who feel their way along the road?"

"Very true."

"And do you wish to behold what is blind and crooked and base, when others will tell you of brightness and beauty?"
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly not," he said.

"I'm sure," I said, "that someone who doesn't understand how the beautiful and the just are also good will be a poor guardian of them. And I suspect that no one ignorant of the Good can have true knowledge of anything."

"That's a sharp observation," he said.

"And if our guardian has this knowledge, the state will be perfectly ordered?"

"Of course," he replied. "But I wish you'd tell me whether you think this supreme principle -- the Good -- is knowledge, or pleasure, or something different from both."

"I knew," I said, "that someone as discriminating as you wouldn't be satisfied with other people's opinions on this."

"True, Socrates. But someone who has spent his whole life studying philosophy shouldn't keep repeating what others think and never share his own view."

"But does anyone have the right to state as certain what he doesn't actually know?"

"Not with the assurance of certainty," he said. "But he can share what he thinks, as an opinion."

"And don't you know," I said, "that all mere opinions are deficient -- even the best of them are blind? Those who hold true beliefs without understanding are like blind people feeling their way along the road."

"Very true."

"And would you rather behold what is blind, crooked, and base, when others could show you brightness and beauty?"
D: Elegant: No one ignorant of the Good can truly know what is beautiful or just -- without it, even the best opinions are blind. Socrates is pressed to state his own view of the Good but refuses to assert what he doesn't know with certainty. True opinion without understanding is like a blind person groping along a road.
D: Summary: Socrates insists that without knowledge of the Good, no one can truly understand justice or beauty. When pressed for his own view, he refuses to claim certainty about what he doesn't know, noting that even the best opinions without understanding are blind -- like sightless people groping along a road.
D: Distilled: Without knowledge of the Good, all other knowledge is compromised -- even the best opinions without genuine understanding are blind.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'The beautiful and the just are likewise good' = the Forms of Beauty and Justice derive their value from the Form of the Good -- Socrates' refusal to state his view directly sets up the indirect approach through analogies -- 'All mere opinions are bad, and the best of them blind' = doxa (opinion) vs. episteme (knowledge), a central Platonic distinction -- The blind person groping = the philosopher without knowledge of the Good -- Adeimantus presses Socrates to go beyond Socratic ignorance, but Socrates maintains his characteristic epistemic humility -- This tension leads to the compromise: Socrates will describe the 'child' of the Good (the Sun analogy) rather than the Good itself

D: Original:
Still, I must implore you, Socrates, said Glaucon, not to turn away just as you are reaching the goal; if you will only give such an explanation of the good as you have already given of justice and temperance and the other virtues, we shall be satisfied.

Yes, my friend, and I shall be at least equally satisfied, but I cannot help fearing that I shall fall, and that my indiscreet zeal will bring ridicule upon me. No, sweet sirs, let us not at present ask what is the actual nature of the good, for to reach what is now in my thoughts would be an effort too great for me. But of the child of the good who is likest him, I would fain speak, if I could be sure that you wished to hear --otherwise, not.
D: Narrative:
"Still, I must implore you, Socrates," said Glaucon, "not to turn away just as you are reaching the goal; if you will only give such an explanation of the good as you have already given of justice and temperance and the other virtues, we shall be satisfied."

"Yes, my friend, and I shall be at least equally satisfied, but I cannot help fearing that I shall fail, and that my indiscreet zeal will bring ridicule upon me. No, sweet sirs, let us not at present ask what is the actual nature of the good, for to reach what is now in my thoughts would be an effort too great for me. But of the child of the good who is likest him, I would fain speak, if I could be sure that you wished to hear -- otherwise, not."
D: Refashioned:
"Still, I must beg you, Socrates," said Glaucon, "not to turn away just when you're reaching the finish line. If you could give us even the kind of explanation of the Good that you gave of justice, temperance, and the other virtues, we'd be satisfied."

"My friend, I'd be equally satisfied. But I'm afraid I'll fail and my overreaching will make me look ridiculous. No, let's not try to define the actual nature of the Good right now -- reaching what's in my mind would be too much for me.

But I'm willing to describe the child of the Good -- the offspring that most closely resembles it -- if you want to hear. Otherwise, I'll stop here."
D: Elegant: Glaucon begs Socrates not to stop at the threshold of the Good. Socrates declines to define the Good itself -- the effort would be too great. Instead, he offers to describe its 'child,' the offspring that most closely resembles it.
D: Summary: Glaucon implores Socrates not to turn back now. Socrates admits he cannot define the Good itself directly -- the task exceeds his ability. Instead, he proposes to describe the 'child of the Good,' the closest likeness to it.
D: Distilled: Socrates cannot define the Good itself but offers to describe its 'child' -- the offspring that most closely resembles it.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- Glaucon replaces Adeimantus as interlocutor, signaling a new section -- 'The child of the good' (ekgonos tou agathou) = the Sun, which will serve as the analogy for the Good -- Socrates' profession of inability is characteristic but also philosophically significant: the Good may be beyond direct definition -- 'Too great an effort for me' = the Good transcends ordinary philosophical discourse -- This introduces the method of indirect illumination through analogy that defines the rest of Book VI -- The three famous analogies follow: Sun (6.63-66), Divided Line (6.67-75), and Cave (Book VII)

D: Original:
By all means, he said, tell us about the child, and you shall remain in our debt for the account of the parent.

I do indeed wish, I replied, that I could pay, and you receive, the account of the parent, and not, as now, of the offspring only; take, however, this latter by way of interest, and at the same time have a care that i do not render a false account, although I have no intention of deceiving you.

Yes, we will take all the care that we can: proceed. Yes, I said, but I must first come to an understanding with you, and remind you of what I have mentioned in the course of this discussion, and at many other times.

What? The old story, that there is a many beautiful and a many good, and so of other things which we describe and define; to all of them 'many' is applied.

True, he said. And there is an absolute beauty and an absolute good, and of other things to which the term 'many' is applied there is an absolute; for they may be brought under a single idea, which is called the essence of each.

Very true. The many, as we say, are seen but not known, and the ideas are known but not seen.

Exactly. And what is the organ with which we see the visible things?

The sight, he said. And with the hearing, I said, we hear, and with the other senses perceive the other objects of sense?

True. But have you remarked that sight is by far the most costly and complex piece of workmanship which the artificer of the senses ever contrived?

No, I never have, he said. Then reflect; has the ear or voice need of any third or additional nature in order that the one may be able to hear and the other to be heard?

Nothing of the sort. No, indeed, I replied; and the same is true of most, if not all, the other senses --you would not say that any of them requires such an addition?
D: Narrative:
"By all means," he said, "tell us about the child, and you shall remain in our debt for the account of the parent."

"I do indeed wish," I replied, "that I could pay, and you receive, the account of the parent, and not, as now, of the offspring only; take, however, this latter by way of interest, and at the same time have a care that I do not render a false account, although I have no intention of deceiving you."

"Yes, we will take all the care that we can: proceed."

"Yes," I said, "but I must first come to an understanding with you, and remind you of what I have mentioned in the course of this discussion, and at many other times."

"What?"

"The old story, that there is a many beautiful and a many good, and so of other things which we describe and define; to all of them 'many' is applied."

"True," he said.

"And there is an absolute beauty and an absolute good, and of other things to which the term 'many' is applied there is an absolute; for they may be brought under a single idea, which is called the essence of each."

"Very true."

"The many, as we say, are seen but not known, and the ideas are known but not seen."

"Exactly."

"And what is the organ with which we see the visible things?"

"The sight," he said.

"And with the hearing," I said, "we hear, and with the other senses perceive the other objects of sense?"

"True."

"But have you remarked that sight is by far the most costly and complex piece of workmanship which the artificer of the senses ever contrived?"

"No, I never have," he said.

"Then reflect; has the ear or voice need of any third or additional nature in order that the one may be able to hear and the other to be heard?"

"Nothing of the sort."

"No, indeed," I replied; "and the same is true of most, if not all, the other senses -- you would not say that any of them requires such an addition?"
D: Refashioned:
"By all means," he said. "Tell us about the child, and you'll owe us for the parent."

"I wish I could pay in full -- give you the account of the parent, not just the offspring. But take this as interest on the debt, and be careful that I don't give you a false account, though I have no intention of deceiving you."

"We'll be as careful as we can. Go ahead."

"First, let me remind you of something we've discussed before -- many times, in fact."

"What's that?"

"The familiar distinction: there are many beautiful things and many good things, and so on for everything we describe and classify."

"True," he said.

"And then there is absolute beauty and absolute good. For each class of 'many' things, there is a single, absolute Form -- the essence of each."

"Exactly."

"The many particular things are seen but not truly known; the Forms are known but not seen."

"Precisely."

"Now, what organ do we use to see visible things?"

"Sight."

"And we hear through hearing, and perceive other sensible objects through the other senses?"

"Yes."

"But have you noticed that sight is by far the most elaborate and costly piece of workmanship among all the senses?"

"No, I hadn't thought about it."

"Then consider this: does hearing require any third element -- beyond the ear and the sound -- for hearing to take place?"

"No."

"And the same is true of most other senses -- they don't require any additional element?"
D: Elegant: There are many beautiful things, but also absolute beauty; many good things, but also absolute good -- the Form or essence of each class. The many are seen but not known; the Forms are known but not seen. Among the senses, sight is uniquely elaborate -- unlike hearing, which needs only ear and sound, sight requires a third element.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes the fundamental distinction between the many particular things (seen but not known) and the absolute Forms (known but not seen). He then draws attention to sight's unique status among the senses: unlike hearing, sight requires a third element beyond the eye and the object.
D: Distilled: The many particulars are seen but not known, while the Forms are known but not seen -- and sight, unlike other senses, requires a crucial third element.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- 'Interest' on the debt = the Sun analogy as partial payment for the full account of the Good -- 'The many beautiful and the many good' vs. 'absolute beauty and absolute good' = the fundamental distinction between particulars and Forms (cf. Book V, 475e-480a) -- 'Seen but not known / known but not seen' = the epistemological divide between sensation and intellection -- Sight as the 'most costly' sense sets up the Sun analogy: sight alone requires light (a third element) -- The parallel: as sight needs light, so the mind needs the Good to know the Forms -- The financial metaphor (debt, interest, payment) adds warmth to the abstract discussion

D: Original:
Certainly not. But you see that without the addition of some other nature there is no seeing or being seen?

How do you mean? Sight being, as I conceive, in the eyes, and he who has eyes wanting to see; colour being also present in them, still unless there be a third nature specially adapted to the purpose, the owner of the eyes will see nothing and the colours will be invisible.

Of what nature are you speaking? Of that which you term light, I replied. True, he said. Noble, then, is the bond which links together sight and visibility, and great beyond other bonds by no small difference of nature; for light is their bond, and light is no ignoble thing?

Nay, he said, the reverse of ignoble. And which, I said, of the gods in heaven would you say was the lord of this element? Whose is that light which makes the eye to see perfectly and the visible to appear?

You mean the sun, as you and all mankind say. May not the relation of sight to this deity be described as follows?

How? Neither sight nor the eye in which sight resides is the sun?

No. Yet of all the organs of sense the eye is the most like the sun?

By far the most like. And the power which the eye possesses is a sort of effluence which is dispensed from the sun?

Exactly. Then the sun is not sight, but the author of sight who is recognised by sight.

True, he said. And this is he whom I call the child of the good, whom the good begat in his own likeness, to be in the visible world, in relation to sight and the things of sight, what the good is in the intellectual world in relation to mind and the things of mind.

Will you be a little more explicit? he said. Why, you know, I said, that the eyes, when a person directs them towards objects on which the light of day is no longer shining, but the moon and stars only, see dimly, and are nearly blind; they seem to have no clearness of vision in them?
D: Narrative:
"Certainly not."

"But you see that without the addition of some other nature there is no seeing or being seen?"

"How do you mean?"

"Sight being, as I conceive, in the eyes, and he who has eyes wanting to see; colour being also present in them, still unless there be a third nature specially adapted to the purpose, the owner of the eyes will see nothing and the colours will be invisible."

"Of what nature are you speaking?"

"Of that which you term light," I replied.

"True," he said.

"Noble, then, is the bond which links together sight and visibility, and great beyond other bonds by no small difference of nature; for light is their bond, and light is no ignoble thing?"

"Nay," he said, "the reverse of ignoble."

"And which," I said, "of the gods in heaven would you say was the lord of this element? Whose is that light which makes the eye to see perfectly and the visible to appear?"

"You mean the sun, as you and all mankind say."

"May not the relation of sight to this deity be described as follows?"

"How?"

"Neither sight nor the eye in which sight resides is the sun?"

"No."

"Yet of all the organs of sense the eye is the most like the sun?"

"By far the most like."

"And the power which the eye possesses is a sort of effluence which is dispensed from the sun?"

"Exactly."

"Then the sun is not sight, but the author of sight who is recognised by sight."

"True," he said.

"And this is he whom I call the child of the good, whom the good begat in his own likeness, to be in the visible world, in relation to sight and the things of sight, what the good is in the intellectual world in relation to mind and the things of mind."

"Will you be a little more explicit?" he said.

"Why, you know," I said, "that the eyes, when a person directs them towards objects on which the light of day is no longer shining, but the moon and stars only, see dimly, and are nearly blind; they seem to have no clearness of vision in them?"
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly not."

"But notice: without some additional element, there is no seeing and no being seen."

"What do you mean?"

"Even if sight exists in the eyes and the person wants to see, and color is present in objects -- without a third element designed for this purpose, the person will see nothing and the colors will remain invisible."

"What element are you talking about?"

"Light," I replied.

"Of course," he said.

"So the bond linking sight and visibility is noble -- far greater than any other sensory bond. For light is that bond, and light is no trivial thing."

"Far from trivial," he agreed.

"And which god in the heavens would you say rules over this element? Whose is the light that enables the eye to see and visible things to appear?"

"You mean the sun, as everyone would say."

"Now consider the relationship between sight and the sun."

"Go on."

"Neither sight itself nor the eye that houses it is the sun."

"No."

"Yet of all sense organs, the eye is most like the sun."

"By far."

"And the eye's power of vision is a kind of emanation dispensed from the sun."

"Exactly."

"So the sun is not sight itself, but the source of sight -- recognized through sight."

"True."

"THIS is what I call the child of the Good. The Good begat the sun in its own likeness -- to be in the visible world, in relation to sight and visible things, what the Good is in the intelligible world in relation to mind and the objects of mind."

"Can you be a little more specific?" he asked.

"Consider this: when you direct your eyes toward objects illuminated only by moonlight or starlight, rather than sunlight, they see dimly -- nearly blind, with no clarity of vision."
D: Elegant: Light is the noble bond between sight and visibility -- and the sun is its source. The sun is not sight itself but the author of sight. THIS is the child of the Good: as the sun is to the visible world, enabling sight and visibility, so the Good is to the intelligible world, enabling mind and knowledge. Without the sun's light, eyes see dimly; without the Good's illumination, the mind gropes in darkness.
D: Summary: Socrates introduces the Sun analogy: light is the crucial third element that makes sight possible, and the sun is its source. The sun is the 'child of the Good' -- as the sun enables sight in the visible world, the Good enables understanding in the intelligible world. Without sunlight, eyes see dimly; the parallel to intellectual darkness follows.
D: Distilled: The sun is the child of the Good: as the sun enables sight through light in the visible world, so the Good enables knowledge through truth in the intelligible world.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- THE SUN ANALOGY: one of philosophy's most famous images -- Light as the 'noble bond' between seeing and being seen -- The sun = the child/offspring (ekgonos) of the Good, its analogue in the visible world -- The structural parallel: Sun:visible world :: Good:intelligible world -- 'Effluence from the sun' = the eye's power derives from the sun, just as the mind's power derives from the Good -- The sun is not sight but the 'author of sight' -- parallel: the Good is not knowledge but the source of knowledge -- 'Dimly by moonlight' = the mind operating without the Good sees only shadows and opinions -- This connects directly to the Cave allegory (Book VII): the sun outside the Cave represents the Good -- Cf. Parmenides fr. 1: the path from darkness to light as philosophical journey

D: Original:
Very true. But when they are directed towards objects on which the sun shines, they see clearly and there is sight in them?

Certainly. And the soul is like the eye: when resting upon that on which truth and being shine, the soul perceives and understands and is radiant with intelligence; but when turned towards the twilight of becoming and perishing, then she has opinion only, and goes blinking about, and is first of one opinion and then of another, and seems to have no intelligence?
D: Narrative:
"Very true."

"But when they are directed towards objects on which the sun shines, they see clearly and there is sight in them?"

"Certainly."

"And the soul is like the eye: when resting upon that on which truth and being shine, the soul perceives and understands and is radiant with intelligence; but when turned towards the twilight of becoming and perishing, then she has opinion only, and goes blinking about, and is first of one opinion and then of another, and seems to have no intelligence?"
D: Refashioned:
"Very true."

"But when eyes are directed toward objects the sun illuminates, they see clearly -- there is genuine sight."

"Certainly."

"The soul works the same way. When it rests on what truth and being illuminate, it perceives, understands, and is radiant with intelligence. But when it turns toward the twilight realm of becoming and perishing, it has only opinion -- blinking and stumbling, shifting from one opinion to another, appearing to have no intelligence at all."
D: Elegant: As the eye sees clearly in sunlight and dimly in darkness, so the soul: resting on what truth and being illuminate, it is radiant with intelligence; turned toward the twilight of becoming and perishing, it has only shifting, blinking opinion.
D: Summary: Socrates draws the parallel: as eyes see clearly in sunlight, the soul perceives clearly when focused on truth and being. When turned toward the changeable world of becoming, the soul has only unstable opinion, shifting from one view to another.
D: Distilled: The soul focused on truth and being is radiant with intelligence; turned toward the world of becoming, it has only shifting, uncertain opinion.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- The analogy applied: sunlight:clarity of vision :: truth/being:clarity of understanding -- 'Truth and being shine' = the Good illuminates the Forms, making them knowable -- 'Twilight of becoming and perishing' = the sensible world of flux -- 'Blinking about' (skotodinia) = intellectual disorientation without the Good -- The soul 'radiant with intelligence' (nous) vs. having 'opinion only' (doxa) = the fundamental epistemological divide -- This maps onto the Divided Line that follows: the upper segments (knowledge) vs. lower segments (opinion) -- Cf. Book VII Cave allegory: the prisoner blinded by light, then adjusting to see reality

D: Original: Just so. Now, that which imparts truth to the known and the power of knowing to the knower is what I would have you term the idea of good, and this you will deem to be the cause of science, and of truth in so far as the latter becomes the subject of knowledge; beautiful too, as are both truth and knowledge, you will be right in esteeming this other nature as more beautiful than either; and, as in the previous instance, light and sight may be truly said to be like the sun, and yet not to be the sun, so in this other sphere, science and truth may be deemed to be like the good, but not the good; the good has a place of honour yet higher.
D: Narrative:
"Just so."

"Now, that which imparts truth to the known and the power of knowing to the knower is what I would have you term the idea of good, and this you will deem to be the cause of science, and of truth in so far as the latter becomes the subject of knowledge; beautiful too, as are both truth and knowledge, you will be right in esteeming this other nature as more beautiful than either; and, as in the previous instance, light and sight may be truly said to be like the sun, and yet not to be the sun, so in this other sphere, science and truth may be deemed to be like the good, but not the good; the good has a place of honour yet higher."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly."

"Now, what gives truth to the objects of knowledge and gives the knower the power to know -- that is what I want you to call the Idea of the Good. It is the cause of knowledge and of truth, insofar as truth becomes an object of knowledge.

Beautiful as truth and knowledge both are, you'll be right to regard the Good as something still more beautiful than either. Just as light and sight resemble the sun but are not the sun itself, so knowledge and truth resemble the Good but are not the Good. The Good holds a place of honor higher still."
D: Elegant: The Idea of the Good gives truth to what is known and the power of knowing to the knower. It is the cause of knowledge and truth. Yet beautiful as truth and knowledge are, the Good is more beautiful still -- as light and sight resemble the sun but are not the sun, so knowledge and truth resemble the Good but are not it. The Good holds a place of honor yet higher.
D: Summary: Socrates defines the Good's function: it gives truth to objects of knowledge and the capacity for knowing to the mind. Though truth and knowledge are beautiful, the Good surpasses them both, just as the sun surpasses the light and sight it produces.
D: Distilled: The Good is the cause of all truth and knowledge yet surpasses both -- holding a place of honor higher than the beautiful things it makes possible.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- This is the most precise statement of the Form of the Good's role in Plato's metaphysics -- The Good has two functions: (1) it gives truth to objects of knowledge (ontological), (2) it gives the power of knowing to the knower (epistemological) -- The Good > truth > knowledge in the hierarchy -- The sun analogy structure: Sun produces light, which enables sight; Good produces truth, which enables knowledge -- But the Good is not identical with truth or knowledge, just as the sun is not identical with light or sight -- 'A place of honour yet higher' (time) = the Good transcends even the categories it makes possible -- This is one of the most commented-upon passages in the history of philosophy -- Cf. Plotinus, Enneads V.1: the One/Good as beyond being and knowledge

D: Original:
What a wonder of beauty that must be, he said, which is the author of science and truth, and yet surpasses them in beauty; for you surely cannot mean to say that pleasure is the good?

God forbid, I replied; but may I ask you to consider the image in another point of view?

In what point of view? You would say, would you not, that the sun is only the author of visibility in all visible things, but of generation and nourishment and growth, though he himself is not generation?

Certainly. In like manner the good may be said to be not only the author of knowledge to all things known, but of their being and essence, and yet the good is not essence, but far exceeds essence in dignity and power.

Glaucon said, with a ludicrous earnestness: By the light of heaven, how amazing!

Yes, I said, and the exaggeration may be set down to you; for you made me utter my fancies.

And pray continue to utter them; at any rate let us hear if there is anything more to be said about the similitude of the sun.

Yes, I said, there is a great deal more. Then omit nothing, however slight. I will do my best, I said; but I should think that a great deal will have to be omitted.

You have to imagine, then, that there are two ruling powers, and that one of them is set over the intellectual world, the other over the visible. I do not say heaven, lest you should fancy that I am playing upon the name ('ourhanoz, orhatoz'). May I suppose that you have this distinction of the visible and intelligible fixed in your mind?
D: Narrative:
"What a wonder of beauty that must be," he said, "which is the author of science and truth, and yet surpasses them in beauty; for you surely cannot mean to say that pleasure is the good?"

"God forbid," I replied; "but may I ask you to consider the image in another point of view?"

"In what point of view?"

"You would say, would you not, that the sun is only the author of visibility in all visible things, but of generation and nourishment and growth, though he himself is not generation?"

"Certainly."

"In like manner the good may be said to be not only the author of knowledge to all things known, but of their being and essence, and yet the good is not essence, but far exceeds essence in dignity and power."

Glaucon said, with a ludicrous earnestness: "By the light of heaven, how amazing!"

"Yes," I said, "and the exaggeration may be set down to you; for you made me utter my fancies."

"And pray continue to utter them; at any rate let us hear if there is anything more to be said about the similitude of the sun."

"Yes," I said, "there is a great deal more."

"Then omit nothing, however slight."

"I will do my best," I said; "but I should think that a great deal will have to be omitted."

"You have to imagine, then, that there are two ruling powers, and that one of them is set over the intellectual world, the other over the visible. I do not say heaven, lest you should fancy that I am playing upon the name ('ourhanoz, orhatoz'). May I suppose that you have this distinction of the visible and intelligible fixed in your mind?"
D: Refashioned:
"What an astonishing beauty," he said, "that is the source of knowledge and truth yet surpasses them both! Surely you don't mean that pleasure is the Good?"

"Certainly not," I replied. "But let me ask you to consider the analogy from another angle."

"What angle?"

"The sun doesn't just make things visible -- it's also the source of generation, nourishment, and growth for all visible things. And yet the sun itself is not generation."

"True."

"In the same way, the Good is not only the source of knowledge for everything that is known, but also the source of their very being and essence. And yet the Good itself is not essence -- it far exceeds essence in dignity and power."

Glaucon exclaimed with comic intensity: "By the light of heaven, how extraordinary!"

"You can blame yourself for that outburst," I said. "You made me share my fancies."

"Please keep going -- let's hear whatever else can be said about the sun comparison."

"There's a great deal more," I said.

"Then leave nothing out, however small."

"I'll do my best, but much will have to be left unsaid."

"Imagine two ruling powers: one over the intelligible world, the other over the visible world. I won't say 'heaven,' in case you think I'm making a pun on the words. Do you have the distinction between the visible and the intelligible firmly in mind?"
D: Elegant: The sun doesn't just make things visible -- it gives them generation, nourishment, and growth, without itself being generation. Likewise, the Good gives things not only knowledge but their very being and essence, without itself being essence. The Good far exceeds essence in dignity and power. Two ruling powers govern reality: one over the visible world, one over the intelligible.
D: Summary: Socrates makes the Republic's most radical metaphysical claim: the Good is the source not only of knowledge but of the very being and essence of everything, yet it transcends essence itself. As the sun gives life and growth to visible things, the Good gives being to intelligible things. Glaucon is astonished.
D: Distilled: The Good is the source of all being and essence, yet itself transcends essence -- far exceeding it in dignity and power.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- 'THE GOOD IS NOT ESSENCE BUT FAR EXCEEDS ESSENCE' (epekeina tes ousias) = perhaps the single most influential sentence in Western metaphysics -- The sun analogy deepened: sun not only illuminates but causes generation/growth; the Good not only enables knowledge but causes being -- The Good is beyond being (epekeina tes ousias) -- a claim that inspired Neoplatonism (Plotinus' One), Christian theology (God beyond being), and Heidegger's ontological difference -- Glaucon's 'By the light of heaven!' (literally 'By Apollo!') is comically appropriate given the sun imagery -- 'Two ruling powers' = the two realms of the Divided Line about to be introduced -- The pun on ouranos (heaven) and horatos (visible) = a Greek wordplay Socrates avoids -- This passage transitions from the Sun analogy to the Divided Line

D: Original: I have. Now take a line which has been cut into two unequal parts, and divide each of them again in the same proportion, and suppose the two main divisions to answer, one to the visible and the other to the intelligible, and then compare the subdivisions in respect of their clearness and want of clearness, and you will find that the first section in the sphere of the visible consists of images. And by images I mean, in the first place, shadows, and in the second place, reflections in water and in solid, smooth and polished bodies and the like: Do you understand?
D: Narrative:
"I have."

"Now take a line which has been cut into two unequal parts, and divide each of them again in the same proportion, and suppose the two main divisions to answer, one to the visible and the other to the intelligible, and then compare the subdivisions in respect of their clearness and want of clearness, and you will find that the first section in the sphere of the visible consists of images. And by images I mean, in the first place, shadows, and in the second place, reflections in water and in solid, smooth and polished bodies and the like: Do you understand?"
D: Refashioned:
"I do."

"Now imagine a line divided into two unequal parts. Divide each part again in the same ratio. Let the two main divisions represent the visible and the intelligible. Then compare the subdivisions in terms of their relative clarity.

You'll find that the first section within the visible realm consists of images -- by which I mean, first, shadows, and second, reflections in water, in smooth polished surfaces, and similar things. Do you follow?"
D: Elegant: Imagine a line divided into two unequal parts -- visible and intelligible -- each subdivided again in the same ratio. The first section of the visible realm consists of images: shadows and reflections in water and polished surfaces.
D: Summary: Socrates introduces the Divided Line: a line split into two unequal parts representing the visible and intelligible realms, each subdivided in the same ratio. The lowest section of the visible realm consists of images -- shadows and reflections.
D: Distilled: The Divided Line divides reality into visible and intelligible realms, each subdivided by clarity -- the lowest section being mere images: shadows and reflections.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- THE DIVIDED LINE: one of Plato's most systematic metaphysical schemas -- Four segments from bottom to top: (1) images/shadows, (2) physical objects, (3) mathematical objects, (4) Forms -- The line is cut into UNEQUAL parts -- the intelligible is larger (more real, more clear) -- Each main section is subdivided in the SAME RATIO -- 'Images' (eikones) = shadows and reflections, the lowest grade of reality -- The four segments correspond to four mental states: imagination, belief, understanding, reason -- The Divided Line maps directly onto the Cave allegory (Book VII): shadows on the cave wall = segment 1 -- Cf. Book VII 514a-517a: the prisoners see only shadows (eikones)

D: Original:
Yes, I understand. Imagine, now, the other section, of which this is only the resemblance, to include the animals which we see, and everything that grows or is made.

Very good. Would you not admit that both the sections of this division have different degrees of truth, and that the copy is to the original as the sphere of opinion is to the sphere of knowledge?

Most undoubtedly. Next proceed to consider the manner in which the sphere of the intellectual is to be divided.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, I understand."

"Imagine, now, the other section, of which this is only the resemblance, to include the animals which we see, and everything that grows or is made."

"Very good."

"Would you not admit that both the sections of this division have different degrees of truth, and that the copy is to the original as the sphere of opinion is to the sphere of knowledge?"

"Most undoubtedly."

"Next proceed to consider the manner in which the sphere of the intellectual is to be divided."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, I understand."

"Now picture the second section -- the one that the first is merely an image of. This includes the actual animals we see, everything that grows, and everything that is made."

"Very good."

"You'd agree that these two sections have different degrees of truth? That the copy relates to the original as the sphere of opinion relates to the sphere of knowledge?"

"Absolutely."

"Now let's consider how the intelligible sphere should be divided."
D: Elegant: The second section of the visible includes actual animals, plants, and artifacts -- the originals of which images are copies. The copy relates to its original as opinion relates to knowledge. Now the intelligible sphere must be similarly divided.
D: Summary: The second section of the visible realm contains actual physical objects -- animals, plants, artifacts -- which are the originals that shadows and reflections merely copy. The ratio of copy to original mirrors the ratio of opinion to knowledge. Socrates turns to dividing the intelligible realm.
D: Distilled: Physical objects are to their shadows as knowledge is to opinion -- and now the intelligible realm must be similarly divided.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- Segment 2 of the Divided Line: physical objects (animals, plants, artifacts) -- The proportional relationship: images:objects :: opinion:knowledge -- 'Copy to original' = the mimetic relationship that structures Plato's metaphysics -- This establishes the principle that each higher segment is 'more real' and 'more clear' than the one below -- The transition to the intelligible realm introduces the more philosophically significant upper segments -- Segments 3 and 4 will distinguish mathematical understanding from pure dialectical reason -- Cf. the Cave: physical objects inside the cave = segment 2; the artifacts casting shadows

D: Original: In what manner? Thus: --There are two subdivisions, in the lower or which the soul uses the figures given by the former division as images; the enquiry can only be hypothetical, and instead of going upwards to a principle descends to the other end; in the higher of the two, the soul passes out of hypotheses, and goes up to a principle which is above hypotheses, making no use of images as in the former case, but proceeding only in and through the ideas themselves.
D: Narrative:
"In what manner?"

"Thus: -- There are two subdivisions, in the lower of which the soul uses the figures given by the former division as images; the enquiry can only be hypothetical, and instead of going upwards to a principle descends to the other end; in the higher of the two, the soul passes out of hypotheses, and goes up to a principle which is above hypotheses, making no use of images as in the former case, but proceeding only in and through the ideas themselves."
D: Refashioned:
"How should it be divided?"

"Like this: there are two subdivisions. In the lower one, the soul uses the figures from the visible realm as images. Its inquiry can only proceed hypothetically -- instead of ascending to a first principle, it descends to conclusions.

In the higher subdivision, the soul moves beyond hypotheses altogether, ascending to a principle that is above all hypotheses. It makes no use of images, proceeding entirely through the Forms themselves."
D: Elegant: The intelligible realm has two subdivisions. In the lower, the soul uses visible figures as images and proceeds hypothetically downward to conclusions. In the higher, the soul transcends hypotheses entirely, ascending to a first principle and proceeding through the Forms themselves without images.
D: Summary: The intelligible realm divides into two: a lower subdivision where the soul uses images and hypotheses (descending to conclusions), and a higher subdivision where the soul transcends hypotheses, ascends to a first principle, and proceeds purely through the Forms.
D: Distilled: The lower intelligible proceeds hypothetically using images; the higher transcends all hypotheses, ascending to a first principle and proceeding through pure Forms.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- Segment 3: dianoia (understanding/mathematical reasoning) -- uses hypotheses and visible aids, descends to conclusions -- Segment 4: noesis (pure reason/dialectic) -- transcends hypotheses, ascends to the unhypothetical first principle (the Good), proceeds through Forms alone -- 'Hypothetical' = mathematics assumes its axioms without justifying them -- 'A principle above hypotheses' = the Form of the Good, which grounds all other truths -- 'Proceeding through the ideas themselves' = pure dialectic, the highest intellectual activity -- The contrast: mathematics works downward from assumed axioms; dialectic works upward to the ultimate ground -- Cf. Book VII 533c-d: dialectic 'does away with hypotheses' to reach the first principle

D: Original: I do not quite understand your meaning, he said. Then I will try again; you will understand me better when I have made some preliminary remarks. You are aware that students of geometry, arithmetic, and the kindred sciences assume the odd and the even and the figures and three kinds of angles and the like in their several branches of science; these are their hypotheses, which they and everybody are supposed to know, and therefore they do not deign to give any account of them either to themselves or others; but they begin with them, and go on until they arrive at last, and in a consistent manner, at their conclusion?
D: Narrative:
"I do not quite understand your meaning," he said.

"Then I will try again; you will understand me better when I have made some preliminary remarks. You are aware that students of geometry, arithmetic, and the kindred sciences assume the odd and the even and the figures and three kinds of angles and the like in their several branches of science; these are their hypotheses, which they and everybody are supposed to know, and therefore they do not deign to give any account of them either to themselves or others; but they begin with them, and go on until they arrive at last, and in a consistent manner, at their conclusion?"
D: Refashioned:
"I don't fully understand," he said.

"Let me try again. Some preliminary examples will help. You know that students of geometry, arithmetic, and related sciences assume certain things as given -- odd and even numbers, various geometric figures, three types of angles, and so on. These are their hypotheses. They and everyone else take them as self-evident, so they don't bother to justify them to themselves or anyone else. They simply start from these assumptions and work their way logically and consistently to their conclusions."
D: Elegant: Mathematicians assume their basic concepts -- odd, even, geometric figures, types of angles -- as hypotheses requiring no justification. They start from these assumptions and work logically to their conclusions without ever questioning the foundations.
D: Summary: To clarify the lower intelligible section, Socrates uses mathematics as an example: geometers and arithmeticians assume basic concepts (odd, even, figures, angles) as unquestioned hypotheses and work logically from these assumptions to their conclusions.
D: Distilled: Mathematicians take their basic concepts as unquestioned hypotheses and reason downward to conclusions without justifying their starting points.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- This explains segment 3 (dianoia) of the Divided Line through the example of mathematics -- 'Hypotheses' (hypotheseis) = literally 'things placed under' -- foundational assumptions -- Examples: odd/even (arithmetic), figures (geometry), three kinds of angles (acute, right, obtuse) -- The key point: mathematicians never justify their axioms -- they take them as self-evident -- This makes mathematical knowledge genuine but incomplete: it reaches true conclusions but from ungrounded premises -- The contrast with dialectic (segment 4): dialectic examines and grounds the very hypotheses mathematics takes for granted -- Cf. Euclid's Elements: the axiomatic method that begins with postulates -- This passage may have influenced Euclid's formalization of geometry

D: Original: Yes, he said, I know. And do you not know also that although they make use of the visible forms and reason about them, they are thinking not of these, but of the ideals which they resemble; not of the figures which they draw, but of the absolute square and the absolute diameter, and so on --the forms which they draw or make, and which have shadows and reflections in water of their own, are converted by them into images, but they are really seeking to behold the things themselves, which can only be seen with the eye of the mind?
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "I know."

"And do you not know also that although they make use of the visible forms and reason about them, they are thinking not of these, but of the ideals which they resemble; not of the figures which they draw, but of the absolute square and the absolute diameter, and so on -- the forms which they draw or make, and which have shadows and reflections in water of their own, are converted by them into images, but they are really seeking to behold the things themselves, which can only be seen with the eye of the mind?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," he said, "I know."

"And you also know that although mathematicians use visible forms and reason about them, they're not actually thinking about those visible forms. They're thinking about the ideals those forms resemble. Not about the specific squares they draw, but about the absolute square and the absolute diagonal.

The figures they draw or construct -- which themselves have shadows and reflections -- are used merely as images. What mathematicians are really seeking to behold are the things themselves, which can only be seen with the eye of the mind."
D: Elegant: Mathematicians use visible figures but think not about them -- they think about the ideal forms those figures resemble. The drawn square points to the absolute square; the physical diagram is merely an image. What they truly seek can only be seen with the eye of the mind.
D: Summary: Socrates explains that mathematicians use visible diagrams as aids but are actually thinking about ideal, absolute forms -- the perfect square, the perfect diagonal. The physical figures are merely images pointing toward realities that can only be grasped intellectually.
D: Distilled: Mathematicians use visible diagrams as images of ideal forms that can only be seen with the eye of the mind.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- This further clarifies segment 3 (dianoia/mathematical understanding) -- 'Absolute square and absolute diameter' = mathematical objects as intermediate between physical objects and pure Forms -- Mathematicians use sensible aids (diagrams) but reason about intelligible objects -- The drawn circle is an image of the perfect circle -- 'Eye of the mind' (tes dianoias opsis) = intellectual vision, as opposed to sensory sight -- This is why mathematics is placed in the intelligible realm despite using visible aids -- Mathematicians operate between the visible and the fully intelligible -- Cf. the mathematical curriculum of Book VII: arithmetic, geometry, stereometry, astronomy, harmonics as preparation for dialectic

D: Original:
That is true. And of this kind I spoke as the intelligible, although in the search after it the soul is compelled to use hypotheses; not ascending to a first principle, because she is unable to rise above the region of hypothesis, but employing the objects of which the shadows below are resemblances in their turn as images, they having in relation to the shadows and reflections of them a greater distinctness, and therefore a higher value.

I understand, he said, that you are speaking of the province of geometry and the sister arts.
D: Narrative:
"That is true."

"And of this kind I spoke as the intelligible, although in the search after it the soul is compelled to use hypotheses; not ascending to a first principle, because she is unable to rise above the region of hypothesis, but employing the objects of which the shadows below are resemblances in their turn as images, they having in relation to the shadows and reflections of them a greater distinctness, and therefore a higher value."

"I understand," he said, "that you are speaking of the province of geometry and the sister arts."
D: Refashioned:
"That's true."

"This is what I meant by the lower part of the intelligible. In pursuing it, the soul must use hypotheses. It cannot rise above the realm of hypothesis to a first principle. Instead, it uses objects -- which are themselves the originals of the shadows below -- as images in their own right. These objects are clearer and more valuable than their shadows and reflections, but they are still being used as images."

"I understand," he said. "You're talking about geometry and the related mathematical sciences."
D: Elegant: In the lower intelligible, the soul is compelled to use hypotheses and cannot rise to a first principle. It uses mathematical objects as images -- objects that are clearer than the shadows below but still not grasped in their ultimate ground. This is the province of geometry and the sister sciences.
D: Summary: Socrates clarifies that the lower intelligible section represents geometry and related sciences: the soul uses hypotheses and cannot ascend to a first principle, employing mathematical objects as higher-grade images that are clearer than physical shadows but still not fully grounded.
D: Distilled: Geometry and the mathematical sciences occupy the lower intelligible: they use hypotheses and images of a higher grade but cannot reach the unhypothetical first principle.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- 'Compelled to use hypotheses' = the limitation of mathematical reasoning -- 'Unable to rise above the region of hypothesis' = mathematics cannot question its own foundations -- 'Objects...as images' = a layered ontology: physical objects image mathematical objects, which image the Forms -- 'Greater distinctness and higher value' = each level is clearer and more real than the one below -- 'Geometry and the sister arts' = arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, harmonics (the mathematical sciences of Book VII) -- The key limitation: mathematics cannot reach the Good because it cannot examine its own assumptions -- Only dialectic (segment 4) can do this -- Cf. Book VII 533c: dialectic alone can 'give an account' of hypotheses

D: Original: And when I speak of the other division of the intelligible, you will understand me to speak of that other sort of knowledge which reason herself attains by the power of dialectic, using the hypotheses not as first principles, but only as hypotheses --that is to say, as steps and points of departure into a world which is above hypotheses, in order that she may soar beyond them to the first principle of the whole; and clinging to this and then to that which depends on this, by successive steps she descends again without the aid of any sensible object, from ideas, through ideas, and in ideas she ends.
D: Narrative: "And when I speak of the other division of the intelligible, you will understand me to speak of that other sort of knowledge which reason herself attains by the power of dialectic, using the hypotheses not as first principles, but only as hypotheses -- that is to say, as steps and points of departure into a world which is above hypotheses, in order that she may soar beyond them to the first principle of the whole; and clinging to this and then to that which depends on this, by successive steps she descends again without the aid of any sensible object, from ideas, through ideas, and in ideas she ends."
D: Refashioned:
"When I speak of the other division of the intelligible, I mean the kind of knowledge that reason itself achieves through the power of dialectic. Here, hypotheses are used not as first principles but only as genuine hypotheses -- as stepping stones and launching points into a realm above all hypotheses.

Reason soars beyond them to the first principle of everything. Then, grasping this first principle and following what depends on it, she descends step by step -- without any sensible aids whatsoever -- moving from Forms, through Forms, and ending in Forms."
D: Elegant: Dialectic uses hypotheses not as foundations but as stepping stones, soaring beyond them to the first principle of everything. From there, reason descends through pure Forms alone -- from ideas, through ideas, to ideas -- without any sensible aid.
D: Summary: The highest division of the intelligible is dialectic: it uses hypotheses merely as stepping stones, ascending to the first principle of all reality. From that summit, reason descends through pure Forms alone, without any sensible aids.
D: Distilled: Dialectic uses hypotheses as stepping stones to the first principle, then descends through pure Forms alone -- from ideas, through ideas, ending in ideas.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- Segment 4: noesis (pure reason) achieved through dialectic -- 'Hypotheses not as first principles but as genuine hypotheses' = dialectic treats assumptions as things to be questioned and transcended -- 'Steps and points of departure' (epibaseis and hormas) = hypotheses as springboards, not resting places -- 'First principle of the whole' (arche tou pantos) = the Form of the Good -- 'From ideas, through ideas, and in ideas she ends' = pure dialectical reasoning with no sensible component -- The movement: ascent to the Good, then descent through the connected system of Forms -- This describes the highest intellectual achievement in Plato's philosophy -- Cf. Book VII 532a-534e: the full description of dialectic as the capstone of education -- The 'descent' parallels the philosopher's return to the Cave (Book VII) to apply knowledge

D: Original: I understand you, he replied; not perfectly, for you seem to me to be describing a task which is really tremendous; but, at any rate, I understand you to say that knowledge and being, which the science of dialectic contemplates, are clearer than the notions of the arts, as they are termed, which proceed from hypotheses only: these are also contemplated by the understanding, and not by the senses: yet, because they start from hypotheses and do not ascend to a principle, those who contemplate them appear to you not to exercise the higher reason upon them, although when a first principle is added to them they are cognizable by the higher reason. And the habit which is concerned with geometry and the cognate sciences I suppose that you would term understanding and not reason, as being intermediate between opinion and reason.
D: Narrative: "I understand you," he replied; "not perfectly, for you seem to me to be describing a task which is really tremendous; but, at any rate, I understand you to say that knowledge and being, which the science of dialectic contemplates, are clearer than the notions of the arts, as they are termed, which proceed from hypotheses only: these are also contemplated by the understanding, and not by the senses: yet, because they start from hypotheses and do not ascend to a principle, those who contemplate them appear to you not to exercise the higher reason upon them, although when a first principle is added to them they are cognizable by the higher reason. And the habit which is concerned with geometry and the cognate sciences I suppose that you would term understanding and not reason, as being intermediate between opinion and reason."
D: Refashioned:
"I understand you," he replied, "though not perfectly -- what you're describing sounds truly enormous. But at least I understand this much: knowledge and being, as contemplated by dialectic, are clearer than the concepts of the mathematical sciences, which proceed only from hypotheses. These mathematical sciences engage the understanding, not the senses. Yet because they start from hypotheses without ascending to a first principle, you don't consider them to exercise the higher reason -- though they become fully knowable through higher reason when a first principle is added.

And the mental faculty concerned with geometry and related sciences -- you would call that understanding rather than reason, positioning it as intermediate between mere opinion and true reason."
D: Elegant: Dialectic's knowledge is clearer than mathematical understanding, which proceeds from ungrounded hypotheses. Mathematics engages understanding, not the senses, but falls short of reason because it doesn't ascend to a first principle. Understanding is intermediate between opinion and reason.
D: Summary: Glaucon summarizes: dialectic yields clearer knowledge than mathematics, which starts from hypotheses without reaching a first principle. The mathematical faculty (understanding) is positioned between opinion and reason -- higher than sensation but lower than dialectic.
D: Distilled: Understanding (mathematics) is intermediate between opinion and reason -- it grasps intelligible objects but cannot reach the first principle that dialectic attains.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- Glaucon's summary maps the four mental faculties onto the four line segments -- The four faculties: (1) eikasia (imagination/conjecture), (2) pistis (belief/faith), (3) dianoia (understanding), (4) noesis (reason/intellection) -- Dianoia (understanding) = mathematical cognition, intermediate between doxa (opinion) and noesis (reason) -- 'When a first principle is added' = when dialectic grounds mathematical hypotheses in the Good -- 'A task which is really tremendous' = Glaucon acknowledges the enormity of what Socrates describes -- The hierarchy: noesis > dianoia > pistis > eikasia, corresponding to Forms > mathematical objects > physical objects > images -- Cf. Book VII 534a: Socrates names these four states of mind

D: Original:
You have quite conceived my meaning, I said; and now, corresponding to these four divisions, let there be four faculties in the soul-reason answering to the highest, understanding to the second, faith (or conviction) to the third, and perception of shadows to the last-and let there be a scale of them, and let us suppose that the several faculties have clearness in the same degree that their objects have truth.

I understand, he replied, and give my assent, and accept your arrangement.
D: Narrative:
"You have quite conceived my meaning," I said; "and now, corresponding to these four divisions, let there be four faculties in the soul -- reason answering to the highest, understanding to the second, faith (or conviction) to the third, and perception of shadows to the last -- and let there be a scale of them, and let us suppose that the several faculties have clearness in the same degree that their objects have truth."

"I understand," he replied, "and give my assent, and accept your arrangement."
D: Refashioned:
"You've grasped my meaning exactly," I said. "Now, corresponding to these four divisions of reality, let there be four faculties of the soul: reason for the highest, understanding for the second, belief for the third, and perception of shadows for the last.

Arrange them in a scale, and suppose that each faculty possesses clarity in exact proportion to the truth possessed by its corresponding objects."

"I understand," he replied, "and I agree. I accept your arrangement."
D: Elegant: Four divisions of reality correspond to four faculties of the soul: reason (highest), understanding, belief, and perception of shadows (lowest). Each faculty possesses clarity in exact proportion to the truth of its objects.
D: Summary: Socrates completes the Divided Line by mapping four faculties of the soul onto the four divisions of reality: reason corresponds to Forms, understanding to mathematical objects, belief to physical objects, and perception of shadows to images. Each faculty's clarity matches its objects' truth. Glaucon accepts the arrangement.
D: Distilled: Four faculties of the soul -- reason, understanding, belief, and shadow-perception -- correspond to four levels of reality, each possessing clarity proportional to its objects' truth.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- The complete Divided Line schema: (1) eikasia (shadow-perception/imagination) -> images, (2) pistis (belief/conviction) -> physical objects, (3) dianoia (understanding) -> mathematical objects, (4) noesis (reason/intellection) -> Forms -- 'Clearness in the same degree that their objects have truth' = the isomorphism between epistemology and ontology -- This is the capstone of Book VI's metaphysics, completed by the Cave allegory in Book VII -- 'Faith or conviction' (pistis) = not religious faith but confident belief based on sensory experience -- The proportional structure: as images are to objects, so objects are to mathematical forms, so mathematical forms are to pure Forms -- This schema influenced all subsequent Western metaphysics, epistemology, and philosophy of mathematics -- Cf. Book VII 514a-521b: the Cave allegory dramatizes the ascent through these four levels -- The Divided Line is the theoretical architecture; the Cave is its narrative expression

D: Original: And now, I said, let me show in a figure how far our nature is enlightened or unenlightened: --Behold! human beings living in a underground den, which has a mouth open towards the light and reaching all along the den; here they have been from their childhood, and have their legs and necks chained so that they cannot move, and can only see before them, being prevented by the chains from turning round their heads. Above and behind them a fire is blazing at a distance, and between the fire and the prisoners there is a raised way; and you will see, if you look, a low wall built along the way, like the screen which marionette players have in front of them, over which they show the puppets.
D: Narrative: "And now," I said, "let me show in a figure how far our nature is enlightened or unenlightened: -- Behold! human beings living in a underground den, which has a mouth open towards the light and reaching all along the den; here they have been from their childhood, and have their legs and necks chained so that they cannot move, and can only see before them, being prevented by the chains from turning round their heads. Above and behind them a fire is blazing at a distance, and between the fire and the prisoners there is a raised way; and you will see, if you look, a low wall built along the way, like the screen which marionette players have in front of them, over which they show the puppets."
D: Refashioned: "Now," Socrates said, "let me paint a picture of the human condition -- how enlightened or unenlightened we really are. Imagine people living in an underground cave, open at one end to the light but stretching deep inside. They've been there since childhood, chained at the legs and neck so they can't move or turn around -- they can only look straight ahead. Far behind and above them, a fire blazes. Between the fire and the prisoners runs a raised walkway, and along it stands a low wall -- like the screen that puppet operators use to hide behind while they hold their figures up for the audience to see."
D: Elegant: Socrates asks Glaucon to imagine prisoners chained since childhood in an underground cave, able to see only the wall before them. Behind them burns a fire, and between fire and prisoners runs a raised walkway with a low wall -- like a puppet show screen -- over which objects are carried.
D: Summary: Socrates introduces the Allegory of the Cave: prisoners chained since birth in an underground den can only see shadows on the wall before them, cast by a fire behind them and objects carried along a raised walkway.
D: Distilled: Human beings are like prisoners chained in a cave, seeing only shadows and mistaking them for reality.
Notes: Socrates is speaking, setting up the Allegory of the Cave for Glaucon -- This is the most famous passage in all of Plato's works -- The cave allegory maps onto the Divided Line from Book VI (509d-511e): shadows in the cave = images/eikasia, the fire = the visible sun, the world outside = the intelligible realm -- The puppet-show screen (thaumatopoioi) references traveling entertainers familiar to Athenians -- Cross-ref: the allegory extends the Sun analogy from Book VI (507b-509c)

D: Original:
I see. And do you see, I said, men passing along the wall carrying all sorts of vessels, and statues and figures of animals made of wood and stone and various materials, which appear over the wall? Some of them are talking, others silent.

You have shown me a strange image, and they are strange prisoners.

Like ourselves, I replied; and they see only their own shadows, or the shadows of one another, which the fire throws on the opposite wall of the cave?

True, he said; how could they see anything but the shadows if they were never allowed to move their heads?

And of the objects which are being carried in like manner they would only see the shadows?
