D: Elegant: Even warfare requires number. Palamedes in tragedy boasts of inventing number to organize the army at Troy, making Agamemnon -- who presumably couldn't even count his feet -- look absurdly unfit as a general.
D: Summary: Socrates uses the myth of Palamedes to argue that arithmetic is essential even for military command. In tragedy, Palamedes claims to have invented counting to organize the Greek forces at Troy, implying Agamemnon was so ignorant of number he couldn't count his own feet.
D: Distilled: Even a military general requires arithmetic -- without number, you cannot organize an army or count your own feet.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- Palamedes was a legendary Greek hero credited with inventing number, weights and measures, and the alphabet; he appears in lost tragedies by Aeschylus, Sophocles, and Euripides -- Agamemnon was commander-in-chief of the Greek forces at Troy -- The humorous point satisfies the requirement that the curriculum be useful in war -- Cross-ref: the practical/military value of mathematics is the entry point, but Socrates will soon elevate it to philosophical value (7.27 onward)

D: Narrative:
"I should say a very strange one, if this was as you say."

"Can we deny that a warrior should have a knowledge of arithmetic?"

"Certainly he should, if he is to have the smallest understanding of military tactics, or indeed, I should rather say, if he is to be a man at all."

"I should like to know whether you have the same notion which I have of this study?"

"What is your notion?"

"It appears to me to be a study of the kind which we are seeking, and which leads naturally to reflection, but never to have been rightly used; for the true use of it is simply to draw the soul towards being."

"Will you explain your meaning?" he said.

"I will try," I said; "and I wish you would share the enquiry with me, and say 'yes' or 'no' when I attempt to distinguish in my own mind what branches of knowledge have this attracting power, in order that we may have clearer proof that arithmetic is, as I suspect, one of them."

"Explain," he said.

"I mean to say that objects of sense are of two kinds; some of them do not invite thought because the sense is an adequate judge of them; while in the case of other objects sense is so untrustworthy that further enquiry is imperatively demanded."

"You are clearly referring," he said, "to the manner in which the senses are imposed upon by distance, and by painting in light and shade."
D: Refashioned:
"A very strange general indeed," said Glaucon.

"So can we deny that a warrior needs arithmetic?"

"Not at all -- he needs it for the most basic military tactics, and really, to function as a human being at all."

"Do you share my view of this study's deeper significance?"

"What's your view?"

"Arithmetic seems to be exactly the kind of study we're looking for -- one that naturally stimulates reflection. But it has never been properly used. Its true purpose is to draw the soul toward being."

"Explain what you mean," said Glaucon.

"I'll try," said Socrates, "and I'd like you to join the inquiry. Tell me whether you agree as I try to distinguish which branches of knowledge have this power to attract the soul upward, so we can confirm that arithmetic is one of them."

"Go on."

"Here's the key distinction: some sense-objects don't provoke thought because the senses judge them adequately. But other objects are such that the senses are completely unreliable, and further inquiry is urgently required."

"You must be talking about optical illusions," said Glaucon -- "how the senses are deceived by distance, and by tricks of light and shadow."
D: Elegant: Arithmetic's true purpose is not practical but philosophical: to draw the soul toward being. The key distinction is between sense-objects that the senses judge adequately and those that provoke the mind to further inquiry because sensory evidence is unreliable.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that arithmetic's true purpose is drawing the soul toward being, not just practical calculation. He introduces a crucial distinction: some sensory objects don't provoke thought, while others do because the senses give unreliable reports about them, demanding rational inquiry.
D: Distilled: The true value of arithmetic is not practical but philosophical -- it awakens the mind to seek being when the senses prove unreliable.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- Socrates distinguishes between objects that 'invite thought' and those that don't -- this becomes the criterion for the philosophical curriculum -- Glaucon guesses wrong (optical illusions); Socrates will correct him with the finger example (7.28-7.29) -- 'Draw the soul towards being' = the fundamental criterion for including a subject in the philosopher's curriculum -- Cross-ref: Divided Line (Book VI, 509d-511e) -- dianoia (mathematical reasoning) as the bridge between opinion and noesis; Theaetetus 184b-186e on the inadequacy of sensation for knowledge

D: Original: No, I said, that is not at all my meaning. Then what is your meaning? When speaking of uninviting objects, I mean those which do not pass from one sensation to the opposite; inviting objects are those which do; in this latter case the sense coming upon the object, whether at a distance or near, gives no more vivid idea of anything in particular than of its opposite. An illustration will make my meaning clearer: --here are three fingers --a little finger, a second finger, and a middle finger.
D: Narrative:
"No," I said, "that is not at all my meaning."

"Then what is your meaning?"

"When speaking of uninviting objects, I mean those which do not pass from one sensation to the opposite; inviting objects are those which do; in this latter case the sense coming upon the object, whether at a distance or near, gives no more vivid idea of anything in particular than of its opposite. An illustration will make my meaning clearer: -- here are three fingers -- a little finger, a second finger, and a middle finger."
D: Refashioned:
"No," said Socrates, "that's not what I mean at all."

"Then what do you mean?"

"By 'uninviting' objects, I mean those that don't produce contradictory sensations. 'Inviting' objects are those that do -- where the sense, whether close up or far away, reports one quality no more vividly than its opposite. Let me illustrate. Here are three fingers -- the little finger, the ring finger, and the middle finger."
D: Elegant: Socrates clarifies: objects that provoke thought are those where sensation simultaneously reports a quality and its opposite. He illustrates with three fingers.
D: Summary: Socrates corrects Glaucon: he doesn't mean optical illusions. Thought-provoking objects are those where sensation simultaneously presents contradictory qualities -- perceiving something as both one thing and its opposite. He introduces the example of three fingers to demonstrate.
D: Distilled: The mind is awakened not by optical illusions but by sensory contradictions -- when the same object appears to possess opposite qualities simultaneously.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- The 'inviting/uninviting' distinction is central to Plato's epistemology: contradictory sense data forces the soul to engage reason -- This passage develops the theory that intellectual inquiry begins when the senses deliver contradictory reports -- The finger example that follows (7.29-7.31) is one of Plato's most concrete philosophical illustrations -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 74a-75d (the 'equal sticks' argument: sensible equals appear both equal and unequal, pointing toward the Form of Equality); Theaetetus 184-186

D: Original:
Very good. You may suppose that they are seen quite close: And here comes the point.

What is it? Each of them equally appears a finger, whether seen in the middle or at the extremity, whether white or black, or thick or thin --it makes no difference; a finger is a finger all the same. In these cases a man is not compelled to ask of thought the question, what is a finger? for the sight never intimates to the mind that a finger is other than a finger.

True. And therefore, I said, as we might expect, there is nothing here which invites or excites intelligence.
D: Narrative:
"Very good."

"You may suppose that they are seen quite close. And here comes the point."

"What is it?"

"Each of them equally appears a finger, whether seen in the middle or at the extremity, whether white or black, or thick or thin -- it makes no difference; a finger is a finger all the same. "True."

"And therefore," I said, "as we might expect, there is nothing here which invites or excites intelligence."
D: Refashioned:
"Very good," said Glaucon.

"Suppose you're looking at them up close. Here's the point."

"What is it?"

"Each one equally appears to be a finger, whether it's in the middle or at the edge of the hand, whether it's light or dark, thick or thin -- none of that matters. A finger is a finger. In this case, no one is compelled to ask the mind, 'What is a finger?' because sight never suggests that a finger is anything other than a finger."

"True."

"And therefore, as we'd expect, there's nothing here that invites or stimulates intellectual inquiry."
D: Elegant: A finger always appears to be a finger regardless of its position, color, or thickness. Sight never contradicts itself about its nature as a finger, so no intellectual inquiry is provoked.
D: Summary: Socrates points out that a finger is always perceived unambiguously as a finger, regardless of position, color, or size. Since the senses never deliver a contradictory report about its identity as a finger, the mind is not stimulated to inquire further.
D: Distilled: When perception is unambiguous -- a finger is just a finger -- no deeper inquiry is provoked.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- The finger example is the negative case: 'finger-ness' doesn't provoke thought because the senses are consistent -- Socrates will now contrast this with relative properties (big/small, hard/soft) which DO provoke thought (7.30-7.31) -- The argument structure: unambiguous sense data = no philosophical stimulus; contradictory sense data = stimulus to seek intelligible reality -- Cross-ref: Parmenides 129a-130a where Socrates distinguishes unproblematic predication from paradoxical predication

Is not their mode of operation on this wise --the sense which is concerned with the quality of hardness is necessarily concerned also with the quality of softness, and only intimates to the soul that the same thing is felt to be both hard and soft?

"There is not," he said.

And in like manner does the touch adequately perceive the qualities of thickness or thinness, or softness or hardness? And so of the other senses; do they give perfect intimations of such matters? Is not their mode of operation on this wise -- the sense which is concerned with the quality of hardness is necessarily concerned also with the quality of softness, and only intimates to the soul that the same thing is felt to be both hard and soft?"
D: Refashioned:
"No, there isn't," Glaucon agreed.

"But is the same true of the fingers' size -- their greatness and smallness?" Socrates asked. "Can sight adequately judge them? Doesn't it matter whether a finger is in the middle or at the edge? And can touch reliably perceive thickness or thinness, hardness or softness? Don't the senses work like this: the sense that perceives hardness necessarily also perceives softness, and it reports to the soul that the very same thing is both hard and soft?"
D: Elegant: Unlike 'finger-ness,' relative qualities like large/small, hard/soft, thick/thin cannot be adequately judged by the senses. The same finger is both large (compared to one) and small (compared to another). The senses report contradictory qualities simultaneously.
D: Summary: Socrates contrasts the unambiguous perception of 'finger' with the ambiguous perception of relative qualities. The senses cannot adequately judge size, hardness, or thickness because they inevitably report contradictions -- the same finger is both large and small, both hard and soft.
D: Distilled: Relative qualities like large and small, hard and soft, cannot be settled by the senses because the same object simultaneously possesses opposite properties.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- This is the positive case: relative/comparative properties generate the contradictions that provoke thought -- The argument anticipates the distinction between absolute and relative properties -- 'The same thing is felt to be both hard and soft' = the same object is hard relative to one thing, soft relative to another -- This is a version of the argument from opposites found also in the Phaedo and Hippias Major -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 74a-75d (equal sticks appearing both equal and unequal); Hippias Major 289a-c (the beautiful girl both beautiful and ugly by comparison)

Yes, he said, these intimations which the soul receives are very curious and require to be explained.

D: Refashioned:

"True."

D: Elegant: When the senses report contradictions -- hard and soft, great and small in the same object -- the soul summons reason to separate what perception has confused. This forces the question 'What IS greatness?' as distinct from 'What IS smallness?' -- and thus the distinction between the visible and intelligible worlds is born.
D: Summary: Sensory contradictions -- perceiving the same thing as both hard and soft, great and small -- force the soul to engage reason. The mind separates what the senses confuse, asking 'What IS greatness?' This is how the distinction between visible and intelligible reality arises. Impressions presenting simultaneous opposites are what provoke intellectual inquiry.
D: Distilled: Philosophy begins when contradictory sense experience forces the mind to ask 'What IS this?' -- separating the intelligible from the visible.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon in rapid dialectical exchange -- This passage gives Plato's genetic account of how philosophy begins: sensory contradiction forces abstraction -- 'What is great? What is small?' = the turn from particular sensible instances to universal intelligible Forms -- 'The distinction of the visible and the intelligible' directly maps onto the Divided Line from Book VI -- The argument that opposites provoke inquiry connects to the theory of Forms: we need Forms precisely because sensible things participate in opposite properties -- Cross-ref: Divided Line (Book VI, 509d-511e); Phaedo 74a-75d (argument from sensible equals to the Form of Equality); Parmenides 129a-130a

D: Original:
I understand, he said, and agree with you. And to which class do unity and number belong? I do not know, he replied. Think a little and you will see that what has preceded will supply the answer; for if simple unity could be adequately perceived by the sight or by any other sense, then, as we were saying in the case of the finger, there would be nothing to attract towards being; but when there is some contradiction always present, and one is the reverse of one and involves the conception of plurality, then thought begins to be aroused within us, and the soul perplexed and wanting to arrive at a decision asks 'What is absolute unity?' This is the way in which the study of the one has a power of drawing and converting the mind to the contemplation of true being.

And surely, he said, this occurs notably in the case of one; for we see the same thing to be both one and infinite in multitude?

Yes, I said; and this being true of one must be equally true of all number?

Certainly. And all arithmetic and calculation have to do with number?
D: Narrative:
"I understand," he said, "and agree with you."

"And to which class do unity and number belong?"

"I do not know," he replied.

"Think a little and you will see that what has preceded will supply the answer; for if simple unity could be adequately perceived by the sight or by any other sense, then, as we were saying in the case of the finger, there would be nothing to attract towards being; but when there is some contradiction always present, and one is the reverse of one and involves the conception of plurality, then thought begins to be aroused within us, and the soul perplexed and wanting to arrive at a decision asks 'What is absolute unity?' This is the way in which the study of the one has a power of drawing and converting the mind to the contemplation of true being."

"And surely," he said, "this occurs notably in the case of one; for we see the same thing to be both one and infinite in multitude?"

"Yes," I said; "and this being true of one must be equally true of all number?"

"Certainly."

"And all arithmetic and calculation have to do with number?"
D: Refashioned:
"I understand and agree," said Glaucon.

"Now, to which class do unity and number belong -- the thought-provoking or the non-provoking?"

"I'm not sure."

"Think about it," said Socrates. "The answer follows from what we've just said. If simple unity could be adequately perceived by sight or any other sense, it would be like the finger -- nothing would attract the mind toward being. But there IS a contradiction always present: one is the reverse of one and implies plurality. This arouses thought. The perplexed soul, wanting to resolve the contradiction, asks: 'What is absolute unity?' This is how the study of the one draws and converts the mind to contemplate true being."

"That makes sense," said Glaucon. "We see the same thing as both one and infinitely many."

"Yes, and if this is true of one, it's equally true of all number."

"Certainly."

"And all arithmetic and calculation deal with number?"
D: Elegant: Unity provokes thought because the same thing is simultaneously one and many. The perplexed soul asks 'What is absolute unity?' -- and this question draws the mind toward being. Since this is true of all number, arithmetic and calculation are inherently philosophical disciplines.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that unity belongs to the thought-provoking class because the same thing appears as both one and many, creating a contradiction that forces the soul to ask 'What is absolute unity?' Since this applies to all number, arithmetic inherently draws the mind toward contemplation of true being.
D: Distilled: The paradox of the one and the many -- that every thing is simultaneously one and infinite in multitude -- makes arithmetic a gateway to philosophical inquiry.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- The One and the Many problem is among the most fundamental in Greek philosophy, treated extensively in the Parmenides -- 'What is absolute unity?' = the Form of Unity, an intelligible reality grasped by reason alone -- Every sensible thing is both one (itself) and many (having parts, properties, relations) -- this contradiction provokes philosophical inquiry -- Cross-ref: Parmenides 128e-130a (the problem of the one and many); Philebus 14c-18d (the one and many as the greatest philosophical problem); Divided Line (mathematical reasoning as dianoia)

That is true. And our guardian is both warrior and philosopher? Certainly. D: Elegant: The law should prescribe arithmetic studied deeply, for the mind alone -- not for commercial profit but for the soul's passage from becoming to being. Pursued philosophically rather than commercially, arithmetic is both charming and powerful.
D: Summary: Socrates prescribes arithmetic as law for future rulers, insisting it be studied deeply and purely -- not for commercial gain like merchants, but to grasp numbers with the mind alone, enabling the soul's passage from becoming to being.
D: Distilled: Arithmetic must be studied philosophically for the soul's ascent, not commercially for profit -- the spirit of inquiry determines whether knowledge liberates or remains mundane.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- The contrast between philosophical and commercial arithmetic is fundamental: the same subject can serve opposite purposes depending on the spirit of inquiry -- 'See the nature of numbers with the mind only' = pure mathematical reasoning, grasping intelligible number distinct from sensible counting -- 'Not like merchants or retail-traders' reflects the aristocratic Greek disdain for commercial pursuits (banausic arts) -- Cross-ref: the Divided Line places mathematical reasoning (dianoia) above opinion but below dialectical knowledge (noesis) -- Book VI, 510b-511b

D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean?" asked Glaucon.

"Arithmetic has a profoundly elevating effect," Socrates explained. "It compels the soul to reason about abstract number and resists any attempt to introduce visible or tangible objects into the argument. You know how mathematicians react when someone tries to divide absolute unity during calculation -- they immediately multiply it back, insisting that one must remain one and never dissolve into fractions."
D: Elegant: Arithmetic compels the soul to reason abstractly, resisting the introduction of sensible objects. Mathematicians refuse to divide unity -- if you split one, they multiply it back, insisting that one must remain indivisible.
D: Summary: Socrates explains that arithmetic elevates the soul by forcing abstract reasoning about number, rejecting visible or tangible objects. Mathematicians fiercely protect the indivisibility of unity -- if someone divides one, they multiply to restore it, ensuring one remains one.
D: Distilled: Arithmetic's philosophical power lies in forcing the mind to reason abstractly, treating numbers as indivisible intelligible realities rather than divisible physical things.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- The mathematicians' refusal to divide unity reflects a deep Greek mathematical concern: the unit (monas) was considered indivisible; fractions were handled as ratios between whole numbers -- This connects to the Pythagorean tradition where numbers were fundamental realities -- 'Abstract number' vs. 'visible objects' = the distinction between intelligible and sensible, the upper and lower halves of the Divided Line -- Cross-ref: the Pythagorean influence on Plato's philosophy of mathematics; Aristotle's Metaphysics 987b on Plato's mathematical ontology

D: Refashioned:
"Very true," said Glaucon.

"Now suppose someone asked these mathematicians: 'What are these remarkable numbers you reason about -- these units you insist are equal, invariable, and indivisible? What would they say?'"

"They'd say they're speaking of numbers that can only exist in thought."

"Then you can see why this knowledge truly deserves to be called necessary -- it necessitates the use of pure intelligence to attain pure truth."

"Yes, that's clearly its distinguishing feature."

"No, you won't."

"I agree."

"You mean geometry?"

"The part of geometry relevant to war is obvious," said Glaucon. "Pitching camps, taking positions, extending or contracting battle lines, maneuvering in combat or on the march -- a general who knows geometry has a huge advantage over one who doesn't."

True, he said. Then if geometry compels us to view being, it concerns us; if becoming only, it does not concern us?

Yes, that is what we assert. Yet anybody who has the least acquaintance with geometry will not deny that such a conception of the science is in flat contradiction to the ordinary language of geometricians.

How so? They have in view practice only, and are always speaking? in a narrow and ridiculous manner, of squaring and extending and applying and the like --they confuse the necessities of geometry with those of daily life; whereas knowledge is the real object of the whole science.

Certainly, he said. Then must not a further admission be made? What admission? That the knowledge at which geometry aims is knowledge of the eternal, and not of aught perishing and transient.

That, he replied, may be readily allowed, and is true. Then, my noble friend, geometry will draw the soul towards truth, and create the spirit of philosophy, and raise up that which is now unhappily allowed to fall down.

Nothing will be more likely to have such an effect. Then nothing should be more sternly laid down than that the inhabitants of your fair city should by all means learn geometry. Moreover the science has indirect effects, which are not small.

Of what kind? he said. There are the military advantages of which you spoke, I said; and in all departments of knowledge, as experience proves, any one who has studied geometry is infinitely quicker of apprehension than one who has not.

Yes indeed, he said, there is an infinite difference between them.

Then shall we propose this as a second branch of knowledge which our youth will study?

Let us do so, he replied. And suppose we make astronomy the third --what do you say?

I am strongly inclined to it, he said; the observation of the seasons and of months and years is as essential to the general as it is to the farmer or sailor.

"Certainly," he said.

"Then must not a further admission be made?"

"What admission?"

"That the knowledge at which geometry aims is knowledge of the eternal, and not of aught perishing and transient."

"That," he replied, "may be readily allowed, and is true."

"Then, my noble friend, geometry will draw the soul towards truth, and create the spirit of philosophy, and raise up that which is now unhappily allowed to fall down."

"Nothing will be more likely to have such an effect."

"Then nothing should be more sternly laid down than that the inhabitants of your fair city should by all means learn geometry. Moreover the science has indirect effects, which are not small."

"Of what kind?" he said.

"There are the military advantages of which you spoke," I said; "and in all departments of knowledge, as experience proves, any one who has studied geometry is infinitely quicker of apprehension than one who has not."

"Yes indeed," he said, "there is an infinite difference between them."

"Then shall we propose this as a second branch of knowledge which our youth will study?"

"Let us do so," he replied.

"And suppose we make astronomy the third -- what do you say?"

"I am strongly inclined to it," he said; "the observation of the seasons and of months and years is as essential to the general as it is to the farmer or sailor."
D: Refashioned:
"True," said Glaucon.

"So if geometry compels us to view being, it's relevant to our purpose. If it deals only with becoming, it isn't."

"That's our principle."

"But anyone who knows even a little geometry would say this conception flatly contradicts how geometers actually talk."

"How so?"

"They focus entirely on practice -- always talking in a narrow, ridiculous way about squaring, extending, applying, and so on. They confuse the demands of geometry with the demands of daily life, when knowledge is really the point of the entire science."

"Certainly."

"Then we must make a further admission."

"What?"

"That geometry ultimately aims at knowledge of the eternal -- not of anything that perishes or changes."

"That's readily granted, and true."

"Then geometry will draw the soul toward truth, cultivate the spirit of philosophy, and lift up what is now sadly allowed to sink down."

"Nothing is more likely to have that effect."

"Then we should firmly require that everyone in our city learn geometry. And beyond its philosophical value, it has significant secondary benefits."

"Like what?"

"The military advantages you mentioned. And in every field of knowledge, experience shows that anyone who has studied geometry grasps things infinitely faster than someone who hasn't."

"That's absolutely true -- the difference is enormous."

"Then geometry becomes the second branch of study for our youth?"

"Yes, let's establish that."

"And shall we make astronomy the third?"

"I'm strongly inclined to agree," said Glaucon. "Observing seasons, months, and years is as essential for a general as for a farmer or sailor."
D: Elegant: Geometry aims at eternal knowledge, not practical applications like squaring and extending. It draws the soul toward truth and creates the philosophical spirit. Established as the second subject, Socrates then proposes astronomy as the third. Glaucon again focuses on practical value -- tracking seasons -- which Socrates will again redirect.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that geometry, despite geometers' practical focus on squaring and extending, truly aims at knowledge of the eternal. It draws the soul toward truth and philosophy. Established as the second subject after arithmetic, astronomy is proposed as the third. Glaucon appreciates its practical value for tracking seasons, which Socrates will challenge.
D: Distilled: Geometry aims not at practical construction but at knowledge of the eternal, drawing the soul toward truth and philosophy.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Squaring and extending and applying' = the practical language of working geometers, which Socrates dismisses as missing the philosophical point -- 'Knowledge of the eternal' = geometry studies Forms (the perfect circle, the ideal triangle) not physical shapes -- Glaucon's response to astronomy ('observation of seasons') again focuses on practical value, setting up Socrates' correction in 7.39 and 7.42-7.43 -- Cross-ref: Divided Line (geometers work with hypotheses and diagrams but point toward intelligible realities, Book VI 510b-511b); Meno 82b-86c (geometric proof as path to recollection)

Now there are two classes of persons: one class of those who will agree with you and will take your words as a revelation; another class to whom they will be utterly unmeaning, and who will naturally deem them to be idle tales, for they see no sort of profit which is to be obtained from them. You will very likely say with neither, and that your chief aim in carrying on the argument is your own improvement; at the same time you do not grudge to others any benefit which they may receive.

D: Refashioned:
"I'm amused," said Socrates, "by your concern about public opinion -- your eagerness to avoid seeming to champion useless studies. I grant it's hard to believe that every person has an eye of the soul which, when dimmed and lost through other pursuits, can be purified and re-illuminated by these studies. Yet this eye is worth far more than ten thousand bodily eyes, because truth can only be seen through it.

"Now, there are two kinds of people: those who will hear this and receive it as revelation, and those who will find it meaningless -- idle tales with no practical profit. So you'd better decide which group you're addressing. D: Elegant: Socrates gently mocks Glaucon's concern about appearing impractical. Every soul has an inner eye, dimmed by worldly pursuits but purifiable through these studies -- worth more than ten thousand bodily eyes, for it alone sees truth. Some will receive this as revelation; others will dismiss it as useless.
D: Summary: Socrates teases Glaucon for worrying about seeming impractical and affirms that every soul has an inner eye -- dimmed by other pursuits but re-illuminated by these studies -- worth more than ten thousand physical eyes because it alone perceives truth. People divide into those who find this revelatory and those who find it meaningless.
D: Distilled: The eye of the soul, worth more than ten thousand bodily eyes, is dimmed by worldly pursuits and re-illuminated by philosophical study -- but only some will believe this.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Eye of the soul' (tes psyches omma) is one of Plato's most powerful metaphors, used also at 533d -- The valuation of inner sight over physical sight connects to the Cave allegory: the freed prisoner's physical eyes suffer, but his soul's eye is opened -- 'Ten thousand bodily eyes' -- a characteristically Platonic hyperbole -- The two classes of listeners anticipates the divided reception of philosophy throughout history -- Cross-ref: Book VI (508d) where the Good illuminates the intelligible world as the sun illuminates the visible; Phaedrus 247c-d (the soul's vision of the Forms)

I think that I should prefer to carry on the argument mainly on my own behalf.

Then take a step backward, for we have gone wrong in the order of the sciences.

What was the mistake? he said. After plane geometry, I said, we proceeded at once to solids in revolution, instead of taking solids in themselves; whereas after the second dimension the third, which is concerned with cubes and dimensions of depth, ought to have followed.

That is true, Socrates; but so little seems to be known as yet about these subjects.
D: Narrative:
"I think that I should prefer to carry on the argument mainly on my own behalf."

"Then take a step backward, for we have gone wrong in the order of the sciences."

"What was the mistake?" he said.

"After plane geometry," I said, "we proceeded at once to solids in revolution, instead of taking solids in themselves; whereas after the second dimension the third, which is concerned with cubes and dimensions of depth, ought to have followed."

"That is true, Socrates; but so little seems to be known as yet about these subjects."
D: Refashioned:
"I'd rather pursue the argument mainly for my own sake," said Glaucon.

"Then let's step back," Socrates said, "because we've gotten the order of the sciences wrong."

"What was the mistake?"

"After plane geometry -- two dimensions -- we jumped straight to solids in motion, which is astronomy. But we should have first studied solids in themselves -- the third dimension, concerned with cubes and depth."

"That's true, Socrates, but so little seems to be known about solid geometry yet."
D: Elegant: Socrates corrects the curriculum order: after plane geometry (two dimensions), solid geometry (three dimensions -- cubes and depth) should come before astronomy (solids in motion). But Glaucon notes the field is barely developed.
D: Summary: Socrates catches a mistake in their curriculum ordering: after plane geometry, they should study solid geometry (three-dimensional figures) before astronomy (solids in motion). Glaucon agrees but notes that solid geometry is still a poorly developed field.
D: Distilled: The proper order of mathematical education proceeds by dimension: plane geometry, then solid geometry, then the motion of solids (astronomy).
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Solids in revolution' = astronomy, the study of moving three-dimensional bodies -- The correction reveals Plato's systematic approach: education should follow the logical order of mathematical objects (1D -> 2D -> 3D -> 3D in motion) -- Solid geometry (stereometry) was indeed underdeveloped in Plato's time; Theaetetus and Eudoxus made important advances -- Cross-ref: this passage influenced the structure of Euclid's Elements (Books XI-XIII on solid geometry); the curriculum: arithmetic -> plane geometry -> solid geometry -> astronomy -> harmonics -> dialectic

But then a director can hardly be found, and even if he could, as matters now stand, the students, who are very conceited, would not attend to him. That, however, would be otherwise if the whole State became the director of these studies and gave honour to them; then disciples would want to come, and there would be continuous and earnest search, and discoveries would be made; since even now, disregarded as they are by the world, and maimed of their fair proportions, and although none of their votaries can tell the use of them, still these studies force their way by their natural charm, and very likely, if they had the help of the State, they would some day emerge into light.

But then a director can hardly be found, and even if he could, as matters now stand, the students, who are very conceited, would not attend to him. That, however, would be otherwise if the whole State became the director of these studies and gave honour to them; then disciples would want to come, and there would be continuous and earnest search, and discoveries would be made; since even now, disregarded as they are by the world, and maimed of their fair proportions, and although none of their votaries can tell the use of them, still these studies force their way by their natural charm, and very likely, if they had the help of the State, they would some day emerge into light."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," said Socrates, "and for two reasons. First, no government funds or supports these studies, so there's no energy behind pursuing them -- and they're inherently difficult. Second, students can't learn without a teacher, but qualified teachers are nearly impossible to find. And even if one appeared, today's students are too arrogant to listen.

"But things would be different if the state itself directed these studies and honored them. Students would flock to learn, research would be sustained and serious, and discoveries would follow. Even now, neglected by the world, stunted in their development, with nobody able to explain their usefulness, these studies push forward by sheer intellectual charm. With state support, they would surely come into their own."
D: Elegant: Solid geometry languishes because no government supports it and qualified teachers are scarce. Yet even neglected, these studies advance by their natural intellectual charm. With state patronage, they would flourish and yield discoveries.
D: Summary: Socrates identifies why solid geometry remains undeveloped: lack of government support and scarcity of qualified teachers. Yet even neglected and dismissed as useless, these studies advance through their inherent intellectual appeal. State patronage would transform the field.
D: Distilled: Without state support, even naturally compelling studies languish -- but their inherent intellectual charm ensures they advance despite neglect.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- This is a remarkable passage about research funding and institutional support for pure science -- 'No government patronises them' = a complaint about the lack of state investment in theoretical research, strikingly modern -- 'Force their way by their natural charm' suggests mathematical truth has an inherent attractiveness that survives institutional neglect -- Cross-ref: this passage may reflect conditions at Plato's Academy, where stereometry was being actively developed; Theaetetus and Eudoxus of Cnidus were key figures

D: Original:
Yes, he said, there is a remarkable charm in them. But I do not clearly understand the change in the order. First you began with a geometry of plane surfaces?

Yes, I said. And you placed astronomy next, and then you made a step backward?

Yes, and I have delayed you by my hurry; the ludicrous state of solid geometry, which, in natural order, should have followed, made me pass over this branch and go on to astronomy, or motion of solids.

True, he said. Then assuming that the science now omitted would come into existence if encouraged by the State, let us go on to astronomy, which will be fourth.

The right order, he replied. And now, Socrates, as you rebuked the vulgar manner in which I praised astronomy before, my praise shall be given in your own spirit. For every one, as I think, must see that astronomy compels the soul to look upwards and leads us from this world to another.

Every one but myself, I said; to every one else this may be clear, but not to me.

And what then would you say? I should rather say that those who elevate astronomy into philosophy appear to me to make us look downwards and not upwards.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "there is a remarkable charm in them. But I do not clearly understand the change in the order. First you began with a geometry of plane surfaces?"

"Yes," I said.

"And you placed astronomy next, and then you made a step backward?"

"Yes, and I have delayed you by my hurry; the ludicrous state of solid geometry, which, in natural order, should have followed, made me pass over this branch and go on to astronomy, or motion of solids."

"True," he said.

"Then assuming that the science now omitted would come into existence if encouraged by the State, let us go on to astronomy, which will be fourth."

"The right order," he replied.

"And now, Socrates, as you rebuked the vulgar manner in which I praised astronomy before, my praise shall be given in your own spirit. For every one, as I think, must see that astronomy compels the soul to look upwards and leads us from this world to another."

"Every one but myself," I said; "to every one else this may be clear, but not to me."

"And what then would you say?"

"I should rather say that those who elevate astronomy into philosophy appear to me to make us look downwards and not upwards."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," said Glaucon, "they certainly have a charm. But I'm confused about the order. You started with plane geometry?"

"Yes."

"Then placed astronomy next, and now you've stepped backward?"

"Right -- I rushed ahead. The sad state of solid geometry, which logically should come next, made me skip it and jump to astronomy, the study of solids in motion."

"I see."

"So let's assume the state will develop solid geometry in due time, and proceed to astronomy as our fourth subject."

"That's the right order," Glaucon agreed. "And now, Socrates, since you criticized my earlier praise of astronomy as too vulgar, let me try again in your spirit. Surely everyone can see that astronomy compels the soul to look upward and leads us from this world to a higher one."

"Everyone except me," said Socrates. "That may be clear to everyone else, but not to me."

"What would you say instead?"

"I'd say that those who elevate astronomy into philosophy this way actually make us look downward, not upward."
D: Elegant: With solid geometry assumed as the third subject, astronomy becomes the fourth. Glaucon praises astronomy for compelling the soul to look upward. Socrates paradoxically counters: popular astronomy makes us look downward, not upward.
D: Summary: Having placed solid geometry third (assuming state support), Socrates makes astronomy fourth. When Glaucon praises astronomy for turning the soul upward, Socrates paradoxically replies that the way most people approach astronomy actually directs the soul downward, not upward.
D: Distilled: Simply gazing at the physical sky is not true philosophical astronomy -- staring upward at sensible objects can actually direct the soul downward.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- Socrates' paradox ('looking upward makes you look downward') captures his key educational principle: the direction of the body's eyes is irrelevant; what matters is the direction of the soul's eye -- Glaucon's praise of astronomy for 'looking up' is literally correct but philosophically naive -- Cross-ref: the four subjects in order: arithmetic, plane geometry, solid geometry, astronomy; the distinction between sensible and intelligible astronomy will be developed in 7.43-7.47

D: Original:
What do you mean? he asked. You, I replied, have in your mind a truly sublime conception of our knowledge of the things above. And I dare say that if a person were to throw his head back and study the fretted ceiling, you would still think that his mind was the percipient, and not his eyes. And you are very likely right, and I may be a simpleton: but, in my opinion, that knowledge only which is of being and of the unseen can make the soul look upwards, and whether a man gapes at the heavens or blinks on the ground, seeking to learn some particular of sense, I would deny that he can learn, for nothing of that sort is matter of science; his soul is looking downwards, not upwards, whether his way to knowledge is by water or by land, whether he floats, or only lies on his back.

I acknowledge, he said, the justice of your rebuke. Still, I should like to ascertain how astronomy can be learned in any manner more conducive to that knowledge of which we are speaking?
D: Narrative:
"What do you mean?" he asked.

"You," I replied, "have in your mind a truly sublime conception of our knowledge of the things above. And I dare say that if a person were to throw his head back and study the fretted ceiling, you would still think that his mind was the percipient, and not his eyes. And you are very likely right, and I may be a simpleton: but, in my opinion, that knowledge only which is of being and of the unseen can make the soul look upwards, and whether a man gapes at the heavens or blinks on the ground, seeking to learn some particular of sense, I would deny that he can learn, for nothing of that sort is matter of science; his soul is looking downwards, not upwards, whether his way to knowledge is by water or by land, whether he floats, or only lies on his back."

"I acknowledge," he said, "the justice of your rebuke. Still, I should like to ascertain how astronomy can be learned in any manner more conducive to that knowledge of which we are speaking?"
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean?" Glaucon asked.

"You have a very elevated idea of studying 'things above,'" Socrates replied. "I suspect that if someone threw their head back to study a decorated ceiling, you'd say their mind was doing the perceiving, not their eyes. You may well be right, and I may be a simpleton. But in my view, only knowledge of being and the unseen can truly make the soul look upward. Whether someone gapes at the sky or stares at the ground trying to learn some fact of sense experience, I would deny that they are learning at all -- because sense particulars are not the stuff of science. Their soul is looking downward, not upward, whether they study by sea or by land, floating or lying on their back."

"I accept the justice of your rebuke," Glaucon said. "But then how should astronomy be studied in a way that leads to the knowledge we're after?"
D: Elegant: Only knowledge of being and the unseen makes the soul look upward. Whether you gape at the sky or stare at the ground, if you're seeking sensory particulars, your soul is looking downward. Sense experience is not science.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that physical direction is irrelevant -- only knowledge of being and the unseen turns the soul upward. Whether gazing at the sky or the ground, seeking sense particulars means the soul looks downward. Glaucon accepts the rebuke and asks how astronomy should properly be studied.
D: Distilled: True intellectual ascent has nothing to do with physical direction -- staring at the sky seeking sense data is just as 'downward' as staring at the ground.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- The 'fretted ceiling' example is both humorous and pointed: physical upwardness does not equal intellectual upwardness -- 'Nothing of that sort is matter of science' (episteme) = sensory particulars cannot ground genuine knowledge -- 'Whether he floats or lies on his back' is likely a jab at observational astronomers -- This passage establishes the distinction between empirical and rational astronomy that will be developed in 7.44-7.47 -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 65a-67b (the senses as impediments to knowledge); the Divided Line's distinction between dianoia and eikasia

Although it's the most beautiful and perfect of all visible objects, it is necessarily far inferior to the true motions -- the ideal relationships of absolute speed and absolute slowness, moving in true number and perfect geometric figure. D: Summary: Socrates explains that the visible starry sky, though the most beautiful physical object, is far inferior to the true astronomical motions -- ideal relationships of speed, slowness, number, and figure that can only be apprehended by reason, not by the eyes.
D: Distilled: The visible heavens, however beautiful, are merely imperfect copies of the true mathematical motions that only reason can apprehend.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Absolute swiftness and absolute slowness' = the intelligible mathematical ratios governing celestial motion, the Forms behind observable astronomy -- 'True number and every true figure' = the mathematical structures underlying astronomical phenomena -- This passage establishes Plato's distinction between empirical and rational astronomy, which profoundly influenced later astronomical theory -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 37d-39e (the cosmos as a moving image of eternity); the Divided Line places sensible astronomical observations below the mathematical reasoning they should serve

D: Narrative:
"True," he replied.

"The spangled heavens should be used as a pattern and with a view to that higher knowledge; their beauty is like the beauty of figures or pictures excellently wrought by the hand of Daedalus, or some other great artist, which we may chance to behold; any geometrician who saw them would appreciate the exquisiteness of their workmanship, but he would never dream of thinking that in them he could find the true equal or the true double, or the truth of any other proportion."
D: Refashioned:
"True," Glaucon replied.

"The starry sky should be used as a model pointing toward higher knowledge," Socrates continued. "Its beauty is like that of diagrams or pictures superbly crafted by Daedalus or some other master artist. A geometer who saw such artwork would admire the exquisite workmanship, but would never imagine that these drawings could reveal the true equal, the true double, or the truth of any mathematical proportion."
D: Elegant: The visible heavens should serve as pointers toward higher mathematical knowledge, just as a geometer admires Daedalus's beautiful diagrams without mistaking them for the mathematical truths they illustrate.
D: Summary: Socrates compares the visible heavens to beautiful diagrams by Daedalus: a geometer would admire their craftsmanship but never mistake them for the mathematical truths they represent. Similarly, the starry sky should point toward higher mathematical knowledge, not be treated as the truth itself.
D: Distilled: The visible heavens are beautiful illustrations of mathematical truth, not the truth itself -- just as a perfect diagram is not the theorem it represents.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- Daedalus was the legendary master craftsman of Greek mythology, builder of the Labyrinth and creator of lifelike statues -- The analogy between astronomical observation and geometric diagrams is precise: both are sensible representations of intelligible truths -- 'The true equal or the true double' = mathematical Forms, perfectly precise relationships that physical objects can only approximate -- Cross-ref: Meno 97d-98a (Daedalus's statues that could move); this passage influenced the mathematical astronomy of Eudoxus and later Ptolemy

D: Narrative:
"No," he replied, "such an idea would be ridiculous."

"And will not a true astronomer have the same feeling when he looks at the movements of the stars? Will he not think that heaven and the things in heaven are framed by the Creator of them in the most perfect manner? But he will never imagine that the proportions of night and day, or of both to the month, or of the month to the year, or of the stars to these and to one another, and any other things that are material and visible can also be eternal and subject to no deviation -- that would be absurd; and it is equally absurd to take so much pains in investigating their exact truth."
D: Refashioned:
"No," said Glaucon, "that would be ridiculous."

"And won't a true astronomer feel the same way about the movements of the stars?" Socrates asked. "He'll recognize that the heavens are crafted by their creator as perfectly as possible. But he'll never imagine that the proportions of night to day, or day to month, or month to year, or the relations of stars to each other -- or anything material and visible -- can be truly eternal and perfectly invariable. D: Elegant: A true astronomer admires the Creator's work but knows that material, visible proportions -- night to day, month to year -- cannot be perfectly eternal or invariable. It is absurd to labor over their exact empirical measurement as though they were.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that a genuine astronomer will admire the heavens as divinely crafted but never mistake their visible proportions for eternal truths. Material ratios of night, day, months, and years cannot be perfectly invariable, and obsessive empirical measurement of them is misguided.
D: Distilled: The visible heavens are beautifully made but imperfect -- a true astronomer studies mathematical principles, not the precise measurements of material phenomena.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Framed by the Creator' (demiourgos) foreshadows the Timaeus where the Demiurge creates the cosmos as an image of the eternal -- The claim that visible astronomical ratios cannot be perfectly eternal anticipates the distinction between ideal mathematical models and observational data -- 'Equally absurd to take so much pains' -- a provocative claim that pure empirical astronomy is misguided -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 28a-29d (the cosmos as an image of the eternal model); this passage influenced debates about the relationship between mathematical models and observations throughout the history of science

D: Original:
I quite agree, though I never thought of this before. Then, I said, in astronomy, as in geometry, we should employ problems, and let the heavens alone if we would approach the subject in the right way and so make the natural gift of reason to be of any real use.

That, he said, is a work infinitely beyond our present astronomers.

Yes, I said; and there are many other things which must also have a similar extension given to them, if our legislation is to be of any value. But can you tell me of any other suitable study?

No, he said, not without thinking. Motion, I said, has many forms, and not one only; two of them are obvious enough even to wits no better than ours; and there are others, as I imagine, which may be left to wiser persons.

But where are the two? There is a second, I said, which is the counterpart of the one already named.

And what may that be? The second, I said, would seem relatively to the ears to be what the first is to the eyes; for I conceive that as the eyes are designed to look up at the stars, so are the ears to hear harmonious motions; and these are sister sciences --as the Pythagoreans say, and we, Glaucon, agree with them?

Yes, he replied. But this, I said, is a laborious study, and therefore we had better go and learn of them; and they will tell us whether there are any other applications of these sciences. At the same time, we must not lose sight of our own higher object.

What is that? There is a perfection which all knowledge ought to reach, and which our pupils ought also to attain, and not to fall short of, as I was saying that they did in astronomy. For in the science of harmony, as you probably know, the same thing happens. The teachers of harmony compare the sounds and consonances which are heard only, and their labour, like that of the astronomers, is in vain.
