"There is a second," I said, "which is the counterpart of the one already named."

"And what may that be?"

"The second," I said, "would seem relatively to the ears to be what the first is to the eyes; for I conceive that as the eyes are designed to look up at the stars, so are the ears to hear harmonious motions; and these are sister sciences -- as the Pythagoreans say, and we, Glaucon, agree with them?"

"Yes," he replied.

"But this," I said, "is a laborious study, and therefore we had better go and learn of them; and they will tell us whether there are any other applications of these sciences. At the same time, we must not lose sight of our own higher object."

"What is that?"

"There is a perfection which all knowledge ought to reach, and which our pupils ought also to attain, and not to fall short of, as I was saying that they did in astronomy. For in the science of harmony, as you probably know, the same thing happens. The teachers of harmony compare the sounds and consonances which are heard only, and their labour, like that of the astronomers, is in vain."
D: Refashioned:
"I completely agree, though I'd never considered this before."

"So in astronomy, as in geometry," Socrates said, "we should work with mathematical problems and leave the physical heavens alone -- that's the right approach and the only way to make genuine use of our rational faculty."

"That's infinitely beyond what our current astronomers do."

"True. And many other fields need the same kind of reform if our educational legislation is to have any value. Can you think of another suitable subject?"

"Not offhand."

"Consider this: motion has many forms, not just one. Two are obvious even to ordinary minds; others can be left to wiser people."

"What are the two?"

"The second is the counterpart of the first -- astronomy."

"What is it?"

"As astronomy relates to the eyes, this second science relates to the ears. As the eyes perceive celestial motion, the ears perceive harmonious motion. These are sister sciences -- as the Pythagoreans say, and as we agree."

"Yes."

"But harmonics is a laborious study, so we should consult the Pythagoreans about it and about any further applications. Meanwhile, we must keep our higher purpose in view."

"What purpose?"

"Every science should reach a certain perfection, and our students must attain it. In harmonics, the same error occurs as in astronomy. Music theorists compare sounds and consonances that they merely hear, and their labor -- like that of empirical astronomers -- is ultimately in vain."
D: Elegant: Astronomy should work with mathematical problems, not physical observation. Its sister science is harmonics -- what astronomy is to the eyes, harmonics is to the ears. But music theorists, like empirical astronomers, make the same mistake: they compare heard sounds rather than seeking the mathematical principles behind them.
D: Summary: Socrates prescribes mathematical problem-solving in astronomy rather than observation, then introduces harmonics as astronomy's sister science -- relating to ears as astronomy relates to eyes, per Pythagorean teaching. But harmonics suffers the same error as empirical astronomy: theorists study heard sounds rather than the mathematical principles of harmony.
D: Distilled: Astronomy and harmonics are sister sciences of motion -- one visual, one auditory -- but both must be pursued mathematically rather than empirically to reach genuine knowledge.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'As the Pythagoreans say' -- explicit acknowledgment of Pythagorean influence; Pythagoras discovered the mathematical ratios of musical intervals -- Astronomy = motion perceived by eyes; harmonics = motion perceived by ears; both are studies of mathematical proportion -- 'Let the heavens alone' is Plato's most radical statement about the method of astronomy: study mathematical problems, not observable phenomena -- The curriculum is now: arithmetic, plane geometry, solid geometry, astronomy, harmonics -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 35b-36d (the World Soul structured by musical ratios); the Pythagorean tradition of mathematical harmonics

D: Original: Yes, by heaven! he said; and 'tis as good as a play to hear them talking about their condensed notes, as they call them; they put their ears close alongside of the strings like persons catching a sound from their neighbour's wall --one set of them declaring that they distinguish an intermediate note and have found the least interval which should be the unit of measurement; the others insisting that the two sounds have passed into the same --either party setting their ears before their understanding.
D: Narrative: "Yes, by heaven!" he said; "and 'tis as good as a play to hear them talking about their condensed notes, as they call them; they put their ears close alongside of the strings like persons catching a sound from their neighbour's wall -- one set of them declaring that they distinguish an intermediate note and have found the least interval which should be the unit of measurement; the others insisting that the two sounds have passed into the same -- either party setting their ears before their understanding."
D: Refashioned: "Yes, by heaven!" Glaucon exclaimed. "And it's like watching a comedy to see them debating their 'condensed notes,' as they call them. They press their ears right up against the strings like people eavesdropping through a neighbor's wall -- one group claiming they can detect an intermediate tone and have found the smallest interval to use as a unit of measurement, the other insisting the two sounds are identical. Both sides put their ears ahead of their understanding."
D: Elegant: Glaucon describes empirical music theorists as comical: pressing ears to strings like eavesdroppers, quarreling over tiny intervals, always prioritizing what they hear over what they understand.
D: Summary: Glaucon vividly describes the absurdity of empirical music theorists who press their ears against strings arguing about microtones, some claiming to hear intermediate notes, others insisting two tones are the same -- all of them prioritizing hearing over understanding.
D: Distilled: Empirical music theorists are absurd, pressing their ears to strings and arguing over what they hear rather than seeking mathematical understanding.
Notes: Glaucon is speaking -- This passage satirizes the empirical school of harmonics (likely associated with Aristoxenus's predecessors), who sought to determine intervals by ear alone -- 'Condensed notes' (pyknomata) = microtonal intervals, a technical term in Greek music theory -- 'Setting their ears before their understanding' captures the fundamental Platonic critique: sense experience without rational analysis is not knowledge -- The contrast is between empirical harmonics (listening) and mathematical harmonics (reasoning about numerical ratios) -- Cross-ref: Philolaus and the Pythagorean mathematical approach to music vs. the empiricists

D: Original: You mean, I said, those gentlemen who tease and torture the strings and rack them on the pegs of the instrument: might carry on the metaphor and speak after their manner of the blows which the plectrum gives, and make accusations against the strings, both of backwardness and forwardness to sound; but this would be tedious, and therefore I will only say that these are not the men, and that I am referring to the Pythagoreans, of whom I was just now proposing to enquire about harmony. For they too are in error, like the astronomers; they investigate the numbers of the harmonies which are heard, but they never attain to problems-that is to say, they never reach the natural harmonies of number, or reflect why some numbers are harmonious and others not.
D: Narrative: "You mean," I said, "those gentlemen who tease and torture the strings and rack them on the pegs of the instrument: I might carry on the metaphor and speak after their manner of the blows which the plectrum gives, and make accusations against the strings, both of backwardness and forwardness to sound; but this would be tedious, and therefore I will only say that these are not the men, and that I am referring to the Pythagoreans, of whom I was just now proposing to enquire about harmony. For they too are in error, like the astronomers; they investigate the numbers of the harmonies which are heard, but they never attain to problems -- that is to say, they never reach the natural harmonies of number, or reflect why some numbers are harmonious and others not."
D: Refashioned:
"You're describing the experimentalists," Socrates said, "who torture strings on the pegs of their instruments. I could extend the metaphor -- talk about the blows of the plectrum, accuse the strings of being too eager or too reluctant to sound -- but that would be tedious.

"Those aren't the people I mean. I'm talking about the Pythagoreans, whom I proposed we consult about harmonics. They too are in error, like the empirical astronomers. They investigate the numerical ratios of the harmonies they hear, but they never reach the level of genuine problems -- they never discover the natural harmonies of number itself, or ask why some numbers are harmonious and others are not."
D: Elegant: Even the Pythagoreans, despite their mathematical approach to music, fall short. They investigate the numerical ratios of heard harmonies but never ask the deeper question: why are some numbers inherently harmonious and others not?
D: Summary: Socrates distinguishes between the string-torturing experimentalists and the mathematically-minded Pythagoreans. Even the Pythagoreans fall short: they study the numerical ratios of audible harmonies but never reach the deeper question of why some numbers are inherently harmonious and others are not.
D: Distilled: Even the Pythagoreans, who approach music mathematically, fail to ask the truly philosophical question: why are some numbers harmonious by nature?
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- The critique of the Pythagoreans is significant: even their mathematical approach to music doesn't go far enough because they remain tied to audible phenomena -- 'Natural harmonies of number' = the intelligible mathematical properties that make certain ratios consonant -- The distinction between empiricists (who torture strings) and Pythagoreans (who count ratios) and Plato's ideal (who seek the formal principles behind harmony) represents three levels of inquiry -- Cross-ref: the Divided Line's hierarchy; Pythagorean music theory discovered ratios like 2:1 (octave), 3:2 (fifth), 4:3 (fourth); Philebus 17c-e on the science of intervals

D: Original:
That, he said, is a thing of more than mortal knowledge.

A thing, I replied, which I would rather call useful; that is, if sought after with a view to the beautiful and good; but if pursued in any other spirit, useless. Very true, he said.

Now, when all these studies reach the point of inter-communion and connection with one another, and come to be considered in their mutual affinities, then, I think, but not till then, will the pursuit of them have a value for our objects; otherwise there is no profit in them.

I suspect so; but you are speaking, Socrates, of a vast work.

What do you mean? I said; the prelude or what? Do you not know that all this is but the prelude to the actual strain which we have to learn? For you surely would not regard the skilled mathematician as a dialectician?

Assuredly not, he said; I have hardly ever known a mathematician who was capable of reasoning.

But do you imagine that men who are unable to give and take a reason will have the knowledge which we require of them?
D: Narrative:
"That," he said, "is a thing of more than mortal knowledge."

"A thing," I replied, "which I would rather call useful; that is, if sought after with a view to the beautiful and good; but if pursued in any other spirit, useless."

"Very true," he said.

"Now, when all these studies reach the point of inter-communion and connection with one another, and come to be considered in their mutual affinities, then, I think, but not till then, will the pursuit of them have a value for our objects; otherwise there is no profit in them."

"I suspect so; but you are speaking, Socrates, of a vast work."

"What do you mean?" I said; "the prelude or what? Do you not know that all this is but the prelude to the actual strain which we have to learn? For you surely would not regard the skilled mathematician as a dialectician?"

"Assuredly not," he said; "I have hardly ever known a mathematician who was capable of reasoning."

"But do you imagine that men who are unable to give and take a reason will have the knowledge which we require of them?"
D: Refashioned:
"That sounds like more than human knowledge," said Glaucon.

"I'd call it useful rather than superhuman," Socrates replied, "-- useful if pursued with a view to the beautiful and the good. Pursued in any other spirit, it's useless."

"Very true."

"Now here's the crucial point: all these studies only become valuable for our purpose when they reach the stage of interconnection -- when their mutual relationships and affinities are understood. Until then, there's no real profit in them."

"I can see that, but you're describing an enormous undertaking, Socrates."

"What do you mean -- the vast work? This is merely the prelude! Don't you realize that everything we've discussed is just the overture to the main theme we still need to learn? Surely you wouldn't call a skilled mathematician a dialectician?"

"Certainly not," Glaucon said. "I've hardly ever met a mathematician capable of genuine philosophical reasoning."

"And can you imagine that people who can't give and receive rational arguments will possess the knowledge we require?"
D: Elegant: All the mathematical sciences only become valuable when their interconnections are understood. But even then, they are merely the prelude. The real subject is dialectic. Mathematicians are rarely capable of genuine philosophical reasoning.
D: Summary: Socrates declares that the mathematical sciences gain value only when their interconnections are understood -- but even then, they are merely a prelude. The real subject, dialectic, still awaits. Glaucon notes that mathematicians are rarely capable of genuine reasoning. Socrates insists that the ability to give and take rational arguments is essential.
D: Distilled: All mathematical sciences are merely the prelude to dialectic -- and even skilled mathematicians rarely possess the capacity for genuine philosophical reasoning.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Prelude to the actual strain' (prooimion vs. nomos) = the mathematical curriculum is only preparation for dialectic, the crown of education -- The musical metaphor (prelude/strain) is apt given they've just discussed harmonics -- 'I have hardly ever known a mathematician who was capable of reasoning' -- a striking claim that mathematical skill doesn't guarantee philosophical ability -- 'Give and take a reason' (logon didonai kai dechesthai) is Plato's definition of dialectical competence -- Cross-ref: Book VI (510b-511d) where mathematics is placed below dialectic because it relies on unexamined hypotheses; the Divided Line's distinction between dianoia and noesis

D: Original: Neither can this be supposed. And so, Glaucon, I said, we have at last arrived at the hymn of dialectic. This is that strain which is of the intellect only, but which the faculty of sight will nevertheless be found to imitate; for sight, as you may remember, was imagined by us after a while to behold the real animals and stars, and last of all the sun himself. And so with dialectic; when a person starts on the discovery of the absolute by the light of reason only, and without any assistance of sense, and perseveres until by pure intelligence he arrives at the perception of the absolute good, he at last finds himself at the end of the intellectual world, as in the case of sight at the end of the visible.
D: Narrative:
"Neither can this be supposed."

"And so, Glaucon," I said, "we have at last arrived at the hymn of dialectic. This is that strain which is of the intellect only, but which the faculty of sight will nevertheless be found to imitate; for sight, as you may remember, was imagined by us after a while to behold the real animals and stars, and last of all the sun himself. And so with dialectic; when a person starts on the discovery of the absolute by the light of reason only, and without any assistance of sense, and perseveres until by pure intelligence he arrives at the perception of the absolute good, he at last finds himself at the end of the intellectual world, as in the case of sight at the end of the visible."
D: Refashioned:
"No, it cannot."

"And so, Glaucon," said Socrates, "we've finally arrived at the main theme -- the hymn of dialectic. This is the discipline of pure intellect alone, though the faculty of sight provides an analogy. Remember how in our allegory, sight eventually progressed to beholding real animals, then stars, and finally the sun itself? Dialectic follows the same pattern: when someone sets out to discover the absolute through reason alone, without any help from the senses, and perseveres until pure intelligence reaches the perception of the absolute good -- that person arrives at the summit of the intelligible world, just as sight arrives at the summit of the visible world."
D: Elegant: Dialectic is the hymn of pure intellect. As sight in the allegory progressed from shadows to the sun, dialectic progresses through pure reason -- without sensory assistance -- until it arrives at the absolute good, the summit of the intelligible world.
D: Summary: Socrates announces the arrival at dialectic, the crown of the curriculum -- pure intellect operating without sensory assistance. As the freed prisoner's sight eventually reached the sun, dialectic progresses through reason alone until it perceives the absolute good, the summit of the intelligible world.
D: Distilled: Dialectic is the culmination of all education -- pure reason ascending without sensory aid to the perception of the absolute good.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'The hymn of dialectic' (nomos) = the main composition, following the mathematical 'prelude' (prooimion) -- Dialectic corresponds to noesis, the highest segment of the Divided Line (Book VI, 511b-e) -- 'Without any assistance of sense' = pure intellectual inquiry, no empirical data, no physical diagrams -- The parallel between sight's ascent (Cave allegory) and reason's ascent (dialectic) unifies the two great images of Book VII -- Cross-ref: Cave allegory (7.1-7.9); Divided Line (Book VI, 509d-511e); Symposium 210a-212a (Diotima's ascent to absolute Beauty follows the same pattern)

D: Original: Exactly, he said. Then this is the progress which you call dialectic? True. But the release of the prisoners from chains, and their translation from the shadows to the images and to the light, and the ascent from the underground den to the sun, while in his presence they are vainly trying to look on animals and plants and the light of the sun, but are able to perceive even with their weak eyes the images in the water (which are divine), and are the shadows of true existence (not shadows of images cast by a light of fire, which compared with the sun is only an image) --this power of elevating the highest principle in the soul to the contemplation of that which is best in existence, with which we may compare the raising of that faculty which is the very light of the body to the sight of that which is brightest in the material and visible world --this power is given, as I was saying, by all that study and pursuit of the arts which has been described.
D: Narrative:
"Exactly," he said.

"Then this is the progress which you call dialectic?"

"True."

"But the release of the prisoners from chains, and their translation from the shadows to the images and to the light, and the ascent from the underground den to the sun, while in his presence they are vainly trying to look on animals and plants and the light of the sun, but are able to perceive even with their weak eyes the images in the water (which are divine), and are the shadows of true existence (not shadows of images cast by a light of fire, which compared with the sun is only an image) -- this power of elevating the highest principle in the soul to the contemplation of that which is best in existence, with which we may compare the raising of that faculty which is the very light of the body to the sight of that which is brightest in the material and visible world -- this power is given, as I was saying, by all that study and pursuit of the arts which has been described."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly," said Glaucon. "So that progress is what you call dialectic."

"Yes. But consider the earlier stages of the allegory: the prisoners freed from chains, their movement from shadows to the actual objects and toward the light, the ascent from the underground cave to the sunlit world above. There, even with weak eyes, they can perceive reflections in water -- which are divine shadows of true reality, not shadows of artificial objects cast by firelight (which is itself only a copy of the sun).

"The power to elevate the soul's highest principle to contemplate the best in existence -- comparable to raising the body's faculty of sight to perceive the brightest thing in the visible world -- this power is what all the mathematical studies and arts we've described provide."
D: Elegant: The mathematical curriculum provides the power to elevate the soul's highest faculty to contemplate ultimate reality -- corresponding to the cave allegory's progression from chains and shadows through reflections and objects to the sun itself.
D: Summary: Socrates connects the Cave allegory to the educational curriculum. The entire mathematical program -- arithmetic through harmonics -- provides the power to elevate the soul's highest principle to contemplate the greatest reality, just as the freed prisoner's eyes gradually adjust from shadows to the sun.
D: Distilled: The mathematical sciences are the means by which the soul's highest faculty is gradually elevated from shadow to sunlight -- from becoming to the contemplation of ultimate being.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- This passage explicitly maps the mathematical curriculum onto the stages of the Cave allegory -- 'Images in the water which are divine' = mathematical objects, which are reflections of the Forms but more real than sensory appearances -- 'Shadows of images cast by firelight' = the lowest level of reality (sensory images of physical objects) -- The hierarchy: firelight shadows < physical objects < reflections in sunlight < the sun = shadows of images < images < mathematical objects < Forms < the Good -- Cross-ref: Cave allegory (7.1-7.9); Divided Line (Book VI, 509d-511e); the mathematical curriculum maps onto the dianoia section of the Line

D: Original: I agree in what you are saying, he replied, which may be hard to believe, yet, from another point of view, is harder still to deny. This, however, is not a theme to be treated of in passing only, but will have to be discussed again and again. And so, whether our conclusion be true or false, let us assume all this, and proceed at once from the prelude or preamble to the chief strain, and describe that in like manner. Say, then, what is the nature and what are the divisions of dialectic, and what are the paths which lead thither; for these paths will also lead to our final rest?
D: Narrative: "I agree in what you are saying," he replied, "which may be hard to believe, yet, from another point of view, is harder still to deny. This, however, is not a theme to be treated of in passing only, but will have to be discussed again and again. And so, whether our conclusion be true or false, let us assume all this, and proceed at once from the prelude or preamble to the chief strain, and describe that in like manner. Say, then, what is the nature and what are the divisions of dialectic, and what are the paths which lead thither; for these paths will also lead to our final rest?"
D: Refashioned: "I agree with what you're saying," Glaucon replied. "It's hard to believe, and yet even harder to deny. But this is not a subject for passing treatment -- it needs to be discussed again and again. So, whether our conclusions are correct or not, let's accept them for now and move from the prelude to the main theme. Tell me: what is the nature of dialectic? What are its divisions? And what are the paths that lead to it? Because those paths will also lead to our final destination -- our place of rest."
D: Elegant: Glaucon finds the argument hard to believe yet harder to deny. He asks Socrates to describe dialectic itself: its nature, divisions, and the paths leading to it -- for those paths lead to the soul's final rest.
D: Summary: Glaucon acknowledges the argument is difficult to accept yet impossible to refute. He asks Socrates to move from the mathematical prelude to the main subject: the nature, divisions, and pathways of dialectic -- which will lead to the soul's ultimate destination.
D: Distilled: The argument for dialectic as the crown of knowledge is hard to believe but harder to deny -- now Glaucon demands its full description.
Notes: Glaucon is speaking -- 'Hard to believe yet harder to deny' captures the characteristic response to Platonic metaphysics -- 'Our final rest' (anapaula) = the contemplation of the Good as the soul's ultimate fulfillment -- 'Discussed again and again' = Plato acknowledging that these matters require repeated investigation across multiple dialogues -- Cross-ref: the Phaedrus, Sophist, and Parmenides all continue investigating dialectic's nature; the Philebus explores the Good further

D: Original:
Dear Glaucon, I said, you will not be able to follow me here, though I would do my best, and you should behold not an image only but the absolute truth, according to my notion. Whether what I told you would or would not have been a reality I cannot venture to say; but you would have seen something like reality; of that I am confident.

Doubtless, he replied. But I must also remind you, that the power of dialectic alone can reveal this, and only to one who is a disciple of the previous sciences.

Of that assertion you may be as confident as of the last.
D: Narrative:
"Dear Glaucon," I said, "you will not be able to follow me here, though I would do my best, and you should behold not an image only but the absolute truth, according to my notion. Whether what I told you would or would not have been a reality I cannot venture to say; but you would have seen something like reality; of that I am confident."

"Doubtless," he replied.

"But I must also remind you, that the power of dialectic alone can reveal this, and only to one who is a disciple of the previous sciences."

"Of that assertion you may be as confident as of the last."
D: Refashioned:
"Dear Glaucon," said Socrates, "you won't be able to follow me all the way here, though I would do my best. Ideally, you would see not just an image but absolute truth itself -- or at least my understanding of it. Whether what I'd show you would be the actual reality, I can't say for certain. But you would see something resembling reality -- of that I'm confident."

"I'm sure of it," Glaucon replied.

"But I must remind you: only the power of dialectic can reveal this truth, and only to someone who has mastered the preliminary sciences."

"I'm as confident of that as of everything else you've said."
D: Elegant: Socrates warns that Glaucon cannot fully follow where dialectic leads -- to absolute truth, not mere images. Only dialectic can reveal this, and only to those who have mastered the mathematical sciences.
D: Summary: Socrates warns Glaucon he cannot fully follow the discussion of dialectic. Ideally one would see absolute truth, not images -- but only dialectic reveals this, and only to those who have completed the mathematical curriculum.
D: Distilled: Dialectic reveals absolute truth -- but only to those disciplined by the preparatory sciences; without them, the soul cannot follow.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'You will not be able to follow me' = Socrates acknowledges the limits of discursive communication for the highest truths -- This echoes the Seventh Letter (341c-d) where Plato says the highest knowledge 'cannot be put into words' -- 'Not an image only but the absolute truth' = the difference between dianoia (mathematical images/hypotheses) and noesis (direct intellectual apprehension) -- 'A disciple of the previous sciences' = the mathematical curriculum is a prerequisite, not optional -- Cross-ref: Seventh Letter 341c-d; Book VI (511b-e) on dialectic's method of ascending beyond hypotheses

D: Original: And assuredly no one will argue that there is any other method of comprehending by any regular process all true existence or of ascertaining what each thing is in its own nature; for the arts in general are concerned with the desires or opinions of men, or are cultivated with a view to production and construction, or for the preservation of such productions and constructions; and as to the mathematical sciences which, as we were saying, have some apprehension of true being --geometry and the like --they only dream about being, but never can they behold the waking reality so long as they leave the hypotheses which they use unexamined, and are unable to give an account of them. For when a man knows not his own first principle, and when the conclusion and intermediate steps are also constructed out of he knows not what, how can he imagine that such a fabric of convention can ever become science?
D: Narrative: "And assuredly no one will argue that there is any other method of comprehending by any regular process all true existence or of ascertaining what each thing is in its own nature; for the arts in general are concerned with the desires or opinions of men, or are cultivated with a view to production and construction, or for the preservation of such productions and constructions; and as to the mathematical sciences which, as we were saying, have some apprehension of true being -- geometry and the like -- they only dream about being, but never can they behold the waking reality so long as they leave the hypotheses which they use unexamined, and are unable to give an account of them. For when a man knows not his own first principle, and when the conclusion and intermediate steps are also constructed out of he knows not what, how can he imagine that such a fabric of convention can ever become science?"
D: Refashioned:
"And no one can seriously argue," Socrates continued, "that any other method systematically comprehends all true existence or determines what each thing is in its own nature. The arts generally deal with human desires, opinions, production, or the preservation of what's been produced. And the mathematical sciences -- geometry and the rest -- which we said do apprehend being to some degree? They merely dream about being. They can never behold waking reality as long as they leave their foundational hypotheses unexamined and cannot give an account of them.

"Think about it: when someone doesn't know his own first principle, and both his conclusion and every intermediate step are built on what he cannot justify -- how can such a structure of convention ever qualify as genuine science?"
D: Elegant: No method other than dialectic systematically grasps true existence. The arts deal with opinions and production. Mathematics dreams about being but cannot awaken because it leaves its hypotheses unexamined. Knowledge built on unjustified foundations is convention, not science.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that only dialectic can systematically comprehend all true existence. The practical arts deal with opinions and production. Even mathematics, which partially grasps being, merely dreams about it -- because it relies on unexamined hypotheses. Knowledge built on unjustified first principles cannot be genuine science.
D: Distilled: Mathematics dreams about being because it rests on unexamined hypotheses; only dialectic, which questions first principles, can achieve waking knowledge.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Dream about being' vs. 'waking reality' = the crucial distinction between dianoia and noesis on the Divided Line -- The critique of mathematics: it assumes its axioms (e.g., the existence of numbers, points, lines) without justifying them -- 'Unable to give an account' (logon didonai) = cannot provide a rational justification of first principles -- This is the core argument for dialectic's supremacy: it alone examines and justifies its own foundations, reaching 'the unhypothetical first principle' -- Cross-ref: Book VI (510b-511d) where this argument about mathematical hypotheses is first presented; the 'unhypothetical first principle' (arche anhypothetos) = the Form of the Good

D: Original: Impossible, he said. Then dialectic, and dialectic alone, goes directly to the first principle and is the only science which does away with hypotheses in order to make her ground secure; the eye of the soul, which is literally buried in an outlandish slough, is by her gentle aid lifted upwards; and she uses as handmaids and helpers in the work of conversion, the sciences which we have been discussing. Custom terms them sciences, but they ought to have some other name, implying greater clearness than opinion and less clearness than science: and this, in our previous sketch, was called understanding. But why should we dispute about names when we have realities of such importance to consider?
D: Narrative:
"Impossible," he said.

"Then dialectic, and dialectic alone, goes directly to the first principle and is the only science which does away with hypotheses in order to make her ground secure; the eye of the soul, which is literally buried in an outlandish slough, is by her gentle aid lifted upwards; and she uses as handmaids and helpers in the work of conversion, the sciences which we have been discussing. Custom terms them sciences, but they ought to have some other name, implying greater clearness than opinion and less clearness than science: and this, in our previous sketch, was called understanding. But why should we dispute about names when we have realities of such importance to consider?"
D: Refashioned:
"Impossible," Glaucon agreed.

"Then dialectic alone goes directly to the first principle," Socrates said. "It is the only discipline that eliminates hypotheses in order to establish itself on secure ground. The eye of the soul, which is quite literally buried in a barbarous swamp, is gently lifted upward by dialectic. And dialectic uses the mathematical sciences we've discussed as handmaids and helpers in this work of conversion.

"Custom calls them 'sciences,' but they deserve a different name -- something indicating more clarity than opinion but less than true science. In our earlier discussion, we called this level 'understanding.' But why argue about terminology when we have realities of such importance to consider?"
D: Elegant: Dialectic alone reaches the first principle by eliminating hypotheses. It lifts the soul's buried eye upward, using the mathematical sciences as handmaids. These sciences occupy a middle position: clearer than opinion, less clear than dialectical knowledge -- what was earlier called 'understanding.'
D: Summary: Socrates declares that dialectic alone reaches the unhypothetical first principle, eliminating all assumptions. It lifts the soul from its buried state, using mathematics as handmaids. The mathematical disciplines deserve a name between 'opinion' and 'science' -- the 'understanding' (dianoia) of the Divided Line.
D: Distilled: Dialectic alone reaches the first principle without hypotheses; the mathematical sciences serve as its handmaids, occupying a middle rank between opinion and true knowledge.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Does away with hypotheses' (tas hypotheseis anairousa) = dialectic's defining method: it examines and transcends the assumptions that mathematics takes for granted -- 'Eye of the soul buried in an outlandish slough' = a powerful image of the soul's degraded condition before philosophical education -- 'Handmaids' (proschronomene) = the mathematical sciences serve dialectic, not the reverse -- The four-part epistemological hierarchy: eikasia (imagination) < pistis (belief) < dianoia (understanding/mathematics) < noesis (dialectical knowledge/science) -- Cross-ref: Divided Line (Book VI, 509d-511e); the 'unhypothetical first principle' (511b)

D: Original:
Why indeed, he said, when any name will do which expresses the thought of the mind with clearness?

At any rate, we are satisfied, as before, to have four divisions; two for intellect and two for opinion, and to call the first division science, the second understanding, the third belief, and the fourth perception of shadows, opinion being concerned with becoming, and intellect with being; and so to make a proportion: --

And as intellect is to opinion, so is science to belief, and understanding to the perception of shadows. But let us defer the further correlation and subdivision of the subjects of opinion and of intellect, for it will be a long enquiry, many times longer than this has been.

D: Narrative:
"Why indeed," he said, "when any name will do which expresses the thought of the mind with clearness?"

"At any rate, we are satisfied, as before, to have four divisions; two for intellect and two for opinion, and to call the first division science, the second understanding, the third belief, and the fourth perception of shadows, opinion being concerned with becoming, and intellect with being; and so to make a proportion: --

As being is to becoming, so is pure intellect to opinion.

And as intellect is to opinion, so is science to belief, and understanding to the perception of shadows. But let us defer the further correlation and subdivision of the subjects of opinion and of intellect, for it will be a long enquiry, many times longer than this has been."

"As far as I understand," he said, "I agree."

"And do you also agree, I said, in describing the dialectician as one who attains a conception of the essence of each thing? And he who does not possess and is therefore unable to impart this conception, in whatever degree he fails, may in that degree also be said to fail in intelligence? Will you admit so much?"

"Yes," he said; "how can I deny it?"

"And you would say the same of the conception of the good?"
D: Refashioned:
"Why argue about names," Glaucon said, "when any name that clearly expresses the thought will do?"

"Fair enough. So we maintain our four divisions: two under intellect and two under opinion. The first is science (noesis), the second understanding (dianoia), the third belief (pistis), the fourth perception of shadows (eikasia). Opinion deals with becoming; intellect deals with being. And the proportion holds:

As being is to becoming, so is intellect to opinion.
As intellect is to opinion, so is science to belief, and understanding to the perception of shadows.

But the detailed correlation and subdivision of these domains would require an inquiry many times longer than this one, so let's defer it."

"As far as I understand, I agree."

"And would you agree," Socrates continued, "that a dialectician is someone who grasps the essence of each thing? And that anyone who cannot grasp -- and therefore cannot communicate -- this essence fails in intelligence to exactly that degree?"

"Yes, how could I deny it?"

"And the same applies to the conception of the Good?"
D: Elegant: The four-part epistemological scheme stands: science and understanding (intellect, concerned with being) above belief and shadow-perception (opinion, concerned with becoming). The dialectician is defined as one who grasps the essence of each thing -- and the supreme test is grasping the essence of the Good.
D: Summary: Socrates restates the four-part epistemology: science and understanding (dealing with being) above belief and shadow-perception (dealing with becoming). He defines the dialectician as one who grasps the essence of each thing and can communicate it. The ultimate test is grasping the essence of the Good itself.
D: Distilled: The dialectician grasps the essence of each thing through the fourfold hierarchy of knowledge -- and the supreme essence to grasp is the Good.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage explicitly restates the Divided Line from Book VI with its four segments: noesis/episteme, dianoia, pistis, eikasia -- The proportion being:becoming :: intellect:opinion :: science:belief :: understanding:shadow-perception formalizes the entire epistemological framework -- 'Attains a conception of the essence of each thing' = grasps the Form of each thing through dialectical reasoning (logos) -- Cross-ref: Divided Line (Book VI, 509d-511e); the definition of dialectic here anticipates its treatment in the Sophist (253d-e) as the science of combination and division of Forms

D: Original: Until the person is able to abstract and define rationally the idea of good, and unless he can run the gauntlet of all objections, and is ready to disprove them, not by appeals to opinion, but to absolute truth, never faltering at any step of the argument --unless he can do all this, you would say that he knows neither the idea of good nor any other good; he apprehends only a shadow, if anything at all, which is given by opinion and not by science; --dreaming and slumbering in this life, before he is well awake here, he arrives at the world below, and has his final quietus.
D: Narrative: "Until the person is able to abstract and define rationally the idea of good, and unless he can run the gauntlet of all objections, and is ready to disprove them, not by appeals to opinion, but to absolute truth, never faltering at any step of the argument -- unless he can do all this, you would say that he knows neither the idea of good nor any other good; he apprehends only a shadow, if anything at all, which is given by opinion and not by science; -- dreaming and slumbering in this life, before he is well awake here, he arrives at the world below, and has his final quietus."
D: Refashioned: "Until someone can separate out and rationally define the idea of the Good," Socrates said, "and can survive every objection thrown at him -- refuting them not by appealing to opinion but to absolute truth, never stumbling at any step of the argument -- until he can do all this, you'd say he knows neither the Good nor any other good. He grasps only a shadow, if anything at all -- a shadow given by opinion, not by knowledge. He dreams and slumbers through this life, and before he ever truly wakes up, he passes into the world below and finds his final rest."
D: Elegant: One who cannot rationally define the Good and withstand every objection through absolute truth -- not opinion -- knows nothing truly. He grasps only shadows, dreams through life, and arrives at death without ever having awakened.
D: Summary: Socrates declares that anyone who cannot rationally define the Good and defend that definition against all objections through absolute truth rather than opinion knows nothing of the Good or any good. Such a person lives in a dream, grasping only shadows, and dies without ever truly waking.
D: Distilled: Without the ability to rationally define and defend the Good against all objections through absolute truth, one sleepwalks through life grasping nothing but shadows.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Abstract and define rationally' (logoi) = the dialectical method of giving an account -- 'Run the gauntlet of all objections' = the elenctic method, Socratic cross-examination -- 'Dreaming and slumbering' revisits the cave allegory: most people live in a dream state, never perceiving reality -- 'His final quietus' = death; the unexamined life leads to death without awakening, echoing Apology 38a: 'the unexamined life is not worth living' -- Cross-ref: Apology 38a; Book VI (505a-509c) on the Form of the Good; Phaedo 64a (philosophy as preparation for death)

D: Original: In all that I should most certainly agree with you. And surely you would not have the children of your ideal State, whom you are nurturing and educating --if the ideal ever becomes a reality --you would not allow the future rulers to be like posts, having no reason in them, and yet to be set in authority over the highest matters?
D: Narrative:
"In all that I should most certainly agree with you."

"And surely you would not have the children of your ideal State, whom you are nurturing and educating -- if the ideal ever becomes a reality -- you would not allow the future rulers to be like posts, having no reason in them, and yet to be set in authority over the highest matters?"
D: Refashioned:
"I would most certainly agree with all of that," said Glaucon.

"Then surely," Socrates continued, "you wouldn't want the children of your ideal state -- the ones you're raising and educating, if this ideal ever becomes reality -- to be like wooden posts, devoid of reason, and yet placed in authority over the most important matters?"
D: Elegant: The future rulers of the ideal state must not be like wooden posts -- devoid of reason yet set in authority over the highest matters.
D: Summary: Socrates insists that the ideal state's future rulers must not be like senseless wooden posts placed in authority over the greatest matters. They must possess genuine reason and dialectical understanding.
D: Distilled: Rulers without reason are like wooden posts in positions of authority -- the ideal state cannot tolerate mindless governance.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Like posts' (hosper grammai) = boundary markers or wooden posts, having no intelligence -- The image is deliberately degrading: rulers without dialectical training are mere objects, not thinking beings -- This drives home the necessity of the entire educational program -- Cross-ref: the philosopher-king argument (Book V, 473c-e); the warning about unphilosophical rulers throughout Books V-VII

"Certainly," he said; "he must have natural gifts."

D: Refashioned:
"Certainly not," Glaucon agreed.

"Then we'll legislate that they receive an education enabling them to achieve the highest skill in asking and answering questions?"

"Yes -- you and I together will make that law."

"So dialectic is the capstone of the sciences, set above all others. No science can be placed higher -- knowledge can go no further?"

"I agree."

"But we still need to determine: who receives this education, and how is it structured?"

"Clearly."

"You remember how we selected rulers before?"

"I do."

"The same kinds of natures must be chosen -- the most steadfast and courageous, and if possible the most noble in appearance. They should have generous temperaments and the natural gifts that facilitate this education."

"What gifts?"

"Intellectual sharpness and quick learning ability. The mind gives out from the strain of study more readily than the body gives out from physical exercise -- because intellectual work falls entirely on the mind and can't be shared with the body."

"Very true."

"The person we're looking for must also have a strong memory and be tireless -- a solid person who loves hard work in every domain. Otherwise he'll never endure both the physical training and the intellectual discipline we require."

"He must certainly have natural gifts."

Philosophy's current disrepute comes from being practiced by pretenders rather than those with genuine vocation.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes dialectic as the capstone of all sciences and turns to selecting who should study it. Candidates need intellectual sharpness, strong memory, tireless dedication, and noble temperament. Philosophy's current bad reputation stems from being practiced by those without genuine philosophical vocation.
D: Distilled: Dialectic crowns all knowledge, but it must be studied only by those with genuine philosophical vocation -- pretenders have brought philosophy into disrepute.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Coping-stone' (thrigkos) = the topmost stone that completes a wall, a perfect metaphor for dialectic's position atop the curriculum -- 'Asking and answering questions' = the Socratic method is explicitly identified as the skill dialectic develops -- 'True sons vs. bastards' echoes the metaphor from 7.14 about wisdom's divine element -- The qualities required (keen, brave, fair, generous, good memory, tireless) describe the ideal philosophical nature from Book VI (485a-487a) -- Cross-ref: Book VI (485a-487a, 489b-497a) on the philosophical nature and its corruption; Book III (412b-414a) on selecting rulers

D: Original: What do you mean? In the first place, her votary should not have a lame or halting industry --I mean, that he should not be half industrious and half idle: as, for example, when a man is a lover of gymnastic and hunting, and all other bodily exercises, but a hater rather than a lover of the labour of learning or listening or enquiring. Or the occupation to which he devotes himself may be of an opposite kind, and he may have the other sort of lameness.
D: Narrative:
"What do you mean?"

"In the first place, her votary should not have a lame or halting industry -- I mean, that he should not be half industrious and half idle: as, for example, when a man is a lover of gymnastic and hunting, and all other bodily exercises, but a hater rather than a lover of the labour of learning or listening or enquiring. Or the occupation to which he devotes himself may be of an opposite kind, and he may have the other sort of lameness."
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean?" asked Glaucon.

"First," said Socrates, "the philosopher's dedication must not be lame or halting -- that is, he shouldn't be industrious in one area and lazy in another. For example, someone who loves athletics, hunting, and physical exercise but hates the work of learning, listening, and inquiry. Or the reverse -- someone devoted entirely to the mind but neglecting the body. Either kind of imbalance is a form of lameness."
D: Elegant: The philosopher must not be 'lame' -- devoted to physical pursuits but hating intellectual work, or vice versa. True philosophical dedication requires wholeness, not one-sided enthusiasm.
D: Summary: Socrates insists that a philosopher's dedication must not be lame or one-sided. Loving physical exercise while hating intellectual inquiry -- or the reverse -- represents an imbalance that disqualifies someone from genuine philosophical vocation.
D: Distilled: The true philosopher must be wholehearted in both body and mind -- partial dedication in either direction is a disqualifying lameness.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Lame' (cholos) is used metaphorically: one-sided development is a kind of disability -- This echoes the dual education in gymnastics and music from Books II-III -- The ideal philosopher-ruler must be complete: physically robust AND intellectually passionate -- Cross-ref: Books II-III (education in gymnastics and music); Book VI (485a-487a) on the complete philosophical nature

D: Original: Certainly, he said. And as to truth, I said, is not a soul equally to be deemed halt and lame which hates voluntary falsehood and is extremely indignant at herself and others when they tell lies, but is patient of involuntary falsehood, and does not mind wallowing like a swinish beast in the mire of ignorance, and has no shame at being detected?
D: Narrative:
"Certainly," he said.

"And as to truth," I said, "is not a soul equally to be deemed halt and lame which hates voluntary falsehood and is extremely indignant at herself and others when they tell lies, but is patient of involuntary falsehood, and does not mind wallowing like a swinish beast in the mire of ignorance, and has no shame at being detected?"
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly," said Glaucon.

"And regarding truth," Socrates continued, "isn't a soul equally lame if it hates deliberate lying and is furious at itself and others for telling lies, yet tolerates involuntary falsehood? Such a soul wallows like a pig in the mud of ignorance without any shame at being caught in error."
D: Elegant: A soul that hates deliberate lying but tolerates its own ignorance -- wallowing like a pig in the mud of untruth without shame -- is equally lame.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that a soul is equally lame if it hates deliberate falsehood but tolerates involuntary ignorance, wallowing without shame in the mire of unexamined error like a pig in mud.
D: Distilled: Hating deliberate lies while tolerating comfortable ignorance is a form of intellectual dishonesty just as crippling as outright falsehood.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- The distinction between voluntary and involuntary falsehood is significant: voluntary = deliberate deception; involuntary = ignorance one doesn't bother to correct -- 'Wallowing like a swinish beast' = one of Plato's most vivid images of intellectual laziness -- This connects to Socrates' famous claim that he is wiser than others because he knows what he doesn't know (Apology 21d) -- Cross-ref: Apology 21d (Socratic ignorance); Book II (382a-c) on the distinction between 'true falsehood' (genuine ignorance in the soul) and 'falsehood in words'

D: Original: To be sure. And, again, in respect of temperance, courage, magnificence, and every other virtue, should we not carefully distinguish between the true son and the bastard? for where there is no discernment of such qualities States and individuals unconsciously err and the State makes a ruler, and the individual a friend, of one who, being defective in some part of virtue, is in a figure lame or a bastard.
D: Narrative:
"To be sure."

"And, again, in respect of temperance, courage, magnificence, and every other virtue, should we not carefully distinguish between the true son and the bastard? for where there is no discernment of such qualities States and individuals unconsciously err and the State makes a ruler, and the individual a friend, of one who, being defective in some part of virtue, is in a figure lame or a bastard."
D: Refashioned:
"Absolutely."

"And the same applies to temperance, courage, magnificence, and every other virtue," Socrates continued. "We must carefully distinguish the genuine article from the counterfeit. When states and individuals fail to make this distinction, they make grave mistakes without realizing it -- the state appoints a ruler, and the individual chooses a friend, who is defective in some part of virtue. Such a person is, metaphorically speaking, lame or illegitimate."
D: Elegant: In temperance, courage, and every virtue, the genuine must be distinguished from the counterfeit. States that fail to make this distinction appoint defective rulers; individuals who fail choose defective friends.
D: Summary: Socrates extends the principle to all virtues: temperance, courage, magnificence, and the rest must be carefully distinguished from their counterfeits. States and individuals who fail to make this distinction unknowingly appoint defective rulers and choose defective friends.
D: Distilled: Failure to distinguish genuine virtue from counterfeit leads states to appoint defective rulers and individuals to choose defective friends.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'True son and bastard' continues the legitimacy metaphor from 7.60 -- The virtues listed (temperance/sophrosyne, courage/andreia, magnificence/megaloprepeia) are cardinal virtues from Book IV -- 'Lame or a bastard' = possessing incomplete or counterfeit virtue, which is worse than none because it deceives -- Cross-ref: Book IV (427e-434d) on the four cardinal virtues; Book VI (485a-487a) on the complete philosophical nature; the problem of corrupted philosophical natures (Book VI, 490e-495b)

D: Narrative:
"That is very true," he said.

D: Refashioned:
"That's very true," said Glaucon.

"All of this must be carefully considered," Socrates continued. "If the people we admit to this vast system of education and training are sound in body and mind, justice herself will have no complaint against us. We'll be the saviors of the constitution and the state. D: Elegant: If the right people are selected for this education -- sound in body and mind -- they will save the state. If the wrong people are selected, philosophy will be further disgraced.

D: Distilled: The success or failure of the entire educational program depends on selecting students who are genuinely sound in body and mind.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Justice herself will have nothing to say against us' = the educational program will satisfy the demands of justice established in Book IV -- 'Saviours of the constitution' = the philosopher-rulers as guardians of the ideal state -- 'Greater flood of ridicule on philosophy' = Plato's awareness that philosophy is already mocked (see Aristophanes' Clouds satirizing Socrates) -- Cross-ref: Book VI (487b-497a) on why philosophy has a bad reputation; Aristophanes' Clouds (423 BCE); the selection criteria echo Book III (412b-414a)

That would not be creditable. Certainly not, I said; and yet perhaps, in thus turning jest into earnest I am equally ridiculous.

In what respect? I had forgotten, I said, that we were not serious, and spoke with too much excitement. For when I saw philosophy so undeservedly trampled under foot of men I could not help feeling a sort of indignation at the authors of her disgrace: and my anger made me too vehement.
D: Narrative:
"That would not be creditable."

"Certainly not," I said; "and yet perhaps, in thus turning jest into earnest I am equally ridiculous."

"In what respect?"

"I had forgotten," I said, "that we were not serious, and spoke with too much excitement. For when I saw philosophy so undeservedly trampled under foot of men I could not help feeling a sort of indignation at the authors of her disgrace: and my anger made me too vehement."
D: Refashioned:
"That would be disgraceful."

"Indeed," said Socrates. "And yet perhaps I'm being equally ridiculous in turning this playful discussion into something so earnest."

"How so?"

"I'd forgotten that we weren't being entirely serious, and I spoke with too much passion. When I see philosophy so undeservedly trampled underfoot, I can't help feeling indignant at those who've brought her into disgrace. My anger made me more vehement than the occasion warranted."
D: Elegant: Socrates catches himself speaking too passionately in defense of philosophy. His indignation at seeing her trampled underfoot made him more vehement than their playful discussion required.
D: Summary: Socrates pauses to acknowledge he has been speaking with too much passion, carried away by indignation at seeing philosophy unjustly disgraced. He recognizes this earnestness may be inappropriate in what was meant as a more exploratory discussion.
D: Distilled: Socrates' passionate defense of philosophy reveals his deep personal commitment -- an anger at her unjust disgrace that momentarily overwhelms his characteristic irony.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- This is a rare moment of personal emotion from Socrates, breaking through his usual ironic composure -- 'Philosophy trampled under foot' personifies philosophy as a woman abused by unworthy practitioners -- 'We were not serious' is itself ironic -- the Republic is deeply serious, but Socrates maintains the fiction that it's a thought experiment -- Cross-ref: Book VI (497a-497c) where Socrates similarly restrains himself; the personification of philosophy echoes Book VI (495c-496a) where philosophy is compared to an orphan surrounded by unworthy relatives

D: Narrative:
"Indeed! I was listening, and did not think so."

"But I, who am the speaker, felt that I was. And now let me remind you that, although in our former selection we chose old men, we must not do so in this. Solon was under a delusion when he said that a man when he grows old may learn many things -- for he can no more learn much than he can run much; youth is the time for any extraordinary toil."
D: Refashioned:
"Really? I was listening and didn't think you were excessive at all."

"But I, as the speaker, felt it," said Socrates. "Now let me make an important point: although in our earlier selection of rulers we chose older men, we must not do so here. Solon was mistaken when he said that old age is a time for learning many things. An old man can no more learn intensively than he can run intensively. Youth is the time for extraordinary effort."
D: Elegant: Solon was wrong: old age is no time for intensive learning, just as it is no time for running. Youth is when extraordinary intellectual effort must begin.
D: Summary: Socrates corrects Solon's famous saying that one can learn in old age. Just as an old man cannot run intensively, he cannot learn intensively. The extraordinary toil of philosophical education must begin in youth.
D: Distilled: Contrary to Solon, youth -- not old age -- is the time for the extraordinary intellectual effort that philosophical education demands.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- Solon was the legendary Athenian lawgiver (c. 630-560 BCE); the quote is from his elegiac poetry (fr. 18 West): 'I grow old ever learning many things' -- Socrates' disagreement with Solon is notable given Solon's revered status -- The point about learning capacity echoes modern educational psychology -- 'In our former selection we chose old men' refers to Book III (412b-414a) -- Cross-ref: the educational timeline about to be outlined: childhood through age 50; Book III (412b-414a) on selecting rulers

D: Original:
Of course. And, therefore, calculation and geometry and all the other elements of instruction, which are a preparation for dialectic, should be presented to the mind in childhood; not, however, under any notion of forcing our system of education.

Why not? Because a freeman ought not to be a slave in the acquisition of knowledge of any kind. Bodily exercise, when compulsory, does no harm to the body; but knowledge which is acquired under compulsion obtains no hold on the mind.

Very true. Then, my good friend, I said, do not use compulsion, but let early education be a sort of amusement; you will then be better able to find out the natural bent.

That is a very rational notion, he said. Do you remember that the children, too, were to be taken to see the battle on horseback; and that if there were no danger they were to be brought close up and, like young hounds, have a taste of blood given them?

Yes, I remember. The same practice may be followed, I said, in all these things --labours, lessons, dangers --and he who is most at home in all of them ought to be enrolled in a select number.

At what age? At the age when the necessary gymnastics are over: the period whether of two or three years which passes in this sort of training is useless for any other purpose; for sleep and exercise are unpropitious to learning; and the trial of who is first in gymnastic exercises is one of the most important tests to which our youth are subjected.

Certainly, he replied. After that time those who are selected from the class of twenty years old will be promoted to higher honour, and the sciences which they learned without any order in their early education will now be brought together, and they will be able to see the natural relationship of them to one another and to true being.

Yes, he said, that is the only kind of knowledge which takes lasting root.

Yes, I said; and the capacity for such knowledge is the great criterion of dialectical talent: the comprehensive mind is always the dialectical.
D: Narrative:
"Of course."

"And, therefore, calculation and geometry and all the other elements of instruction, which are a preparation for dialectic, should be presented to the mind in childhood; not, however, under any notion of forcing our system of education."

"Why not?"

"Because a freeman ought not to be a slave in the acquisition of knowledge of any kind. Bodily exercise, when compulsory, does no harm to the body; but knowledge which is acquired under compulsion obtains no hold on the mind."

"Very true."

"Then, my good friend," I said, "do not use compulsion, but let early education be a sort of amusement; you will then be better able to find out the natural bent."

"That is a very rational notion," he said.

"Do you remember that the children, too, were to be taken to see the battle on horseback; and that if there were no danger they were to be brought close up and, like young hounds, have a taste of blood given them?"

"Yes, I remember."

"The same practice may be followed," I said, "in all these things -- labours, lessons, dangers -- and he who is most at home in all of them ought to be enrolled in a select number."

"At what age?"

"At the age when the necessary gymnastics are over: the period whether of two or three years which passes in this sort of training is useless for any other purpose; for sleep and exercise are unpropitious to learning; and the trial of who is first in gymnastic exercises is one of the most important tests to which our youth are subjected."

"Certainly," he replied.

"After that time those who are selected from the class of twenty years old will be promoted to higher honour, and the sciences which they learned without any order in their early education will now be brought together, and they will be able to see the natural relationship of them to one another and to true being."

"Yes," he said, "that is the only kind of knowledge which takes lasting root."

"Yes," I said; "and the capacity for such knowledge is the great criterion of dialectical talent: the comprehensive mind is always the dialectical."
D: Refashioned:
"Of course."

"Therefore," Socrates said, "arithmetic, geometry, and all the preparatory subjects should be introduced in childhood -- but never by force."

"Why not?"

"Because a free person should never be enslaved in the pursuit of knowledge. Compulsory physical exercise does no harm to the body, but knowledge forced on a mind takes no hold."

"Very true."

"So don't use compulsion. Let early education be a form of play. That way you'll better discover each child's natural aptitude."

"That's very sensible."

"Remember, the children were also to be taken to observe battles on horseback, and if it's safe, brought close -- like young hounds given a taste of blood?"

"Yes, I remember."

"The same principle applies across all domains -- work, study, danger. Those who excel in everything should be enrolled in a select group."

"At what age?"

"When their required gymnastics are complete. The two or three years spent on intensive physical training are useless for anything else -- heavy exercise and sleep are enemies of learning. But this period serves as one of the most important tests of character."

"Certainly."

"Then at twenty, the selected students are promoted. The subjects they learned unsystematically in childhood are now brought together so they can see how the sciences relate to each other and to true being."

"Yes -- that's the only kind of knowledge that truly takes root."

"Exactly. And the capacity for this synthetic understanding is the definitive test of dialectical talent. The comprehensive mind is always the dialectical mind."
D: Elegant: Early education should be play, not compulsion -- forced knowledge takes no hold. Children's aptitudes are observed through study, work, and danger. At twenty, the best are selected and shown how the sciences interconnect and relate to being. The capacity for this comprehensive vision is the mark of dialectical talent.
D: Summary: Socrates outlines the educational timeline: childhood learning through play (never compulsion), physical training as a character test, then at age twenty, the best students see how the sciences interconnect and relate to true being. The ability to comprehend this unity is the defining mark of dialectical talent.
D: Distilled: Education must begin as play in childhood, progress through physical and intellectual testing, and at twenty reveal the unity of all knowledge -- for the comprehensive mind is always the dialectical mind.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'A freeman ought not to be a slave in the acquisition of knowledge' = one of the most progressive educational principles in ancient philosophy -- 'Let early education be a sort of amusement' anticipates modern theories of play-based learning -- 'Like young hounds given a taste of blood' refers to Book V (466e-467a) -- The educational timeline: childhood (play-based learning) -> physical training (17-20) -> systematic study revealing connections (20+) -> dialectic (30+) -- 'The comprehensive mind is always the dialectical' = synoptikos = dialektikos, the ability to see connections is the essence of philosophical ability -- Cross-ref: Book V (466e-467a); the educational program as a whole; the synoptic capacity echoes Book VI (486a)

D: Original:
I agree with you, he said. These, I said, are the points which you must consider; and those who have most of this comprehension, and who are more steadfast in their learning, and in their military and other appointed duties, when they have arrived at the age of thirty have to be chosen by you out of the select class, and elevated to higher honour; and you will have to prove them by the help of dialectic, in order to learn which of them is able to give up the use of sight and the other senses, and in company with truth to attain absolute being: And here, my friend, great caution is required.

Why great caution? Do you not remark, I said, how great is the evil which dialectic has introduced?

What evil? he said. The students of the art are filled with lawlessness. Quite true, he said. Do you think that there is anything so very unnatural or inexcusable in their case? or will you make allowance for them?

"I agree with you," he said.

"Why great caution?"

"Do you not remark," I said, "how great is the evil which dialectic has introduced?"

"What evil?" he said.

"The students of the art are filled with lawlessness."

"Quite true," he said.

"Do you think that there is anything so very unnatural or inexcusable in their case? or will you make allowance for them?"
D: Refashioned:
"I agree," said Glaucon.

"So here is the plan," Socrates continued. "Those who show the greatest comprehension, who are most steadfast in learning and in their military and civic duties -- at age thirty, you select them from the elite class and elevate them further. Then you test them with dialectic to discover which can abandon reliance on the senses and, in the company of truth, attain absolute being.

"But here, my friend, great caution is required."

"Why great caution?"

"Haven't you noticed the enormous damage dialectic has caused?"

"What damage?"

"Its students become filled with lawlessness."

"That's very true."

"But do you find their behavior unnatural or inexcusable? Or would you make some allowance for them?"

D: Summary: Socrates prescribes that at age thirty, the best students begin dialectic -- the test of whether they can transcend the senses and attain absolute being. But he warns that dialectic is dangerous: its students tend to become lawless. He asks Glaucon whether this is inexcusable or understandable.
D: Distilled: Dialectic must begin at thirty for the most steadfast students, but it carries grave danger: premature exposure breeds lawlessness rather than wisdom.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- Age thirty as the starting point for dialectic reflects Socrates' concern about premature philosophical education -- 'Filled with lawlessness' (paranomia) = the most serious charge against dialectic: it can undermine moral convictions before establishing rational ones -- This warning likely reflects Plato's observation of what happened to Alcibiades and other brilliant but morally unmoored students of Socrates -- Cross-ref: the corruption of philosophical natures in Book VI (490e-495b); Alcibiades' historical trajectory as a cautionary tale; the educational timeline continues: 30 = dialectic begins

"In what way make allowance?"

"I want you," I said, "by way of parallel, to imagine a supposititious son who is brought up in great wealth; he is one of a great and numerous family, and has many flatterers. When he grows up to manhood, he learns that his alleged are not his real parents; but who the real are he is unable to discover. D: Refashioned:
"Make allowance how?"

"Let me offer a parallel," said Socrates. "Imagine a child raised in great wealth within a large, prominent family, surrounded by flatterers. When he reaches adulthood, he discovers that the people he thought were his parents are not his real parents -- but he can't find out who his real parents are. Can you predict how he'd behave toward his supposed parents and his flatterers -- first when he doesn't know the truth, and then after he discovers it? Or shall I tell you?"
D: Elegant: Socrates offers a parallel: imagine a wealthy child who discovers his supposed parents aren't real, but can't find his true parents. How would his behavior toward family and flatterers change?
D: Summary: Socrates constructs a parallel to explain how dialectic creates lawlessness. He asks Glaucon to imagine a wealthy child raised by supposed parents who discovers they aren't his real parents, without being able to find his true ones. The question: how would this change his behavior?
D: Distilled: The analogy of a child discovering his parents are false illuminates how dialectic destabilizes moral convictions before replacing them with truth.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- The 'supposititious son' (hypobolimatos) = a substituted or falsely attributed child -- This analogy explains the moral danger of dialectic: moral beliefs are like the supposed parents; dialectic reveals they aren't the 'real' foundations but doesn't immediately provide the true ones -- The 'flatterers' represent sensual pleasures and sophistic arguments that exploit the moral vacuum -- Cross-ref: this passage illuminates the corruption of Alcibiades and other talented but ultimately lawless associates of Socrates; Book VI (490e-495b) on corrupted philosophical natures

D: Elegant: While ignorant of the false relation, the child honors his supposed parents, obeys them, and resists the flatterers' influence.
D: Summary: Socrates predicts that while the child doesn't know his parents are false, he will honor and obey them, resist the flatterers, and remain loyal to his family in important matters.

Notes: Socrates is speaking -- In the analogy: supposed parents = conventional moral beliefs; flatterers = pleasures and sophistic arguments -- The child's obedience while ignorant represents the pre-philosophical person's adherence to inherited moral conventions -- This works because conventional morality, even if not grounded in true understanding, provides stability -- Cross-ref: Book II-III where conventional moral education through stories and music creates this initial moral orientation

D: Narrative:

"That is true."

"There are also opposite maxims and habits of pleasure which flatter and attract the soul, but do not influence those of us who have any sense of right, and they continue to obey and honour the maxims of their fathers."
D: Refashioned:
"That all sounds probable," said Glaucon. "But how does this apply to students of philosophy?"

"Like this," Socrates replied. "We all grew up with certain principles about justice and honor, taught to us in childhood. We were raised under their parental authority, obeying and honoring them."

"True."

"There are also opposite maxims -- habits of pleasure that flatter and attract the soul. But those of us with any sense of right resist their pull and continue to obey the principles our fathers taught us."
D: Elegant: We grow up obeying principles of justice and honor learned in childhood, as from parents. Pleasures flatter and attract, but those with moral sense resist them and honor their inherited principles.
D: Summary: Socrates applies the analogy: we all grow up with inherited principles of justice and honor that serve as parental authority. Opposing pleasures flatter the soul, but those with moral sense resist and continue honoring the principles of their upbringing.
D: Distilled: Inherited moral principles serve as parental authority over the soul, keeping it loyal against the flattery of pleasure -- until philosophical questioning arrives.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- Now the analogy is decoded: supposed parents = inherited moral principles about justice and honor; flatterers = pleasures and contrary maxims -- 'Under their parental authority' = moral conventions function like parental guidance: accepted without rational justification, but providing necessary structure -- 'Those who have any sense of right' continue to obey -- but dialectic will challenge even them -- Cross-ref: Books II-III on moral education through habituation; the challenge to inherited morality echoes the Euthyphro's questioning of conventional piety

D: Narrative:

"Now, when a man is in this state, and the questioning spirit asks what is fair or honourable, and he answers as the legislator has taught him, and then arguments many and diverse refute his words, until he is driven into believing that nothing is honourable any more than dishonourable, or just and good any more than the reverse, and so of all the notions which he most valued, do you think that he will still honour and obey them as before?"
D: Refashioned:
"True."

"Now," Socrates continued, "imagine this person encounters the questioning spirit of dialectic. He's asked 'What is fair?' or 'What is honorable?' He answers as the legislator taught him. But then argument after argument refutes his answers, until he's driven to believe that nothing is more honorable than dishonorable, nothing more just or good than its opposite -- and the same for all the values he once held most dear. D: Elegant: When dialectic questions someone's inherited moral beliefs and argument after argument refutes them, that person may conclude that nothing is more just than unjust, nothing more honorable than dishonorable. Will they continue to obey beliefs they can no longer defend?
D: Summary: Socrates describes how dialectic can destabilize moral convictions: when questioning refutes inherited beliefs about justice and honor, a person may conclude that nothing is inherently just or unjust, honorable or dishonorable. At that point, obedience to those principles collapses.
D: Distilled: Dialectic can destroy inherited moral beliefs without immediately replacing them, leaving a person who concludes that nothing is more just than unjust.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'The questioning spirit' = the elenctic method, Socratic cross-examination -- 'Nothing is honourable any more than dishonourable' = moral relativism or nihilism, the danger Socrates identifies in premature dialectic -- This describes the moral crisis that dialectic can provoke: conventional beliefs are shown to be unjustified, but the student lacks the philosophical maturity to find the rational foundations -- Cross-ref: the Thrasymachus challenge in Book I (338c-354c) represents exactly this moral nihilism; the Gorgias (481b-527e) on rhetoric's ability to make the worse appear the better cause

D: Refashioned:
"Impossible."

"And when he stops considering his inherited values natural and honorable, and can't discover the true foundation, what life will he pursue except one that flatters his desires?"

"None other."
