Young people treat argumentation as a game, like puppies tearing at everything, giving philosophy a bad name. Only maturity brings the moderation that distinguishes genuine dialectic from eristic contradiction. Philosophy should be studied exclusively for twice the years spent on physical training.

Only maturity brings the discipline to seek truth rather than score points. He prescribes exclusive philosophical study for twice the duration of physical training, with careful selection of students.
D: Distilled: Premature dialectic turns young minds into argumentative puppies who destroy their own beliefs, disgrace philosophy, and become lawless -- only maturity and careful selection prevent this.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Like puppy-dogs' (skutaria) is one of Plato's most memorable images: young minds that discover argumentation use it destructively, like puppies chewing everything -- The distinction between dialectician and eristic is crucial: dialectic seeks truth; eristic seeks victory -- 'Any chance aspirant or intruder' = philosophy should not be open to all comers -- a controversial but characteristic Platonic position -- 'Twice the number of years' = if physical training is 2-3 years (age 17-20), philosophical study would be 4-6 years -- Cross-ref: Euthydemus (eristic argumentation satirized); the Sophists as practitioners of eristic; Book VI (487b-497a) on philosophy's bad reputation caused by unworthy practitioners

D: Narrative:
"Would you say six or four years?" he asked.

"Say five years," I replied; "at the end of the time they must be sent down again into the den and compelled to hold any military or other office which young men are qualified to hold: in this way they will get their experience of life, and there will be an opportunity of trying whether, when they are drawn all manner of ways by temptation, they will stand firm or flinch."
D: Refashioned:
"Would you say six or four years?" Glaucon asked.

"Let's say five," Socrates replied. "At the end of that time, they must be sent back down into the cave and required to hold military or other offices appropriate for young people. This gives them real-world experience and tests whether they stand firm or flinch when pulled in every direction by temptation."
D: Elegant: Five years of dialectic, then back into the cave for practical experience: military and civic offices that test whether they remain steadfast under every kind of temptation.
D: Summary: Socrates prescribes five years of dialectical study, after which students must return to practical life -- holding military and civic offices. This period tests whether they stand firm or yield when tempted in every direction.
D: Distilled: After five years of dialectic, the philosophers must be tested in the cave of practical life to prove their character under real temptation.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- The educational timeline: ages 20-30 (mathematical sciences), 30-35 (five years of dialectic), 35+ (practical testing in civic/military roles) -- 'Sent down again into the den' = return to the cave, a crucial requirement: philosophers must prove themselves in the world they'll govern -- 'Stand firm or flinch' = the ultimate character test: can they maintain philosophical integrity under worldly pressures? -- Cross-ref: the Cave allegory's insistence that the freed prisoner must return (7.17-7.20); Book III (413c-414a) on testing rulers

D: Original: And how long is this stage of their lives to last? Fifteen years, I answered; and when they have reached fifty years of age, then let those who still survive and have distinguished themselves in every action of their lives and in every branch of knowledge come at last to their consummation; the time has now arrived at which they must raise the eye of the soul to the universal light which lightens all things, and behold the absolute good; for that is the, pattern according to which they are to order the State and the lives of individuals, and the remainder of their own lives also; making philosophy their chief pursuit, but, when their turn comes, toiling also at politics and ruling for the public good, not as though they were performing some heroic action, but simply as a matter of duty; and when they have brought up in each generation others like themselves and left them in their place to be governors of the State, then they will depart to the Islands of the Blest and dwell there; and the city will give them public memorials and sacrifices and honour them, if the Pythian oracle consent, as demi-gods, but if not, as in any case blessed and divine.
D: Narrative:
"And how long is this stage of their lives to last?"

"Fifteen years," I answered; "and when they have reached fifty years of age, then let those who still survive and have distinguished themselves in every action of their lives and in every branch of knowledge come at last to their consummation; the time has now arrived at which they must raise the eye of the soul to the universal light which lightens all things, and behold the absolute good; for that is the pattern according to which they are to order the State and the lives of individuals, and the remainder of their own lives also; making philosophy their chief pursuit, but, when their turn comes, toiling also at politics and ruling for the public good, not as though they were performing some heroic action, but simply as a matter of duty; and when they have brought up in each generation others like themselves and left them in their place to be governors of the State, then they will depart to the Islands of the Blest and dwell there; and the city will give them public memorials and sacrifices and honour them, if the Pythian oracle consent, as demi-gods, but if not, as in any case blessed and divine."
D: Refashioned:
"How long does this practical stage last?"

"Fifteen years," Socrates answered. "At fifty, those who have survived and distinguished themselves in every action and every branch of knowledge finally reach their culmination. Now the time has come to raise the eye of the soul to the universal light that illuminates everything -- to behold the absolute Good. That is the pattern by which they must order the state, the lives of individuals, and the remainder of their own lives.

"Philosophy becomes their primary pursuit, but when their turn comes, they also labor at politics and governance -- not as though performing something heroic, but simply as a duty. When they've raised the next generation of philosopher-rulers to take their place, they depart to the Islands of the Blessed. The city will honor them with public memorials and sacrifices -- as demigods, if the oracle at Delphi approves, or at least as blessed and divine beings."
D: Elegant: At fifty, after fifteen years of practical testing, the survivors behold the absolute Good and use it as the pattern for governing. Philosophy is their chief pursuit; governance is a duty, not a glory. When they've trained successors, they depart honored as demigods or blessed beings.
D: Summary: After fifteen years of practical experience, at age fifty, the surviving philosopher-rulers finally behold the absolute Good and use it as the model for governance. They make philosophy their primary pursuit while governing as duty, not heroism. Having trained successors, they retire to the Islands of the Blessed, honored by the city.
D: Distilled: At fifty, after a lifetime of preparation, the philosopher-ruler finally beholds the Good, governs by its pattern as a matter of duty, trains successors, and retires honored as a near-divine being.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- The complete educational timeline: childhood (play-based learning), 17-20 (gymnastics), 20-30 (mathematical sciences), 30-35 (dialectic), 35-50 (practical testing), 50+ (vision of the Good, governance, and eventually retirement) -- 'The pattern according to which they are to order the State' = the Form of the Good as the blueprint for governance, connecting to the demiurge who orders the cosmos by looking at the Forms in the Timaeus -- 'Islands of the Blest' = the paradise for heroes in Greek mythology (Hesiod, Works and Days 170-173) -- 'If the Pythian oracle consent' = the oracle at Delphi; even the ideal state defers to traditional religious authority -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 28a-29a (the demiurge looks at eternal patterns); Book VI (505a-509c) on the Form of the Good as the supreme principle

D: Original:
You are a sculptor, Socrates, and have made statues of our governors faultless in beauty.

Yes, I said, Glaucon, and of our governesses too; for you must not suppose that what I have been saying applies to men only and not to women as far as their natures can go.

There you are right, he said, since we have made them to share in all things like the men.
D: Narrative:
"You are a sculptor, Socrates, and have made statues of our governors faultless in beauty."

"Yes," I said, "Glaucon, and of our governesses too; for you must not suppose that what I have been saying applies to men only and not to women as far as their natures can go."

"There you are right," he said, "since we have made them to share in all things like the men."
D: Refashioned:
"You're a sculptor, Socrates -- you've carved statues of our rulers that are faultless in beauty."

"Yes," said Socrates, "and of our female rulers too. Don't assume that everything I've said applies only to men and not to women, insofar as their natures allow."

"You're right," said Glaucon, "since we've established that they share in all things equally with the men."
D: Elegant: Glaucon praises Socrates as a sculptor of ideal rulers. Socrates reminds him this applies to women equally -- as they established earlier, women share in all civic roles.
D: Summary: Glaucon compliments Socrates on sculpting beautiful ideal rulers. Socrates reminds him that the entire program applies to women as well as men, as they previously established that women share in all civic responsibilities.
D: Distilled: The ideal educational program and the role of philosopher-ruler apply equally to women and men, as previously established.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Governesses too' = Socrates explicitly includes women in the philosopher-ruler class, a radical position in 4th-century Athens -- 'As far as their natures can go' qualifies the equality slightly: women can do the same things but perhaps not at the same level in all areas -- This refers back to Book V (451c-457b) where Socrates argued that women should receive the same education and hold the same offices as men -- 'Sculptor' metaphor = Socrates has been crafting an ideal model (paradeigma) of governance -- Cross-ref: Book V (451c-457b, 466d) on women guardians; the 'sculptor' image echoes Book VI (484c-d) where the philosopher is compared to a painter using the divine pattern

D: Original: Well, I said, and you would agree (would you not?) that what has been said about the State and the government is not a mere dream, and although difficult not impossible, but only possible in the way which has been supposed; that is to say, when the true philosopher kings are born in a State, one or more of them, despising the honours of this present world which they deem mean and worthless, esteeming above all things right and the honour that springs from right, and regarding justice as the greatest and most necessary of all things, whose ministers they are, and whose principles will be exalted by them when they set in order their own city?
D: Narrative: "Well," I said, "and you would agree (would you not?) that what has been said about the State and the government is not a mere dream, and although difficult not impossible, but only possible in the way which has been supposed; that is to say, when the true philosopher kings are born in a State, one or more of them, despising the honours of this present world which they deem mean and worthless, esteeming above all things right and the honour that springs from right, and regarding justice as the greatest and most necessary of all things, whose ministers they are, and whose principles will be exalted by them when they set in order their own city?"
D: Refashioned: "Well," said Socrates, "you'd agree, wouldn't you, that what we've described about the state and its government is not merely a dream? Difficult, yes -- but not impossible. It becomes possible in one way only: when true philosopher-kings are born in a state, one or more of them, who despise the honors of the present world as mean and worthless, who esteem above all things what is right and the honor that comes from right action, who regard justice as the greatest and most necessary of all things. They are justice's servants, and they will exalt its principles as they set their city in order."
D: Elegant: The ideal state is not a dream but a difficult possibility -- realizable only when philosopher-kings arise who despise worldly honors, esteem justice above all else, and set their city in order as servants of right.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that the ideal state is not merely a dream. It is difficult but possible -- but only when true philosopher-kings arise who despise worldly honors, hold justice as the greatest and most necessary of all things, and dedicate themselves to ordering the city by its principles.
D: Distilled: The ideal state becomes possible when philosopher-kings arise who value justice above all worldly honors and govern as its servants.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- 'Not a mere dream' responds to the persistent charge that the ideal state is utopian fantasy -- 'True philosopher kings' = the culmination of the argument begun in Book V (473c-e): the conditions under which the ideal state is possible -- 'Despising the honours of this present world' echoes the freed prisoner who scorns the cave's shadow competitions (7.7) -- 'Ministers of justice' = rulers as servants of justice, not masters over subjects -- Cross-ref: Book V (473c-e) on the philosopher-king thesis; Book IV (427e-434d) on justice in the state; the entire Republic's argument reaches its practical conclusion here

D: Original: How will they proceed? They will begin by sending out into the country all the inhabitants of the city who are more than ten years old, and will take possession of their children, who will be unaffected by the habits of their parents; these they will train in their own habits and laws, I mean in the laws which we have given them: and in this way the State and constitution of which we were speaking will soonest and most easily attain happiness, and the nation which has such a constitution will gain most.
D: Narrative:
"How will they proceed?"

"They will begin by sending out into the country all the inhabitants of the city who are more than ten years old, and will take possession of their children, who will be unaffected by the habits of their parents; these they will train in their own habits and laws, I mean in the laws which we have given them: and in this way the State and constitution of which we were speaking will soonest and most easily attain happiness, and the nation which has such a constitution will gain most."
D: Refashioned:
"How will they proceed?"

"They'll begin," said Socrates, "by sending all inhabitants over ten years old out into the countryside and taking charge of their children -- children still unaffected by their parents' habits. These children they will educate according to the principles and laws we've outlined. This is how the state and constitution we've been describing will most quickly and easily achieve happiness, and the nation with such a constitution will prosper most."
D: Elegant: The philosopher-kings will start by separating children under ten from parental influence, educating them according to the new principles. This is the quickest path to realizing the ideal state.
D: Summary: Socrates describes how philosopher-kings would implement the ideal state: by separating all children under ten from their parents' influence and educating them according to the new laws and principles. This offers the quickest and most effective path to the state's happiness.
D: Distilled: The ideal state begins by educating a generation of children free from the corrupting habits of their parents.
Notes: Socrates is speaking -- This is the most radical practical proposal in the Republic: a clean break with existing society by removing children from parental influence -- 'More than ten years old' = already corrupted by existing habits; only children under ten are still malleable -- This echoes the communal child-rearing proposed in Book V (457c-461e) -- The proposal strikes modern readers as totalitarian, but reflects Plato's conviction that existing social institutions reproduce injustice across generations -- Cross-ref: Book V (457c-461e) on communal child-rearing; Book III (386a-392c) on the importance of controlling children's early exposure to stories

D: Original:
Yes, that will be the best way. And I think, Socrates, that you have very well described how, if ever, such a constitution might come into being.

Enough then of the perfect State, and of the man who bears its image --there is no difficulty in seeing how we shall describe him.

There is no difficulty, he replied; and I agree with you in thinking that nothing more need be said.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, that will be the best way. And I think, Socrates, that you have very well described how, if ever, such a constitution might come into being."

"Enough then of the perfect State, and of the man who bears its image -- there is no difficulty in seeing how we shall describe him."

"There is no difficulty," he replied; "and I agree with you in thinking that nothing more need be said."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that would be the best approach. And I think, Socrates, that you've described very well how such a constitution might actually come into being."

"Then we've said enough about the perfect state and the person who embodies its image. It won't be difficult to describe him."

"No difficulty at all," Glaucon replied. "And I agree that nothing more needs to be said."
D: Elegant: Glaucon agrees the ideal state has been well described. Socrates concludes: enough has been said about the perfect state and the person who embodies it.
D: Summary: Glaucon affirms that Socrates has successfully described how the ideal constitution might be realized. Socrates closes the discussion of the perfect state, noting that the character of the person who embodies it follows naturally and needs no further elaboration.
D: Distilled: Book VII concludes with agreement that the ideal state and its philosopher-rulers have been sufficiently described -- the discussion is complete.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the conclusion of Book VII and the completion of the positive political program of the Republic (Books II-VII) -- 'The man who bears its image' = the just person whose soul mirrors the just state, the central analogy of the Republic -- 'Nothing more need be said' transitions to Book VIII, which describes the degeneration of constitutions from the ideal through timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, to tyranny -- The Republic's structure: Books I-IV (justice defined), Book V (radical proposals), Books VI-VII (philosopher-kings and their education), Books VIII-IX (degenerate constitutions and souls), Book X (poetry and the afterlife) -- Cross-ref: Book II (368d-369a) on the city-soul analogy; Book VIII begins the downward trajectory from the ideal

D: Original:
And so, Glaucon, we have arrived at the conclusion that in the perfect State wives and children are to be in common; and that all education and the pursuits of war and peace are also to be common, and the best philosophers and the bravest warriors are to be their kings?

That, replied Glaucon, has been acknowledged. Yes, I said; and we have further acknowledged that the governors, when appointed themselves, will take their soldiers and place them in houses such as we were describing, which are common to all, and contain nothing private, or individual; and about their property, you remember what we agreed?

Yes, I remember that no one was to have any of the ordinary possessions of mankind; they were to be warrior athletes and guardians, receiving from the other citizens, in lieu of annual payment, only their maintenance, and they were to take care of themselves and of the whole State.

True, I said; and now that this division of our task is concluded, let us find the point at which we digressed, that we may return into the old path.
D: Narrative:
"And so, Glaucon, we have arrived at the conclusion that in the perfect State wives and children are to be in common; and that all education and the pursuits of war and peace are also to be common, and the best philosophers and the bravest warriors are to be their kings?"

"That," replied Glaucon, "has been acknowledged."

"Yes," I said; "and we have further acknowledged that the governors, when appointed themselves, will take their soldiers and place them in houses such as we were describing, which are common to all, and contain nothing private, or individual; and about their property, you remember what we agreed?"

"Yes, I remember that no one was to have any of the ordinary possessions of mankind; they were to be warrior athletes and guardians, receiving from the other citizens, in lieu of annual payment, only their maintenance, and they were to take care of themselves and of the whole State."

"True," I said; "and now that this division of our task is concluded, let us find the point at which we digressed, that we may return into the old path."
D: Refashioned:
"So, Glaucon, we've reached the conclusion that in the ideal state, families are shared, education is universal, and the wisest philosophers and bravest soldiers serve as rulers?"

"That's what we agreed," Glaucon replied.

"Yes, and we also established that the rulers would house their soldiers in communal dwellings with no private property. You recall what we said about their possessions?"

"I do -- no one was to own the usual things people accumulate. They were to be warrior-athletes and guardians, supported by the other citizens with nothing more than their upkeep, devoted to their own discipline and the welfare of the whole state."

"Exactly. Now that we've finished that part of our discussion, let's find the point where we went off track and pick up where we left off."
D: Elegant: Socrates recaps the ideal state: communal families, shared education, philosopher-kings, guardian-soldiers without private property, maintained by the citizens they protect. With those foundations established, he proposes returning to the thread of argument they had left behind.
D: Summary: Socrates summarizes the key features of the ideal state they have constructed -- communal living, philosopher-kings, propertyless guardians -- and proposes returning to a previously interrupted line of inquiry.
D: Distilled: Having established the ideal state's foundations, Socrates seeks to resume the interrupted discussion of inferior constitutions.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- Recap of the ideal state from Books III-V: communal families (Book V, 457c-d), philosopher-kings (Book V, 473c-d), guardian class without property (Book III, 416d-417b). -- This passage serves as a transitional bridge back to the classification of constitutions promised earlier but deferred by the long digression on justice, women guardians, and the philosopher-king.

D: Original: There is no difficulty in returning; you implied, then as now, that you had finished the description of the State: you said that such a State was good, and that the man was good who answered to it, although, as now appears, you had more excellent things to relate both of State and man. And you said further, that if this was the true form, then the others were false; and of the false forms, you said, as I remember, that there were four principal ones, and that their defects, and the defects of the individuals corresponding to them, were worth examining. When we had seen all the individuals, and finally agreed as to who was the best and who was the worst of them, we were to consider whether the best was not also the happiest, and the worst the most miserable. I asked you what were the four forms of government of which you spoke, and then Polemarchus and Adeimantus put in their word; and you began again, and have found your way to the point at which we have now arrived.
D: Narrative: "There is no difficulty in returning," Glaucon said. "You implied, then as now, that you had finished the description of the State: you said that such a State was good, and that the man was good who answered to it, although, as now appears, you had more excellent things to relate both of State and man. And you said further, that if this was the true form, then the others were false; and of the false forms, you said, as I remember, that there were four principal ones, and that their defects, and the defects of the individuals corresponding to them, were worth examining. When we had seen all the individuals, and finally agreed as to who was the best and who was the worst of them, we were to consider whether the best was not also the happiest, and the worst the most miserable. I asked you what were the four forms of government of which you spoke, and then Polemarchus and Adeimantus put in their word; and you began again, and have found your way to the point at which we have now arrived."
D: Refashioned: "It's easy enough to pick up the thread," Glaucon said. "You had claimed to be finished describing the state. You called it good, and said the person who embodied it was good too -- though it turns out you had much more to say about both. You also said that if this was the true form of government, all others were corruptions of it, and you identified four principal corrupt forms. You proposed examining the defects of each, along with the corresponding character types. Once we'd surveyed them all, we were to determine whether the best person is also the happiest and the worst the most miserable. I asked you to name the four forms, but then Polemarchus and Adeimantus interrupted, and the conversation took a long detour to where we are now."
D: Elegant: Glaucon recalls the interrupted plan: Socrates had identified one true form of government and four corrupt ones. The task was to examine each corrupt form alongside its corresponding character type, ultimately determining whether justice or injustice leads to greater happiness.
D: Summary: Glaucon recounts the original plan of the discussion: having established the ideal state, they were to examine four inferior forms of government and their corresponding personality types to determine whether justice correlates with happiness.
D: Distilled: Glaucon reminds Socrates of the promised investigation into four corrupt constitutions and whether justice produces happiness.
Notes: Speakers: Glaucon recounting the earlier discussion. -- References the interruption at Book V, 449b, where Polemarchus and Adeimantus diverted the conversation toward women, children, and philosopher-kings. -- The four-five state scheme: aristocracy (ideal), timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, tyranny. -- The happiness question connects to Book IX's final verdict on the tyrant's misery. -- The state-soul parallel is the central methodological device of the Republic.

D: Original:
Your recollection, I said, is most exact. Then, like a wrestler, he replied, you must put yourself again in the same position; and let me ask the same questions, and do you give me the same answer which you were about to give me then.

Yes, if I can, I will, I said. I shall particularly wish to hear what were the four constitutions of which you were speaking.
D: Narrative:
"Your recollection," I said, "is most exact."

"Then, like a wrestler," he replied, "you must put yourself again in the same position; and let me ask the same questions, and do you give me the same answer which you were about to give me then."

"Yes, if I can, I will," I said.

"I shall particularly wish to hear what were the four constitutions of which you were speaking."
D: Refashioned:
"Your memory is perfect," I said.

"Then like a wrestler returning to his stance," Glaucon replied, "get back into position. Let me ask the same questions, and you give me the answers you were about to give before."

"I'll do my best," I said.

"What I most want to hear about are the four constitutions you mentioned."
D: Elegant: Glaucon, comparing the discussion to a wrestling match, asks Socrates to resume his position and finally name the four inferior constitutions.
D: Summary: Glaucon uses a wrestling metaphor to urge Socrates back to the interrupted argument, pressing him to identify the four defective forms of government.
D: Distilled: Glaucon insists Socrates resume the interrupted analysis of the four inferior constitutions.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- The wrestling metaphor (palaistes) reflects the agonistic culture of Athenian intellectual life. -- Greek philosophical dialogue as competitive sport -- see also Protagoras 339e. -- The four constitutions to be named: timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, tyranny.

D: Original: That question, I said, is easily answered: the four governments of which I spoke, so far as they have distinct names, are, first, those of Crete and Sparta, which are generally applauded; what is termed oligarchy comes next; this is not equally approved, and is a form of government which teems with evils: thirdly, democracy, which naturally follows oligarchy, although very different: and lastly comes tyranny, great and famous, which differs from them all, and is the fourth and worst disorder of a State. I do not know, do you? of any other constitution which can be said to have a distinct character. There are lordships and principalities which are bought and sold, and some other intermediate forms of government. But these are nondescripts and may be found equally among Hellenes and among barbarians.
D: Narrative:
"That question," I said, "is easily answered: the four governments of which I spoke, so far as they have distinct names, are, first, those of Crete and Sparta, which are generally applauded; what is termed oligarchy comes next; this is not equally approved, and is a form of government which teems with evils: thirdly, democracy, which naturally follows oligarchy, although very different: and lastly comes tyranny, great and famous, which differs from them all, and is the fourth and worst disorder of a State."

"I do not know, do you? of any other constitution which can be said to have a distinct character. There are lordships and principalities which are bought and sold, and some other intermediate forms of government. But these are nondescripts and may be found equally among Hellenes and among barbarians."
D: Refashioned:
"That's easily answered," I said. "The four forms of government I had in mind, at least those with recognized names, are: first, the Cretan and Spartan model, which most people admire; second, oligarchy, which is far less respected and riddled with problems; third, democracy, which naturally emerges from oligarchy despite being very different from it; and fourth and worst, tyranny -- the ultimate disease of the state."

"I can't think of any other constitution with a truly distinct identity. There are hereditary lordships and governments that are essentially bought and sold, along with various hybrid forms. But these are hard to classify and can be found among Greeks and non-Greeks alike."
D: Elegant: Socrates names the four degenerating constitutions: timocracy (the Spartan model), oligarchy, democracy, and tyranny -- each worse than the last. Other governmental forms exist but lack distinct character.
D: Summary: Socrates identifies the four inferior constitutions in descending order: the Spartan-Cretan system (timocracy), oligarchy, democracy, and tyranny, dismissing other governmental forms as hybrid and indistinct.
D: Distilled: The four degenerate constitutions are timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, and tyranny, each representing a progressive decline from the ideal.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- Crete and Sparta: admired by many Greeks for military discipline and stability; see also Plato's Laws, Books I-III. -- The descending sequence of degeneration is central to Book VIII's architecture. -- 'Lordships and principalities' likely refers to Persian-style monarchies and purchased tyrannies. -- Hellenes and barbarians: the Greek/non-Greek distinction. -- Aristotle later expands this taxonomy in Politics Books III-VI.

D: Original:
Yes, he replied, we certainly hear of many curious forms of government which exist among them.

Do you know, I said, that governments vary as the dispositions of men vary, and that there must be as many of the one as there are of the other? For we cannot suppose that States are made of 'oak and rock,' and not out of the human natures which are in them, and which in a figure turn the scale and draw other things after them?

Yes, he said, the States are as the men are; they grow out of human characters.

Then if the constitutions of States are five, the dispositions of individual minds will also be five?

Certainly. Him who answers to aristocracy, and whom we rightly call just and good, we have already described.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he replied, "we certainly hear of many curious forms of government which exist among them."

"Do you know," I said, "that governments vary as the dispositions of men vary, and that there must be as many of the one as there are of the other? For we cannot suppose that States are made of 'oak and rock,' and not out of the human natures which are in them, and which in a figure turn the scale and draw other things after them?"

"Yes," he said, "the States are as the men are; they grow out of human characters."

"Then if the constitutions of States are five, the dispositions of individual minds will also be five?"

"Certainly."

"Him who answers to aristocracy, and whom we rightly call just and good, we have already described."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," Glaucon replied, "we hear about all sorts of unusual governments among them."

"Do you see," I said, "that forms of government vary exactly as human characters vary? There must be as many types of one as of the other. States aren't made of oak and rock -- they're made of the people in them, whose natures tip the balance and pull everything else along."

"The states mirror the men," he agreed. "They grow out of human character."

"So if there are five types of constitution, there must also be five types of individual soul?"

"Certainly."

"And the person who corresponds to aristocracy -- the one we rightly call just and good -- we've already described."
D: Elegant: States are not made of wood and stone but of human character. Five constitutions correspond to five types of soul. The aristocratic person -- just and good -- has already been described; the four inferior types remain.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes that constitutions mirror the characters of their citizens, so five forms of government correspond to five types of individual soul. The just, aristocratic type has been described; four inferior types await examination.
D: Distilled: Governments grow from human character: five constitutions correspond to five soul-types, of which only the aristocratic has been described.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- 'Oak and rock': an allusion to Homer, Odyssey 19.163, a proverbial expression meaning 'from nowhere' or 'from inanimate origins.' -- The state-soul analogy is the Republic's central structural device, established in Book II (368c-369a). -- Five constitutions: aristocracy, timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, tyranny. -- The aristocratic/just person was described in Book IV (441d-442d).

D: Original: We have. Then let us now proceed to describe the inferior sort of natures, being the contentious and ambitious, who answer to the Spartan polity; also the oligarchical, democratical, and tyrannical. Let us place the most just by the side of the most unjust, and when we see them we shall be able to compare the relative happiness or unhappiness of him who leads a life of pure justice or pure injustice. The enquiry will then be completed. And we shall know whether we ought to pursue injustice, as Thrasymachus advises, or in accordance with the conclusions of the argument to prefer justice.
D: Narrative:
"We have."

"Then let us now proceed to describe the inferior sort of natures, being the contentious and ambitious, who answer to the Spartan polity; also the oligarchical, democratical, and tyrannical. Let us place the most just by the side of the most unjust, and when we see them we shall be able to compare the relative happiness or unhappiness of him who leads a life of pure justice or pure injustice. The enquiry will then be completed. And we shall know whether we ought to pursue injustice, as Thrasymachus advises, or in accordance with the conclusions of the argument to prefer justice."
D: Refashioned:
"We have."

"Then let's move on to the inferior character types: the competitive and ambitious person who corresponds to the Spartan system, then the oligarchic, democratic, and tyrannical types. By placing the most just person beside the most unjust, we can compare their relative happiness and misery. That will complete our investigation. And we'll finally know whether we should pursue injustice, as Thrasymachus urged, or follow the argument and choose justice."
D: Elegant: The plan: describe the four inferior character types -- timocratic, oligarchic, democratic, tyrannical -- and compare the most just with the most unjust to settle whether justice or injustice leads to happiness.
D: Summary: Socrates outlines the plan to examine four inferior character types alongside their constitutions, ultimately comparing the most just person with the most unjust to determine which life is happier -- resolving the challenge Thrasymachus posed in Book I.
D: Distilled: The investigation will compare just and unjust lives to determine whether justice or injustice produces happiness.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- Thrasymachus' challenge from Book I (338c-339a): justice is the advantage of the stronger, and injustice is more profitable. -- This frames the entire Republic: the answer comes in Book IX (580a-588a). -- 'Spartan polity': timocracy, the honor-loving constitution. -- The comparison of most just (philosopher-king) vs. most unjust (tyrant) is the Republic's dramatic climax.

D: Original:
Certainly, he replied, we must do as you say. Shall we follow our old plan, which we adopted with a view to clearness, of taking the State first and then proceeding to the individual, and begin with the government of honour? --I know of no name for such a government other than timocracy, or perhaps timarchy. We will compare with this the like character in the individual; and, after that, consider oligarchical man; and then again we will turn our attention to democracy and the democratical man; and lastly, we will go and view the city of tyranny, and once more take a look into the tyrant's soul, and try to arrive at a satisfactory decision.

That way of viewing and judging of the matter will be very suitable.

First, then, I said, let us enquire how timocracy (the government of honour) arises out of aristocracy (the government of the best). Clearly, all political changes originate in divisions of the actual governing power; a government which is united, however small, cannot be moved.
D: Narrative:
"Certainly," he replied, "we must do as you say."

"Shall we follow our old plan, which we adopted with a view to clearness, of taking the State first and then proceeding to the individual, and begin with the government of honour? -- I know of no name for such a government other than timocracy, or perhaps timarchy. We will compare with this the like character in the individual; and, after that, consider oligarchical man; and then again we will turn our attention to democracy and the democratical man; and lastly, we will go and view the city of tyranny, and once more take a look into the tyrant's soul, and try to arrive at a satisfactory decision."

"That way of viewing and judging of the matter will be very suitable."

"First, then," I said, "let us enquire how timocracy (the government of honour) arises out of aristocracy (the government of the best). Clearly, all political changes originate in divisions of the actual governing power; a government which is united, however small, cannot be moved."
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly," he replied, "we should do as you say."

"Shall we follow our established method -- examining the state first, then the corresponding individual? We'll begin with the government based on honor. I don't know any name for it other than timocracy, or perhaps timarchy. We'll compare that constitution with its matching character type, then move on to the oligarchic person, then the democratic person, and finally we'll examine the city under tyranny and peer into the tyrant's soul, aiming for a definitive conclusion."

"That approach sounds perfectly suited to the task."

"First, then," I said, "let's ask how timocracy -- the government of honor -- arises from aristocracy -- the government of the best. One principle is clear: all political revolutions originate in divisions within the ruling class itself. A government that remains united, however small, cannot be overthrown."
D: Elegant: Following the method of examining each state before its corresponding individual, Socrates begins with timocracy. His foundational principle: all political change originates in divisions within the ruling class. A united government, however small, is unassailable.
D: Summary: Socrates proposes examining each constitution and its corresponding soul type in order, starting with timocracy. He establishes the key principle that political revolution always begins with division within the ruling class -- a united government cannot be overthrown.
D: Distilled: Political decay begins when the ruling class divides against itself; a united government, however small, cannot be moved.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- Timocracy (from time, 'honor'): the government of honor, modeled on Sparta and Crete. -- The method of state-before-individual was established in Book II (368d-369a). -- The principle that revolution requires internal division anticipates Aristotle's analysis of stasis in Politics Book V. -- This passage initiates the descending sequence: aristocracy -> timocracy -> oligarchy -> democracy -> tyranny.

D: Original: Very true, he said. In what way, then, will our city be moved, and in what manner the two classes of auxiliaries and rulers disagree among themselves or with one another? Shall we, after the manner of Homer, pray the Muses to tell us 'how discord first arose'? Shall we imagine them in solemn mockery, to play and jest with us as if we were children, and to address us in a lofty tragic vein, making believe to be in earnest?
D: Narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"In what way, then, will our city be moved, and in what manner the two classes of auxiliaries and rulers disagree among themselves or with one another? Shall we, after the manner of Homer, pray the Muses to tell us 'how discord first arose'? Shall we imagine them in solemn mockery, to play and jest with us as if we were children, and to address us in a lofty tragic vein, making believe to be in earnest?"
D: Refashioned:
"Very true," he said.

"So how will our city be destabilized? How will the auxiliaries and rulers come into conflict with each other? Should we, like Homer, invoke the Muses to tell us 'how discord first arose'? We could imagine them speaking to us in mock-solemn tones, playing with us as though we were children, adopting a grand tragic style while only pretending to be serious."
D: Elegant: How does the ideal state begin to fracture? Socrates playfully invokes the Muses in Homeric fashion, asking them to explain in mock-tragic tones how discord first entered the perfect city.
D: Summary: Socrates asks how the ideal state could be destabilized and its ruling classes brought into conflict, humorously invoking the Muses in Homer's style to explain the origin of discord.
D: Distilled: Socrates playfully invokes the Muses to explain how discord first arises in the ideal state.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- 'How discord first arose': echoes Homer, Iliad 1.6 (the wrath of Achilles) and the convention of invoking the Muse. -- The 'solemn mockery' signals that what follows (the Allegory of the Allegory and the Allegory of the Allegory) is partly playful, partly serious -- a characteristic Platonic move. -- Auxiliaries and rulers: the two upper classes of the ideal state from Books III-IV.

D: Original: How would they address us? After this manner: --A city which is thus constituted can hardly be shaken; but, seeing that everything which has a beginning has also an end, even a constitution such as yours will not last for ever, but will in time be dissolved. And this is the dissolution: --In plants that grow in the earth, as well as in animals that move on the earth's surface, fertility and sterility of soul and body occur when the circumferences of the circles of each are completed, which in short-lived existences pass over a short space, and in long-lived ones over a long space.
D: Narrative:
"How would they address us?"

"After this manner: -- 'A city which is thus constituted can hardly be shaken; but, seeing that everything which has a beginning has also an end, even a constitution such as yours will not last for ever, but will in time be dissolved. And this is the dissolution: -- In plants that grow in the earth, as well as in animals that move on the earth's surface, fertility and sterility of soul and body occur when the circumferences of the circles of each are completed, which in short-lived existences pass over a short space, and in long-lived ones over a long space.'"
D: Refashioned:
"How would they address us?"

"Something like this: 'A city built on these principles is nearly unshakeable. But everything that has a beginning also has an end, and even your constitution will eventually be dissolved. Here is how the dissolution works: in both plants and animals, cycles of fertility and barrenness in body and soul follow their own rhythms -- short cycles for short-lived beings, long cycles for long-lived ones.'"
D: Elegant: The Muses declare: even the ideal state must eventually fall, because everything with a beginning has an end. All living things follow cycles of fertility and decline -- and so do constitutions.
D: Summary: Speaking as the Muses, Socrates declares that even the most perfect state must eventually dissolve, since everything with a beginning has an end. Natural cycles of fertility and decline govern all living things, including political orders.
D: Distilled: Everything that begins must end; even the ideal state is subject to natural cycles of decline.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates speaking as the Muses. -- The principle that everything generated must perish echoes Heraclitean flux and anticipates the Timaeus cosmology. -- The 'circles' refer to cosmic cycles governing generation -- a Pythagorean and pre-Socratic concept. -- This passage introduces the famous 'Allegory of the allegory' or Speech of the Muses, which leads into the notoriously obscure Allegory Number (8.10).

D: Original: But to the knowledge of human fecundity and sterility all the wisdom and education of your rulers will not attain; the laws which regulate them will not be discovered by an intelligence which is alloyed with sense, but will escape them, and they will bring children into the world when they ought not. Now that which is of divine birth has a period which is contained in a perfect number, but the period of human birth is comprehended in a number in which first increments by involution and evolution (or squared and cubed) obtaining three intervals and four terms of like and unlike, waxing and waning numbers, make all the terms commensurable and agreeable to one another. The base of these (3) with a third added (4) when combined with five (20) and raised to the third power furnishes two harmonies; the first a square which is a hundred times as great (400 = 4 X 100), and the other a figure having one side equal to the former, but oblong, consisting of a hundred numbers squared upon rational diameters of a square (i. e. omitting fractions), the side of which is five (7 X 7 = 49 X 100 = 4900), each of them being less by one (than the perfect square which includes the fractions, sc. 50) or less by two perfect squares of irrational diameters (of a square the side of which is five = 50 + 50 = 100); and a hundred cubes of three (27 X 100 = 2700 + 4900 + 400 = 8000).
D: Narrative:
"But to the knowledge of human fecundity and sterility all the wisdom and education of your rulers will not attain; the laws which regulate them will not be discovered by an intelligence which is alloyed with sense, but will escape them, and they will bring children into the world when they ought not.

Now that which is of divine birth has a period which is contained in a perfect number, but the period of human birth is comprehended in a number in which first increments by involution and evolution (or squared and cubed) obtaining three intervals and four terms of like and unlike, waxing and waning numbers, make all the terms commensurable and agreeable to one another.

The base of these (3) with a third added (4) when combined with five (20) and raised to the third power furnishes two harmonies; the first a square which is a hundred times as great (400 = 4 X 100), and the other a figure having one side equal to the former, but oblong, consisting of a hundred numbers squared upon rational diameters of a square (i.e. omitting fractions), the side of which is five (7 X 7 = 49 X 100 = 4900), each of them being less by one (than the perfect square which includes the fractions, sc. 50) or less by two perfect squares of irrational diameters (of a square the side of which is five = 50 + 50 = 100); and a hundred cubes of three (27 X 100 = 2700 + 4900 + 400 = 8000)."
D: Refashioned:
"But your rulers, for all their wisdom and education, will never fully grasp the laws governing human fertility and sterility. Intelligence mixed with mere sense perception will miss these patterns, and they will produce children at the wrong times.

Divine births follow a cycle contained in a perfect number, but human births are governed by a more complex number involving squares and cubes, three intervals and four terms, ratios of similarity and difference, growth and decline -- all made commensurable with one another.

The base number (3), increased by a third (giving 4), combined with five (giving 20), and raised to the third power, produces two harmonies: one a square one hundred times as great (400), the other an oblong figure with one side equal to the first, consisting of one hundred numbers squared on the rational diameters of a square whose side is five (49 times 100 equals 4,900) -- each being less by one than the perfect square including fractions, or less by 100 when using irrational diameters -- plus one hundred cubes of three (2,700). Together: 4,900 plus 2,700 plus 400 equals 8,000."
D: Elegant: Even the wisest rulers cannot master the mathematics of human fertility. They will inevitably breed at the wrong times. The 'nuptial number' -- a complex calculation involving squares, cubes, and harmonic ratios totaling 8,000 -- governs the cycles of generational quality. When rulers fail to align births with this cycle, decline begins.
D: Summary: The Muses explain that even the best rulers will fail to discover the mathematical laws governing optimal human reproduction. The infamous 'nuptial number' -- an elaborate calculation yielding 8,000 -- determines when children of quality will be born. Ignorance of this number triggers the state's decline.
D: Distilled: The rulers' inability to calculate the 'nuptial number' governing optimal births is the first cause of the ideal state's degeneration.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, continuing as the Muses. -- The 'Allegory Number' or 'Allegory Nuptial Number' (546b-c) is one of the most debated passages in all of Plato. Ancient and modern commentators disagree on the exact calculation. -- The number 8,000 (= 20 cubed) is one common interpretation. -- 'Intelligence alloyed with sense': rulers who are not pure philosophers but rely partly on empirical observation. -- The passage draws on Pythagorean number theory and musical harmony. -- Hesiod's ages of gold, silver, bronze, and iron (Works and Days 109-201) are referenced in 8.11.

D: Original:
Now this number represents a geometrical figure which has control over the good and evil of births. For when your guardians are ignorant of the law of births, and unite bride and bridegroom out of season, the children will not be goodly or fortunate. And though only the best of them will be appointed by their predecessors, still they will be unworthy to hold their fathers' places, and when they come into power as guardians, they will soon be found to fall in taking care of us, the Muses, first by under-valuing music; which neglect will soon extend to gymnastic; and hence the young men of your State will be less cultivated. In the succeeding generation rulers will be appointed who have lost the guardian power of testing the metal of your different races, which, like Hesiod's, are of gold and silver and brass and iron. And so iron will be mingled with silver, and brass with gold, and hence there will arise dissimilarity and inequality and irregularity, which always and in all places are causes of hatred and war. This the Muses affirm to be the stock from which discord has sprung, wherever arising; and this is their answer to us.

Yes, and we may assume that they answer truly. Why, yes, I said, of course they answer truly; how can the Muses speak falsely?
D: Narrative:
"Now this number represents a geometrical figure which has control over the good and evil of births. For when your guardians are ignorant of the law of births, and unite bride and bridegroom out of season, the children will not be goodly or fortunate. And though only the best of them will be appointed by their predecessors, still they will be unworthy to hold their fathers' places, and when they come into power as guardians, they will soon be found to fall in taking care of us, the Muses, first by under-valuing music; which neglect will soon extend to gymnastic; and hence the young men of your State will be less cultivated.

In the succeeding generation rulers will be appointed who have lost the guardian power of testing the metal of your different races, which, like Hesiod's, are of gold and silver and brass and iron. And so iron will be mingled with silver, and brass with gold, and hence there will arise dissimilarity and inequality and irregularity, which always and in all places are causes of hatred and war. This the Muses affirm to be the stock from which discord has sprung, wherever arising; and this is their answer to us."

"Yes, and we may assume that they answer truly."

"Why, yes," I said, "of course they answer truly; how can the Muses speak falsely?"
D: Refashioned:
"This number governs the quality of births. When your guardians don't understand the law of births and pair couples at the wrong times, the children born will be neither gifted nor fortunate. Even if only the best among them are chosen as successors, they'll prove unworthy of their predecessors. When these inferior guardians take power, they'll begin neglecting the Muses -- first devaluing intellectual and artistic education, then letting physical training deteriorate as well. The young will grow less cultivated with each generation.

Eventually, rulers will emerge who have lost the ability to discern the different metals in human nature -- gold, silver, bronze, and iron, as Hesiod described them. Iron will be mixed with silver, bronze with gold, producing inequality, inconsistency, and disharmony -- which are always and everywhere the seeds of hatred and war. This, the Muses declare, is the origin of all discord."

"We can assume they're telling the truth."

"Of course they are," I said. "How could the Muses lie?"
D: Elegant: Ill-timed births produce unworthy guardians who neglect education -- first the arts, then physical training. Over generations, rulers lose the ability to distinguish the natural metals (gold, silver, bronze, iron) in human souls. The metals mix. Inequality and disharmony follow, breeding hatred and war. This is the origin of all political discord.
D: Summary: When guardians fail to time births correctly, each generation of rulers grows less capable. They neglect education, lose the ability to distinguish natural aptitudes among citizens, and allow the mixing of superior and inferior natures -- producing the inequality and discord that destroy the ideal state.
D: Distilled: Decline begins when inferior guardians neglect education and lose the ability to sort human natures, mixing gold with iron and breeding discord.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates (as the Muses) and Glaucon. -- Hesiod's metallic ages: Works and Days 109-201 (gold, silver, bronze, iron races). -- The 'allegory of the metals' was introduced in Book III (415a-c) as the 'noble lie' or 'myth of the metals.' -- Music (mousike) in the Greek sense encompasses all arts presided over by the Muses, including poetry, philosophy, and what we call 'liberal education.' -- Gymnastic (gymnastike) refers to physical and military training. -- The neglect of mousike before gymnastike signals the shift from wisdom-governance to honor-governance (timocracy).

D: Original: And what do the Muses say next? When discord arose, then the two races were drawn different ways: the iron and brass fell to acquiring money and land and houses and gold and silver; but the gold and silver races, not wanting money but having the true riches in their own nature, inclined towards virtue and the ancient order of things. There was a battle between them, and at last they agreed to distribute their land and houses among individual owners; and they enslaved their friends and maintainers, whom they had formerly protected in the condition of freemen, and made of them subjects and servants; and they themselves were engaged in war and in keeping a watch against them.
D: Narrative:
"And what do the Muses say next?"

"When discord arose, then the two races were drawn different ways: the iron and brass fell to acquiring money and land and houses and gold and silver; but the gold and silver races, not wanting money but having the true riches in their own nature, inclined towards virtue and the ancient order of things. There was a battle between them, and at last they agreed to distribute their land and houses among individual owners; and they enslaved their friends and maintainers, whom they had formerly protected in the condition of freemen, and made of them subjects and servants; and they themselves were engaged in war and in keeping a watch against them."
D: Refashioned:
"And what do the Muses say next?"

"When discord set in, the two groups pulled in opposite directions. The iron and bronze natures turned to acquiring money, land, houses, gold, and silver. But the gold and silver natures, who didn't want money because they already possessed true wealth within themselves, inclined toward virtue and the old order. A conflict broke out between them, and eventually they compromised: they divided the land and houses into private property. Then they enslaved the very people they had previously protected as free citizens, reducing them to subjects and servants. From then on, the rulers occupied themselves with war and with guarding against the people they had subjugated."
D: Elegant: Discord splits the ruling class: the baser natures pursue wealth while the nobler ones cling to virtue. They compromise by introducing private property -- a fatal step. The former protectors enslave those they once guarded, becoming warriors and overseers rather than philosophers.
D: Summary: When discord divides the ruling class, the baser natures pursue material wealth while the nobler ones defend virtue. They compromise by introducing private property and enslaving the citizens they once protected, transforming the state from an aristocracy into a militaristic regime.
D: Distilled: The ideal state falls when its rulers divide between virtue and greed, compromise by privatizing property, and enslave those they once protected.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates (as the Muses). -- The introduction of private property marks the decisive break from the communal ideal of Books III-V. -- 'Enslaved their friends and maintainers': the producer class, formerly free citizens, becomes a subject population -- paralleling Spartan helotry. -- This passage describes the birth of timocracy: a warrior-aristocracy concerned with military dominance rather than philosophical wisdom. -- Compare Rousseau's Discourse on Inequality, which echoes this narrative of property as the origin of social division.

D: Original:
I believe that you have rightly conceived the origin of the change.

And the new government which thus arises will be of a form intermediate between oligarchy and aristocracy?

Very true. Such will be the change, and after the change has been made, how will they proceed? Clearly, the new State, being in a mean between oligarchy and the perfect State, will partly follow one and partly the other, and will also have some peculiarities.

True, he said. In the honour given to rulers, in the abstinence of the warrior class from agriculture, handicrafts, and trade in general, in the institution of common meals, and in the attention paid to gymnastics and military training --in all these respects this State will resemble the former.

True. But in the fear of admitting philosophers to power, because they are no longer to be had simple and earnest, but are made up of mixed elements; and in turning from them to passionate and less complex characters, who are by nature fitted for war rather than peace; and in the value set by them upon military stratagems and contrivances, and in the waging of everlasting wars --this State will be for the most part peculiar.

Yes. Yes, I said; and men of this stamp will be covetous of money, like those who live in oligarchies; they will have, a fierce secret longing after gold and silver, which they will hoard in dark places, having magazines and treasuries of their own for the deposit and concealment of them; also castles which are just nests for their eggs, and in which they will spend large sums on their wives, or on any others whom they please.
D: Narrative:
"I believe that you have rightly conceived the origin of the change."

"And the new government which thus arises will be of a form intermediate between oligarchy and aristocracy?"

"Very true."

"Such will be the change, and after the change has been made, how will they proceed? Clearly, the new State, being in a mean between oligarchy and the perfect State, will partly follow one and partly the other, and will also have some peculiarities."

"True," he said.

"In the honour given to rulers, in the abstinence of the warrior class from agriculture, handicrafts, and trade in general, in the institution of common meals, and in the attention paid to gymnastics and military training -- in all these respects this State will resemble the former."

"True."

"But in the fear of admitting philosophers to power, because they are no longer to be had simple and earnest, but are made up of mixed elements; and in turning from them to passionate and less complex characters, who are by nature fitted for war rather than peace; and in the value set by them upon military stratagems and contrivances, and in the waging of everlasting wars -- this State will be for the most part peculiar."

"Yes."

"Yes," I said; "and men of this stamp will be covetous of money, like those who live in oligarchies; they will have a fierce secret longing after gold and silver, which they will hoard in dark places, having magazines and treasuries of their own for the deposit and concealment of them; also castles which are just nests for their eggs, and in which they will spend large sums on their wives, or on any others whom they please."
D: Refashioned:
"I think you've correctly identified the origin of the change."

"And the resulting government will be somewhere between oligarchy and aristocracy?"

"Exactly."

"After this transformation, how will they govern? Since the new state occupies a middle ground, it will borrow from both the ideal state and oligarchy, with some features unique to itself."

"True," he said.

"It will resemble the aristocracy in honoring its rulers, keeping the warrior class separate from farming and commerce, maintaining communal meals, and emphasizing physical and military training."

"True."

"But it will differ in its fear of placing philosophers in power -- because genuine philosophers are no longer available, only people of mixed and compromised character. Instead, it will elevate passionate, simpler personalities suited for war rather than peace. It will prize military cunning and wage perpetual wars."

"Yes."

"And men of this type," I said, "will secretly crave money, like those in oligarchies. They'll have a fierce hidden lust for gold and silver, which they'll hoard in vaults and private treasuries, hidden away in fortified estates -- little nests for their wealth -- where they'll lavish money on their wives or anyone else they fancy."
D: Elegant: Timocracy is a hybrid: it preserves aristocratic features like communal meals, warrior separation from commerce, and military training. But it replaces philosophers with warriors, prizes military cunning over wisdom, and wages perpetual war. Beneath the surface, its rulers secretly covet wealth -- hoarding gold in private vaults while publicly despising commerce.
D: Summary: The timocratic state blends aristocratic and oligarchic features: it maintains communal institutions and military training but excludes philosophers from power, replacing them with warrior-types. Its rulers secretly lust after wealth, hoarding gold in hidden treasuries while publicly disdaining commerce.
D: Distilled: Timocracy preserves aristocratic military structure but replaces wisdom with warrior spirit and conceals a growing appetite for wealth.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- Common meals (syssitia): a hallmark of Spartan and Cretan society, also prescribed in Plato's Laws (780d-781d). -- The exclusion of philosophers marks the key difference from aristocracy: honor replaces wisdom as the ruling principle. -- 'Fierce secret longing after gold': the critical flaw that will eventually transform timocracy into oligarchy. -- The private hoarding of wealth despite public prohibition mirrors Spartan hypocrisy -- Spartans were officially forbidden from possessing gold and silver but notoriously accumulated it in secret. -- Plutarch, Life of Lycurgus 9-10, describes similar Spartan contradictions.

D: Original: That is most true, he said. And they are miserly because they have no means of openly acquiring the money which they prize; they will spend that which is another man's on the gratification of their desires, stealing their pleasures and running away like children from the law, their father: they have been schooled not by gentle influences but by force, for they have neglected her who is the true Muse, the companion of reason and philosophy, and have honoured gymnastic more than music.
D: Narrative:
"That is most true," he said.

"And they are miserly because they have no means of openly acquiring the money which they prize; they will spend that which is another man's on the gratification of their desires, stealing their pleasures and running away like children from the law, their father: they have been schooled not by gentle influences but by force, for they have neglected her who is the true Muse, the companion of reason and philosophy, and have honoured gymnastic more than music."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly right," he said.

"And they're secretive with money because they have no legitimate way to acquire what they crave. They gratify their desires with other people's money, stealing their pleasures and running from the law like children hiding from a father. They were shaped not by gentle persuasion but by force, because they abandoned the true Muse -- the companion of reason and philosophy -- and valued physical training above intellectual cultivation."
D: Elegant: Timocratic men are miserly in public and indulgent in secret, stealing pleasures like children evading a father. Their character was formed by force rather than reason, because they honored the body over the mind and abandoned philosophy.
D: Summary: Timocratic rulers are secretly greedy, indulging desires with others' money while hiding from the law. Their character was shaped by force rather than persuasion, because they neglected philosophy in favor of physical training.
D: Distilled: Timocratic men are covertly greedy and shaped by force rather than reason, having abandoned philosophy for gymnastics.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- 'The true Muse, companion of reason and philosophy': mousike in its highest sense -- intellectual and artistic cultivation that shapes the soul through persuasion rather than compulsion. -- Law as father: an early instance of the metaphor developed more fully in the Laws. -- The priority of gymnastic over music is the defining educational failure of timocracy. -- Compare Book III (401d-402a) on the role of mousike in forming character through gentle habituation. -- Running from the law 'like children' anticipates the democratic man's childish relationship with desire (8.42-8.46).

D: Original:
Undoubtedly, he said, the form of government which you describe is a mixture of good and evil.

Why, there is a mixture, I said; but one thing, and one thing only, is predominantly seen, --the spirit of contention and ambition; and these are due to the prevalence of the passionate or spirited element.

Assuredly, he said. Such is the origin and such the character of this State, which has been described in outline only; the more perfect execution was not required, for a sketch is enough to show the type of the most perfectly just and most perfectly unjust; and to go through all the States and all the characters of men, omitting none of them, would be an interminable labour.

Very true, he replied. Now what man answers to this form of government-how did he come into being, and what is he like?
D: Narrative:
"Undoubtedly," he said, "the form of government which you describe is a mixture of good and evil."

"Why, there is a mixture," I said; "but one thing, and one thing only, is predominantly seen, -- the spirit of contention and ambition; and these are due to the prevalence of the passionate or spirited element."

"Assuredly," he said.

"Such is the origin and such the character of this State, which has been described in outline only; the more perfect execution was not required, for a sketch is enough to show the type of the most perfectly just and most perfectly unjust; and to go through all the States and all the characters of men, omitting none of them, would be an interminable labour."

"Very true," he replied.

"Now what man answers to this form of government -- how did he come into being, and what is he like?"
D: Refashioned:
"The government you're describing," he said, "is clearly a mixture of good and evil."

"It is a mixture," I said, "but one quality dominates everything else: the spirit of competition and ambition, which comes from the dominance of the passionate, spirited element of the soul."

"Undoubtedly."

"That's the origin and character of this state, sketched in outline only. A more detailed treatment isn't necessary -- a sketch is enough to show the types of the most just and most unjust. To catalog every possible state and character type exhaustively would be an endless task."

"Very true."

"Now, what kind of person corresponds to this form of government? How does he come into being, and what is he like?"
D: Elegant: Timocracy is a mixture of good and evil, but one trait dominates: the spirit of competition and ambition, driven by the spirited element of the soul. With the state sketched, Socrates turns to the timocratic individual.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes that timocracy mixes good and evil qualities but is dominated by competitive ambition rooted in the spirited element of the soul. Having sketched the state, he turns to describe the corresponding individual.
D: Distilled: Timocracy is dominated by competitive ambition -- the spirited element -- and Socrates now turns from the state to the timocratic individual.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- The 'spirited element' (thumos/thumoeides): the middle part of the tripartite soul from Book IV (439e-441c), associated with anger, honor, and competitive drive. -- In the ideal state, thumos serves reason; in timocracy, it rules. -- 'Sketch is enough': Plato's characteristic methodological modesty about the level of precision appropriate to political philosophy. -- Transition from state to individual follows the established pattern.

D: Original:
I think, said Adeimantus, that in the spirit of contention which characterises him, he is not unlike our friend Glaucon.

Perhaps, I said, he may be like him in that one point; but there are other respects in which he is very different.
D: Narrative:
"I think," said Adeimantus, "that in the spirit of contention which characterises him, he is not unlike our friend Glaucon."

"Perhaps," I said, "he may be like him in that one point; but there are other respects in which he is very different."
D: Refashioned:
"I think," said Adeimantus, "that in his competitive spirit, he's not unlike our friend Glaucon."

"Perhaps in that one respect," I said, "but in many other ways he's quite different."
D: Elegant: Adeimantus playfully compares the timocratic man's competitive spirit to Glaucon's. Socrates concedes the resemblance but insists the differences are greater.
D: Summary: Adeimantus humorously suggests that the timocratic man's competitive nature resembles Glaucon's. Socrates agrees on that one point but notes they differ in many other respects.
D: Distilled: Adeimantus jokes that the competitive timocratic man resembles Glaucon, but Socrates notes deeper differences.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus (who replaces Glaucon as interlocutor here). -- This is a rare moment of personal humor in the dialogue. Glaucon was characterized as spirited and competitive in Book II (357a) when he insisted on a stronger defense of justice than Socrates initially offered. -- Adeimantus takes over as the primary respondent for the remainder of Book VIII, a shift that occurs without explanation. -- The joke works because Glaucon is present and can hear the comparison.

D: Original: In what respects? He should have more of self-assertion and be less cultivated, and yet a friend of culture; and he should be a good listener, but no speaker. Such a person is apt to be rough with slaves, unlike the educated man, who is too proud for that; and he will also be courteous to freemen, and remarkably obedient to authority; he is a lover of power and a lover of honour; claiming to be a ruler, not because he is eloquent, or on any ground of that sort, but because he is a soldier and has performed feats of arms; he is also a lover of gymnastic exercises and of the chase.
D: Narrative:
"In what respects?"

"He should have more of self-assertion and be less cultivated, and yet a friend of culture; and he should be a good listener, but no speaker. Such a person is apt to be rough with slaves, unlike the educated man, who is too proud for that; and he will also be courteous to freemen, and remarkably obedient to authority; he is a lover of power and a lover of honour; claiming to be a ruler, not because he is eloquent, or on any ground of that sort, but because he is a soldier and has performed feats of arms; he is also a lover of gymnastic exercises and of the chase."
D: Refashioned:
"In what respects?"

"The timocratic man should be more assertive and less refined, yet still appreciate culture. He's a good listener but not much of a speaker. He tends to be harsh with slaves -- unlike the truly educated person, who is above such behavior -- yet he's polite to free citizens and remarkably obedient to authority. He loves power and honor, and claims the right to rule not on the basis of eloquence or intellectual merit, but because he's a soldier who has proven himself in battle. He's also devoted to athletics and hunting."
D: Elegant: The timocratic man: assertive but uncultivated, a listener not a speaker, harsh with slaves but respectful of authority. He claims power through military achievement, not intellectual merit. He loves athletics and hunting.
D: Summary: The timocratic individual is self-assertive but lacks refinement. He appreciates culture without being cultivated, is harsh with inferiors but obedient to authority, and claims leadership through military deeds rather than eloquence or learning. He loves athletics and hunting.
D: Distilled: The timocratic man rules by military achievement rather than intellect -- assertive, obedient to authority, devoted to athletics and combat.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The portrait closely matches the Spartan ideal: laconic speech (good listener, no speaker), military valor as the basis of authority, love of hunting and athletics. -- 'Rough with slaves': contrasts with the philosopher-ruler's temperance; anticipates the discussion of how each regime treats its lower classes. -- 'Too proud for that': the aristocratic man's refinement prevents cruelty, not mere obedience. -- The love of the chase (hunting) was a hallmark of aristocratic and Spartan education; see Xenophon's Cynegeticus.

D: Original:
Yes, that is the type of character which answers to timocracy.

Such an one will despise riches only when he is young; but as he gets older he will be more and more attracted to them, because he has a piece of the avaricious nature in him, and is not singleminded towards virtue, having lost his best guardian.

Who was that? said Adeimantus. Philosophy, I said, tempered with music, who comes and takes her abode in a man, and is the only saviour of his virtue throughout life.

Good, he said. Such, I said, is the timocratical youth, and he is like the timocratical State.

Exactly. His origin is as follows: --He is often the young son of a grave father, who dwells in an ill-governed city, of which he declines the honours and offices, and will not go to law, or exert himself in any way, but is ready to waive his rights in order that he may escape trouble.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, that is the type of character which answers to timocracy."

"Such an one will despise riches only when he is young; but as he gets older he will be more and more attracted to them, because he has a piece of the avaricious nature in him, and is not singleminded towards virtue, having lost his best guardian."

"Who was that?" said Adeimantus.

"Philosophy," I said, "tempered with music, who comes and takes her abode in a man, and is the only saviour of his virtue throughout life."

"Good," he said.

"Such," I said, "is the timocratical youth, and he is like the timocratical State."

"Exactly."
