"His origin is as follows: -- He is often the young son of a grave father, who dwells in an ill-governed city, of which he declines the honours and offices, and will not go to law, or exert himself in any way, but is ready to waive his rights in order that he may escape trouble."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that's exactly the character type that matches timocracy."

"This kind of man will scorn wealth when he's young, but as he ages, he'll be drawn to it more and more. He carries a seed of avarice within him, and he's not single-mindedly devoted to virtue because he has lost his best guardian."

"Who was that?" asked Adeimantus.

"Philosophy," I said, "combined with the arts -- the only force that can protect a person's virtue throughout life."

"A good point," he said.

"So that is the timocratic youth, and he mirrors the timocratic state."

"Exactly."

"Here is how he comes into being: He is typically the young son of a serious, philosophical father who lives in a badly governed city. This father refuses honors and public offices, avoids lawsuits, and doesn't exert himself in civic life. He's willing to give up his rights just to avoid trouble."
D: Elegant: The timocratic man scorns wealth when young but grows avaricious with age, having lost his guardian -- philosophy combined with the arts. He is typically the son of a philosophical father who withdraws from public life in a corrupt city, declining honors and avoiding conflict.
D: Summary: The timocratic man gradually abandons virtue for avarice because he lacks philosophy's protection. He typically originates as the son of a philosophical father who has withdrawn from public life in a corrupt city, refusing offices and avoiding conflict.
D: Distilled: Without philosophy as guardian, the timocratic man drifts from virtue toward greed -- the son of a withdrawn philosopher-father in a corrupt city.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- Philosophy 'tempered with music' (mousike): intellectual cultivation combined with artistic education -- the Republic's ideal formation. -- The father figure represents the aristocratic/philosophical type living under non-ideal conditions. -- His withdrawal from public life mirrors the philosopher's dilemma discussed in Book VI (496c-e): the just man who refuses to participate in corrupt politics. -- The generational transmission of character is a key mechanism in Plato's theory of political change -- each constitution breeds the next through family dynamics.

D: Original:
And how does the son come into being? The character of the son begins to develop when he hears his mother complaining that her husband has no place in the government, of which the consequence is that she has no precedence among other women. Further, when she sees her husband not very eager about money, and instead of battling and railing in the law courts or assembly, taking whatever happens to him quietly; and when she observes that his thoughts always centre in himself, while he treats her with very considerable indifference, she is annoyed, and says to her son that his father is only half a man and far too easy-going: adding all the other complaints about her own ill-treatment which women are so fond of rehearsing.

Yes, said Adeimantus, they give us plenty of them, and their complaints are so like themselves.
D: Narrative:
"And how does the son come into being?"

"The character of the son begins to develop when he hears his mother complaining that her husband has no place in the government, of which the consequence is that she has no precedence among other women. Further, when she sees her husband not very eager about money, and instead of battling and railing in the law courts or assembly, taking whatever happens to him quietly; and when she observes that his thoughts always centre in himself, while he treats her with very considerable indifference, she is annoyed, and says to her son that his father is only half a man and far too easy-going: adding all the other complaints about her own ill-treatment which women are so fond of rehearsing."

"Yes," said Adeimantus, "they give us plenty of them, and their complaints are so like themselves."
D: Refashioned:
"And how does the son develop this character?"

"It begins when he hears his mother complaining that her husband holds no position in the government, which means she has no status among other women. She sees him indifferent to money, refusing to fight in the courts or the assembly, quietly accepting whatever comes his way. She notices his thoughts are always turned inward and that he treats her with considerable neglect. Resentful, she tells her son that his father is only half a man, far too passive -- adding all the usual grievances about her mistreatment that women love to recite."

"Yes," said Adeimantus, "they give us plenty of those, and the complaints are very much in character."
D: Elegant: The timocratic son's formation begins at home: his mother, resentful that her philosophical husband holds no office and earns no status, tells the boy his father is weak and passive. The mother's complaints about lost social standing plant the seeds of ambition in the son.
D: Summary: The timocratic character develops when a boy hears his mother complaining about his philosophical father's lack of ambition, social standing, and concern for money. She tells the son his father is only half a man, planting seeds of competitive ambition.
D: Distilled: The timocratic son is shaped by his mother's resentment of his philosophical father's lack of ambition and social status.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The mother's role in corrupting the son is striking: she represents social pressure and conventional values opposed to philosophical withdrawal. -- 'Only half a man' (hemiandr): a pointed insult connecting manliness with public ambition. -- Adeimantus' somewhat dismissive remark about women's complaints reflects Greek gender attitudes but also functions dramatically -- he is Plato's brother and speaks from personal observation. -- The domestic scene as political allegory: the household microcosm mirrors the state's internal tensions. -- Compare Laws 694c-d on the role of women in corrupting Spartan youth.

D: Original: And you know, I said, that the old servants also, who are supposed to be attached to the family, from time to time talk privately in the same strain to the son; and if they see any one who owes money to his father, or is wronging him in any way, and he falls to prosecute them, they tell the youth that when he grows up he must retaliate upon people of this sort, and be more of a man than his father. He has only to walk abroad and he hears and sees the same sort of thing: those who do their own business in the city are called simpletons, and held in no esteem, while the busy-bodies are honoured and applauded. The result is that the young man, hearing and seeing all these thing --hearing too, the words of his father, and having a nearer view of his way of life, and making comparisons of him and others --is drawn opposite ways: while his father is watering and nourishing the rational principle in his soul, the others are encouraging the passionate and appetitive; and he being not originally of a bad nature, but having kept bad company, is at last brought by their joint influence to a middle point, and gives up the kingdom which is within him to the middle principle of contentiousness and passion, and becomes arrogant and ambitious.
D: Narrative:
"And you know," I said, "that the old servants also, who are supposed to be attached to the family, from time to time talk privately in the same strain to the son; and if they see any one who owes money to his father, or is wronging him in any way, and he fails to prosecute them, they tell the youth that when he grows up he must retaliate upon people of this sort, and be more of a man than his father.

He has only to walk abroad and he hears and sees the same sort of thing: those who do their own business in the city are called simpletons, and held in no esteem, while the busy-bodies are honoured and applauded.

The result is that the young man, hearing and seeing all these things -- hearing, too, the words of his father, and having a nearer view of his way of life, and making comparisons of him and others -- is drawn opposite ways: while his father is watering and nourishing the rational principle in his soul, the others are encouraging the passionate and appetitive; and he being not originally of a bad nature, but having kept bad company, is at last brought by their joint influence to a middle point, and gives up the kingdom which is within him to the middle principle of contentiousness and passion, and becomes arrogant and ambitious."
D: Refashioned:
"And you know," I said, "that the old household servants also whisper the same things to the boy. When they see someone who owes his father money or wrongs him in some way, and the father does nothing about it, they urge the son to be more of a man when he grows up -- to stand up for himself and fight back.

Whenever the boy goes out into the city, he sees the same pattern: quiet, self-sufficient people are called simpletons and get no respect, while aggressive self-promoters are honored and applauded.

The young man, hearing all this and comparing his father's way of life with what the world rewards, is pulled in opposite directions. His father nurtures the rational element in his soul, while everyone else feeds the spirited and appetitive parts. Since he isn't inherently bad but has been exposed to bad influences, he eventually settles at a middle point: he surrenders inner sovereignty to the spirited element -- contentiousness and passion -- and becomes arrogant and ambitious."
D: Elegant: The boy is pulled between his philosophical father, who cultivates reason, and everyone else -- mother, servants, society -- who encourages spiritedness and appetite. Not inherently bad but surrounded by bad influences, he compromises: reason abdicates, and the spirited element takes the throne. He becomes the competitive, ambitious timocratic man.
D: Summary: The young man is caught between his father's rational influence and the surrounding culture's celebration of aggression and ambition. Servants, peers, and public opinion all urge him to be more competitive. Not naturally bad, he is pulled to a middle point where the spirited element rules his soul, making him arrogant and ambitious.
D: Distilled: Torn between his father's reason and society's celebration of aggression, the young man surrenders his soul's governance to the spirited element and becomes ambitious.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Gives up the kingdom within him to the middle principle': the tripartite soul's internal regime change mirrors the political transition from aristocracy to timocracy. Reason (logistikon) is dethroned; spirit (thumoeides) takes over. -- 'Not originally of a bad nature': Plato emphasizes that corruption comes from environment, not inherent vice -- a key pedagogical principle. -- 'Those who do their own business are called simpletons': ta heautou prattein (minding one's own business) was Socrates' own definition of justice in Book IV (433a-b). The timocratic society inverts this value. -- The servants represent the lower social strata whose values reinforce conventional ambition.

D: Original:
You seem to me to have described his origin perfectly. Then we have now, I said, the second form of government and the second type of character?

We have. Next, let us look at another man who, as Aeschylus says,

Is set over against another State; or rather, as our plan requires, begin with the State.

By all means. I believe that oligarchy follows next in order. And what manner of government do you term oligarchy? A government resting on a valuation of property, in which the rich have power and the poor man is deprived of it.

I understand, he replied. Ought I not to begin by describing how the change from timocracy to oligarchy arises?

Yes. Well, I said, no eyes are required in order to see how the one passes into the other.

How? The accumulation of gold in the treasury of private individuals is ruin the of timocracy; they invent illegal modes of expenditure; for what do they or their wives care about the law?

Yes, indeed. And then one, seeing another grow rich, seeks to rival him, and thus the great mass of the citizens become lovers of money.

Likely enough. And so they grow richer and richer, and the more they think of making a fortune the less they think of virtue; for when riches and virtue are placed together in the scales of the balance, the one always rises as the other falls.

True. And in proportion as riches and rich men are honoured in the State, virtue and the virtuous are dishonoured.

Clearly. And what is honoured is cultivated, and that which has no honour is neglected.

That is obvious. And so at last, instead of loving contention and glory, men become lovers of trade and money; they honour and look up to the rich man, and make a ruler of him, and dishonour the poor man.

They do so. They next proceed to make a law which fixes a sum of money as the qualification of citizenship; the sum is higher in one place and lower in another, as the oligarchy is more or less exclusive; and they allow no one whose property falls below the amount fixed to have any share in the government. These changes in the constitution they effect by force of arms, if intimidation has not already done their work.
D: Narrative:
"You seem to me to have described his origin perfectly."

"Then we have now," I said, "the second form of government and the second type of character?"

"We have."

"Next, let us look at another man who, as Aeschylus says, 'Is set over against another State'; or rather, as our plan requires, begin with the State."

"By all means."

"I believe that oligarchy follows next in order."

"And what manner of government do you term oligarchy?"

"A government resting on a valuation of property, in which the rich have power and the poor man is deprived of it."

"I understand," he replied.

"Ought I not to begin by describing how the change from timocracy to oligarchy arises?"

"Yes."

"Well," I said, "no eyes are required in order to see how the one passes into the other."

"How?"

"The accumulation of gold in the treasury of private individuals is the ruin of timocracy; they invent illegal modes of expenditure; for what do they or their wives care about the law?"

"Yes, indeed."

"And then one, seeing another grow rich, seeks to rival him, and thus the great mass of the citizens become lovers of money."

"Likely enough."

"And so they grow richer and richer, and the more they think of making a fortune the less they think of virtue; for when riches and virtue are placed together in the scales of the balance, the one always rises as the other falls."

"True."

"And in proportion as riches and rich men are honoured in the State, virtue and the virtuous are dishonoured."

"Clearly."

"And what is honoured is cultivated, and that which has no honour is neglected."

"That is obvious."

"And so at last, instead of loving contention and glory, men become lovers of trade and money; they honour and look up to the rich man, and make a ruler of him, and dishonour the poor man."

"They do so."

"They next proceed to make a law which fixes a sum of money as the qualification of citizenship; the sum is higher in one place and lower in another, as the oligarchy is more or less exclusive; and they allow no one whose property falls below the amount fixed to have any share in the government. These changes in the constitution they effect by force of arms, if intimidation has not already done their work."
D: Refashioned:
"You've described his origin perfectly."

"So we now have the second form of government and the second character type?"

"We do."

"Next, let's examine another man 'set over against another state,' as Aeschylus puts it. But following our method, we'll start with the state."

"By all means."

"Oligarchy comes next, I believe."

"And what is oligarchy exactly?"

"Government based on property qualifications -- the rich rule, and the poor are excluded from power."

"I see," he replied.

"Shall I begin with how timocracy transforms into oligarchy?"

"Yes."

"It's easy enough to see how one becomes the other."

"How?"

"Private accumulation of gold destroys timocracy. The wealthy invent illegal ways to spend their money -- what do they or their wives care about the law?"

"Indeed."

"Then everyone sees their neighbors getting rich and tries to match them. Before long, the whole citizen body becomes obsessed with money."

"That's likely."

"And the richer they get, the less they care about virtue. Wealth and virtue sit on opposite ends of a scale -- when one rises, the other falls."

"True."

"As the state honors wealth and the wealthy, it dishonors virtue and the virtuous."

"Clearly."

"And whatever is honored gets cultivated; whatever is dishonored gets neglected."

"Obviously."

"So instead of loving honor and glory, people become lovers of money. They look up to the rich, make them rulers, and despise the poor."

"They do."

"Then they pass laws setting a minimum property qualification for citizenship -- higher in more exclusive oligarchies, lower in less exclusive ones. Anyone whose property falls below the threshold is shut out of government. They enforce these changes by force of arms if intimidation doesn't do the job."
D: Elegant: Oligarchy grows from timocracy's hidden vice: private gold accumulation. As wealth spreads and envy drives competition, citizens abandon honor for money. Wealth and virtue are inversely related -- what society honors, it cultivates. The timocratic love of glory gives way to worship of riches. Property qualifications replace merit, and the poor are excluded from power by law or by force.
D: Summary: Timocracy degenerates into oligarchy through private wealth accumulation. Competitive greed spreads as citizens imitate one another's riches, and virtue declines as wealth is honored. Eventually, property qualifications are established that exclude the poor from government, enforced by arms if necessary.
D: Distilled: Timocracy becomes oligarchy when private wealth accumulation replaces honor as the ruling principle, and property qualifications exclude the poor from power.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- Aeschylus, Seven Against Thebes 451: 'set over against another state' -- using opposing pairs for comparison. -- 'Riches and virtue on the scales': a famous Platonic image of inverse proportionality. -- Property qualification (timema): historical Athens used the Solonian property classes (pentakosiomedimnoi, hippeis, zeugitai, thetes) to determine political rights. -- 'Force of arms, if intimidation has not already done their work': describes the typical oligarchic coup. -- The transition mechanism is psychological: competitive envy (zelos) transforms collective values from honor to wealth.

D: Original:
Very true. And this, speaking generally, is the way in which oligarchy is established.

Yes, he said; but what are the characteristics of this form of government, and what are the defects of which we were speaking?

First of all, I said, consider the nature of the qualification just think what would happen if pilots were to be chosen according to their property, and a poor man were refused permission to steer, even though he were a better pilot?

You mean that they would shipwreck? Yes; and is not this true of the government of anything?

I should imagine so. Except a city? --or would you include a city? Nay, he said, the case of a city is the strongest of all, inasmuch as the rule of a city is the greatest and most difficult of all.

This, then, will be the first great defect of oligarchy?

Clearly. And here is another defect which is quite as bad. What defect? The inevitable division: such a State is not one, but two States, the one of poor, the other of rich men; and they are living on the same spot and always conspiring against one another.
D: Narrative:
"Very true."

"And this, speaking generally, is the way in which oligarchy is established."

"Yes," he said; "but what are the characteristics of this form of government, and what are the defects of which we were speaking?"

"First of all," I said, "consider the nature of the qualification. Just think what would happen if pilots were to be chosen according to their property, and a poor man were refused permission to steer, even though he were a better pilot?"

"You mean that they would shipwreck?"

"Yes; and is not this true of the government of anything?"

"I should imagine so."

"Except a city? -- or would you include a city?"

"Nay," he said, "the case of a city is the strongest of all, inasmuch as the rule of a city is the greatest and most difficult of all."

"This, then, will be the first great defect of oligarchy?"

"Clearly."

"And here is another defect which is quite as bad."

"What defect?"

"The inevitable division: such a State is not one, but two States, the one of poor, the other of rich men; and they are living on the same spot and always conspiring against one another."
D: Refashioned:
"Very true."

"And that, broadly speaking, is how oligarchy is established."

"Yes, but what are its defining features and its defects?"

"First, consider the property qualification itself. Imagine if ship captains were chosen by wealth rather than skill, and a poor man were forbidden to steer even though he was the better pilot."

"They'd shipwreck."

"And doesn't the same principle apply to governing anything?"

"I'd think so."

"Including a city? Or especially a city?"

"Especially a city," he said, "since governing a city is the greatest and most difficult task of all."

"So that's the first major defect of oligarchy: selecting leaders by wealth rather than competence."

"Clearly."

"And here's a second defect just as serious."

"What?"

"The inevitable division. An oligarchy is not one state but two: one of the rich and one of the poor, living in the same place and perpetually conspiring against each other."
D: Elegant: Oligarchy's first defect: choosing rulers by wealth instead of competence is like choosing ship captains by property -- a recipe for shipwreck. Its second defect: it inevitably splits the state into two hostile camps, rich and poor, permanently conspiring against each other.
D: Summary: Socrates identifies two fundamental defects of oligarchy: first, selecting rulers by wealth rather than ability, which is as absurd as choosing pilots by property; second, the inevitable division of the state into two hostile classes -- rich and poor -- living side by side in permanent antagonism.
D: Distilled: Oligarchy's fatal flaws are selecting rulers by wealth instead of merit and splitting the state into two warring classes of rich and poor.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The pilot analogy (kubernetes): a favorite Socratic metaphor for expertise-based governance, used in Statesman 297e and elsewhere. -- 'Not one but two states': this analysis of class division anticipates Marx's concept of class conflict, though Plato's remedy (philosophical rule) differs radically. -- Aristotle develops this critique in Politics III.8-9 and V.1. -- The rich-poor division will be the mechanism by which oligarchy degenerates into democracy (the poor revolt).

D: Original: That, surely, is at least as bad. Another discreditable feature is, that, for a like reason, they are incapable of carrying on any war. Either they arm the multitude, and then they are more afraid of them than of the enemy; or, if they do not call them out in the hour of battle, they are oligarchs indeed, few to fight as they are few to rule. And at the same time their fondness for money makes them unwilling to pay taxes.
D: Narrative:
"That, surely, is at least as bad."

"Another discreditable feature is, that, for a like reason, they are incapable of carrying on any war. Either they arm the multitude, and then they are more afraid of them than of the enemy; or, if they do not call them out in the hour of battle, they are oligarchs indeed, few to fight as they are few to rule. And at the same time their fondness for money makes them unwilling to pay taxes."
D: Refashioned:
"That's at least as serious."

"Here's another failing: oligarchies are incapable of waging war effectively. If they arm the masses, they fear their own people more than the enemy. If they don't call up the people, they fight with the same tiny minority that rules -- too few to govern and too few to fight. And their love of money makes them unwilling to pay for any of it."
D: Elegant: Oligarchy cannot wage war: arming the poor majority is dangerous, but fighting with only the rich minority is futile. And their love of money makes them unwilling to fund either option.
D: Summary: Oligarchies cannot conduct war effectively because arming the poor majority threatens the regime, while relying solely on the rich minority provides too few soldiers. Their love of money also makes them reluctant to pay taxes for defense.
D: Distilled: Oligarchy is militarily impotent -- too afraid to arm the poor, too few to fight alone, too miserly to fund a war.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The military weakness of oligarchy contrasts sharply with timocracy's martial strength -- each constitution excels at what the previous one valued. -- The dilemma of arming the poor anticipates the revolutionary mechanism: once the poor realize their military power, they can overthrow the oligarchs. -- Historical parallel: Athenian oligarchs during the coup of 411 BCE and the Thirty Tyrants (404 BCE) faced exactly this problem. -- Aristotle discusses oligarchic military weakness in Politics V.6.

D: Original:
How discreditable! And, as we said before, under such a constitution the same persons have too many callings --they are husbandmen, tradesmen, warriors, all in one. Does that look well?

Anything but well. There is another evil which is, perhaps, the greatest of all, and to which this State first begins to be liable.

What evil? A man may sell all that he has, and another may acquire his property; yet after the sale he may dwell in the city of which he is no longer a part, being neither trader, nor artisan, nor horseman, nor hoplite, but only a poor, helpless creature.

Yes, that is an evil which also first begins in this State.

The evil is certainly not prevented there; for oligarchies have both the extremes of great wealth and utter poverty.

True. But think again: In his wealthy days, while he was spending his money, was a man of this sort a whit more good to the State for the purposes of citizenship? Or did he only seem to be a member of the ruling body, although in truth he was neither ruler nor subject, but just a spendthrift?

As you say, he seemed to be a ruler, but was only a spendthrift.

May we not say that this is the drone in the house who is like the drone in the honeycomb, and that the one is the plague of the city as the other is of the hive?

Just so, Socrates. And God has made the flying drones, Adeimantus, all without stings, whereas of the walking drones he has made some without stings but others have dreadful stings; of the stingless class are those who in their old age end as paupers; of the stingers come all the criminal class, as they are termed.

Most true, he said. Clearly then, whenever you see paupers in a State, somewhere in that neighborhood there are hidden away thieves, and cutpurses and robbers of temples, and all sorts of malefactors.

Clearly. Well, I said, and in oligarchical States do you not find paupers?

Yes, he said; nearly everybody is a pauper who is not a ruler.
D: Narrative:
"How discreditable!"

"And, as we said before, under such a constitution the same persons have too many callings -- they are husbandmen, tradesmen, warriors, all in one. Does that look well?"

"Anything but well."

"There is another evil which is, perhaps, the greatest of all, and to which this State first begins to be liable."

"What evil?"

"A man may sell all that he has, and another may acquire his property; yet after the sale he may dwell in the city of which he is no longer a part, being neither trader, nor artisan, nor horseman, nor hoplite, but only a poor, helpless creature."

"Yes, that is an evil which also first begins in this State."

"The evil is certainly not prevented there; for oligarchies have both the extremes of great wealth and utter poverty."

"True."

"But think again: In his wealthy days, while he was spending his money, was a man of this sort a whit more good to the State for the purposes of citizenship? Or did he only seem to be a member of the ruling body, although in truth he was neither ruler nor subject, but just a spendthrift?"

"As you say, he seemed to be a ruler, but was only a spendthrift."

"May we not say that this is the drone in the house who is like the drone in the honeycomb, and that the one is the plague of the city as the other is of the hive?"

"Just so, Socrates."

"And God has made the flying drones, Adeimantus, all without stings, whereas of the walking drones he has made some without stings but others have dreadful stings; of the stingless class are those who in their old age end as paupers; of the stingers come all the criminal class, as they are termed."

"Most true," he said.

"Clearly then, whenever you see paupers in a State, somewhere in that neighborhood there are hidden away thieves, and cutpurses and robbers of temples, and all sorts of malefactors."

"Clearly."

"Well," I said, "and in oligarchical States do you not find paupers?"

"Yes," he said; "nearly everybody is a pauper who is not a ruler."
D: Refashioned:
"How shameful!"

"And as we noted, in such a state the same people wear too many hats -- they're farmers, merchants, and soldiers all at once. Does that seem right?"

"Not at all."

"There's another evil, perhaps the worst of all, that first appears in this kind of state."

"What evil?"

"A man can sell everything he owns, and someone else can buy it all. The seller then continues living in a city where he no longer belongs -- he's not a merchant, not a craftsman, not a cavalry officer, not a foot soldier. He's just a poor, helpless nobody."

"Yes, that's an evil that begins here."

"And oligarchy doesn't prevent it -- it produces both extremes of enormous wealth and total destitution."

"True."

"But consider this: even when this man still had money and was spending it freely, was he actually useful to the state as a citizen? Or was he just pretending to be part of the ruling class when in reality he was neither ruler nor subject -- just a spendthrift?"

"He was just a spendthrift, as you say."

"Can we call him a drone -- like the drone in a beehive -- a plague on the city just as the drone is on the hive?"

"Exactly, Socrates."

"Now, nature made the flying drones without stings. But among the walking, human drones, some are stingless and some have terrible stings. The stingless ones end their days as paupers. The ones with stings become the criminal class."

"Very true."

"So whenever you see paupers in a state, you can be sure that somewhere nearby there are thieves, pickpockets, temple robbers, and criminals of every kind."

"Clearly."

"And in oligarchies, do you find paupers?"

"Yes -- nearly everyone who isn't a ruler is a pauper."
D: Elegant: Oligarchy's worst evil: it creates a class of dispossessed citizens who have lost everything but still inhabit the city -- human drones. Some drones are merely helpless paupers (stingless); others become criminals (stingers). Where there is poverty, crime follows. In oligarchies, nearly everyone who isn't a ruler is a pauper.
D: Summary: Oligarchy permits citizens to lose all property and become useless drones in the city. These drones come in two varieties: harmless paupers (stingless) and criminals (with stings). Wherever paupers exist, crime follows. In oligarchies, virtually everyone outside the ruling class is impoverished.
D: Distilled: Oligarchy creates human drones -- dispossessed citizens who become either helpless paupers or dangerous criminals, breeding poverty and crime.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The drone (kephren) metaphor is central to Books VIII-IX: drones represent parasitic, unproductive citizens who destabilize the state. -- Stingless drones = passive poor; stinging drones = active criminals. The metaphor recurs in the analysis of democracy (8.40, 8.53-54). -- 'Cutpurses and robbers of temples': a catalog of Athenian criminal types. -- The specialization principle from Book II (369e-370c) -- each person should do one job -- is violated in oligarchy. -- 'Nearly everybody is a pauper who is not a ruler': this extreme concentration of wealth is what triggers the democratic revolution.

D: Original:
And may we be so bold as to affirm that there are also many criminals to be found in them, rogues who have stings, and whom the authorities are careful to restrain by force?

Certainly, we may be so bold. The existence of such persons is to be attributed to want of education, ill-training, and an evil constitution of the State?

True. Such, then, is the form and such are the evils of oligarchy; and there may be many other evils.

Very likely. Then oligarchy, or the form of government in which the rulers are elected for their wealth, may now be dismissed. Let us next proceed to consider the nature and origin of the individual who answers to this State.

By all means. Does not the timocratical man change into the oligarchical on this wise?
D: Narrative:
"And may we be so bold as to affirm that there are also many criminals to be found in them, rogues who have stings, and whom the authorities are careful to restrain by force?"

"Certainly, we may be so bold."

"The existence of such persons is to be attributed to want of education, ill-training, and an evil constitution of the State?"

"True."

"Such, then, is the form and such are the evils of oligarchy; and there may be many other evils."

"Very likely."

"Then oligarchy, or the form of government in which the rulers are elected for their wealth, may now be dismissed. Let us next proceed to consider the nature and origin of the individual who answers to this State."

"By all means."

"Does not the timocratical man change into the oligarchical on this wise?"
D: Refashioned:
"And can we say with confidence that these states also harbor many criminals -- rogues with stings whom the authorities must restrain by force?"

"We certainly can."

"And the existence of such criminals is due to lack of education, poor upbringing, and a corrupt constitution?"

"True."

"So those are the features and evils of oligarchy, though there are likely many others."

"Very likely."

"Then let's move on from oligarchy -- government by wealth -- and consider the nature and origin of the oligarchic individual."

"By all means."

"Doesn't the timocratic man transform into the oligarchic man in this way?"
D: Elegant: Oligarchy breeds criminals through poor education and corrupt institutions. Having cataloged its evils, Socrates transitions from the oligarchic state to the oligarchic individual, asking how the timocratic man transforms into the money-lover.
D: Summary: Socrates attributes oligarchy's criminal class to lack of education and corrupt institutions. Having surveyed the state's defects, he transitions to examining the oligarchic individual and how he develops from the timocratic type.
D: Distilled: Oligarchy breeds criminals through failed education; Socrates now turns to trace how the timocratic man becomes the oligarchic man.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Want of education, ill-training': Plato consistently traces social ills to educational failure rather than inherent human wickedness. -- The transition from state to individual follows the established methodological pattern. -- The transformation from timocratic to oligarchic man parallels the political transition: both involve the displacement of honor by wealth as the ruling value.

D: Original: How? A time arrives when the representative of timocracy has a son: at first he begins by emulating his father and walking in his footsteps, but presently he sees him of a sudden foundering against the State as upon a sunken reef, and he and all that he has is lost; he may have been a general or some other high officer who is brought to trial under a prejudice raised by informers, and either put to death, or exiled, or deprived of the privileges of a citizen, and all his property taken from him.
D: Narrative:
"How?"

"A time arrives when the representative of timocracy has a son: at first he begins by emulating his father and walking in his footsteps, but presently he sees him of a sudden foundering against the State as upon a sunken reef, and he and all that he has is lost; he may have been a general or some other high officer who is brought to trial under a prejudice raised by informers, and either put to death, or exiled, or deprived of the privileges of a citizen, and all his property taken from him."
D: Refashioned:
"How?"

"Eventually the timocratic man has a son. At first the boy admires his father and follows in his footsteps. But then he watches his father crash against the state like a ship on a hidden reef -- everything is lost. Perhaps the father was a general or high official who gets dragged into court on charges whipped up by informers. He's put to death, or exiled, or stripped of his citizenship, and all his property is confiscated."
D: Elegant: The oligarchic man begins as the son of a timocratic father who is destroyed by the state -- tried on false charges, exiled or executed, stripped of everything. The son's trauma reshapes his values.
D: Summary: The oligarchic man originates as the son of a timocratic father who is ruined by the state -- a general or official brought down by informers, tried, and stripped of property, citizenship, or life. This traumatic experience transforms the son.
D: Distilled: The oligarchic character is born when a timocratic father is destroyed by the state, teaching his son that honor is dangerous and only wealth provides security.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Foundering as upon a sunken reef': a vivid nautical metaphor for political ruin. -- Informers (sykophantai): a notorious feature of Athenian democratic politics. Professional accusers who brought charges for personal gain were widely despised. -- The trial and ruin of prominent citizens was common in Athens: Themistocles, Cimon, and later Socrates himself all faced prosecution. -- The generational mechanism: each character type is produced by the traumatic failure of the previous type's values.

D: Original: Nothing more likely. And the son has seen and known all this --he is a ruined man, and his fear has taught him to knock ambition and passion head-foremost from his bosom's throne; humbled by poverty he takes to money-making and by mean and miserly savings and hard work gets a fortune together. Is not such an one likely to seat the concupiscent and covetous element on the vacant throne and to suffer it to play the great king within him, girt with tiara and chain and scimitar?
D: Narrative:
"Nothing more likely."

"And the son has seen and known all this -- he is a ruined man, and his fear has taught him to knock ambition and passion head-foremost from his bosom's throne; humbled by poverty he takes to money-making and by mean and miserly savings and hard work gets a fortune together. Is not such an one likely to seat the concupiscent and covetous element on the vacant throne and to suffer it to play the great king within him, girt with tiara and chain and scimitar?"
D: Refashioned:
"Nothing more likely."

"The son has witnessed all of this -- he's a ruined man. Fear teaches him to overthrow ambition and passion from the throne within his soul. Humbled by poverty, he turns to money-making. Through miserly saving and relentless hard work, he builds a fortune. And isn't it natural that he would then seat the appetitive, covetous element on that empty throne, letting it play the great king inside him, decked out in royal tiara, golden chain, and sword?"
D: Elegant: Traumatized by his father's ruin, the son dethrones ambition and passion from his soul. Humbled by poverty, he devotes himself to money-making through miserly labor. The appetitive element -- greed -- takes the throne, ruling him like an Eastern despot adorned with tiara and scimitar.
D: Summary: The son, traumatized by his father's political destruction, overthrows ambition and spirit from his soul's throne. Driven by fear and poverty, he turns to money-making, and the appetitive, covetous element takes sovereign control of his inner life.
D: Distilled: Fear of his father's fate drives the son to dethrone ambition and enthrone greed as the ruling principle of his soul.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Knock ambition head-foremost from his bosom's throne': a violent metaphor for the internal regime change -- spirit (thumos) is overthrown by appetite (epithumia). -- 'Tiara and chain and scimitar': Persian royal insignia, suggesting that appetite rules as an Oriental despot -- absolute, luxurious, and arbitrary. -- The imagery of the Great King echoes Greek anti-Persian rhetoric and Herodotus. -- This is the second stage of degeneration: aristocracy -> timocracy (reason dethroned by spirit) -> oligarchy (spirit dethroned by appetite for wealth).

D: Original: Most true, he replied. And when he has made reason and spirit sit down on the ground obediently on either side of their sovereign, and taught them to know their place, he compels the one to think only of how lesser sums may be turned into larger ones, and will not allow the other to worship and admire anything but riches and rich men, or to be ambitious of anything so much as the acquisition of wealth and the means of acquiring it.
D: Narrative:
"Most true," he replied.

"And when he has made reason and spirit sit down on the ground obediently on either side of their sovereign, and taught them to know their place, he compels the one to think only of how lesser sums may be turned into larger ones, and will not allow the other to worship and admire anything but riches and rich men, or to be ambitious of anything so much as the acquisition of wealth and the means of acquiring it."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly right," he replied.

"Once appetite is enthroned, reason and spirit are made to sit obediently on the ground on either side of their new master. Reason is forced to calculate nothing but how to turn small sums into large ones. Spirit is forbidden to admire or pursue anything except wealth and wealthy people -- its only ambition becomes the acquisition of money and the means to get more."
D: Elegant: Under the oligarchic soul's new regime, reason becomes a mere accountant and spirit a worshipper of wealth. Both are enslaved to appetite, serving only the goal of accumulation.
D: Summary: In the oligarchic soul, reason and spirit are subordinated to the appetitive element. Reason is reduced to calculating profit, and spirit is redirected from honor to admiring wealth and wealthy people -- both serve only the goal of money-making.
D: Distilled: The oligarchic soul enslaves reason as an accountant and spirit as a wealth-worshipper, both serving appetite's drive to accumulate.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The image of reason and spirit sitting on the ground beside the enthroned appetite inverts the ideal arrangement from Book IV (441e-442a), where reason rules with spirit as its ally. -- Reason (logistikon) degraded to mere instrumental calculation: a key Platonic insight about how intelligence can serve base ends. -- Spirit (thumoeides) redirected from honor to wealth-worship: the timocratic love of glory becomes the oligarchic love of money. -- This passage anticipates Aristotle's distinction between practical wisdom (phronesis) and mere cleverness (deinotes) in Nicomachean Ethics VI.12.

D: Original:
Of all changes, he said, there is none so speedy or so sure as the conversion of the ambitious youth into the avaricious one.

And the avaricious, I said, is the oligarchical youth? Yes, he said; at any rate the individual out of whom he came is like the State out of which oligarchy came.

Let us then consider whether there is any likeness between them.

Very good. First, then, they resemble one another in the value which they set upon wealth?

Certainly. Also in their penurious, laborious character; the individual only satisfies his necessary appetites, and confines his expenditure to them; his other desires he subdues, under the idea that they are unprofitable.

True. He is a shabby fellow, who saves something out of everything and makes a purse for himself; and this is the sort of man whom the vulgar applaud. Is he not a true image of the State which he represents?

He appears to me to be so; at any rate money is highly valued by him as well as by the State.

You see that he is not a man of cultivation, I said. I imagine not, he said; had he been educated he would never have made a blind god director of his chorus, or given him chief honour.

Excellent! I said. Yet consider: Must we not further admit that owing to this want of cultivation there will be found in him dronelike desires as of pauper and rogue, which are forcibly kept down by his general habit of life?

True. Do you know where you will have to look if you want to discover his rogueries?

Where must I look? You should see him where he has some great opportunity of acting dishonestly, as in the guardianship of an orphan.

Aye. It will be clear enough then that in his ordinary dealings which give him a reputation for honesty he coerces his bad passions by an enforced virtue; not making them see that they are wrong, or taming them by reason, but by necessity and fear constraining them, and because he trembles for his possessions.
D: Narrative:
"Of all changes," he said, "there is none so speedy or so sure as the conversion of the ambitious youth into the avaricious one."

"And the avaricious," I said, "is the oligarchical youth?"

"Yes," he said; "at any rate the individual out of whom he came is like the State out of which oligarchy came."

"Let us then consider whether there is any likeness between them."

"Very good."

"First, then, they resemble one another in the value which they set upon wealth?"

"Certainly."

"Also in their penurious, laborious character; the individual only satisfies his necessary appetites, and confines his expenditure to them; his other desires he subdues, under the idea that they are unprofitable."

"True."

"He is a shabby fellow, who saves something out of everything and makes a purse for himself; and this is the sort of man whom the vulgar applaud. Is he not a true image of the State which he represents?"

"He appears to me to be so; at any rate money is highly valued by him as well as by the State."

"You see that he is not a man of cultivation," I said.

"I imagine not," he said; "had he been educated he would never have made a blind god director of his chorus, or given him chief honour."

"Excellent!" I said. "Yet consider: Must we not further admit that owing to this want of cultivation there will be found in him dronelike desires as of pauper and rogue, which are forcibly kept down by his general habit of life?"

"True."

"Do you know where you will have to look if you want to discover his rogueries?"

"Where must I look?"

"You should see him where he has some great opportunity of acting dishonestly, as in the guardianship of an orphan."

"Aye."

"It will be clear enough then that in his ordinary dealings which give him a reputation for honesty he coerces his bad passions by an enforced virtue; not making them see that they are wrong, or taming them by reason, but by necessity and fear constraining them, and because he trembles for his possessions."
D: Refashioned:
"Of all transformations," he said, "none is faster or more certain than the conversion of the ambitious youth into the greedy one."

"And the greedy one is the oligarchic youth?"

"Yes -- at least, the man he came from resembles the state that oligarchy came from."

"Then let's see if the oligarchic man resembles the oligarchic state."

"Very good."

"First, they both place supreme value on wealth?"

"Certainly."

"And both are stingy and hard-working. The oligarchic man satisfies only his necessary appetites and restricts his spending to bare essentials. All other desires he suppresses as unprofitable."

"True."

"He's a shabby fellow who pinches pennies on everything and builds up his savings -- and ordinary people actually admire this. Isn't he a perfect image of the state he represents?"

"He seems to be. Money is everything to both him and his state."

"You can see he's not a cultivated man," I said.

"Certainly not," he replied. "If he had real education, he'd never have made a blind god the director of his life's chorus or given him the place of highest honor."

"Well said! But consider: doesn't his lack of cultivation also mean he harbors dronelike desires -- the appetites of a pauper and a rogue -- which he forcibly suppresses through his general habits?"

"True."

"And do you know where to look if you want to see his hidden dishonesty?"

"Where?"

"Watch him when he has a major opportunity to cheat -- like serving as guardian of an orphan."

"Exactly."

"Then you'll see clearly that his everyday reputation for honesty is maintained not by genuine virtue but by force. He doesn't persuade his bad impulses that they're wrong or tame them through reason -- he restrains them through necessity and fear, trembling for his possessions."
D: Elegant: The oligarchic man mirrors his state: obsessed with wealth, stingy, hard-working, suppressing all non-essential desires. But his virtue is enforced, not genuine -- he restrains bad impulses through fear for his property, not through reason or understanding. Give him an opportunity like guardianship of an orphan, and his hidden roguery emerges. He has made Plutus, the blind god of wealth, the director of his life.
D: Summary: The oligarchic individual mirrors the oligarchic state in valuing wealth above all else. He is stingy, hard-working, and suppresses unnecessary desires -- but his apparent virtue is maintained by fear, not reason. When given opportunities for dishonesty, like managing an orphan's estate, his suppressed roguish impulses surface.
D: Distilled: The oligarchic man's virtue is counterfeit -- maintained by fear of losing wealth, not by rational understanding; given the chance, his suppressed desires emerge as dishonesty.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Blind god director of his chorus': Plutus (Wealth) was traditionally depicted as blind -- wealth is distributed without regard to merit. Making him choregus (chorus director, a prestigious civic role) means giving wealth the highest honor. Aristophanes' play Plutus explores similar themes. -- 'Guardianship of an orphan': a proverbial test of honesty in Athens, where orphans' estates were frequently embezzled by guardians. See Demosthenes' speeches against his guardians. -- 'Enforced virtue' vs. genuine virtue: a crucial Platonic distinction. True virtue comes from rational understanding (Book IV, 441e-442b); the oligarchic man's restraint is merely prudential fear. -- The dronelike desires kept forcibly down will eventually break free in the democratic son.

D: Original:
To be sure. Yes, indeed, my dear friend, but you will find that the natural desires of the drone commonly exist in him all the same whenever he has to spend what is not his own.

Yes, and they will be strong in him too. The man, then, will be at war with himself; he will be two men, and not one; but, in general, his better desires will be found to prevail over his inferior ones.

True. For these reasons such an one will be more respectable than most people; yet the true virtue of a unanimous and harmonious soul will flee far away and never come near him.
D: Narrative:
"To be sure."

"Yes, indeed, my dear friend, but you will find that the natural desires of the drone commonly exist in him all the same whenever he has to spend what is not his own."

"Yes, and they will be strong in him too."

"The man, then, will be at war with himself; he will be two men, and not one; but, in general, his better desires will be found to prevail over his inferior ones."

"True."

"For these reasons such an one will be more respectable than most people; yet the true virtue of a unanimous and harmonious soul will flee far away and never come near him."
D: Refashioned:
"To be sure."

"But you'll find that whenever he gets to spend other people's money, the drone's natural desires come alive in him."

"And they'll be strong."

"So this man is at war with himself -- he's two men, not one. Generally, his better impulses prevail over the worse ones."

"True."

"For this reason he'll seem more respectable than most people. But the true virtue that comes from a unified, harmonious soul will never come anywhere near him."
D: Elegant: The oligarchic man is internally divided -- respectable on the surface, but at war with himself. His better impulses usually win, but true virtue, which requires a harmonious soul, remains forever beyond his reach.
D: Summary: The oligarchic man is at war with himself -- a divided soul where better impulses usually prevail over worse ones. He appears respectable, but genuine virtue, which requires inner harmony, is permanently out of reach.
D: Distilled: The oligarchic man is internally divided and superficially respectable, but true virtue -- which requires inner harmony -- is beyond him.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Two men, not one': the internal division (stasis) of the oligarchic soul mirrors the class division of the oligarchic state (8.22). -- 'Unanimous and harmonious soul': the just soul described in Book IV (443c-e), where all three parts agree on who should rule. -- 'Better desires prevail over inferior ones': a qualified good -- the oligarchic man is better than the democratic or tyrannical man, but his self-control is prudential rather than principled. -- The theme of inner warfare anticipates the democratic man's complete loss of internal order (8.44-8.46).

D: Original: I should expect so. And surely, the miser individually will be an ignoble competitor in a State for any prize of victory, or other object of honourable ambition; he will not spend his money in the contest for glory; so afraid is he of awakening his expensive appetites and inviting them to help and join in the struggle; in true oligarchical fashion he fights with a small part only of his resources, and the result commonly is that he loses the prize and saves his money.
D: Narrative: "And surely, the miser individually will be an ignoble competitor in a State for any prize of victory, or other object of honourable ambition; he will not spend his money in the contest for glory; so afraid is he of awakening his expensive appetites and inviting them to help and join in the struggle; in true oligarchical fashion he fights with a small part only of his resources, and the result commonly is that he loses the prize and saves his money."
D: Refashioned: "And the miser will be a poor competitor for any civic prize or honor. He won't spend money pursuing glory -- he's terrified of awakening his expensive desires and recruiting them into the contest. True to his oligarchic nature, he fights with only a fraction of his resources. The usual result? He loses the prize but keeps his money."
D: Elegant: The oligarchic man competes half-heartedly, unwilling to spend on glory for fear of awakening his expensive desires. He fights with a fraction of his resources and typically loses the prize while saving his money.
D: Summary: The oligarchic individual is a poor competitor for public honors because he refuses to spend money in pursuit of glory, deploying only a fraction of his resources. He typically loses the prize but keeps his wealth intact.
D: Distilled: The miser competes half-heartedly and loses the prize but saves his money -- the oligarchic pattern of fighting with minimal resources.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The miser's fear of 'awakening expensive appetites' reveals the precariousness of his self-control: his repressed desires are not tamed, merely suppressed. Any breach threatens total collapse. -- 'Fights with a small part only': mirrors oligarchy's military weakness (8.23) -- too few resources deployed because the rest must be hoarded. -- The parallel between personal stinginess and political weakness reinforces the state-soul analogy. -- This foreshadows the oligarchic man's inability to resist his son's democratic transformation.

D: Original:
Very true. Can we any longer doubt, then, that the miser and money-maker answers to the oligarchical State?

There can be no doubt. Next comes democracy; of this the origin and nature have still to be considered by us; and then we will enquire into the ways of the democratic man, and bring him up for judgement.

That, he said, is our method. Well, I said, and how does the change from oligarchy into democracy arise? Is it not on this wise? --The good at which such a State alms is to become as rich as possible, a desire which is insatiable?

What then? The rulers, being aware that their power rests upon their wealth, refuse to curtail by law the extravagance of the spendthrift youth because they gain by their ruin; they take interest from them and buy up their estates and thus increase their own wealth and importance?

To be sure. There can be no doubt that the love of wealth and the spirit of moderation cannot exist together in citizens of the same State to any considerable extent; one or the other will be disregarded.

That is tolerably clear. And in oligarchical States, from the general spread of carelessness and extravagance, men of good family have often been reduced to beggary?

Yes, often. And still they remain in the city; there they are, ready to sting and fully armed, and some of them owe money, some have forfeited their citizenship; a third class are in both predicaments; and they hate and conspire against those who have got their property, and against everybody else, and are eager for revolution.

That is true. On the other hand, the men of business, stooping as they walk, and pretending not even to see those whom they have already ruined, insert their sting --that is, their money --into some one else who is not on his guard against them, and recover the parent sum many times over multiplied into a family of children: and so they make drone and pauper to abound in the State.

Yes, he said, there are plenty of them --that is certain.
D: Narrative:
"Can we any longer doubt, then, that the miser and money-maker answers to the oligarchical State?"

"There can be no doubt."

"Next comes democracy; of this the origin and nature have still to be considered by us; and then we will enquire into the ways of the democratic man, and bring him up for judgement."

"That," he said, "is our method."

"Well," I said, "and how does the change from oligarchy into democracy arise? Is it not on this wise? -- The good at which such a State aims is to become as rich as possible, a desire which is insatiable?"

"What then?"

"The rulers, being aware that their power rests upon their wealth, refuse to curtail by law the extravagance of the spendthrift youth because they gain by their ruin; they take interest from them and buy up their estates and thus increase their own wealth and importance?"

"To be sure."

"There can be no doubt that the love of wealth and the spirit of moderation cannot exist together in citizens of the same State to any considerable extent; one or the other will be disregarded."

"That is tolerably clear."

"And in oligarchical States, from the general spread of carelessness and extravagance, men of good family have often been reduced to beggary?"

"Yes, often."

"And still they remain in the city; there they are, ready to sting and fully armed, and some of them owe money, some have forfeited their citizenship; a third class are in both predicaments; and they hate and conspire against those who have got their property, and against everybody else, and are eager for revolution."

"That is true."

"On the other hand, the men of business, stooping as they walk, and pretending not even to see those whom they have already ruined, insert their sting -- that is, their money -- into some one else who is not on his guard against them, and recover the parent sum many times over multiplied into a family of children: and so they make drone and pauper to abound in the State."

"Yes," he said, "there are plenty of them -- that is certain."
D: Refashioned:
"Can we still doubt that the miser corresponds to the oligarchic state?"

"No doubt at all."

"Next comes democracy. We need to examine its origin and nature, then study the democratic man and bring him before our tribunal."

"That's our method."

"How does oligarchy transform into democracy? Doesn't it work like this? The state's sole aim is maximum wealth -- an insatiable desire."

"What then?"

"The rulers, knowing their power rests on wealth, refuse to pass laws restraining the extravagance of reckless young spendthrifts -- because they profit from their ruin. They charge them interest, buy up their estates, and grow richer and more powerful."

"To be sure."

"The love of wealth and the spirit of moderation can't coexist in the same state. One must be sacrificed to the other."

"That's clear enough."

"And in oligarchies, through widespread carelessness and extravagance, well-born men are regularly reduced to poverty?"

"Often."

"Yet they stay in the city -- stinging drones, fully armed. Some are in debt, some have lost their citizenship, some both. They hate the people who took their property, they hate everyone else, and they hunger for revolution."

"True."

"Meanwhile, the moneyed men walk around hunched over, pretending not to see the people they've ruined. They inject their sting -- their money -- into anyone who lets their guard down, recovering the original investment many times over in compounding interest. And so they fill the state with drones and paupers."

"Yes, there are plenty of them."
D: Elegant: Oligarchy's insatiable greed destroys itself. Rulers profit from the ruin of spendthrift youth, buying their estates and charging interest. The dispossessed remain in the city as armed, embittered drones eager for revolution. Meanwhile, moneylenders create ever more paupers through usurious lending. The state fills with drones until revolution becomes inevitable.
D: Summary: Oligarchy degenerates into democracy through its own greed. Rulers profit from ruining spendthrift citizens through usurious lending, creating a growing class of dispossessed, embittered men who remain in the city, armed and hungry for revolution. The love of wealth and moderation cannot coexist.
D: Distilled: Oligarchy destroys itself as rulers profit from ruining citizens through usury, creating an armed, embittered underclass that eventually revolts into democracy.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The usury mechanism: oligarchs deliberately refuse to regulate lending because they profit from the cycle of debt, dispossession, and estate acquisition. -- 'Insert their sting -- that is, their money': the stinging drone metaphor applied to moneylenders. -- 'Parent sum multiplied into a family of children': interest (tokos in Greek literally means 'offspring') breeds like living things -- a metaphor Aristotle uses in Politics I.10 to condemn usury. -- The dispossessed armed class ready for revolution: this is the immediate cause of democratic revolution. -- Historical parallels: Solon's seisachtheia (debt relief) in Athens c. 594 BCE addressed exactly this crisis.

D: Original:
The evil blazes up like a fire; and they will not extinguish it, either by restricting a man's use of his own property, or by another remedy:

What other? One which is the next best, and has the advantage of compelling the citizens to look to their characters: --Let there be a general rule that every one shall enter into voluntary contracts at his own risk, and there will be less of this scandalous money-making, and the evils of which we were speaking will be greatly lessened in the State.

Yes, they will be greatly lessened. At present the governors, induced by the motives which I have named, treat their subjects badly; while they and their adherents, especially the young men of the governing class, are habituated to lead a life of luxury and idleness both of body and mind; they do nothing, and are incapable of resisting either pleasure or pain.

Very true. They themselves care only for making money, and are as indifferent as the pauper to the cultivation of virtue.
D: Narrative:
"The evil blazes up like a fire; and they will not extinguish it, either by restricting a man's use of his own property, or by another remedy:"

"What other?"

"One which is the next best, and has the advantage of compelling the citizens to look to their characters: -- Let there be a general rule that every one shall enter into voluntary contracts at his own risk, and there will be less of this scandalous money-making, and the evils of which we were speaking will be greatly lessened in the State."

"Yes, they will be greatly lessened."

"At present the governors, induced by the motives which I have named, treat their subjects badly; while they and their adherents, especially the young men of the governing class, are habituated to lead a life of luxury and idleness both of body and mind; they do nothing, and are incapable of resisting either pleasure or pain."

"Very true."

"They themselves care only for making money, and are as indifferent as the pauper to the cultivation of virtue."
D: Refashioned:
"The evil blazes up like a fire, and the oligarchs won't put it out -- not by restricting what people do with their property, nor by any other remedy."

"What other remedy?"

"The next best option: a rule that all voluntary contracts are entered at the parties' own risk. This would force citizens to evaluate each other's character before lending, which would reduce predatory lending and greatly diminish the state's problems."

"Yes, that would help considerably."

"But as things stand, the rulers treat the people badly for the reasons I described. Meanwhile, they and their followers -- especially the young men of the ruling class -- live lives of luxury and total idleness, both physical and mental. They do nothing and can't resist either pleasure or pain."

"Very true."

"They care about nothing except making money, and they're just as indifferent to cultivating virtue as the paupers are."
D: Elegant: The oligarchic crisis blazes unchecked. A reform requiring that all contracts be entered at the parties' own risk would force character assessment and reduce predatory lending -- but the rulers refuse. They and their idle, luxurious heirs care only about money and are as indifferent to virtue as the paupers they create.
D: Summary: Socrates suggests that requiring all contracts to be entered at the lender's own risk would reduce predatory money-making, but oligarchic rulers refuse all reform. They and their idle heirs live in luxury, incapable of resisting pleasure or pain, indifferent to virtue -- as indifferent as the paupers below them.
D: Distilled: Oligarchy refuses reform while its rulers and heirs grow idle and indifferent to virtue, caring only about money -- mirroring the paupers they create.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The 'caveat emptor' reform: making lenders bear the risk of their own loans would incentivize character assessment and reduce reckless lending. This anticipates modern discussions of moral hazard. -- 'Luxury and idleness both of body and mind': the oligarchic elite's softness makes them vulnerable to overthrow -- they cannot resist pain (military weakness) or pleasure (moral weakness). -- 'As indifferent as the pauper to virtue': rich and poor converge in their lack of virtue, though for different reasons. -- The groundwork for revolution: when the rulers become soft and the dispossessed become numerous and armed, the transition to democracy is imminent.

D: Original:
Yes, quite as indifferent. Such is the state of affairs which prevails among them. And often rulers and their subjects may come in one another's way, whether on a pilgrimage or a march, as fellow-soldiers or fellow-sailors; aye, and they may observe the behaviour of each other in the very moment of danger --for where danger is, there is no fear that the poor will be despised by the rich --and very likely the wiry sunburnt poor man may be placed in battle at the side of a wealthy one who has never spoilt his complexion and has plenty of superfluous flesh --when he sees such an one puffing and at his wit's end, how can he avoid drawing the conclusion that men like him are only rich because no one has the courage to despoil them? And when they meet in private will not people be saying to one another 'Our warriors are not good for much'?

Yes, he said, I am quite aware that this is their way of talking.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, quite as indifferent."

"Such is the state of affairs which prevails among them. And often rulers and their subjects may come in one another's way, whether on a pilgrimage or a march, as fellow-soldiers or fellow-sailors; aye, and they may observe the behaviour of each other in the very moment of danger -- for where danger is, there is no fear that the poor will be despised by the rich -- and very likely the wiry sunburnt poor man may be placed in battle at the side of a wealthy one who has never spoilt his complexion and has plenty of superfluous flesh -- when he sees such an one puffing and at his wit's end, how can he avoid drawing the conclusion that men like him are only rich because no one has the courage to despoil them? And when they meet in private will not people be saying to one another 'Our warriors are not good for much'?"

"Yes," he said, "I am quite aware that this is their way of talking."
D: Refashioned:
"Quite as indifferent."

"This is the state of affairs. And sometimes rulers and subjects find themselves side by side -- on a march, a voyage, or a military campaign. In moments of danger, the poor won't be intimidated by the rich. Imagine the lean, sun-hardened poor man standing in battle next to a wealthy, pale, overweight man who has never missed a meal. When the poor man watches the rich man gasping and helpless, how can he avoid concluding that such people are only rich because no one has had the nerve to take their wealth? And when the poor meet privately, won't they say to each other, 'These warriors of ours are worthless'?"

"Yes," he said, "I know very well that's what they say."
D: Elegant: When poor and rich stand side by side in battle, the contrast is devastating. The lean, hardened poor man watches the soft, breathless rich man and realizes: these people are only wealthy because no one has dared to take what they have. The conclusion is inevitable -- revolution becomes thinkable.
D: Summary: When rich and poor serve together in military campaigns, the poor discover the physical weakness of the wealthy. Seeing soft, gasping rich men beside lean, hardened poor men, the poor conclude that the rich are only wealthy because no one has had the courage to overthrow them -- a revolutionary realization.
D: Distilled: Shared military service reveals the rich as physically weak, leading the poor to realize that wealth is maintained only by their own passivity.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The physical contrast between rich and poor soldiers is vivid and psychologically acute: the moment the poor stop fearing the rich, revolution becomes possible. -- 'Wiry sunburnt poor man' vs. the pale, overweight rich man: physical condition as class marker -- laborers are tanned, aristocrats are pale. -- This mechanism of revolutionary consciousness -- the governed discovering the weakness of the governors -- recurs throughout political theory. Compare Machiavelli, Discourses III.6. -- The passage echoes Thersites' challenge to the Greek aristocracy in Iliad II.211-277.

D: Original: And, as in a body which is diseased the addition of a touch from without may bring on illness, and sometimes even when there is no external provocation a commotion may arise within-in the same way wherever there is weakness in the State there is also likely to be illness, of which the occasions may be very slight, the one party introducing from without their oligarchical, the other their democratical allies, and then the State falls sick, and is at war with herself; and may be at times distracted, even when there is no external cause.
D: Narrative: "And, as in a body which is diseased the addition of a touch from without may bring on illness, and sometimes even when there is no external provocation a commotion may arise within -- in the same way wherever there is weakness in the State there is also likely to be illness, of which the occasions may be very slight, the one party introducing from without their oligarchical, the other their democratical allies, and then the State falls sick, and is at war with herself; and may be at times distracted, even when there is no external cause."
D: Refashioned: "Just as a diseased body can be pushed into illness by the slightest external contact -- or even break down from within with no external trigger at all -- so too a weakened state is vulnerable to crisis from the smallest provocation. One faction brings in oligarchic allies from outside, the other brings in democratic allies, and the state falls into civil war. Sometimes it tears itself apart even without any outside interference."
D: Elegant: A weakened state, like a diseased body, can be tipped into crisis by the slightest touch -- or collapse from within without any external cause. Factions invite outside allies, and civil war erupts.
D: Summary: Like a diseased body that can fall ill from the slightest contact or even from internal disturbance alone, a weakened state is vulnerable to civil war. Opposing factions invite foreign allies -- oligarchic or democratic -- and the state tears itself apart, sometimes without any external provocation.
D: Distilled: A weakened state, like a diseased body, can collapse from the slightest provocation or from purely internal causes, erupting into civil war.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The body-politic analogy: disease of the state as disease of the body is a persistent Platonic metaphor. -- External intervention in civil conflicts: a common pattern in Greek politics. Sparta and Athens routinely supported oligarchic and democratic factions in other cities, respectively. -- The Peloponnesian War (431-404 BCE) was largely fought along these factional lines; see Thucydides III.82-83 on stasis in Corcyra. -- 'Even when there is no external cause': internal contradictions alone can destroy a state -- a key insight of Plato's political theory.

D: Original:
Yes, surely. And then democracy comes into being after the poor have conquered their opponents, slaughtering some and banishing some, while to the remainder they give an equal share of freedom and power; and this is the form of government in which the magistrates are commonly elected by lot.

Yes, he said, that is the nature of democracy, whether the revolution has been effected by arms, or whether fear has caused the opposite party to withdraw.

And now what is their manner of life, and what sort of a government have they? for as the government is, such will be the man.

Clearly, he said. In the first place, are they not free; and is not the city full of freedom and frankness --a man may say and do what he likes?

'Tis said so, he replied. And where freedom is, the individual is clearly able to order for himself his own life as he pleases?

Clearly. Then in this kind of State there will be the greatest variety of human natures?

There will. This, then, seems likely to be the fairest of States, being an embroidered robe which is spangled with every sort of flower. And just as women and children think a variety of colours to be of all things most charming, so there are many men to whom this State, which is spangled with the manners and characters of mankind, will appear to be the fairest of States.

Yes. Yes, my good Sir, and there will be no better in which to look for a government.

Why? Because of the liberty which reigns there --they have a complete assortment of constitutions; and he who has a mind to establish a State, as we have been doing, must go to a democracy as he would to a bazaar at which they sell them, and pick out the one that suits him; then, when he has made his choice, he may found his State.

"Yes, surely."

"Yes," he said, "that is the nature of democracy, whether the revolution has been effected by arms, or whether fear has caused the opposite party to withdraw."

"And now what is their manner of life, and what sort of a government have they? for as the government is, such will be the man."

"Clearly," he said.

"In the first place, are they not free; and is not the city full of freedom and frankness -- a man may say and do what he likes?"

"'Tis said so," he replied.

"And where freedom is, the individual is clearly able to order for himself his own life as he pleases?"

"Clearly."

"Then in this kind of State there will be the greatest variety of human natures?"

"There will."

"This, then, seems likely to be the fairest of States, being an embroidered robe which is spangled with every sort of flower. And just as women and children think a variety of colours to be of all things most charming, so there are many men to whom this State, which is spangled with the manners and characters of mankind, will appear to be the fairest of States."

"Yes."

"Yes, my good Sir, and there will be no better in which to look for a government."

"Why?"

"Because of the liberty which reigns there -- they have a complete assortment of constitutions; and he who has a mind to establish a State, as we have been doing, must go to a democracy as he would to a bazaar at which they sell them, and pick out the one that suits him; then, when he has made his choice, he may found his State."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, surely."

"Democracy comes into being after the poor defeat their opponents -- killing some, banishing others, and giving equal shares of freedom and power to those who remain. In this system, public officials are typically chosen by lottery."

"Yes, that's the nature of democracy, whether the revolution was violent or the ruling class withdrew out of fear."

"What is their way of life, and what kind of government do they have? As the government is, so will the man be."

"Clearly."

"First, are they not free? Isn't the city overflowing with freedom and openness -- everyone saying and doing whatever they like?"

"So they say."

"And where freedom reigns, each individual can arrange his life however he pleases?"

"Clearly."

"Then this state will contain the greatest variety of human types?"

"It will."

"This might seem the most beautiful of states -- like an embroidered robe decorated with every kind of flower. Just as women and children find a riot of colors the most charming thing in the world, many people will think this state, decorated with every type of human character, is the most beautiful of all."

"Yes."
