"And there's no better place to go shopping for a constitution."

"Why?"

"Because of the freedom that reigns there -- they have a complete inventory of every kind of government. Anyone who wants to found a state, as we've been doing, should go to a democracy like a bazaar, browse the selection, and pick whichever constitution suits him."
D: Elegant: Democracy is born when the poor overthrow the oligarchs, distributing equal freedom and power and selecting officials by lottery. The result is maximum freedom and variety -- a state like a colorful embroidered robe, containing every type of human nature. It's a bazaar of constitutions: beautiful to behold, but is beauty the same as goodness?
D: Summary: Democracy emerges when the poor defeat the oligarchs and distribute equal freedom and power, with officials chosen by lottery. The result is maximum freedom and diversity -- every human type flourishes. Socrates compares it to a colorful embroidered robe and a bazaar of constitutions, noting its superficial beauty while hinting at deeper problems.
D: Distilled: Democracy arises from the poor's victory over oligarchs, producing maximum freedom and variety -- beautiful like a colorful robe, but beauty is not virtue.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- Election by lot (sortition): the characteristically Athenian democratic practice, as opposed to election by vote (which Aristotle considers oligarchic). -- 'Embroidered robe spangled with flowers': the imagery is deliberately seductive -- democracy's variety is aesthetically appealing but philosophically dangerous. -- 'Women and children' find variety charming: a pointed comparison suggesting that democratic taste is immature. -- The 'bazaar of constitutions': democracy's tolerance contains all possibilities, including its own destruction. -- Socrates' tone is ironic throughout -- he praises democracy's beauty while systematically undermining its claim to goodness.

D: Original:
He will be sure to have patterns enough. And there being no necessity, I said, for you to govern in this State, even if you have the capacity, or to be governed, unless you like, or go to war when the rest go to war, or to be at peace when others are at peace, unless you are so disposed --there being no necessity also, because some law forbids you to hold office or be a dicast, that you should not hold office or be a dicast, if you have a fancy --is not this a way of life which for the moment is supremely delightful

For the moment, yes. And is not their humanity to the condemned in some cases quite charming? Have you not observed how, in a democracy, many persons, although they have been sentenced to death or exile, just stay where they are and walk about the world --the gentleman parades like a hero, and nobody sees or cares?
D: Narrative:
"He will be sure to have patterns enough."

"And there being no necessity," I said, "for you to govern in this State, even if you have the capacity, or to be governed, unless you like, or go to war when the rest go to war, or to be at peace when others are at peace, unless you are so disposed -- there being no necessity also, because some law forbids you to hold office or be a dicast, that you should not hold office or be a dicast, if you have a fancy -- is not this a way of life which for the moment is supremely delightful?"

"For the moment, yes."

"And is not their humanity to the condemned in some cases quite charming? Have you not observed how, in a democracy, many persons, although they have been sentenced to death or exile, just stay where they are and walk about the world -- the gentleman parades like a hero, and nobody sees or cares?"
D: Refashioned:
"He'll have plenty of options to choose from."

"And consider this: in a democracy, there's no obligation to govern even if you're capable, or to be governed if you don't feel like it. You don't have to fight when others fight, or keep the peace when others do. Even if a law bars you from holding office or serving on a jury, you can go ahead and do it anyway if you feel like it. Isn't this way of life, at least for the moment, utterly delightful?"

"For the moment, yes."

"And isn't their leniency toward the condemned rather charming? Haven't you noticed that in a democracy, people who have been sentenced to death or exile simply stay put and walk around freely? The condemned man strolls about like a hero, and nobody notices or cares."
D: Elegant: Democracy is delightful in the moment: no obligation to govern, fight, or obey. Even the condemned walk free -- sentenced to death or exile, they stroll about unbothered. Charming, perhaps -- but what does this leniency reveal about the state's relationship to its own laws?
D: Summary: In a democracy, no one is compelled to govern, be governed, fight, or obey. Even people sentenced to death or exile simply walk about freely, ignored by everyone. Socrates calls this way of life supremely delightful 'for the moment' -- an ironic qualification hinting at its unsustainability.
D: Distilled: Democracy offers delightful freedom from all obligation, where even the condemned walk free -- charming but unsustainable.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'For the moment': Adeimantus' qualification is telling -- democratic pleasure is temporary. -- The condemned walking free: Socrates may be thinking of his own situation -- he was later condemned to death and famously refused to flee, unlike the democratic pattern described here. -- 'Nobody sees or cares': democratic indifference to law enforcement reflects a deeper indifference to justice. -- The passage combines genuine appreciation for democratic freedom with systematic irony about its consequences. -- Dicast (dikastes): a member of the Athenian jury-courts, a key democratic institution.

D: Original: Yes, he replied, many and many a one. See too, I said, the forgiving spirit of democracy, and the 'don't care' about trifles, and the disregard which she shows of all the fine principles which we solemnly laid down at the foundation of the city --as when we said that, except in the case of some rarely gifted nature, there never will be a good man who has not from his childhood been used to play amid things of beauty and make of them a joy and a study --how grandly does she trample all these fine notions of ours under her feet, never giving a thought to the pursuits which make a statesman, and promoting to honour any one who professes to be the people's friend.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he replied, "many and many a one."

"See too," I said, "the forgiving spirit of democracy, and the 'don't care' about trifles, and the disregard which she shows of all the fine principles which we solemnly laid down at the foundation of the city -- as when we said that, except in the case of some rarely gifted nature, there never will be a good man who has not from his childhood been used to play amid things of beauty and make of them a joy and a study -- how grandly does she trample all these fine notions of ours under her feet, never giving a thought to the pursuits which make a statesman, and promoting to honour any one who professes to be the people's friend."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," he replied, "many of them."

"And notice democracy's forgiving spirit -- her casual 'who cares?' attitude, her complete disregard for all the principles we so carefully established when founding our ideal city. We said that no one could become truly good without being raised from childhood surrounded by beauty, making it both a source of joy and a subject of study. Democracy tramples all these fine notions magnificently underfoot, never considering what training makes a good statesman. She promotes to honor anyone who simply claims to be a friend of the people."
D: Elegant: Democracy cheerfully tramples every principle of careful education and philosophical formation. She doesn't care what training produces a statesman -- she honors anyone who claims to be the people's friend, regardless of qualifications.
D: Summary: Socrates notes democracy's 'forgiving spirit' and casual disregard for the educational principles they established -- that good men must be raised amid beauty and careful study. Democracy ignores what makes a true statesman and promotes anyone who professes to be the people's friend.
D: Distilled: Democracy tramples educational principles and promotes anyone who claims to be the people's friend, regardless of qualifications or character.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Played amid things of beauty': references the educational program of Books II-III (401c-402a), where children are surrounded by beautiful art, music, and environment to shape their souls. -- 'Friend of the people' (demagogos): literally 'leader of the demos,' the term quickly acquired negative connotations of flattery and manipulation. -- Democracy personified as feminine ('she') is characteristic of Greek political discourse. -- The ironic tone is sharp: 'how grandly does she trample' -- Socrates admires democracy's magnificent indifference to philosophy. -- This passage directly connects Book VIII's critique to the educational foundations laid in Books II-III.

D: Original:
Yes, she is of a noble spirit. These and other kindred characteristics are proper to democracy, which is a charming form of government, full of variety and disorder, and dispensing a sort of equality to equals and unequals alike.

We know her well. Consider now, I said, what manner of man the individual is, or rather consider, as in the case of the State, how he comes into being.

Very good, he said. Is not this the way --he is the son of the miserly and oligarchical father who has trained him in his own habits?

Exactly. And, like his father, he keeps under by force the pleasures which are of the spending and not of the getting sort, being those which are called unnecessary?

Obviously. Would you like, for the sake of clearness, to distinguish which are the necessary and which are the unnecessary pleasures?

I should. Are not necessary pleasures those of which we cannot get rid, and of which the satisfaction is a benefit to us? And they are rightly so, because we are framed by nature to desire both what is beneficial and what is necessary, and cannot help it.

True. We are not wrong therefore in calling them necessary? We are not. And the desires of which a man may get rid, if he takes pains from his youth upwards --of which the presence, moreover, does no good, and in some cases the reverse of good --shall we not be right in saying that all these are unnecessary?

Yes, certainly. Suppose we select an example of either kind, in order that we may have a general notion of them?

Very good. Will not the desire of eating, that is, of simple food and condiments, in so far as they are required for health and strength, be of the necessary class?

That is what I should suppose. The pleasure of eating is necessary in two ways; it does us good and it is essential to the continuance of life?

Yes. But the condiments are only necessary in so far as they are good for health?

Certainly. And the desire which goes beyond this, or more delicate food, or other luxuries, which might generally be got rid of, if controlled and trained in youth, and is hurtful to the body, and hurtful to the soul in the pursuit of wisdom and virtue, may be rightly called unnecessary?
D: Narrative:
"Yes, she is of a noble spirit."

"These and other kindred characteristics are proper to democracy, which is a charming form of government, full of variety and disorder, and dispensing a sort of equality to equals and unequals alike."

"We know her well."

"Consider now," I said, "what manner of man the individual is, or rather consider, as in the case of the State, how he comes into being."

"Very good," he said.

"Is not this the way -- he is the son of the miserly and oligarchical father who has trained him in his own habits?"

"Exactly."

"And, like his father, he keeps under by force the pleasures which are of the spending and not of the getting sort, being those which are called unnecessary?"

"Obviously."

"Would you like, for the sake of clearness, to distinguish which are the necessary and which are the unnecessary pleasures?"

"I should."

"Are not necessary pleasures those of which we cannot get rid, and of which the satisfaction is a benefit to us? And they are rightly so, because we are framed by nature to desire both what is beneficial and what is necessary, and cannot help it."

"True."

"We are not wrong therefore in calling them necessary?"

"We are not."

"And the desires of which a man may get rid, if he takes pains from his youth upwards -- of which the presence, moreover, does no good, and in some cases the reverse of good -- shall we not be right in saying that all these are unnecessary?"

"Yes, certainly."

"Suppose we select an example of either kind, in order that we may have a general notion of them?"

"Very good."

"Will not the desire of eating, that is, of simple food and condiments, in so far as they are required for health and strength, be of the necessary class?"

"That is what I should suppose."

"The pleasure of eating is necessary in two ways; it does us good and it is essential to the continuance of life?"

"Yes."

"But the condiments are only necessary in so far as they are good for health?"

"Certainly."

"And the desire which goes beyond this, or more delicate food, or other luxuries, which might generally be got rid of, if controlled and trained in youth, and is hurtful to the body, and hurtful to the soul in the pursuit of wisdom and virtue, may be rightly called unnecessary?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, she has a noble spirit indeed."

"These are democracy's defining traits: a charming form of government, full of variety and disorder, distributing a kind of equality to equals and unequals alike."

"We know her well."

"Now let's consider the democratic individual. How does he come into being?"

"Very good."

"He's the son of the miserly, oligarchic father who raised him according to his own habits?"

"Exactly."

"And like his father, he forcibly suppresses the spending pleasures -- the ones called unnecessary?"

"Obviously."

"Should we clarify the distinction between necessary and unnecessary pleasures?"

"We should."

"Necessary pleasures are those we can't eliminate and whose satisfaction actually benefits us. We're built by nature to desire what is both beneficial and necessary."

"True."

"So we're right to call them necessary?"

"We are."

"And desires that can be trained away from youth, whose presence does no good -- or actively does harm -- these are properly called unnecessary?"

"Certainly."

"Let's take an example of each to make this concrete."

"Good idea."

"The desire for simple food, insofar as it sustains health and strength -- that's necessary?"

"I'd say so."

"Eating is necessary in two ways: it benefits us and it's essential for survival."

"Yes."

"And seasoning is necessary only insofar as it contributes to health?"

"Certainly."

"But the desire for elaborate cuisine and luxuries beyond this -- desires that could be eliminated through early training, and that harm both body and soul in the pursuit of wisdom -- these are rightly called unnecessary?"
D: Elegant: Democracy distributes equality to equals and unequals alike -- charming, varied, disordered. The democratic man originates as the oligarchic father's son. To understand him, Socrates distinguishes necessary pleasures (which sustain life and health and cannot be eliminated) from unnecessary ones (which can be trained away and harm both body and soul). Simple food is necessary; luxury cuisine is not.
D: Summary: Socrates characterizes democracy as charming but disordered, distributing equality indiscriminately. Before examining the democratic individual, he draws a crucial distinction between necessary pleasures (those essential for life and health) and unnecessary pleasures (those that can be eliminated through discipline and harm the pursuit of wisdom).
D: Distilled: Democracy gives equality to equals and unequals alike; understanding the democratic man requires distinguishing necessary pleasures from unnecessary ones that harm body and soul.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Equality to equals and unequals alike': a pointed critique -- true justice requires proportional equality (treating unequals unequally), not arithmetic equality. See Aristotle, Politics III.9 and Nicomachean Ethics V.3. -- The necessary/unnecessary pleasure distinction is foundational for the rest of Book VIII and Book IX. Necessary desires: food, shelter, basic needs. Unnecessary desires: luxury, excess, refinement. -- The food example grounds abstract categories in concrete experience. -- 'Hurtful to the soul in the pursuit of wisdom': unnecessary pleasures are not merely bodily indulgences but obstacles to philosophical development. -- This distinction will become the mechanism of the democratic man's formation: his father suppresses all unnecessary desires; the son embraces them.

D: Original:
Very true. May we not say that these desires spend, and that the others make money because they conduce to production?

Certainly. And of the pleasures of love, and all other pleasures, the same holds good?

True. And the drone of whom we spoke was he who was surfeited in pleasures and desires of this sort, and was the slave of the unnecessary desires, whereas he who was subject o the necessary only was miserly and oligarchical?

Very true. Again, let us see how the democratical man grows out of the oligarchical: the following, as I suspect, is commonly the process.

What is the process? When a young man who has been brought up as we were just now describing, in a vulgar and miserly way, has tasted drones' honey and has come to associate with fierce and crafty natures who are able to provide for him all sorts of refinements and varieties of pleasure --then, as you may imagine, the change will begin of the oligarchical principle within him into the democratical?

Inevitably. And as in the city like was helping like, and the change was effected by an alliance from without assisting one division of the citizens, so too the young man is changed by a class of desires coming from without to assist the desires within him, that which is and alike again helping that which is akin and alike?

Certainly. And if there be any ally which aids the oligarchical principle within him, whether the influence of a father or of kindred, advising or rebuking him, then there arises in his soul a faction and an opposite faction, and he goes to war with himself.

It must be so. And there are times when the democratical principle gives way to the oligarchical, and some of his desires die, and others are banished; a spirit of reverence enters into the young man's soul and order is restored.

Yes, he said, that sometimes happens. And then, again, after the old desires have been driven out, fresh ones spring up, which are akin to them, and because he, their father, does not know how to educate them, wax fierce and numerous.
D: Narrative:
"Very true."

"May we not say that these desires spend, and that the others make money because they conduce to production?"

"Certainly."

"And of the pleasures of love, and all other pleasures, the same holds good?"

"True."

"And the drone of whom we spoke was he who was surfeited in pleasures and desires of this sort, and was the slave of the unnecessary desires, whereas he who was subject to the necessary only was miserly and oligarchical?"

"Very true."

"Again, let us see how the democratical man grows out of the oligarchical: the following, as I suspect, is commonly the process."

"What is the process?"

"When a young man who has been brought up as we were just now describing, in a vulgar and miserly way, has tasted drones' honey and has come to associate with fierce and crafty natures who are able to provide for him all sorts of refinements and varieties of pleasure -- then, as you may imagine, the change will begin of the oligarchical principle within him into the democratical?"

"Inevitably."

"And as in the city like was helping like, and the change was effected by an alliance from without assisting one division of the citizens, so too the young man is changed by a class of desires coming from without to assist the desires within him, that which is akin and alike again helping that which is akin and alike?"

"Certainly."

"And if there be any ally which aids the oligarchical principle within him, whether the influence of a father or of kindred, advising or rebuking him, then there arises in his soul a faction and an opposite faction, and he goes to war with himself."

"It must be so."

"And there are times when the democratical principle gives way to the oligarchical, and some of his desires die, and others are banished; a spirit of reverence enters into the young man's soul and order is restored."

"Yes," he said, "that sometimes happens."

"And then, again, after the old desires have been driven out, fresh ones spring up, which are akin to them, and because he, their father, does not know how to educate them, wax fierce and numerous."
D: Refashioned:
"Very true."

"Can we say that unnecessary desires spend money, while necessary desires actually produce it?"

"Certainly."

"And the same applies to sexual pleasures and all other desires?"

"True."

"So the drone we described was the person enslaved to unnecessary desires, while the miser enslaved only to necessary ones was the oligarchic type?"

"Exactly."

"Now let's trace how the democratic man grows out of the oligarchic one. I believe the process typically goes like this."

"What process?"

"A young man raised in the vulgar, miserly way we described gets his first taste of the drone's honey. He falls in with wild, clever companions who can supply him with every variety of refined pleasure. And so the oligarchic principle within him begins to transform into the democratic."

"Inevitably."

"Just as in the state, where like helps like and outside allies assist one faction against another, so in the young man's soul -- external desires ally with the internal ones that resemble them."

"Certainly."

"And if the oligarchic principle within him has its own allies -- a father's influence, family pressure, advice and rebuke -- then civil war breaks out within his soul."

"It must."

"Sometimes the democratic impulse retreats before the oligarchic one. Some desires die, others are banished, a spirit of reverence enters the young man's soul, and order is restored."

"Yes, that sometimes happens."

"But once the old desires are driven out, new ones spring up -- desires akin to those expelled. And because the father doesn't know how to educate these new desires, they grow fierce and numerous."
D: Elegant: The democratic man emerges when the oligarchic father's son tastes forbidden pleasures and falls in with companions who supply every variety. External desires ally with suppressed internal ones, just as foreign factions ally with domestic rebels. Civil war erupts in the soul. Sometimes order is restored, but new desires keep springing up, and the father -- who knows only suppression, not education -- cannot contain them.
D: Summary: The democratic man develops from the oligarchic father's son who discovers unnecessary pleasures through bad companions. His soul becomes a battleground between oligarchic restraint and democratic desire, with external influences reinforcing internal appetites. Even when order is temporarily restored, new desires spring up that the father cannot educate -- only suppress.
D: Distilled: The democratic soul forms when forbidden pleasures infiltrate the oligarchic son's repressed psyche, sparking an internal civil war that the father's suppressive methods cannot win.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Drones' honey': the pleasures provided by the parasitic drone class -- luxury, refinement, entertainment. -- The parallel between political faction and psychological conflict is explicit: just as cities fall through alliances between internal dissidents and external powers, so souls change through alliances between suppressed desires and external temptations. -- 'Does not know how to educate them': the oligarchic father's fatal flaw is that he suppresses desires by force rather than educating them through reason (compare 8.29). When new desires arise, he has no tools beyond prohibition. -- 'Wax fierce and numerous': biological language suggesting that desires breed and multiply when not properly managed -- anticipating the tyrannical soul's complete enslavement to appetite.

D: Original:
Yes, he said, that is apt to be the way. They draw him to his old associates, and holding secret intercourse with them, breed and multiply in him.

Very true. At length they seize upon the citadel of the young man's soul, which they perceive to be void of all accomplishments and fair pursuits and true words, which make their abode in the minds of men who are dear to the gods, and are their best guardians and sentinels.

None better. False and boastful conceits and phrases mount upwards and take their place.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is apt to be the way."

"They draw him to his old associates, and holding secret intercourse with them, breed and multiply in him."

"Very true."

"At length they seize upon the citadel of the young man's soul, which they perceive to be void of all accomplishments and fair pursuits and true words, which make their abode in the minds of men who are dear to the gods, and are their best guardians and sentinels."

"None better."

"False and boastful conceits and phrases mount upwards and take their place."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that's exactly how it goes."

"The new desires draw him back to his old companions, and through secret association, they breed and multiply inside him."

"Very true."

"Eventually they seize the citadel of the young man's soul, which they find empty of genuine accomplishments, noble pursuits, and true ideas -- the things that serve as guardians and sentinels in the minds of those who are dear to the gods."

"The best guardians there are."

"In their place, false and boastful ideas ascend and take the throne."
D: Elegant: Desires multiply secretly and seize the citadel of the soul, which they find undefended -- empty of accomplishments, noble pursuits, and true ideas. False and boastful conceits take the place of the absent guardians.
D: Summary: The proliferating desires seize the undefended citadel of the young man's soul, which lacks genuine accomplishments and true ideas -- the natural guardians of the mind. In their absence, false and boastful conceits take control.
D: Distilled: Desires seize the soul's undefended citadel, where false conceits replace the absent guardians of truth and genuine accomplishment.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The military metaphor of the soul's 'citadel' (acropolis) continues the political-psychological parallel: just as a city's acropolis can be seized, so can the governing center of the soul. -- 'Void of accomplishments and fair pursuits': the oligarchic father's failure to provide genuine education (only suppression) leaves the soul undefended. -- 'True words as guardians': logos (rational discourse) as the soul's protective garrison -- a characteristically Platonic idea. -- 'Dear to the gods': those with philosophically formed souls. -- 'False and boastful conceits': the democratic soul's self-deception -- renaming vices as virtues (developed further in 8.42-8.43).

D: Original: They are certain to do so. And so the young man returns into the country of the lotus-eaters, and takes up his dwelling there in the face of all men; and if any help be sent by his friends to the oligarchical part of him, the aforesaid vain conceits shut the gate of the king's fastness; and they will neither allow the embassy itself to enter, private if private advisers offer the fatherly counsel of the aged will they listen to them or receive them. There is a battle and they gain the day, and then modesty, which they call silliness, is ignominiously thrust into exile by them, and temperance, which they nickname unmanliness, is trampled in the mire and cast forth; they persuade men that moderation and orderly expenditure are vulgarity and meanness, and so, by the help of a rabble of evil appetites, they drive them beyond the border.
D: Narrative:
"They are certain to do so."

"And so the young man returns into the country of the lotus-eaters, and takes up his dwelling there in the face of all men; and if any help be sent by his friends to the oligarchical part of him, the aforesaid vain conceits shut the gate of the king's fastness; and they will neither allow the embassy itself to enter, nor, if private advisers offer the fatherly counsel of the aged, will they listen to them or receive them. There is a battle and they gain the day, and then modesty, which they call silliness, is ignominiously thrust into exile by them, and temperance, which they nickname unmanliness, is trampled in the mire and cast forth; they persuade men that moderation and orderly expenditure are vulgarity and meanness, and so, by the help of a rabble of evil appetites, they drive them beyond the border."
D: Refashioned:
"They certainly do."

"And so the young man settles in the land of the lotus-eaters, living there openly for all to see. When friends try to send help to the oligarchic part of his soul, those boastful conceits bar the gates of the inner citadel. They refuse to admit the rescue party. If older, wiser advisers offer fatherly counsel, they won't listen or let them in.

A battle follows, and the conceits win. Modesty is renamed 'foolishness' and driven into disgraceful exile. Temperance is rebranded as 'weakness' and trampled in the mud. They convince the young man that moderation and orderly spending are merely 'vulgarity' and 'stinginess,' and with a mob of evil appetites as allies, they drive these virtues beyond the border."
D: Elegant: The young man settles in the land of the lotus-eaters. His soul's citadel bars entry to all counsel. Virtues are renamed as vices: modesty becomes 'foolishness,' temperance becomes 'weakness,' moderation becomes 'vulgarity.' With a mob of appetites as enforcers, genuine virtues are exiled.
D: Summary: The democratic soul enters the land of the lotus-eaters. Wise counsel is rejected, and virtues are systematically renamed: modesty is called foolishness, temperance is called weakness, moderation is called vulgarity. Aided by a mob of appetites, these genuine virtues are driven into exile.
D: Distilled: The democratic soul exiles genuine virtues by renaming them as vices -- modesty becomes foolishness, temperance becomes weakness, moderation becomes vulgarity.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Country of the lotus-eaters': Homer, Odyssey 9.82-104 -- the land where eating lotus fruit makes one forget home and duty, an image of pleasant oblivion. -- The renaming of virtues as vices is one of Plato's most psychologically penetrating observations: moral corruption proceeds through linguistic corruption. Compare Thucydides III.82 on the revolution in language during the Corcyrean civil war: 'reckless audacity came to be considered the courage of a loyal ally.' -- 'King's fastness': the acropolis/citadel metaphor continued. -- 'Fatherly counsel of the aged': traditional wisdom rejected by the democratic temperament. -- This passage anticipates Orwell's analysis of political language in 'Politics and the English Language.'

D: Original: Yes, with a will. And when they have emptied and swept clean the soul of him who is now in their power and who is being initiated by them in great mysteries, the next thing is to bring back to their house insolence and anarchy and waste and impudence in bright array having garlands on their heads, and a great company with them, hymning their praises and calling them by sweet names; insolence they term breeding, and anarchy liberty, and waste magnificence, and impudence courage. And so the young man passes out of his original nature, which was trained in the school of necessity, into the freedom and libertinism of useless and unnecessary pleasures.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, with a will."

"And when they have emptied and swept clean the soul of him who is now in their power and who is being initiated by them in great mysteries, the next thing is to bring back to their house insolence and anarchy and waste and impudence in bright array having garlands on their heads, and a great company with them, hymning their praises and calling them by sweet names; insolence they term breeding, and anarchy liberty, and waste magnificence, and impudence courage. And so the young man passes out of his original nature, which was trained in the school of necessity, into the freedom and libertinism of useless and unnecessary pleasures."
D: Refashioned:
"With great enthusiasm."

"Once they've emptied and swept clean the soul of their new convert -- who is now being initiated into what they call the 'great mysteries' -- they bring back the exiled vices in a grand procession, decked in garlands and bright array, with a chorus singing their praises and giving them flattering new names. Insolence they call 'good breeding.' Anarchy they call 'liberty.' Waste they call 'magnificence.' Impudence they call 'courage.'

And so the young man leaves behind his original nature, trained in the school of necessity, and enters the freedom and license of useless, unnecessary pleasures."
D: Elegant: The soul is emptied and swept clean, then refilled with vices parading as virtues: insolence renamed 'good breeding,' anarchy renamed 'liberty,' waste renamed 'magnificence,' impudence renamed 'courage.' The young man abandons the school of necessity for the license of unnecessary pleasures.
D: Summary: After purging the soul of its old restraints, the democratic appetites stage a triumphal procession of vices disguised as virtues: insolence becomes 'good breeding,' anarchy becomes 'liberty,' waste becomes 'magnificence,' and impudence becomes 'courage.' The young man passes from disciplined necessity to unrestrained pleasure.
D: Distilled: The democratic soul replaces virtues with vices wearing flattering names -- insolence as breeding, anarchy as liberty, waste as magnificence, impudence as courage.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Great mysteries': an ironic reference to the Eleusinian Mysteries, the most sacred religious initiations in Athens -- the young man's corruption is presented as a parody of spiritual initiation. -- The four renamings form a systematic inversion: each cardinal vice is given the name of the virtue it displaces. -- 'Emptied and swept clean': echoes a passage in Matthew 12:43-45 about an unclean spirit returning to a swept house (though the Biblical parallel is chronologically later, the structural parallel is notable). -- 'School of necessity': the oligarchic training in restraint, which though imperfect, at least maintained order. -- The passage describes a complete transvaluation of values -- compare Nietzsche's use of this concept.

D: Original: Yes, he said, the change in him is visible enough. After this he lives on, spending his money and labour and time on unnecessary pleasures quite as much as on necessary ones; but if he be fortunate, and is not too much disordered in his wits, when years have elapsed, and the heyday of passion is over --supposing that he then re-admits into the city some part of the exiled virtues, and does not wholly give himself up to their successors --in that case he balances his pleasures and lives in a sort of equilibrium, putting the government of himself into the hands of the one which comes first and wins the turn; and when he has had enough of that, then into the hands of another; he despises none of them but encourages them all equally.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "the change in him is visible enough."

"After this he lives on, spending his money and labour and time on unnecessary pleasures quite as much as on necessary ones; but if he be fortunate, and is not too much disordered in his wits, when years have elapsed, and the heyday of passion is over -- supposing that he then re-admits into the city some part of the exiled virtues, and does not wholly give himself up to their successors -- in that case he balances his pleasures and lives in a sort of equilibrium, putting the government of himself into the hands of the one which comes first and wins the turn; and when he has had enough of that, then into the hands of another; he despises none of them but encourages them all equally."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, the change in him is clear enough."

"After this transformation, he lives spending his money, effort, and time on unnecessary pleasures just as much as necessary ones. If he's lucky and doesn't go completely off the rails, then as the years pass and youthful passion fades, he may readmit some of the exiled virtues and not entirely surrender to their replacements. In that case, he reaches a kind of equilibrium, handing control of himself to whichever desire happens to come along first. When he tires of that one, he hands control to another. He despises none of his desires and encourages them all equally."
D: Elegant: The democratic man divides himself equally between necessary and unnecessary pleasures. If lucky, he reaches an unstable equilibrium in middle age, handing governance to whichever desire arrives first. He refuses to rank his pleasures -- all are equal, none is despised, each takes its turn ruling.
D: Summary: The democratic man spends equally on necessary and unnecessary pleasures. If fortunate, he reaches a kind of equilibrium in later life, handing inner governance to whichever desire happens to arrive first. He treats all pleasures as equal, refusing to privilege any over another.
D: Distilled: The democratic man lives in unstable equilibrium, handing governance to whichever desire arrives first and treating all pleasures as equal.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Putting the government into the hands of the one which comes first': the democratic soul's governance is literally random -- rule by lot, mirroring the democratic state's use of sortition. -- 'Despises none, encourages all equally': the democratic principle of equality applied to desires -- no pleasure is judged better or worse than any other. This is the psychological equivalent of giving 'equality to equals and unequals alike' (8.39). -- The democratic man's best case is a fortunate moderation -- not principled virtue but mere balance of competing impulses. -- 'Heyday of passion': suggests that democratic excess is partly a function of youth, and maturity may bring some natural correction.

D: Original: Very true, he said. Neither does he receive or let pass into the fortress any true word of advice; if any one says to him that some pleasures are the satisfactions of good and noble desires, and others of evil desires, and that he ought to use and honour some and chastise and master the others --whenever this is repeated to him he shakes his head and says that they are all alike, and that one is as good as another.
D: Narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"Neither does he receive or let pass into the fortress any true word of advice; if any one says to him that some pleasures are the satisfactions of good and noble desires, and others of evil desires, and that he ought to use and honour some and chastise and master the others -- whenever this is repeated to him he shakes his head and says that they are all alike, and that one is as good as another."
D: Refashioned:
"Very true."

"And he refuses to admit any true counsel into his inner fortress. If someone tells him that some pleasures come from good and noble desires while others come from base ones, and that he should cultivate the first and discipline the second -- he just shakes his head and insists that all pleasures are the same. One is as good as another."
D: Elegant: The democratic man rejects all counsel that distinguishes between noble and base pleasures. He insists all desires are equal -- one is as good as another -- and bars the gate against any wisdom that would introduce hierarchy.
D: Summary: The democratic man refuses any advice that distinguishes between noble and base pleasures. When told he should honor some desires and discipline others, he shakes his head and insists all pleasures are alike and equally valid.
D: Distilled: The democratic man refuses to distinguish noble from base pleasures, insisting all desires are equal and rejecting any counsel that introduces hierarchy.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The refusal to rank pleasures is the democratic soul's defining philosophical error. For Plato, not all pleasures are equal: some are tied to genuine goods (knowledge, virtue) and others to mere appetite. See Book IX (580d-587a) for the hierarchy of pleasures. -- 'Fortress' continues the military metaphor of the soul's acropolis. -- 'Shakes his head': a vivid gesture of willful ignorance. -- This egalitarianism of desire directly contradicts the Republic's central claim that reason should rule the soul, just as philosophers should rule the state. -- The passage critiques moral relativism: if all pleasures are equal, there is no basis for virtue.

D: Original: Yes, he said; that is the way with him. Yes, I said, he lives from day to day indulging the appetite of the hour; and sometimes he is lapped in drink and strains of the flute; then he becomes a water-drinker, and tries to get thin; then he takes a turn at gymnastics; sometimes idling and neglecting everything, then once more living the life of a philosopher; often he-is busy with politics, and starts to his feet and says and does whatever comes into his head; and, if he is emulous of any one who is a warrior, off he is in that direction, or of men of business, once more in that. His life has neither law nor order; and this distracted existence he terms joy and bliss and freedom; and so he goes on.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said; "that is the way with him."

"Yes," I said, "he lives from day to day indulging the appetite of the hour; and sometimes he is lapped in drink and strains of the flute; then he becomes a water-drinker, and tries to get thin; then he takes a turn at gymnastics; sometimes idling and neglecting everything, then once more living the life of a philosopher; often he is busy with politics, and starts to his feet and says and does whatever comes into his head; and, if he is emulous of any one who is a warrior, off he is in that direction, or of men of business, once more in that. His life has neither law nor order; and this distracted existence he terms joy and bliss and freedom; and so he goes on."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that's how he is."

"He lives day to day, indulging whatever appetite strikes him at the moment. One day he's deep in drink and music; the next he's on a water diet trying to lose weight. Then he takes up exercise. Then he's idle, neglecting everything. Then he fancies himself a philosopher. Then he's suddenly consumed by politics, jumping up and saying and doing whatever comes into his head. If he admires a soldier, he rushes off in that direction. If he admires a businessman, off he goes that way instead. His life has no law and no order -- and this scattered, aimless existence he calls joy, happiness, and freedom."
D: Elegant: The democratic man lives from impulse to impulse: drinking, dieting, exercising, philosophizing, politicking, soldiering, doing business -- whatever strikes his fancy at the moment. His life has no law and no order. He calls this chaos joy, happiness, and freedom.
D: Summary: The democratic man lives moment to moment, cycling through drinking, dieting, exercise, philosophy, politics, and business as each impulse strikes. His life follows no law or order, yet he calls this aimless existence joy, happiness, and freedom.
D: Distilled: The democratic man's life is a chaotic sequence of passing impulses -- drinking, philosophizing, politicking, soldiering -- which he calls freedom and happiness.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- This is one of the Republic's most famous and psychologically acute passages. The portrait of the democratic personality type -- restless, impulsive, cycling through enthusiasms without commitment -- resonates across centuries. -- 'Strains of the flute': the aulos, associated with Dionysian ecstasy and emotional abandon. -- 'Living the life of a philosopher': philosophy becomes just another hobby, not a life commitment -- a pointed critique. -- 'Neither law nor order': anomia and ataxia -- the same charges leveled against the democratic state. -- 'This distracted existence he terms joy and bliss and freedom': the key irony -- what the democratic man calls freedom is actually enslavement to random impulse. -- Compare Kierkegaard's 'aesthetic stage' in Either/Or.

D: Original: Yes, he replied, he is all liberty and equality. Yes, I said; his life is motley and manifold and an epitome of the lives of many; --he answers to the State which we described as fair and spangled. And many a man and many a woman will take him for their pattern, and many a constitution and many an example of manners is contained in him.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he replied, "he is all liberty and equality."

"Yes," I said; "his life is motley and manifold and an epitome of the lives of many; -- he answers to the State which we described as fair and spangled. And many a man and many a woman will take him for their pattern, and many a constitution and many an example of manners is contained in him."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes," he replied, "he is the very embodiment of liberty and equality."

"His life is motley and multifaceted -- an epitome of many lives in one. He's the human counterpart of the state we called fair and spangled. Many men and women will take him as their model, and many a constitution and way of life is contained within him."
D: Elegant: The democratic man is the embodiment of liberty and equality: a motley, manifold life that epitomizes many lives at once. He is the human counterpart of the 'fair and spangled' democratic state, and many will take him as their model.
D: Summary: The democratic man embodies liberty and equality, living a motley, manifold life that contains many lives within it. He corresponds to the 'fair and spangled' democratic state and serves as a model for many men and women.
D: Distilled: The democratic man is a motley epitome of many lives -- the human mirror of democracy's spangled, attractive disorder.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Motley and manifold' (poikilos kai pantodapos): the same language used to describe the democratic state as an 'embroidered robe' in 8.36. -- 'Epitome of the lives of many': the democratic personality contains all possible lifestyles within one person -- a microcosm of democratic variety. -- 'Many a man and many a woman': unusually inclusive language for Plato, reflecting democracy's extension of the model across gender. -- The democratic man is attractive precisely because of his variety -- but for Plato, variety without order is a defect, not a virtue.

D: Original:
Just so. Let him then be set over against democracy; he may truly be called the democratic man.

Let that be his place, he said. Last of all comes the most beautiful of all, man and State alike, tyranny and the tyrant; these we have now to consider.

Quite true, he said. Say then, my friend, in what manner does tyranny arise? --that it has a democratic origin is evident.

Clearly. And does not tyranny spring from democracy in the same manner as democracy from oligarchy --I mean, after a sort?

How? The good which oligarchy proposed to itself and the means by which it was maintained was excess of wealth --am I not right?

Yes. And the insatiable desire of wealth and the neglect of all other things for the sake of money-getting was also the ruin of oligarchy?

True. And democracy has her own good, of which the insatiable desire brings her to dissolution?

What good? Freedom, I replied; which, as they tell you in a democracy, is the glory of the State --and that therefore in a democracy alone will the freeman of nature deign to dwell.

Yes; the saying is in everybody's mouth. I was going to observe, that the insatiable desire of this and the neglect of other things introduces the change in democracy, which occasions a demand for tyranny.

How so? When a democracy which is thirsting for freedom has evil cupbearers presiding over the feast, and has drunk too deeply of the strong wine of freedom, then, unless her rulers are very amenable and give a plentiful draught, she calls them to account and punishes them, and says that they are cursed oligarchs.

Yes, he replied, a very common occurrence. Yes, I said; and loyal citizens are insultingly termed by her slaves who hug their chains and men of naught; she would have subjects who are like rulers, and rulers who are like subjects: these are men after her own heart, whom she praises and honours both in private and public. Now, in such a State, can liberty have any limit?

Certainly not. By degrees the anarchy finds a way into private houses, and ends by getting among the animals and infecting them.
D: Narrative:
"Just so."

"Let him then be set over against democracy; he may truly be called the democratic man."

"Let that be his place," he said.

"Last of all comes the most beautiful of all, man and State alike, tyranny and the tyrant; these we have now to consider."

"Quite true," he said.

"Say then, my friend, in what manner does tyranny arise? -- that it has a democratic origin is evident."

"Clearly."

"And does not tyranny spring from democracy in the same manner as democracy from oligarchy -- I mean, after a sort?"

"How?"

"The good which oligarchy proposed to itself and the means by which it was maintained was excess of wealth -- am I not right?"

"Yes."

"And the insatiable desire of wealth and the neglect of all other things for the sake of money-getting was also the ruin of oligarchy?"

"True."

"And democracy has her own good, of which the insatiable desire brings her to dissolution?"

"What good?"

"Freedom," I replied; "which, as they tell you in a democracy, is the glory of the State -- and that therefore in a democracy alone will the freeman of nature deign to dwell."

"Yes; the saying is in everybody's mouth."

"I was going to observe, that the insatiable desire of this and the neglect of other things introduces the change in democracy, which occasions a demand for tyranny."

"How so?"

"When a democracy which is thirsting for freedom has evil cupbearers presiding over the feast, and has drunk too deeply of the strong wine of freedom, then, unless her rulers are very amenable and give a plentiful draught, she calls them to account and punishes them, and says that they are cursed oligarchs."

"Yes," he replied, "a very common occurrence."

"Yes," I said; "and loyal citizens are insultingly termed by her slaves who hug their chains and men of naught; she would have subjects who are like rulers, and rulers who are like subjects: these are men after her own heart, whom she praises and honours both in private and public. Now, in such a State, can liberty have any limit?"

"Certainly not."

"By degrees the anarchy finds a way into private houses, and ends by getting among the animals and infecting them."
D: Refashioned:
"Just so."

"Then let him stand as the democratic man."

"Let that be his place."

"Last of all -- and most 'beautiful' of all -- comes tyranny and the tyrant, both state and man. These we must now examine."

"Quite true."

"Tell me, then: how does tyranny arise? Its democratic origin is clear enough."

"Clearly."

"And doesn't tyranny spring from democracy in the same way democracy springs from oligarchy -- through excess of its own defining good?"

"How?"

"Oligarchy's defining good was wealth, and insatiable greed for wealth was also its ruin."

"Yes."

"Democracy likewise has its own defining good, and insatiable desire for that good brings about its destruction."

"What good?"

"Freedom," I replied. "In a democracy, they tell you that freedom is the state's highest glory, and that only in a democracy can a naturally free person bear to live."

"Yes, you hear that everywhere."

"My point is that the insatiable desire for freedom, combined with the neglect of everything else, creates the conditions that demand tyranny."

"How so?"

"When a democracy thirsting for freedom gets bad cupbearers presiding over the feast and drinks the strong wine of freedom too deeply, then -- unless its leaders are extremely compliant and pour generously -- it punishes them and denounces them as cursed oligarchs."

"Yes, that happens all the time."

"And loyal citizens get insulted as 'slaves who love their chains' and 'nobodies.' Democracy wants subjects who act like rulers and rulers who act like subjects -- those are the people she honors in public and private. In such a state, can liberty have any limit?"

"Certainly not."

"Gradually the anarchy creeps into private households and eventually infects even the animals."
D: Elegant: Each constitution is destroyed by excess of its own defining good: oligarchy by insatiable greed, democracy by insatiable freedom. Democracy drunk on freedom denounces all authority as oligarchy, honors the insubordinate, insults the loyal, and demands rulers who act like subjects. The anarchy spreads from public life into homes and eventually infects even the animals.
D: Summary: Tyranny arises from democracy through the same mechanism that produced democracy from oligarchy: the insatiable pursuit of the regime's own defining good. Oligarchy was destroyed by excess wealth; democracy is destroyed by excess freedom. The democracy, drunk on liberty, denounces all authority, honors insubordination, and lets anarchy spread from public life into homes and even to animals.
D: Distilled: Each regime is destroyed by excess of its own good -- oligarchy by greed, democracy by freedom -- and democracy's insatiable liberty creates the conditions for tyranny.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Most beautiful': deeply ironic -- tyranny is the worst, not the most beautiful. -- The structural parallel between oligarchy's fall (excess wealth) and democracy's fall (excess freedom) is one of Plato's most elegant analytical moves. -- 'Evil cupbearers' and 'strong wine of freedom': the metaphor of the feast suggests democracy as Dionysian excess. -- 'Slaves who hug their chains': the democratic inversion where obedience is mocked and insubordination celebrated. -- 'Rulers who are like subjects': the collapse of natural hierarchy. -- 'Anarchy among the animals': Plato pushes the democratic leveling principle to absurdity to show where the logic leads. -- This passage is the pivot point of Book VIII: from democratic analysis to the genesis of tyranny.

D: Original: How do you mean? I mean that the father grows accustomed to descend to the level of his sons and to fear them, and the son is on a level with his father, he having no respect or reverence for either of his parents; and this is his freedom, and metic is equal with the citizen and the citizen with the metic, and the stranger is quite as good as either.
D: Narrative:
"How do you mean?"

"I mean that the father grows accustomed to descend to the level of his sons and to fear them, and the son is on a level with his father, he having no respect or reverence for either of his parents; and this is his freedom, and the metic is equal with the citizen and the citizen with the metic, and the stranger is quite as good as either."
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean?"

"I mean that the father gets used to lowering himself to his sons' level and starts fearing them. The son considers himself the equal of his father, showing no respect or reverence for either parent -- and this he calls freedom. The resident alien is equal to the citizen, and the citizen to the alien, and the foreigner is considered as good as either."
D: Elegant: Democratic freedom dissolves all natural hierarchies: fathers fear their sons, sons disrespect their parents, citizens and foreigners become interchangeable. Every distinction is leveled in the name of liberty.
D: Summary: Democratic freedom erodes all hierarchies: fathers descend to their sons' level and fear them, sons show no reverence for parents, and distinctions between citizens, resident aliens, and foreigners are eliminated -- all in the name of liberty.
D: Distilled: Democratic freedom dissolves every natural hierarchy -- between parents and children, citizens and foreigners -- leveling all distinctions.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The father-son reversal is the domestic mirror of political anarchy. -- Metic (metoikos): a resident alien in Athens who had certain obligations but not full citizenship rights. The democratic leveling described here would dissolve the legal distinctions between citizen, metic, and xenos (foreigner). -- 'This is his freedom': the ironic identification of disrespect with liberty. -- Compare Aristotle, Politics V.9 on the democratic tendency to equalize all differences. -- The passage reflects conservative Athenian anxieties about social dissolution -- but Plato's critique is philosophical, not merely reactionary: hierarchy for him is about natural aptitude and education, not birth.

D: Original: Yes, he said, that is the way. And these are not the only evils, I said --there are several lesser ones: In such a state of society the master fears and flatters his scholars, and the scholars despise their masters and tutors; young and old are all alike; and the young man is on a level with the old, and is ready to compete with him in word or deed; and old men condescend to the young and are full of pleasantry and gaiety; they are loth to be thought morose and authoritative, and therefore they adopt the manners of the young.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is the way."

"And these are not the only evils," I said -- "there are several lesser ones: In such a state of society the master fears and flatters his scholars, and the scholars despise their masters and tutors; young and old are all alike; and the young man is on a level with the old, and is ready to compete with him in word or deed; and old men condescend to the young and are full of pleasantry and gaiety; they are loth to be thought morose and authoritative, and therefore they adopt the manners of the young."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that's how it works."

"And there are more evils besides. In such a society, teachers fear and flatter their students, and students despise their teachers and tutors. Young and old become indistinguishable. Young people compete openly with their elders in speech and action. Old people, meanwhile, adopt youthful manners, full of jokes and lightheartedness -- they're terrified of being thought stern or authoritarian."
D: Elegant: In democratic society, teachers flatter students, students despise teachers. Young and old become indistinguishable as the old adopt youthful manners to avoid seeming authoritarian. Every hierarchy of experience and wisdom is dissolved.
D: Summary: Democratic leveling extends to education and age: teachers fear and flatter students, students despise teachers, and old people adopt youthful manners to avoid appearing stern. The hierarchy between young and old dissolves as elders compete to seem youthful and easy-going.
D: Distilled: Democratic equality dissolves the authority of teachers over students and elders over youth, as everyone races to appear casual and non-hierarchical.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'The master fears and flatters his scholars': one of Plato's most frequently quoted observations, recurring in educational debates across millennia. -- The reversal of the teacher-student relationship directly undermines the Republic's educational program (Books II-III, VII). -- 'Old men condescend to the young': the elderly abandon their natural role as bearers of wisdom and authority, adopting youth culture instead. -- 'Loth to be thought morose and authoritative': the democratic fear of appearing serious or demanding. -- Compare Aristophanes' Clouds, where the corruption of education is a central theme. -- Aristotle discusses the democratic tendency toward permissiveness in Politics V.9.

D: Original:
Quite true, he said. The last extreme of popular liberty is when the slave bought with money, whether male or female, is just as free as his or her purchaser; nor must I forget to tell of the liberty and equality of the two sexes in relation to each other.

Why not, as Aeschylus says, utter the word which rises to our lips?
D: Narrative:
"Quite true," he said.

"The last extreme of popular liberty is when the slave bought with money, whether male or female, is just as free as his or her purchaser; nor must I forget to tell of the liberty and equality of the two sexes in relation to each other."

"Why not, as Aeschylus says, utter the word which rises to our lips?"
D: Refashioned:
"Quite true."

"The ultimate extreme of popular liberty is when slaves -- purchased with money, whether male or female -- are just as free as their owners. And I shouldn't neglect to mention the liberty and equality of the sexes in their relation to each other."

"Why not, as Aeschylus says, speak the word that rises to our lips?"
D: Elegant: The extreme of democratic liberty: even purchased slaves become as free as their owners, and the sexes achieve full equality. Adeimantus quotes Aeschylus to encourage the observation.
D: Summary: Socrates identifies the ultimate extreme of democratic freedom: slaves become as free as their masters, and the sexes achieve equality. Adeimantus encourages him to speak freely, quoting Aeschylus.
D: Distilled: Democratic liberty reaches its extreme when even slaves are as free as their owners and the sexes are fully equalized.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The reference to slave equality would have been striking to ancient Greek audiences, for whom slavery was a foundational institution. -- 'Liberty and equality of the two sexes': Plato himself advocated equality of education and opportunity for women in Book V (451d-457b), making this passage complexly ironic -- he approves of gender equality in principle but criticizes the unphilosophical, undisciplined form it takes in democracy. -- Aeschylus: the quotation is likely from a lost play. The phrase encourages frankness (parrhesia), itself a characteristic democratic value. -- The passage reveals a tension in Plato's thought: some democratic developments (gender equality) align with his own proposals, but he objects to their anarchic, unprincipled implementation.

D: Original: That is what I am doing, I replied; and I must add that no one who does not know would believe, how much greater is the liberty which the animals who are under the dominion of man have in a democracy than in any other State: for truly, the she-dogs, as the proverb says, are as good as their she-mistresses, and the horses and asses have a way of marching along with all the rights and dignities of freemen; and they will run at anybody who comes in their way if he does not leave the road clear for them: and all things are just ready to burst with liberty.
D: Narrative: "That is what I am doing," I replied; "and I must add that no one who does not know would believe, how much greater is the liberty which the animals who are under the dominion of man have in a democracy than in any other State: for truly, the she-dogs, as the proverb says, are as good as their she-mistresses, and the horses and asses have a way of marching along with all the rights and dignities of freemen; and they will run at anybody who comes in their way if he does not leave the road clear for them: and all things are just ready to burst with liberty."
D: Refashioned: "That's what I'm doing," I replied. "And I must add something that no one would believe who hadn't seen it: even the animals under human control have far more liberty in a democracy than anywhere else. As the proverb goes, the female dogs become just like their mistresses. Horses and donkeys strut down the street with all the rights and dignities of free citizens, and they'll charge at anyone who doesn't get out of their way. Everything is practically bursting with liberty."
D: Elegant: Even the animals are freer in a democracy: dogs become like their mistresses, horses and donkeys strut with the dignity of free citizens and charge anyone in their path. Everything is bursting with liberty.
D: Summary: Socrates extends the democratic leveling principle to its comic extreme: even animals are freer under democracy. Dogs behave like their owners, and horses and donkeys strut through the streets with the dignity of citizens, running down anyone in their way. Everything is bursting with liberty.
D: Distilled: Democratic liberty extends even to animals -- dogs, horses, and donkeys strut like free citizens, and everything bursts with unrestrained freedom.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- This passage is often read as comic hyperbole, but it makes a serious philosophical point: if the principle of universal equality is followed consistently, it has no natural stopping point. -- 'She-dogs as good as their she-mistresses': a proverb suggesting like master, like pet. -- The image of animals strutting with 'rights and dignities' is deliberately absurd, designed to show where the democratic logic of unlimited freedom leads. -- Plato's humor is at its sharpest here. -- Compare Aristophanes' Wasps and Birds for similar comic treatment of democratic excess.

D: Original:
When I take a country walk, he said, I often experience what you describe. You and I have dreamed the same thing.

And above all, I said, and as the result of all, see how sensitive the citizens become; they chafe impatiently at the least touch of authority and at length, as you know, they cease to care even for the laws, written or unwritten; they will have no one over them.

Yes, he said, I know it too well. Such, my friend, I said, is the fair and glorious beginning out of which springs tyranny.

Glorious indeed, he said. But what is the next step? The ruin of oligarchy is the ruin of democracy; the same disease magnified and intensified by liberty overmasters democracy --the truth being that the excessive increase of anything often causes a reaction in the opposite direction; and this is the case not only in the seasons and in vegetable and animal life, but above all in forms of government.

True. The excess of liberty, whether in States or individuals, seems only to pass into excess of slavery.

Yes, the natural order. And so tyranny naturally arises out of democracy, and the most aggravated form of tyranny and slavery out of the most extreme form of liberty?

As we might expect. That, however, was not, as I believe, your question-you rather desired to know what is that disorder which is generated alike in oligarchy and democracy, and is the ruin of both?

Just so, he replied. Well, I said, I meant to refer to the class of idle spendthrifts, of whom the more courageous are the-leaders and the more timid the followers, the same whom we were comparing to drones, some stingless, and others having stings.

A very just comparison. These two classes are the plagues of every city in which they are generated, being what phlegm and bile are to the body. And the good physician and lawgiver of the State ought, like the wise bee-master, to keep them at a distance and prevent, if possible, their ever coming in; and if they have anyhow found a way in, then he should have them and their cells cut out as speedily as possible.
D: Narrative:
"When I take a country walk," he said, "I often experience what you describe. You and I have dreamed the same thing."

"And above all," I said, "and as the result of all, see how sensitive the citizens become; they chafe impatiently at the least touch of authority and at length, as you know, they cease to care even for the laws, written or unwritten; they will have no one over them."

"Yes," he said, "I know it too well."

"Such, my friend," I said, "is the fair and glorious beginning out of which springs tyranny."

"Glorious indeed," he said. "But what is the next step?"

"The ruin of oligarchy is the ruin of democracy; the same disease magnified and intensified by liberty overmasters democracy -- the truth being that the excessive increase of anything often causes a reaction in the opposite direction; and this is the case not only in the seasons and in vegetable and animal life, but above all in forms of government."

"True."

"The excess of liberty, whether in States or individuals, seems only to pass into excess of slavery."

"Yes, the natural order."

"And so tyranny naturally arises out of democracy, and the most aggravated form of tyranny and slavery out of the most extreme form of liberty?"

"As we might expect."

"That, however, was not, as I believe, your question -- you rather desired to know what is that disorder which is generated alike in oligarchy and democracy, and is the ruin of both?"

"Just so," he replied.

"Well," I said, "I meant to refer to the class of idle spendthrifts, of whom the more courageous are the leaders and the more timid the followers, the same whom we were comparing to drones, some stingless, and others having stings."

"A very just comparison."

"These two classes are the plagues of every city in which they are generated, being what phlegm and bile are to the body. And the good physician and lawgiver of the State ought, like the wise bee-master, to keep them at a distance and prevent, if possible, their ever coming in; and if they have anyhow found a way in, then he should have them and their cells cut out as speedily as possible."
D: Refashioned:
"When I take a walk in the country," he said, "I often see exactly what you're describing. We've had the same vision."

"And above all," I said, "notice the end result: the citizens become hypersensitive. They chafe at the slightest touch of authority. Eventually they stop caring about laws altogether, written or unwritten. They refuse to have anyone above them."

"Yes, I know it too well."

"This, my friend, is the fair and glorious beginning from which tyranny springs."

"Glorious indeed. But what comes next?"

"The disease that destroyed oligarchy destroys democracy too -- the same sickness, magnified and intensified by liberty. The truth is that the excessive increase of anything tends to provoke a violent reaction in the opposite direction. This is true of the seasons, of plants and animals, and above all of forms of government."

"True."

"Excess of liberty, whether in states or individuals, passes into excess of slavery."

"Yes, naturally."

"So tyranny arises naturally out of democracy -- the most extreme slavery from the most extreme freedom?"

"As we might expect."

"But that wasn't exactly your question. You wanted to know what specific disorder ruins both oligarchy and democracy?"

"Just so."

"I was referring to the class of idle spendthrifts -- the drones. The braver ones are leaders, the more timid are followers. Some are stingless, others have stings."

"A very apt comparison."

"These two types are the plague of every city they appear in -- like phlegm and bile in the body. A good physician and lawgiver should act like a wise beekeeper: keep them out if possible, and if they get in, cut them and their cells out as fast as possible."
D: Elegant: Citizens hypersensitive to authority eventually reject all law. This 'glorious' beginning spawns tyranny. The principle: excess of anything provokes the opposite extreme -- excess liberty becomes excess slavery. The specific agent of ruin, shared by oligarchy and democracy alike, is the drone class -- idle spendthrifts who plague every city like phlegm and bile in a body. The wise lawgiver, like a beekeeper, must prevent or remove them.
D: Summary: As citizens grow hypersensitive to all authority and reject every law, the stage is set for tyranny. Socrates identifies the universal principle: excess of anything provokes the opposite extreme, so excess freedom becomes excess slavery. The specific agent destroying both oligarchy and democracy is the drone class -- idle spendthrifts who must be prevented or excised like disease from a body.
D: Distilled: Excess freedom produces excess slavery; the drone class of idle spendthrifts is the disease that destroys both oligarchy and democracy and opens the door to tyranny.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Fair and glorious beginning': deeply ironic -- democratic freedom is the seedbed of tyranny. -- The law of excess: 'excessive increase of anything causes a reaction in the opposite direction' -- a universal principle Plato applies to seasons, biology, and politics. Compare Aristotle, Politics V.9. -- 'Phlegm and bile': medical language from Hippocratic theory, where disease results from imbalance of bodily humors. -- The beekeeper analogy extends the drone metaphor: the lawgiver must manage the body politic as the beekeeper manages the hive. -- The drone class appears in both oligarchy (8.24) and democracy (8.54), serving as the agent of transition in both cases. -- 'Excess of liberty passes into excess of slavery': one of the Republic's most quoted formulations.

D: Original:
Yes, by all means, he said. Then, in order that we may see clearly what we are doing, let us imagine democracy to be divided, as indeed it is, into three classes; for in the first place freedom creates rather more drones in the democratic than there were in the oligarchical State.

That is true. And in the democracy they are certainly more intensified.

How so? Because in the oligarchical State they are disqualified and driven from office, and therefore they cannot train or gather strength; whereas in a democracy they are almost the entire ruling power, and while the keener sort speak and act, the rest keep buzzing about the bema and do not suffer a word to be said on the other side; hence in democracies almost everything is managed by the drones.

Very true, he said. Then there is another class which is always being severed from the mass.

What is that? They are the orderly class, which in a nation of traders sure to be the richest.

Naturally so. They are the most squeezable persons and yield the largest amount of honey to the drones.

Why, he said, there is little to be squeezed out of people who have little.

And this is called the wealthy class, and the drones feed upon them.

That is pretty much the case, he said. The people are a third class, consisting of those who work with their own hands; they are not politicians, and have not much to live upon. This, when assembled, is the largest and most powerful class in a democracy.

True, he said; but then the multitude is seldom willing to congregate unless they get a little honey.

And do they not share? I said. Do not their leaders deprive the rich of their estates and distribute them among the people; at the same time taking care to reserve the larger part for themselves?

Why, yes, he said, to that extent the people do share. And the persons whose property is taken from them are compelled to defend themselves before the people as they best can?
D: Narrative:
"Yes, by all means," he said.

"Then, in order that we may see clearly what we are doing, let us imagine democracy to be divided, as indeed it is, into three classes; for in the first place freedom creates rather more drones in the democratic than there were in the oligarchical State."

"That is true."

"And in the democracy they are certainly more intensified."

"How so?"

"Because in the oligarchical State they are disqualified and driven from office, and therefore they cannot train or gather strength; whereas in a democracy they are almost the entire ruling power, and while the keener sort speak and act, the rest keep buzzing about the bema and do not suffer a word to be said on the other side; hence in democracies almost everything is managed by the drones."

"Very true," he said.

"Then there is another class which is always being severed from the mass."

"What is that?"

"They are the orderly class, which in a nation of traders is sure to be the richest."

"Naturally so."

"They are the most squeezable persons and yield the largest amount of honey to the drones."

"Why," he said, "there is little to be squeezed out of people who have little."

"And this is called the wealthy class, and the drones feed upon them."

"That is pretty much the case," he said.

"The people are a third class, consisting of those who work with their own hands; they are not politicians, and have not much to live upon. This, when assembled, is the largest and most powerful class in a democracy."

"True," he said; "but then the multitude is seldom willing to congregate unless they get a little honey."

"And do they not share?" I said. "Do not their leaders deprive the rich of their estates and distribute them among the people; at the same time taking care to reserve the larger part for themselves?"

"Why, yes," he said, "to that extent the people do share."

"And the persons whose property is taken from them are compelled to defend themselves before the people as they best can?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, absolutely."

"To see this clearly, let's divide democracy into its three actual classes. First, freedom produces even more drones in a democracy than in an oligarchy."

"True."

"And in a democracy, they're far more powerful."

"How so?"

"In an oligarchy, drones are excluded from office and can't build strength. In a democracy, they practically run everything. The sharpest ones make speeches and take action, while the rest buzz around the speaker's platform, shouting down anyone who disagrees. In democracies, the drones manage almost everything."

"Very true."

"The second class is always being separated from the mass."

"Which class?"

"The orderly, business-minded citizens who, in a commercial nation, are naturally the wealthiest."

"Naturally."

"They're the most squeezable -- they yield the most honey to the drones."

"Of course," he said. "There's not much to squeeze from people who have nothing."

"This wealthy class is what the drones feed on."

"Pretty much."

"The third class is the people themselves -- those who work with their hands, who aren't politically active, and who have limited means. When assembled, this is the largest and most powerful group in a democracy."

"True, but the masses rarely assemble unless they get a share of the honey."

"And don't they get some?" I said. "Don't their leaders strip the rich of their property and distribute it to the people -- while keeping the lion's share for themselves?"

"Yes, that's about the extent of the people's share."

"And those whose property is confiscated are forced to defend themselves before the people as best they can?"
D: Elegant: Democracy has three classes: the drones (who dominate politics, buzzing around the speaker's platform and shouting down opposition), the wealthy (the 'squeezable' class that feeds the drones), and the working people (the largest group, who assemble only when offered honey). Drone-leaders confiscate wealth from the rich, distribute some to the people, and keep the largest portion for themselves.
