D: Summary: Socrates divides democracy into three classes: the drones who dominate political life, the wealthy class they feed upon, and the working masses who are the largest but least active group. Drone-leaders confiscate the property of the rich, distribute some to the people as honey, and keep the largest share for themselves.
D: Distilled: Democracy divides into three classes: drones who dominate politics, wealthy citizens they exploit, and working masses who assemble only when offered a share of confiscated wealth.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The bema: the speaker's platform in the Athenian Pnyx (assembly meeting place). -- Three classes of democracy: (1) drones/demagogues who run politics, (2) the wealthy/orderly class who are exploited, (3) the working people who form the mass. -- 'Buzzing about the bema': a vivid image of democratic noise drowning out rational debate. -- 'Reserve the larger part for themselves': a pointed critique of demagogic corruption. -- 'Squeezable': a metaphor from beekeeping (extracting honey from comb) applied to taxation and confiscation. -- The three-class analysis anticipates the mechanism by which the demagogue-protector becomes tyrant: he uses the people against the wealthy, then turns on both.

D: Original:
What else can they do? And then, although they may have no desire of change, the others charge them with plotting against the people and being friends of oligarchy? True.

And the end is that when they see the people, not of their own accord, but through ignorance, and because they are deceived by informers, seeking to do them wrong, then at last they are forced to become oligarchs in reality; they do not wish to be, but the sting of the drones torments them and breeds revolution in them.

That is exactly the truth. Then come impeachments and judgments and trials of one another.

True. The people have always some champion whom they set over them and nurse into greatness.

Yes, that is their way. This and no other is the root from which a tyrant springs; when he first appears above ground he is a protector.

Yes, that is quite clear. How then does a protector begin to change into a tyrant? Clearly when he does what the man is said to do in the tale of the Arcadian temple of Lycaean Zeus.

What tale? The tale is that he who has tasted the entrails of a single human victim minced up with the entrails of other victims is destined to become a wolf. Did you never hear it?
D: Narrative:
"What else can they do?"

"And then, although they may have no desire of change, the others charge them with plotting against the people and being friends of oligarchy?"

"True."

"And the end is that when they see the people, not of their own accord, but through ignorance, and because they are deceived by informers, seeking to do them wrong, then at last they are forced to become oligarchs in reality; they do not wish to be, but the sting of the drones torments them and breeds revolution in them."

"That is exactly the truth."

"Then come impeachments and judgments and trials of one another."

"True."

"The people have always some champion whom they set over them and nurse into greatness."

"Yes, that is their way."

"This and no other is the root from which a tyrant springs; when he first appears above ground he is a protector."

"Yes, that is quite clear."

"How then does a protector begin to change into a tyrant? Clearly when he does what the man is said to do in the tale of the Arcadian temple of Lycaean Zeus."

"What tale?"

"The tale is that he who has tasted the entrails of a single human victim minced up with the entrails of other victims is destined to become a wolf. Did you never hear it?"
D: Refashioned:
"What else can they do?"

"And even though the wealthy may have no desire for political change, the drones accuse them of plotting against the people and being secret oligarchs."

"True."

"The result is that the wealthy, seeing the people -- not acting on their own judgment but deceived by informers -- trying to wrong them, are eventually forced to become actual oligarchs. They don't want to be, but the constant stinging of the drones torments them into it."

"That's exactly what happens."

"Then come impeachments, trials, and prosecutions."

"True."

"The people always find some champion whom they elevate and nurture into greatness."

"Yes, that's their way."

"This -- and nothing else -- is the root from which a tyrant springs. When he first appears, he is a protector of the people."

"Clearly."

"How does the protector begin to change into a tyrant? It happens when he does what they say happens to a man in the tale of the Arcadian temple of Lycaean Zeus."

"What tale?"

"The tale that whoever tastes the entrails of a single human victim, mixed in with the entrails of other sacrifices, is destined to become a wolf. Have you never heard it?"
D: Elegant: The wealthy, falsely accused of oligarchic plots, are driven to become actual oligarchs by the drones' persecution. The people then elevate a champion-protector -- and this is always the root from which the tyrant springs. The protector becomes a tyrant the way a man becomes a wolf in the legend of Lycaean Zeus: by tasting human blood.
D: Summary: The wealthy, falsely accused of oligarchic conspiracy, are tormented into actually becoming oligarchs. The people respond by elevating a champion-protector -- the root of every tyranny. The protector transforms into a tyrant through the same mechanism as the legend of Lycaean Zeus: tasting human blood transforms a man into a wolf.
D: Distilled: The tyrant begins as the people's protector and transforms into a wolf by tasting human blood -- false accusations drive the wealthy into oligarchy, creating the crisis the protector exploits.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The self-fulfilling prophecy: accusing the wealthy of oligarchy drives them to actually become oligarchs, creating the very threat the demagogue claims to protect against. -- 'Champion whom they set over them': the prostates tou demou (champion/protector of the people) -- a recognized political role in Athens. Historical examples include Peisistratus, who became tyrant of Athens c. 561 BCE through exactly this mechanism. -- The myth of Lycaean Zeus: at the temple of Zeus Lycaeus in Arcadia, human sacrifice was reportedly practiced, and whoever tasted human flesh was said to become a wolf (lykos). -- Lycanthropy as political metaphor: the protector who sheds kindred blood becomes something inhuman. -- This passage is the hinge between democracy and tyranny in Plato's sequence.

D: Original: Oh, yes. And the protector of the people is like him; having a mob entirely at his disposal, he is not restrained from shedding the blood of kinsmen; by the favourite method of false accusation he brings them into court and murders them, making the life of man to disappear, and with unholy tongue and lips tasting the blood of his fellow citizen; some he kills and others he banishes, at the same time hinting at the abolition of debts and partition of lands: and after this, what will be his destiny? Must he not either perish at the hands of his enemies, or from being a man become a wolf --that is, a tyrant?
D: Narrative:
"Oh, yes."

"And the protector of the people is like him; having a mob entirely at his disposal, he is not restrained from shedding the blood of kinsmen; by the favourite method of false accusation he brings them into court and murders them, making the life of man to disappear, and with unholy tongue and lips tasting the blood of his fellow citizen; some he kills and others he banishes, at the same time hinting at the abolition of debts and partition of lands: and after this, what will be his destiny? Must he not either perish at the hands of his enemies, or from being a man become a wolf -- that is, a tyrant?"
D: Refashioned:
"Oh, yes."

"The people's protector follows the same pattern. With a mob at his command, he doesn't hold back from shedding the blood of his own people. Through his favorite weapon -- false accusation -- he drags people into court and destroys them, extinguishing human lives and tasting the blood of fellow citizens with unholy lips. He kills some, banishes others, and all the while he hints at abolishing debts and redistributing land. After all this, what is his destiny? He must either be destroyed by his enemies or complete his transformation -- from man into wolf. That is, into a tyrant."
D: Elegant: The protector becomes the tyrant: commanding a mob, shedding kindred blood through false accusations, killing and banishing while promising debt relief and land redistribution. He must either be destroyed or complete his transformation from man into wolf -- into tyrant.
D: Summary: The people's protector transforms into a tyrant by using false accusations to eliminate opponents, killing and banishing while promising debt abolition and land redistribution. Having tasted the blood of fellow citizens, he faces a binary destiny: destruction by enemies, or full transformation from man into wolf -- from protector into tyrant.
D: Distilled: The protector becomes tyrant by shedding kindred blood through false accusations while promising debt relief -- he must either be destroyed or complete the transformation from man to wolf.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Abolition of debts and partition of lands': the two classic revolutionary promises in ancient Greece. Debt abolition (chreon apokope) and land redistribution (ges anadasmos) were the standard demands of popular movements and the standard promises of aspiring tyrants. -- 'Tasting the blood of fellow citizens with unholy tongue': the lycanthropy metaphor made explicit -- political murder as cannibalistic transformation. -- The binary fate (perish or become tyrant) reflects historical patterns: aspiring tyrants like Peisistratus either succeeded or were exiled/killed. -- Compare Herodotus on Peisistratus (I.59-64) and Thucydides on the Pisistratid tyranny.

D: Original:
Inevitably. This, I said, is he who begins to make a party against the rich?

The same. After a while he is driven out, but comes back, in spite of his enemies, a tyrant full grown.

That is clear. And if they are unable to expel him, or to get him condemned to death by a public accusation, they conspire to assassinate him.

Yes, he said, that is their usual way. Then comes the famous request for a bodyguard, which is the device of all those who have got thus far in their tyrannical career --'Let not the people's friend,' as they say, 'be lost to them.'

Exactly. The people readily assent; all their fears are for him --they have none for themselves.

Very true. And when a man who is wealthy and is also accused of being an enemy of the people sees this, then, my friend, as the oracle said to Croesus,

By pebbly Hermus' shore he flees and rests not and is not ashamed to be a coward.

And quite right too, said he, for if he were, he would never be ashamed again.

But if he is caught he dies. Of course. And he, the protector of whom we spoke, is to be seen, not 'larding the plain' with his bulk, but himself the overthrower of many, standing up in the chariot of State with the reins in his hand, no longer protector, but tyrant absolute.

No doubt, he said. And now let us consider the happiness of the man, and also of the State in which a creature like him is generated.

Yes, he said, let us consider that. At first, in the early days of his power, he is full of smiles, and he salutes every one whom he meets; --he to be called a tyrant, who is making promises in public and also in private! liberating debtors, and distributing land to the people and his followers, and wanting to be so kind and good to every one!

Of course, he said. But when he has disposed of foreign enemies by conquest or treaty, and there is nothing to fear from them, then he is always stirring up some war or other, in order that the people may require a leader.

"Inevitably."

"The same."

"Eventually he's driven out, but he returns despite his enemies -- now a tyrant fully formed."

"That's clear."

"Yes, that's their usual method."

"Exactly."

"Very true."

"And rightly so," he said, "because if he stayed, he'd never have the chance to be ashamed again."

"If he's caught, he dies."

"Of course."

"And our protector -- he is no longer seen 'larding the plain' with his bulk. He stands in the chariot of state, reins in hand, overthrower of many -- no longer protector but absolute tyrant."

"No doubt."

"Yes, let's."

"At first, in his early days of power, he's all smiles. He greets everyone he meets. He -- called a tyrant? He who makes promises publicly and privately, frees debtors, distributes land to the people and his followers, wanting to be so kind and good to everyone!"

"Of course."

"But once he's dealt with foreign enemies through conquest or treaty and has nothing to fear from abroad, then he is always stirring up some war -- so that the people will continue to need a leader."
D: Elegant: The tyrant's career follows a pattern: driven out, he returns fully formed. When opponents try assassination, he requests a bodyguard -- the classic tyrannical move. The people readily agree, fearing for him, not themselves. At first he's all smiles, promises, debt forgiveness, and land distribution. But once foreign threats are eliminated, he manufactures wars to keep the people dependent on his leadership.
D: Summary: The aspiring tyrant is driven out but returns fully formed. When opponents plot assassination, he secures a bodyguard with the people's enthusiastic consent. Initially he is generous -- full of smiles, freeing debtors, distributing land. But once foreign enemies are neutralized, he perpetually stirs up wars to maintain his necessity as leader.
D: Distilled: The tyrant requests a bodyguard with the people's consent, begins with smiles and generosity, then manufactures perpetual wars to maintain his indispensability.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The bodyguard request: the classic signal of tyranny. Peisistratus famously obtained a bodyguard from the Athenian assembly by staging a self-inflicted wound (Herodotus I.59). Dionysius I of Syracuse obtained his through similar means. -- 'Larding the plain': a Homeric phrase, originally describing a fallen warrior's body; here ironically applied -- the tyrant is no fallen hero but a standing oppressor. -- Oracle to Croesus: Herodotus I.55 -- the oracle warns Croesus to flee when a mule rules the Medes (i.e., Cyrus, of mixed parentage). -- The initial generosity of tyrants is a well-documented pattern: Peisistratus, Caesar, Augustus all began with popular benefactions. -- Perpetual war as a tool of tyranny: manufacturing external threats to maintain domestic control. Compare Orwell's 1984.

D: Original:
To be sure. Has he not also another object, which is that they may be impoverished by payment of taxes, and thus compelled to devote themselves to their daily wants and therefore less likely to conspire against him? Clearly.

And if any of them are suspected by him of having notions of freedom, and of resistance to his authority, he will have a good pretext for destroying them by placing them at the mercy of the enemy; and for all these reasons the tyrant must be always getting up a war.

He must. Now he begins to grow unpopular. A necessary result. Then some of those who joined in setting him up, and who are in power, speak their minds to him and to one another, and the more courageous of them cast in his teeth what is being done.

Yes, that may be expected. And the tyrant, if he means to rule, must get rid of them; he cannot stop while he has a friend or an enemy who is good for anything.

He cannot. And therefore he must look about him and see who is valiant, who is high-minded, who is wise, who is wealthy; happy man, he is the enemy of them all, and must seek occasion against them whether he will or no, until he has made a purgation of the State.

Yes, he said, and a rare purgation. Yes, I said, not the sort of purgation which the physicians make of the body; for they take away the worse and leave the better part, but he does the reverse.

If he is to rule, I suppose that he cannot help himself.

What a blessed alternative, I said: --to be compelled to dwell only with the many bad, and to be by them hated, or not to live at all!

Yes, that is the alternative. And the more detestable his actions are to the citizens the more satellites and the greater devotion in them will he require?

Certainly. And who are the devoted band, and where will he procure them?

They will flock to him, he said, of their own accord, if lie pays them.

"To be sure."

"Clearly."

"He must."

"A necessary result."

"Yes," he said, "and a rare purgation."

"If he is to rule, I suppose that he cannot help himself."

"What a blessed alternative," I said: "-- to be compelled to dwell only with the many bad, and to be by them hated, or not to live at all!"

"Yes, that is the alternative."

"Certainly."

D: Refashioned:
"To be sure."

"He has another motive too: the wars impoverish the people through taxes, forcing them to focus on daily survival rather than conspiring against him."

"Clearly."

"And if he suspects anyone of harboring ideas about freedom or resistance, he has a convenient pretext: he sends them to the front lines and lets the enemy destroy them. For all these reasons, the tyrant must always be starting wars."

"He must."

"But eventually he starts to lose popularity."

"Inevitably."

"Then some of the very people who helped put him in power begin to speak out, and the bravest among them confront him directly about what he's doing."

"That's to be expected."

"And the tyrant, if he intends to keep ruling, must eliminate them. He can't stop as long as he has a friend or enemy of any quality."

"He can't."

"So he must survey the field: who is brave, who is noble-minded, who is wise, who is wealthy? Lucky man -- they are all his enemies, and he must find pretexts to destroy them, one by one, until he has purged the state."

"Yes -- quite a purge."

"Indeed, but the opposite of a medical purge. Doctors remove the bad and keep the good. The tyrant removes the good and keeps the bad."

"If he wants to rule, he has no choice."

"What a blessed dilemma," I said: "to live surrounded only by the worst people, hated even by them -- or to not live at all."

"That is the dilemma."

"And the more the citizens detest his actions, the larger and more devoted a bodyguard he needs?"

"Certainly."

"And who are these devoted guards? Where does he find them?"

"They flock to him voluntarily," he said, "as long as he pays."
D: Elegant: The tyrant uses perpetual war to impoverish citizens through taxes, keep them focused on survival, and destroy anyone suspected of independent thought. As he grows unpopular, he purges the brave, wise, noble, and wealthy -- a reverse medical purge that removes the good and keeps the bad. His blessed dilemma: live among the worst, hated by all, or die. He surrounds himself with paid mercenaries.
D: Summary: The tyrant uses war to impoverish and distract citizens, sending suspected free-thinkers to die at the front. As he grows unpopular and faces criticism from former allies, he purges the brave, wise, noble, and wealthy -- the opposite of a medical purge. Left with only the worst citizens, hated by all, he must maintain power through paid mercenary bodyguards.
D: Distilled: The tyrant purges the state's best citizens, creates perpetual war to impoverish and distract the rest, and relies on paid mercenaries as everyone of quality becomes his enemy.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Purgation of the state': katharsis -- the medical term applied ironically. The tyrant's purge is the inverse of the doctor's: removing the healthy tissue and preserving the diseased. -- The tyrant's paradox: to maintain power, he must destroy everyone of quality, leaving himself surrounded exclusively by the servile and corrupt. -- 'Happy man, he is the enemy of them all': bitter irony. -- The progression from popular champion to universally hated despot follows the internal logic of tyranny. -- Historical parallels: the purges of Dionysius I of Syracuse, and later the proscriptions of Sulla and the Roman emperors. -- 'They will flock to him if he pays them': mercenaries replace citizen-soldiers -- a sign of political degeneration in Greek thought.

D: Original:
By the dog! I said, here are more drones, of every sort and from every land.

Yes, he said, there are. But will he not desire to get them on the spot? How do you mean? He will rob the citizens of their slaves; he will then set them free and enrol them in his bodyguard.

To be sure, he said; and he will be able to trust them best of all.

What a blessed creature, I said, must this tyrant be; he has put to death the others and has these for his trusted friends.

Yes, he said; they are quite of his sort. Yes, I said, and these are the new citizens whom he has called into existence, who admire him and are his companions, while the good hate and avoid him.

Of course. Verily, then, tragedy is a wise thing and Euripides a great tragedian.

Why so? Why, because he is the author of the pregnant saying,

Tyrants are wise by living with the wise; and he clearly meant to say that they are the wise whom the tyrant makes his companions.

Yes, he said, and he also praises tyranny as godlike; and many other things of the same kind are said by him and by the other poets.

And therefore, I said, the tragic poets being wise men will forgive us and any others who live after our manner if we do not receive them into our State, because they are the eulogists of tyranny.

Yes, he said, those who have the wit will doubtless forgive us.

But they will continue to go to other cities and attract mobs, and hire voices fair and loud and persuasive, and draw the cities over to tyrannies and democracies.

Very true. Moreover, they are paid for this and receive honour --the greatest honour, as might be expected, from tyrants, and the next greatest from democracies; but the higher they ascend our constitution hill, the more their reputation fails, and seems unable from shortness of breath to proceed further.

True. But we are wandering from the subject: Let us therefore return and enquire how the tyrant will maintain that fair and numerous and various and ever-changing army of his.
D: Narrative:
"By the dog!" I said, "here are more drones, of every sort and from every land."

"Yes," he said, "there are."

"But will he not desire to get them on the spot?"

"How do you mean?"

"He will rob the citizens of their slaves; he will then set them free and enrol them in his bodyguard."

"To be sure," he said; "and he will be able to trust them best of all."

"What a blessed creature," I said, "must this tyrant be; he has put to death the others and has these for his trusted friends."

"Yes," he said; "they are quite of his sort."

"Yes," I said, "and these are the new citizens whom he has called into existence, who admire him and are his companions, while the good hate and avoid him."

"Of course."

"Verily, then, tragedy is a wise thing and Euripides a great tragedian."

"Why so?"

"Why, because he is the author of the pregnant saying, 'Tyrants are wise by living with the wise'; and he clearly meant to say that they are the wise whom the tyrant makes his companions."

"Yes," he said, "and he also praises tyranny as godlike; and many other things of the same kind are said by him and by the other poets."

"And therefore," I said, "the tragic poets being wise men will forgive us and any others who live after our manner if we do not receive them into our State, because they are the eulogists of tyranny."

"Yes," he said, "those who have the wit will doubtless forgive us."

"But they will continue to go to other cities and attract mobs, and hire voices fair and loud and persuasive, and draw the cities over to tyrannies and democracies."

"Very true."

"Moreover, they are paid for this and receive honour -- the greatest honour, as might be expected, from tyrants, and the next greatest from democracies; but the higher they ascend our constitution hill, the more their reputation fails, and seems unable from shortness of breath to proceed further."

"True."

"But we are wandering from the subject: Let us therefore return and enquire how the tyrant will maintain that fair and numerous and various and ever-changing army of his."
D: Refashioned:
"Good heavens!" I said. "More drones, of every kind and from every country."

"Yes, there are."

"But won't he also want to recruit locally?"

"How?"

"He'll confiscate citizens' slaves, free them, and enroll them in his bodyguard."

"Of course -- and they'll be the ones he trusts most."

"What a blessed creature this tyrant must be! He's killed all the good people and has freed slaves for his trusted companions."

"Yes, they're just his type."

"These are the new citizens he has created -- they admire him and are his companions, while every decent person hates and avoids him."

"Of course."

"So tragedy is truly wise, and Euripides is a great tragedian."

"Why?"

"Because he wrote the memorable line: 'Tyrants are wise by living with the wise.' He clearly meant that the 'wise' are whoever the tyrant chooses as companions."

"Yes, and Euripides also praises tyranny as godlike, along with many other such things -- as do the other poets."

"And that is why the tragic poets, wise as they are, will forgive us for not admitting them into our state -- since they are the eulogists of tyranny."

"Those with any sense will forgive us."

"But they'll continue visiting other cities, attracting crowds, hiring beautiful, powerful, persuasive voices, and drawing cities toward tyranny and democracy."

"Very true."

"And they're paid well for it -- most generously by tyrants, next most by democracies. But the higher up our constitutional hierarchy they go, the more their reputation fails, as if gasping for breath on the climb."

"True."

"But we're wandering from the subject. Let's return to the question: how will the tyrant maintain that fine, numerous, various, ever-changing army of his?"
D: Elegant: The tyrant frees citizens' slaves and makes them his bodyguard -- the only people he can trust. Having killed the good, he surrounds himself with freed slaves and foreign mercenaries. Euripides praised tyranny as godlike and said 'tyrants are wise by living with the wise' -- the 'wise' being whoever the tyrant chooses. Tragic poets are eulogists of tyranny, honored most by tyrants, next by democrats, least by better regimes.
D: Summary: The tyrant confiscates and frees citizens' slaves to form a loyal bodyguard. Socrates ironically quotes Euripides' praise of tyranny and notes that tragic poets are natural allies of tyrants, receiving the most honor from tyrannies and democracies while their reputation fades in better constitutions.
D: Distilled: The tyrant surrounds himself with freed slaves as his only trusted companions; tragic poets are tyranny's natural eulogists, honored most by the worst regimes.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'By the dog': Socrates' characteristic oath (ne ton kuna), a mild euphemism. -- Freeing slaves for the bodyguard: a historical practice. Dionysius I of Syracuse armed freed slaves, and the practice was common among Greek tyrants. -- Euripides quoted: the line 'Tyrants are wise by living with the wise' is from a lost play (possibly Aeolus or another). Socrates turns it ironically: the tyrant's 'wise' companions are just his cronies. -- 'Eulogists of tyranny': the expulsion of poets from the ideal state (Book III, 398a; Book X, 595a-607a) is here given political justification. -- The hierarchy: poets are most honored where there is least wisdom -- tyrannies first, democracies second, aristocracies least. -- 'Shortness of breath on the climb': poets cannot function where philosophical standards prevail.

D: Original:
If, he said, there are sacred treasures in the city, he will confiscate and spend them; and in so far as the fortunes of attainted persons may suffice, he will be able to diminish the taxes which he would otherwise have to impose upon the people.

And when these fail? Why, clearly, he said, then he and his boon companions, whether male or female, will be maintained out of his father's estate.

You mean to say that the people, from whom he has derived his being, will maintain him and his companions?
D: Narrative:
"If," he said, "there are sacred treasures in the city, he will confiscate and spend them; and in so far as the fortunes of attainted persons may suffice, he will be able to diminish the taxes which he would otherwise have to impose upon the people."

"And when these fail?"

"Why, clearly," he said, "then he and his boon companions, whether male or female, will be maintained out of his father's estate."

"You mean to say that the people, from whom he has derived his being, will maintain him and his companions?"
D: Refashioned:
"If there are sacred treasures in the city," he said, "the tyrant will confiscate and spend them. And as long as the wealth of those he has condemned holds out, he can reduce the taxes he'd otherwise impose on the people."

"And when that money runs out?"

"Obviously," he said, "he and his companions -- male and female alike -- will be maintained from his father's estate."

"You mean the people -- who gave him his power -- will have to support him and his entourage?"
D: Elegant: The tyrant funds himself first from sacred treasures and confiscated wealth. When those run out, he feeds off 'his father' -- the people who gave him power -- supporting his entourage at their expense.
D: Summary: The tyrant first funds his regime by confiscating sacred treasures and the property of condemned citizens, reducing taxes temporarily. When these sources are exhausted, he turns to 'his father's estate' -- the people themselves must maintain him and his companions.
D: Distilled: The tyrant exhausts sacred treasures and confiscated wealth, then turns to feed off the people themselves -- the 'father' who created him.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- Sacred treasures: temple wealth was theoretically inviolable but was frequently confiscated by tyrants and even democracies under financial pressure. Athens itself drew on the treasury of Athena during the Peloponnesian War. -- 'Father's estate': the people are the tyrant's 'father' because democracy gave birth to tyranny. The metaphor is developed fully in 8.61. -- 'Boon companions, whether male or female': the tyrant's court of favorites. -- The economic progression: confiscation of the condemned -> confiscation of sacred property -> direct taxation of the people -- each stage more desperate and more unpopular.

D: Original:
Yes, he said; they cannot help themselves. But what if the people fly into a passion, and aver that a grown-up son ought not to be supported by his father, but that the father should be supported by the son? The father did not bring him into being, or settle him in life, in order that when his son became a man he should himself be the servant of his own servants and should support him and his rabble of slaves and companions; but that his son should protect him, and that by his help he might be emancipated from the government of the rich and aristocratic, as they are termed. And so he bids him and his companions depart, just as any other father might drive out of the house a riotous son and his undesirable associates.

By heaven, he said, then the parent will discover what a monster he has been fostering in his bosom; and, when he wants to drive him out, he will find that he is weak and his son strong.

Why, you do not mean to say that the tyrant will use violence? What! beat his father if he opposes him?
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said; "they cannot help themselves."

"But what if the people fly into a passion, and aver that a grown-up son ought not to be supported by his father, but that the father should be supported by the son? The father did not bring him into being, or settle him in life, in order that when his son became a man he should himself be the servant of his own servants and should support him and his rabble of slaves and companions; but that his son should protect him, and that by his help he might be emancipated from the government of the rich and aristocratic, as they are termed. And so he bids him and his companions depart, just as any other father might drive out of the house a riotous son and his undesirable associates."

"By heaven," he said, "then the parent will discover what a monster he has been fostering in his bosom; and, when he wants to drive him out, he will find that he is weak and his son strong."

"Why, you do not mean to say that the tyrant will use violence? What! beat his father if he opposes him?"
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, they have no choice."

"But what happens when the people get angry and declare that a grown son shouldn't be supported by his father -- the father should be supported by the son? After all, the people didn't create the tyrant and set him up in life so that when he grew powerful, they would become servants of their own servants, supporting him and his rabble of slaves and cronies. They created him to protect them and free them from the domination of the rich and aristocratic. Now they order him and his companions to leave -- just as any father would throw out a riotous son and his disreputable friends."

"By heaven," he said, "then the parent will discover what a monster he's been nurturing. When he tries to drive the son out, he'll find that he is weak and the son is strong."

"You don't mean the tyrant would use violence? He would actually strike his father?"
D: Elegant: When the people finally rebel against supporting their creation, ordering the tyrant to leave like a father ejecting a riotous son, they discover the terrible truth: the monster they nurtured is now stronger than they are. The father is weak; the son is powerful.
D: Summary: When the people realize they have become servants of the tyrant they created and demand his departure -- like a father ejecting a riotous son -- they discover that the monster they nurtured is now stronger than they are. The parent is too weak to expel the child.
D: Distilled: The people who created the tyrant discover too late that the monster they nurtured is now stronger than they are, and they cannot expel him.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The father-son metaphor: democracy (the father) creates tyranny (the son) to protect itself from oligarchy. But the son grows monstrous and cannot be expelled. -- 'Monster he has been fostering': the language of monstrosity (teras) connects the tyrant to mythological beasts. -- 'Beat his father': parricide -- the ultimate Greek crime, here presented as the tyrant's relation to the people who created him. -- The political parable is psychologically acute: revolutionary movements that create strong leaders often find they cannot control them. -- Compare Herodotus I.96-100 on Deioces, who became tyrant of the Medes through a similar process. -- The people's helplessness is the tragedy of democratic self-destruction.

D: Original:
Yes, he will, having first disarmed him. Then he is a parricide, and a cruel guardian of an aged parent; and this is real tyranny, about which there can be no longer a mistake: as the saying is, the people who would escape the smoke which is the slavery of freemen, has fallen into the fire which is the tyranny of slaves. Thus liberty, getting out of all order and reason, passes into the harshest and bitterest form of slavery.

True, he said. Very well; and may we not rightly say that we have sufficiently discussed the nature of tyranny, and the manner of the transition from democracy to tyranny?

Yes, quite enough, he said.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, he will, having first disarmed him."

"Then he is a parricide, and a cruel guardian of an aged parent; and this is real tyranny, about which there can be no longer a mistake: as the saying is, the people who would escape the smoke which is the slavery of freemen, has fallen into the fire which is the tyranny of slaves. Thus liberty, getting out of all order and reason, passes into the harshest and bitterest form of slavery."

"True," he said.

"Very well; and may we not rightly say that we have sufficiently discussed the nature of tyranny, and the manner of the transition from democracy to tyranny?"

"Yes, quite enough," he said.
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, he will -- after first disarming the people."

"Then he is a parricide and a cruel guardian of an aged parent. This is tyranny in its true form, unmistakable now. As the saying goes, the people who tried to escape the smoke of servitude to the free have fallen into the fire of servitude to slaves. Liberty, pushed beyond all order and reason, transforms into the harshest and most bitter form of slavery."

"True."

"Can we say, then, that we have adequately discussed the nature of tyranny and the transition from democracy to tyranny?"

"Yes, quite enough."
D: Elegant: The tyrant disarms and beats his father -- the people. He is a parricide and a cruel guardian. The people who fled the smoke of servitude to free men have fallen into the fire of servitude to slaves. Liberty without order or reason becomes the harshest slavery.
D: Summary: The tyrant completes his parricide by disarming the people who created him. Socrates concludes: the people who tried to escape the 'smoke' of servitude to the free have fallen into the 'fire' of servitude to slaves. Liberty pushed beyond all order and reason transforms into the most extreme slavery.
D: Distilled: Liberty without order becomes the harshest slavery: the people flee the smoke of free men's rule and fall into the fire of tyranny -- parricide complete.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Having first disarmed him': the tyrant must disarm the people before striking -- a key practical step in establishing tyranny. -- Parricide (patroktonia): the ultimate violation of Greek sacred law, used here as the defining metaphor for tyranny's relationship to democracy. -- 'Smoke and fire': a proverbial expression meaning to escape a lesser evil only to encounter a greater one. -- 'Slavery of freemen' vs. 'tyranny of slaves': under oligarchy/aristocracy, the people serve free citizens (merely restrictive); under tyranny, they serve freed slaves and mercenaries (degrading and absolute). -- 'Liberty getting out of all order and reason': the Republic's final verdict on unlimited freedom. -- This passage completes Book VIII's descending sequence: aristocracy -> timocracy -> oligarchy -> democracy -> tyranny. -- The discussion of the tyrant's soul continues in Book IX, where Socrates examines the tyrant's inner misery and delivers the final judgment on justice vs. injustice.

D: Original:
Last of all comes the tyrannical man; about whom we have once more to ask, how is he formed out of the democratical? and how does he live, in happiness or in misery?

Yes, he said, he is the only one remaining. There is, however, I said, a previous question which remains unanswered.

What question? I do not think that we have adequately determined the nature and number of the appetites, and until this is accomplished the enquiry will always be confused.

Well, he said, it is not too late to supply the omission.

Very true, I said; and observe the point which I want to understand: Certain of the unnecessary pleasures and appetites I conceive to be unlawful; every one appears to have them, but in some persons they are controlled by the laws and by reason, and the better desires prevail over them-either they are wholly banished or they become few and weak; while in the case of others they are stronger, and there are more of them.
D: Narrative:
"Last of all comes the tyrannical man; about whom we have once more to ask, how is he formed out of the democratical? And how does he live, in happiness or in misery?"

"Yes," said Adeimantus, "he is the only one remaining."

"There is, however," Socrates said, "a previous question which remains unanswered."

"What question?"

"I do not think that we have adequately determined the nature and number of the appetites, and until this is accomplished the enquiry will always be confused."

"Well," he said, "it is not too late to supply the omission."

"Very true," Socrates said; "and observe the point which I want to understand: Certain of the unnecessary pleasures and appetites I conceive to be unlawful; every one appears to have them, but in some persons they are controlled by the laws and by reason, and the better desires prevail over them -- either they are wholly banished or they become few and weak; while in the case of others they are stronger, and there are more of them."
D: Refashioned:
"Now we come to the last figure in our sequence -- the tyrannical man. How does he develop out of the democratic type? And does he live happily or miserably?"

"Yes," said Adeimantus, "he's the only one we haven't examined yet."

"But there's a prior question we still haven't properly answered."

"What question?"

"We haven't adequately mapped out the nature and variety of human appetites. Until we do, our whole inquiry will remain muddled."

"Well, it's not too late to fix that."

"Good. Here's the key point I want us to understand: There are certain unnecessary pleasures and appetites that I'd call lawless. Everyone has them, but in some people they're kept in check by laws and reason -- the better desires overpower them, and they either vanish entirely or become weak and few. In others, though, these lawless appetites grow stronger and multiply."
D: Elegant: The tyrannical man emerges from the democratic type, but first we must map the lawless appetites. Everyone possesses unlawful desires, yet in some people reason and law suppress them until they become weak or vanish entirely. In others, these same desires grow strong and numerous.
D: Summary: Socrates begins the examination of how the tyrannical man develops from the democratic man, but first insists on clarifying the nature of lawless appetites. Everyone possesses unlawful desires, but they vary in strength depending on whether reason or appetite governs the soul.
D: Distilled: Everyone harbors lawless desires; the difference between people lies in whether reason suppresses or appetite amplifies them.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- Transition from Book VIII's democratic man to the tyrannical man -- Continues the degeneration sequence: aristocratic > timocratic > oligarchic > democratic > tyrannical -- The 'unnecessary pleasures' were first distinguished in Book VIII (558d-559d) -- Lawless appetites foreshadow the dream analysis that follows -- Cross-ref: Book IV's tripartite soul (reason, spirit, appetite)

D: Original: Which appetites do you mean? I mean those which are awake when the reasoning and human and ruling power is asleep; then the wild beast within us, gorged with meat or drink, starts up and having shaken off sleep, goes forth to satisfy his desires; and there is no conceivable folly or crime --not excepting incest or any other unnatural union, or parricide, or the eating of forbidden food --which at such a time, when he has parted company with all shame and sense, a man may not be ready to commit.
D: Narrative:
"Which appetites do you mean?"

"I mean those which are awake when the reasoning and human and ruling power is asleep; then the wild beast within us, gorged with meat or drink, starts up and having shaken off sleep, goes forth to satisfy his desires; and there is no conceivable folly or crime -- not excepting incest or any other unnatural union, or parricide, or the eating of forbidden food -- which at such a time, when he has parted company with all shame and sense, a man may not be ready to commit."
D: Refashioned:
"Which appetites exactly?"

"The ones that come alive when our reasoning, civilized, governing faculty falls asleep. That's when the wild beast inside us -- bloated with food or drink -- shakes off sleep and goes hunting to satisfy its cravings. At such moments there is no imaginable folly or crime -- not even incest, unnatural acts, parricide, or the eating of forbidden food -- that a person won't be ready to commit, having abandoned all shame and sense."
D: Elegant: The lawless appetites reveal themselves when reason sleeps. Then the wild beast within us, freed from all shame and sense, is ready to commit any conceivable crime -- incest, parricide, or any forbidden act.
D: Summary: Socrates identifies lawless appetites as those that emerge when reason sleeps: the inner 'wild beast,' freed from shame, is capable of any crime including incest, parricide, and forbidden acts.
D: Distilled: When reason sleeps, the wild beast within is capable of every conceivable crime without shame.
Notes: Socrates speaking -- The 'wild beast' metaphor is central to Plato's moral psychology -- Anticipates Freud's theory of the unconscious and dream symbolism by over two millennia -- The list of crimes (incest, parricide, forbidden food) recurs in 9.12 as waking acts of the tyrannical man -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 493a-494a on the insatiable soul -- The 'eating of forbidden food' may reference ritual prohibitions or cannibalism taboos

D: Original: Most true, he said. But when a man's pulse is healthy and temperate, and when before going to sleep he has awakened his rational powers, and fed them on noble thoughts and enquiries, collecting himself in meditation; after having first indulged his appetites neither too much nor too little, but just enough to lay them to sleep, and prevent them and their enjoyments and pains from interfering with the higher principle --which he leaves in the solitude of pure abstraction, free to contemplate and aspire to the knowledge of the unknown, whether in past, present, or future: when again he has allayed the passionate element, if he has a quarrel against any one --I say, when, after pacifying the two irrational principles, he rouses up the third, which is reason, before he takes his rest, then, as you know, he attains truth most nearly, and is least likely to be the sport of fantastic and lawless visions.
D: Narrative:
"Most true," he said.

"But when a man's pulse is healthy and temperate, and when before going to sleep he has awakened his rational powers, and fed them on noble thoughts and enquiries, collecting himself in meditation; after having first indulged his appetites neither too much nor too little, but just enough to lay them to sleep, and prevent them and their enjoyments and pains from interfering with the higher principle -- which he leaves in the solitude of pure abstraction, free to contemplate and aspire to the knowledge of the unknown, whether in past, present, or future: when again he has allayed the passionate element, if he has a quarrel against any one -- I say, when, after pacifying the two irrational principles, he rouses up the third, which is reason, before he takes his rest, then, as you know, he attains truth most nearly, and is least likely to be the sport of fantastic and lawless visions."
D: Refashioned:
"Absolutely true."

"But consider the opposite case. When a man is healthy and moderate, and before sleep he has engaged his rational mind with noble thoughts and inquiry, gathering himself in reflection; when he has satisfied his appetites just enough to quiet them -- neither starving them nor overfeeding them -- so they won't disturb the higher faculty; when he leaves that higher faculty free in pure contemplation to reach toward knowledge of what it doesn't yet understand, whether past, present, or future; and when he has also calmed his spirited element, settling any grudges -- when, having quieted these two irrational parts, he activates the third, reason, before resting: then he comes closest to truth, and his dreams are least likely to be lawless and fantastical."
D: Elegant: The person who quiets both appetite and spirit before sleep, engaging reason through noble contemplation, comes closest to truth in their dreams. Balanced moderation of the lower parts frees the rational faculty for genuine insight.
D: Summary: Socrates describes the ideal preparation for sleep: moderate the appetites, calm the spirited element, and activate reason through contemplation. Under these conditions, a person's dreams approach truth rather than lawless fantasy.
D: Distilled: When reason is nourished and appetite quieted before sleep, dreams become vehicles for truth rather than lawless fantasy.
Notes: Socrates speaking -- This passage is Plato's theory of dream psychology and rational dreaming -- The three parts of the soul from Book IV recur: appetite, spirit (passion), reason -- 'Pure abstraction' (auto kath' hauto) connects to the Forms discussed in Books V-VII -- The technique described resembles contemplative practices found in Pythagorean traditions -- Cross-ref: Philebus on mixed vs. pure pleasures -- Anticipates the divided line and philosopher's ascent of Book VII

D: Original:
I quite agree. In saying this I have been running into a digression; but the point which I desire to note is that in all of us, even in good men, there is a lawless wild-beast nature, which peers out in sleep. Pray, consider whether I am right, and you agree with me.

Yes, I agree. And now remember the character which we attributed to the democratic man. He was supposed from his youth upwards to have been trained under a miserly parent, who encouraged the saving appetites in him, but discountenanced the unnecessary, which aim only at amusement and ornament?
D: Narrative:
"In saying this I have been running into a digression; but the point which I desire to note is that in all of us, even in good men, there is a lawless wild-beast nature, which peers out in sleep. Pray, consider whether I am right, and you agree with me."

"Yes, I agree."

"And now remember the character which we attributed to the democratic man. He was supposed from his youth upwards to have been trained under a miserly parent, who encouraged the saving appetites in him, but discountenanced the unnecessary, which aim only at amusement and ornament?"
D: Refashioned:
"I've been digressing. But the essential point is this: in all of us, even the best of us, there lurks a lawless, beastly nature that reveals itself in sleep. Do you agree?"

"Yes, I agree."

"Good. Now recall the character we gave the democratic man. We said he was raised from youth under a miserly father who encouraged the money-saving appetites but suppressed the unnecessary ones -- those aimed only at amusement and luxury."
D: Elegant: In all of us, even the virtuous, a lawless beast-nature lurks and reveals itself in sleep. Returning to the main argument: the democratic man was raised by an oligarchic father who cultivated thrift and suppressed unnecessary pleasures.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes his digression on dreams by affirming that even good people harbor a lawless inner nature visible in sleep. He then returns to the democratic man's origin: raised by a miserly oligarchic father who suppressed unnecessary pleasures.
D: Distilled: Even good people harbor a lawless beast-nature revealed in sleep; the democratic man was raised under an oligarchic father's thrift.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- 'Even in good men' universalizes the lawless appetites -- a key psychological insight -- Transitions back from the dream digression to the main genealogy of character types -- The democratic man's origin under an oligarchic father was established in Book VIII (558c-562a) -- 'Saving appetites' vs. 'unnecessary appetites' maps onto the oligarchic soul's internal conflict -- Cross-ref: Book VIII on the oligarchic man's character

D: Original:
True. And then he got into the company of a more refined, licentious sort of people, and taking to all their wanton ways rushed into the opposite extreme from an abhorrence of his father's meanness. At last, being a better man than his corruptors, he was drawn in both directions until he halted midway and led a life, not of vulgar and slavish passion, but of what he deemed moderate indulgence in various pleasures. After this manner the democrat was generated out of the oligarch?

Yes, he said; that was our view of him, and is so still.

And now, I said, years will have passed away, and you must conceive this man, such as he is, to have a son, who is brought up in his father's principles.
D: Narrative:
"True. And then he got into the company of a more refined, licentious sort of people, and taking to all their wanton ways rushed into the opposite extreme from an abhorrence of his father's meanness. At last, being a better man than his corruptors, he was drawn in both directions until he halted midway and led a life, not of vulgar and slavish passion, but of what he deemed moderate indulgence in various pleasures. After this manner the democrat was generated out of the oligarch?"

"Yes," he said; "that was our view of him, and is so still."

"And now," Socrates said, "years will have passed away, and you must conceive this man, such as he is, to have a son, who is brought up in his father's principles."
D: Refashioned:
"Right. Then this young man fell in with a more sophisticated, pleasure-seeking crowd. Revolted by his father's stinginess, he swung to the opposite extreme, embracing their indulgent ways. But since he was fundamentally better than his corruptors, he was pulled in both directions until he settled in the middle -- living not a life of crude, slavish passion, but what he considered moderate enjoyment of varied pleasures. That's how the democratic man emerged from the oligarchic one?"

"Yes, that was our account, and it still holds."

"Now, imagine years have passed. This democratic man has a son, raised according to his father's principles."
D: Elegant: The democratic man, reacting against his oligarchic father's miserliness, settled into what he considered moderate indulgence. Now imagine this man has a son, raised on those same democratic principles.
D: Summary: The democratic man emerged as a compromise between his oligarchic father's thrift and the licentiousness of his companions, settling into moderate indulgence. Now Socrates asks us to imagine this man's son, raised on democratic principles.
D: Distilled: The democratic man's son, raised on principles of moderate indulgence, becomes the raw material for tyrannical transformation.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- Recap of Book VIII's account of the democratic man's formation (559d-561e) -- 'Better man than his corruptors' -- the democratic man retains some moral sense -- The generational pattern continues: each regime-type produces a son who degenerates further -- 'Moderate indulgence in various pleasures' characterizes democratic equality of desires -- The son of the democrat will become the tyrant -- the final stage of degeneration

D: Original: I can imagine him. Then you must further imagine the same thing to happen to the son which has already happened to the father: --he is drawn into a perfectly lawless life, which by his seducers is termed perfect liberty; and his father and friends take part with his moderate desires, and the opposite party assist the opposite ones. As soon as these dire magicians and tyrant-makers find that they are losing their hold on him, they contrive to implant in him a master passion, to be lord over his idle and spendthrift lusts --a sort of monstrous winged drone --that is the only image which will adequately describe him.
D: Narrative:
"I can imagine him."

"Then you must further imagine the same thing to happen to the son which has already happened to the father: he is drawn into a perfectly lawless life, which by his seducers is termed 'perfect liberty'; and his father and friends take part with his moderate desires, and the opposite party assist the opposite ones. As soon as these dire magicians and tyrant-makers find that they are losing their hold on him, they contrive to implant in him a master passion, to be lord over his idle and spendthrift lusts -- a sort of monstrous winged drone -- that is the only image which will adequately describe him."
D: Refashioned:
"I can picture him."

"Then imagine the same pattern repeating with the son. He's drawn into a completely lawless lifestyle, which his seducers call 'perfect freedom.' His father and moderate friends pull him one way; the opposite faction pulls him the other. When these sinister manipulators -- these tyrant-makers -- sense they're losing their grip on him, they engineer a master passion to implant in his soul, one that will rule over all his idle, wasteful desires. Picture it as a kind of monstrous winged drone -- that's the only image that fits."
D: Elegant: The democrat's son is drawn into lawlessness that his seducers call 'perfect freedom.' When the tyrant-makers feel their hold slipping, they implant a master passion in him -- a monstrous winged drone that lords over all other desires.
D: Summary: The democratic man's son is seduced into lawlessness disguised as freedom. When his corruptors fear losing influence, they implant a dominant master passion -- likened to a monstrous winged drone -- to rule over all his other desires.
D: Distilled: Tyrant-makers implant a master passion -- a monstrous drone -- to dominate all other desires in the proto-tyrannical soul.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- 'Perfect liberty' as a euphemism for lawlessness -- central to Plato's critique of radical democracy -- 'Dire magicians and tyrant-makers' refers to demagogues and flatterers -- The 'monstrous winged drone' develops the drone metaphor from Book VIII (552c, 564b) -- The 'master passion' (eros) is the tyrant within the soul -- Cross-ref: Book VIII's drones (stingless and stinging) as parasitic elements -- The drone image connects appetite to parasitism

D: Original: Yes, he said, that is the only adequate image of him. And when his other lusts, amid clouds of incense and perfumes and garlands and wines, and all the pleasures of a dissolute life, now let loose, come buzzing around him, nourishing to the utmost the sting of desire which they implant in his drone-like nature, then at last this lord of the soul, having Madness for the captain of his guard, breaks out into a frenzy: and if he finds in himself any good opinions or appetites in process of formation, and there is in him any sense of shame remaining, to these better principles he puts an end, and casts them forth until he has purged away temperance and brought in madness to the full.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is the only adequate image of him."

"And when his other lusts, amid clouds of incense and perfumes and garlands and wines, and all the pleasures of a dissolute life, now let loose, come buzzing around him, nourishing to the utmost the sting of desire which they implant in his drone-like nature, then at last this lord of the soul, having Madness for the captain of his guard, breaks out into a frenzy: and if he finds in himself any good opinions or appetites in process of formation, and there is in him any sense of shame remaining, to these better principles he puts an end, and casts them forth until he has purged away temperance and brought in madness to the full."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that's the only image that does him justice."

"And when his other lusts -- set loose amid clouds of incense, perfumes, garlands, wine, and every dissolute pleasure -- come buzzing around him, feeding the sting of desire in his drone-like nature to its maximum intensity, then this master passion, with Madness as captain of its bodyguard, erupts into frenzy. If it discovers any good opinions or healthy appetites still forming within him, any lingering trace of shame, it destroys them. It drives out every better impulse until it has purged the soul of all temperance and filled it with sheer madness."
D: Elegant: The master passion, surrounded by dissolute pleasures, grows to frenzy with Madness as its bodyguard captain. It purges the soul of every remaining good impulse, driving out temperance and replacing it with complete madness.
D: Summary: The tyrannical soul's master passion, nourished by dissolute pleasures, erupts into frenzy with Madness as its captain. It systematically destroys all remaining good opinions and shame, purging temperance and replacing it with total madness.
D: Distilled: The master passion, captained by Madness, purges every trace of temperance from the soul and replaces it with frenzy.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- Personification of Madness (Mania) as 'captain of the guard' parallels political bodyguards of actual tyrants -- The buzzing lusts extend the drone/bee metaphor -- 'Purged away temperance and brought in madness' inverts the philosophical purgation of the soul -- Clouds of incense, perfumes, garlands evoke Dionysian revelry and symposium culture -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 244a-245c on divine vs. pathological madness -- The tyrannical soul undergoes a dark parody of philosophical conversion

D: Original:
Yes, he said, that is the way in which the tyrannical man is generated.

And is not this the reason why of old love has been called a tyrant?

I should not wonder. Further, I said, has not a drunken man also the spirit of a tyrant?

He has. And you know that a man who is deranged and not right in his mind, will fancy that he is able to rule, not only over men, but also over the gods?

That he will. And the tyrannical man in the true sense of the word comes into being when, either under the influence of nature, or habit, or both, he becomes drunken, lustful, passionate? O my friend, is not that so?

Assuredly. Such is the man and such is his origin. And next, how does he live?

Suppose, as people facetiously say, you were to tell me.

I imagine, I said, at the next step in his progress, that there will be feasts and carousals and revellings and courtezans, and all that sort of thing; Love is the lord of the house within him, and orders all the concerns of his soul.

That is certain. Yes; and every day and every night desires grow up many and formidable, and their demands are many.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is the way in which the tyrannical man is generated."

"And is not this the reason why of old love has been called a tyrant?"

"I should not wonder."

"Further," Socrates said, "has not a drunken man also the spirit of a tyrant?"

"He has."

"And you know that a man who is deranged and not right in his mind, will fancy that he is able to rule, not only over men, but also over the gods?"

"That he will."

"And the tyrannical man in the true sense of the word comes into being when, either under the influence of nature, or habit, or both, he becomes drunken, lustful, passionate? O my friend, is not that so?"

"Assuredly."

"Such is the man and such is his origin. And next, how does he live?"

"Suppose, as people facetiously say, you were to tell me."

"I imagine," Socrates said, "at the next step in his progress, that there will be feasts and carousals and revellings and courtezans, and all that sort of thing; Love is the lord of the house within him, and orders all the concerns of his soul."

"That is certain."

"Yes; and every day and every night desires grow up many and formidable, and their demands are many."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that's exactly how the tyrannical man comes into being."

"And isn't this why love has traditionally been called a tyrant?"

"That wouldn't surprise me."

"And doesn't a drunk man also have the spirit of a tyrant?"

"He does."

"And you know that a deranged person imagines he can rule not only over other people but even over the gods?"

"Absolutely."

"So the truly tyrannical man comes into being when, through nature or habit or both, he becomes drunk with lust and passion. Isn't that right, my friend?"

"It is."

"That's his character and his origin. Now -- how does he actually live?"

"Why don't you tell me, as the saying goes."

"I imagine the next stage involves feasts, drinking parties, orgies, and prostitutes -- the whole catalogue of debauchery. Erotic love is master of the house within him, directing everything in his soul."

"No doubt."

"And every day and every night, new desires multiply, growing fiercer and more demanding."
D: Elegant: Love has always been called a tyrant. The tyrannical man, made drunk by lust through nature or habit, lives a life of feasts and debauchery. Eros rules his soul as master of the house, and his desires multiply daily, growing ever more demanding.
D: Summary: Socrates draws parallels between the tyrannical soul and the states of erotic obsession, drunkenness, and madness. The tyrannical man, driven by Eros as master of his soul, lives amid constant feasting and debauchery, with desires multiplying daily.
D: Distilled: Eros rules the tyrannical soul like a despot, filling life with debauchery as desires multiply without limit.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- 'Love has been called a tyrant' references Greek poetic tradition (Sophocles, Euripides) -- Eros as tyrant of the soul is Plato's master metaphor for the tyrannical personality -- The triad of drunkenness, lust, and madness maps onto appetite, eros, and irrationality -- 'Rule over the gods' indicates hubris (cf. Xerxes in Herodotus) -- Cross-ref: Symposium 177d on Eros as powerful god; Phaedrus on the two horses of the soul -- The facetious tone ('suppose you were to tell me') shows the dialogue's informal register

D: Original: They are indeed, he said. His revenues, if he has any, are soon spent. True. Then comes debt and the cutting down of his property. Of course. When he has nothing left, must not his desires, crowding in the nest like young ravens, be crying aloud for food; and he, goaded on by them, and especially by love himself, who is in a manner the captain of them, is in a frenzy, and would fain discover whom he can defraud or despoil of his property, in order that he may gratify them?
D: Narrative:
"They are indeed," he said.

"His revenues, if he has any, are soon spent."

"True."

"Then comes debt and the cutting down of his property."

"Of course."

"When he has nothing left, must not his desires, crowding in the nest like young ravens, be crying aloud for food; and he, goaded on by them, and especially by love himself, who is in a manner the captain of them, is in a frenzy, and would fain discover whom he can defraud or despoil of his property, in order that he may gratify them?"
D: Refashioned:
"They certainly are."

"Whatever income he has is quickly spent."

"True."

"Then come debts and the liquidation of his property."

"Naturally."

"And when he has nothing left, won't his desires -- crowding in the nest like hungry young ravens crying out for food -- goad him on? With Eros himself as their ringleader, he falls into a frenzy, desperately searching for someone to defraud or rob in order to feed those cravings."
D: Elegant: The tyrannical man burns through his money, falls into debt, and liquidates his property. His desires, like hungry ravens in a nest, drive him to frenzy -- with Eros as their captain -- seeking anyone to defraud or rob.
D: Summary: The tyrannical man quickly exhausts his resources, falls into debt, and sells off property. His insatiable desires, led by Eros, then drive him to seek victims to defraud and rob.
D: Distilled: Bankrupted by insatiable desire, the tyrannical man turns to fraud and theft under the captaincy of Eros.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- The raven simile (young ravens crying for food) emphasizes the insatiable, predatory nature of desire -- Economic ruin as the first consequence of tyrannical character -- 'Love himself, who is in a manner the captain' continues the Eros-as-tyrant metaphor -- The progression: spend > debt > sell property > defraud others mirrors political tyranny's economic trajectory -- Cross-ref: Book VIII on the tyrant's need to keep making war to maintain power

D: Original:
Yes, that is sure to be the case. He must have money, no matter how, if he is to escape horrid pains and pangs.

He must. And as in himself there was a succession of pleasures, and the new got the better of the old and took away their rights, so he being younger will claim to have more than his father and his mother, and if he has spent his own share of the property, he will take a slice of theirs.

No doubt he will. And if his parents will not give way, then he will try first of all to cheat and deceive them.

Very true. And if he fails, then he will use force and plunder them.

Yes, probably. And if the old man and woman fight for their own, what then, my friend? Will the creature feel any compunction at tyrannizing over them?

Nay, he said, I should not feel at all comfortable about his parents.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, that is sure to be the case."

"He must have money, no matter how, if he is to escape horrid pains and pangs."

"He must."

"And as in himself there was a succession of pleasures, and the new got the better of the old and took away their rights, so he being younger will claim to have more than his father and his mother, and if he has spent his own share of the property, he will take a slice of theirs."

"No doubt he will."

"And if his parents will not give way, then he will try first of all to cheat and deceive them."

"Very true."

"And if he fails, then he will use force and plunder them."

"Yes, probably."

"And if the old man and woman fight for their own, what then, my friend? Will the creature feel any compunction at tyrannizing over them?"

"Nay," he said, "I should not feel at all comfortable about his parents."
D: Refashioned:
"That's certain to happen."

"He must have money by any means necessary, or face terrible suffering."

"He must."

"And just as within himself new pleasures displaced old ones and seized their place, so this younger man will claim a larger share than his parents. Once he's blown through his own inheritance, he'll help himself to theirs."

"No doubt."

"And if they won't hand it over, he'll first try to cheat and manipulate them."

"Absolutely."

"And if that fails, he'll use force and simply take it."

"Probably, yes."

"And if the old man and woman fight back to protect what's theirs -- will this creature feel any guilt about tyrannizing his own parents?"

"I wouldn't feel at all safe if I were his parents."
D: Elegant: Desperate for money, the tyrannical man first exhausts his own inheritance, then cheats his parents, then robs them by force. Just as new pleasures displaced old ones within his soul, the son displaces his parents' rights without compunction.
D: Summary: The tyrannical man, desperate for funds, progresses from spending his own inheritance to cheating his parents, to robbing them by force. He feels no compunction about tyrannizing those who raised him.
D: Distilled: The tyrannical man's escalating desperation leads him from fraud to violence against his own parents without remorse.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- The internal logic of appetite mirrors external behavior: new pleasures displace old ones, so the son displaces parents' rights -- The escalation (cheat > deceive > force > plunder) shows moral degeneration as a progressive sequence -- 'The creature' (to therion) dehumanizes the tyrannical man -- Adeimantus' discomfort ('not feel at all comfortable') shows the emotional weight of the argument -- Cross-ref: Book IV on justice as each part doing its own work vs. this inversion -- Parricide theme connects to 9.2's dream-crimes becoming waking reality

D: Original:
But, O heavens! Adeimantus, on account of some newfangled love of a harlot, who is anything but a necessary connection, can you believe that he would strike the mother who is his ancient friend and necessary to his very existence, and would place her under the authority of the other, when she is brought under the same roof with her; or that, under like circumstances, he would do the same to his withered old father, first and most indispensable of friends, for the sake of some newly found blooming youth who is the reverse of indispensable?

Yes, indeed, he said; I believe that he would. Truly, then, I said, a tyrannical son is a blessing to his father and mother.
D: Narrative:
"But, O heavens! Adeimantus, on account of some newfangled love of a harlot, who is anything but a necessary connection, can you believe that he would strike the mother who is his ancient friend and necessary to his very existence, and would place her under the authority of the other, when she is brought under the same roof with her; or that, under like circumstances, he would do the same to his withered old father, first and most indispensable of friends, for the sake of some newly found blooming youth who is the reverse of indispensable?"

"Yes, indeed," he said; "I believe that he would."

"Truly, then," Socrates said, "a tyrannical son is a blessing to his father and mother."
D: Refashioned:
"But good heavens, Adeimantus! Can you believe that for the sake of some new infatuation with a prostitute -- someone entirely unnecessary to his life -- he would actually strike his own mother, his oldest friend, essential to his very existence? That he'd subject her to the authority of this newcomer under the same roof? Or that he'd treat his frail old father the same way -- his first and most indispensable companion -- all for some fresh young lover who is anything but indispensable?"

"Yes, I genuinely believe he would."

"What a blessing, then, is a tyrannical son to his father and mother!"
D: Elegant: The tyrannical man would strike his own mother and subjugate his aged father -- his oldest, most essential bonds -- for the sake of a passing infatuation with someone entirely dispensable. A tyrannical son is quite the blessing to his parents.
D: Summary: Socrates expresses horror that the tyrannical man would abuse his own parents for the sake of a new lover or prostitute, subordinating his deepest family bonds to fleeting desire. He calls a tyrannical son a 'blessing' with bitter irony.
D: Distilled: The tyrannical man would abuse his own parents to serve a passing infatuation, subordinating essential bonds to dispensable desires.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- 'O heavens!' marks Socrates' rhetorical outrage at the moral enormity -- The contrast between 'ancient friend and necessary' (mother) vs. 'newfangled' and 'reverse of indispensable' (lover) sharpens the irony -- 'A tyrannical son is a blessing' is bitter Socratic irony -- The abuse of parents fulfills the parricide threatened in 9.2's dream-crimes -- Cross-ref: Laws 881a-882c on penalties for striking parents -- The passage dramatizes how eros-tyranny inverts natural bonds of philia

D: Original: He is indeed, he replied. He first takes their property, and when that falls, and pleasures are beginning to swarm in the hive of his soul, then he breaks into a house, or steals the garments of some nightly wayfarer; next he proceeds to clear a temple. Meanwhile the old opinions which he had when a child, and which gave judgment about good and evil, are overthrown by those others which have just been emancipated, and are now the bodyguard of love and share his empire. These in his democratic days, when he was still subject to the laws and to his father, were only let loose in the dreams of sleep. But now that he is under the dominion of love, he becomes always and in waking reality what he was then very rarely and in a dream only; he will commit the foulest murder, or eat forbidden food, or be guilty of any other horrid act. Love is his tyrant, and lives lordly in him and lawlessly, and being himself a king, leads him on, as a tyrant leads a State, to the performance of any reckless deed by which he can maintain himself and the rabble of his associates, whether those whom evil communications have brought in from without, or those whom he himself has allowed to break loose within him by reason of a similar evil nature in himself. Have we not here a picture of his way of life?
D: Narrative:
"He is indeed," he replied.

"He first takes their property, and when that falls, and pleasures are beginning to swarm in the hive of his soul, then he breaks into a house, or steals the garments of some nightly wayfarer; next he proceeds to clear a temple. Meanwhile the old opinions which he had when a child, and which gave judgment about good and evil, are overthrown by those others which have just been emancipated, and are now the bodyguard of love and share his empire. These in his democratic days, when he was still subject to the laws and to his father, were only let loose in the dreams of sleep. But now that he is under the dominion of love, he becomes always and in waking reality what he was then very rarely and in a dream only; he will commit the foulest murder, or eat forbidden food, or be guilty of any other horrid act. Love is his tyrant, and lives lordly in him and lawlessly, and being himself a king, leads him on, as a tyrant leads a State, to the performance of any reckless deed by which he can maintain himself and the rabble of his associates, whether those whom evil communications have brought in from without, or those whom he himself has allowed to break loose within him by reason of a similar evil nature in himself. Have we not here a picture of his way of life?"
D: Refashioned:
"He certainly is."

"First he takes his parents' property. When that runs out and pleasures keep swarming in the hive of his soul, he breaks into houses, robs travelers at night, and plunders temples. Meanwhile, the moral convictions he held as a child -- his sense of good and evil -- are overthrown by the newly liberated desires that now serve as the bodyguard of Eros and share his tyranny. In his democratic days, when he still obeyed laws and respected his father, these desires only emerged in dreams. But now, under the dominion of Eros, he becomes in waking life what he previously was only rarely, in sleep: he commits the foulest murders, eats forbidden food, and is capable of any atrocity. Love is his tyrant, ruling within him like a king, driving him to every reckless act needed to sustain itself and the mob of desires -- whether imported from corrupt company or unleashed from within by his own corrupt nature. Isn't this the picture of how he lives?"
D: Elegant: After exhausting his parents' wealth, the tyrannical man turns to burglary, robbery, and temple-plundering. His childhood moral convictions are overthrown by desires that now guard Eros. What once surfaced only in dreams becomes waking reality: murder, sacrilege, every atrocity. Love rules him as tyrant, driving every reckless act.
D: Summary: The tyrannical man, having depleted family resources, turns to crime -- housebreaking, robbery, temple-plundering. His childhood morality is overthrown by desires serving Eros. The crimes that once appeared only in dreams become his waking reality, as Love rules him like an internal tyrant.
D: Distilled: The tyrannical man's dream-crimes become waking reality as Eros overthrows childhood morality and rules the soul as absolute tyrant.
