Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- Central passage connecting the dream analysis (9.2-9.3) to waking tyrannical behavior -- 'Hive of his soul' continues the bee/drone imagery from 9.6-9.7 -- 'Bodyguard of love' parallels the tyrant's political bodyguard (Book VIII, 566b-567d) -- Temple robbery was among the gravest crimes in Athens -- 'What he was in a dream only' is the key thesis: the tyrannical man lives the nightmare -- Cross-ref: Book II's challenge (360b-362c) on the unjust man who appears just -- 'Evil communications' echoes Menander's famous line later quoted by Paul (1 Corinthians 15:33)

D: Original:
Yes, indeed, he said. And if there are only a few of them in the State, the rest of the people are well disposed, they go away and become the bodyguard or mercenary soldiers of some other tyrant who may probably want them for a war; and if there is no war, they stay at home and do many little pieces of mischief in the city.

What sort of mischief? For example, they are the thieves, burglars, cutpurses, footpads, robbers of temples, man-stealers of the community; or if they are able to speak they turn informers, and bear false witness, and take bribes.

A small catalogue of evils, even if the perpetrators of them are few in number.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, indeed," he said.

"And if there are only a few of them in the State, the rest of the people are well disposed, they go away and become the bodyguard or mercenary soldiers of some other tyrant who may probably want them for a war; and if there is no war, they stay at home and do many little pieces of mischief in the city."

"What sort of mischief?"

"For example, they are the thieves, burglars, cutpurses, footpads, robbers of temples, man-stealers of the community; or if they are able to speak they turn informers, and bear false witness, and take bribes."

"A small catalogue of evils, even if the perpetrators of them are few in number."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, indeed."

"Now, if there are only a few of these tyrannical types in a state and the rest of the population is well-disposed, they leave and become bodyguards or mercenaries for some other tyrant who needs soldiers for a war. If there's no war to fight, they stay home and commit all manner of petty crimes in the city."

"What kind of crimes?"

"They become the city's thieves, burglars, pickpockets, muggers, temple-robbers, and kidnappers. If they're articulate, they become informers who bear false witness and take bribes."

"That's quite a catalogue of evils, even if the criminals are few."
D: Elegant: When few tyrannical men exist in a state, they become mercenaries abroad or criminals at home -- thieves, burglars, pickpockets, muggers, temple-robbers, kidnappers, false witnesses, and bribe-takers.
D: Summary: When only a few tyrannical types exist in a city, they either serve as mercenaries for foreign tyrants or remain as domestic criminals -- thieves, robbers, kidnappers, false witnesses, and bribe-takers.
D: Distilled: Tyrannical individuals who lack political power become mercenaries abroad or criminals at home.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- Transition from the individual tyrannical man to his social role -- 'Bodyguard or mercenary soldiers' connects to the tyrant's need for armed supporters (Book VIII, 567d-568a) -- 'Man-stealers' (andrapodistai) refers to slave-traders/kidnappers -- 'If they are able to speak' distinguishes between violent criminals and intellectual criminals (corrupt orators, sycophants) -- The sycophant (professional informer/false accuser) was a well-known Athenian social type -- 'A small catalogue of evils' is Adeimantus' ironic understatement

D: Original: Yes, I said; but small and great are comparative terms, and all these things, in the misery and evil which they inflict upon a State, do not come within a thousand miles of the tyrant; when this noxious class and their followers grow numerous and become conscious of their strength, assisted by the infatuation of the people, they choose from among themselves the one who has most of the tyrant in his own soul, and him they create their tyrant.
D: Narrative: "Yes," Socrates said; "but small and great are comparative terms, and all these things, in the misery and evil which they inflict upon a State, do not come within a thousand miles of the tyrant; when this noxious class and their followers grow numerous and become conscious of their strength, assisted by the infatuation of the people, they choose from among themselves the one who has most of the tyrant in his own soul, and him they create their tyrant."
D: Refashioned: "Yes, but 'small' and 'great' are relative terms. All these crimes, in the misery and evil they inflict on a state, don't come within a thousand miles of what happens when a full tyrant emerges. When this toxic class and their followers grow numerous enough to become aware of their collective strength -- aided by the people's own delusion -- they select from among themselves the one with the most tyrannical soul, and they make him their tyrant."
D: Elegant: These individual crimes pale beside full-blown tyranny. When the tyrannical class grows numerous and conscious of its strength, aided by the people's delusion, they choose the most tyrannical soul among them as their ruler.
D: Summary: Individual tyrannical criminals are minor compared to full political tyranny. When enough such people gather and recognize their collective power, aided by popular delusion, they elevate the most tyrannical soul among them to be their tyrant.
D: Distilled: When tyrannical individuals reach critical mass, aided by popular delusion, they elevate the most tyrannical soul to absolute power.
Notes: Socrates speaking -- Bridges the individual tyrannical character to political tyranny -- 'Infatuation of the people' (aphrosyne tou demou) -- the masses' complicity in tyranny's rise -- 'Most of the tyrant in his own soul' -- the worst soul gets the most power, an anti-meritocracy -- Cross-ref: Book VIII (565c-566d) on how the people's champion becomes tyrant -- Echoes Thucydides on the rise of demagogues during democratic crises -- The selection mechanism inverts the philosopher-king ideal of Books V-VII

D: Original:
Yes, he said, and he will be the most fit to be a tyrant.

If the people yield, well and good; but if they resist him, as he began by beating his own father and mother, so now, if he has the power, he beats them, and will keep his dear old fatherland or motherland, as the Cretans say, in subjection to his young retainers whom he has introduced to be their rulers and masters. This is the end of his passions and desires.

Exactly. When such men are only private individuals and before they get power, this is their character; they associate entirely with their own flatterers or ready tools; or if they want anything from anybody, they in their turn are equally ready to bow down before them: they profess every sort of affection for them; but when they have gained their point they know them no more.

Yes, truly. They are always either the masters or servants and never the friends of anybody; the tyrant never tastes of true freedom or friendship.

Certainly not. And may we not rightly call such men treacherous? No question. Also they are utterly unjust, if we were right in our notion of justice?

Yes, he said, and we were perfectly right. Let us then sum up in a word, I said, the character of the worst man: he is the waking reality of what we dreamed.

Most true. And this is he who being by nature most of a tyrant bears rule, and the longer he lives the more of a tyrant he becomes.
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "and he will be the most fit to be a tyrant."

"If the people yield, well and good; but if they resist him, as he began by beating his own father and mother, so now, if he has the power, he beats them, and will keep his dear old fatherland or motherland, as the Cretans say, in subjection to his young retainers whom he has introduced to be their rulers and masters. This is the end of his passions and desires."

"Exactly."

"When such men are only private individuals and before they get power, this is their character; they associate entirely with their own flatterers or ready tools; or if they want anything from anybody, they in their turn are equally ready to bow down before them: they profess every sort of affection for them; but when they have gained their point they know them no more."

"Yes, truly."

"They are always either the masters or servants and never the friends of anybody; the tyrant never tastes of true freedom or friendship."

"Certainly not."

"And may we not rightly call such men treacherous?"

"No question."

"Also they are utterly unjust, if we were right in our notion of justice?"

"Yes," he said, "and we were perfectly right."

"Let us then sum up in a word," Socrates said, "the character of the worst man: he is the waking reality of what we dreamed."

"Most true."

"And this is he who being by nature most of a tyrant bears rule, and the longer he lives the more of a tyrant he becomes."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, he'll be the most naturally suited to be a tyrant."

"If the people submit, fine. But if they resist -- well, just as he started by beating his own father and mother, he'll now beat his fatherland if he has the power. He'll keep his 'dear old motherland,' as the Cretans put it, in subjection to the young enforcers he's imported as its rulers and masters. This is where his passions and desires ultimately lead."

"Exactly."

"Before such men gain power, while they're still private citizens, here's what they're like: they surround themselves exclusively with flatterers and lackeys. If they need something from someone, they're perfectly willing to grovel before them, professing every kind of affection. But the moment they've gotten what they want, they don't know these people anymore."

"That's exactly right."

"They are always either masters or servants, never the friends of anyone. The tyrant never experiences true freedom or genuine friendship."

"Never."

"Can we rightly call such men treacherous?"

"Without question."

"And utterly unjust, if our definition of justice was correct?"

"Yes -- and our definition was perfectly correct."

"Then let me sum up the character of the worst man in a single phrase: he is the waking reality of what we only dreamed."

"Exactly right."

"And this is the man who, being the most tyrannical by nature, holds power -- and the longer he rules, the more tyrannical he becomes."
D: Elegant: The tyrant treats his fatherland as he treated his parents -- with violence. He knows only masters and servants, never friends, never true freedom. He is treacherous, utterly unjust, and the waking reality of what we only dreamed. The longer he rules, the worse he becomes.
D: Summary: The tyrannical man, if he gains power, subjugates his country as he did his parents. He is incapable of friendship, knowing only domination or servility. Socrates sums up: the worst man is the waking embodiment of humanity's darkest dreams, and power only makes him worse over time.
D: Distilled: The worst man is the waking reality of our darkest dreams: incapable of friendship, knowing only domination, and worsening with every day in power.
Notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- 'Waking reality of what we dreamed' ties together the dream analysis (9.2-9.4) with the tyrannical man's portrait -- 'Dear old fatherland/motherland' (Cretan expression) parallels the literal father and mother he abused (9.10-9.11) -- 'Never the friends of anybody' anticipates Aristotle's similar analysis in Politics V -- 'Either masters or servants' defines tyranny as the negation of political friendship (philia) -- 'The longer he lives the more of a tyrant he becomes' -- power corrupts progressively -- Cross-ref: Book II's challenge about the unjust man who appears just (360b-362c) -- Marks the end of the character portrait and transition to the happiness question

D: Original:
That is certain, said Glaucon, taking his turn to answer.

And will not he who has been shown to be the wickedest, be also the most miserable? and he who has tyrannized longest and most, most continually and truly miserable; although this may not be the opinion of men in general?

Yes, he said, inevitably. And must not the tyrannical man be like the tyrannical, State, and the democratical man like the democratical State; and the same of the others?

Certainly. And as State is to State in virtue and happiness, so is man in relation to man?

To be sure. Then comparing our original city, which was under a king, and the city which is under a tyrant, how do they stand as to virtue?

They are the opposite extremes, he said, for one is the very best and the other is the very worst.
D: Narrative:
"That is certain," said Glaucon, taking his turn to answer.

"And will not he who has been shown to be the wickedest, be also the most miserable? And he who has tyrannized longest and most, most continually and truly miserable; although this may not be the opinion of men in general?"

"Yes," he said, "inevitably."

"And must not the tyrannical man be like the tyrannical State, and the democratical man like the democratical State; and the same of the others?"

"Certainly."

"And as State is to State in virtue and happiness, so is man in relation to man?"

"To be sure."

"Then comparing our original city, which was under a king, and the city which is under a tyrant, how do they stand as to virtue?"

"They are the opposite extremes," he said, "for one is the very best and the other is the very worst."
D: Refashioned:
"That is certain," said Glaucon, taking his turn to answer.

"And won't the man shown to be the most wicked also be the most miserable? And the one who has been a tyrant longest and most completely will be the most continuously and genuinely miserable -- even if that's not what most people believe?"

"Yes, inevitably."

"And the tyrannical man must resemble the tyrannical state, just as the democratic man resembles the democratic state, and so on for the others?"

"Of course."

"And as one state compares to another in virtue and happiness, so does one type of person compare to another?"

"Certainly."

"Then compare our original city -- the one ruled by a philosopher-king -- with the city under a tyrant. Where do they stand in terms of virtue?"

"They are polar opposites: one is the very best, the other the very worst."
D: Elegant: The wickedest man is also the most miserable, and the longest-reigning tyrant the most continuously so. As state parallels individual, the kingly city and the tyrannical city are polar opposites in virtue -- one the best, the other the worst.
D: Summary: Glaucon takes over as interlocutor. Socrates establishes that the wickedest man is the most miserable, that individuals mirror their corresponding state-types, and that the kingly and tyrannical cities represent the extremes of virtue and vice.
D: Distilled: The most wicked man is the most miserable; the philosopher-king's city and the tyrant's city are the extremes of virtue and vice.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon now speaking (Glaucon replaces Adeimantus) -- Begins the FIRST PROOF that justice is superior to injustice: the argument from the city-soul analogy -- The five regime types ranked: royal/aristocratic, timocratic, oligarchic, democratic, tyrannical -- 'Although this may not be the opinion of men in general' acknowledges popular opinion favors the tyrant -- Cross-ref: Book II (358a-362c) where Glaucon and Adeimantus challenged Socrates to prove justice superior -- The city-soul analogy was established in Book II (368c-369a) -- This begins Socrates' systematic response to Book II's challenge

D: Original:
There can be no mistake, I said, as to which is which, and therefore I will at once enquire whether you would arrive at a similar decision about their relative happiness and misery. And here we must not allow ourselves to be panic-stricken at the apparition of the tyrant, who is only a unit and may perhaps have a few retainers about him; but let us go as we ought into every corner of the city and look all about, and then we will give our opinion.

A fair invitation, he replied; and I see, as every one must, that a tyranny is the wretchedest form of government, and the rule of a king the happiest.
D: Narrative:
"There can be no mistake," Socrates said, "as to which is which, and therefore I will at once enquire whether you would arrive at a similar decision about their relative happiness and misery. And here we must not allow ourselves to be panic-stricken at the apparition of the tyrant, who is only a unit and may perhaps have a few retainers about him; but let us go as we ought into every corner of the city and look all about, and then we will give our opinion."

"A fair invitation," he replied; "and I see, as every one must, that a tyranny is the wretchedest form of government, and the rule of a king the happiest."
D: Refashioned:
"There can be no confusion about which is which. So let me now ask: would you reach a similar verdict about their relative happiness and misery? And here we must not be intimidated by the outward spectacle of the tyrant, who is just one person with perhaps a few retainers around him. We need to go into every corner of the city and examine everything before reaching our verdict."

"That's a fair approach. And I can see, as everyone must, that tyranny is the most wretched form of government, and kingship the happiest."
D: Elegant: Do not be dazzled by the tyrant's outward spectacle -- examine every corner of the city. Tyranny is the wretchedest form of government, kingship the happiest.
D: Summary: Socrates warns against being dazzled by the tyrant's external appearance and insists on examining the city thoroughly. Glaucon agrees that tyranny is the most wretched form of government and kingship the happiest.
D: Distilled: Look past the tyrant's spectacle to the city's reality: tyranny is the most wretched government, kingship the happiest.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'Panic-stricken at the apparition' warns against being deceived by the tyrant's outward grandeur -- a key epistemological theme -- 'Only a unit' (heis monos) emphasizes the tyrant's isolation -- 'Every corner of the city' insists on comprehensive inquiry, not superficial impressions -- Cross-ref: Book VII's Cave allegory -- those who return must look past appearances -- Glaucon's ready agreement prepares for the deeper analysis to come -- The method of thorough inspection connects to the philosopher's duty to examine reality

D: Original: And in estimating the men too, may I not fairly make a like request, that I should have a judge whose mind can enter into and see through human nature? He must not be like a child who looks at the outside and is dazzled at the pompous aspect which the tyrannical nature assumes to the beholder, but let him be one who has a clear insight. May I suppose that the judgment is given in the hearing of us all by one who is able to judge, and has dwelt in the same place with him, and been present at his dally life and known him in his family relations, where he may be seen stripped of his tragedy attire, and again in the hour of public danger --he shall tell us about the happiness and misery of the tyrant when compared with other men?
D: Narrative: "And in estimating the men too, may I not fairly make a like request, that I should have a judge whose mind can enter into and see through human nature? He must not be like a child who looks at the outside and is dazzled at the pompous aspect which the tyrannical nature assumes to the beholder, but let him be one who has a clear insight. May I suppose that the judgment is given in the hearing of us all by one who is able to judge, and has dwelt in the same place with him, and been present at his daily life and known him in his family relations, where he may be seen stripped of his tragedy attire, and again in the hour of public danger -- he shall tell us about the happiness and misery of the tyrant when compared with other men?"
D: Refashioned: "And in judging the men themselves, may I make the same request? I want a judge whose mind can penetrate human nature. Not someone who, like a child, looks only at exteriors and is dazzled by the pompous facade the tyrannical man presents to the world. Let the judge be someone with genuine insight -- someone who has lived alongside the tyrant, witnessed his daily life, known him in his private family relations where he is stripped of his theatrical costume, and also seen him in moments of public crisis. Let this person tell us about the tyrant's happiness and misery compared with other men."
D: Elegant: Judge the tyrant not by his public spectacle but through the eyes of someone who has seen him in private -- in family life, stripped of theatrical costume, and in moments of crisis. Only such a judge can assess his true happiness.
D: Summary: Socrates demands a judge of the tyrant who can see past outward appearances -- one who has witnessed the tyrant in private life, family relations, and public danger, stripped of his theatrical facade.
D: Distilled: Only a judge who has seen the tyrant stripped of his public facade in private moments can assess his true happiness.
Notes: Socrates speaking -- 'Tragedy attire' (tragike skene) explicitly compares the tyrant to a theatrical character -- appearance vs. reality -- 'Like a child' who is dazzled by appearances recalls the Cave's prisoners (Book VII) -- The demand for an experienced, penetrating judge anticipates the philosopher-judge theme of the Second Proof -- 'Family relations' where he is 'stripped' connects to 9.10-9.11's abuse of parents -- Cross-ref: Book II (360e-361d) on the perfectly unjust man who maintains the appearance of justice -- The theatrical metaphor runs throughout the Republic

D: Original:
That again, he said, is a very fair proposal. Shall I assume that we ourselves are able and experienced judges and have before now met with such a person? We shall then have some one who will answer our enquiries.

By all means. Let me ask you not to forget the parallel of the individual and the State; bearing this in mind, and glancing in turn from one to the other of them, will you tell me their respective conditions?

What do you mean? he asked. Beginning with the State, I replied, would you say that a city which is governed by a tyrant is free or enslaved?

No city, he said, can be more completely enslaved. And yet, as you see, there are freemen as well as masters in such a State?

Yes, he said, I see that there are --a few; but the people, speaking generally, and the best of them, are miserably degraded and enslaved.

Then if the man is like the State, I said, must not the same rule prevail? his soul is full of meanness and vulgarity --the best elements in him are enslaved; and there is a small ruling part, which is also the worst and maddest.

Inevitably. And would you say that the soul of such an one is the soul of a freeman, or of a slave?

He has the soul of a slave, in my opinion. And the State which is enslaved under a tyrant is utterly incapable of acting voluntarily?

Utterly incapable. And also the soul which is under a tyrant (I am speaking of the soul taken as a whole) is least capable of doing what she desires; there is a gadfly which goads her, and she is full of trouble and remorse?

Certainly. And is the city which is under a tyrant rich or poor? Poor. And the tyrannical soul must be always poor and insatiable?

True. And must not such a State and such a man be always full of fear?

Yes, indeed. Is there any State in which you will find more of lamentation and sorrow and groaning and pain?
D: Narrative:
"That again," he said, "is a very fair proposal."

"Shall I assume that we ourselves are able and experienced judges and have before now met with such a person? We shall then have some one who will answer our enquiries."

"By all means."

"Let me ask you not to forget the parallel of the individual and the State; bearing this in mind, and glancing in turn from one to the other of them, will you tell me their respective conditions?"

"What do you mean?" he asked.

"Beginning with the State," Socrates replied, "would you say that a city which is governed by a tyrant is free or enslaved?"

"No city," he said, "can be more completely enslaved."

"And yet, as you see, there are freemen as well as masters in such a State?"

"Yes," he said, "I see that there are -- a few; but the people, speaking generally, and the best of them, are miserably degraded and enslaved."

"Then if the man is like the State," Socrates said, "must not the same rule prevail? His soul is full of meanness and vulgarity -- the best elements in him are enslaved; and there is a small ruling part, which is also the worst and maddest."

"Inevitably."

"And would you say that the soul of such an one is the soul of a freeman, or of a slave?"

"He has the soul of a slave, in my opinion."

"And the State which is enslaved under a tyrant is utterly incapable of acting voluntarily?"

"Utterly incapable."

"And also the soul which is under a tyrant (I am speaking of the soul taken as a whole) is least capable of doing what she desires; there is a gadfly which goads her, and she is full of trouble and remorse?"

"Certainly."

"And is the city which is under a tyrant rich or poor?"

"Poor."

"And the tyrannical soul must be always poor and insatiable?"

"True."

"And must not such a State and such a man be always full of fear?"

"Yes, indeed."

"Is there any State in which you will find more of lamentation and sorrow and groaning and pain?"
D: Refashioned:
"That's a very fair proposal."

"Let's assume we ourselves are qualified judges who have encountered such a person. Then we can answer these questions directly."

"Agreed."

"Just remember to keep the parallel between individual and state in mind. Looking back and forth between them, tell me about their respective conditions."

"What do you mean?"

"Start with the state. Is a city governed by a tyrant free or enslaved?"

"No city could be more completely enslaved."

"Yet there are free men as well as masters in such a state?"

"Yes -- a few. But the people as a whole, especially the best among them, are miserably degraded and enslaved."

"Then if the man mirrors the state, the same must hold: his soul is full of baseness and vulgarity, the best parts of him are enslaved, and a small ruling element -- the worst and most deranged part -- commands everything."

"Inevitably."

"Would you say such a soul is the soul of a free man or a slave?"

"A slave, in my view."

"And a state enslaved under a tyrant is utterly incapable of voluntary action?"

"Utterly incapable."

"Likewise the tyrannized soul, taken as a whole, is least capable of doing what it truly wants. It's goaded by a stinging fly, full of turmoil and remorse."

"Certainly."

"Is the city under a tyrant rich or poor?"

"Poor."

"And the tyrannical soul -- always poor and insatiable?"

"True."

"And must not such a state and such a man be perpetually full of fear?"

"Yes, indeed."

"Can you find any state with more lamentation, sorrow, groaning, and pain?"
D: Elegant: The tyrannical state is utterly enslaved, with the best citizens degraded. So too the tyrannical soul: its best parts are enslaved by the worst and maddest element. Such a soul is slavish, incapable of free action, goaded by internal torment, perpetually poor, insatiable, and full of fear.
D: Summary: Socrates systematically parallels the tyrannical state and tyrannical soul: both are enslaved, with the best elements subjugated by the worst. Both are incapable of voluntary action, perpetually poor, insatiable, and consumed by fear, lamentation, and remorse.
D: Distilled: The tyrannical soul, like the tyrannical state, is enslaved by its worst element -- perpetually poor, fearful, and incapable of freedom.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- Systematic application of the city-soul analogy (Book II, 368c-369a) -- 'Soul of a slave' inverts the tyrant's apparent power -- he who seems most powerful is most enslaved -- 'Gadfly' (oistros) recalls Socrates' self-description as Athens' gadfly in the Apology (30e) -- The series of rapid questions and answers creates a mounting catalogue of misery -- Poverty, insatiability, fear, lamentation form the four marks of tyrannical existence -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 466b-468e on the tyrant who does what seems best but not what he truly wills -- 'The best elements enslaved' inverts Book IV's definition of justice as each part doing its proper work

D: Elegant: The tyrannical man is deeply miserable, but worse still is the tyrant who actually holds power. Like a slaveholder transported to a wilderness with no city to protect him, the public tyrant is surrounded by those he has enslaved -- and lives in constant terror of them.
D: Summary: Socrates surprises Glaucon by arguing that the private tyrannical man is not yet the most miserable -- the public tyrant is worse. He illustrates with a slaveholder analogy: wealthy slaveholders feel safe because the city protects them, but imagine one transported to a wilderness alone with his slaves and no protection.
D: Distilled: The public tyrant is more miserable than the private tyrannical man -- like a slaveholder stranded without the city's protection, surrounded by those he has enslaved.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- Key distinction between tyrannical nature (private) and actual tyranny (public) -- the latter is worse -- 'Of all questions, this respecting good and evil is the greatest' underscores the stakes of the Republic's central argument -- The slaveholder analogy is vivid and politically charged in the context of Athenian slave-owning society -- 'Carried off by a god into the wilderness' is a thought experiment isolating the power relationship -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 469-474 on whether the tyrant or the tyrant's victim is worse off -- The argument moves from internal misery to the additional misery of political power

D: Original:
Yes, he said, he will be in the utmost fear. The time has arrived when he will be compelled to flatter divers of his slaves, and make many promises to them of freedom and other things, much against his will --he will have to cajole his own servants.

Yes, he said, that will be the only way of saving himself.

And suppose the same god, who carried him away, to surround him with neighbours who will not suffer one man to be the master of another, and who, if they could catch the offender, would take his life?

D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "he will be in the utmost fear."

"The time has arrived when he will be compelled to flatter divers of his slaves, and make many promises to them of freedom and other things, much against his will -- he will have to cajole his own servants."

"Yes," he said, "that will be the only way of saving himself."

"And suppose the same god, who carried him away, to surround him with neighbours who will not suffer one man to be the master of another, and who, if they could catch the offender, would take his life?"

"His case will be still worse, if you suppose him to be everywhere surrounded and watched by enemies."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, he would be terrified."

"And now he'd be forced to flatter various slaves, making promises of freedom and other rewards -- completely against his will. He'd have to grovel before his own servants."

"Yes, that would be his only chance of survival."

"Now suppose the same god who transported him also surrounds him with neighbors who refuse to tolerate one person being master over another -- people who would kill any such offender on sight."

"His situation would be even worse -- completely surrounded and watched by enemies on all sides."
D: Elegant: The isolated slaveholder must grovel before his own slaves to survive. Worse still if surrounded by neighbors who despise mastery itself -- he becomes a prisoner watched by enemies on every side.
D: Summary: The stranded slaveholder is forced to flatter and bribe his own slaves. His situation worsens further if surrounded by neighbors hostile to slavery itself, leaving him trapped and watched by enemies on all sides.
D: Distilled: The isolated master is forced to grovel before his own slaves, becoming a prisoner surrounded by enemies.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- Continues the slaveholder thought experiment -- 'Flatter his own slaves' inverts the master-slave relationship, just as the tyrant's apparent power is actually servitude -- 'Neighbours who will not suffer one man to be master of another' may allude to democratic or egalitarian communities -- The escalating thought experiment parallels the tyrant's escalating isolation -- Cross-ref: Hiero (Xenophon) on the tyrant's inability to trust anyone -- 'Much against his will' emphasizes the loss of authentic agency -- the tyrant does not do what he truly wills (cf. Gorgias 466b-468e)

D: Original: And is not this the sort of prison in which the tyrant will be bound --he who being by nature such as we have described, is full of all sorts of fears and lusts? His soul is dainty and greedy, and yet alone, of all men in the city, he is never allowed to go on a journey, or to see the things which other freemen desire to see, but he lives in his hole like a woman hidden in the house, and is jealous of any other citizen who goes into foreign parts and sees anything of interest.
D: Narrative: "And is not this the sort of prison in which the tyrant will be bound -- he who being by nature such as we have described, is full of all sorts of fears and lusts? His soul is dainty and greedy, and yet alone, of all men in the city, he is never allowed to go on a journey, or to see the things which other freemen desire to see, but he lives in his hole like a woman hidden in the house, and is jealous of any other citizen who goes into foreign parts and sees anything of interest."
D: Refashioned: "And isn't this exactly the kind of prison the tyrant inhabits? By nature he is full of every kind of fear and craving. His soul is both fastidious and voracious. Yet he alone, of everyone in the city, can never travel or see the sights that other free people enjoy. He lives in his lair like a shut-in, envious of any citizen who travels abroad and experiences something worthwhile."
D: Elegant: The tyrant is imprisoned by his own nature -- full of fears and lusts, unable to travel or experience what free people enjoy. He lives as a shut-in, envying the freedom of ordinary citizens.
D: Summary: The tyrant lives in a prison of his own making, full of fears and lusts. Unlike free citizens, he can never travel or see the world, and envies those who can.
D: Distilled: The tyrant is the most imprisoned person in his own city -- unable to travel, envying the freedom of ordinary citizens.
Notes: Socrates speaking -- 'Lives in his hole like a woman hidden in the house' references the gendered seclusion of Athenian women in the gynaeceum -- The tyrant's immobility contrasts sharply with Greek ideals of travel and cosmopolitan experience -- 'Dainty and greedy' (tryphe kai pleonexia) combines luxury with acquisitiveness -- The tyrant's jealousy of ordinary citizens' freedom is deeply ironic -- Cross-ref: Xenophon's Hiero 1.11-2.1 on the tyrant's inability to enjoy festivals, travel, or public life -- The prison metaphor anticipates and inverts the Cave of Book VII: the tyrant voluntarily imprisons himself

D: Original:
Very true, he said. And amid evils such as these will not he who is ill-governed in his own person --the tyrannical man, I mean --whom you just now decided to be the most miserable of all --will not he be yet more miserable when, instead of leading a private life, he is constrained by fortune to be a public tyrant? He has to be master of others when he is not master of himself: he is like a diseased or paralytic man who is compelled to pass his life, not in retirement, but fighting and combating with other men.

Yes, he said, the similitude is most exact. Is not his case utterly miserable? and does not the actual tyrant lead a worse life than he whose life you determined to be the worst?
D: Narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"And amid evils such as these will not he who is ill-governed in his own person -- the tyrannical man, I mean -- whom you just now decided to be the most miserable of all -- will not he be yet more miserable when, instead of leading a private life, he is constrained by fortune to be a public tyrant? He has to be master of others when he is not master of himself: he is like a diseased or paralytic man who is compelled to pass his life, not in retirement, but fighting and combating with other men."

"Yes," he said, "the similitude is most exact."

"Is not his case utterly miserable? And does not the actual tyrant lead a worse life than he whose life you determined to be the worst?"
D: Refashioned:
"Very true."

"So consider this: the man who is badly governed within himself -- the tyrannical man you just declared the most miserable of all -- won't he be even more miserable when, instead of living privately, fate forces him to become an actual public tyrant? He must be master of others while unable to master himself. He's like a sick or paralyzed man compelled not to rest and recover, but to spend his life fighting and competing with other men."

"Yes, that comparison is exactly right."

"Isn't his situation utterly miserable? Doesn't the actual tyrant live a worse life than even the man you called the worst?"
D: Elegant: The man who cannot govern himself is forced to govern others -- like a paralytic compelled to fight instead of rest. The public tyrant is even more miserable than the private tyrannical man.
D: Summary: The public tyrant, unable to master himself yet forced to master others, is even more miserable than the private tyrannical man. Socrates compares him to a paralytic compelled to fight rather than rest.
D: Distilled: The public tyrant must master others while unable to master himself -- a paralytic forced to fight.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'Master of others when he is not master of himself' is the central paradox of tyranny -- 'Diseased or paralytic man' compelled to fight is a vivid analogy for the tyrannical condition -- power as affliction -- 'Constrained by fortune' (tyche) -- the tyrant doesn't choose his situation freely -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 466-468 on the powerlessness of tyrants who seem powerful -- The private/public tyrant distinction deepens the argument: political power adds misery to an already miserable soul -- Anticipates Aristotle's claim that the tyrant is unhappy (Nicomachean Ethics, Politics V)

D: Original: Certainly. He who is the real tyrant, whatever men may think, is the real slave, and is obliged to practise the greatest adulation and servility, and to be the flatterer of the vilest of mankind. He has desires which he is utterly unable to satisfy, and has more wants than any one, and is truly poor, if you know how to inspect the whole soul of him: all his life long he is beset with fear and is full of convulsions, and distractions, even as the State which he resembles: and surely the resemblance holds?
D: Narrative:
"Certainly."

"He who is the real tyrant, whatever men may think, is the real slave, and is obliged to practise the greatest adulation and servility, and to be the flatterer of the vilest of mankind. He has desires which he is utterly unable to satisfy, and has more wants than any one, and is truly poor, if you know how to inspect the whole soul of him: all his life long he is beset with fear and is full of convulsions, and distractions, even as the State which he resembles: and surely the resemblance holds?"
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly."

"The real tyrant, whatever people may think, is the real slave. He must practice the most extreme flattery and servility, groveling before the vilest of people. He has desires he can never satisfy and more needs than anyone -- he is truly poor, if you know how to examine the whole soul. His entire life is besieged by fear, full of convulsions and agitation, just like the state he resembles. And surely the resemblance holds?"
D: Elegant: The real tyrant is the real slave -- obliged to flatter the vilest people, tortured by insatiable desires, truly poor in soul, besieged by fear and inner convulsions, mirroring the wretched state he governs.
D: Summary: The real tyrant is the real slave: forced to flatter the worst people, unable to satisfy his desires, truly poor in soul despite apparent wealth, and perpetually consumed by fear and inner turmoil -- a mirror of his wretched state.
D: Distilled: The real tyrant is the real slave -- flattering the vile, insatiably poor in soul, and perpetually consumed by fear.
Notes: Socrates speaking -- 'The real tyrant is the real slave' is one of the Republic's most famous paradoxes -- 'Whatever men may think' contrasts appearance with reality -- the Republic's core epistemological theme -- 'Flatterer of the vilest' inverts the expected master-subject relationship -- 'Truly poor if you know how to inspect the whole soul' requires philosophical insight to perceive -- not visible to ordinary judgment -- 'Convulsions and distractions' (spasmos, tarache) use medical/physical language for psychological states -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 466b-468e (tyrants lack real power); Book IV (justice as internal order vs. injustice as disease) -- Concludes the First Proof

D: Original: Very true, he said. Moreover, as we were saying before, he grows worse from having power: he becomes and is of necessity more jealous, more faithless, more unjust, more friendless, more impious, than he was at first; he is the purveyor and cherisher of every sort of vice, and the consequence is that he is supremely miserable, and that he makes everybody else as miserable as himself.
D: Narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"Moreover, as we were saying before, he grows worse from having power: he becomes and is of necessity more jealous, more faithless, more unjust, more friendless, more impious, than he was at first; he is the purveyor and cherisher of every sort of vice, and the consequence is that he is supremely miserable, and that he makes everybody else as miserable as himself."
D: Refashioned:
"Very true."

"And as we said before, power makes him worse. He necessarily becomes more jealous, more faithless, more unjust, more friendless, and more impious than he was to begin with. He becomes the purveyor and cultivator of every vice. The result is that he is supremely miserable -- and he makes everyone around him equally miserable."
D: Elegant: Power makes the tyrant progressively worse: more jealous, faithless, unjust, friendless, and impious. He cultivates every vice, becomes supremely miserable himself, and spreads that misery to everyone around him.
D: Summary: Power makes the tyrant progressively worse across every dimension -- more jealous, faithless, unjust, friendless, and impious. He cultivates every vice, becoming supremely miserable and making everyone else miserable too.
D: Distilled: Power makes the tyrant worse in every way, supremely miserable, and a source of universal misery.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- The progressive corruption thesis: power doesn't just reveal but actively worsens character -- 'More jealous, more faithless, more unjust, more friendless, more impious' -- a five-fold catalogue of deterioration -- 'Purveyor and cherisher of every sort of vice' -- the tyrant as vice's patron -- 'Makes everybody else as miserable as himself' extends the harm from individual to social -- Cross-ref: Lord Acton's later formulation 'power tends to corrupt, absolute power corrupts absolutely' -- Cross-ref: Book VIII (567b-568d) on the tyrant's progressive need to eliminate the best citizens -- The tyrant as anti-philosopher: where the philosopher cultivates virtue and benefits the city, the tyrant cultivates vice and harms it

D: Original: No man of any sense will dispute your words. Come then, I said, and as the general umpire in theatrical contests proclaims the result, do you also decide who in your opinion is first in the scale of happiness, and who second, and in what order the others follow: there are five of them in all --they are the royal, timocratical, oligarchical, democratical, tyrannical.
D: Narrative:
"No man of any sense will dispute your words."

"Come then," Socrates said, "and as the general umpire in theatrical contests proclaims the result, do you also decide who in your opinion is first in the scale of happiness, and who second, and in what order the others follow: there are five of them in all -- they are the royal, timocratical, oligarchical, democratical, tyrannical."
D: Refashioned:
"No sensible person would dispute that."

"Then come, act as the chief judge at a theatrical competition and announce the results. Rank these five types in order of happiness: the royal, the timocratic, the oligarchic, the democratic, and the tyrannical."
D: Elegant: Like a judge at a theatrical contest, rank the five types of men in order of happiness: royal, timocratic, oligarchic, democratic, tyrannical.
D: Summary: Socrates asks Glaucon to rank the five character types -- royal, timocratic, oligarchic, democratic, and tyrannical -- in order of happiness, as if judging a theatrical contest.
D: Distilled: Rank the five souls in order of happiness: the just king is happiest, the tyrant most miserable.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- The theatrical contest metaphor (krisis) casts the philosophical argument as a dramatic competition -- 'General umpire' (the agonothetes) was the official who proclaimed winners at Athenian dramatic festivals -- Five types in descending order: royal/aristocratic, timocratic, oligarchic, democratic, tyrannical -- This ranking summarizes the entire degeneration sequence of Books VIII-IX -- Cross-ref: Book VIII's analysis of the five regime types and their corresponding souls -- Marks the transition from the First Proof to its formal conclusion

D: Original:
The decision will be easily given, he replied; they shall be choruses coming on the stage, and I must judge them in the order in which they enter, by the criterion of virtue and vice, happiness and misery.

Need we hire a herald, or shall I announce, that the son of Ariston (the best) has decided that the best and justest is also the happiest, and that this is he who is the most royal man and king over himself; and that the worst and most unjust man is also the most miserable, and that this is he who being the greatest tyrant of himself is also the greatest tyrant of his State?

Make the proclamation yourself, he said. And shall I add, 'whether seen or unseen by gods and men'?

Let the words be added. Then this, I said, will be our first proof; and there is another, which may also have some weight.

What is that? The second proof is derived from the nature of the soul: seeing that the individual soul, like the State, has been divided by us into three principles, the division may, I think, furnish a new demonstration.

Of what nature? It seems to me that to these three principles three pleasures correspond; also three desires and governing powers.
D: Narrative:
"The decision will be easily given," he replied; "they shall be choruses coming on the stage, and I must judge them in the order in which they enter, by the criterion of virtue and vice, happiness and misery."

"Need we hire a herald, or shall I announce, that the son of Ariston (the best) has decided that the best and justest is also the happiest, and that this is he who is the most royal man and king over himself; and that the worst and most unjust man is also the most miserable, and that this is he who being the greatest tyrant of himself is also the greatest tyrant of his State?"

"Make the proclamation yourself," he said.

"And shall I add, 'whether seen or unseen by gods and men'?"

"Let the words be added."

"Then this," Socrates said, "will be our first proof; and there is another, which may also have some weight."

"What is that?"

"The second proof is derived from the nature of the soul: seeing that the individual soul, like the State, has been divided by us into three principles, the division may, I think, furnish a new demonstration."

"Of what nature?"

"It seems to me that to these three principles three pleasures correspond; also three desires and governing powers."
D: Refashioned:
"The decision is easy," Glaucon replied. "Let them come on stage like competing choruses, and I'll judge them in order by the standard of virtue and vice, happiness and misery."

"Do we need to hire a herald, or shall I make the announcement myself? The son of Ariston -- whose name means 'the best' -- has decided that the best and most just man is also the happiest, and he is the one who is most kingly, most sovereign over himself. The worst and most unjust man is the most miserable, and he is the one who is the greatest tyrant of himself and of his state."

"Make the proclamation yourself."

"And shall I add: 'whether seen or unseen by gods and men'?"

"Add it."

"Then this is our first proof. But there's a second that may carry equal weight."

"What is it?"

"The second proof comes from the nature of the soul itself. We divided the soul, like the state, into three parts. This division can furnish a fresh demonstration."

"How so?"

"To these three parts of the soul correspond three distinct pleasures -- and three desires and governing principles."
D: Elegant: First Proof concluded: the most just man, sovereign over himself, is the happiest; the tyrant of himself and his state is the most miserable -- whether seen or unseen by gods and men. The Second Proof begins from the soul's three parts, each with its own pleasure, desire, and governing principle.
D: Summary: Glaucon's verdict concludes the First Proof: the just, kingly man is happiest and the tyrannical man most miserable, regardless of external appearances. Socrates then introduces the Second Proof, based on the tripartite soul's three corresponding pleasures.
D: Distilled: First Proof: the just king is happiest, the self-tyrannizing tyrant most miserable, seen or unseen. Second Proof begins: three soul-parts yield three distinct pleasures.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'Son of Ariston' is Glaucon himself (Plato's brother) -- ariston means 'best,' creating a pun -- 'Whether seen or unseen by gods and men' directly answers Book II's challenge (360b-362c) where Glaucon asked Socrates to prove justice good regardless of reputation -- FIRST PROOF CONCLUDED: justice = happiness, injustice = misery, based on city-soul parallel -- SECOND PROOF BEGINS: from the tripartite soul -- Cross-ref: Book IV (435b-441c) for the original division of the soul into reason, spirit, appetite -- The three pleasures correspond to the three soul-parts: philosophic pleasure (reason), honor (spirit), gain (appetite) -- The theatrical metaphor continues with 'choruses' and 'herald'

D: Original: How do you mean? he said. "How do you mean?" he said.

D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean?"

"As we've established, there's one part of the soul with which we learn, another with which we feel anger and indignation. The third has many forms and no single name, but we call it the 'appetitive' part because of the extraordinary strength and intensity of its desires for eating, drinking, sex, and other bodily pleasures. We could also call it 'money-loving,' since money is the primary means of satisfying such desires."
D: Elegant: The three soul-parts: one that learns (reason), one that feels anger (spirit), and the appetitive part -- the strongest and most multiform, driven by desires for food, drink, and sensual pleasure, also called money-loving since money serves these cravings.
D: Summary: Socrates identifies the three soul-parts: the learning principle (reason), the spirited principle (anger/indignation), and the appetitive principle -- the strongest and most varied, driven by desires for food, drink, and sensual pleasure, also called money-loving.
D: Distilled: The soul has three parts: the learning (reason), the spirited (anger), and the appetitive (desire/money-loving) -- each with its own pleasure.

There can be no doubt of that, he replied. Since, then, the pleasures of each class and the life of each are in dispute, and the question is not which life is more or less honourable, or better or worse, but which is the more pleasant or painless --how shall we know who speaks truly?

I cannot myself tell, he said. Well, but what ought to be the criterion? Is any better than experience and wisdom and reason?

There cannot be a better, he said. Then, I said, reflect. Of the three individuals, which has the greatest experience of all the pleasures which we enumerated? Has the lover of gain, in learning the nature of essential truth, greater experience of the pleasure of knowledge than the philosopher has of the pleasure of gain?

The philosopher, he replied, has greatly the advantage; for he has of necessity always known the taste of the other pleasures from his childhood upwards: but the lover of gain in all his experience has not of necessity tasted --or, I should rather say, even had he desired, could hardly have tasted --the sweetness of learning and knowing truth.

Then the lover of wisdom has a great advantage over the lover of gain, for he has a double experience?

Yes, very great. Again, has he greater experience of the pleasures of honour, or the lover of honour of the pleasures of wisdom?

Nay, he said, all three are honoured in proportion as they attain their object; for the rich man and the brave man and the wise man alike have their crowd of admirers, and as they all receive honour they all have experience of the pleasures of honour; but the delight which is to be found in the knowledge of true being is known to the philosopher only.

His experience, then, will enable him to judge better than any one?

Far better. And he is the only one who has wisdom as well as experience?

Certainly. Further, the very faculty which is the instrument of judgment is not possessed by the covetous or ambitious man, but only by the philosopher?

"Yes, very great."

D: Refashioned:
"No doubt about it."

"So the pleasures and lives of each type are in dispute, and the question isn't which life is more honorable or better, but which is genuinely more pleasant. How do we determine who's telling the truth?"

"I honestly don't know."

"What should the criterion be? Is there anything better than experience, wisdom, and reason?"

"Nothing better."

"Then consider this: of the three types, which has the broadest experience of all the pleasures we listed? Has the money-lover experienced more of the philosopher's pleasure in knowing truth than the philosopher has experienced of the money-lover's pleasure in gain?"

"The philosopher has a huge advantage. He's necessarily tasted the other pleasures since childhood -- everyone has. But the money-lover hasn't necessarily experienced, and even if he wanted to could hardly have tasted, the sweetness of learning and knowing truth."

"So the philosopher has a double advantage in experience over the money-lover?"

"A very great one."

"And compared to the honor-lover -- does the philosopher have more experience of honor's pleasures, or the honor-lover of wisdom's?"

"All three types receive honor in proportion to their success -- the rich man, the brave man, and the wise man each have their admirers. So all three know the pleasures of honor. But the delight found in knowing true reality is known to the philosopher alone."

"So his experience qualifies him to judge better than anyone?"

"Far better."

"And he alone possesses wisdom alongside experience?"

"Certainly."

"Furthermore, the very instrument of judgment -- reason -- belongs not to the acquisitive or ambitious man, but only to the philosopher?"
D: Elegant: The criterion for judging pleasures must be experience, wisdom, and reason. The philosopher has tasted all three pleasures since childhood, while the others have not tasted philosophic pleasure. Only the philosopher possesses all three qualifications for judgment: broadest experience, wisdom, and the instrument of reason itself.
D: Summary: To resolve the dispute over which pleasure is greatest, Socrates proposes three criteria: experience, wisdom, and reason. The philosopher alone has experienced all three types of pleasure, possesses wisdom, and wields reason -- the very instrument of judgment. He is therefore the only qualified judge.
D: Distilled: Only the philosopher can judge which pleasure is truest, because he alone has experienced all three and possesses both wisdom and reason.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- The three criteria for judging pleasures: experience (empeiria), wisdom (phronesis), and reason (logos) -- The philosopher's 'double experience' argument: everyone experiences bodily and honor pleasures, but only philosophers experience intellectual pleasure -- 'Even had he desired, could hardly have tasted' -- philosophic pleasure requires long training, not just desire -- 'The instrument of judgment' (organon) is reason/logos itself -- Cross-ref: Philebus 11a-12b on the dispute between pleasure and wisdom -- Cross-ref: Book VII on the philosopher's return to the Cave, having seen all levels of reality -- This is the heart of the Second Proof: the philosopher as uniquely qualified arbiter

What faculty? Reason, with whom, as we were saying, the decision ought to rest.

Yes. And reasoning is peculiarly his instrument? Certainly. If wealth and gain were the criterion, then the praise or blame of the lover of gain would surely be the most trustworthy?

Assuredly. Or if honour or victory or courage, in that case the judgement of the ambitious or pugnacious would be the truest?

Clearly. But since experience and wisdom and reason are the judges--

The only inference possible, he replied, is that pleasures which are approved by the lover of wisdom and reason are the truest.

And so we arrive at the result, that the pleasure of the intelligent part of the soul is the pleasantest of the three, and that he of us in whom this is the ruling principle has the pleasantest life.

Unquestionably, he said, the wise man speaks with authority when he approves of his own life.

And what does the judge affirm to be the life which is next, and the pleasure which is next?

Clearly that of the soldier and lover of honour; who is nearer to himself than the money-maker.

Last comes the lover of gain? Very true, he said. Twice in succession, then, has the just man overthrown the unjust in this conflict; and now comes the third trial, which is dedicated to Olympian Zeus the saviour: a sage whispers in my ear that no pleasure except that of the wise is quite true and pure --all others are a shadow only; and surely this will prove the greatest and most decisive of falls?

Yes, the greatest; but will you explain yourself? I will work out the subject and you shall answer my questions.

Proceed. Say, then, is not pleasure opposed to pain? True. And there is a neutral state which is neither pleasure nor pain?

There is. A state which is intermediate, and a sort of repose of the soul about either --that is what you mean?

Yes. You remember what people say when they are sick? What do they say? That after all nothing is pleasanter than health. But then they never knew this to be the greatest of pleasures until they were ill.

"Which faculty?"

"Yes."

D: Elegant: Second Proof concluded: since experience, wisdom, and reason are the proper judges, the philosopher's pleasure is truest, the honor-lover's second, the money-lover's last. The just man defeats the unjust twice. Now begins the Third Proof: only philosophic pleasure is true and pure -- all others are shadow. What seems pleasant often is merely the cessation of pain.
D: Summary: The Second Proof concludes: the philosopher's pleasure is judged truest by the only valid criteria. Socrates introduces the Third Proof -- dedicated to 'Olympian Zeus the Savior' -- arguing that most pleasures are merely shadows. He begins by examining the neutral state between pleasure and pain, noting that sick people mistake the relief of pain for the greatest pleasure.
D: Distilled: The philosopher's pleasure wins by experience, wisdom, and reason; now the Third Proof begins: most pleasures are mere shadows of the cessation of pain.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- SECOND PROOF CONCLUDED: philosopher's pleasure > honor-lover's > money-lover's -- THIRD PROOF BEGINS: the argument from real vs. apparent pleasure -- 'Olympian Zeus the Savior' -- Greek custom of dedicating the third libation to Zeus Soter; the third proof is the decisive one -- 'A sage whispers' may allude to Orphic or Pythagorean wisdom traditions -- The pleasure/pain/neutral triad introduces a sophisticated hedonistic analysis -- 'Nothing pleasanter than health' illustrates confusing relief-from-pain with genuine pleasure -- Cross-ref: Philebus 31b-55c on true and false pleasures, the central parallel text -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 496c-497a on the relationship between pleasure and the cessation of pain -- This proof is the most philosophically original of the three

Yes, I know, he said. And when persons are suffering from acute pain, you must. have heard them say that there is nothing pleasanter than to get rid of their pain?

I have. And there are many other cases of suffering in which the mere rest and cessation of pain, and not any positive enjoyment, is extolled by them as the greatest pleasure?

Yes, he said; at the time they are pleased and well content to be at rest.

Again, when pleasure ceases, that sort of rest or cessation will be painful?

Doubtless, he said. Then the intermediate state of rest will be pleasure and will also be pain?

So it would seem. But can that which is neither become both? I should say not. And both pleasure and pain are motions of the soul, are they not?

Yes. But that which is neither was just now shown to be rest and not motion, and in a mean between them?

Yes. How, then, can we be right in supposing that the absence of pain is pleasure, or that the absence of pleasure is pain?

Impossible. This then is an appearance only and not a reality; that is tc say, the rest is pleasure at the moment and in comparison of what is painful, and painful in comparison of what is pleasant; but all these representations, when tried by the test of true pleasure, are not real but a sort of imposition?

That is the inference. Look at the other class of pleasures which have no antecedent pains and you will no longer suppose, as you perhaps may at present, that pleasure is only the cessation of pain, or pain of pleasure.

What are they, he said, and where shall I find them? There are many of them: take as an example the pleasures, of smell, which are very great and have no antecedent pains; they come in a moment, and when they depart leave no pain behind them.

Most true, he said. Let us not, then, be induced to believe that pure pleasure is the cessation of pain, or pain of pleasure.

"I have."

"And there are many other cases of suffering in which the mere rest and cessation of pain, and not any positive enjoyment, is extolled by them as the greatest pleasure?"

"Yes," he said; "at the time they are pleased and well content to be at rest."

"Doubtless," he said.

"Then the intermediate state of rest will be pleasure and will also be pain?"

"So it would seem."

"But can that which is neither become both?"

"I should say not."

"And both pleasure and pain are motions of the soul, are they not?"

"Yes."

"But that which is neither was just now shown to be rest and not motion, and in a mean between them?"

"Yes."

"How, then, can we be right in supposing that the absence of pain is pleasure, or that the absence of pleasure is pain?"

"Impossible."

"This then is an appearance only and not a reality; that is to say, the rest is pleasure at the moment and in comparison of what is painful, and painful in comparison of what is pleasant; but all these representations, when tried by the test of true pleasure, are not real but a sort of imposition?"

"That is the inference."

"Look at the other class of pleasures which have no antecedent pains and you will no longer suppose, as you perhaps may at present, that pleasure is only the cessation of pain, or pain of pleasure."

"What are they," he said, "and where shall I find them?"

"There are many of them: take as an example the pleasures of smell, which are very great and have no antecedent pains; they come in a moment, and when they depart leave no pain behind them."

"Most true," he said.

"Let us not, then, be induced to believe that pure pleasure is the cessation of pain, or pain of pleasure."
D: Refashioned:
"I know."

"And people suffering acute pain will say there's nothing more pleasant than to be rid of it?"

"I've heard them say it."

"In many cases of suffering, the mere cessation of pain -- not any positive enjoyment -- is praised as the greatest pleasure?"

"Yes, at the time they're perfectly content just to be at rest."

"But when pleasure ceases, that same state of rest feels painful?"

"No doubt."

"So the intermediate state of rest is sometimes called pleasure and sometimes pain?"

"It seems so."

"But can something that is neither pleasure nor pain actually be both?"

"It shouldn't be."

"Both pleasure and pain are movements of the soul, correct?"

"Yes."

"But we just agreed the neutral state is rest, not movement -- positioned between the two?"

"Yes."

"Then how can we be right to suppose the absence of pain is pleasure, or the absence of pleasure is pain?"

"We can't."

"So this is appearance, not reality. Rest seems pleasant only by contrast with pain, and painful only by contrast with pleasure. These impressions, tested against genuine pleasure, are not real but a kind of illusion."

"That follows."

"Now consider pleasures that have no prior pain at all, and you'll stop assuming pleasure is just the cessation of pain."

"What pleasures? Where do I find them?"

"There are many. The pleasures of smell, for instance -- they can be intense, they appear suddenly without any preceding pain, and when they disappear they leave no pain behind."

"Absolutely true."

"So let's not be deceived into thinking pure pleasure is merely the cessation of pain, or pain merely the cessation of pleasure."
D: Elegant: Rest is neither pleasure nor pain but a neutral state. When we mistake the cessation of pain for pleasure, we are deceived by contrast -- it is appearance, not reality. True pleasures, like the pleasures of smell, arise without antecedent pain and leave none behind. Pure pleasure is not merely relief.
D: Summary: Socrates demonstrates that the neutral state between pleasure and pain is neither but merely rest. Mistaking relief from pain for genuine pleasure is an illusion of contrast. True pleasures, like the pleasures of smell, arise without prior pain and prove that pure pleasure exists independently of pain's cessation.
D: Distilled: Mistaking the cessation of pain for pleasure is an illusion; true pleasure, like the pleasure of smell, exists independently of any prior suffering.
Notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- Core of the Third Proof: the distinction between real and apparent pleasure -- The argument structure: (1) pleasure and pain are motions; (2) the neutral state is rest; (3) rest cannot be both; (4) therefore relief from pain is not true pleasure -- 'Pleasures of smell' as paradigm of pure, painless pleasure -- also discussed in Philebus 51b-52b -- 'A sort of imposition' (goeteia, enchantment/illusion) -- deceptive pleasures bewitch the soul -- Cross-ref: Philebus 42c-44d on false pleasures and the mixture of pleasure and pain -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 496c-497a on pleasure as replenishment -- This analysis anticipates modern distinctions between hedonic adaptation and genuine well-being

No. Still, the more numerous and violent pleasures which reach the soul through the body are generally of this sort --they are reliefs of pain.

That is true. And the anticipations of future pleasures and pains are of a like nature?

Yes. Shall I give you an illustration of them? Let me hear. You would allow, I said, that there is in nature an upper and lower and middle region?

I should. And if a person were to go from the lower to the middle region, would he not imagine that he is going up; and he who is standing in the middle and sees whence he has come, would imagine that he is already in the upper region, if he has never seen the true upper world?

To be sure, he said; how can he think otherwise? But if he were taken back again he would imagine, and truly imagine, that he was descending?

No doubt. All that would arise out of his ignorance of the true upper and middle and lower regions?

"That is true."

"Yes."
