Glaucon: An imitator of what the others make.

Socrates: Good -- so the painter is third in descent from nature. He's an imitator.
D: Elegant: Three beds exist in a hierarchy: God's unique Form (the one true bed), the carpenter's particular copy, and the painter's imitation of that copy. God necessarily made only one ideal bed, since two would require a third Form behind them. The painter, who neither creates the Form nor builds a physical bed, stands third in descent from reality -- he is the imitator.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes a three-tier hierarchy of creation: God creates the one true Form of a bed, the carpenter copies it into a particular bed, and the painter merely imitates the carpenter's copy. The Form must be unique because multiple ideal beds would require yet another Form behind them.
D: Distilled: God creates the single true Form, the craftsman copies it, and the painter imitates the copy -- making the imitator third in descent from reality.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The "Third Bed Argument" is a famous problem in Platonic metaphysics (also called the Third Man Argument, see Parmenides 132a-b) -- God as creator of Forms is distinctive to Republic X -- The three-level ontology (Form, particular, image) is the backbone of the poetry critique -- Cross-ref: Timaeus (God as Demiurge)

D: Original:
Certainly, he said. And the tragic poet is an imitator, and therefore, like all other imitators, he is thrice removed from the king and from the truth?

That appears to be so. Then about the imitator we are agreed. And what about the painter? --I would like to know whether he may be thought to imitate that which originally exists in nature, or only the creations of artists?

The latter. As they are or as they appear? You have still to determine this.

What do you mean? I mean, that you may look at a bed from different points of view, obliquely or directly or from any other point of view, and the bed will appear different, but there is no difference in reality. And the same of all things.

Yes, he said, the difference is only apparent. Now let me ask you another question: Which is the art of painting designed to be --an imitation of things as they are, or as they appear --of appearance or of reality?
D: Narrative:
"Certainly," he said.

"And the tragic poet is an imitator, and therefore, like all other imitators, he is thrice removed from the king and from the truth?"

"That appears to be so."

"Then about the imitator we are agreed. And what about the painter? -- I would like to know whether he may be thought to imitate that which originally exists in nature, or only the creations of artists?"

"The latter."

"As they are or as they appear? You have still to determine this."

"What do you mean?"

"I mean, that you may look at a bed from different points of view, obliquely or directly or from any other point of view, and the bed will appear different, but there is no difference in reality. And the same of all things."

"Yes," he said, "the difference is only apparent."

"Now let me ask you another question: Which is the art of painting designed to be -- an imitation of things as they are, or as they appear -- of appearance or of reality?"
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: Certainly.

Socrates: So the tragic poet is also an imitator, and like all imitators, he stands three steps removed from the king -- from truth itself?

Glaucon: It appears so.

Socrates: Good, we agree about the imitator. But here's a further question about the painter: does he imitate what originally exists in nature, or only the things craftsmen make?

Glaucon: Only what craftsmen make.

Socrates: And does he imitate those things as they truly are, or as they appear? Think carefully.

Glaucon: What's the difference?

Socrates: Look at a bed from different angles -- from the side, straight on, from above. It appears different each time, but the bed itself hasn't changed at all. The same goes for everything.

Glaucon: Right, the differences are only in appearance.

Socrates: So here's the key question: is painting designed to imitate things as they are, or as they appear? Is it an imitation of reality or of appearance?
D: Elegant: The tragic poet, like all imitators, is thrice removed from truth. The painter doesn't imitate the Form or even the real object -- he imitates how things appear from a particular angle. A bed looks different from every viewpoint though the bed itself doesn't change. The critical question: does painting imitate reality or mere appearance?
D: Summary: Socrates extends the three-levels argument to the tragic poet, placing him thrice removed from truth. He then sharpens the critique: painters don't even imitate real objects but only their appearances from particular viewpoints, raising the question of whether art imitates reality or mere appearance.
D: Distilled: The tragic poet is thrice removed from truth, and the painter imitates not even real objects but only how they appear from a single angle.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- "Thrice removed from the king and from the truth" -- the key formula of Book X's mimesis argument -- The perspectival argument (bed looks different from different angles) adds a fourth degree of removal -- Cross-ref: Books II-III (386a-403c) for the earlier, less philosophically grounded critique of poetry -- Cross-ref: Sophist 235d-236c on eikastic vs. phantastic imitation

D: Original: Of appearance. Then the imitator, I said, is a long way off the truth, and can do all things because he lightly touches on a small part of them, and that part an image. For example: A painter will paint a cobbler, carpenter, or any other artist, though he knows nothing of their arts; and, if he is a good artist, he may deceive children or simple persons, when he shows them his picture of a carpenter from a distance, and they will fancy that they are looking at a real carpenter.
D: Narrative:
"Of appearance."

"Then the imitator," I said, "is a long way off the truth, and can do all things because he lightly touches on a small part of them, and that part an image. For example: A painter will paint a cobbler, carpenter, or any other artist, though he knows nothing of their arts; and, if he is a good artist, he may deceive children or simple persons, when he shows them his picture of a carpenter from a distance, and they will fancy that they are looking at a real carpenter."
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: Of appearance.

Socrates: Then the imitator is far from the truth. He appears capable of doing everything precisely because he only lightly touches a small surface of each thing -- and that surface is just an image. Consider: a painter can paint a cobbler, a carpenter, or any other craftsman without knowing anything about their crafts. And if he's skilled enough, he can fool children or naive people into thinking that his picture of a carpenter, seen from a distance, is an actual carpenter.
D: Elegant: The imitator seems to know everything because he only skims the surface of each subject -- and that surface is merely an image. A painter who knows nothing of cobbling or carpentry can still produce convincing pictures of cobblers and carpenters, deceiving the naive who view them from a distance.
D: Summary: The imitator appears all-knowing because he only touches the superficial appearance of things. A skilled painter can depict any craft convincingly without understanding it, fooling those who lack discernment.
D: Distilled: The imitator's apparent mastery of everything is an illusion -- he copies only surfaces and deceives only the naive.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The deception-from-a-distance motif parallels the cave allegory (Book VII) -- The painter-as-deceiver argument will be extended to poets, especially Homer -- Cross-ref: Sophist 234b on the young being deceived by imitations -- Cross-ref: Ion 531a-542b on rhapsodes' apparent but illusory knowledge

D: Original: Certainly. And whenever any one informs us that he has found a man knows all the arts, and all things else that anybody knows, and every single thing with a higher degree of accuracy than any other man --whoever tells us this, I think that we can only imagine to be a simple creature who is likely to have been deceived by some wizard or actor whom he met, and whom he thought all-knowing, because he himself was unable to analyse the nature of knowledge and ignorance and imitation.
D: Narrative: "Certainly. And whenever any one informs us that he has found a man who knows all the arts, and all things else that anybody knows, and every single thing with a higher degree of accuracy than any other man -- whoever tells us this, I think that we can only imagine him to be a simple creature who is likely to have been deceived by some wizard or actor whom he met, and whom he thought all-knowing, because he himself was unable to analyse the nature of knowledge and ignorance and imitation."
D: Refashioned: Socrates: And whenever someone tells us they've found a person who knows every art and every subject with greater accuracy than any specialist -- we can only conclude that this person is naive. They've been taken in by some kind of magician or performer whom they mistook for an expert, because they themselves couldn't distinguish between real knowledge, ignorance, and mere imitation.
D: Elegant: Anyone who claims to have found a master of all knowledge has simply been fooled by a clever imitator. The deception works because the deceived person cannot tell the difference between genuine knowledge and imitation.
D: Summary: Claims of universal expertise reveal naivety in the claimant, who has been deceived by a skilled imitator and lacks the ability to distinguish real knowledge from imitation.
D: Distilled: Those who believe someone knows everything have simply been deceived because they cannot distinguish knowledge from imitation.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates (with Glaucon assenting) -- This targets the popular belief that Homer was an authority on all subjects -- The inability to "analyse the nature of knowledge and ignorance and imitation" is the core vulnerability exploited by poets -- Cross-ref: Ion 531a-542b where Ion claims Homer knows all arts -- Cross-ref: Hippias Minor on Hippias's claim to universal knowledge

D: Original: Most true. And so, when we hear persons saying that the tragedians, and Homer, who is at their head, know all the arts and all things human, virtue as well as vice, and divine things too, for that the good poet cannot compose well unless he knows his subject, and that he who has not this knowledge can never be a poet, we ought to consider whether here also there may not be a similar illusion. Perhaps they may have come across imitators and been deceived by them; they may not have remembered when they saw their works that these were but imitations thrice removed from the truth, and could easily be made without any knowledge of the truth, because they are appearances only and not realities? Or, after all, they may be in the right, and poets do really know the things about which they seem to the many to speak so well?
D: Narrative: "Most true. And so, when we hear persons saying that the tragedians, and Homer, who is at their head, know all the arts and all things human, virtue as well as vice, and divine things too, for that the good poet cannot compose well unless he knows his subject, and that he who has not this knowledge can never be a poet, we ought to consider whether here also there may not be a similar illusion. Perhaps they may have come across imitators and been deceived by them; they may not have remembered when they saw their works that these were but imitations thrice removed from the truth, and could easily be made without any knowledge of the truth, because they are appearances only and not realities? Or, after all, they may be in the right, and poets do really know the things about which they seem to the many to speak so well?"
D: Refashioned: Socrates: So when people tell us that the tragedians -- with Homer at their head -- understand all arts, all human affairs (virtue and vice alike), and even divine matters, because a good poet supposedly must know his subject thoroughly, we should ask whether this is the same illusion at work. Perhaps these admirers encountered imitators and were fooled. They forgot that what they were seeing were imitations three steps removed from truth, produced without any real knowledge, because they're appearances, not realities. Or perhaps the admirers are right, and poets genuinely know what they seem to speak about so well?
D: Elegant: When people claim Homer and the tragedians know all arts, human affairs, and divine matters, we should suspect the same illusion. Their audiences forgot they were seeing imitations thrice removed from truth -- appearances made without real knowledge. The question remains: do poets genuinely know what they appear to speak about so well?
D: Summary: The popular claim that Homer and the tragedians possess universal knowledge of arts, virtue, and divinity may be an illusion. Their audiences may have been deceived by appearances thrice removed from truth, forgetting that imitations require no real knowledge to produce.
D: Distilled: The widespread belief that poets know everything they depict may be the grandest case of mistaking imitation for knowledge.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Homer is specifically named as head of the tragic tradition -- The claim that "a good poet must know his subject" was widely held in Greek culture -- "Thrice removed from the truth" is the key formula -- Cross-ref: Ion 532c-534e where Socrates challenges this assumption -- Cross-ref: Protagoras 316d on poets as disguised sophists

D: Original:
The question, he said, should by all means be considered.

Now do you suppose that if a person were able to make the original as well as the image, he would seriously devote himself to the image-making branch? Would he allow imitation to be the ruling principle of his life, as if he had nothing higher in him?

I should say not. The real artist, who knew what he was imitating, would be interested in realities and not in imitations; and would desire to leave as memorials of himself works many and fair; and, instead of being the author of encomiums, he would prefer to be the theme of them.

Yes, he said, that would be to him a source of much greater honour and profit.
D: Narrative:
"The question," he said, "should by all means be considered."

"Now do you suppose that if a person were able to make the original as well as the image, he would seriously devote himself to the image-making branch? Would he allow imitation to be the ruling principle of his life, as if he had nothing higher in him?"

"I should say not."

"The real artist, who knew what he was imitating, would be interested in realities and not in imitations; and would desire to leave as memorials of himself works many and fair; and, instead of being the author of encomiums, he would prefer to be the theme of them."

"Yes," he said, "that would be to him a source of much greater honour and profit."
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: That question absolutely needs to be examined.

Socrates: Consider this: if someone could produce both the real thing and an image of it, would he seriously devote his life to making images? Would he let imitation govern his entire existence, as though he had nothing better in him?

Glaucon: Of course not.

Socrates: A person with real knowledge would focus on realities, not imitations. He'd want to leave behind actual accomplishments as his legacy. Rather than writing praise poems about others, he'd prefer to be the one praised for real achievements.

Glaucon: Yes, that would bring far greater honor and benefit.
D: Elegant: If someone could create both the real thing and its image, he would never devote himself to image-making. A person with genuine knowledge pursues realities, not imitations, and seeks to be the subject of praise rather than its author.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that anyone who possessed real knowledge would pursue actual accomplishments rather than spend their life creating imitations. The true expert would rather be celebrated for real deeds than write celebrations of others.
D: Distilled: No one who could produce the real thing would settle for making copies -- genuine knowledge drives people toward reality, not imitation.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This argument challenges poets' authority by questioning their life choices -- If Homer truly understood warfare, politics, and virtue, why did he only write about them? -- The preference for being "the theme" rather than "the author" of encomiums reflects Greek values of practical achievement -- Cross-ref: Ion 541b-542a (similar challenge to Homer's practical credentials)

D: Original: Then, I said, we must put a question to Homer; not about medicine, or any of the arts to which his poems only incidentally refer: we are not going to ask him, or any other poet, whether he has cured patients like Asclepius, or left behind him a school of medicine such as the Asclepiads were, or whether he only talks about medicine and other arts at second hand; but we have a right to know respecting military tactics, politics, education, which are the chiefest and noblest subjects of his poems, and we may fairly ask him about them. 'Friend Homer,' then we say to him, 'if you are only in the second remove from truth in what you say of virtue, and not in the third --not an image maker or imitator --and if you are able to discern what pursuits make men better or worse in private or public life, tell us what State was ever better governed by your help? The good order of Lacedaemon is due to Lycurgus, and many other cities great and small have been similarly benefited by others; but who says that you have been a good legislator to them and have done them any good? Italy and Sicily boast of Charondas, and there is Solon who is renowned among us; but what city has anything to say about you?' Is there any city which he might name?
D: Narrative: "Then," I said, "we must put a question to Homer; not about medicine, or any of the arts to which his poems only incidentally refer: we are not going to ask him, or any other poet, whether he has cured patients like Asclepius, or left behind him a school of medicine such as the Asclepiads were, or whether he only talks about medicine and other arts at second hand; but we have a right to know respecting military tactics, politics, education, which are the chiefest and noblest subjects of his poems, and we may fairly ask him about them. 'Friend Homer,' then we say to him, 'if you are only in the second remove from truth in what you say of virtue, and not in the third -- not an image maker or imitator -- and if you are able to discern what pursuits make men better or worse in private or public life, tell us what State was ever better governed by your help? The good order of Lacedaemon is due to Lycurgus, and many other cities great and small have been similarly benefited by others; but who says that you have been a good legislator to them and have done them any good? Italy and Sicily boast of Charondas, and there is Solon who is renowned among us; but what city has anything to say about you?' Is there any city which he might name?"
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: So let's put the question directly to Homer. We won't quiz him on medicine or other arts his poems only mention in passing -- we won't ask if he cured patients like Asclepius or founded a medical school like the Asclepiads. But we have every right to examine him on the chief subjects of his poetry: military strategy, politics, and education.

"Dear Homer," we'd say, "if your knowledge of virtue puts you only second from truth rather than third -- if you're not merely an image-maker but someone who truly understands what makes people better or worse in private and public life -- then tell us: what city was ever better governed because of you? Sparta owes its order to Lycurgus. Many cities owe theirs to various lawgivers. Italy and Sicily credit Charondas. Athens celebrates Solon. But what city credits you with anything?" Can he name a single one?
D: Elegant: Homer should be tested not on incidental subjects but on what his poems mainly address: military strategy, politics, and education. If he truly understood virtue, some city should have benefited from his guidance. Sparta has Lycurgus, Athens has Solon, Italy has Charondas -- but what city can Homer claim?
D: Summary: Socrates proposes testing Homer on the subjects central to his poetry -- war, politics, and education. If Homer truly understood virtue, some city would have been better governed through his influence. Yet unlike real lawgivers such as Lycurgus, Solon, and Charondas, Homer can claim no such achievement.
D: Distilled: If Homer truly understood the virtue he wrote about, some city would have benefited from his wisdom -- yet none can be named.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Asclepius: legendary healer/physician, son of Apollo; Asclepiads: medical guild claiming descent from him -- Lycurgus: legendary lawgiver of Sparta -- Charondas: lawgiver of Catana in Sicily -- Solon: Athenian statesman and lawgiver (c. 630-560 BC) -- The challenge to Homer's practical credentials is central to the argument -- Cross-ref: Ion 540d-541d (similar test of Homer's practical knowledge) -- Cross-ref: Laws 858e-859a on lawgivers

D: Original:
I think not, said Glaucon; not even the Homerids themselves pretend that he was a legislator.

Well, but is there any war on record which was carried on successfully by him, or aided by his counsels, when he was alive?

There is not. Or is there any invention of his, applicable to the arts or to human life, such as Thales the Milesian or Anacharsis the Scythian, and other ingenious men have conceived, which is attributed to him?

There is absolutely nothing of the kind. But, if Homer never did any public service, was he privately a guide or teacher of any? Had he in his lifetime friends who loved to associate with him, and who handed down to posterity an Homeric way of life, such as was established by Pythagoras who was so greatly beloved for his wisdom, and whose followers are to this day quite celebrated for the order which was named after him?

Nothing of the kind is recorded of him. For surely, Socrates, Creophylus, the companion of Homer, that child of flesh, whose name always makes us laugh, might be more justly ridiculed for his stupidity, if, as is said, Homer was greatly neglected by him and others in his own day when he was alive?
D: Narrative:
"I think not," said Glaucon; "not even the Homerids themselves pretend that he was a legislator."

"Well, but is there any war on record which was carried on successfully by him, or aided by his counsels, when he was alive?"

"There is not."

"Or is there any invention of his, applicable to the arts or to human life, such as Thales the Milesian or Anacharsis the Scythian, and other ingenious men have conceived, which is attributed to him?"

"There is absolutely nothing of the kind."

"But, if Homer never did any public service, was he privately a guide or teacher of any? Had he in his lifetime friends who loved to associate with him, and who handed down to posterity an Homeric way of life, such as was established by Pythagoras who was so greatly beloved for his wisdom, and whose followers are to this day quite celebrated for the order which was named after him?"

"Nothing of the kind is recorded of him."

"For surely, Socrates," Glaucon said, "Creophylus, the companion of Homer, that child of flesh, whose name always makes us laugh, might be more justly ridiculed for his stupidity, if, as is said, Homer was greatly neglected by him and others in his own day when he was alive?"
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: I don't think so. Not even Homer's own devoted followers, the Homerids, claim he was a lawgiver.

Socrates: Was there any war that he conducted successfully or guided with his counsel?

Glaucon: None.

Socrates: Did he make any practical invention for the arts or daily life, like Thales of Miletus or Anacharsis the Scythian?

Glaucon: Absolutely nothing.

Socrates: Well, if Homer performed no public service, did he at least serve as a private teacher or mentor? Did he have devoted followers who passed down a "Homeric way of life" to future generations, the way Pythagoras did? Pythagoras was so beloved for his wisdom that his followers are still famous today for the disciplined way of life named after him.

Glaucon: Nothing like that is recorded about Homer. In fact, Socrates, Homer's companion Creophylus -- whose name, "child of flesh," always gets a laugh -- would deserve even more ridicule for his foolishness if it's true that he and others neglected Homer during his own lifetime.
D: Elegant: Homer produced no legislation, won no wars, made no practical inventions like Thales or Anacharsis, and founded no school of followers like Pythagoras. Even his companion Creophylus apparently neglected him. If Homer possessed real wisdom, his contemporaries would not have ignored him.
D: Summary: Glaucon confirms that Homer has no record of lawgiving, military success, practical invention, or founding a school of followers. Even his own companion Creophylus reportedly neglected him -- hardly what one would expect if Homer possessed genuine wisdom.
D: Distilled: Homer left behind no laws, no victories, no inventions, and no school of disciples -- evidence that his apparent wisdom was merely imitation.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Homerids: guild of rhapsodes on Chios claiming descent from Homer -- Thales of Miletus: pre-Socratic philosopher, one of the Seven Sages -- Anacharsis the Scythian: legendary wise traveler, credited with inventing the anchor or potter's wheel -- Pythagoras: philosopher and founder of the Pythagorean school/way of life -- Creophylus: legendary companion of Homer from Samos; his name puns on "kreas" (flesh) -- Cross-ref: Ion 530d on the Homerids

D: Original: Yes, I replied, that is the tradition. But can you imagine, Glaucon, that if Homer had really been able to educate and improve mankind --if he had possessed knowledge and not been a mere imitator --can you imagine, I say, that he would not have had many followers, and been honoured and loved by them? Protagoras of Abdera, and Prodicus of Ceos, and a host of others, have only to whisper to their contemporaries: 'You will never be able to manage either your own house or your own State until you appoint us to be your ministers of education' --and this ingenious device of theirs has such an effect in making them love them that their companions all but carry them about on their shoulders. And is it conceivable that the contemporaries of Homer, or again of Hesiod, would have allowed either of them to go about as rhapsodists, if they had really been able to make mankind virtuous? Would they not have been as unwilling to part with them as with gold, and have compelled them to stay at home with them? Or, if the master would not stay, then the disciples would have followed him about everywhere, until they had got education enough?
D: Narrative: "Yes," I replied, "that is the tradition. But can you imagine, Glaucon, that if Homer had really been able to educate and improve mankind -- if he had possessed knowledge and not been a mere imitator -- can you imagine, I say, that he would not have had many followers, and been honoured and loved by them? Protagoras of Abdera, and Prodicus of Ceos, and a host of others, have only to whisper to their contemporaries: 'You will never be able to manage either your own house or your own State until you appoint us to be your ministers of education' -- and this ingenious device of theirs has such an effect in making them love them that their companions all but carry them about on their shoulders. And is it conceivable that the contemporaries of Homer, or again of Hesiod, would have allowed either of them to go about as rhapsodists, if they had really been able to make mankind virtuous? Would they not have been as unwilling to part with them as with gold, and have compelled them to stay at home with them? Or, if the master would not stay, then the disciples would have followed him about everywhere, until they had got education enough?"
D: Refashioned: Socrates: That's the tradition. But think about it, Glaucon -- if Homer had truly been able to educate and improve people, if he'd possessed real knowledge rather than being a mere imitator, wouldn't he have attracted devoted followers who honored and loved him? Look at Protagoras of Abdera and Prodicus of Ceos. They merely whisper to people, "You'll never manage your household or your city until you put us in charge of your education," and their contemporaries practically carry them around on their shoulders out of admiration. Is it really conceivable that Homer's or Hesiod's contemporaries would have let them wander around as traveling performers if they could truly make people virtuous? They would have clung to them like gold, insisting they stay. And if the master refused to stay, the students would have followed him everywhere until they'd learned enough.
D: Elegant: If Homer truly possessed the knowledge to improve people, he would have attracted devoted followers, as the Sophists Protagoras and Prodicus do merely by promising education. Would Homer's contemporaries really have let him wander as a traveling performer if he could genuinely make people virtuous? They would have held onto him like gold.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that if Homer had genuine knowledge, he would have attracted passionate followers like the Sophists Protagoras and Prodicus do. The fact that Homer and Hesiod were allowed to roam as traveling performers proves their contemporaries didn't find real wisdom in them.
D: Distilled: If Homer could truly make people virtuous, his contemporaries would never have let him wander as a mere traveling reciter.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Protagoras of Abdera: most famous Sophist, charged fees for teaching virtue and politics -- Prodicus of Ceos: Sophist known for precision with words and his "Choice of Heracles" -- Hesiod: epic poet, author of Theogony and Works and Days, roughly contemporary with Homer -- The comparison with Sophists is ironic given Plato's critique of them elsewhere -- Rhapsodists: professional reciters of epic poetry -- Cross-ref: Protagoras 316c-317c on Sophists and their reception

D: Original: Yes, Socrates, that, I think, is quite true. Then must we not infer that all these poetical individuals, beginning with Homer, are only imitators; they copy images of virtue and the like, but the truth they never reach? The poet is like a painter who, as we have already observed, will make a likeness of a cobbler though he understands nothing of cobbling; and his picture is good enough for those who know no more than he does, and judge only by colours and figures.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, Socrates, that, I think, is quite true."

"Then must we not infer that all these poetical individuals, beginning with Homer, are only imitators; they copy images of virtue and the like, but the truth they never reach? The poet is like a painter who, as we have already observed, will make a likeness of a cobbler though he understands nothing of cobbling; and his picture is good enough for those who know no more than he does, and judge only by colours and figures."
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: Yes, Socrates, I think that's entirely right.

Socrates: Then we must conclude that all these poets, starting with Homer, are merely imitators. They copy images of virtue and similar things, but they never grasp the truth itself. The poet is like the painter we discussed -- someone who can produce a convincing picture of a cobbler without understanding cobbling at all. His picture looks good enough to people who know no more than he does and judge only by colors and shapes.
D: Elegant: All poets beginning with Homer are imitators who copy images of virtue without reaching truth. Like a painter who renders a convincing cobbler without knowing cobbling, the poet satisfies only those who judge by surface appearance.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes that all poets from Homer onward are mere imitators who copy images of virtue without grasping truth itself, much like painters who produce convincing pictures of craftsmen without understanding the crafts depicted.
D: Distilled: Poets copy images of virtue the way painters copy appearances of craftsmen -- convincing only to those who know no better.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the conclusion of the argument from 10.9-10.11 -- "Images of virtue" is key: poets don't convey virtue itself but only its appearance -- The cobbler analogy recurs from 10.5 -- "Judge only by colours and figures" = superficial assessment without understanding substance -- Cross-ref: Books II-III where specific poetic misrepresentations of virtue were catalogued

D: Original: Quite so. In like manner the poet with his words and phrases may be said to lay on the colours of the several arts, himself understanding their nature only enough to imitate them; and other people, who are as ignorant as he is, and judge only from his words, imagine that if he speaks of cobbling, or of military tactics, or of anything else, in metre and harmony and rhythm, he speaks very well --such is the sweet influence which melody and rhythm by nature have. And I think that you must have observed again and again what a poor appearance the tales of poets make when stripped of the colours which music puts upon them, and recited in simple prose.
D: Narrative: "Quite so. In like manner the poet with his words and phrases may be said to lay on the colours of the several arts, himself understanding their nature only enough to imitate them; and other people, who are as ignorant as he is, and judge only from his words, imagine that if he speaks of cobbling, or of military tactics, or of anything else, in metre and harmony and rhythm, he speaks very well -- such is the sweet influence which melody and rhythm by nature have. And I think that you must have observed again and again what a poor appearance the tales of poets make when stripped of the colours which music puts upon them, and recited in simple prose."
D: Refashioned: Socrates: Exactly. The poet does with words what the painter does with colors -- he applies the surface appearance of various arts and sciences, understanding them just enough to imitate them. And other people, equally ignorant, judge only by his words and assume that when he speaks of cobbling or military strategy or anything else in meter, harmony, and rhythm, he's speaking with real authority. That's the seductive power that melody and rhythm naturally possess. And I'm sure you've noticed how pitiful the tales of poets look when you strip away the musical coloring and recite them in plain prose.
D: Elegant: The poet applies the colors of various arts through words, understanding them just enough to imitate. Audiences, equally ignorant, mistake meter, harmony, and rhythm for authority. Strip away the music, recite poetry in plain prose, and you'll see how thin the content really is.
D: Summary: Poets use words the way painters use colors -- applying surface appearances of various arts. The seductive power of meter, harmony, and rhythm makes audiences mistake imitation for expertise. Stripped of musical embellishment and read as plain prose, poetic content is revealed as thin.
D: Distilled: Poetry's authority is an illusion created by meter and rhythm -- strip away the music and its content is exposed as shallow.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The "colours of music" metaphor connects painting and poetry as parallel forms of imitation -- The idea of stripping poetry of meter to test its content is a powerful critical method -- This anticipates modern debates about style vs. substance in literature -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 502c-d on rhetoric as flattery that uses pleasure to bypass reason -- Cross-ref: Laws 669b-670a on separating rhythm and melody from words

D: Original:
Yes, he said. They are like faces which were never really beautiful, but only blooming; and now the bloom of youth has passed away from them?

Exactly. Here is another point: The imitator or maker of the image knows nothing of true existence; he knows appearances only. Am I not right?

Yes. Then let us have a clear understanding, and not be satisfied with half an explanation.

Proceed. Of the painter we say that he will paint reins, and he will paint a bit?

Yes. And the worker in leather and brass will make them? Certainly. But does the painter know the right form of the bit and reins? Nay, hardly even the workers in brass and leather who make them; only the horseman who knows how to use them --he knows their right form.

Most true. And may we not say the same of all things? What? That there are three arts which are concerned with all things: one which uses, another which makes, a third which imitates them?

Yes. And the excellence or beauty or truth of every structure, animate or inanimate, and of every action of man, is relative to the use for which nature or the artist has intended them.

True. Then the user of them must have the greatest experience of them, and he must indicate to the maker the good or bad qualities which develop themselves in use; for example, the flute-player will tell the flute-maker which of his flutes is satisfactory to the performer; he will tell him how he ought to make them, and the other will attend to his instructions?

Of course. The one knows and therefore speaks with authority about the goodness and badness of flutes, while the other, confiding in him, will do what he is told by him?

True. The instrument is the same, but about the excellence or badness of it the maker will only attain to a correct belief; and this he will gain from him who knows, by talking to him and being compelled to hear what he has to say, whereas the user will have knowledge?

D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: Yes.

Socrates: They're like faces that were never truly beautiful, only youthful -- and now the bloom has faded.

Glaucon: Exactly.

Socrates: Here's a further point: the imitator knows nothing of true existence, only appearances. Agreed?

Glaucon: Yes.

Socrates: Let's get complete clarity. A painter can paint reins and a bit. A leatherworker and metalworker can make them. But does the painter know the correct form of the bit and reins? Hardly. Not even the craftsmen who make them truly know -- only the horseman who actually uses them understands their proper form.

Glaucon: Very true.

Socrates: Can we generalize this? For all things, there are three arts: one that uses, one that makes, and one that imitates.

Glaucon: Yes.

Socrates: And the excellence or truth of anything -- any object or action -- is determined by its intended use. Therefore the user has the most experience and must guide the maker about what works well and what doesn't. The flute-player tells the flute-maker which instruments perform well and how to improve them.

Glaucon: Of course.

Socrates: The player speaks with knowledge about quality, while the maker, listening and learning, attains only correct belief. The user has genuine knowledge; the maker has informed opinion.
D: Elegant: Poetry stripped of music is like a face that was never beautiful, only youthful -- now faded. Three arts relate to every object: using, making, and imitating. Only the user truly knows what excellence means, since quality is determined by function. The maker attains correct belief by learning from the user; the imitator, who consults neither, knows nothing at all.
D: Summary: Socrates introduces a three-art hierarchy: using, making, and imitating. The user alone has genuine knowledge of an object's excellence, since quality is defined by function. The maker achieves only correct belief by learning from the user, while the imitator consults neither and knows nothing of true quality.
D: Distilled: Only the user has real knowledge of a thing's excellence; the maker has informed belief; the imitator has neither.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The user/maker/imitator triad parallels the Form/particular/image triad from earlier -- The distinction between knowledge (episteme) and correct belief (orthe doxa) echoes Meno 97a-98b and Theaetetus -- The horseman/bit example and flute-player/flute-maker example ground the abstract argument in practical experience -- Cross-ref: Cratylus 390b-d on the user as judge of the maker's work

D: Original:
True. But will the imitator have either? Will he know from use whether or no his drawing is correct or beautiful? Or will he have right opinion from being compelled to associate with another who knows and gives him instructions about what he should draw?

Neither. Then he will no more have true opinion than he will have knowledge about the goodness or badness of his imitations?

I suppose not. The imitative artist will be in a brilliant state of intelligence about his own creations?

Nay, very much the reverse. And still he will go on imitating without knowing what makes a thing good or bad, and may be expected therefore to imitate only that which appears to be good to the ignorant multitude?

Just so. Thus far then we are pretty well agreed that the imitator has no knowledge worth mentioning of what he imitates. Imitation is only a kind of play or sport, and the tragic poets, whether they write in iambic or in Heroic verse, are imitators in the highest degree?

Very true. And now tell me, I conjure you, has not imitation been shown by us to be concerned with that which is thrice removed from the truth?

Certainly. And what is the faculty in man to which imitation is addressed?

What do you mean? I will explain: The body which is large when seen near, appears small when seen at a distance?
D: Narrative:
"True. But will the imitator have either? Will he know from use whether or no his drawing is correct or beautiful? Or will he have right opinion from being compelled to associate with another who knows and gives him instructions about what he should draw?"

"Neither."

"Then he will no more have true opinion than he will have knowledge about the goodness or badness of his imitations?"

"I suppose not."

"Nay, very much the reverse."

"Just so."

"Thus far then we are pretty well agreed that the imitator has no knowledge worth mentioning of what he imitates. Imitation is only a kind of play or sport, and the tragic poets, whether they write in iambic or in Heroic verse, are imitators in the highest degree?"

"Very true."

"And now tell me, I conjure you, has not imitation been shown by us to be concerned with that which is thrice removed from the truth?"

"Certainly."

"What do you mean?"

D: Refashioned:
Socrates: But what about the imitator? Does he have knowledge from use, or even correct belief from working alongside an expert?

Glaucon: Neither.

Socrates: So he has neither true opinion nor knowledge about whether his imitations are good or bad?

Glaucon: I suppose not.

Socrates: The imitative artist must be brilliantly intelligent about his own work, then?

Glaucon: Quite the opposite.

Socrates: Yet he'll keep imitating without understanding what makes something good or bad, so he'll naturally imitate whatever looks good to the ignorant majority.

Glaucon: Exactly.

Socrates: Then we agree: the imitator has no real knowledge of what he imitates. Imitation is merely a form of play. The tragic poets, whether writing in iambic or heroic verse, are imitators of the highest order.

Glaucon: Very true.

Socrates: And we've shown that imitation deals with what is three steps removed from truth?

Glaucon: Certainly.

Socrates: Now I want to ask: what faculty in the human mind does imitation appeal to? Let me explain what I mean. An object that appears large close up looks small from a distance, right?
D: Elegant: The imitator possesses neither the user's knowledge nor the maker's informed belief. He imitates blindly, copying whatever appears good to the ignorant majority. Imitation is mere play. Having established that imitation is thrice removed from truth, Socrates now turns to a new question: which faculty of the mind does imitation target? He begins with the observation that perception deceives -- objects appear different sizes at different distances.
D: Summary: The imitator has neither knowledge nor correct belief about what he copies, so he imitates whatever appeals to the ignorant majority. With imitation confirmed as thrice removed from truth, Socrates pivots to ask which part of the mind imitation targets, introducing optical illusion as his starting point.
D: Distilled: The imitator knows nothing of what he copies and appeals to the ignorant; now the question is which part of the soul he corrupts.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- "Imitation is only a kind of play" (paidia) -- this is a strong dismissal -- Iambic verse: meter of dramatic dialogue; Heroic verse: dactylic hexameter of epic -- The transition to perceptual illusion (large/small at distance) bridges the metaphysical argument about Forms to the psychological argument about the soul's faculties -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IV (435a-441c) on the tripartite soul -- Cross-ref: Theaetetus 152a-186e on perception and knowledge

D: Original: True. And the same object appears straight when looked at out of the water, and crooked when in the water; and the concave becomes convex, owing to the illusion about colours to which the sight is liable. Thus every sort of confusion is revealed within us; and this is that weakness of the human mind on which the art of conjuring and of deceiving by light and shadow and other ingenious devices imposes, having an effect upon us like magic.
D: Narrative: "True. And the same object appears straight when looked at out of the water, and crooked when in the water; and the concave becomes convex, owing to the illusion about colours to which the sight is liable. Thus every sort of confusion is revealed within us; and this is that weakness of the human mind on which the art of conjuring and of deceiving by light and shadow and other ingenious devices imposes, having an effect upon us like magic."
D: Refashioned: Socrates: And a straight stick appears bent when partly submerged in water. Concave surfaces can appear convex because of color illusions that trick our sight. Every kind of confusion exists within us, and this mental weakness is exactly what conjurers exploit -- deceiving us with light, shadow, and other clever tricks that work on us like magic.
D: Elegant: Our perception is riddled with confusion: sticks look bent in water, concave appears convex. This inherent weakness of the mind is exactly what conjurers and deceivers exploit, working upon us like magic through illusions of light and shadow.
D: Summary: Socrates catalogs perceptual illusions -- bent sticks in water, inverted surfaces -- to show that the human mind harbors inherent confusion, which is precisely the weakness exploited by conjurers, artists, and poets.
D: Distilled: The mind's vulnerability to perceptual illusion is the very weakness that all imitative arts exploit.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates (with Glaucon assenting) -- The bent stick in water is a classic example of perceptual illusion in ancient philosophy -- "Art of conjuring" (goeteia) links imitators to magicians/deceivers -- This passage connects the metaphysical argument (imitation is far from truth) to the psychological argument (imitation targets the irrational part of the soul) -- Cross-ref: Theaetetus 152d-e on conflicting perceptions -- Cross-ref: Sophist 235b-236c on deceptive appearances

D: Original:
True. And the arts of measuring and numbering and weighing come to the rescue of the human understanding-there is the beauty of them --and the apparent greater or less, or more or heavier, no longer have the mastery over us, but give way before calculation and measure and weight?

Most true. And this, surely, must be the work of the calculating and rational principle in the soul

To be sure. And when this principle measures and certifies that some things are equal, or that some are greater or less than others, there occurs an apparent contradiction?

True. But were we not saying that such a contradiction is the same faculty cannot have contrary opinions at the same time about the same thing?

Very true. Then that part of the soul which has an opinion contrary to measure is not the same with that which has an opinion in accordance with measure?

True. And the better part of the soul is likely to be that which trusts to measure and calculation?

Certainly. And that which is opposed to them is one of the inferior principles of the soul?

No doubt. This was the conclusion at which I was seeking to arrive when I said that painting or drawing, and imitation in general, when doing their own proper work, are far removed from truth, and the companions and friends and associates of a principle within us which is equally removed from reason, and that they have no true or healthy aim.

Exactly. The imitative art is an inferior who marries an inferior, and has inferior offspring.

Very true. And is this confined to the sight only, or does it extend to the hearing also, relating in fact to what we term poetry?

Probably the same would be true of poetry. Do not rely, I said, on a probability derived from the analogy of painting; but let us examine further and see whether the faculty with which poetical imitation is concerned is good or bad.

By all means. We may state the question thus: --Imitation imitates the actions of men, whether voluntary or involuntary, on which, as they imagine, a good or bad result has ensued, and they rejoice or sorrow accordingly. Is there anything more?
D: Narrative:
"True. And the arts of measuring and numbering and weighing come to the rescue of the human understanding -- there is the beauty of them -- and the apparent greater or less, or more or heavier, no longer have the mastery over us, but give way before calculation and measure and weight?"

"Most true."

"And this, surely, must be the work of the calculating and rational principle in the soul?"

"To be sure."

"And when this principle measures and certifies that some things are equal, or that some are greater or less than others, there occurs an apparent contradiction?"

"True."

"But were we not saying that such a contradiction is impossible -- the same faculty cannot have contrary opinions at the same time about the same thing?"

"Very true."

"Then that part of the soul which has an opinion contrary to measure is not the same with that which has an opinion in accordance with measure?"

"True."

"And the better part of the soul is likely to be that which trusts to measure and calculation?"

"Certainly."

"And that which is opposed to them is one of the inferior principles of the soul?"

"No doubt."

"This was the conclusion at which I was seeking to arrive when I said that painting or drawing, and imitation in general, when doing their own proper work, are far removed from truth, and the companions and friends and associates of a principle within us which is equally removed from reason, and that they have no true or healthy aim."

"Exactly."

"The imitative art is an inferior who marries an inferior, and has inferior offspring."

"Very true."

"And is this confined to the sight only, or does it extend to the hearing also, relating in fact to what we term poetry?"

"Probably the same would be true of poetry."

"Do not rely," I said, "on a probability derived from the analogy of painting; but let us examine further and see whether the faculty with which poetical imitation is concerned is good or bad."

"By all means."

"We may state the question thus: -- Imitation imitates the actions of men, whether voluntary or involuntary, on which, as they imagine, a good or bad result has ensued, and they rejoice or sorrow accordingly. Is there anything more?"

"No, there is nothing else."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: But the arts of measuring, counting, and weighing rescue our understanding. Once we have calculation and measurement, illusions of greater and lesser lose their power over us.

Glaucon: Absolutely.

Socrates: And this must be the work of the rational, calculating part of the soul?

Glaucon: Of course.

Socrates: But when reason measures and declares things equal or unequal, something in us still perceives them differently -- an apparent contradiction?

Glaucon: Yes.

Socrates: Yet we established that the same faculty can't hold contrary opinions about the same thing simultaneously. So the part of the soul that contradicts measurement must be a different part from the one that follows measurement.

Glaucon: True.

Socrates: The part that trusts calculation is the better part, and the part that opposes it is an inferior principle?

Glaucon: No doubt.

Socrates: This is exactly my point. Painting and all imitative art, when doing their proper work, are far from truth and allied with a principle in us equally far from reason. They have no true or healthy aim. Imitative art is an inferior partnered with an inferior, producing inferior offspring.

Glaucon: Very true.

Socrates: Now, does this apply only to sight, or also to hearing -- that is, to poetry?

Glaucon: Probably to poetry as well.

Socrates: Don't rely on probability from analogy. Let's examine directly whether the faculty that poetic imitation engages is good or bad. Here's the question: imitation depicts human actions, voluntary or involuntary, and the joy or sorrow people feel depending on whether they think the outcome was good or bad. Is there more to it?

Glaucon: No, that covers it.
D: Elegant: Measuring, counting, and weighing rescue us from perceptual illusion -- the work of the rational soul. When reason declares objects equal but perception disagrees, two different faculties are at work. Imitation allies with the inferior, irrational faculty, far from truth. "Imitative art is an inferior who marries an inferior, and has inferior offspring." Extending this from painting to poetry: imitation depicts human actions and the joy or sorrow that follows perceived good or bad outcomes.
D: Summary: Rational tools like measurement and calculation overcome perceptual illusion, proving that two distinct faculties operate in the soul. Imitative art allies with the inferior, irrational faculty. Socrates extends this critique from visual art to poetry, which imitates human actions and the emotions that follow them.
D: Distilled: Imitative art partners with the irrational part of the soul that resists reason, producing inferior offspring in both painting and poetry.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The principle of non-contradiction (same faculty cannot hold contrary opinions about the same thing) was established in Book IV (436b-437a) -- "Inferior who marries an inferior" is a striking metaphor for the alliance between imitation and irrationality -- The transition from visual to poetic imitation marks a crucial turn in the argument -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IV (435a-441c) on the tripartite soul and the principle of non-contradiction -- Cross-ref: Philebus 41a-42c on false pleasures and perceptual illusion

D: Original: No, there is nothing else. Though I need hardly raise the question again, for I remember that all this has been already admitted; and the soul has been acknowledged by us to be full of these and ten thousand similar oppositions occurring at the same moment?
D: Narrative: "No, there is nothing else. But in all this variety of circumstances is the man at unity with himself -- or rather, as in the instance of sight there was confusion and opposition in his opinions about the same things, so here also is there not strife and inconsistency in his life? Though I need hardly raise the question again, for I remember that all this has been already admitted; and the soul has been acknowledged by us to be full of these and ten thousand similar oppositions occurring at the same moment?"
D: Refashioned: Socrates: But in all these varied circumstances, is a person consistent within himself? Or rather, just as our sight produced conflicting impressions about the same objects, doesn't the same conflict and inconsistency appear in our emotional lives? Though I hardly need to raise this again -- we've already acknowledged that the soul is full of these internal oppositions, thousands of them occurring simultaneously.
D: Elegant: Just as sight produces conflicting impressions of the same object, our emotional lives contain constant internal conflict. The soul is full of countless simultaneous oppositions -- this was already established.
D: Summary: Socrates draws a parallel between perceptual illusions and emotional conflicts: just as our eyes send contradictory signals about the same object, our souls experience constant internal opposition in how we respond to events.
D: Distilled: The soul's internal conflicts in emotional life parallel the contradictions of perceptual illusion.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates (with Glaucon assenting) -- "Ten thousand similar oppositions" refers to the tripartite soul argument from Book IV (435a-441c) -- The parallel between visual and emotional conflict is central to extending the critique from painting to poetry -- This sets up the argument about grief and emotional indulgence that follows -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IV (439c-440e) on the soul's internal conflicts -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 246a-249d on the charioteer metaphor for the soul's internal struggles

D: Original:
And we were right, he said. Yes, I said, thus far we were right; but there was an omission which must now be supplied.

What was the omission? Were we not saying that a good man, who has the misfortune to lose his son or anything else which is most dear to him, will bear the loss with more equanimity than another?

Yes. But will he have no sorrow, or shall we say that although he cannot help sorrowing, he will moderate his sorrow?

The latter, he said, is the truer statement. Tell me: will he be more likely to struggle and hold out against his sorrow when he is seen by his equals, or when he is alone?

It will make a great difference whether he is seen or not.

When he is by himself he will not mind saying or doing many things which he would be ashamed of any one hearing or seeing him do?

True. There is a principle of law and reason in him which bids him resist, as well as a feeling of his misfortune which is forcing him to indulge his sorrow?

True. But when a man is drawn in two opposite directions, to and from the same object, this, as we affirm, necessarily implies two distinct principles in him?
D: Narrative:
"And we were right," he said.

"Yes," I said, "thus far we were right; but there was an omission which must now be supplied."

"What was the omission?"

"Were we not saying that a good man, who has the misfortune to lose his son or anything else which is most dear to him, will bear the loss with more equanimity than another?"

"Yes."

"But will he have no sorrow, or shall we say that although he cannot help sorrowing, he will moderate his sorrow?"

"The latter," he said, "is the truer statement."

"Tell me: will he be more likely to struggle and hold out against his sorrow when he is seen by his equals, or when he is alone?"

"It will make a great difference whether he is seen or not."

"When he is by himself he will not mind saying or doing many things which he would be ashamed of any one hearing or seeing him do?"

"True."

"There is a principle of law and reason in him which bids him resist, as well as a feeling of his misfortune which is forcing him to indulge his sorrow?"

"True."

"But when a man is drawn in two opposite directions, to and from the same object, this, as we affirm, necessarily implies two distinct principles in him?"
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: And we were right about that.

Socrates: Yes, but we left something out that needs to be addressed now.

Glaucon: What was the omission?

Socrates: We said that a good person who loses a child or anything else most precious will bear the loss with greater composure than others. But will he feel no grief at all, or will he simply moderate his grief?

Glaucon: He'll moderate it -- that's more accurate.

Socrates: And will he be better at controlling his sorrow when others are watching, or when he's alone?

Glaucon: It makes a huge difference whether he's being observed or not.

Socrates: When alone, he'll say and do things he'd be ashamed of if anyone could see or hear him?

Glaucon: True.

Socrates: So there's a principle of reason and law within him that tells him to resist, and simultaneously a feeling of grief that pushes him to indulge his sorrow?

Glaucon: True.

Socrates: And when someone is pulled in two opposite directions regarding the same thing, that necessarily means two distinct principles are operating within him?
D: Elegant: A good person who suffers loss will moderate grief but not eliminate it. He controls himself better when observed than when alone -- revealing two opposing inner principles: reason commanding restraint, and feeling demanding indulgence. Being pulled in opposite directions about the same thing proves two distinct faculties at work.
D: Summary: Socrates examines how even a good person experiences internal conflict when grieving. Reason commands restraint while emotion demands indulgence, and the struggle is harder in private than in public -- proving that two distinct principles operate within the soul during suffering.
D: Distilled: Grief reveals the soul's internal war: reason commands restraint while emotion demands indulgence, especially when no one is watching.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This applies the tripartite soul from Book IV to the specific case of grief -- The observation that people restrain emotion more in public anticipates the argument about theater audiences -- The "two distinct principles" argument follows the same logic as Book IV (436b-437a) -- Cross-ref: Republic Book III (387d-388d) where excessive grieving by heroes was censured -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 117c-118a where Socrates himself demonstrates rational composure at death

D: Original: Certainly. One of them is ready to follow the guidance of the law? How do you mean? The law would say that to be patient under suffering is best, and that we should not give way to impatience, as there is no knowing whether such things are good or evil; and nothing is gained by impatience; also, because no human thing is of serious importance, and grief stands in the way of that which at the moment is most required.
D: Narrative:
"Certainly. One of them is ready to follow the guidance of the law?"

"How do you mean?"

"The law would say that to be patient under suffering is best, and that we should not give way to impatience, as there is no knowing whether such things are good or evil; and nothing is gained by impatience; also, because no human thing is of serious importance, and grief stands in the way of that which at the moment is most required."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: One of these principles is ready to follow law and reason.

Glaucon: What do you mean?

Socrates: The law tells us that patience in suffering is best. We shouldn't surrender to impatience, since we can never be certain whether such events are ultimately good or evil. Nothing is gained by impatience. Besides, no human affair is truly of great importance, and grief only blocks us from doing what the moment most requires.
D: Elegant: One principle in the soul follows reason and law, which counsels patience in suffering: we cannot know if events are ultimately good or evil, nothing is gained by impatience, no human affair is truly of great importance, and grief obstructs what the situation most requires.
D: Summary: The rational principle counsels patience in suffering, arguing that we cannot know whether events are ultimately good or evil, that impatience achieves nothing, and that no human affair is of such importance that grief should prevent us from responding wisely.
D: Distilled: Reason counsels patience in suffering because no human affair is of ultimate importance and grief obstructs wise response.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- "No human thing is of serious importance" echoes Laws 803b ("human affairs are not worthy of great seriousness") -- This reflects the Socratic-Platonic view of human life sub specie aeternitatis -- The law/reason principle represents the rational part of the tripartite soul -- Cross-ref: Apology 41c-42a (Socrates on death as potentially good) -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 84a-b on the philosopher's composure before death

D: Original: What is most required? he asked. That we should take counsel about what has happened, and when the dice have been thrown order our affairs in the way which reason deems best; not, like children who have had a fall, keeping hold of the part struck and wasting time in setting up a howl, but always accustoming the soul forthwith to apply a remedy, raising up that which is sickly and fallen, banishing the cry of sorrow by the healing art.
D: Narrative:
"What is most required?" he asked.

"That we should take counsel about what has happened, and when the dice have been thrown order our affairs in the way which reason deems best; not, like children who have had a fall, keeping hold of the part struck and wasting time in setting up a howl, but always accustoming the soul forthwith to apply a remedy, raising up that which is sickly and fallen, banishing the cry of sorrow by the healing art."
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: What is most required?

Socrates: That we think rationally about what has happened and, once the dice have been thrown, arrange our affairs in the way reason judges best. We shouldn't be like children who fall down, clutch the injured spot, and waste time howling. Instead, we should train the soul to immediately apply a remedy -- to raise up what has fallen, to replace the cry of sorrow with the healing art of reason.
D: Elegant: When misfortune strikes, reason demands that we assess the situation and respond as best we can -- not like children who fall and waste time crying while clutching the bruise. The soul should be trained to apply rational remedy immediately, replacing sorrow with the healing art.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that after misfortune, reason demands practical response rather than emotional indulgence. Like children who fall and waste time crying instead of getting up, those who surrender to grief prevent the soul from applying its healing remedy.
D: Distilled: After misfortune, reason demands we get up and respond wisely rather than waste time howling like fallen children.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The dice metaphor for fate/fortune is common in Greek thought -- The child-falling analogy is vivid and memorable -- "Healing art" (iatrike techne) applied metaphorically to the soul -- This passage encapsulates the Socratic ideal of rational composure -- Cross-ref: Laws 644d-e on humans as puppets pulled by different strings -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 521e-522a on the soul's health as the true aim

D: Original:
Yes, he said, that is the true way of meeting the attacks of fortune.

Yes, I said; and the higher principle is ready to follow this suggestion of reason?

Clearly. And the other principle, which inclines us to recollection of our troubles and to lamentation, and can never have enough of them, we may call irrational, useless, and cowardly?

Indeed, we may. And does not the latter --I mean the rebellious principle --furnish a great variety of materials for imitation? Whereas the wise and calm temperament, being always nearly equable, is not easy to imitate or to appreciate when imitated, especially at a public festival when a promiscuous crowd is assembled in a theatre. For the feeling represented is one to which they are strangers.

Certainly. Then the imitative poet who aims at being popular is not by nature made, nor is his art intended, to please or to affect the principle in the soul; but he will prefer the passionate and fitful temper, which is easily imitated?
D: Narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is the true way of meeting the attacks of fortune."

"Yes," I said; "and the higher principle is ready to follow this suggestion of reason?"

"Clearly."

"And the other principle, which inclines us to recollection of our troubles and to lamentation, and can never have enough of them, we may call irrational, useless, and cowardly?"

"Indeed, we may."

"And does not the latter -- I mean the rebellious principle -- furnish a great variety of materials for imitation? Whereas the wise and calm temperament, being always nearly equable, is not easy to imitate or to appreciate when imitated, especially at a public festival when a promiscuous crowd is assembled in a theatre. For the feeling represented is one to which they are strangers."

"Certainly."

"Then the imitative poet who aims at being popular is not by nature made, nor is his art intended, to please or to affect the principle in the soul; but he will prefer the passionate and fitful temper, which is easily imitated?"
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: Yes, that's the right way to handle fortune's blows.

Socrates: And the higher principle in the soul follows reason's guidance?

Glaucon: Clearly.

Socrates: While the other principle -- the one that pulls us toward dwelling on our troubles and lamenting endlessly -- can be called irrational, useless, and cowardly?

Glaucon: Absolutely.

Socrates: Now here's the key point: this rebellious, irrational principle provides rich material for imitation. But the wise, calm temperament, being steady and equable, is very difficult to imitate or appreciate when imitated -- especially before a mixed crowd at a public festival. Such composure is foreign to their experience.

Glaucon: Certainly.

Socrates: So the imitative poet who wants to be popular isn't naturally inclined -- nor is his art designed -- to engage the rational principle. He'll gravitate instead toward the passionate, volatile temperament, which is easy to imitate.
D: Elegant: The irrational principle that craves lamentation provides rich material for imitation, while rational composure is difficult to portray and harder for audiences to appreciate. The popular poet therefore gravitates toward passionate, volatile emotion -- it's easier to imitate and more crowd-pleasing. Poetry naturally allies with the inferior part of the soul.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that irrational, emotional distress provides much better material for dramatic imitation than rational composure, which audiences find boring. The popular poet therefore naturally caters to the passionate, irrational part of the soul rather than the rational part.
D: Distilled: Drama naturally gravitates toward emotional turmoil because rational composure is boring to portray and foreign to most audiences.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- "Promiscuous crowd at a public festival" describes the Athenian theater audience at dramatic festivals (Dionysia, Lenaia) -- The argument that calm virtue is dramatically uninteresting has been enormously influential in literary criticism -- This explains why tragedy focuses on suffering and emotional extremes -- Cross-ref: Republic Book III (387d-388d) where excessive displays of grief by heroes were censured -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Poetics (1449b) which takes the opposite view, defending tragedy's emotional power as catharsis

D: Original:
Clearly. And now we may fairly take him and place him by the side of the painter, for he is like him in two ways: first, inasmuch as his creations have an inferior degree of truth --in this, I say, he is like him; and he is also like him in being concerned with an inferior part of the soul; and therefore we shall be right in refusing to admit him into a well-ordered State, because he awakens and nourishes and strengthens the feelings and impairs the reason. As in a city when the evil are permitted to have authority and the good are put out of the way, so in the soul of man, as we maintain, the imitative poet implants an evil constitution, for he indulges the irrational nature which has no discernment of greater and less, but thinks the same thing at one time great and at another small-he is a manufacturer of images and is very far removed from the truth.

Exactly. But we have not yet brought forward the heaviest count in our accusation: --the power which poetry has of harming even the good (and there are very few who are not harmed), is surely an awful thing?
D: Narrative:
"Clearly. And now we may fairly take him and place him by the side of the painter, for he is like him in two ways: first, inasmuch as his creations have an inferior degree of truth -- in this, I say, he is like him; and he is also like him in being concerned with an inferior part of the soul; and therefore we shall be right in refusing to admit him into a well-ordered State, because he awakens and nourishes and strengthens the feelings and impairs the reason. As in a city when the evil are permitted to have authority and the good are put out of the way, so in the soul of man, as we maintain, the imitative poet implants an evil constitution, for he indulges the irrational nature which has no discernment of greater and less, but thinks the same thing at one time great and at another small -- he is a manufacturer of images and is very far removed from the truth."

"Exactly."

"But we have not yet brought forward the heaviest count in our accusation: -- the power which poetry has of harming even the good (and there are very few who are not harmed), is surely an awful thing?"
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: So we can fairly place the poet beside the painter. They resemble each other in two ways: first, their creations have an inferior degree of truth; second, they engage an inferior part of the soul. We're therefore justified in excluding the imitative poet from a well-ordered State. He awakens, nourishes, and strengthens the emotions while weakening reason. Just as a city decays when evil people are given authority and good ones are sidelined, the imitative poet installs a corrupt constitution in the soul. He indulges the irrational nature that can't distinguish greater from lesser and judges the same thing as great one moment and small the next. He's a manufacturer of images, very far from truth.

Glaucon: Exactly.

Socrates: But we haven't yet made our most serious charge. Poetry has the power to harm even good people -- and very few escape this harm. Isn't that a terrible thing?
D: Elegant: The poet resembles the painter in two ways: both produce inferior truths and both engage the inferior part of the soul. The imitative poet must be excluded from the well-ordered State because he strengthens emotion and weakens reason -- installing a corrupt constitution in the soul, like a city where the wicked rule and the good are sidelined. But the heaviest charge remains: poetry harms even the good, and very few escape.
D: Summary: The poet, like the painter, creates inferior truths and engages the inferior soul. He must be excluded from the ideal State because he strengthens emotions at reason's expense, corrupting the soul's internal order like a city where the wicked seize power. The gravest charge: poetry harms even virtuous people.
D: Distilled: The imitative poet corrupts the soul's constitution by empowering emotion over reason, and even the virtuous are not immune.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- "Evil constitution" (ponera politeia) in the soul parallels the political degenerations described in Books VIII-IX -- "Manufacturer of images" (eidolopoios) is the final verdict on the poet's craft -- The city-soul analogy is the central structural device of the Republic -- "Heaviest count in our accusation" introduces the most dangerous aspect of poetry -- Cross-ref: Republic Books II-III (376e-403c) for the earlier, less philosophically grounded ban on imitative poetry -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IV (441d-442b) on the just soul as one where reason rules

D: Original: Yes, certainly, if the effect is what you say. Hear and judge: The best of us, as I conceive, when we listen to a passage of Homer, or one of the tragedians, in which he represents some pitiful hero who is drawling out his sorrows in a long oration, or weeping, and smiting his breast --the best of us, you know, delight in giving way to sympathy, and are in raptures at the excellence of the poet who stirs our feelings most.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, certainly, if the effect is what you say."

"Hear and judge: The best of us, as I conceive, when we listen to a passage of Homer, or one of the tragedians, in which he represents some pitiful hero who is drawling out his sorrows in a long oration, or weeping, and smiting his breast -- the best of us, you know, delight in giving way to sympathy, and are in raptures at the excellence of the poet who stirs our feelings most."
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: Certainly, if the effect is as you describe.

Socrates: Then hear and judge. Even the best among us, when we listen to Homer or one of the tragedians portraying some pitiful hero drawing out his sorrows in a long speech, or weeping and beating his breast -- even the best of us take pleasure in surrendering to sympathy. We're enraptured by the poet who stirs our emotions most powerfully.
D: Elegant: Even the best of us, listening to Homer or a tragedian portray a hero wallowing in grief -- weeping, beating his breast, delivering long laments -- delight in surrendering to sympathy and admire most the poet who stirs our feelings most powerfully.
D: Summary: Even the most virtuous listeners surrender to sympathy when Homer or the tragedians depict heroes in grief, and they most admire the poet who stirs their emotions most powerfully.
D: Distilled: Even the best of us delight in surrendering to sympathy when a skilled poet portrays suffering.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This begins the "heaviest count" against poetry: its power to corrupt even the virtuous -- Homer is again named specifically -- The description of heroes "drawling out sorrows" and "smiting his breast" recalls specific scenes from the Iliad (e.g., Achilles' grief for Patroclus, Iliad XVIII) -- Cross-ref: Republic Book III (387d-388d) where excessive mourning by heroes was censured -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Poetics 1449b on catharsis as the proper response to tragic suffering

D: Original:
Yes, of course I know. But when any sorrow of our own happens to us, then you may observe that we pride ourselves on the opposite quality --we would fain be quiet and patient; this is the manly part, and the other which delighted us in the recitation is now deemed to be the part of a woman.

Very true, he said. Now can we be right in praising and admiring another who is doing that which any one of us would abominate and be ashamed of in his own person?
D: Narrative:
"Yes, of course I know."

"But when any sorrow of our own happens to us, then you may observe that we pride ourselves on the opposite quality -- we would fain be quiet and patient; this is the manly part, and the other which delighted us in the recitation is now deemed to be the part of a woman."

"Very true," he said.

"Now can we be right in praising and admiring another who is doing that which any one of us would abominate and be ashamed of in his own person?"
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: Yes, I know exactly what you mean.
