Glaucon: Yes -- if he resembles God, he surely won't be neglected by God.

Socrates: And the opposite for the unjust?

Glaucon: Certainly.

Socrates: These, then, are the prizes the gods award to the just.
D: Elegant: The gods know the just from the unjust. The just person is God's friend and receives all things for the best, even if he suffers apparent misfortune -- all things ultimately work for his good, in life and death. The gods care for anyone who pursues virtue and seeks to become like God. The unjust receive the opposite.
D: Summary: Since the gods know the just from the unjust, the just person is God's friend and all things ultimately work for his good, even apparent misfortunes. The gods care for anyone who seeks to become like God through virtue. The unjust receive the opposite fate.
D: Distilled: The gods know and reward the just -- all things work together for his good -- while the unjust receive the opposite.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- "All things will work together for good" (panta eis agathon teleutai) anticipates Romans 8:28 by several centuries -- "To be like God" (homoiosis theo) becomes a central concept in later Platonism -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (362d-367e) for the original concession now being restored -- Cross-ref: Theaetetus 176b on becoming like God as the aim of philosophy -- Cross-ref: Laws 716c-d on the virtuous person as dear to God -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 90a-d on becoming like the divine

D: Original: That is my conviction. And what do they receive of men? Look at things as they really are, and you will see that the clever unjust are in the case of runners, who run well from the starting-place to the goal but not back again from the goal: they go off at a great pace, but in the end only look foolish, slinking away with their ears draggling on their shoulders, and without a crown; but the true runner comes to the finish and receives the prize and is crowned. And this is the way with the just; he who endures to the end of every action and occasion of his entire life has a good report and carries off the prize which men have to bestow.
D: Narrative:
"That is my conviction."

"And what do they receive of men? Look at things as they really are, and you will see that the clever unjust are in the case of runners, who run well from the starting-place to the goal but not back again from the goal: they go off at a great pace, but in the end only look foolish, slinking away with their ears draggling on their shoulders, and without a crown; but the true runner comes to the finish and receives the prize and is crowned. And this is the way with the just; he who endures to the end of every action and occasion of his entire life has a good report and carries off the prize which men have to bestow."
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: That's my conviction too.

Socrates: And what do the just receive from other people? Look at how things really play out. The clever unjust are like runners who sprint impressively from start to finish but can't make it back -- they start fast but end up slinking away looking foolish, ears drooping, with no crown. The true runner finishes the race and receives the prize. So it is with the just: the person who endures to the end in every action and situation of his entire life earns a good reputation and wins the prizes that humanity has to offer.
D: Elegant: The clever unjust are like runners who sprint well but can't finish -- they end up slinking away crownless. The just person, who endures to the end of his whole life, finishes the race and wins the prize from both gods and men.
D: Summary: Using the runner analogy, Socrates shows that clever unjust people start strong but finish poorly, while the just person who endures to the end of life wins the full prize of human reputation and honor.
D: Distilled: The unjust sprint well but can't finish the race; the just person endures to the end and wins the crown.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The athletic metaphor (diaulos race -- there and back) was central to Greek culture -- "Ears draggling on shoulders" is a vivid image of defeated runners -- This counters the Book II claim that the unjust person would always enjoy a good reputation -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (360e-362c) where the unjust man was described as having a perfect reputation throughout life -- Cross-ref: 2 Timothy 4:7-8 ("I have finished the race") for a later echo of this metaphor

D: Original: True. And now you must allow me to repeat of the just the blessings which you were attributing to the fortunate unjust. I shall say of them, what you were saying of the others, that as they grow older, they become rulers in their own city if they care to be; they marry whom they like and give in marriage to whom they will; all that you said of the others I now say of these. And, on the other hand, of the unjust I say that the greater number, even though they escape in their youth, are found out at last and look foolish at the end of their course, and when they come to be old and miserable are flouted alike by stranger and citizen; they are beaten and then come those things unfit for ears polite, as you truly term them; they will be racked and have their eyes burned out, as you were saying. And you may suppose that I have repeated the remainder of your tale of horrors. But will you let me assume, without reciting them, that these things are true?
D: Narrative:
"True."

"And now you must allow me to repeat of the just the blessings which you were attributing to the fortunate unjust. I shall say of them, what you were saying of the others, that as they grow older, they become rulers in their own city if they care to be; they marry whom they like and give in marriage to whom they will; all that you said of the others I now say of these. And, on the other hand, of the unjust I say that the greater number, even though they escape in their youth, are found out at last and look foolish at the end of their course, and when they come to be old and miserable are flouted alike by stranger and citizen; they are beaten and then come those things unfit for ears polite, as you truly term them; they will be racked and have their eyes burned out, as you were saying. And you may suppose that I have repeated the remainder of your tale of horrors. But will you let me assume, without reciting them, that these things are true?"
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: Now let me attribute to the just all the blessings you once attributed to the fortunate unjust. As they grow older, the just become rulers in their city if they choose. They marry whom they like and give their children in marriage to whomever they wish. Everything you said about the unjust, I now say about the just.

And of the unjust, I say the reverse: most of them, even if they escape detection in their youth, are found out in the end and look foolish at the close of their lives. When they're old and miserable, they're scorned by strangers and citizens alike. They're beaten, and then come the things you aptly called "unfit for polite ears" -- they'll be racked and have their eyes burned out, as you described. You can assume I've repeated all the rest of your catalogue of horrors. Will you let me take it as established that these things are true?
D: Elegant: The blessings once attributed to the fortunate unjust now belong to the just: political power, favorable marriages, social standing. The unjust, mostly caught in the end, face old age in misery -- scorned, beaten, and subjected to the very tortures once reserved for the just in Glaucon's Book II scenario.
D: Summary: Socrates reverses the Book II scenario: the just person now enjoys all the blessings previously attributed to the successful unjust -- power, marriage, respect. The unjust, mostly found out eventually, face misery, scorn, and the very punishments Glaucon once described for the just.
D: Distilled: The rewards Glaucon once gave the unjust now belong to the just, and the punishments he assigned the just now await the unjust.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This directly reverses Glaucon's descriptions from Book II (361b-362a) -- "Things unfit for ears polite" echoes Glaucon's own language from his earlier speech -- The reversal completes the argument's symmetry: Book II stripped justice of rewards; Book X restores them -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (360e-362a) for Glaucon's original description of the unjust man's rewards and the just man's sufferings

D: Original:
Certainly, he said, what you say is true. These, then, are the prizes and rewards and gifts which are bestowed upon the just by gods and men in this present life, in addition to the other good things which justice of herself provides.

Yes, he said; and they are fair and lasting. And yet, I said, all these are as nothing, either in number or greatness in comparison with those other recompenses which await both just and unjust after death. And you ought to hear them, and then both just and unjust will have received from us a full payment of the debt which the argument owes to them.

Speak, he said; there are few things which I would more gladly hear.
D: Narrative:
"Certainly," he said, "what you say is true."

"These, then, are the prizes and rewards and gifts which are bestowed upon the just by gods and men in this present life, in addition to the other good things which justice of herself provides."

"Yes," he said; "and they are fair and lasting."

"And yet," I said, "all these are as nothing, either in number or greatness in comparison with those other recompenses which await both just and unjust after death. And you ought to hear them, and then both just and unjust will have received from us a full payment of the debt which the argument owes to them."

"Speak," he said; "there are few things which I would more gladly hear."
D: Refashioned:
Glaucon: What you say is true.

Socrates: These, then, are the prizes, rewards, and gifts that gods and men bestow on the just during this life, in addition to the good things justice provides by her own nature.

Glaucon: Yes, and they are fine and lasting.

Socrates: Yet all of these are nothing -- in number or in greatness -- compared to the rewards that await both the just and unjust after death. You need to hear about those, and then our argument will have fully paid its debt to both the just and the unjust.

Glaucon: Tell me -- there are few things I'd rather hear.
D: Elegant: The earthly rewards of justice from gods and men, fine as they are, are nothing compared to what awaits the just and unjust after death. This final account will complete the argument's full payment of its debt.
D: Summary: Socrates declares that the earthly rewards of justice, however fine, are nothing compared to the afterlife rewards. He prepares to tell the Myth of Er, which will show the full consequences awaiting both the just and unjust after death.
D: Distilled: All earthly rewards of justice are nothing compared to what awaits after death -- the argument's final and greatest debt remains to be paid.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the transition to the Myth of Er, the dialogue's closing myth -- "Full payment of the debt" frames the dialogue as an obligation to demonstrate justice's total worth -- Glaucon's eagerness sets up the dramatic conclusion -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 523a-527a for the eschatological myth in that dialogue -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 107d-114c for the myth of the afterlife in that dialogue -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 246a-249d for the cosmic myth of the soul

D: Original: Well, I said, I will tell you a tale; not one of the tales which Odysseus tells to the hero Alcinous, yet this too is a tale of a hero, Er the son of Armenius, a Pamphylian by birth. He was slain in battle, and ten days afterwards, when the bodies of the dead were taken up already in a state of corruption, his body was found unaffected by decay, and carried away home to be buried. And on the twelfth day, as he was lying on the funeral pile, he returned to life and told them what he had seen in the other world. He said that when his soul left the body he went on a journey with a great company, and that they came to a mysterious place at which there were two openings in the earth; they were near together, and over against them were two other openings in the heaven above. In the intermediate space there were judges seated, who commanded the just, after they had given judgment on them and had bound their sentences in front of them, to ascend by the heavenly way on the right hand; and in like manner the unjust were bidden by them to descend by the lower way on the left hand; these also bore the symbols of their deeds, but fastened on their backs. He drew near, and they told him that he was to be the messenger who would carry the report of the other world to men, and they bade him hear and see all that was to be heard and seen in that place. Then he beheld and saw on one side the souls departing at either opening of heaven and earth when sentence had been given on them; and at the two other openings other souls, some ascending out of the earth dusty and worn with travel, some descending out of heaven clean and bright. And arriving ever and anon they seemed to have come from a long journey, and they went forth with gladness into the meadow, where they encamped as at a festival; and those who knew one another embraced and conversed, the souls which came from earth curiously enquiring about the things above, and the souls which came from heaven about the things beneath.
D: Narrative:
"Well," I said, "I will tell you a tale; not one of the tales which Odysseus tells to the hero Alcinous, yet this too is a tale of a hero, Er the son of Armenius, a Pamphylian by birth. He was slain in battle, and ten days afterwards, when the bodies of the dead were taken up already in a state of corruption, his body was found unaffected by decay, and carried away home to be buried. And on the twelfth day, as he was lying on the funeral pile, he returned to life and told them what he had seen in the other world.

He said that when his soul left the body he went on a journey with a great company, and that they came to a mysterious place at which there were two openings in the earth; they were near together, and over against them were two other openings in the heaven above. In the intermediate space there were judges seated, who commanded the just, after they had given judgment on them and had bound their sentences in front of them, to ascend by the heavenly way on the right hand; and in like manner the unjust were bidden by them to descend by the lower way on the left hand; these also bore the symbols of their deeds, but fastened on their backs.

He drew near, and they told him that he was to be the messenger who would carry the report of the other world to men, and they bade him hear and see all that was to be heard and seen in that place. Then he beheld and saw on one side the souls departing at either opening of heaven and earth when sentence had been given on them; and at the two other openings other souls, some ascending out of the earth dusty and worn with travel, some descending out of heaven clean and bright. And arriving ever and anon they seemed to have come from a long journey, and they went forth with gladness into the meadow, where they encamped as at a festival; and those who knew one another embraced and conversed, the souls which came from earth curiously enquiring about the things above, and the souls which came from heaven about the things beneath."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: I will tell you a tale -- not one of those tales Odysseus told to King Alcinous, but a tale of a hero nonetheless. Er, son of Armenius, was a Pamphylian who was killed in battle. Ten days later, when the other bodies were already decomposing, his was found perfectly preserved. He was carried home for burial, and on the twelfth day, lying on the funeral pyre, he came back to life and described what he had seen in the world beyond.

He said that after his soul left his body, he traveled with a great company of souls to a mysterious place. There he found two openings in the earth close together, and directly above them two openings in the sky. Between them sat judges who examined each soul and pronounced sentence: the just were directed to ascend through the right-hand heavenly opening, wearing their judgments displayed on their chests; the unjust were sent downward through the left-hand earthly opening, with their judgments fastened to their backs.

When Er approached, the judges told him he was to be the messenger who would report on the other world to the living. They instructed him to observe everything carefully. He watched as judged souls departed through the openings of heaven and earth, while from the other two openings souls arrived -- some rising from the earth, dusty and travel-worn, others descending from heaven, clean and radiant. They seemed to have come from long journeys, and they gathered with joy in the meadow between, camping as if at a festival. Those who recognized each other embraced and talked, the souls from below asking eagerly about heaven, and those from above about the underworld.
D: Elegant: Socrates tells the Myth of Er: a Pamphylian soldier killed in battle whose body didn't decay and who revived on the funeral pyre to report what he'd seen. His soul traveled to a place of judgment with four openings -- two in earth, two in heaven. Judges sent the just upward to the right and the unjust downward to the left. Er was appointed messenger. He watched souls departing after judgment and arriving from completed journeys -- dusty pilgrims from below, radiant travelers from above -- gathering joyfully in a meadow to share stories.
D: Summary: Socrates begins the Myth of Er: a soldier who died in battle, remained uncorrupted, and revived to tell of the afterlife. His soul traveled to a place of judgment with four openings, where the just ascended to heaven and the unjust descended underground, while returning souls gathered in a meadow to share their experiences.
D: Distilled: Er, a soldier who returned from death, describes a cosmic judgment place where the just ascend to heaven and the unjust descend to the underworld.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- Er (Heros) the son of Armenius, a Pamphylian: possibly symbolic ("Er" may relate to "Eros"; Pamphylia means "land of all tribes") -- Alcinous: king of the Phaeacians in the Odyssey, to whom Odysseus tells his tale of the underworld (Odyssey Books 9-12, especially the Nekyia in Book 11) -- The four-opening cosmology (two earth, two heaven) is unique to this myth -- The meadow gathering echoes Odyssey 11 (the Nekyia) -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 523a-527a for a different Platonic afterlife myth -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 107d-114c for the Phaedo's underworld geography -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 248c-249b on the cycle of reincarnation

D: Original: And they told one another of what had happened by the way, those from below weeping and sorrowing at the remembrance of the things which they had endured and seen in their journey beneath the earth (now the journey lasted a thousand years), while those from above were describing heavenly delights and visions of inconceivable beauty. The Story, Glaucon, would take too long to tell; but the sum was this: --He said that for every wrong which they had done to any one they suffered tenfold; or once in a hundred years --such being reckoned to be the length of man's life, and the penalty being thus paid ten times in a thousand years. If, for example, there were any who had been the cause of many deaths, or had betrayed or enslaved cities or armies, or been guilty of any other evil behaviour, for each and all of their offences they received punishment ten times over, and the rewards of beneficence and justice and holiness were in the same proportion. I need hardly repeat what he said concerning young children dying almost as soon as they were born. Of piety and impiety to gods and parents, and of murderers, there were retributions other and greater far which he described. He mentioned that he was present when one of the spirits asked another, 'Where is Ardiaeus the Great?' (Now this Ardiaeus lived a thousand years before the time of Er: he had been the tyrant of some city of Pamphylia, and had murdered his aged father and his elder brother, and was said to have committed many other abominable crimes.) The answer of the other spirit was: 'He comes not hither and will never come. And this,' said he, 'was one of the dreadful sights which we ourselves witnessed. We were at the mouth of the cavern, and, having completed all our experiences, were about to reascend, when of a sudden Ardiaeus appeared and several others, most of whom were tyrants; and there were also besides the tyrants private individuals who had been great criminals: they were just, as they fancied, about to return into the upper world, but the mouth, instead of admitting them, gave a roar, whenever any of these incurable sinners or some one who had not been sufficiently punished tried to ascend; and then wild men of fiery aspect, who were standing by and heard the sound, seized and carried them off; and Ardiaeus and others they bound head and foot and hand, and threw them down and flayed them with scourges, and dragged them along the road at the side, carding them on thorns like wool, and declaring to the passers-by what were their crimes, and that they were being taken away to be cast into hell.' And of all the many terrors which they had endured, he said that there was none like the terror which each of them felt at that moment, lest they should hear the voice; and when there was silence, one by one they ascended with exceeding joy. These, said Er, were the penalties and retributions, and there were blessings as great.
D: Narrative:
"And they told one another of what had happened by the way, those from below weeping and sorrowing at the remembrance of the things which they had endured and seen in their journey beneath the earth (now the journey lasted a thousand years), while those from above were describing heavenly delights and visions of inconceivable beauty.

The Story, Glaucon, would take too long to tell; but the sum was this: -- He said that for every wrong which they had done to any one they suffered tenfold; or once in a hundred years -- such being reckoned to be the length of man's life, and the penalty being thus paid ten times in a thousand years. If, for example, there were any who had been the cause of many deaths, or had betrayed or enslaved cities or armies, or been guilty of any other evil behaviour, for each and all of their offences they received punishment ten times over, and the rewards of beneficence and justice and holiness were in the same proportion.

I need hardly repeat what he said concerning young children dying almost as soon as they were born. Of piety and impiety to gods and parents, and of murderers, there were retributions other and greater far which he described.

He mentioned that he was present when one of the spirits asked another, 'Where is Ardiaeus the Great?' (Now this Ardiaeus lived a thousand years before the time of Er: he had been the tyrant of some city of Pamphylia, and had murdered his aged father and his elder brother, and was said to have committed many other abominable crimes.)

The answer of the other spirit was: 'He comes not hither and will never come. And this,' said he, 'was one of the dreadful sights which we ourselves witnessed. We were at the mouth of the cavern, and, having completed all our experiences, were about to reascend, when of a sudden Ardiaeus appeared and several others, most of whom were tyrants; and there were also besides the tyrants private individuals who had been great criminals: they were just, as they fancied, about to return into the upper world, but the mouth, instead of admitting them, gave a roar, whenever any of these incurable sinners or some one who had not been sufficiently punished tried to ascend; and then wild men of fiery aspect, who were standing by and heard the sound, seized and carried them off; and Ardiaeus and others they bound head and foot and hand, and threw them down and flayed them with scourges, and dragged them along the road at the side, carding them on thorns like wool, and declaring to the passers-by what were their crimes, and that they were being taken away to be cast into hell.'

And of all the many terrors which they had endured, he said that there was none like the terror which each of them felt at that moment, lest they should hear the voice; and when there was silence, one by one they ascended with exceeding joy. These, said Er, were the penalties and retributions, and there were blessings as great."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: The souls shared their stories. Those from below wept and grieved, remembering what they had endured during their thousand-year journey underground. Those from above described heavenly delights and visions of inconceivable beauty.

The full story, Glaucon, would take too long, but here's the essence: for every wrong committed, the punishment was tenfold. A human life was reckoned at a hundred years, so the penalty was paid ten times over across a thousand years. Those who caused many deaths, betrayed cities, enslaved armies, or committed other grave evils received punishment tenfold for each offense. The rewards for beneficence, justice, and holiness were proportional.

Regarding infants who died at birth, and regarding crimes of impiety toward gods and parents and murder, there were punishments far greater still.

Er reported being present when one spirit asked another: "Where is Ardiaeus the Great?" Ardiaeus had been a tyrant in Pamphylia a thousand years earlier -- he had murdered his elderly father and elder brother and committed many other horrible crimes.

The answer came: "He does not come here and never will." The spirit continued: "This was one of the most terrifying sights we witnessed. We were at the mouth of the cavern, ready to reascend, when suddenly Ardiaeus appeared along with several others -- mostly tyrants, plus some private citizens who had been great criminals. They believed they were about to return to the upper world. But the mouth of the cavern gave a great roar whenever any incurable sinner or insufficiently punished soul tried to ascend. Then wild, fiery-looking men who stood by, hearing the roar, seized them and carried them off. They bound Ardiaeus and the others hand and foot, threw them down, flayed them with whips, and dragged them along the roadside, tearing them on thorns like wool being carded, announcing their crimes to everyone passing by: they were being taken to be cast into Tartarus."

Er said that of all the terrors endured in that place, nothing compared to the dread each soul felt at that moment -- the fear of hearing the roar. When silence came, each ascended with overwhelming joy. Such were the punishments and retributions; the blessings were equally great.
D: Elegant: Souls from below wept remembering their thousand-year punishments; souls from above described heavenly beauty. Every wrong was repaid tenfold across a thousand years, and rewards for virtue were proportional. The tyrant Ardiaeus, who murdered his father and brother, was denied ascent permanently -- when he and other incurable sinners tried to leave, the cavern roared and fiery men seized them, bound them, flayed them on thorns, and dragged them toward Tartarus. Of all terrors, the worst was the dread of hearing that roar. When silence came, the eligible souls ascended with overwhelming joy.
D: Summary: Er describes the afterlife's system of justice: every wrong is repaid tenfold over a thousand years, with proportional rewards for virtue. The tyrant Ardiaeus, a patricide, is permanently denied return. Incurable sinners who attempt to ascend are seized by fiery beings, tortured, and cast into Tartarus -- a terror surpassing all others.
D: Distilled: Every wrong is repaid tenfold over a thousand years, and incurable sinners like the tyrant Ardiaeus are permanently cast into Tartarus.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- Ardiaeus the Great: a Pamphylian tyrant, patricide and fratricide -- his permanent damnation represents the worst category of sinner -- Tartarus: the deepest region of the underworld, reserved for the worst offenders -- The tenfold punishment over 1000 years establishes proportional cosmic justice -- "Carding them on thorns like wool" is a memorably horrific image -- The roar of the cavern as a mechanism of judgment is unique to this myth -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 525c-526d on incurable sinners permanently punished in Tartarus -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 113d-114b on Tartarus and different levels of post-mortem punishment -- Cross-ref: Laws 904d-905b on proportional divine justice

D: Original: Now when the spirits which were in the meadow had tarried seven days, on the eighth they were obliged to proceed on their journey, and, on the fourth day after, he said that they came to a place where they could see from above a line of light, straight as a column, extending right through the whole heaven and through the earth, in colour resembling the rainbow, only brighter and purer; another day's journey brought them to the place, and there, in the midst of the light, they saw the ends of the chains of heaven let down from above: for this light is the belt of heaven, and holds together the circle of the universe, like the under-girders of a trireme. From these ends is extended the spindle of Necessity, on which all the revolutions turn. The shaft and hook of this spindle are made of steel, and the whorl is made partly of steel and also partly of other materials.
D: Narrative: "Now when the spirits which were in the meadow had tarried seven days, on the eighth they were obliged to proceed on their journey, and, on the fourth day after, he said that they came to a place where they could see from above a line of light, straight as a column, extending right through the whole heaven and through the earth, in colour resembling the rainbow, only brighter and purer; another day's journey brought them to the place, and there, in the midst of the light, they saw the ends of the chains of heaven let down from above: for this light is the belt of heaven, and holds together the circle of the universe, like the under-girders of a trireme. From these ends is extended the spindle of Necessity, on which all the revolutions turn. The shaft and hook of this spindle are made of steel, and the whorl is made partly of steel and also partly of other materials."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: After the spirits had rested in the meadow for seven days, on the eighth they were required to continue their journey. Four days later, they came to a vantage point where they could see a column of light stretching straight through all of heaven and earth -- like a rainbow but far brighter and purer. After another day's travel they reached this place and saw, in the midst of the light, the ends of heaven's chains suspended from above. This light is the belt of heaven -- it holds the universe together like the undergirding ropes of a warship.

From these chain-ends extends the Spindle of Necessity, on which all cosmic revolutions turn. The shaft and hook of this spindle are made of adamant, and the whorl is made partly of adamant and partly of other materials.
D: Elegant: After seven days in the meadow, the spirits journeyed for four more days to a place where a column of brilliant light, brighter than a rainbow, extended through all heaven and earth. This light is the belt holding the universe together, like a trireme's undergirding. From it extends the Spindle of Necessity, on which all cosmic revolutions turn.
D: Summary: The spirits journey to the cosmic axis: a column of light stretching through heaven and earth, brighter than a rainbow, which holds the universe together. From it extends the Spindle of Necessity, the cosmic mechanism on which all celestial revolutions turn.
D: Distilled: At the cosmic center, a pillar of light holds the universe together, and from it extends the Spindle of Necessity governing all celestial motion.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- The Spindle of Necessity (Ananke) is a cosmological model of the universe -- The light column may represent the Milky Way or the cosmic axis -- Trireme undergirding (hypozomata): ropes binding the hull of a Greek warship -- The cosmology presented here is geocentric, with nested spheres -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 34b-40d for Plato's more detailed cosmological account -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 108c-113c for a different cosmological picture -- Cross-ref: Laws 898d-899b on the cosmic soul governing celestial motion

D: Original: Now the whorl is in form like the whorl used on earth; and the description of it implied that there is one large hollow whorl which is quite scooped out, and into this is fitted another lesser one, and another, and another, and four others, making eight in all, like vessels which fit into one another; the whorls show their edges on the upper side, and on their lower side all together form one continuous whorl. This is pierced by the spindle, which is driven home through the centre of the eighth. The first and outermost whorl has the rim broadest, and the seven inner whorls are narrower, in the following proportions --the sixth is next to the first in size, the fourth next to the sixth; then comes the eighth; the seventh is fifth, the fifth is sixth, the third is seventh, last and eighth comes the second. The largest (of fixed stars) is spangled, and the seventh (or sun) is brightest; the eighth (or moon) coloured by the reflected light of the seventh; the second and fifth (Saturn and Mercury) are in colour like one another, and yellower than the preceding; the third (Venus) has the whitest light; the fourth (Mars) is reddish; the sixth (Jupiter) is in whiteness second.
D: Narrative: "Now the whorl is in form like the whorl used on earth; and the description of it implied that there is one large hollow whorl which is quite scooped out, and into this is fitted another lesser one, and another, and another, and four others, making eight in all, like vessels which fit into one another; the whorls show their edges on the upper side, and on their lower side all together form one continuous whorl. This is pierced by the spindle, which is driven home through the centre of the eighth. The first and outermost whorl has the rim broadest, and the seven inner whorls are narrower, in the following proportions -- the sixth is next to the first in size, the fourth next to the sixth; then comes the eighth; the seventh is fifth, the fifth is sixth, the third is seventh, last and eighth comes the second. The largest (of fixed stars) is spangled, and the seventh (or sun) is brightest; the eighth (or moon) coloured by the reflected light of the seventh; the second and fifth (Saturn and Mercury) are in colour like one another, and yellower than the preceding; the third (Venus) has the whitest light; the fourth (Mars) is reddish; the sixth (Jupiter) is in whiteness second."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: The whorl resembles an earthly spindle whorl in shape. Imagine one large hollow whorl, completely hollowed out, with a smaller one fitted inside it, then another inside that, and so on -- eight in all, nested like bowls stacked within each other. Their rims show at the top, while below they form a single continuous surface. The spindle passes through the center of all eight.

The outermost whorl has the broadest rim. The inner seven vary in width: the sixth is next largest after the first, then the fourth, then the eighth, then the seventh, then the fifth, then the third, and the second is narrowest. The outermost (the sphere of fixed stars) is spangled with stars. The seventh (the Sun) is the brightest. The eighth (the Moon) gets its light from the seventh. The second (Saturn) and fifth (Mercury) are similar in color -- yellower than the others. The third (Venus) has the whitest light. The fourth (Mars) is reddish. The sixth (Jupiter) is second in whiteness.
D: Elegant: Eight nested whorls, like bowls within bowls, form the cosmic spindle. The outermost sphere of fixed stars is spangled; the Sun is brightest; the Moon reflects the Sun's light; Saturn and Mercury are yellow; Venus is whitest; Mars is reddish; Jupiter is second in whiteness. Each planet's position and color correspond to its whorl.
D: Summary: Socrates describes the cosmic structure as eight nested whorls spinning on the Spindle of Necessity, each representing a celestial sphere. He details their relative sizes and colors: the fixed stars are spangled, the Sun brightest, the Moon reflective, and each planet has its characteristic color.
D: Distilled: The universe consists of eight nested celestial spheres spinning on the Spindle of Necessity, each with its own characteristic size and color.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- The eight whorls represent: (1) fixed stars, (2) Saturn, (3) Venus, (4) Mars, (5) Mercury, (6) Jupiter, (7) Sun, (8) Moon -- This is a geocentric model with nested spheres -- The descriptions of planetary colors match ancient astronomical observation -- The Moon's reflected light from the Sun is scientifically correct -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 38c-39e for a more detailed account of planetary arrangement -- Cross-ref: Laws 821b-822d on the true motions of celestial bodies -- The mathematical proportions of rim widths may reflect Pythagorean cosmology

D: Original: Now the whole spindle has the same motion; but, as the whole revolves in one direction, the seven inner circles move slowly in the other, and of these the swiftest is the eighth; next in swiftness are the seventh, sixth, and fifth, which move together; third in swiftness appeared to move according to the law of this reversed motion the fourth; the third appeared fourth and the second fifth. The spindle turns on the knees of Necessity; and on the upper surface of each circle is a siren, who goes round with them, hymning a single tone or note. The eight together form one harmony; and round about, at equal intervals, there is another band, three in number, each sitting upon her throne: these are the Fates, daughters of Necessity, who are clothed in white robes and have chaplets upon their heads, Lachesis and Clotho and Atropos, who accompany with their voices the harmony of the sirens --Lachesis singing of the past, Clotho of the present, Atropos of the future; Clotho from time to time assisting with a touch of her right hand the revolution of the outer circle of the whorl or spindle, and Atropos with her left hand touching and guiding the inner ones, and Lachesis laying hold of either in turn, first with one hand and then with the other.
D: Narrative:
"Now the whole spindle has the same motion; but, as the whole revolves in one direction, the seven inner circles move slowly in the other, and of these the swiftest is the eighth; next in swiftness are the seventh, sixth, and fifth, which move together; third in swiftness appeared to move according to the law of this reversed motion the fourth; the third appeared fourth and the second fifth.

The spindle turns on the knees of Necessity; and on the upper surface of each circle is a siren, who goes round with them, hymning a single tone or note. The eight together form one harmony; and round about, at equal intervals, there is another band, three in number, each sitting upon her throne: these are the Fates, daughters of Necessity, who are clothed in white robes and have chaplets upon their heads, Lachesis and Clotho and Atropos, who accompany with their voices the harmony of the sirens -- Lachesis singing of the past, Clotho of the present, Atropos of the future; Clotho from time to time assisting with a touch of her right hand the revolution of the outer circle of the whorl or spindle, and Atropos with her left hand touching and guiding the inner ones, and Lachesis laying hold of either in turn, first with one hand and then with the other."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: The whole spindle revolves together, but within that single motion the seven inner spheres rotate slowly in the opposite direction. Among these, the fastest is the eighth (the Moon); next in speed are the seventh (Sun), sixth (Jupiter), and fifth (Mercury), which move together; the fourth (Mars) appeared to move third-fastest in this reversed rotation; the third (Venus) appeared fourth in speed, and the second (Saturn) fifth.

The spindle turns on the knees of Necessity herself. On the upper surface of each sphere sits a Siren, revolving with it, each singing a single continuous note. The eight notes together create one harmony.

Around them, seated at equal intervals on thrones, are three figures: the Fates, daughters of Necessity, robed in white with garlands on their heads -- Lachesis, Clotho, and Atropos. Their voices accompany the Sirens' harmony: Lachesis sings of the past, Clotho of the present, Atropos of the future. Clotho periodically assists the outermost sphere's revolution with a touch of her right hand; Atropos guides the inner spheres with her left; and Lachesis touches both in turn, alternating hands.
D: Elegant: The spindle revolves as one, while the inner spheres counter-rotate at different speeds. Eight Sirens, one per sphere, each sing a single note that together form one cosmic harmony. Three Fates, daughters of Necessity, preside: Lachesis (the past), Clotho (the present), and Atropos (the future) -- their voices accompanying the music as their hands guide the revolution of the spheres.
D: Summary: The cosmic spindle's spheres counter-rotate at different speeds. Eight Sirens produce one harmony, each singing a single note. The three Fates -- Lachesis (past), Clotho (present), and Atropos (future) -- daughters of Necessity, sing along and guide the revolution of the spheres with their hands.
D: Distilled: Eight Sirens create cosmic harmony while the three Fates -- past, present, future -- guide the universe's revolutions on the Spindle of Necessity.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- The Sirens here are benevolent cosmic musicians, not the dangerous Homeric Sirens (Odyssey XII) -- The "Harmony of the Spheres" (music of the spheres) influenced Pythagorean and later Western cosmological thought -- The three Fates (Moirai): Lachesis ("allotter"), Clotho ("spinner"), Atropos ("unturnable/inevitable") -- Necessity (Ananke) is the supreme cosmic principle in this myth -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 35b-36d on the World Soul's composition and harmony -- Cross-ref: Cratylus 403b on the Fates -- Cross-ref: Laws 960c on cosmic order -- Pythagorean influence on the harmony of the spheres concept

D: Original: When Er and the spirits arrived, their duty was to go at once to Lachesis; but first of all there came a prophet who arranged them in order; then he took from the knees of Lachesis lots and samples of lives, and having mounted a high pulpit, spoke as follows: 'Hear the word of Lachesis, the daughter of Necessity. Mortal souls, behold a new cycle of life and mortality. Your genius will not be allotted to you, but you choose your genius; and let him who draws the first lot have the first choice, and the life which he chooses shall be his destiny. Virtue is free, and as a man honours or dishonours her he will have more or less of her; the responsibility is with the chooser --God is justified.' When the Interpreter had thus spoken he scattered lots indifferently among them all, and each of them took up the lot which fell near him, all but Er himself (he was not allowed), and each as he took his lot perceived the number which he had obtained. Then the Interpreter placed on the ground before them the samples of lives; and there were many more lives than the souls present, and they were of all sorts. There were lives of every animal and of man in every condition. And there were tyrannies among them, some lasting out the tyrant's life, others which broke off in the middle and came to an end in poverty and exile and beggary; and there were lives of famous men, some who were famous for their form and beauty as well as for their strength and success in games, or, again, for their birth and the qualities of their ancestors; and some who were the reverse of famous for the opposite qualities. And of women likewise; there was not, however, any definite character them, because the soul, when choosing a new life, must of necessity become different.
D: Narrative:
"When Er and the spirits arrived, their duty was to go at once to Lachesis; but first of all there came a prophet who arranged them in order; then he took from the knees of Lachesis lots and samples of lives, and having mounted a high pulpit, spoke as follows:

'Hear the word of Lachesis, the daughter of Necessity. Mortal souls, behold a new cycle of life and mortality. Your genius will not be allotted to you, but you choose your genius; and let him who draws the first lot have the first choice, and the life which he chooses shall be his destiny. Virtue is free, and as a man honours or dishonours her he will have more or less of her; the responsibility is with the chooser -- God is justified.'

When the Interpreter had thus spoken he scattered lots indifferently among them all, and each of them took up the lot which fell near him, all but Er himself (he was not allowed), and each as he took his lot perceived the number which he had obtained.

Then the Interpreter placed on the ground before them the samples of lives; and there were many more lives than the souls present, and they were of all sorts. There were lives of every animal and of man in every condition. And there were tyrannies among them, some lasting out the tyrant's life, others which broke off in the middle and came to an end in poverty and exile and beggary; and there were lives of famous men, some who were famous for their form and beauty as well as for their strength and success in games, or, again, for their birth and the qualities of their ancestors; and some who were the reverse of famous for the opposite qualities. And of women likewise; there was not, however, any definite character in them, because the soul, when choosing a new life, must of necessity become different."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: When Er and the spirits arrived, they were directed to go immediately to Lachesis. First, a prophet arranged them in order, then took lots and samples of lives from Lachesis's knees. Mounting a high platform, he proclaimed:

"Hear the word of Lachesis, daughter of Necessity! Mortal souls, a new cycle of life and death begins. Your guiding spirit will not be assigned to you -- you will choose your spirit. Whoever draws the first lot chooses first, and the life chosen becomes destiny. Virtue has no master; each person will have more or less of her according to how they honor or dishonor her. The responsibility lies with the chooser. God is not to blame."

The prophet then scattered lots among them randomly. Each soul picked up the lot that fell nearby -- all except Er, who was forbidden to participate. Each soul saw the number on their lot, determining their order of choice.

The prophet laid out samples of lives on the ground before them. There were far more lives than souls, and every imaginable kind was represented: lives of every animal and every human condition. There were tyrannies -- some lasting a full lifetime, others cut short by poverty, exile, and beggary. There were lives of famous men, celebrated for beauty, athletic prowess, noble birth, or ancestral distinction -- and lives of obscurity marked by the opposite qualities. The same variety existed for women's lives. But no life came with a fixed character, because the soul, in choosing a new life, necessarily becomes different.
D: Elegant: A prophet proclaims Lachesis's decree: souls will choose their own guiding spirits and lives. "Virtue has no master -- the responsibility is with the chooser. God is justified." Lots determine the order of choice, not the outcome. Lives of every kind are laid before them -- tyrannies, fame, obscurity, every animal and human condition -- far more lives than souls. No life has a fixed character, because the choosing soul must change.
D: Summary: The prophet of Lachesis announces the central doctrine: souls choose their own lives and guiding spirits. Virtue is free and responsibility belongs to the chooser, not God. Lots determine only the order of choice, and lives of every conceivable kind are available -- but none comes with a fixed character.
D: Distilled: "Virtue is free; the responsibility is with the chooser -- God is justified": souls freely choose their own lives and destinies.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating; the Prophet of Lachesis speaking -- "God is justified" (theos anaitios) is one of Plato's most important theological claims -- the problem of evil is solved by free choice -- "Your genius will not be allotted to you, but you choose your genius" (daimon): each soul selects its own daimon/guardian spirit -- The lots determine only order of choice, not destiny itself -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (379a-380c) on God as not the cause of evil -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 41d-42e on the Demiurge's assignment of souls -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 248c-249b on the cycle of reincarnation and choice -- Cross-ref: Laws 904b-905d on divine justice and personal responsibility

D: Original: But there was every other quality, and the all mingled with one another, and also with elements of wealth and poverty, and disease and health; and there were mean states also. And here, my dear Glaucon, is the supreme peril of our human state; and therefore the utmost care should be taken. Let each one of us leave every other kind of knowledge and seek and follow one thing only, if peradventure he may be able to learn and may find some one who will make him able to learn and discern between good and evil, and so to choose always and everywhere the better life as he has opportunity. He should consider the bearing of all these things which have been mentioned severally and collectively upon virtue; he should know what the effect of beauty is when combined with poverty or wealth in a particular soul, and what are the good and evil consequences of noble and humble birth, of private and public station, of strength and weakness, of cleverness and dullness, and of all the soul, and the operation of them when conjoined; he will then look at the nature of the soul, and from the consideration of all these qualities he will be able to determine which is the better and which is the worse; and so he will choose, giving the name of evil to the life which will make his soul more unjust, and good to the life which will make his soul more just; all else he will disregard.
D: Narrative:
"But there was every other quality, and they all mingled with one another, and also with elements of wealth and poverty, and disease and health; and there were mean states also. And here, my dear Glaucon, is the supreme peril of our human state; and therefore the utmost care should be taken. Let each one of us leave every other kind of knowledge and seek and follow one thing only, if peradventure he may be able to learn and may find some one who will make him able to learn and discern between good and evil, and so to choose always and everywhere the better life as he has opportunity.

He should consider the bearing of all these things which have been mentioned severally and collectively upon virtue; he should know what the effect of beauty is when combined with poverty or wealth in a particular soul, and what are the good and evil consequences of noble and humble birth, of private and public station, of strength and weakness, of cleverness and dullness, and of all the soul, and the operation of them when conjoined; he will then look at the nature of the soul, and from the consideration of all these qualities he will be able to determine which is the better and which is the worse; and so he will choose, giving the name of evil to the life which will make his soul more unjust, and good to the life which will make his soul more just; all else he will disregard."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: Every other quality was mixed in -- wealth and poverty, disease and health, mediocrity and distinction, all blended together.

And here, dear Glaucon, lies the supreme danger of our human condition. This is why the greatest possible care is needed. Let each of us set aside every other kind of knowledge and pursue one thing above all: the ability to discern good from evil, so that we can always choose the better life whenever the opportunity arises.

We should understand how all these qualities -- considered separately and in combination -- affect virtue. What happens when beauty combines with poverty or wealth in a particular soul? What are the consequences of noble versus humble birth, of public versus private life, of strength versus weakness, of cleverness versus dullness? We need to understand how all these qualities interact within the soul. With this understanding, we can determine which life is better and which worse. We should call evil any life that makes the soul more unjust, and good any life that makes the soul more just. Everything else should be disregarded.
D: Elegant: Here lies the supreme peril of human existence: the choice of lives. We must pursue above all else the knowledge that distinguishes good from evil, understanding how every quality -- beauty, wealth, birth, strength, cleverness -- affects the soul's justice. A life that makes the soul more just is good; one that makes it more unjust is evil. All else is irrelevant.
D: Summary: Socrates identifies the supreme human danger: choosing poorly when lives are assigned. The one essential knowledge is discerning good from evil -- understanding how all life qualities affect the soul's justice. A good life makes the soul more just; an evil life makes it more unjust. Everything else is secondary.
D: Distilled: The supreme human task is learning to discern which life will make the soul more just -- all other knowledge is secondary.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates (addressing Glaucon directly) -- This passage breaks from mythic narration to direct philosophical exhortation -- "The supreme peril of our human state" connects the myth to practical philosophy -- The emphasis on choosing the life that makes the soul more just brings the entire Republic full circle -- Cross-ref: Republic Book I (352d) on justice as the soul's virtue -- Cross-ref: Republic Book VII (518b-519b) on turning the soul toward the Good -- Cross-ref: Apology 38a ("the unexamined life is not worth living") -- Cross-ref: Meno 87c-89a on virtue as the one essential good

D: Original:
For we have seen and know that this is the best choice both in life and after death. A man must take with him into the world below an adamantine faith in truth and right, that there too he may be undazzled by the desire of wealth or the other allurements of evil, lest, coming upon tyrannies and similar villainies, he do irremediable wrongs to others and suffer yet worse himself; but let him know how to choose the mean and avoid the extremes on either side, as far as possible, not only in this life but in all that which is to come.

For this is the way of happiness.
D: Narrative:
"For we have seen and know that this is the best choice both in life and after death. A man must take with him into the world below an adamantine faith in truth and right, that there too he may be undazzled by the desire of wealth or the other allurements of evil, lest, coming upon tyrannies and similar villainies, he do irremediable wrongs to others and suffer yet worse himself; but let him know how to choose the mean and avoid the extremes on either side, as far as possible, not only in this life but in all that which is to come.

For this is the way of happiness."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: We have seen and know that this is the best choice, both in life and after death. A person must carry into the underworld an unbreakable faith in truth and justice, so that even there he won't be dazzled by the lure of wealth or other enticements of evil. Otherwise, he might choose a tyranny or similar villainy, inflicting irreparable harm on others and suffering worse himself.

The key is knowing how to choose the mean and avoid extremes on either side -- not only in this life but in every life to come.

For this is the way of happiness.
D: Elegant: One must carry into the afterlife an adamantine faith in truth and right, to resist the allure of tyranny, wealth, and other evils when choosing one's next life. Know how to choose the mean and avoid extremes, in this life and all that follow. This is the way of happiness.
D: Summary: Socrates urges carrying an unshakeable commitment to truth and justice even into the afterlife, so that when choosing a new life, one resists the allure of power and wealth. The path to happiness lies in choosing the mean and avoiding extremes across all lifetimes.
D: Distilled: Carry an adamantine faith in justice into every life, choose the mean over extremes, and resist the allure of power -- this is the way of happiness.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates -- "Adamantine faith" (adamantinos) -- unbreakable, like the hardest metal -- "Choose the mean and avoid extremes" connects to Aristotle's later Doctrine of the Mean (Nicomachean Ethics II.6) -- "For this is the way of happiness" is the moral lesson of the entire Myth of Er -- The warning against choosing tyranny directly connects to the soul who chose first (10.60) -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IX (571a-580c) on the tyrannical soul as the most miserable -- Cross-ref: Laws 716a on God as the measure and the mean -- Cross-ref: Philebus 64d-66a on the measured life as the best

D: Original: And according to the report of the messenger from the other world this was what the prophet said at the time: 'Even for the last comer, if he chooses wisely and will live diligently, there is appointed a happy and not undesirable existence. Let not him who chooses first be careless, and let not the last despair.' And when he had spoken, he who had the first choice came forward and in a moment chose the greatest tyranny; his mind having been darkened by folly and sensuality, he had not thought out the whole matter before he chose, and did not at first sight perceive that he was fated, among other evils, to devour his own children.
D: Narrative:
"And according to the report of the messenger from the other world this was what the prophet said at the time: 'Even for the last comer, if he chooses wisely and will live diligently, there is appointed a happy and not undesirable existence. Let not him who chooses first be careless, and let not the last despair.'

And when he had spoken, he who had the first choice came forward and in a moment chose the greatest tyranny; his mind having been darkened by folly and sensuality, he had not thought out the whole matter before he chose, and did not at first sight perceive that he was fated, among other evils, to devour his own children."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: According to the messenger, the prophet also declared: "Even the last to choose, if he chooses wisely and lives diligently, can have a happy and desirable existence. Let the first to choose not be careless, and let the last not despair."

When he finished speaking, the soul who drew the first lot stepped forward and immediately chose the greatest tyranny. His mind had been darkened by folly and self-indulgence, and he hadn't thought the matter through before choosing. He didn't realize at first that this life included, among other horrors, the fate of devouring his own children.
D: Elegant: The prophet declares that even the last to choose can be happy if he chooses wisely, and the first must not be careless. Yet the soul with the first choice immediately seizes the greatest tyranny, blinded by folly and sensuality, not realizing he has chosen a fate that includes devouring his own children.
D: Summary: The prophet warns against carelessness in choosing, yet the first soul immediately seizes the greatest tyranny, blinded by folly. He fails to see that his chosen life includes devouring his own children -- a devastating illustration of unwise choice.
D: Distilled: Even with the first choice, folly leads to ruin: the soul seizes tyranny without realizing he has chosen to devour his own children.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating; the Prophet speaking -- "Even for the last comer" -- the lot determines order, not fate; wisdom matters more than position -- "Devour his own children" likely alludes to mythic tyrants like Thyestes or Kronos -- The first chooser's folly exemplifies exactly the danger Socrates warned against -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IX (571c-576b) on the tyrannical soul consumed by appetites -- Cross-ref: Republic Book VIII (565d-566a) on the tyrant's transformation -- Cross-ref: Herodotus I.108-130 on Astyages and the myth of kings consuming their children's flesh

D: Original: But when he had time to reflect, and saw what was in the lot, he began to beat his breast and lament over his choice, forgetting the proclamation of the prophet; for, instead of throwing the blame of his misfortune on himself, he accused chance and the gods, and everything rather than himself. Now he was one of those who came from heaven, and in a former life had dwelt in a well-ordered State, but his virtue was a matter of habit only, and he had no philosophy. And it was true of others who were similarly overtaken, that the greater number of them came from heaven and therefore they had never been schooled by trial, whereas the pilgrims who came from earth, having themselves suffered and seen others suffer, were not in a hurry to choose. And owing to this inexperience of theirs, and also because the lot was a chance, many of the souls exchanged a good destiny for an evil or an evil for a good.
D: Narrative:
"But when he had time to reflect, and saw what was in the lot, he began to beat his breast and lament over his choice, forgetting the proclamation of the prophet; for, instead of throwing the blame of his misfortune on himself, he accused chance and the gods, and everything rather than himself.

Now he was one of those who came from heaven, and in a former life had dwelt in a well-ordered State, but his virtue was a matter of habit only, and he had no philosophy. And it was true of others who were similarly overtaken, that the greater number of them came from heaven and therefore they had never been schooled by trial, whereas the pilgrims who came from earth, having themselves suffered and seen others suffer, were not in a hurry to choose.

And owing to this inexperience of theirs, and also because the lot was a chance, many of the souls exchanged a good destiny for an evil or an evil for a good."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: But when he had time to reflect and saw what his choice actually contained, he beat his breast and wailed over his decision, completely forgetting the prophet's proclamation. Instead of blaming himself for his misfortune, he accused luck, the gods, everything except himself.

This soul had come from heaven. In his previous life he had lived in a well-ordered state, but his virtue was merely habitual -- he had no philosophy. This was true of many others who made similar mistakes: most of them came from heaven and had never been tested by suffering. The souls who came from earth, having suffered themselves and watched others suffer, were far more cautious in their choosing.

Because of this inexperience, and because the lottery was random, many souls traded a good destiny for an evil one, or an evil for a good.
D: Elegant: The first chooser, discovering his fate, wailed and blamed the gods -- anyone but himself. He had come from heaven, where his virtue was habitual, not philosophical. Most who chose poorly came from heaven, untested by suffering. The earth-worn souls, schooled by hardship, chose more carefully. Inexperience and the randomness of the lots caused many to exchange good destinies for evil ones.
D: Summary: The first chooser, realizing his terrible mistake, blames the gods rather than himself. He had lived virtuously by habit in a previous life but lacked philosophical understanding. Those from heaven, untested by suffering, generally chose poorly, while souls who had suffered on earth chose more wisely.
D: Distilled: Habitual virtue without philosophy fails the ultimate test -- only those schooled by suffering and guided by wisdom choose well.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- "His virtue was a matter of habit only, and he had no philosophy" -- this is one of the most important lines in the Republic, distinguishing genuine philosophical virtue from mere habituation -- It validates the entire Republic's project: philosophical education, not just good habits, is necessary for true virtue -- Those from heaven (who lived good lives) are more vulnerable than those from earth (who suffered) -- Cross-ref: Republic Book VII (514a-521b) on the necessity of philosophical education beyond mere opinion -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 82a-b on the distinction between philosophical and customary virtue -- Cross-ref: Meno 97a-98b on the instability of true opinion without knowledge

D: Original: For if a man had always on his arrival in this world dedicated himself from the first to sound philosophy, and had been moderately fortunate in the number of the lot, he might, as the messenger reported, be happy here, and also his journey to another life and return to this, instead of being rough and underground, would be smooth and heavenly. Most curious, he said, was the spectacle --sad and laughable and strange; for the choice of the souls was in most cases based on their experience of a previous life. There he saw the soul which had once been Orpheus choosing the life of a swan out of enmity to the race of women, hating to be born of a woman because they had been his murderers; he beheld also the soul of Thamyras choosing the life of a nightingale; birds, on the other hand, like the swan and other musicians, wanting to be men. The soul which obtained the twentieth lot chose the life of a lion, and this was the soul of Ajax the son of Telamon, who would not be a man, remembering the injustice which was done him the judgment about the arms.
D: Narrative:
"For if a man had always on his arrival in this world dedicated himself from the first to sound philosophy, and had been moderately fortunate in the number of the lot, he might, as the messenger reported, be happy here, and also his journey to another life and return to this, instead of being rough and underground, would be smooth and heavenly.

Most curious, he said, was the spectacle -- sad and laughable and strange; for the choice of the souls was in most cases based on their experience of a previous life. There he saw the soul which had once been Orpheus choosing the life of a swan out of enmity to the race of women, hating to be born of a woman because they had been his murderers; he beheld also the soul of Thamyras choosing the life of a nightingale; birds, on the other hand, like the swan and other musicians, wanting to be men.

The soul which obtained the twentieth lot chose the life of a lion, and this was the soul of Ajax the son of Telamon, who would not be a man, remembering the injustice which was done him in the judgment about the arms."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: If a person had always dedicated himself to genuine philosophy from the start, and been reasonably fortunate in the lottery, he could be happy in this world, and his passage between lives -- rather than being rough and underground -- would be smooth and heavenly.

The spectacle of the choosing, Er reported, was extraordinary -- at once sad, laughable, and strange. Most souls based their choices on their previous life's experiences.

He saw the soul that had once been Orpheus choose the life of a swan, out of hatred for the race of women -- since women had murdered him, he refused to be born from a woman. The soul of Thamyras chose the life of a nightingale. Meanwhile, birds like swans and other musical creatures wanted to become human.

The soul that drew the twentieth lot chose the life of a lion. This was Ajax, son of Telamon, who refused to be a man again, still remembering the injustice done to him in the judgment over Achilles' armor.
D: Elegant: Philosophy ensures happiness across lifetimes and smooth passage between them. The choosing spectacle was sad, laughable, and strange -- souls chose based on previous experience. Orpheus, murdered by women, chose to be a swan rather than be born from one. Thamyras became a nightingale. Ajax, still bitter over the judgment of Achilles' arms, refused to be human again and chose a lion's life.
D: Summary: Philosophy guarantees happiness across lifetimes. The choosing was a strange spectacle: Orpheus chose to be a swan out of hatred for women who killed him; Thamyras became a nightingale; Ajax chose a lion's life, still bitter about the unjust judgment over Achilles' armor.
D: Distilled: Souls choose their next lives based on past experience -- philosophy alone ensures wise choosing across all lifetimes.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- Orpheus: legendary musician torn apart by Maenads (Bacchantes/women of Thrace) -- Thamyras (Thamyris): mythic poet/musician who challenged the Muses and was blinded (Iliad II.594-600) -- Ajax, son of Telamon: Greek hero who went mad and killed himself after the armor of the dead Achilles was awarded to Odysseus instead (dramatized in Sophocles' Ajax) -- The Ajax story is also told in Odyssey 11.543-567, where Ajax's shade refuses to speak to Odysseus -- Cross-ref: Odyssey 11.543-567 (Ajax in the underworld) -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 248d-249b on the cycle of reincarnation -- Cross-ref: Republic Book III (386b-387c) on heroic behavior in the face of death

D: Original: The next was Agamemnon, who took the life of an eagle, because, like Ajax, he hated human nature by reason of his sufferings. About the middle came the lot of Atalanta; she, seeing the great fame of an athlete, was unable to resist the temptation: and after her there followed the soul of Epeus the son of Panopeus passing into the nature of a woman cunning in the arts; and far away among the last who chose, the soul of the jester Thersites was putting on the form of a monkey. There came also the soul of Odysseus having yet to make a choice, and his lot happened to be the last of them all.
D: Narrative:
"The next was Agamemnon, who took the life of an eagle, because, like Ajax, he hated human nature by reason of his sufferings. About the middle came the lot of Atalanta; she, seeing the great fame of an athlete, was unable to resist the temptation: and after her there followed the soul of Epeus the son of Panopeus passing into the nature of a woman cunning in the arts; and far away among the last who chose, the soul of the jester Thersites was putting on the form of a monkey.

There came also the soul of Odysseus having yet to make a choice, and his lot happened to be the last of them all."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: Next came Agamemnon, who chose the life of an eagle -- like Ajax, he had grown to hate human nature because of his sufferings. Around the middle of the choosing, Atalanta's lot came up. Seeing the great glory attached to an athlete's life, she couldn't resist the temptation. After her, the soul of Epeus, son of Panopeus, passed into the nature of a woman skilled in the arts. Far toward the end, among the last to choose, the soul of the buffoon Thersites was putting on the form of a monkey.

Then came the soul of Odysseus, who still had to make his choice. His lot happened to be the very last of all.
D: Elegant: Agamemnon, hating humanity after his sufferings, chose an eagle. Atalanta couldn't resist an athlete's fame. Epeus became a craftswoman. Thersites became a monkey. Last of all came Odysseus, with the final lot.
D: Summary: More famous souls make their choices: Agamemnon becomes an eagle out of hatred for humanity, Atalanta is drawn to athletic fame, Epeus becomes a craftswoman, and Thersites becomes a monkey. Odysseus, with the very last lot, has yet to choose.
D: Distilled: Each famous soul's choice reflects its previous life's experience and character -- Agamemnon an eagle, Atalanta an athlete, Thersites a monkey -- with Odysseus choosing last.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- Agamemnon: king of Mycenae, leader of the Greek army at Troy, murdered by his wife Clytemnestra on his return -- Atalanta: mythic huntress and athlete, known for racing her suitors -- Epeus: builder of the Trojan Horse (Odyssey 8.493, 11.523); his choice of a craftswoman's life fits his artisan nature -- Thersites: the ugly, insolent commoner in the Iliad (II.211-277), beaten by Odysseus -- The monkey choice for Thersites is comic and fitting -- Cross-ref: Iliad II.211-277 (Thersites) -- Cross-ref: Odyssey 11.405-434 (Agamemnon's shade tells Odysseus of his murder) -- Cross-ref: Apollodorus, Library III.9.2 (Atalanta's footrace)

D: Original: Now the recollection of former tolls had disenchanted him of ambition, and he went about for a considerable time in search of the life of a private man who had no cares; he had some difficulty in finding this, which was lying about and had been neglected by everybody else; and when he saw it, he said that he would have done the had his lot been first instead of last, and that he was delighted to have it. And not only did men pass into animals, but I must also mention that there were animals tame and wild who changed into one another and into corresponding human natures --the good into the gentle and the evil into the savage, in all sorts of combinations.
D: Narrative:
"Now the recollection of former toils had disenchanted him of ambition, and he went about for a considerable time in search of the life of a private man who had no cares; he had some difficulty in finding this, which was lying about and had been neglected by everybody else; and when he saw it, he said that he would have done the same had his lot been first instead of last, and that he was delighted to have it.

And not only did men pass into animals, but I must also mention that there were animals tame and wild who changed into one another and into corresponding human natures -- the good into the gentle and the evil into the savage, in all sorts of combinations."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: The memory of his former struggles had cured Odysseus of all ambition. He spent a long time searching for the life of an ordinary private person free from cares. He had difficulty finding it -- it was lying off to the side, ignored by everyone else. When he found it, he said he would have chosen the same life even if he'd drawn the first lot instead of the last, and he was delighted with it.

I should also mention that the transformations went in every direction: not only did human souls pass into animal forms, but tame and wild animals changed into one another and into human natures -- the good becoming gentle creatures and the evil becoming savage ones, in every possible combination.
D: Elegant: Odysseus, cured of ambition by his past sufferings, searched long for the life of a quiet, carefree private man. He found it neglected by everyone else, and said he'd have chosen it even with the first lot. Souls also crossed between human and animal forms in every direction -- good into gentle, evil into savage.
D: Summary: Odysseus, his ambition extinguished by past hardships, deliberately chooses the quiet life of an ordinary private citizen -- a life ignored by everyone else. He declares he would have chosen the same even with the first pick. Souls also transformed freely between human and animal forms.
D: Distilled: Odysseus, wisened by suffering, chooses the quiet life of an obscure private man over all the glories of the world -- and would have chosen it first or last.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- Odysseus's choice is the moral climax of the Myth of Er -- The man of action and cunning chooses obscurity over glory -- This resonates with Socrates' own preference for the examined private life over political ambition -- The neglected life "lying about" suggests that the best choice is often overlooked -- Cross-ref: Odyssey 11.488-491 where Achilles' shade tells Odysseus he'd rather be a living slave than king of the dead -- Cross-ref: Republic Book VII (519c-521b) on philosophers preferring contemplation to political power -- Cross-ref: Apology 36b-37a on Socrates' choice of private philosophical life

D: Original: All the souls had now chosen their lives, and they went in the order of their choice to Lachesis, who sent with them the genius whom they had severally chosen, to be the guardian of their lives and the fulfiller of the choice: this genius led the souls first to Clotho, and drew them within the revolution of the spindle impelled by her hand, thus ratifying the destiny of each; and then, when they were fastened to this, carried them to Atropos, who spun the threads and made them irreversible, whence without turning round they passed beneath the throne of Necessity; and when they had all passed, they marched on in a scorching heat to the plain of Forgetfulness, which was a barren waste destitute of trees and verdure; and then towards evening they encamped by the river of Unmindfulness, whose water no vessel can hold; of this they were all obliged to drink a certain quantity, and those who were not saved by wisdom drank more than was necessary; and each one as he drank forgot all things. Now after they had gone to rest, about the middle of the night there was a thunderstorm and earthquake, and then in an instant they were driven upwards in all manner of ways to their birth, like stars shooting. He himself was hindered from drinking the water. But in what manner or by what means he returned to the body he could not say; only, in the morning, awaking suddenly, he found himself lying on the pyre.
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: Once all souls had chosen, they proceeded in the order of their choice to Lachesis. She assigned each soul the guiding spirit it had chosen, to serve as guardian and fulfiller of its destiny. This spirit led each soul first to Clotho, drawing it into the revolution of the spindle driven by her hand, thus ratifying the chosen destiny. Then it carried the soul to Atropos, who spun the threads and made them irreversible. From there, without looking back, they passed beneath the throne of Necessity.

Then they all marched through scorching heat across the Plain of Forgetfulness -- a barren wasteland without trees or greenery. Toward evening they camped by the River of Unmindfulness, whose water no vessel can contain. Every soul was required to drink a certain amount. Those not preserved by wisdom drank far more than necessary, and each one forgot everything as they drank.

After they lay down to rest, around midnight a thunderstorm and earthquake struck. In an instant, the souls were hurled upward in all directions toward their births, like shooting stars.

Er himself was prevented from drinking the water. How he returned to his body he couldn't say. He only knew that in the morning he awoke suddenly and found himself lying on the funeral pyre.
D: Elegant: Each soul received its chosen guardian spirit, was ratified by Clotho, made irreversible by Atropos, and passed beneath Necessity's throne. Then they crossed the scorching Plain of Forgetfulness to the River of Unmindfulness, whose water erases all memory. Those without wisdom drank too deeply and forgot everything.
