At midnight, a thunderstorm launched them toward rebirth like shooting stars. Er alone was forbidden to drink and returned to his body, waking on the pyre.
D: Summary: After choosing, each soul receives its guardian spirit and has its destiny ratified by the Fates. They cross the Plain of Forgetfulness and drink from the River of Unmindfulness -- the unwise drinking deeply and forgetting everything. At midnight they are launched toward rebirth. Er alone, forbidden to drink, returns to his body and wakes on the funeral pyre.
D: Distilled: The chosen destinies are sealed by the Fates, then all memory is erased by the River of Unmindfulness -- except for Er, who returns to tell the tale.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- The three Fates complete their roles: Lachesis (allots), Clotho (ratifies), Atropos (makes irreversible) -- The Plain of Forgetfulness (Lethe pedion) and River of Unmindfulness (Ameles potamos) echo the Greek underworld geography -- "Those not saved by wisdom drank more" -- philosophy preserves memory/understanding even across incarnations -- Souls launched "like shooting stars" is a vivid image of rebirth -- Er's exemption from drinking allows the tale to be told -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 248c-249b on the role of forgetfulness in the cycle of reincarnation -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 72e-77d on recollection (anamnesis) as evidence of previous knowledge -- Cross-ref: Virgil's Aeneid VI.713-751 (the River Lethe, clearly influenced by this passage)

D: Original: And thus, Glaucon, the tale has been saved and has not perished, and will save us if we are obedient to the word spoken; and we shall pass safely over the river of Forgetfulness and our soul will not be defiled. Wherefore my counsel is that we hold fast ever to the heavenly way and follow after justice and virtue always, considering that the soul is immortal and able to endure every sort of good and every sort of evil. Thus shall we live dear to one another and to the gods, both while remaining here and when, like conquerors in the games who go round to gather gifts, we receive our reward. And it shall be well with us both in this life and in the pilgrimage of a thousand years which we have been describing.
D: Narrative: "And thus, Glaucon, the tale has been saved and has not perished, and will save us if we are obedient to the word spoken; and we shall pass safely over the river of Forgetfulness and our soul will not be defiled. Wherefore my counsel is that we hold fast ever to the heavenly way and follow after justice and virtue always, considering that the soul is immortal and able to endure every sort of good and every sort of evil. Thus shall we live dear to one another and to the gods, both while remaining here and when, like conquerors in the games who go round to gather gifts, we receive our reward. And it shall be well with us both in this life and in the pilgrimage of a thousand years which we have been describing."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates: And so, Glaucon, the tale has been saved and has not perished -- and it will save us, if we heed its message. We shall pass safely over the River of Forgetfulness and our souls will not be corrupted.

My counsel is this: hold fast always to the heavenly way. Pursue justice and virtue in everything, remembering that the soul is immortal and able to endure every form of good and evil. In this way we shall live beloved by one another and by the gods, both during our time here and when -- like victorious athletes making their rounds to collect prizes -- we receive our reward.

And it shall be well with us, both in this life and in the pilgrimage of a thousand years we have been describing.
D: Elegant: The tale is saved and will save us if we heed it. Hold fast to the heavenly way, pursue justice and virtue always, knowing the soul is immortal. So shall we live dear to each other and to the gods, and receive our reward like victorious athletes. It shall be well with us in this life and in the thousand-year pilgrimage to come.
D: Summary: Socrates concludes the Republic with a call to hold fast to justice and virtue, knowing the soul is immortal. The tale of Er will save us if we heed it: we shall live beloved by gods and men, and receive our reward both in this life and across the thousand-year journey of the soul.
D: Distilled: Hold fast to the heavenly way, pursue justice always, and it shall be well with us in this life and in the thousand-year pilgrimage of the immortal soul.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates addressing Glaucon -- This is the final passage of the Republic -- "The tale has been saved" (mythos esothe) puns on salvation through story/myth -- "Like conquerors in the games" returns to the athletic metaphor from 10.49 -- "The pilgrimage of a thousand years" refers to the cycle described in the Myth of Er -- The closing exhortation unites all the Republic's themes: justice, virtue, the immortal soul, divine reward, the good life -- Cross-ref: Republic Book I (352d) where the question of justice first arose -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 114d-115a for a similar closing exhortation about the soul's fate -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 527a-e for a parallel closing appeal to live justly -- Cross-ref: Laws 715e-716b on following God and the heavenly way
